topics.title: Basic Concepts of Thermodynamics - Microscopic & Macroscopic Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Thermodynamics - Microscopic & Macroscopic Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Thermodynamics?

**Thermodynamics** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the study of **energy**, its various forms, the laws governing the transformation of energy from one form to another, and the **ability of energy to do work** or transfer heat. It is fundamentally concerned with the macroscopic behavior of matter and the relationships between heat, work, temperature, and energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO1):**
> The course outcome PCMUT403.1 expects students to *understand the fundamental concepts of thermodynamics, distinguish between microscopic and macroscopic approaches, define thermodynamic systems, properties, state, processes, and equilibrium.*

In the KTU 2024 B.Tech Mechanical / Allied Engineering curriculum, thermodynamics forms the **foundational pillar** for subsequent core subjects such as Thermal Engineering, Heat Transfer, Refrigeration & Air Conditioning, Internal Combustion Engines, and Gas Dynamics. Every advanced energy system — from a steam power plant in Kayamkulam to a cryogenic engine in ISRO — is analyzed using thermodynamics.

### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Approaches to Thermodynamics

Thermodynamics describes physical systems in **two distinct, complementary ways**:

#### A) Macroscopic Approach (Classical Thermodynamics)

- The **macroscopic approach** considers matter as a **continuous, homogeneous medium** (continuum) without worrying about what is happening at the molecular level.
- It deals with **bulk, average, measurable quantities** like pressure ($P$), temperature ($T$), volume ($V$), mass ($m$), and density ($\rho$) — quantities that can be directly measured with laboratory instruments.
- Only a **small number of variables** (typically 2–3) are needed to fully describe the state of a simple system.
- The classical laws (Zeroth, First, Second, Third) are derived purely from **human experience and experimental observations** of bulk matter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
> Imagine you are observing a massive **cricket stadium** filled with 50,000 people. You, sitting at the top, don't see individual people — you see the **crowd as a whole**, the noise level, the overall energy, and the temperature of the air. You describe the system with average values: average temperature, total noise, total energy. This is the **macroscopic approach**. You don't need to track each person individually to understand the system's behavior.

#### B) Microscopic Approach (Statistical Thermodynamics)

- The **microscopic approach** considers matter as a **collection of a huge number of discrete molecules** (typically on the order of $10^{23}$ per kg-mol) and tries to explain the bulk behavior from the **statistical average** of molecular motion.
- It deals with **molecular-level properties** like the velocity of individual molecules, their kinetic energy, intermolecular forces, and energy distributions.
- The macroscopic properties like pressure and temperature are interpreted as the **statistical averages** of molecular behavior:
  - **Temperature** → measure of average kinetic energy of molecules
  - **Pressure** → rate of change of momentum of molecules striking a wall
- This approach uses **statistical mechanics** (Maxwell-Boltzmann, Fermi-Dirac, Bose-Einstein statistics) and **quantum mechanics** to predict bulk properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
> Now imagine you have a **super-powerful microscope** and you can see each of the 50,000 people individually. You track every person, their speed, their collisions, their direction. The macroscopic "crowd temperature" emerges as the *average energy* of all individuals. The macroscopic "pressure" at the stadium gate is the *average rate at which people push against the gate*. This is the **microscopic approach** — analyzing the system by aggregating individual molecular behavior.

### 1.3 Why Both Approaches?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two approaches are **not competitors** — they are **complementary**. Classical (macroscopic) thermodynamics is sufficient for **engineering design and analysis** (the KTU focus), while statistical (microscopic) thermodynamics provides the **theoretical foundation** explaining *why* classical laws work. For KTU examinations, you will primarily use the **macroscopic approach**, but the microscopic viewpoint is essential for understanding concepts like entropy, specific heats, and the kinetic theory of gases.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants You Must Memorize

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Units |
|----------|--------|-------|-------|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ or $R_u$ or $\bar{R}$ | **8.314** | kJ/kgmol·K |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8314** | J/kgmol·K |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **0.0821** | L·atm/kgmol·K |
| Boltzmann's Constant | $k_B$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³** | J/K |
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³** | molecules/kgmol |
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325** | kPa |
| Standard Temperature | $T_{std}$ | **273.15** | K (0 °C) |
| NTP Conditions | $P, T$ | **101.325 kPa, 293.15 K** | — |
| STP Conditions | $P, T$ | **101.325 kPa, 273.15 K** | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Comparison: Microscopic vs. Macroscopic Approach

Let us analyze the two approaches systematically along multiple dimensions that KTU examiners love to ask as short answer / comparison questions:

| Parameter | Macroscopic Approach | Microscopic Approach |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Alternate Name** | Classical / Continuum / Phenomenological Approach | Statistical / Molecular Approach |
| **Treatment of Matter** | Continuous medium (continuum) | Discrete molecules (atoms, particles) |
| **Variables Used** | Few — $P, V, T, m$ (measurable) | Many — molecular positions, velocities, energies |
| **Number of Variables** | Small (2–3 for simple systems) | Extremely large ($10^{23}$ order) |
| **Mathematical Complexity** | Low — uses algebra, calculus | Very high — uses probability, statistics, quantum mechanics |
| **Tools Required** | Pressure gauge, thermometer, manometer | Hypothetical molecular models, statistical averaging |
| **Laws Used** | Zeroth, First, Second, Third Laws | Statistical mechanics + Quantum theory |
| **Validity** | Engineering design, large-scale systems | Theoretical foundation, small-scale phenomena |
| **KTU Application Focus** | **Primary focus of this course** | Supporting role (kinetic theory, entropy derivation) |
| **Examples of Use** | Design of I.C. engines, boilers, turbines, refrigerators | Justification of gas laws, specific heat theory, radiation |

### 2.2 The Continuum Hypothesis (Foundation of Macroscopic Approach)

A critical prerequisite for the macroscopic approach is the **Continuum Hypothesis**:

> **Continuum Hypothesis:** Matter is assumed to be a continuous, structureless substance whose properties (density, pressure, temperature) are well-defined at every point in space and vary continuously with position and time.

**Validity Condition:** The continuum hypothesis is valid as long as the **length scale of the physical phenomenon** ($L$) is much larger than the **mean free path** ($\lambda$) of the molecules:

$$\frac{L}{\lambda} \gg 1$$

This means the system contains a statistically meaningful number of molecules in any infinitesimal volume $dV$. For all standard engineering systems (engines, turbines, pipes, compressors), this condition is amply satisfied.

> [!NOTE]
> **When the Continuum Hypothesis Fails:**
> In extremely rarefied gases, very high-altitude flight (stratosphere, near-space), MEMS devices, and nanofluids, $L/\lambda \to 1$ or less. There, the microscopic approach (or rarefied gas dynamics / molecular dynamics) must be used.

### 2.3 Concept of a Thermodynamic System

A **thermodynamic system** is defined as a **definite quantity of matter or a region in space chosen for study**, upon which the attention is focused for analysis. Everything outside the system is called the **surroundings**. The system is separated from the surroundings by a **boundary** (real or imaginary, fixed or movable, with or without thickness).

### 2.4 Classification of Thermodynamic Systems

This is a **very high-yield topic** in KTU Module 1. Be sure to memorize the following classification:

#### (a) Closed System (Control Mass)

- **Definition:** A system in which the **mass (amount of matter) is fixed**, but energy (heat and work) can cross the boundary.
- **Key Property:** No mass transfer occurs across the boundary. Energy transfer (in the form of heat and work) is permitted.
- **Analogy:** A sealed pressure cooker on a stove — matter cannot leave, but heat enters and work is done on the gas.
- **Typical Examples:** Piston-cylinder assembly, batch chemical reactor, sealed can of gas, a fixed mass of gas in a balloon.

#### (b) Open System (Control Volume)

- **Definition:** A system in which **both mass and energy can cross the boundary**.
- **Key Property:** A **control volume** is a fixed region in space through which mass flows. The contents inside the control volume may change with time, but the volume itself is fixed in space.
- **Analogy:** A pipe with water flowing through it, a turbine, a compressor, an air-conditioner evaporator, a rocket nozzle.
- **Typical Examples:** Steam turbine, compressor, nozzle, diffuser, heat exchanger, pump.

#### (c) Isolated System

- **Definition:** A system in which **neither mass nor energy can cross the boundary**.
- **Key Property:** Completely disconnected from the surroundings. The total energy of an isolated system is **constant** (the First Law of Thermodynamics).
- **Analogy:** A perfectly insulated, rigid, sealed thermos flask (ideal case).
- **Typical Examples:** Universe (the ultimate isolated system), Dewar flask (approximate), closed rigid adiabatic container.

#### (d) Adiabatic System

- A system in which **no heat transfer** ($Q = 0$) occurs across the boundary. The system may or may not allow work and mass transfer.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete formula set** for Module 1 — Basic Concepts. KTU ESE and internal exams for this module will be solved entirely using these equations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **Ideal Gas Equation (Mass form)** | $P \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$ | $m$ = mass, $R$ = specific gas constant | $P$ in kPa, $V$ in m³, $T$ in K |
| 2 | **Ideal Gas Equation (Molar form)** | $P \cdot V = n \cdot \bar{R} \cdot T$ | $n$ = kgmol of gas | $P$ in kPa, $V$ in m³, $T$ in K |
| 3 | **Avogadro's Number** | $N = n \cdot N_A$ | $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules/kgmol | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Specific Gas Constant** | $R = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{M}$ | $M$ = molar mass in kg/kgmol | kJ/kg·K |
| 5 | **Boltzmann Relation** | $P \cdot V = N \cdot k_B \cdot T$ | For $N$ molecules | $P$ in Pa, $V$ in m³, $T$ in K |
| 6 | **Density Definition** | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Mass per unit volume | kg/m³ |
| 7 | **Specific Volume Definition** | $v = \dfrac{V}{m} = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Volume per unit mass | m³/kg |
| 8 | **Pressure Definition** | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Normal force per unit area | Pa = N/m² |
| 9 | **Universal Gas Constant** | $\bar{R} = k_B \cdot N_A$ | Connecting micro and macro constants | **8.314 kJ/kgmol·K** |
| 10 | **Molar Mass Relation** | $M = \dfrac{m}{n}$ | Mass per kgmol | kg/kgmol |
| 11 | **Temperature Conversion** | $T(K) = t(°C) + 273.15$ | Kelvin ↔ Celsius | K |
| 12 | **Pressure Conversion** | $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 760 \text{ mm Hg}$ | Standard atmospheric | various |
| 13 | **No. of Moles** | $n = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{N}{N_A}$ | Conversion between mass and molecules | kgmol |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants (Kerala Context):** The Rajiv Gandhi Combined Cycle Power Plant at Kayamkulam and NTPC's thermal plants are designed using open system analysis of turbines, boilers, condensers, and pumps.
- **IC Engines:** The Otto cycle, Diesel cycle, and Dual cycle are analyzed using the **closed system** approach (piston-cylinder).
- **Rocket Propulsion:** Open system analysis of nozzles using mass, momentum, and energy conservation.
- **Refrigeration:** Closed system approach for the compressor, open system for throttle valves and evaporators.
- **Microscopic Approach Applications:** Justification of specific heat values, prediction of gas behavior at extreme conditions, semiconductor physics, plasma physics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to Other Modules:**
> Module 1 (this) lays the foundation. Module 2 (First Law) uses closed/open system energy balances. Module 3 (Second Law) uses macroscopic properties to define entropy. The microscopic approach resurfaces in Module 5 (Psychrometrics & Gas Mixtures) when discussing partial pressures and Dalton's law.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Derivation Mandate:** In every KTU exam for Module 1, at least one derivation is asked. The three most frequent are: (1) Ideal gas equation, (2) Specific gas constant, (3) Pressure from kinetic theory. All three are derived below in full detail.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pressure of an Ideal Gas from Kinetic Theory (Microscopic Approach)

This derivation shows the **microscopic derivation of pressure** — a very common KTU question worth 7–14 marks.

**Statement:** To derive an expression for the pressure exerted by an ideal gas on the walls of a container using the kinetic theory of gases.

#### Assumptions of Kinetic Theory

1. A gas consists of a **very large number of identical, rigid, perfectly elastic spherical molecules**.
2. The **volume of individual molecules** is negligible compared to the total volume of the gas (molecules are point masses).
3. **No intermolecular forces** act between molecules except during collisions (which are instantaneous).
4. The molecules are in **random, continuous motion**, colliding with each other and with the walls of the container.
5. **Collisions are perfectly elastic** — no loss of kinetic energy.
6. Between collisions, molecules move in **straight lines** with constant velocity (Newton's first law).
7. Statistical equilibrium: the average distribution of molecules is uniform in space, and there is no preferred direction of motion.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Setup:** Consider a cubical container of side length $L$ containing $N$ molecules of an ideal gas, each of mass $m_0$, moving randomly with velocities $c_1, c_2, c_3, \ldots, c_N$. For simplicity, consider only the $x$-component of velocity: $c_{x1}, c_{x2}, \ldots, c_{xN}$.

Consider a single molecule (say molecule $i$) moving with $x$-component of velocity $c_{xi}$.

**Step 1: Time between successive collisions with one wall**

The molecule travels from one wall to the opposite wall and back — a total distance of $2L$. The time taken for one round trip is:

$$\Delta t = \frac{2L}{c_{xi}}$$

**Step 2: Change in momentum during one collision with the wall**

When the molecule strikes a wall perpendicularly (elastic collision), its $x$-component of velocity reverses from $+c_{xi}$ to $-c_{xi}$.

The change in momentum of the molecule (per collision) is:

$$\Delta p_{xi} = m_0 \cdot (-c_{xi}) - m_0 \cdot (+c_{xi}) = -2 m_0 c_{xi}$$

The magnitude of change in momentum is $\vert \Delta p_{xi} \vert = 2 m_0 c_{xi}$.

**Step 3: Force exerted by one molecule on the wall**

By Newton's Second Law, Force = rate of change of momentum:

$$F_i = \frac{\vert \Delta p_{xi} \vert}{\Delta t} = \frac{2 m_0 c_{xi}}{2L/c_{xi}} = \frac{m_0 c_{xi}^{2}}{L}$$

**Step 4: Total force from all $N$ molecules**

Summing over all $N$ molecules in the container:

$$F_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{m_0 c_{xi}^{2}}{L} = \frac{m_0}{L} \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{xi}^{2}$$

**Step 5: Pressure = Force / Area**

The area of one wall is $A = L^2$. Therefore, pressure is:

$$P = \frac{F_{total}}{A} = \frac{m_0}{L \cdot L^2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{xi}^{2} = \frac{m_0}{L^3} \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{xi}^{2} = \frac{m_0}{V} \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{xi}^{2}$$

where $V = L^3$ is the volume of the container.

**Step 6: Introduction of mean square velocity**

Define the mean square $x$-velocity as the average of $c_{xi}^2$ over all $N$ molecules:

$$\overline{c_x^{2}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{xi}^{2}$$

Substituting:

$$P = \frac{m_0 \cdot N \cdot \overline{c_x^{2}}}{V}$$

**Step 7: Isotropic velocity distribution**

By symmetry (no preferred direction), the mean square velocities in all three directions are equal:

$$\overline{c_x^{2}} = \overline{c_y^{2}} = \overline{c_z^{2}}$$

The total mean square velocity is:

$$\overline{c^{2}} = \overline{c_x^{2}} + \overline{c_y^{2}} + \overline{c_z^{2}} = 3 \overline{c_x^{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$\overline{c_x^{2}} = \frac{\overline{c^{2}}}{3}$$

**Step 8: Final expression for pressure**

$$\boxed{P = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{m_0 \cdot N \cdot \overline{c^{2}}}{V} = \frac{1}{3} \rho \overline{c^{2}}}$$

where $\rho = m_0 N / V$ is the mass density of the gas.

This is the famous **fundamental kinetic theory equation**:

$$P = \frac{1}{3} \rho \overline{c^{2}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between Kinetic Energy and Temperature

Building on the pressure expression:

**Step 1:** Start with $P V = \frac{1}{3} m_0 N \overline{c^{2}}$

**Step 2:** Recall from thermodynamics that for an ideal gas: $P V = m R T$, where $m$ is total mass.

**Step 3:** Equating the two expressions:

$$m R T = \frac{1}{3} m_0 N \overline{c^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Since $m = m_0 N$ (total mass = mass per molecule × number of molecules):

$$m_0 N R T = \frac{1}{3} m_0 N \overline{c^{2}}$$

**Step 5:** Canceling $m_0 N$:

$$R T = \frac{1}{3} \overline{c^{2}}$$

**Step 6:** The average translational kinetic energy per molecule is:

$$\bar{E} = \frac{1}{2} m_0 \overline{c^{2}}$$

**Step 7:** Substituting $\overline{c^{2}} = 3 R T$:

$$\bar{E} = \frac{1}{2} m_0 (3 R T) = \frac{3}{2} m_0 R T$$

**Step 8:** Defining specific gas constant per molecule (using Boltzmann constant $k_B = \bar{R} / N_A$):

$$\boxed{\bar{E} = \frac{3}{2} k_B T \quad \text{(per molecule)}}$$

This is a foundational result: **the absolute temperature of a gas is directly proportional to the average translational kinetic energy of its molecules**. This bridges the microscopic and macroscopic viewpoints.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Gas Constant $R = \bar{R} / M$

**Step 1:** Universal (molar) gas constant: $\bar{R} = 8.314$ kJ/kgmol·K

**Step 2:** For a gas of molar mass $M$ (kg/kgmol), 1 kgmol contains $N_A$ molecules.

**Step 3:** Consider 1 kg of gas. The number of kgmol in 1 kg is:

$$n = \frac{1}{M} \text{ kgmol per kg}$$

**Step 4:** The ideal gas equation for 1 kg of gas is:

$$P v = R T$$

where $v$ is the specific volume (m³/kg).

**Step 5:** For 1 kgmol, the equation is:

$$P V_m = \bar{R} T$$

where $V_m$ is the molar volume (m³/kgmol).

**Step 6:** Since $V_m = M \cdot v$ (molar volume = mass per kgmol × specific volume):

$$P \cdot M \cdot v = \bar{R} T$$

**Step 7:** Comparing with $P v = R T$:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{\bar{R}}{M}}$$

**Numerical example:** For air, $M = 28.97$ kg/kgmol, so:

$$R_{air} = \frac{8.314}{28.97} = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

For oxygen ($O_2$), $M = 32$ kg/kgmol:

$$R_{O_2} = \frac{8.314}{32} = 0.2598 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
> *A rigid container of volume 0.5 m³ contains 2 kg of an ideal gas. The gas is heated such that its temperature rises from 300 K to 500 K. If the specific gas constant is 0.287 kJ/kg·K, find (a) the initial and final pressures, and (b) the change in density.*

**Solution:**

Given: $V = 0.5$ m³ (rigid), $m = 2$ kg, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 500$ K.

**Part (a): Initial and Final Pressures**

Using ideal gas equation: $P V = m R T \implies P = \dfrac{m R T}{V}$

Initial pressure:

$$P_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.287 \times 300}{0.5} = \frac{172.2}{0.5} = 344.4 \text{ kPa}$$

Final pressure:

$$P_2 = \frac{2 \times 0.287 \times 500}{0.5} = \frac{287}{0.5} = 574 \text{ kPa}$$

**Part (b): Change in Density**

Since mass and volume are both constant, density is **unchanged**:

$$\rho_1 = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{2}{0.5} = 4 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho_2 = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{2}{0.5} = 4 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\Delta \rho = \rho_2 - \rho_1 = 0 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Insight:** Density of a gas in a rigid closed container depends only on the mass inside and the container volume — it is **independent of temperature and pressure**. The pressure increases with temperature because the molecules strike the walls faster, not because there are more molecules per unit volume.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture: Macroscopic vs. Microscopic Approach

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how the two approaches are interconnected, their inputs, methods, outputs, and how they unite to provide a complete thermodynamic understanding.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Physical System<br/>Real World Setup"]
    node2["Macroscopic Approach<br/>Continuum Treatment"]
    node3["Microscopic Approach<br/>Molecular Treatment"]
    node4["Measurable Variables<br/>P V T m rho"]
    node5["Molecular Variables<br/>Position Velocity Energy"]
    node6["Classical Laws<br/>Zeroth First Second Third"]
    node7["Statistical Mechanics<br/>Maxwell Boltzmann Fermi Dirac"]
    node8["Engineering Design<br/>Turbines Engines Boilers"]
    node9["Theoretical Foundation<br/>Specific Heat Entropy"]
    node10["Complete Understanding<br/>of Thermodynamics"]
    node11["Observable Universe<br/>All Systems"]
    node1 --> node11
    node11 --> node2
    node11 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node2 --> node6
    node3 --> node5
    node3 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node10
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- All thermodynamic analysis starts with a **Physical System** from the real world.
- The system can be studied through **either** the macroscopic or microscopic approach.
- The **macroscopic approach** uses directly measurable variables and the four classical laws → leads to **engineering design**.
- The **microscopic approach** uses molecular variables and statistical mechanics → leads to **theoretical foundation**.
- Both paths ultimately converge to give a **complete understanding of thermodynamics**.

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Classification of Thermodynamic Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Thermodynamic System<br/>Region Selected for Study"]
    nodeB["Closed System<br/>Control Mass"]
    nodeC["Open System<br/>Control Volume"]
    nodeD["Isolated System<br/>No Mass No Energy"]
    nodeE["Adiabatic System<br/>No Heat Transfer"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Mass FIXED<br/>Energy allowed"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2["Examples<br/>Piston Cylinder<br/>Sealed Reactor"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Mass and Energy<br/>Cross Boundary"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2["Examples<br/>Turbine Compressor<br/>Nozzle Pipe"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Completely Sealed<br/>and Insulated"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2["Example<br/>Perfect Dewar Flask<br/>Universe"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["Q equals 0<br/>W may be nonzero"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeE2["Example<br/>Adiabatic Process<br/>Insulated Piston"]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: System – Boundary – Surroundings

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSurr["SURROUNDINGS<br/>Everything outside"]
    nodeBnd["BOUNDARY<br/>Real or Imaginary<br/>Fixed or Movable"]
    nodeSys["SYSTEM<br/>Matter or Region<br/>under Study"]
    nodeSurr --- nodeBnd
    nodeBnd --- nodeSys
    nodeMass["Mass Transfer<br/>m dot"]
    nodeHeat["Heat Transfer<br/>Q"]
    nodeWork["Work Transfer<br/>W"]
    nodeMass -.crosses.-> nodeBnd
    nodeHeat -.crosses.-> nodeBnd
    nodeWork -.crosses.-> nodeBnd
```

**Caption:** The **boundary** is the interface across which **mass** ($\dot{m}$), **heat** ($Q$), and **work** ($W$) can be exchanged between the system and the surroundings. The boundary may be **fixed** (rigid container) or **movable** (piston-cylinder), and it may be **real** (physical wall) or **imaginary** (mathematical surface around a control volume).

---

### 4.4 Property Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP["Thermodynamic<br/>Properties"]
    nodeI["Intensive Properties<br/>Independent of Mass"]
    nodeE["Extensive Properties<br/>Depend on Mass"]
    nodeI1["Examples<br/>Temperature Pressure<br/>Density Specific Volume"]
    nodeE1["Examples<br/>Volume Mass<br/>Total Energy Entropy"]
    nodeP --> nodeI
    nodeP --> nodeE
    nodeI --> nodeI1
    nodeE --> nodeE1
    nodeS["Specific Properties<br/>Extensive per unit mass<br/>v V over m<br/>u U over m<br/>h H over m"]
    nodeE --> nodeS
```

**Caption:** Specific properties (e.g., specific volume $v$, specific internal energy $u$, specific enthalpy $h$) are **intensive** because they are obtained by dividing an extensive property by mass.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):**
> - Part A: Short answer questions of **3 marks each** (typically from Modules 1 & 2 for Paper-I).
> - Part B: Long answer questions of **14 marks each** with **internal choice** between two alternatives.
> - Each Part B question has two sub-parts: typically **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**.
> - Internal choice: You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (both 14 marks, but from different sub-topics).

---

### 📝 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Distinguish between the microscopic and macroscopic approaches in thermodynamics. Which approach is primarily used in engineering thermodynamics and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Macroscopic Approach | Microscopic Approach |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Matter is treated as | Continuum | Discrete molecules |
| Variables used | Few, measurable (P, V, T) | Many, molecular |
| Mathematical tools | Algebra, calculus | Statistical mechanics |
| Number of equations | Few (2–3) | Extremely large |
| Examples | Piston, turbine | Kinetic theory |

**Engineering use:** The **macroscopic approach** is primarily used in engineering thermodynamics because:
1. It deals with **bulk, directly measurable quantities** (no molecular instruments needed).
2. It requires **fewer variables**, making engineering design calculations tractable.
3. The classical laws (Zeroth, First, Second, Third) are derived experimentally and are sufficient for all engineering applications.

The microscopic approach is reserved for theoretical explanation of gas behavior, specific heats, and entropy at a fundamental level.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define a thermodynamic system. Differentiate between an open system and a closed system with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Thermodynamic System:** A definite quantity of matter or a region in space chosen for study, separated from the surroundings by a boundary.

**Open System (Control Volume):** Both mass and energy can cross the boundary.
- **Example:** A steam turbine, an air compressor, a pipe with flowing water.

**Closed System (Control Mass):** Only energy (heat and work) can cross the boundary; mass is fixed.
- **Example:** A piston-cylinder assembly, a sealed pressure cooker.

**Key Difference:** In a **closed system**, the mass remains constant (no mass transfer). In an **open system**, mass continuously flows across the boundary, and we use a control volume (fixed region in space) for analysis.

---

### 📝 PART B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from each choice.

---

### ✍️ CHOICE 1: Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Thermodynamic system, (ii) Boundary, (iii) Surroundings, (iv) Closed system, (v) Open system, (vi) Isolated system. **OR**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **microscopic approach** of thermodynamics. Derive the expression for the **pressure of an ideal gas** from kinetic theory, clearly stating all assumptions.

**Model Solution (b) — Pressure from Kinetic Theory:**

**Step 1 — Assumptions of Kinetic Theory:** (List 7 assumptions as in Section 3.1 above — 2 marks)

**Step 2 — Derivation:**

Consider $N$ molecules in a cube of side $L$, each of mass $m_0$.

Momentum change per collision with wall: $\Delta p = 2 m_0 c_{xi}$ (1 mark)

Time between collisions: $\Delta t = 2L / c_{xi}$ (0.5 mark)

Force by one molecule: $F_i = 2 m_0 c_{xi}^2 / (2L) = m_0 c_{xi}^2 / L$ (1 mark)

Total force from $N$ molecules:

$$F = \frac{m_0}{L} \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{xi}^{2} = \frac{m_0 N \overline{c_x^{2}}}{L}$$

Pressure:

$$P = \frac{F}{L^2} = \frac{m_0 N \overline{c_x^{2}}}{L^3} = \frac{m_0 N \overline{c_x^{2}}}{V}$$

By isotropy: $\overline{c_x^{2}} = \overline{c_y^{2}} = \overline{c_z^{2}} = \overline{c^2}/3$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{1}{3} \frac{m_0 N \overline{c^2}}{V} = \frac{1}{3} \rho \overline{c^2}}$$

(2 marks for the boxed result and 0.5 mark for the final simplification.)

---

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** State and explain the **ideal gas equation** $PV = mRT$. Derive the relation between the universal gas constant $\bar{R}$ and the specific gas constant $R$.

**Model Solution:**

**Ideal Gas Equation:** $PV = mRT$, where:
- $P$ = absolute pressure (kPa)
- $V$ = volume (m³)
- $m$ = mass of gas (kg)
- $R$ = specific gas constant (kJ/kg·K)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Statement:** The product of pressure and volume of a given mass of an ideal gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.

**Derivation of $R = \bar{R}/M$:**

For 1 kgmol: $P V_m = \bar{R} T$ (1 mark)

For 1 kg: $P v = R T$ (1 mark)

Since $V_m = M v$ (molar volume = molar mass × specific volume):

$$P \cdot M \cdot v = \bar{R} T$$ (1.5 marks)

Comparing: $R = \bar{R}/M$ (1.5 marks)

**Numerical example:** For air, $M = 28.97$ kg/kgmol, $R_{air} = 8.314/28.97 = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K. (2 marks)

---

### ✍️ CHOICE 1: Question B (Alternative 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the concept of **macroscopic approach** in thermodynamics. List the **advantages and limitations** of this approach.

**Model Solution (concise):**

The macroscopic approach treats matter as a **continuum** with bulk measurable properties $P, V, T$. (1 mark)

**Sketch:** A simple block showing system boundary with arrows for heat and work. (1.5 marks)

**Advantages:**
1. Simple, requires few variables (2.5 marks)
2. Direct use of measurable quantities
3. No need for molecular models
4. Sufficient for all engineering design

**Limitations:**
1. Cannot explain molecular phenomena (1 mark)
2. Cannot predict specific heat variations
3. Fails in rarefied gases (very low pressure)

(1 mark for limitations)

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A spherical balloon of 2 m diameter contains air at 30 °C and 200 kPa. Calculate: (i) Mass of air inside, (ii) Number of molecules, (iii) Density of air.

Given: $R_{air} = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules/kgmol, $M_{air} = 28.97$ kg/kgmol.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Volume of sphere: $V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi (1)^3 = 4.189$ m³ (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Convert temperature: $T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15$ K (0.5 mark)

**Step 3:** Mass using $PV = mRT$:

$$m = \frac{PV}{RT} = \frac{200 \times 4.189}{0.287 \times 303.15} = \frac{837.8}{87.0} = 9.63 \text{ kg}$$ (2 marks)

**Step 4:** Number of kgmol: $n = m/M = 9.63/28.97 = 0.3323$ kgmol (1 mark)

**Step 5:** Number of molecules: $N = n \cdot N_A = 0.3323 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 2.00 \times 10^{23}$ molecules (1.5 marks)

**Step 6:** Density: $\rho = m/V = 9.63/4.189 = 2.30$ kg/m³ (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** — temperatures in gas equations MUST be in Kelvin. A 1-mark penalty is standard.
> 2. **Mixing up specific gas constant $R$ and universal gas constant $\bar{R}$** — always check whether the problem gives $M$ or $R$ directly.
> 3. **Units mismatch** — if $R$ is in kJ/kg·K, then $P$ must be in kPa and $V$ in m³ for direct calculation without conversion.
> 4. **Forgetting Avogadro's number** — for "number of molecules" questions, always compute $n = m/M$ first, then $N = n \cdot N_A$.
> 5. **Boundary thickness confusion** — the boundary itself is massless and volumeless; it has no thermodynamic properties.
> 6. **Using gauge pressure** — gas laws use **absolute pressure**. Convert: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.
> 7. **Confusing intensive and extensive properties** — multiplying two intensive properties gives an extensive one (e.g., $P \cdot v$); dividing an extensive by mass gives intensive (e.g., $u = U/m$).

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 (Basic Concepts)**

- [x] **Thermodynamics** = science of energy, its forms, transformations, and ability to do work/transfer heat.
- [x] **Macroscopic Approach** = treats matter as a continuum; uses few measurable variables (P, V, T); primary KTU method.
- [x] **Microscopic Approach** = treats matter as a collection of molecules; uses statistical averaging; explains *why* classical laws work.
- [x] **Continuum Hypothesis** valid when $L/\lambda \gg 1$ (true for all normal engineering systems).
- [x] **System** = region chosen for study; **Surroundings** = everything else; **Boundary** = real/imaginary interface.
- [x] **Closed System** = fixed mass, energy can cross (piston-cylinder).
- [x] **Open System / Control Volume** = both mass and energy can cross (turbine, nozzle, compressor).
- [x] **Isolated System** = neither mass nor energy crosses (perfectly insulated rigid flask, universe).
- [x] **Adiabatic System** = no heat transfer ($Q = 0$).
- [x] **Intensive Properties** = independent of mass (T, P, ρ, v, s).
- [x] **Extensive Properties** = depend on system mass (V, m, U, H, S).
- [x] **Specific Properties** = extensive per unit mass → become intensive (u, h, s).
- [x] **Ideal Gas Equation:** $PV = mRT = n\bar{R}T$ with $\bar{R} = 8.314$ kJ/kgmol·K.
- [x] **Specific Gas Constant:** $R = \bar{R}/M$ (kJ/kg·K).
- [x] **Kinetic Theory Pressure:** $P = \frac{1}{3} \rho \overline{c^2}$.
- [x] **Kinetic Energy–Temperature Link:** $\bar{E} = \frac{3}{2} k_B T$ per molecule.
- [x] **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- [x] **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules/kgmol.
- [x] **Always use absolute temperature (K)** in all gas law equations.
- [x] **Always use absolute pressure (kPa or Pa)** in all gas law equations.

> **Final Tip for KTU Exam:** Draw neat labelled diagrams for system classification and clearly mark the boundary in every schematic — examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for labelled diagrams in Part B questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamic System and Control Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamic System and Control Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermodynamic System (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A thermodynamic system is a precisely defined quantity of matter or a region in space chosen for the purpose of thermodynamic analysis, upon which the attention is focused. Everything external to this chosen region is termed the **surroundings**, and the system is separated from the surroundings by a **boundary** (which may be real or imaginary, fixed or moving).

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, every problem begins with this fundamental identification step. If the engineer does not correctly identify the system, the subsequent application of the **First Law**, **Second Law**, and the mass/energy balance equations becomes meaningless.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary:** The closed surface that envelops the system and separates it from the surroundings. The boundary itself is a mathematical/geometric surface and has **zero thickness**, **zero mass**, and **zero volume**. Heat and work cross the boundary; matter may or may not, depending on the system type.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a chef preparing a dish inside a closed **pressure cooker** on your kitchen stove. Your sole interest lies in observing what happens *inside* the cooker — the water boiling, the steam pressure rising, and the food getting cooked.

- The **pressure cooker + its contents** = your **Thermodynamic System**
- The **stove, the kitchen air, the surrounding room** = the **Surroundings**
- The **inner metallic wall of the cooker** = the **Boundary**
- The heat coming from the stove = crosses the boundary into the system
- The whistle releasing steam = mass leaving the system

Now think of an open kitchen scenario where you are boiling water in a **wide-mouthed open vessel**, and you are interested in the water flowing *through* the vessel (water enters from a tap, leaves as steam from the top). Here, you cannot fix a fixed amount of matter — the water is continuously entering and leaving. This is a **Control Volume** scenario.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Volume (CV) / Open System (KTU Definition):** A control volume is a fixed region in space through which mass, energy, and momentum may flow across its boundaries. It is typically used to analyse devices such as **turbines, compressors, nozzles, diffusers, heat exchangers, pumps, and throttling valves**, where matter continuously enters and exits.

The **key conceptual difference** is:
- **Closed System (Control Mass):** You follow a *fixed identity* of matter.
- **Open System (Control Volume):** You observe a *fixed region in space* through which matter flows.

---

### 1.3 Special Boundary Classifications

| Boundary Property | Description | Physical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Rigid** | Does not move; no work due to expansion/compression | A sealed rigid steel tank |
| **Movable (Non-rigid)** | Deforms; can produce boundary work | Piston-cylinder assembly |
| **Real** | Physically tangible | Walls of a boiler |
| **Imaginary** | Mathematically drawn | Perimeter around a jet engine |

### 1.4 Real-World Engineering Context

In the KTU 2024 mechanical/automobile syllabus, identifying the system is the **first step** in analysing:
- **I.C. Engines** (closed system when fuel is burned inside fixed cylinder walls during combustion)
- **Gas Turbines** (open system — air enters compressor, leaves turbine)
- **Refrigeration Cycles** (compressor = open system; condenser/evaporator = open systems)
- **Hydraulic Accumulators** (closed system with flexible bladder)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse "isolated system" with "closed system". An **isolated system** has *no* interaction whatsoever with surroundings (no heat, no work, no mass). A **closed system** allows heat and work but **no mass transfer** across its boundary.

### 1.5 The Three Principal System Types in KTU Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 — Mandatory Classification:**
> 1. **Closed System (Control Mass):** Fixed mass; only energy (heat & work) crosses the boundary. Mass $m = \text{constant}$.
> 2. **Open System (Control Volume):** Fixed region in space; both mass and energy cross the boundary. $\dot{m}_{in} \neq \dot{m}_{out}$ in general, but for steady state, $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$.
> 3. **Isolated System:** Neither mass nor energy crosses the boundary. A truly isolated system is an **idealisation**; the universe is the only truly isolated system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Thermodynamic Systems

#### 2.1.1 Closed System (Control Mass) — Deep Analysis

A **closed system** is one in which there is **no transfer of mass** across the boundary during the period of observation. However, energy in the form of **heat ($Q$)** and **work ($W$)** can cross the boundary.

**Logical Steps in Analysis:**
- **Step 1:** Identify a fixed quantity of matter (e.g., gas trapped in a piston-cylinder).
- **Step 2:** Define a closed boundary around this matter.
- **Step 3:** Since mass is constant, the **First Law of Thermodynamics** is expressed in terms of total energy (not rates):
$$Q - W = \Delta E_{system} = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$$
- **Step 4:** For most engineering applications where the gas is stationary, $\Delta KE = \Delta PE = 0$, simplifying to:
$$Q - W = \Delta U$$
- **Step 5:** For an ideal gas in a quasi-static process, the boundary work is computed as:
$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$
- **Step 6:** The system boundary may **move** (e.g., piston) and so boundary work occurs, but the matter inside remains the same *identity*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the distinction matters in KTU problems:** When the question says "a rigid tank contains 2 kg of air at 300 K", it is a **closed system**. When it says "air flows steadily through a nozzle at 0.5 kg/s", it is an **open system**. The choice of governing equation (transient vs. steady-state) depends entirely on this identification.

---

#### 2.1.2 Open System (Control Volume) — Deep Analysis

A **control volume** is a region in space bounded by a **control surface**. Mass, energy, and momentum can flow across the control surface.

**Logical Steps in Analysis:**
- **Step 1:** Draw an imaginary closed surface (control surface) around the device of interest (pump, turbine, compressor, nozzle, etc.).
- **Step 2:** Identify all **inlet** and **outlet** streams crossing the control surface.
- **Step 3:** Apply the **conservation of mass** principle:
$$\sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} = \frac{dm_{CV}}{dt}$$
- **Step 4:** Apply the **First Law for a Control Volume**:
$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} + \sum \dot{m}_{out} \left( h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz \right)_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in} \left( h + \frac{ke}{2} + gz \right)_{in}$$
- **Step 5:** For **steady state** (the most common KTU exam case), $\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = 0$, simplifying to:
$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} \left( h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz \right)_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in} \left( h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz \right)_{in}$$

---

#### 2.1.3 Isolated System — Deep Analysis

An **isolated system** has **no interaction** with its surroundings:
- No mass transfer: $\sum \dot{m} = 0$
- No heat transfer: $\dot{Q} = 0$
- No work transfer: $\dot{W} = 0$

The **total energy** of an isolated system remains constant (this is the basis of the **First Law of Thermodynamics**). In reality, no finite system is truly isolated, but the concept is useful for analysis of the **universe as a whole** or as an **idealisation for adiabatic + rigid + no mass flow** conditions.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula reference** for Module 1 (System and Control Volume) as per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation) expectations.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Applicable System | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass conservation (general) | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} = \dfrac{dm_{CV}}{dt}$ | Open system / CV | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 2 | Steady-state mass balance | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | Open system (steady) | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 3 | Single-stream steady CV | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{v} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = \dfrac{A V_{avg}}{v}$ | Open system (steady, 1 inlet, 1 outlet) | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 4 | First Law — Closed system | $Q - W = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$ | Closed | $\text{J}$ or $\text{kJ}$ |
| 5 | First Law — Simplified closed | $Q - W = \Delta U$ (for stationary system) | Closed | $\text{kJ}$ |
| 6 | First Law — Open system (general) | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dfrac{dE_{CV}}{dt} + \dot{m}_{out}\theta_{out} - \dot{m}_{in}\theta_{in}$ | Open / CV | $\text{kW}$ |
| 7 | First Law — Steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$ | Open (steady, 1 inlet, 1 outlet) | $\text{kW}$ |
| 8 | Boundary work (quasi-static) | $W_b = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$ | Closed (piston-cylinder) | $\text{J}$ |
| 9 | Enthalpy definition | $h = u + Pv$ | All systems | $\text{kJ/kg}$ |
| 10 | Specific volume relation | $v = 1/\rho$ | All systems | $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ |
| 11 | Volumetric flow rate | $\dot{V} = A \cdot V_{avg}$ | Open system | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 12 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V_{avg}$ | Open system | $\text{kg/s}$ |

**Where:**
- $\theta = h + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$ is the **flow energy per unit mass** (J/kg or kJ/kg)
- $E_{CV} = U_{CV} + KE_{CV} + PE_{CV}$ is total energy of control volume
- $\dot{W}$ is the **total power** (shaft work + any other work; sign convention: work done **by** the system is positive in thermodynamics, but in mechanical engineering KTU convention, work done **on** the system is taken positive in some textbooks — students must check the convention used in the specific question)

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | System Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **I.C. Engine cylinder** (combustion phase) | Closed | Mass of gas trapped inside cylinder; only heat (from combustion) and work (piston motion) cross boundary |
| **Steam turbine** | Open (steady) | Steam enters at one port, exits at another, continuously |
| **Reciprocating compressor** | Closed (per cycle approximation) | Gas is alternately compressed and pushed out; CV approach also valid |
| **Nozzle/Diffuser** | Open (steady) | Mass flows through, no shaft work |
| **Heat exchanger** | Open (steady) | Two streams, mass conserved per stream |
| **Balloon with helium** | Open (slowly deflating) | Mass is leaving → control volume approach |
| **Sealed thermos flask** | Approximately isolated | No heat, no work, no mass (idealised) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mass Conservation for a Control Volume

> **Starting Principle:** Mass is conserved; it is neither created nor destroyed in any non-nuclear process.

Let us consider a generic control volume (CV) with **n** inlet streams and **m** outlet streams. Let $\dot{m}_i$ be the mass flow rate entering through inlet $i$, and $\dot{m}_e$ be the mass flow rate exiting through outlet $e$.

**Step 1:** Write the net rate of mass flow into the control volume.

$$
\text{Net mass inflow rate} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dot{m}_i - \sum_{e=1}^{m} \dot{m}_e
$$

**Step 2:** This net inflow rate must equal the rate of accumulation of mass inside the control volume (since mass is neither created nor destroyed).

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dot{m}_i - \sum_{e=1}^{m} \dot{m}_e = \frac{dm_{CV}}{dt}
$$

> **Textual Explanation of the Logic:** The left-hand side represents the **net mass** crossing the control surface per unit time. The right-hand side is the **time rate of change of mass** contained within the CV. Equating them is the direct mathematical statement of the conservation principle.

**Step 3 (Special Case — Steady State):** At steady state, the mass inside the CV does not change with time.

$$
\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the steady-state condition into the general mass balance.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dot{m}_i = \sum_{e=1}^{m} \dot{m}_e
$$

**Step 5 (Special Case — Single Stream):** For a device with one inlet and one outlet (e.g., a nozzle, turbine, compressor, pump).

$$
\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}
$$

**Step 6:** Expressing the single-stream mass flow rate in terms of measurable quantities (area, velocity, density).

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V_{avg} = \frac{A \cdot V_{avg}}{v}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** This form is the most practically useful in KTU problems. Given the **inlet area** $A_1$, **inlet velocity** $V_1$, and the **specific volume** $v_1$ (from steam tables or ideal gas law), the mass flow rate is uniquely determined.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for a Control Volume

> **Starting Principle:** The First Law of Thermodynamics for an open system, considering mass, heat, and work transfers.

Consider a control volume with one inlet (state 1) and one outlet (state 2), operating at **steady state**.

**Step 1:** Write the general first-law equation for an open system.

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} + \dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{out} - \dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{in}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the steady-state condition.

At steady state, the energy of the CV does not change: $\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = 0$.

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}_{out}\theta_{out} - \dot{m}_{in}\theta_{in}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the single-stream simplification.

For a single inlet (1) and a single outlet (2), $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}$.

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[\left(h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2\right) - \left(h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1\right)\right]
$$

**Step 4:** Recognise that $\dot{W}$ typically includes only **shaft work** in the SFEE (since boundary work at inlets/outlets is already accounted for via the enthalpy $h = u + Pv$).

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{shaft} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** This is the **Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** — the most heavily used equation in KTU 2024 Module 1 and Module 2 problems. The terms represent:
> - $(h_2 - h_1)$: change in **flow enthalpy** (most significant in turbines, compressors, boilers)
> - $\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$: change in **kinetic energy** (significant in nozzles, diffusers)
> - $g(z_2 - z_1)$: change in **potential energy** (significant in pumps moving water to elevated reservoirs)
> - $\dot{Q}$: rate of heat transfer to the system (positive into CV)
> - $\dot{W}_{shaft}$: rate of shaft work done BY the system (positive out of CV, per I.T. convention)

**Step 5 — Per unit mass form:** Divide throughout by $\dot{m}$.

$$
q - w_{shaft} = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)
$$

This per-unit-mass form is the one most commonly tested in KTU ESE Part B questions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Specialised Forms of SFEE for Standard Devices

#### 3.3.1 Nozzle / Diffuser
- $\dot{W}_{shaft} = 0$ (no shaft work)
- $\dot{Q} \approx 0$ (well-insulated, adiabatic assumption typical)
- Negligible $\Delta PE$
- Result:

$$
h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2}
$$

#### 3.3.2 Turbine
- $\dot{Q} \approx 0$ (adiabatic)
- $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$
- $\dot{W}_{shaft} = \dot{W}_{turbine} > 0$ (work output)
- Result:

$$
w_{turbine} = h_1 - h_2
$$

#### 3.3.3 Compressor / Pump
- $\dot{Q} \approx 0$ (adiabatic)
- $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$
- $\dot{W}_{shaft} = \dot{W}_{pump} < 0$ (work input)
- Result:

$$
w_{pump,in} = h_2 - h_1
$$

#### 3.3.4 Throttling Valve
- $\dot{W}_{shaft} = 0$
- $\dot{Q} \approx 0$
- $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$
- Result: $h_1 = h_2$ (isenthalpic process)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** A common test trick is to ask "derive the SFEE and apply it to a [device]". Marks are awarded for **(a)** stating assumptions, **(b)** writing the general SFEE, **(c)** crossing out the zero terms, and **(d)** arriving at the simplified form. Skipping the assumption step loses ~2 marks.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Closed vs. Open System

| Property | Closed System (Control Mass) | Open System (Control Volume) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mass** | Constant ($m = \text{const}$) | May vary with time (transient) or constant (steady) |
| **Identity of matter** | Fixed | Continuously changing |
| **Region in space** | May move (boundary may move) | Fixed in space |
| **Energy crossing boundary** | Heat & work only | Heat, work, and energy carried by mass |
| **First Law form** | $Q - W = \Delta E$ | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dfrac{dE_{CV}}{dt} + \dot{m}\Delta\theta$ |
| **Work term** | Boundary work $\int P dV$ + other work | Shaft work only (boundary work absorbed in $h$) |
| **Typical devices** | Piston-cylinder, sealed tank, balloon | Nozzle, turbine, compressor, pump, boiler, condenser |
| **KTU exam frequency** | High (Module 1–3) | Very High (Module 1–5) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Thermodynamic System Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Thermodynamic System])
    startA --> classA{Is mass crossing the boundary?}
    classA -- No Mass Transfer --> closedSys[Closed System - Control Mass]
    classA -- Mass Transfer Allowed --> openSys[Open System - Control Volume]
    closedSys --> closedCheck{Is there any heat or work interaction?}
    closedCheck -- No Heat, No Work, No Mass --> isoSys[Isolated System - Idealised]
    closedCheck -- Heat and or Work allowed --> closedEx[Piston-Cylinder, Sealed Tank, I C Engine Cylinder]
    openSys --> openCheck{Steady or Transient?}
    openCheck -- Steady State --> steadyEx[Nozzle, Turbine, Compressor, Pump, Heat Exchanger]
    openCheck -- Transient --> transEx[Filling of a Tank, Emptying of a Cylinder]
    isoSys --> isoEx[Universe - the only true example, Thermos flask as approximation]
    classDef defStyle fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class startA,classA,closedCheck,openCheck defStyle
    classDef sysStyle fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class closedSys,openSys,isoSys,closedEx,steadyEx,transEx,isoEx sysStyle
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Control Volume Schematic with Multiple Streams

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env1([Surroundings]) -. heat Qdot .-> cvBox
    cvBox[CONTROL VOLUME - e.g. Turbine] -. shaft Wdot .-> env2([Surroundings])
    inlet1((Inlet 1: state 1)) -- mdot1, h1, V1, z1 --> cvBox
    inlet2((Inlet 2: state 2)) -- mdot2, h2, V2, z2 --> cvBox
    cvBox -- mdote, he, Ve, ze --> outlet1((Outlet e))
    note1[Mass Conservation: sum mdot_in equals sum mdot_out at steady state]:::noteStyle
    note2[Energy Conservation: SFEE applied]:::noteStyle
    cvBox -.- note1
    cvBox -.- note2
    classDef noteStyle fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef cvStyle fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    class cvBox cvStyle
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Closed System — Piston Cylinder (Process Visualisation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subA[Piston Cylinder - Closed System]:::sys
    state1[State 1: P1, V1, T1]:::st
    state2[State 2: P2, V2, T2]:::st
    subA -- Heat Q --> subA
    subA -- Boundary Work W equals integral P dV --> subA
    state1 == Quasi Static Process ==> state2
    fixedMass[Mass m is constant, matter identity is fixed]:::noteStyle
    subA -.- fixedMass
    classDef sys fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef st fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef noteStyle fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Steam Power Plant (CV Approach)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedPump[Feedwater Pump - Open System]:::block
    boiler[Boiler - Open System]:::block
    turbine[Steam Turbine - Open System]:::block
    condenser[Condenser - Open System]:::block
    feedPump -- High Pressure Liquid Water --> boiler
    boiler -- Superheated Steam --> turbine
    turbine -- Low Pressure Steam --> condenser
    condenser -- Liquid Water --> feedPump
    heatIn[Heat Input Qdot in Boiler from Furnace]:::heat
    heatOut[Heat Rejection Qdot out in Condenser to Cooling Water]:::heat
    workOut[Shaft Work Wdot out from Turbine]:::work
    workIn[Shaft Work Wdot in to Pump]:::work
    boiler --- heatIn
    condenser --- heatOut
    turbine --- workOut
    feedPump --- workIn
    classDef block fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef heat fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef work fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> **Engineering Interpretation of Diagram 4.4:** Each rectangular block is a **control volume** with its own inlet/outlet streams. The arrows represent mass flow, the heat-in/heat-out labels represent heat transfer, and the work-in/work-out labels represent shaft work. The student should be able to apply the SFEE to **each block** independently to determine the unknowns.

### 4.5 Schematic Description: Control Surface Identification

For a typical KTU exam problem (e.g., "analyse a centrifugal pump"):

1. **Step A:** Draw a dashed closed curve around the pump casing — this is the **control surface**.
2. **Step B:** Identify the **single inlet** (suction side) — state 1: $P_1, T_1, V_1, z_1$.
3. **Step C:** Identify the **single outlet** (discharge side) — state 2: $P_2, T_2, V_2, z_2$.
4. **Step D:** Note the **shaft work input** $\dot{W}_{pump,in}$ and any heat loss $\dot{Q}_{out}$.
5. **Step E:** Apply mass balance: $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}$.
6. **Step F:** Apply SFEE to find $\dot{W}_{pump}$ or $h_2$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between a **closed system** and an **open system**. Give **one engineering example** for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Closed System | Open System |
|---|---|---|
| Mass transfer across boundary | **Not allowed** | **Allowed** |
| Energy transfer | Heat and work | Heat, work, and energy carried by mass |
| Governing equation | $Q - W = \Delta E$ | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dfrac{dE_{CV}}{dt} + \dot{m}\Delta\theta$ |
| Example | **Piston-cylinder** assembly of an I.C. engine | **Steam turbine** in a power plant |
| Typical analysis | Boundary work $\int P dV$ | Steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) |

**Marking Scheme (KTU pattern):**
- Tabular comparison of 2–3 points — 2 marks
- One example each — 1 mark

---

#### **Question 2 (3 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define **Control Volume**. Explain the term **steady state** with reference to a control volume.

**Model Answer:**

**Control Volume:** A control volume is a **fixed region in space** bounded by a control surface, chosen for thermodynamic analysis, through which mass, energy, and momentum may flow across its boundary. Examples: turbines, compressors, nozzles.

**Steady State:** A control volume is said to be in **steady state** when the properties at every point within the CV and at the control surface **do not change with time**. Mathematically:

$$
\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = 0
$$

This means that although mass and energy continue to flow in and out, the **net accumulation** inside is zero, hence the rates of inflow and outflow are equal.

**Marking Scheme (KTU pattern):**
- Definition of CV — 1 mark
- Definition of steady state with equation — 2 marks

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two questions from each module, answer any ONE. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Define a **thermodynamic system**. Classify thermodynamic systems with neat sketches and explain each with **two examples** in each category.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A thermodynamic system is a precisely defined quantity of matter or a region in space upon which attention is focused for analysis, separated from the rest of the universe (surroundings) by a boundary.

**Classification** (with sketches and examples):

1. **Closed System (Control Mass)** — No mass crosses the boundary, but heat and work may.
   - Examples: (i) Gas in a piston-cylinder, (ii) refrigerant sealed inside a domestic refrigerator's tube coils.

2. **Open System (Control Volume)** — Both mass and energy cross the boundary.
   - Examples: (i) Steam turbine, (ii) water pump.

3. **Isolated System** — No mass, heat, or work crosses the boundary.
   - Examples: (i) Universe (theoretically), (ii) Thermos flask (idealised).

[Neat sketches showing each system type — see SECTION_4 diagrams for reference.]

**Marking Scheme:**
- Definition — 2 marks
- Classification with sketches — 3 marks
- Two examples each — 2 marks

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A nozzle is a device that increases the velocity of a fluid at the expense of pressure drop. Steam enters a **convergent nozzle** at $P_1 = 2 \text{ MPa}$, $T_1 = 300°\text{C}$, and $V_1 = 50 \text{ m/s}$. It leaves at $P_2 = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$ with $V_2 = 350 \text{ m/s}$. Assuming the nozzle is **adiabatic** and the **change in potential energy is negligible**, determine:
(i) The **specific enthalpy at exit** $h_2$.
(ii) The **exit temperature** $T_2$ (use steam table approximation $h_2 \approx 2.7 T_2 + 1800$ in appropriate units — illustrative).

Given: $h_1 = 3023.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (from steam tables for 2 MPa, 300°C).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the SFEE for the nozzle (control volume with one inlet, one outlet).

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the assumptions.

- $\dot{Q} = 0$ (adiabatic)
- $\dot{W} = 0$ (no shaft work in a nozzle)
- $g(z_2 - z_1) \approx 0$ (negligible PE change)
- $\dot{m}$ cancels (single-stream steady CV)

This reduces the SFEE to:

$$
0 = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert the kinetic energy term to consistent units (kJ/kg).

$$
\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} = \frac{(350)^2 - (50)^2}{2} = \frac{122500 - 2500}{2} = 60000 \text{ J/kg} = 60 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $h_2$.

$$
h_2 = h_1 - \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} = 3023.5 - 60 = 2963.5 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5:** (For part ii, illustrative) Solve for $T_2$.

Using the illustrative relation:

$$
h_2 = 2.7 T_2 + 1800
$$

$$
T_2 = \frac{h_2 - 1800}{2.7} = \frac{2963.5 - 1800}{2.7} = \frac{1163.5}{2.7} \approx 430.9°\text{C}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- (i) $h_2 = 2963.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- (ii) $T_2 \approx 431°\text{C}$ (illustrative)

**Marking Scheme:**
- Writing SFEE and stating assumptions — 2 marks
- Converting KE to kJ/kg — 1 mark
- Solving for $h_2$ — 2 marks
- Final answer with units — 1 mark
- Part (ii) computation — 1 mark

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** for a control volume operating at steady state, starting from the **First Law of Thermodynamics**. State all assumptions clearly.

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The control volume operates at **steady state** — properties do not change with time.
2. There is **one inlet** (state 1) and **one outlet** (state 2).
3. The flow is **one-dimensional** at the inlets and outlets.
4. Mass flow rate is constant: $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The First Law for an open system (general) is:

$$
\dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} = \frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} + \sum_{out} \dot{m}_e \left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_e - \sum_{in} \dot{m}_i \left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_i
$$

**Step 2:** Apply steady-state condition: $\dfrac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$.

$$
\dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} = \sum_{out} \dot{m}_e \theta_e - \sum_{in} \dot{m}_i \theta_i
$$

**Step 3:** Apply single-stream assumption.

$$
\dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} = \dot{m}\left[\left(h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2\right) - \left(h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1\right)\right]
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange into the standard SFEE form.

$$
\dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]
$$

**Step 5:** Per unit mass:

$$
q - w_{cv} = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)
$$

**Marking Scheme:**
- Stating 4 assumptions — 2 marks
- Writing the general first law — 1 mark
- Applying steady-state and single-stream — 2 marks
- Final SFEE in standard form — 2 marks

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Air flows steadily through a horizontal **insulated nozzle**. It enters at $P_1 = 400 \text{ kPa}$, $T_1 = 500 \text{ K}$, and $V_1 = 100 \text{ m/s}$, and leaves at $P_2 = 100 \text{ kPa}$ and $V_2 = 300 \text{ m/s}$. Treating air as an **ideal gas with constant specific heat** $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, determine:
(i) The **exit temperature** $T_2$.
(ii) The **mass flow rate** if the inlet area is $A_1 = 80 \text{ cm}^2 = 80 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$. Take $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the SFEE for the nozzle.

$$
q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply assumptions.
- $q = 0$ (insulated, adiabatic)
- $w = 0$ (no shaft work)
- $g(z_2 - z_1) = 0$ (horizontal)

The equation simplifies to:

$$
0 = c_p(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert KE to kJ/kg.

$$
\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} = \frac{(300)^2 - (100)^2}{2} = \frac{90000 - 10000}{2} = 40000 \text{ J/kg} = 40 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $T_2$.

$$
T_2 = T_1 - \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2 c_p} = 500 - \frac{40}{1.005} = 500 - 39.80 = 460.20 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute density at inlet using ideal gas law: $P_1 = \rho_1 R T_1$.

$$
\rho_1 = \frac{P_1}{R T_1} = \frac{400}{0.287 \times 500} = \frac{400}{143.5} = 2.7875 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 6:** Compute mass flow rate.

$$
\dot{m} = \rho_1 \cdot A_1 \cdot V_1 = 2.7875 \times 80 \times 10^{-4} \times 100
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 2.7875 \times 0.008 \times 100 = 2.7875 \times 0.8 = 2.230 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- (i) $T_2 = 460.2 \text{ K}$
- (ii) $\dot{m} = 2.23 \text{ kg/s}$

**Marking Scheme:**
- SFEE with assumptions — 2 marks
- KE conversion — 1 mark
- $T_2$ calculation — 2 marks
- $\rho_1$ from ideal gas law — 1 mark
- $\dot{m}$ final — 1 mark

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Confusion (–2 to –3 marks):** Mixing up work done **by** vs. **on** the system. KTU predominantly follows the convention where $\dot{W}$ is the work done **by** the system (positive for turbines). Students writing $w_{pump} = h_1 - h_2$ (instead of $h_2 - h_1$) lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks):** Enthalpy $h$ is in **kJ/kg**, but $V^2/2$ is initially in **J/kg**. Forgetting to divide by 1000 yields wrong answers.
> 3. **Skipping Assumptions (–2 marks):** KTU examiners explicitly check for **assumption statements** before applying the SFEE. Writing the SFEE without listing $\dot{Q}=0$, $\dot{W}=0$, $\Delta PE = 0$ is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Steam Table Reading Errors (–1 to –3 marks):** Not specifying the **state** (saturated, superheated, etc.) or misreading interpolation values.
> 5. **Forgetting the Control Volume Identification (–1 mark):** Many KTU problems explicitly ask "identify the system" — skipping this first step costs a mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save this for ESE preparation):**

- A **thermodynamic system** is the matter/region chosen for analysis; the rest is **surroundings**; the divider is the **boundary** (zero thickness, zero mass).
- A **closed system (control mass)** has fixed mass, allows heat and work; governed by $Q - W = \Delta E$.
- An **open system (control volume)** has fixed region in space, allows mass and energy flow; governed by the **SFEE** (Steady-Flow Energy Equation).
- An **isolated system** has **no** interaction with surroundings — only the universe is truly isolated.
- The **SFEE** is the most frequently tested equation in KTU 2024 Module 1 and beyond:

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]
$$

- For **steady state**, $\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$; for a single stream, $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}$.
- **Mass flow rate** is given by $\dot{m} = \rho A V_{avg} = A V_{avg} / v$.
- **Enthalpy** $h = u + Pv$ — always use **specific enthalpy** in open system analysis.
- The **boundary work** for a closed system undergoing quasi-static process is $W_b = \int P dV$.
- **Specialised SFEE forms:**
  - **Nozzle/Diffuser:** $h_1 + V_1^2/2 = h_2 + V_2^2/2$
  - **Turbine:** $w_T = h_1 - h_2$
  - **Compressor/Pump:** $w_C = h_2 - h_1$
  - **Throttling valve:** $h_1 = h_2$
- **Sign convention (KTU 2024):** Work done **by** the system is **positive**; heat added **to** the system is **positive**.
- **Always** state **assumptions** (adiabatic, no PE change, no shaft work, etc.) before applying the SFEE — this alone can fetch 2 marks.
- The **kinetic energy term** $V^2/2$ is in J/kg; convert to kJ/kg by dividing by 1000.
- For **ideal gas**: $Pv = RT$, so $v = RT/P$ and $\rho = P/(RT)$.
- Devices analysed as **control volumes** in KTU syllabus: nozzle, diffuser, turbine, compressor, pump, heat exchanger, boiler, condenser, throttle valve.
- Devices analysed as **closed systems**: piston-cylinder, I.C. engine cylinder, sealed tank, balloon.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Thermodynamic System and Control Volume. Proceed to Module 2 for Property relations, Ideal Gas, and the First Law detailed applications.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Thermodynamic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Thermodynamic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Thermodynamic System?

A **Thermodynamic System** is a precisely defined region in space or a specific quantity of matter that is selected for thermodynamic analysis. Everything external to this defined region is termed the **Surroundings** (or environment). The real or imaginary surface that physically or conceptually separates the system from its surroundings is called the **System Boundary**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> "A thermodynamic system is a finite, well-defined region in space, together with the matter and energy contained within it, separated from the surroundings by a boundary across which interactions of mass and energy may be observed."

The study of thermodynamics fundamentally revolves around tracking **mass** and **energy** interactions across the system boundary. Based on the nature and extent of these interactions, thermodynamic systems are classified into three principal types.

## 1.2 The Three Primary Types of Systems

> [!NOTE]
> The classification is determined strictly by what crosses the **boundary** — not what exists inside or outside.

### Type 1: Open System (Control Volume, CV)

An **Open System** is one in which both **mass** and **energy** are permitted to cross the system boundary during the process under consideration. Because the region of study is now defined by a fixed volume in space (like a nozzle, turbine, compressor, or heat exchanger) rather than a fixed parcel of matter, it is technically referred to as a **Control Volume**.

### Type 2: Closed System (Control Mass, CM)

A **Closed System** is one in which the **mass** is fixed and cannot cross the boundary, but **energy** (in the form of heat or work) is allowed to transfer. The selected matter remains constant in identity throughout the process, hence the term **Control Mass**.

### Type 3: Isolated System

An **Isolated System** is the most restrictive category — **neither mass nor energy** of any kind can cross the system boundary. The total mass and total energy of the system remain absolutely constant. The universe itself is treated as an isolated system.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "Sealed Container" Analogy

Imagine three identical pressure cookers placed side by side on a kitchen counter (this is the surroundings):

- **Cooker A (Open System):** The lid is removed, and the safety valve is open. Steam (mass + energy) escapes freely. You can pour water in and drain it out. → **Open System** (e.g., a boiler, jet engine, pump).
- **Cooker B (Closed System):** The lid is tightly sealed (no mass crosses), but the cooker is placed on a lit gas stove. Heat (energy) flows in through the metal base, but steam cannot escape. → **Closed System** (e.g., piston-cylinder device, pressure cooker on stove).
- **Cooker C (Isolated System):** The lid is perfectly sealed AND the cooker is placed inside a perfectly insulated thermos flask, disconnected from any energy source. Nothing crosses in or out. → **Isolated System** (e.g., a thermos bottle, the universe as a whole).

## 1.4 Key Boundary Interactions

The interactions across the system boundary are the very heart of KTU Module 1. They are summarized below:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three "Crossings" — Memorize This!**
> 1. **Mass Transfer** $\longrightarrow$ Occurs ONLY in Open Systems.
> 2. **Heat Transfer (Q)** $\longrightarrow$ Crosses in Open & Closed Systems (but NOT Isolated).
> 3. **Work Transfer (W)** $\longrightarrow$ Crosses in Open & Closed Systems (but NOT Isolated).
> 4. **No Crossings at all** $\longrightarrow$ Purely Isolated System.

The standard KTU notation for these interactions is **Q** for heat transferred **to** the system, and **W** for work done **by** the system (using the **IUPAC convention adopted by KTU 2024**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification Logic

The classification of thermodynamic systems is **purely a function of the chosen boundary**, not of the actual physical hardware. The same physical device can be modeled as an open or closed system depending on what the engineer wishes to investigate.

### 2.1.1 Open System (Control Volume) — In-Depth

- The boundary is a **fixed surface in space** (control surface), so matter can flow in and out.
- Typical components: **turbines, compressors, nozzles, diffusers, pumps, heat exchangers, boilers, condensers, throttling valves**.
- Both **mass flow rate** ($\dot{m}$, in **kg/s**) and **energy flow rates** (heat, work, enthalpy flux) are present.
- Steady-flow open systems are the most commonly analyzed in KTU problems.

### 2.1.2 Closed System (Control Mass) — In-Depth

- The boundary is a **movable or deforming surface that always encloses the same matter**.
- Typical components: **piston-cylinder assembly, balloon, sealed pressure vessel**.
- **No mass crosses** the boundary, but the volume may change (boundary work, $W_b$).
- Quasi-equilibrium processes on closed systems form the backbone of classical thermodynamic theory (Isothermal, Isobaric, Isochoric, Adiabatic, Polytropic).

### 2.1.3 Isolated System — In-Depth

- The system boundary is **completely impermeable to both mass and energy**.
- The **First Law of Thermodynamics** simplifies dramatically: $\Delta E_{system} = 0$.
- Although an *ideal* isolated system cannot exist in reality, a **thermos flask (Dewar flask)** is the closest engineering approximation.

## 2.2 Special Sub-Categories Often Asked in KTU

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test these edge cases as Part A questions:

1. **Adiabatic Closed System:** A closed system with **no heat transfer** across the boundary. $Q = 0$. The First Law reduces to $\Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = -W$.
2. **Steady-Flow Open System:** Properties at the inlet and outlet are **time-invariant**. $\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = 0$.
3. **Uniform-State (Uniform-Flow) Process:** A closed system where the entire mass inside experiences a uniform state change — the basis for the lumped-capacitance model.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Types of Systems

The table below consolidates **every governing equation** you will need for Module 1 numerical problems on system types.

| **System Type** | **Mass Balance** | **Energy Balance (First Law)** | **Common Examples** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open System (CV)** | $\dfrac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | $\dfrac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} + \sum \dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2} + gz\right)_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2} + gz\right)_{out}$ | Nozzle, Turbine, Compressor, Pump, Boiler |
| **Closed System (CM)** | $m = \text{constant}$ (always) | $Q - W = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$ | Piston-Cylinder, Sealed Tank, Balloon |
| **Isolated System** | $m = \text{constant}$ | $Q = 0$, $W = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $\Delta E_{system} = 0$ | Thermos Flask, The Universe |
| **Steady-Flow Open** | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2} + gz\right)_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2} + gz\right)_{in}$ | Steam Power Plant components, Jet Engine |
| **Adiabatic Closed** | $m = \text{constant}$ | $-W = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$ (since $Q=0$) | Insulated Piston, Quick Compression |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of the Open System Concept:**
> Almost every practical energy-conversion device on Earth (IC engine intake manifold, gas turbine, refrigeration cycle, rocket nozzle) is modeled as an open system. Without the control-volume framework, you cannot analyze the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) that powers entire industries. The closed-system framework remains vital for non-flow processes like the Otto cycle combustion stroke analysis.

## 2.4 Choosing the Right System for KTU Problems

> [!WARNING]
> A common valuation mistake is to apply the **closed-system First Law** to a device with a fluid stream flowing through it. Always scan the problem statement for clues: words like "enters," "exits," "inlet," "outlet," "flow rate," and "steady" mean **OPEN SYSTEM**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: General Mass Balance for a Control Volume

We start by tracking the mass contained within an arbitrary control volume at any instant $t$. The total mass inside the CV at time $t$ is:

$$
m_{CV}(t) = \int_{CV} \rho \, dV
$$

The rate of change of mass within the control volume is the time derivative:

$$
\frac{d m_{CV}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV
$$

By the **Conservation of Mass** (a fundamental law of nature, not just a thermodynamic postulate), the net rate of mass accumulation inside the CV must equal the net rate of mass inflow minus the net rate of mass outflow across the control surface (CS):

$$
