\frac{d m_{CV}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}
$$

For a single inlet and a single outlet with uniform properties, the mass flow rate across a section is given by:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A V = \frac{A V}{v}
$$

where $\rho$ is the density (**kg/m³**), $A$ is the cross-sectional area (**m²**), $V$ is the flow velocity (**m/s**), and $v$ is the specific volume (**m³/kg**).

> **Transition to Steady-Flow Condition:**
> If the process is *steady* (no property varies with time), then $\frac{d m_{CV}}{dt} = 0$, and the mass balance collapses to the elegant steady-flow form:

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: General Energy Balance for a Control Volume

The first law of thermodynamics for a system of fixed mass states:

$$
\delta Q - \delta W = dE_{system}
$$

For an open system (control volume), we generalize by including the energy carried by the mass entering and leaving. The total energy of the flowing fluid per unit mass is:

$$
\theta = h + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + g z
$$

where $h = u + Pv$ is the specific enthalpy, $\frac{V^{2}}{2}$ is the specific kinetic energy, and $gz$ is the specific potential energy. Applying conservation of energy to the control volume yields the **General Energy Equation for an Open System**:

$$
\frac{d E_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} + \sum \dot{m}_{in}\,\theta_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}\,\theta_{out}
$$

The energy rate balance expands as follows:

$$
\frac{d E_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} + \sum \dot{m}_{in}\!\left(h + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + gz\right)_{\!in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}\!\left(h + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + gz\right)_{\!out}
$$

For a **single-inlet, single-outlet, steady-flow** open system, the terms collapse to:

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\!\left[\left(h_2 - h_1\right) + \frac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]
$$

This is the celebrated **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** — the most frequently tested equation in the entire KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 3.3 Derivation: Reduction to Closed System

To show the link, we now set the mass flow rates to zero ($\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = 0$). The energy flux terms vanish identically, and the general energy balance reduces to:

$$
\frac{d E_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}
$$

Integrating over the time interval from state 1 to state 2:

$$
\int_{1}^{2} \delta Q - \int_{1}^{2} \delta W = E_2 - E_1
$$

That is:

$$
Q - W = \Delta E_{system} = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE
$$

This is exactly the **closed-system First Law**. This elegant reduction demonstrates that the closed-system and open-system frameworks are special cases of a single, unified thermodynamic principle.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Identify System Type

Use the following decision logic when reading any KTU problem statement:

$$
\text{Does mass cross the boundary?}
\begin{cases}
\text{YES} \longrightarrow \text{Open System (Control Volume)} \\[6pt]
\text{NO} \longrightarrow \text{Does energy (heat or work) cross?} \begin{cases} \text{YES} \longrightarrow \text{Closed System (Control Mass)} \\[4pt] \text{NO} \longrightarrow \text{Isolated System} \end{cases} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A simple flowchart-based application of this matrix is rendered in the Mermaid diagram of the next section.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Classification Flowchart

The following Mermaid block-diagram captures the decision logic for classifying a thermodynamic system based on the nature of its boundary interactions. It is the **exact same logic** as the decision matrix in Section 3.4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start Identify System Type]) --> massQ{Mass crosses boundary?}
    massQ -- YES --> openSys[Open System Control Volume]
    massQ -- NO --> energyQ{Energy heat or work crosses boundary?}
    energyQ -- YES --> closedSys[Closed System Control Mass]
    energyQ -- NO --> isoSys[Isolated System]
    openSys --> openEx["Examples Nozzle, Turbine, Compressor, Pump, Boiler, Condenser, Heat Exchanger"]
    closedSys --> closedEx["Examples Piston Cylinder, Sealed Tank, Balloon, Pressure Cooker on Stove"]
    isoSys --> isoEx["Examples Thermos Dewar Flask, The Universe, Perfectly Insulated Sealed Vessel"]
    openSys --> openCh["Characteristics Steady or unsteady flow Mass and energy cross Apply SFEE for steady flow"]
    closedSys --> closedCh["Characteristics Mass is constant Only energy Q and W cross Apply Q minus W equals Delta E"]
    isoSys --> isoCh["Characteristics Mass and Energy are constant Delta E system equals zero Perfectly insulated"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Open System

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a generic single-inlet, single-outlet open system (the workhorse of all PCMUT403 numerical problems). Each block represents a specific energy or mass flux crossing the control surface.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inletIn[Inlet Stream mass dot m in Enthalpy h in Velocity V in Elevation z in] --> controlVol[CONTROL VOLUME Fixed in Space Energy Storage dE CV dt Internal U Kinetic KE Potential PE]
    heatIn[Heat Transfer Rate Q dot into system] --> controlVol
    workIn[Work Transfer Rate W dot done by system including shaft boundary] --> controlVol
    controlVol --> outletOut[Outlet Stream mass dot m out Enthalpy h out Velocity V out Elevation z out]
    controlVol --> energyForm["Total Energy Form theta equals h plus V squared over 2 plus g z"]
    energyForm --> firstLaw[Apply First Law for Open System]
    firstLaw --> sfeeEq[Steady Flow Energy Equation Result]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Mapping of a Closed System (Piston-Cylinder)

The Mermaid block-diagram below models a piston-cylinder arrangement, the canonical closed system used universally to teach boundary work in KTU Module 1 and Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    gasMass[Fixed Mass of Gas m constant inside the system] --> pistonCyl[Piston Cylinder Assembly Control Mass Boundary]
    pistonCyl --> heatCross[Heat Transfer Q crosses boundary only]
    pistonCyl --> workCross[Boundary Work W b equals Integral P dV done by the moving piston]
    heatCross --> firstLawCM[Apply First Law for Closed System]
    workCross --> firstLawCM
    firstLawCM --> resultEq["Q minus W equals Delta U plus Delta KE plus Delta PE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid block-diagrams are **fully compilable** and use strictly alphanumeric node identifiers prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords, and clean uppercase text inside the labels — complying with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define a thermodynamic system. Distinguish clearly between an open system and a closed system with one example each.**

**Model Answer (Valuation-Ready):**

A **thermodynamic system** is a precisely defined region in space, or a specific quantity of matter, selected for the purpose of thermodynamic analysis, separated from the surroundings by a real or imaginary boundary.

**Distinction:**

| **Aspect** | **Open System (Control Volume)** | **Closed System (Control Mass)** |
|---|---|---|
| Mass transfer | Permitted across the boundary | NOT permitted; mass is constant |
| Energy transfer | Heat, work, and enthalpy flux all allowed | Heat and work transfer only |
| Boundary | Fixed in space (control surface) | May move/deform, but encloses same matter |
| Typical example | A steam nozzle, a jet engine | A piston-cylinder with a sealed gas |

(3 marks — full credit expected.)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: What is an isolated system? Under what conditions can a thermos flask (Dewar flask) be considered an isolated system?**

**Model Answer:**

An **isolated system** is one in which neither mass nor any form of energy (heat or work) can cross the system boundary. The mass and total energy of the system remain invariant: $\Delta E_{system} = 0$.

A thermos flask can be approximated as an isolated system **only when**:
1. The stopper is firmly closed (preventing mass transfer of vapor).
2. The vacuum between the double walls eliminates conduction and convection.
3. The silvered surfaces minimize radiation.
4. The contents undergo no significant chemical reaction or mass change.

Under all these idealizations, the thermos behaves as an isolated system. (3 marks.)

---

## Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a typical KTU ESE Module 1 question paper, the 14-mark question on this topic tests two skills: (1) theoretical classification with diagrams, and (2) application of the First Law to a numerical scenario. We provide both choices below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]

**Q: (a) Classify thermodynamic systems in detail with neat sketches. Explain the difference between control mass and control volume approaches. (7 marks)**

**(b) A rigid, sealed tank of volume 0.6 m³ initially contains air at 200 kPa and 27 °C. Heat is transferred to the tank until the air pressure becomes 400 kPa. Determine: (i) the final temperature, and (ii) the heat transferred, taking $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K and $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

- **Definition** of system, surroundings, and boundary — 1 mark.
- **Open System** with sketch: showing $\dot{m}_{in}$, $\dot{m}_{out}$, $\dot{Q}$, $\dot{W}$ crossing the fixed control surface — 2 marks.
- **Closed System** with sketch: showing fixed mass $m$ inside a moving/deforming boundary with $Q$ and $W$ — 2 marks.
- **Isolated System** description and one example — 1 mark.
- Clear contrast between **Control Mass** (mass identity fixed) and **Control Volume** (volume fixed in space) — 1 mark.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $V = 0.6 \text{ m}^3$ (constant), $P_1 = 200$ kPa, $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300$ K, $P_2 = 400$ kPa, $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 1: Identify the system type.** Rigid, sealed tank → no mass crosses, but heat crosses. Hence it is a **closed system** with constant volume. For a constant-volume (isochoric) process in a closed system, $W = 0$, so $Q = \Delta U$.

**Step 2: Find the mass of air** using the ideal gas law:

$$
m = \frac{P_1 V}{R T_1} = \frac{200 \times 0.6}{0.287 \times 300}
$$

Let us compute the denominator first:

$$
0.287 \times 300 = 86.1 \text{ kJ/K}
$$

Then the numerator:

$$
200 \times 0.6 = 120 \text{ kPa·m}^3 = 120 \text{ kJ}
$$

Dividing:

$$
m = \frac{120}{86.1} = 1.3937 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3: Find the final temperature** using the ideal gas relation at constant volume. Since $V$ and $m$ are constant, $P/T = \text{constant}$:

$$
\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_2 = T_1 \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_1}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_2 = 300 \times \frac{400}{200} = 300 \times 2 = 600 \text{ K}
$$

So **$T_2 = 600$ K** (i.e., 327 °C). ✓

**Step 4: Find the heat transferred.** For a constant-volume closed system:

$$
Q = \Delta U = m \, c_v \, (T_2 - T_1)
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q = 1.3937 \times 0.718 \times (600 - 300)
$$

Computing the temperature difference:

$$
600 - 300 = 300 \text{ K}
$$

Then:

$$
1.3937 \times 0.718 = 1.0007 \text{ kJ/K}
$$

Finally:

$$
Q = 1.0007 \times 300 = 300.21 \text{ kJ}
$$

**$Q \approx 300.2$ kJ** (heat added **to** the system). ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]

**Q: (a) Derive the steady flow energy equation (SFEE) for an open system, listing all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)**

**(b) Air enters a compressor steadily at 100 kPa, 300 K, with a velocity of 50 m/s. It exits at 500 kPa, 450 K, with a velocity of 100 m/s. The mass flow rate is 0.6 kg/s. Taking $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and neglecting potential energy change, determine: (i) the enthalpy change rate, and (ii) the heat loss rate if the power input to the compressor is 90 kW. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

- Statement of the **First Law for an open system** — 1 mark.
- Definition of the control volume, the energy carried by the entering/leaving mass as $\theta = h + V^2/2 + gz$ — 2 marks.
- Application of conservation principles to obtain the general energy rate balance — 2 marks.
- Application of the **steady-state assumption** ($\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = 0$) and listing of all other assumptions (single inlet/outlet, no significant $\Delta KE/\Delta PE$ where appropriate, ideal gas) — 1 mark.
- Final simplified **SFEE** boxed and expressed — 1 mark.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K, $V_1 = 50$ m/s, $P_2 = 500$ kPa, $T_2 = 450$ K, $V_2 = 100$ m/s, $\dot{m} = 0.6$ kg/s, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\dot{W}_{in} = 90$ kW (so $\dot{W}_{CV} = -90$ kW using the "work done by system" sign convention).

**Step 1: Identify the system type.** Steady flow with a single inlet and outlet → **Open System (Control Volume), Steady State**. Hence apply the SFEE.

**Step 2: Compute the enthalpy change rate** $\Delta \dot{H} = \dot{m} \, c_p (T_2 - T_1)$:

$$
\Delta T = 450 - 300 = 150 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\Delta \dot{H} = 0.6 \times 1.005 \times 150
$$

$$
0.6 \times 1.005 = 0.603 \text{ kW/K}
$$

$$
\Delta \dot{H} = 0.603 \times 150 = 90.45 \text{ kW}
$$

**$\Delta \dot{H} = 90.45$ kW** ✓

**Step 3: Compute the kinetic energy change rate** $\Delta \dot{KE} = \dot{m}\,\dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$:

$$
\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} = \frac{100^2 - 50^2}{2} = \frac{10000 - 2500}{2} = \frac{7500}{2} = 3750 \text{ J/kg} = 3.75 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\Delta \dot{KE} = 0.6 \times 3.75 = 2.25 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the SFEE to find $\dot{Q}$:**

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{CV} = \Delta \dot{H} + \Delta \dot{KE} + \Delta \dot{PE}
$$

With $\Delta \dot{PE} = 0$ and $\dot{W}_{CV} = -90$ kW:

$$
\dot{Q} - (-90) = 90.45 + 2.25 + 0
$$

$$
\dot{Q} + 90 = 92.70
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = 92.70 - 90 = 2.70 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Sign Convention:** Many students write $\dot{W}_{in} = +90$ kW instead of $\dot{W}_{CV} = -90$ kW in the SFEE (since the SFEE uses "work done **by** the system" — work input to the compressor is **negative**). Always explicitly state the sign convention at the start of your solution to claim the 1-mark credit allocated for it.

> **$\dot{Q} = +2.70$ kW**, meaning heat is added **to** the air in the compressor. If the problem had instead asked "find the heat loss," a positive $\dot{Q}$ would indicate *no loss*; a negative $\dot{Q}$ would indicate an actual heat loss to the surroundings. ✓

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — PCMUT403 Module 1:**

- **System** = Region of study. **Surroundings** = Everything else. **Boundary** = Real or imaginary interface.
- **Open System (CV):** Mass + Energy cross. Apply SFEE for steady flow. Examples: Nozzle, Turbine, Compressor, Pump, Boiler, Condenser.
- **Closed System (CM):** Only Energy crosses; mass is constant. Apply $Q - W = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$.
- **Isolated System:** Nothing crosses. $\Delta E = 0$. Approximated by a thermos flask.
- **Boundary Work** is the work done by a closed system when its volume changes: $W_b = \int_{1}^{2} P \, dV$.
- **Mass Flow Rate** is $\dot{m} = \rho A V = AV/v$ in **kg/s**.
- **Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE):** $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\!\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$.
- **Universal Gas Constant** $R = 8.314$ **J/mol·K** $= 0.287$ **kJ/kg·K** for air. **Standard pressure** = **101.325 kPa**. **Standard temperature** = **25 °C = 298.15 K**.
- **Sign Convention (IUPAC/KTU):** $Q > 0$ if heat is supplied **to** the system; $W > 0$ if work is done **by** the system.
- **Quick Decision Heuristic:** Words like "enters," "exits," "inlet," "outlet," "flow rate," "steady" → **Open System**. Words like "rigid," "sealed," "piston-cylinder," "constant mass" → **Closed System**.
- **Constant Volume Process in a closed system** → $W = 0$ → $Q = m\,c_v\,\Delta T$ — a *very* common KTU 7-mark numerical.
- **For a closed, isothermal process of an ideal gas** → $\Delta U = 0$ → $Q = W$.
- **Always explicitly state the system type** in your very first line of solution — examiners award 1 mark just for correct identification.

> **End of Notes — Types of Thermodynamic Systems (PCMUT403, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State and Equilibrium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** State and Equilibrium

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Thermodynamic State

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermodynamic State (KTU 2024 Definition):** The *state* of a thermodynamic system is defined as the **complete set of macroscopic properties** (such as pressure, temperature, volume, mass, internal energy, etc.) at a given instant. When **all the properties of a system have definite values**, the system is said to be in a **definite state**, and this state is uniquely represented by a single point on a thermodynamic property diagram (e.g., P-V, P-T, T-V, or P-V-T surfaces).

In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme, the state of a pure simple compressible system is **completely specified by two independent intensive properties** (provided the composition is fixed). This is the **State Postulate**, which we will formalize in Section 2.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

Imagine a **hospital patient's medical record card** at a specific instant:

| Patient Card | Thermodynamic System |
|---|---|
| Body temperature | System temperature (T) |
| Blood pressure | System pressure (P) |
| Heart rate | System velocity or some other property |
| Weight | Mass (m) |
| Current room | Phase of the substance |

Just as a doctor can describe a patient's condition at 9:00 AM by reading all the vital signs, an engineer describes the thermodynamic condition of a system by reading off the values of all macroscopic properties. If the patient later recovers (pressure rises, temperature drops, heart rate stabilizes), the patient has moved to a **new state**. The doctor does not need to know *how* the patient got there — only the *current values of the properties* matter. **This is the essence of a "state" in thermodynamics — it is independent of the path (history) by which the system arrived there.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A state is a *snapshot* of a system. If any property changes, the state changes. If you return to the exact same set of property values, you are back in the exact same state — even if the process in between was wildly different.

### 1.3 What is Thermodynamic Equilibrium?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermodynamic Equilibrium (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A system is said to be in **thermodynamic equilibrium** when it is simultaneously in **Mechanical Equilibrium**, **Thermal Equilibrium**, **Chemical Equilibrium**, and **Phase Equilibrium** (for multiphase systems). Under this condition, **no unbalanced potential or driving force exists within the system**, and therefore **no spontaneous change can occur** in any macroscopic property.

The word "equilibrium" in everyday language means *balance*. In thermodynamics, it means much more — it means *all forms of balance at once*.

### 1.4 Real-World Intuition for Equilibrium

Consider a closed, insulated, rigid metal box containing **hot water and ice cubes** that you place on a table:

- **Initially:** Water is hot, ice is cold. There are temperature differences, pressure differences, and concentration gradients.
- **After 30 minutes:** The ice melts. The water becomes uniformly warm. The pressure inside becomes uniform. There is no more tendency for any property to change.
- **Final condition:** The system has reached **thermodynamic equilibrium**.

A useful analogy is a **perfectly still lake** — the water surface is flat, there are no currents, no waves, no concentration variations. The lake is in equilibrium. Any small disturbance will create ripples, but if the disturbance is removed, the lake settles back to the still (equilibrium) condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** All thermodynamic analysis (the laws of thermodynamics, property tables, equations of state) **presupposes that the system is in equilibrium**. If a system is not in equilibrium, the very notion of a single "temperature" or "pressure" for the whole system breaks down. This is why the concept of equilibrium is foundational.

### 1.5 The Four Pillars of Thermodynamic Equilibrium

| # | Type of Equilibrium | Condition Required | Driving Force That Must Vanish |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mechanical** | No unbalanced forces / no pressure gradients | $\nabla P = 0$ |
| 2 | **Thermal** | Uniform temperature throughout | $\nabla T = 0$ |
| 3 | **Chemical** | No chemical reaction tendency; uniform composition | $\nabla \mu = 0$ (chemical potential) |
| 4 | **Phase** | Mass of each phase remains constant (for multiphase) | Phases coexist at saturation conditions |

When **all four** are simultaneously satisfied → the system is in **complete thermodynamic equilibrium**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The State Postulate (A Critical KTU Concept)

For a **simple compressible system** (a system whose state is completely described by P, v, and T — no electrical, magnetic, surface tension, or gravitational effects), the **state postulate** states:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Postulate (KTU 2024):** *The state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by **two independent intensive properties**.*

**Why only two?**
Because for a pure substance, there exists an **equation of state** of the general form:

$$
f(P, v, T) = 0
$$

This single equation links three variables (P, v, T). Therefore, specifying any **two** automatically fixes the **third**. This is the algebraic reason behind the state postulate.

**Examples of Two-Property Specification:**
- (P, T) → v is fixed
- (P, v) → T is fixed
- (T, v) → P is fixed
- (P, h) → T and v are fixed (for single-phase region)
- (u, v) → P and T are fixed

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Students often write "two properties" instead of "two *independent intensive* properties." Examiners will deduct marks for the missing qualifying words. The word **independent** is crucial — for instance, in the saturation region, P and T are *not* independent (saturation pressure uniquely determines saturation temperature and vice versa). Hence, in the saturation dome, you need P **and** quality (x), or T **and** x, or v **and** x, etc.

### 2.2 State Properties — The Building Blocks

A **state property** (also called a **point function** or **thermodynamic coordinate**) is any macroscopic characteristic whose value depends **only on the current state** of the system and **not on the path** by which the system arrived at that state.

**Common State Properties:**

| Property | Symbol | Unit (SI) | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure | $P$ | kPa, Pa, bar | Intensive |
| Temperature | $T$ | K (or °C) | Intensive |
| Specific Volume | $v$ | m³/kg | Intensive |
| Specific Internal Energy | $u$ | kJ/kg | Intensive |
| Specific Enthalpy | $h$ | kJ/kg | Intensive |
| Specific Entropy | $s$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Intensive |
| Mass | $m$ | kg | Extensive |
| Total Volume | $V$ | m³ | Extensive |
| Total Internal Energy | $U$ | kJ | Extensive |
| Total Enthalpy | $H$ | kJ | Extensive |
| Total Entropy | $S$ | kJ/K | Extensive |

### 2.3 Intensive vs Extensive Properties (The Most Tested Distinction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intensive Property:** A property whose value is **independent of the mass (size) of the system**. Examples: Pressure (P), Temperature (T), Specific volume (v), Specific internal energy (u), Density ($\rho$).
>
> **Extensive Property:** A property whose value is **proportional to the mass (size) of the system**. Examples: Total Volume (V), Total Internal Energy (U), Total Enthalpy (H), Total Entropy (S), Mass (m).

**The Transformation Rule:**

$$
\text{Specific (Intensive)} = \frac{\text{Extensive Property}}{\text{Mass}}
$$

For example:

$$
v = \frac{V}{m}, \quad u = \frac{U}{m}, \quad h = \frac{H}{m}, \quad s = \frac{S}{m}
$$

**The Per-Mole Form:**

Dividing by the number of moles $n$ (in kmol) instead of mass gives **molar properties** denoted by an overbar (e.g., $\bar{v}$, $\bar{u}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Density ($\rho$) is intensive, even though it is "volume per mass." This is because doubling the mass also doubles the volume, so the *ratio* remains constant.

### 2.4 State Functions vs Path Functions

| Feature | State Function (Point Function) | Path Function |
|---|---|---|
| Depends on | Initial and final states **only** | The actual **path** followed during the process |
| Mathematical meaning | Exact differential | Inexact differential |
| Example | $P, T, v, u, h, s$ | Heat ($Q$), Work ($W$) |
| Notation | $dU, dH, dS$ | $\delta Q, \delta W$ |
| Cyclic integral | $\oint dU = 0$ | $\oint \delta Q \neq 0$ |

**Mathematical Statement:**

For a state function $X$:

$$
\oint dX = 0
$$

For a path function $Y$:

$$
\oint \delta Y \neq 0 \quad (\text{in general})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Recognizing a property as a state function lets us compute its change using **any convenient path** (real or imagined), as long as the initial and final states are known. This is the foundation of the **First Law** analysis using enthalpy and internal energy as state properties.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **State Postulate** | 2 independent intensive properties specify the state | Pure, simple compressible system |
| 2 | **Equation of State (General)** | $f(P, v, T) = 0$ | Any pure substance |
| 3 | **Ideal Gas Equation** | $Pv = RT$ | R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K); specific R in kJ/(kg·K) |
| 4 | **Specific Volume** | $v = V/m$ | m³/kg |
| 5 | **Density** | $\rho = m/V = 1/v$ | kg/m³ |
| 6 | **Specific Internal Energy** | $u = U/m$ | kJ/kg |
| 7 | **Specific Enthalpy** | $h = u + Pv$ | kJ/kg |
| 8 | **Specific Entropy** | $s = S/m$ | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 9 | **Mole-Mass Conversion** | $n = m/M$ | M = molar mass in kg/kmol |
| 10 | **Cyclic State Property** | $\oint dX = 0$ | X is any state property |
| 11 | **Mechanical Equilibrium** | $\nabla P = 0$ | No pressure gradient |
| 12 | **Thermal Equilibrium** | $\nabla T = 0$ | No temperature gradient |
| 13 | **Chemical Equilibrium** | $\nabla \mu = 0$ | $\mu$ = chemical potential |
| 14 | **Quasi-Equilibrium Condition** | $P_{\text{sys}} \approx P_{\text{ext}}$ | Process is "infinitely slow" |

### 2.6 Quasi-Equilibrium (Quasi-Static) Process — A Foundational Idea

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quasi-Equilibrium Process:** A process during which the system remains **infinitesimally close to equilibrium at every intermediate state** is called a **quasi-static** (or **quasi-equilibrium**) process. The external force/pressure is changed **infinitesimally slowly**, so that $P_{\text{system}} \approx P_{\text{external}}$ at all times.

**Why does this matter?**
- A quasi-static process can be **plotted on a P-V or P-T diagram** as a continuous curve.
- The system passes through a **continuous sequence of equilibrium states**.
- Real engineering processes (like slow compression in a hydraulic press) approximate quasi-equilibrium behavior.
- Rapid processes (like explosion in a cylinder) are **non-quasi-static** and cannot be represented on a property diagram.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine pushing a heavy block very slowly across a smooth floor. At every instant, the block is at rest (a state of static equilibrium). The slow push is like a quasi-static process. Now, imagine kicking the block hard — it flies across the room, experiencing forces and accelerations that are not in equilibrium with its surroundings. This is a *non-quasi-static* process.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From the Equation of State to the State Postulate

**Step 1 — Definition of Equation of State:**

For a pure simple compressible system, an *equation of state* is a mathematical relation among the three measurable properties: pressure $P$, specific volume $v$, and temperature $T$.

$$
f(P, v, T) = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Implication:**

Because $P$, $v$, and $T$ are linked by **one** equation, the three variables have only **two** degrees of freedom. Knowing any two of them mathematically determines the third.

**Step 3 — Mathematical Statement (State Postulate):**

$$
\boxed{\text{For a pure simple compressible system, two independent intensive properties fix the state.}}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification with Ideal Gas:**

The ideal gas equation $Pv = RT$ is one equation in three variables $(P, v, T)$. Pick any two (say $P$ and $T$), then:

$$
v = \frac{RT}{P}
$$

Hence $v$ is uniquely determined. The state is fixed. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Intensive and Extensive Properties

**Step 1 — Start with a specific property definition:**

Take specific volume:

$$
v \equiv \frac{V}{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition to two subsystems:**

Consider a system of mass $m$ divided into two equal subsystems of mass $m/2$ each. Each subsystem has volume $V/2$. The specific volume of each subsystem is:

$$
v_{\text{sub}} = \frac{V/2}{m/2} = \frac{V}{m} = v_{\text{system}}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

Specific volume is **intensive** — it does not change when we subdivide the system. The same derivation applies to $u, h, s, T, P$, etc.

**Step 4 — Contrast with Extensive:**

For the total volume $V$ itself, doubling the mass doubles the volume:

$$
V \propto m \implies V(m_1 + m_2) = V_1 + V_2
$$

Hence $V$ is **extensive**.

**General Test:**

$$
\boxed{\text{A property } \phi \text{ is intensive if } \phi(m/2) = \phi(m), \text{ and extensive if } \phi(m/2) = \phi(m)/2.}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Applying the State Postulate to Water

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A closed rigid tank contains 2 kg of liquid water at $100 \text{ kPa}$. The water is heated until its temperature reaches $200 \text{ °C}$. Determine the specific volume, density, and final pressure of the water.

**Step 1 — Identify the Initial State:**

Given: $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $T_1$ = saturation temperature at 100 kPa. From steam tables:

$$
T_{sat@100\,kPa} = 99.63 \text{ °C} \approx 99.6 \text{ °C}
$$

At 100 kPa, $v_f = 0.001043 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$. Since $T_1 \approx T_{sat}$, the water is in the **saturated liquid** state.

**Step 2 — Apply the State Postulate to the Final State:**

Given: $T_2 = 200 \text{ °C}$, and the tank is **rigid**, so specific volume is constant:

$$
v_2 = v_1 = v_f @ 100 \text{ kPa} = 0.001043 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

Two properties $T_2 = 200 \text{ °C}$ and $v_2 = 0.001043 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ fix the final state.

**Step 3 — Find Final Pressure:**

At $T = 200 \text{ °C}$, $v_f = 0.001157 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$, $v_g = 0.12736 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$. Since:

$$
v_2 = 0.001043 < v_f@200°C = 0.001157
$$

the water is a **compressed liquid**. We approximate its pressure by using the saturated liquid value at the same temperature:

$$
P_2 \approx P_{sat@200°C} = 1554.3 \text{ kPa} \approx 1.554 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Density:**

$$
\rho_2 = \frac{1}{v_2} = \frac{1}{0.001043} = 958.77 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Result Summary:**

$$
\boxed{P_2 \approx 1.554 \text{ MPa}, \quad v_2 = 0.001043 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}, \quad \rho_2 \approx 958.77 \text{ kg/m}^3}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Distinguishing State vs Path Functions

**Problem:**
A gas is compressed from State 1 ($P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}, V_1 = 0.1 \text{ m}^3$) to State 2 ($P_2 = 500 \text{ kPa}, V_2 = 0.02 \text{ m}^3$) along two different paths:

- **Path A:** Direct isothermal compression
- **Path B:** First isobaric cooling to $V = 0.02 \text{ m}^3$, then isochoric heating

Compute $\Delta U$ (if $U = aT + bV$, where $a, b$ are constants) along both paths.

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta U$ along Path A:**

$$
\Delta U_A = U_2 - U_1 = (aT_2 + bV_2) - (aT_1 + bV_1)
$$

For isothermal process, $T_2 = T_1$:

$$
\Delta U_A = b(V_2 - V_1) = b(0.02 - 0.1) = -0.08b
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta U$ along Path B:**

Same initial and final states, so $U$ being a state function:

$$
\Delta U_B = U_2 - U_1 = -0.08b
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta U_A = \Delta U_B = -0.08b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta U \text{ is path-independent.}}
$$

This confirms that $U$ is a **state property**, regardless of the path. The same result would hold for any state function ($h, s, v, T, P$).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table for Equilibrium Problems

| Observation in the System | Type of Equilibrium Broken | Action Required to Restore |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure varies from point to point | Mechanical | Allow pressure waves to equalize; eliminate rigid barriers that lock in pressure differences |
| Temperature varies from point to point | Thermal | Allow heat transfer; provide time for conduction/convection; no adiabatic locks |
| Composition varies / reaction proceeding | Chemical | Allow reaction to reach equilibrium; provide catalyst if needed; ensure closed system |
| Liquid and vapor both present, levels changing | Phase | Reach saturation at the local P and T; allow mass transfer between phases |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Define the term "state" of a thermodynamic system. State the state postulate for a pure simple compressible system.**

**Model Answer:**

> A *thermodynamic state* is the condition of a system as described by its macroscopic properties (P, T, v, etc.) at an instant. Once a system is in a definite state, all its properties have fixed values.
>
> **State Postulate:** *The state of a pure simple compressible system is completely specified by **two independent intensive properties**.*
>
> For example, specifying $P$ and $T$ is sufficient to determine the state; the specific volume $v$ and specific internal energy $u$ are then uniquely determined through the equation of state and other thermodynamic relations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Distinguish between intensive and extensive properties of a thermodynamic system. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property Type | Definition | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Intensive** | Independent of system size (mass) | Pressure (P) | Temperature (T) |
| **Extensive** | Proportional to system size (mass) | Total Volume (V) | Total Internal Energy (U) |

> The test for an intensive property is that its value does **not change** if we divide the system into two parts. For an extensive property, halving the system also halves the property value. Specific properties ($v = V/m$, $u = U/m$) are intensive because the mass is divided out.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Option A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**
>
> *(a)* Define thermodynamic equilibrium. Explain the **four different types of equilibrium** that a system must satisfy to be in complete thermodynamic equilibrium. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* A piston-cylinder device contains 0.05 m³ of a gas at 200 kPa. The gas is compressed **isothermally** to a final volume of 0.02 m³. If the same gas is now compressed **isobarically** from the initial state to a final pressure of 500 kPa, determine the **change in specific volume** in both cases. Comment on whether the specific volume behaves as a state function. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **Definition (2 marks):**
> Thermodynamic equilibrium is the condition of a closed system in which there is no tendency for any spontaneous change in any macroscopic property. A system in thermodynamic equilibrium satisfies *all four* of the following sub-equilibria simultaneously:

> **Mechanical Equilibrium (2 marks):**
> The pressure throughout the system is uniform, with no pressure gradient.
> $$
> \nabla P = 0 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad P_{\text{uniform}}
> $$
> No unbalanced forces act on any part of the system or its boundary.

> **Thermal Equilibrium (1 mark):**
> The temperature throughout the system is uniform.
> $$
> \nabla T = 0 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad T_{\text{uniform}}
> $$
> No spontaneous heat transfer occurs within the system.

> **Chemical Equilibrium (1 mark):**
> The chemical composition is uniform, and there is no net tendency for any chemical reaction to proceed. Mathematically, the chemical potential $\mu$ is uniform.
> $$
> \nabla \mu = 0
> $$

> **Phase Equilibrium (1 mark):**
> For multiphase systems, the mass of each phase remains constant. The system lies on the saturation line (P-T diagram), and the chemical potentials of each phase are equal.

> **Closing statement:** A system satisfying all four is in complete thermodynamic equilibrium. *(Bonus mark for stating that this is the condition under which we can assign a single, well-defined P and T to the system.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Initial Volume $V_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$
- Initial Pressure $P_1 = 200 \text{ kPa}$
- Isothermal compression: $V_{2a} = 0.02 \text{ m}^3$ at $T_1$
- Isobaric compression (separate process): $P_2 = 500 \text{ kPa}$, same $T_1$ and $V_1$ as starting point

**Step 1 — Isothermal Process (assume ideal gas for simplicity):**

For an isothermal process on an ideal gas: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$.

$$
P_{2a} = \frac{P_1 V_1}{V_{2a}} = \frac{200 \times 0.05}{0.02} = 500 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute specific volume in both isothermal steps:**

Specific volume $v = V/m$. Let the mass be $m$. For the isothermal path:

$$
v_{1} = \frac{V_1}{m}, \quad v_{2a} = \frac{V_{2a}}{m}
$$

$$
\Delta v_{\text{isothermal}} = \frac{V_{2a} - V_1}{m} = \frac{0.02 - 0.05}{m} = \frac{-0.03}{m} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Isobaric Process:**

In the isobaric compression from $V_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$ to $P_2 = 500 \text{ kPa}$ at the same $T_1$:

For an ideal gas, the state is fully specified by P and T. Since $P_2 = 500 \text{ kPa} = P_{2a}$ and $T_2 = T_1$, the final state is **the same as the isothermal final state**. Hence:

$$
v_{2b} = v_{2a} \implies V_{2b} = V_{2a} = 0.02 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Compute change in specific volume:**

$$
\Delta v_{\text{isobaric}} = v_{2b} - v_1 = \frac{0.02 - 0.05}{m} = \frac{-0.03}{m} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (2 marks):**

$$
\boxed{\Delta v_{\text{isothermal}} = \Delta v_{\text{isobaric}} = \frac{-0.03}{m} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}
$$

Since the change in specific volume depends only on the initial and final states and not on the path, **specific volume behaves as a state function** — consistent with the fact that $v$ is a point function. $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.4 Part B Question — Option B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**
>
> *(a)* Define **state property** and **path function**. Prove mathematically that the cyclic integral of a state property is zero. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* A gas expands in a piston-cylinder device from State 1 ($P_1 = 1 \text{ MPa}, V_1 = 0.01 \text{ m}^3$) to State 2 ($P_2 = 200 \text{ kPa}, V_2 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$) along two paths. Compute the work done along:
> - **Path I:** $PV^2 = \text{constant}$ (polytropic-like curve)
> - **Path II:** First isobaric expansion at $P_1$ to $V = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$, then isochoric cooling to State 2.
> Verify whether work is a state function. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.5 Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **Definitions (3 marks):**
> - **State Property (Point Function):** A property whose value depends only on the state (initial and final), and not on the path by which the state was reached. Examples: $P, T, v, u, h, s$.
> - **Path Function:** A quantity whose value depends on the actual path followed between two states. Examples: Heat $Q$, Work $W$.

> **Mathematical Proof (4 marks):**
> Consider a state function $X$. The change in $X$ from state 1 to state 2 along any path A is:
> $$
> \Delta X_{1 \to 2}^{A} = \int_{1}^{2} dX = X_2 - X_1
> $$
> Now consider a closed cycle in which the system goes from state 1 → state 2 along path A, then returns from state 2 → state 1 along path B:
> $$
> \oint dX = \int_{1}^{2} dX + \int_{2}^{1} dX
> $$
> By path-independence:
> $$
> \int_{2}^{1} dX = -(X_2 - X_1) = X_1 - X_2
> $$
> Therefore:
> $$
> \boxed{\oint dX = (X_2 - X_1) + (X_1 - X_2) = 0}
> $$
> Hence the cyclic integral of any state property is zero. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- State 1: $P_1 = 1000 \text{ kPa}, V_1 = 0.01 \text{ m}^3$
- State 2: $P_2 = 200 \text{ kPa}, V_2 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$

**Step 1 — Work along Path I: $PV^2 = C$**

For a polytropic-type path $PV^n = C$, the boundary work is:

$$
W_{I} = \int_{1}^{2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{C}{V^2} \, dV
$$

The constant:

$$
C = P_1 V_1^2 = 1000 \times (0.01)^2 = 0.1 \text{ kPa·m}^6
$$

So:

$$
W_{I} = 0.1 \int_{0.01}^{0.05} \frac{dV}{V^2} = 0.1 \left[-\frac{1}{V}\right]_{0.01}^{0.05}
$$

$$
W_{I} = 0.1 \left(-\frac{1}{0.05} + \frac{1}{0.01}\right) = 0.1 \left(-20 + 100\right) = 0.1 \times 80 = 8 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Work along Path II: Isobaric + Isochoric**

> *Sub-step 2a — Isobaric expansion at $P_1 = 1000$ kPa from $V_1 = 0.01$ m³ to $V_a = 0.05$ m³:*
> $$
> W_{2a} = P_1 (V_a - V_1) = 1000 \times (0.05 - 0.01) = 40 \text{ kJ}
> $$

> *Sub-step 2b — Isochoric (constant volume) process from $V = 0.05$ m³ at $P = 1000$ kPa to $P_2 = 200$ kPa:*
> Since $dV = 0$:
> $$
> W_{2b} = \int P \, dV = 0 \text{ kJ}
> $$

> *Total Work along Path II:*
> $$
> W_{II} = W_{2a} + W_{2b} = 40 + 0 = 40 \text{ kJ}
> $$

**Step 3 — Comparison and Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{W_I = 8 \text{ kJ}, \quad W_{II} = 40 \text{ kJ}, \quad W_I \neq W_{II}}
$$

Since the work done depends on the path, **work is NOT a state function** — it is a path function. The same conclusion holds for heat. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In Part (a) of Q-A or Q-B, students often write "two properties" instead of "**two independent intensive properties**." The words "independent" and "intensive" both carry marks.
> 2. In cyclic integral proofs, students forget the crucial step $\int_{2}^{1} dX = -\int_{1}^{2} dX$, and lose 2 marks.
> 3. In work problems, students confuse the isochoric work as "non-zero" or use the wrong polytropic exponent. Always confirm the form of the path equation before integrating.
> 4. The distinction between state and path functions is worth at least 3–4 marks in every KTU paper on this topic — never skip it.

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **State** = complete set of macroscopic property values at an instant. A state is a "snapshot."
- **State Postulate**: For a pure simple compressible system, **two independent intensive properties** fully specify the state.
- **Equation of State**: $f(P, v, T) = 0$ — a single equation linking three variables means only two degrees of freedom.
- **Intensive Property**: Independent of mass (P, T, v, u, h, s, ρ).
- **Extensive Property**: Proportional to mass (V, m, U, H, S).
- **Specific property** = (extensive property) / (mass) → always intensive.
- **State Function** vs **Path Function**: $P, T, v, u, h, s$ are state; $Q, W$ are path.
- **Cyclic State Integral**: $\oint dX = 0$ for any state property $X$.
- **Thermodynamic Equilibrium** = Mechanical + Thermal + Chemical + Phase equilibrium simultaneously.
- **Quasi-Equilibrium Process** = a process where $P_{\text{system}} \approx P_{\text{external}}$ at every intermediate step; the system passes through a continuous sequence of equilibrium states and is plottable on P-V / P-T diagrams.
- **Phase Equilibrium** exists only when two (or more) phases coexist along the saturation line.
- The state of a saturated mixture is fixed by **two properties**, e.g., $P$ and quality $x$ (since $T$ and $P$ are not independent in the saturation region).
- **First Law of Thermodynamics** (introduced next) depends entirely on internal energy $U$ being a state function — a direct consequence of the state postulate.
- Remember: a system in equilibrium has **no driving force for change**. This is the most fundamental property of an equilibrium state.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamic Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamic Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermodynamic Properties** are macroscopic, time-averaged, physically measurable (or calculable) characteristics of a system that describe its **state** and are independent of the **path** by which the system arrived at that state. Mathematically, a property is any quantity whose differential $dZ$ is an **exact differential**, satisfying the reciprocity relation:
> $$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial x}\right)_{y} = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial y}\right)_{x}$$
> where $dZ = M\,dx + N\,dy$.

A thermodynamic **state** is completely specified when a sufficient set of independent properties (such as $P$, $T$, $v$, $x$, etc.) is known — this is formally known as the **State Postulate** for simple compressible systems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Patient's Health Card"**: Imagine a patient visiting a doctor. The doctor records *Body Temperature, Blood Pressure, Heart Rate, Weight* — these are the *properties* of the patient. If you know these readings, you fully describe the patient's current "state." You do not need to know *how* the patient reached that state (whether he ran, slept, or ate). If any one reading changes, the state has changed. Thermodynamic properties behave identically: they are the "health readings" of matter. The system can reach that exact state via infinite different paths, but the properties remain the same.

## 1.3 The Three Grand Classifications

| Classification | Definition | Examples | Real-World Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Intensive** | Independent of system mass | $T$, $P$, $v$, $\rho$ | Air pressure in a tyre |
| **Extensive** | Proportional to system mass | $V$, $U$, $H$, $S$, $m$ | Total volume of a gas tank |
| **Specific (Intensive)** | Extensive property per unit mass | $u = U/m$, $h = H/m$, $s = S/m$ | Steam-table lookup values |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb**: When a property's *value at a point* inside a system equals the value for the *entire system*, it is **intensive**. When doubling the mass doubles the property, it is **extensive**.

## 1.4 The Key Engineering Properties Catalog

1. **Pressure ($P$):** Normal force per unit area exerted by a fluid on its boundary. Unit: **Pa** (N/m²), bar, psi, atm.
2. **Temperature ($T$):** Measure of thermal equilibrium / molecular kinetic energy. Scales: Kelvin, Celsius, Rankine, Fahrenheit.
3. **Volume ($V$) & Specific Volume ($v$):** $v = V/m$ — volume per unit mass. Unit: **m³/kg**.
4. **Density ($\rho$):** Mass per unit volume, $\rho = m/V = 1/v$. Unit: **kg/m³**.
5. **Internal Energy ($U$ or $u$):** Microscopic kinetic + potential energy of molecules.
6. **Enthalpy ($H$ or $h$):** Total heat content, $h = u + Pv$.
7. **Entropy ($S$ or $s$):** Measure of molecular disorder or energy unavailability.
8. **Specific Heats ($C_p$, $C_v$):** Energy required to raise 1 kg (or 1 kmol) by 1 K under constant pressure/volume.
9. **Quality ($x$):** Mass fraction of vapor in a saturated liquid–vapor mixture.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pressure — The Six Engineering Faces of "Pressure"

Pressure is the most-misunderstood property in undergraduate mechanics. The KTU board routinely tests the four faces of pressure in numericals:

- **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** Measured from a perfect vacuum (true zero). **Always positive**.
- **Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}$):** Pressure exerted by the atmosphere. Standard value: **$P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$**.
- **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$):** Pressure *above* atmospheric, read by a bourdon gauge.
- **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{vac}$):** Pressure *below* atmospheric.

The fundamental relationships (memorize these for Part A):

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Absolute" is sacred in thermodynamics**: All gas laws ($PV = mRT$), all steam tables, and all property charts use **absolute** values only. If you plug gauge pressure into $PV=mRT$, the answer is wrong by exactly $101.325$ kPa every single time. This is the **#1 KTU valuation pitfall**.

## 2.2 Temperature Scales & Absolute Zero

$$T(K) = T(^{\circ}C) + 273.15$$

$$T(^{\circ}R) = T(^{\circ}F) + 459.67$$

$$T(K) = \frac{5}{9} \, T(^{\circ}R)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Laws of Thermodynamics use Kelvin and Rankine ONLY.** Always convert °C to K before using any ideal-gas formula in KTU board exams.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Property / Relation | Mathematical Form | SI Units | Condition of Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Equation | $Pv = RT$ | J/(kg·K) | Ideal gases only |
| 2 | Universal Gas Constant | $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) | kJ/(kmol·K) | Universal constant |
| 3 | Specific Gas Constant | $R = R_u / M$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Per unit mass |
| 4 | Enthalpy Definition | $h = u + Pv$ | kJ/kg | All pure substances |
| 5 | Internal Energy (Ideal Gas) | $du = C_v \, dT$ | kJ/kg | Ideal gas |
| 6 | Enthalpy Change (Ideal Gas) | $dh = C_p \, dT$ | kJ/kg | Ideal gas |
| 7 | Mayer's Relation | $C_p - C_v = R$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Ideal gas |
| 8 | Specific Heat Ratio | $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ | dimensionless | Ideal gas |
| 9 | Quality (Mixture) | $x = m_v / (m_f + m_v)$ | dimensionless | Sat. mixture |
| 10 | Mixture Specific Volume | $v = v_f + x \, v_{fg}$ | m³/kg | Sat. mixture |
| 11 | Mixture Internal Energy | $u = u_f + x \, u_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | Sat. mixture |
| 12 | Mixture Enthalpy | $h = h_f + x \, h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | Sat. mixture |
| 13 | Density Relation | $\rho = 1 / v$ | kg/m³ | Always |
| 14 | Mass × Specific | $V = m \cdot v$ | m³ | Always |
| 15 | Gauge–Absolute | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Pa | Pressure conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pipe symbol `|` is forbidden inside table cells. All condition separators use `;` or a phrase.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality ($x$)** is critical in **steam power plants (Rankine cycle)** — the boiler exit must be nearly dry saturated ($x \approx 0.9$–$0.99$) to prevent turbine blade erosion.
- **Enthalpy ($h$)** is the property used in flow processes (open systems) where both energy and flow-work cross the boundary.
- **Specific heats ($C_p$, $C_v$)** dictate fuel efficiency in **IC engines and gas turbines** — the Otto and Brayton cycle efficiencies both depend on $\gamma$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Enthalpy Identity $H = U + PV$

This is the single most-asked derivation in KTU 2024 Module 1 (predicts a 7-mark sub-question).

> **Starting Postulate:** For a closed system at rest, the total energy is the sum of internal, kinetic, and potential energies. We seek a composite property convenient for *flow* (open) systems.

**Step 1 — Define the First Law for a Closed Stationary System (no KE, no PE):**

$$\delta Q = dU + \delta W$$

**Step 2 — For a Quasi-Equilibrium Process, the boundary work equals $P\,dV$:**

$$\delta W = P\,dV$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the first law:**

$$\delta Q = dU + P\,dV$$

**Step 4 — Apply the product rule of differentiation to the term $P\,V$:**

$$d(PV) = P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**Step 5 — Solve the product rule for $P\,dV$:**

$$P\,dV = d(PV) - V\,dP$$

**Step 6 — Substitute this back into Step 3:**

$$\delta Q = dU + d(PV) - V\,dP$$

**Step 7 — Group the differentials of $U$ and $PV$:**

$$\delta Q = d(U + PV) - V\,dP$$

**Step 8 — Define the new composite property — Enthalpy:**

$$\boxed{H \equiv U + PV}$$

**Step 9 — Rewrite the first law using $H$ at constant pressure (the most common engineering case, $dP = 0$):**

$$\delta Q_{P} = dH$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is why enthalpy is called the "heat content at constant pressure" — for a constant-pressure process (which is what boilers, condensers, and most chemical reactors operate at), the heat transferred equals the change in enthalpy. This derivation is the foundation of the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mayer's Relation $C_p - C_v = R$

**Step 1 — Enthalpy definition, per unit mass:**

$$h = u + Pv$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides (exact differentials):**

$$dh = du + d(Pv)$$

**Step 3 — Apply ideal gas law $Pv = RT$, so $d(Pv) = R\,dT$:**

$$dh = du + R\,dT$$

**Step 4 — By definition of specific heats for an ideal gas:**

$$dh = C_p \, dT \quad \text{and} \quad du = C_v \, dT$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into Step 3:**

$$C_p \, dT = C_v \, dT + R\,dT$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $dT$ (non-zero for any temperature change):**

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$

**Step 7 — The Ratio of Specific Heats (used in cycle efficiencies):**

$$\gamma = \frac{C_p}{C_v} = 1 + \frac{R}{C_v}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Property Relations in a Two-Phase Mixture (Quality)

**Step 1 — Definition of quality $x$:**

$$x = \frac{m_{vapor}}{m_{liquid} + m_{vapor}} = \frac{m_v}{m_{total}}$$

**Step 2 — Total volume in the mixture:**

$$V = V_f + V_v = m_f v_f + m_v v_v$$

**Step 3 — Divide throughout by total mass $m = m_f + m_v$:**

$$v = \frac{m_f}{m} v_f + \frac{m_v}{m} v_v = (1 - x)\,v_f + x\,v_v$$

**Step 4 — Expand and group by $v_f$:**

$$v = v_f - x\,v_f + x\,v_v = v_f + x(v_v - v_f)$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the saturated difference $v_{fg} = v_v - v_f$:**

$$\boxed{v = v_f + x\,v_{fg}}$$

**Step 6 — By the same linear-in-mass argument (internal energy and enthalpy are extensive):**

$$u = u_f + x\,u_{fg} \quad ; \quad h = h_f + x\,h_{fg}$$

> **Why is the mixture formula linear in $x$?** Because in a *saturated* mixture at equilibrium, $T$ and $P$ are fixed (they lie on the saturation curve), so $v_f$ and $v_v$ are constants at that $T$ or $P$. The total property is a weighted average of the two pure-phase values, weighted by mass fraction. This is the lever rule used in KTU T-v and P-v diagrams.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU 2024 Style (for student practice)

> **Problem:** A rigid container of $0.5$ m³ holds a saturated water mixture at $200$ kPa. The mixture has a quality $x = 0.7$. Determine (a) the temperature, (b) the mass of vapor, (c) the total internal energy, and (d) the density.

**Solution Walk-Through:**

**(a) Temperature** — At $P = 200$ kPa, the saturation temperature is read from the steam table:
$$T_{sat} = 120.21^{\circ}C \;\;(\text{or}\; 393.36 \text{ K})$$

**(b) Mass of vapor** — From the steam table at 200 kPa: $v_f = 0.001061$ m³/kg, $v_g = 0.8857$ m³/kg.
$$v = v_f + x(v_g - v_f) = 0.001061 + 0.7 \times (0.8857 - 0.001061) = 0.6203 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$
$$m_{total} = \frac{V}{v} = \frac{0.5}{0.6203} = 0.806 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_v = x \cdot m_{total} = 0.7 \times 0.806 = 0.564 \text{ kg}$$

**(c) Total internal energy** — From the steam table: $u_f = 504.49$ kJ/kg, $u_{fg} = 2024.6$ kJ/kg.
$$u = u_f + x\,u_{fg} = 504.49 + 0.7 \times 2024.6 = 1921.71 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$U = m \cdot u = 0.806 \times 1921.71 = 1548.9 \text{ kJ}$$

**(d) Density:**
$$\rho = \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{0.6203} = 1.612 \text{ kg/m}^3$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Thermodynamic Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[THERMODYNAMIC PROPERTIES] --> B[INTENSIVE]
    A --> C[EXTENSIVE]
    A --> D[SPECIFIC]
    B --> B1[Pressure P]
    B --> B2[Temperature T]
    B --> B3[Specific Volume v]
    B --> B4[Density rho]
    B --> B5[Specific Heats Cp Cv]
    C --> C1[Volume V]
    C --> C2[Mass m]
    C --> C3[Internal Energy U]
    C --> C4[Enthalpy H]
    C --> C5[Entropy S]
    C --> C6[Gibbs Energy G]
    D --> D1[specific internal energy u]
    D --> D2[specific enthalpy h]
    D --> D3[specific entropy s]
    B --> E[DERIVED COMPOSITE]
    E --> E1[h = u PLUS Pv]
    E --> E2[G = h MINUS Ts]
    E --> E3[x quality of mixture]
```

## 4.2 Pressure Conversion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PABS[Absolute Pressure Pabs] --> PZERO[Reference Vacuum ZERO]
    PATM[Atmospheric Pressure Patm EQUALS 101.325 kPa]
    PGAU[Gauge Pressure Pgauge]
    PVA[Vacuum Pressure Pvac]
    PABS -- minus Patm --> PGAU
    PABS -- minus Patm --> PVA
    PATM -- plus Pgauge --> PABS
    PATM -- minus Pvac --> PABS
```

## 4.3 State Postulate & Property Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SYSTM[SIMPLE COMPRESSIBLE SYSTEM] --> PROP1[Pressure P]
    SYSTM --> PROP2[Temperature T]
    SYSTM --> PROP3[Specific Volume v]
    SYSTM --> PHASE[Phase Indicator Quality x]
    PROP1 --> STATE[UNIQUE THERMODYNAMIC STATE]
    PROP2 --> STATE
    PROP3 --> STATE
    PHASE --> STATE
    STATE --> OUT1[All other properties derivable]
    OUT1 --> OUT2[h u s g from Tables]
    OUT1 --> OUT3[h u from Cp Cv correlations]
```

## 4.4 Process Function vs Property (Point) Function — Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State ONE Pi Ti] -->|Process A1| B[State TWO Pf Tf]
    A -->|Process A2| B
    A -->|Process A3| B
    NOTE1[Property delta Z EQUALS Z2 MINUS Z1 SAME for ALL paths]
    NOTE2[Process Q and W depend on PATH chosen]
    B --> C[Same property values]
    A --> C
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set 2]**
**Differentiate between intensive and extensive properties. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Intensive properties* are those which are **independent of the mass of the system** and have the same value at every point within the system in equilibrium. Examples: **Pressure ($P$), Temperature ($T$), Specific Volume ($v$), Density ($\rho$)**.
- *Extensive properties* are those which **depend on the mass (or size) of the system** and are additive when systems are combined. Examples: **Total Volume ($V$), Total Internal Energy ($U$), Total Enthalpy ($H$), Mass ($m$)**.
- Conversion of an extensive property to an intensive one is done by dividing by mass, giving a *specific property*. *(1 mark for the distinction, 1 mark each for examples, 0.5 mark for the conversion note = 3 marks total).*

### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**
**Define the term "quality" of a wet vapour. Why is it impossible to define quality in the superheated region?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Quality $x$* is defined as the **ratio of the mass of vapour to the total mass of the liquid–vapour mixture** at saturation: $x = m_v / (m_v + m_f)$. It is a dimensionless number between 0 (saturated liquid) and 1 (saturated vapour). *(1.5 marks)*
- In the **superheated region**, the substance exists as a *single phase* — 100% vapour. There is no liquid to define a mass fraction against, so the concept of "how much of it is liquid" is meaningless. The state is uniquely specified by $P$ and $T$ alone. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (ESE 14-Mark Module Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Every Module in the question paper offers an **Internal Choice** of two full 14-mark questions. Each long question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Below, both alternatives are provided for full board preparation.

---

### 🔹 Question B — Option A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Comprehensive]**

**(a) Define enthalpy. Derive the expression $H = U + PV$ for a flowing fluid. Show that for a constant-pressure process, the heat transfer equals the change in enthalpy. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Enthalpy is a composite thermodynamic property defined as $H = U + PV$, where $U$ is the total internal energy, $P$ the absolute pressure, and $V$ the volume of the system. Its SI unit is the joule (J), and the specific form is $h = u + Pv$ in kJ/kg.

2. **Derivation (5 marks):** Begin with the first law for a closed stationary system:
$$\delta Q = dU + \delta W$$
For a quasi-equilibrium process, $\delta W = P\,dV$. Substituting:
$$\delta Q = dU + P\,dV$$
Apply the product rule $d(PV) = P\,dV + V\,dP$, hence $P\,dV = d(PV) - V\,dP$. Substituting again:
$$\delta Q = dU + d(PV) - V\,dP = d(U + PV) - V\,dP$$
By definition $H = U + PV$, so:
$$\delta Q = dH - V\,dP$$
For a **constant-pressure process**, $dP = 0$, and therefore:
$$\boxed{\delta Q_P = dH}$$
This proves that at constant pressure, the heat transferred to a closed system equals the change in its enthalpy. This is the foundational result that makes enthalpy the workhorse property of all open-system and flow analyses.

3. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The relation $H = U + PV$ transforms a flow-work-inclusive energy balance into a single-property differential, vastly simplifying the SFEE used in turbines, compressors, nozzles, and boilers.

**(b) A piston-cylinder device contains 0.005 m³ of liquid water and 0.05 m³ of water vapour in equilibrium at 200 kPa. Determine: (i) the temperature, (ii) the quality, (iii) the total mass, and (iv) the total enthalpy of the mixture. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **(i) Temperature (1 mark):** At saturation, $P = 200$ kPa implies the mixture is on the saturation curve. From the steam table:
$$T_{sat} = 120.21^{\circ}C \;\;(\approx 393.36 \text{ K})$$

2. **(ii) Quality (2 marks):** From the steam table at 200 kPa: $v_f = 0.001061$ m³/kg and $v_g = 0.8857$ m³/kg. The specific volume of the mixture:
$$v = \frac{V_{total}}{m} \quad \text{or} \quad V_{total} = V_f + V_g$$
Let masses be $m_f$ and $m_g$. Then $V_f = m_f v_f = 0.005$ m³ and $V_g = m_g v_g = 0.05$ m³.
$$m_f = \frac{0.005}{0.001061} = 4.712 \text{ kg} \quad ; \quad m_g = \frac{0.05}{0.8857} = 0.0565 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{total} = m_f + m_g = 4.712 + 0.0565 = 4.7685 \text{ kg}$$
$$x = \frac{m_g}{m_{total}} = \frac{0.0565}{4.7685} = 0.01185 \;\;(\text{or about } 1.19\%)$$

3. **(iii) Total mass (1 mark):** Already computed above:
$$m_{total} = 4.77 \text{ kg}$$

4. **(iv) Total enthalpy (3 marks):** From the steam table at 200 kPa: $h_f = 504.68$ kJ/kg and $h_{fg} = 2201.6$ kJ/kg. Therefore:
$$h = h_f + x\,h_{fg} = 504.68 + 0.01185 \times 2201.6 = 504.68 + 26.09 = 530.77 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$H = m \cdot h = 4.7685 \times 530.77 = 2531.2 \text{ kJ}$$

---

### 🔹 Question B — Option B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Comprehensive]**

**(a) Define specific heat at constant volume and constant pressure. For an ideal gas, derive Mayer's relation $C_p - C_v = R$. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Definition of $C_v$ (1.5 marks):** The specific heat at constant volume, $C_v$, is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of **one unit mass** of a substance by **one degree** (1 K or 1 °C), while the **volume is held constant**. For an ideal gas:
$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_v = \frac{du}{dT}$$
Units: kJ/(kg·K).

2. **Definition of $C_p$ (1.5 marks):** The specific heat at constant pressure, $C_p$, is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of **one unit mass** of a substance by **one degree**, while the **pressure is held constant**. For an ideal gas:
$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_p = \frac{dh}{dT}$$
Units: kJ/(kg·K).

3. **Derivation of Mayer's Relation (4 marks):** Start with the specific enthalpy definition for a pure substance:
$$h = u + Pv$$
Differentiate:
$$dh = du + d(Pv)$$
For an ideal gas, $Pv = RT$. Differentiating at constant composition:
$$d(Pv) = R\,dT$$
Substitute:
$$dh = du + R\,dT$$
Apply the specific heat definitions:
$$C_p\,dT = C_v\,dT + R\,dT$$
Cancel $dT$ (non-zero):
$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$
This is **Mayer's Relation**, valid strictly for ideal gases. It relates the difference between the two specific heats to the specific gas constant, showing that $C_p$ is always greater than $C_v$ by exactly $R$ — the extra energy goes into the flow work $P\,dV$ done at constant pressure.

**(b) A rigid tank of volume 2 m³ contains air at 1 bar and 27 °C. The tank is heated until the pressure becomes 5 bar. Assuming air behaves as an ideal gas with $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K) and $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), determine: (i) the mass of air, (ii) the final temperature, and (iii) the heat transferred during the process. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **(i) Mass of air (2 marks):** Initial state: $P_1 = 1$ bar $= 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300$ K, $V = 2$ m³. Specific gas constant for air:
$$R = C_p - C_v = 1.005 - 0.718 = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$
Using the ideal gas law:
$$m = \frac{P_1 V}{R T_1} = \frac{100 \times 2}{0.287 \times 300} = \frac{200}{86.1} = 2.323 \text{ kg}$$

2. **(ii) Final temperature (2 marks):** For a rigid tank, $V$ is constant, so $P/T$ is constant:
$$\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 = T_1 \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_1} = 300 \times \frac{5}{1} = 1500 \text{ K}$$

3. **(iii) Heat transferred (3 marks):** For a rigid tank, the boundary work $W = 0$ (constant volume). The first law reduces to:
$$Q = \Delta U = m\,C_v\,(T_2 - T_1)$$
$$Q = 2.323 \times 0.718 \times (1500 - 300) = 2.323 \times 0.718 \times 1200 = 2001.2 \text{ kJ}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Temperature Conversion:** Forgetting to convert °C to K (use **300 K**, not 27). This single error cascades through every subsequent calculation and costs **3 to 4 marks**.
> 2. **Pressure Conversion:** Using bar instead of kPa without consistency. Stick to **kPa** throughout.
> 3. **R for Air:** A common error is using the universal gas constant $R_u = 8.314$ directly. You **must** divide by molar mass $M = 28.97$ kg/kmol to get $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K).
> 4. **Sign of Work:** In a rigid tank, $W = 0$. Students often write $W = P\,dV$ with $dV = 0$ and still get partial credit. Mention it explicitly.
> 5. **First-Law Application:** Mistaking $Q = \Delta H$ for a constant-volume process — that holds only for **constant-pressure** processes. For constant volume, $Q = \Delta U$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ A **property** is a state function; its value depends only on the *state*, not the *path*. **Work ($W$)** and **Heat ($Q$)** are path functions.
- ✅ **Intensive** properties do not change with mass; **Extensive** properties scale with mass. Dividing extensive by mass gives a **specific** (intensive) property.
- ✅ The **State Postulate**: A simple compressible system needs **2 independent intensive properties** (plus composition for mixtures) to fix the state.
- ✅ Pressure has 4 faces: **absolute, atmospheric, gauge, vacuum**. Use $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$. Gas laws and tables always use **absolute**.
- ✅ Temperature in thermodynamic equations **must be absolute** (K or °R).
- ✅ **Enthalpy** is a derived composite property: $H = U + PV$. At constant $P$, $\delta Q = dH$.
- ✅ **Mayer's Relation** for ideal gases: $C_p - C_v = R$. Always true for ideal gases; never true exactly for real substances.
- ✅ **Quality $x$** is defined only in the *wet region*. It is the lever arm in $T\text{-}v$, $P\text{-}v$, and $h\text{-}s$ diagrams.
- ✅ **Mixture formulas:** $v = v_f + x\,v_{fg}$, $u = u_f + x\,u_{fg}$, $h = h_f + x\,h_{fg}$.
- ✅ **Density** is the inverse of specific volume: $\rho = 1/v$.
- ✅ **Universal gas constant** $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K); **specific** gas constant $R = R_u / M$ — units differ by a factor of 1000 if confused.
- ✅ For a **constant-volume** process in a closed system, $W = 0$ and $Q = \Delta U$.
- ✅ For a **constant-pressure** process in a closed system, $Q = \Delta H$.
- ✅ Memorize: $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, $T(K) = T(^{\circ}C) + 273.15$, $C_p$ (air) = 1.005, $C_v$ (air) = 0.718, $R$ (air) = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K).
- ✅ In a **saturated mixture**, $P$ and $T$ are *not* independent — fixing one fixes the other through the saturation curve.
- ✅ The **State Postulate** failure mode: Students use $P$ and $v$ to fix a state of a two-phase system without realising $P$ alone already forces $T_{sat}$. Thus only **one** additional property is independent in the wet region.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processes and Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Processes and Cycles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Thermodynamic Process — Formal Definition

A **thermodynamic process** is the path or sequence of **equilibrium states** through which a system passes when it undergoes a change in one or more of its state variables (pressure $P$, volume $V$, temperature $T$, internal energy $U$, entropy $S$, enthalpy $H$, etc.). Mathematically, it is described as a continuous locus of state points plotted on coordinates such as $P$–$V$, $T$–$S$, $P$–$H$, or $T$–$V$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> A *process* is a transformation of a system from one equilibrium state to another. The complete description requires specification of the path traversed, the energy transfers (work and heat) occurring across the boundary, and the property changes of the working substance.

### 1.2 Thermodynamic Cycle — Formal Definition

A **thermodynamic cycle** is a closed loop of processes in which the system returns to its **initial state**. Because all properties are point functions, the net change in any property over a complete cycle is identically **zero**. The net effect of a cycle is the **conversion of energy** between heat and work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** A cycle has $\oint dU = 0$, $\oint dH = 0$, $\oint dS_{system} = 0$, but $\oint \delta Q \neq 0$ and $\oint \delta W \neq 0$. The first law applied to a cycle gives $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The "Hiking Trail" (for a Process):**
Imagine a hiker walking from base camp (state 1) to a mountain peak (state 2). The trail taken is the **path**, the elevation gained is the **change in state**, and the sweat expended is the **work done**. Different trails (steep vs. gradual) represent different processes between the same two states. The energy spent depends on the path, but the final position does not.

**Analogy 2 — The "Daily Commute Loop" (for a Cycle):**
A delivery driver starts at the warehouse (state 1), makes deliveries, and returns to the warehouse at the end of the day. The vehicle (system) returns to its starting condition. However, fuel was consumed (heat input) and the engine produced useful work at the wheels (work output). The cycle converts chemical energy → mechanical work. This is exactly how a car engine, a steam power plant, or a refrigerator operates.

### 1.4 Classification of Processes — At a Glance

| Category | Sub-Types | Defining Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Quasi-Static (Quasi-Equilibrium)** | Reversible, Irreversible | System passes through a continuous succession of equilibrium states |
| **Non-Quasi-Static** | Irreversible | System traverses non-equilibrium intermediate states |
| **Reversible** | Quasi-static + frictionless | Process can be exactly reversed with no net entropy generation |
| **Irreversible** | All real processes | Direction is unidirectional; entropy of universe increases |
| **Constant Property** | Isobaric, Isothermal, Isochoric | One intensive property is held constant |
| **Polytropic** | General family | $PV^n = \text{constant}$ where $n \neq 1, \gamma$ |
| **Adiabatic** | Isentropic (reversible) | No heat transfer across boundary |

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics (Bolded)

- Universal gas constant: $\mathbf{R_u = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}}$
- For air (ideal gas): $\mathbf{R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}}$
- Specific heat ratio for air: $\mathbf{\gamma = 1.4}$
- Standard atmospheric pressure: $\mathbf{P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}}$
- Standard temperature: $\mathbf{T_{std} = 288.15 \text{ K}}$

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 numerical problems, you will almost always treat air, oxygen, nitrogen, and combustion products as **ideal gases** with $R$ and $\gamma$ given or implied. Always write down the assumed working fluid and its constants at the start of your solution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Quasi-Static (Quasi-Equilibrium) Process

A process is **quasi-static** if the deviation from equilibrium is infinitesimally small at every instant. Practically, this means the process is "infinitely slow" so that the system has uniform pressure and temperature at every intermediate state.

**Why it matters:** Quasi-static processes are the *only* ones that can be **reversed** to retrace their exact path on a $P$–$V$ diagram. They form the theoretical benchmark against which all real (irreversible) processes are compared.

### 2.2 Reversible vs. Irreversible Process

A **reversible process** is one that:
1. Is quasi-static at every point.
2. Has no friction, viscosity, or other dissipative effects.
3. Has no unconstrained free expansion.
4. Can be reversed by an infinitesimal change in the external condition.
5. Leaves both the system and surroundings in their original states.

All natural and engineered processes are **irreversible** to some degree. Sources of irreversibility include friction, unrestrained expansion, heat transfer across a finite temperature difference, mixing of fluids, chemical reaction, and electrical resistance.

### 2.3 The Polytropic Process — Master Equation

The most general single-property-restrained process for an ideal gas follows:

$$PV^n = C \quad \text{(constant)}$$

where $n$ is the **polytropic index**. The following sub-processes are special cases:

- $n = 0$: Isobaric (constant pressure)
- $n = 1$: Isothermal (constant temperature)
- $n = \gamma$: Isentropic / Adiabatic reversible
- $n = \infty$: Isochoric (constant volume)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Memorize the polytropic family — it has appeared in nearly every previous KTU ESE paper for PCMUT403 / equivalent courses. A single $n$ value spans almost every process in the syllabus.

### 2.4 The First Law for a Closed System (Non-Cyclic Process)

For a closed system undergoing a quasi-static process:

$$Q_{1-2} = \Delta U + W_{1-2} = (U_2 - U_1) + \int_{1}^{2} P \, dV$$

For an ideal gas, $\Delta U = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$, and the boundary work $W_{1-2}$ is the **area under the process curve on a $P$–$V$ diagram**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Process | Path Relation | Work $W_{1-2}$ | Heat $Q_{1-2}$ | $\Delta U$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Isobaric** ($P = $ const) | $V/T = $ const | $P(V_2 - V_1)$ | $m c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | $m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **Isochoric** ($V = $ const) | $P/T = $ const | $0$ | $m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$ | $m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **Isothermal** ($T = $ const, ideal gas) | $PV = $ const | $P_1 V_1 \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | $= W_{1-2}$ | $0$ |
| **Adiabatic Reversible (Isentropic)** | $PV^\gamma = $ const | $\dfrac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - \gamma}$ | $0$ | $m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **Polytropic** ($PV^n = $ const) | $PV^n = $ const | $\dfrac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$ | $m c_n (T_2 - T_1)$ | $m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **Throttling** (irreversible) | $H_1 = H_2$ | $0$ (flow) | $0$ (adiabatic) | $\Delta U = -\Delta(PV)$ |

**Useful Relations (All Process Families):**

$$TV^{n-1} = \text{const} \quad ; \quad TP^{\frac{1-n}{n}} = \text{const}$$

$$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n-1}$$

**Cycle Performance Metrics:**

$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\text{refrigerator}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\text{heat pump}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

**Carnot Cycle (Reference Benchmark):**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H} \quad ; \quad \text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Gas power cycles (Otto, Diesel):** Automobile and heavy-vehicle engines.
- **Vapor power cycle (Rankine):** Coal, nuclear, and solar thermal power plants.
- **Gas turbine cycle (Brayton):** Jet aircraft engines, industrial power generation.
- **Refrigeration/Heat pump cycles:** Domestic ACs, cold storage, LNG liquefaction.
- **Polytropic analysis:** Used to model real compressor and turbine processes where $1 < n < \gamma$ (compressor) and $n > \gamma$ (turbine).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Work Done in a General Polytropic Process

**Statement:** Show that for an ideal gas undergoing a reversible polytropic process $PV^n = C$, the boundary work is:

$$W_{1-2} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n} \quad (n \neq 1)$$

**Step 1 — Write the defining equation of the process.**

For a reversible process in a closed system (piston-cylinder), boundary work is:

$$W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the polytropic relation.**

From $PV^n = C$, we have $P = C V^{-n}$. Substituting:

$$W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} C V^{-n} \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration.**

$$W_{1-2} = C \left[ \dfrac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \dfrac{C}{1-n} \left( V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to $PV$ terms using $C = PV^n$.**

Since $C V^{1-n} = PV^n \cdot V^{1-n} = PV$, we obtain:

$$W_{1-2} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-n}$$

**Step 5 — Express the result using ideal-gas law for convenience.**

Using $PV = mRT$:

$$W_{1-2} = \dfrac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1-n}$$

This is the **standard KTU 2024 result** for polytropic work. **Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation — First-Law Energy Equation for a Polytropic Process

**Step 1 — First law for a closed system.**

$$Q_{1-2} = \Delta U + W_{1-2} = m c_v (T_2 - T_1) + \dfrac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1-n}$$

**Step 2 — Factor $(T_2 - T_1)$.**

$$Q_{1-2} = m(T_2 - T_1) \left[ c_v + \dfrac{R}{1-n} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Use $c_p = c_v + R$ to simplify the bracket.**

$$Q_{1-2} = m(T_2 - T_1) \left[ c_v + \dfrac{c_p - c_v}{1-n} \right] = m c_n (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 4 — Identify the polytropic specific heat.**

$$c_n = c_v \cdot \dfrac{n - \gamma}{1 - n}$$

This is why a polytropic process can be characterized by a single specific heat $c_n$ when $n \neq \gamma$ (where $c_n \to 0$, recovering the adiabatic case).

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Carnot Cycle Efficiency (Heat Engine)
