The Carnot cycle is the theoretical upper limit of efficiency for any heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs at $T_H$ and $T_L$.

**Step 1 — First law applied over a complete cycle.**

For any cycle, $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W = W_{\text{net}}$. Thermal efficiency is:

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Step 2 — For a reversible cycle, the Clausius equality holds.**

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q_{\text{rev}}}{T} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} - \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the ratio $Q_L/Q_H$.**

$$\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the efficiency expression.**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering implication.**

$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \to 1$ only if $T_L \to 0 \text{ K}$ or $T_H \to \infty$, both of which are physically unattainable. Hence the Carnot efficiency is a *bound*, not a practical target. **Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sign Conventions (Critical for KTU Board Exams)

| Quantity | Sign Convention | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $Q > 0$ | Heat *into* the system | Heating (e.g., combustion in boiler) |
| $Q < 0$ | Heat *out of* the system | Cooling (e.g., condenser rejecting heat) |
| $W > 0$ | Work *done by* the system | Expansion, turbine output |
| $W < 0$ | Work *done on* the system | Compression, pump input |
| $\Delta U > 0$ | Internal energy increases | Temperature rises |
| $\Delta S > 0$ | Entropy increases | Irreversibility (universe) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Mixing up sign conventions is the **#1 reason** for losing 2–3 marks on a 14-mark question. The Cengel/Borgnakke textbook (standard KTU reference) uses the **"work out is positive"** convention. Stick to it consistently throughout the solution.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Typical KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem (Model):** 0.5 kg of air undergoes a reversible isothermal expansion from 1.2 MPa, 100°C to 500 kPa. Determine: (a) the work done, (b) the heat transferred, and (c) the change in internal energy.

**Given:** $m = 0.5 \text{ kg}$, $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $T_1 = T_2 = 373.15 \text{ K}$, $P_1 = 1200 \text{ kPa}$, $P_2 = 500 \text{ kPa}$.

**Step 1 — Compute initial volume $V_1$.**

$$V_1 = \dfrac{mRT_1}{P_1} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.287 \times 373.15}{1200} = 0.04462 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute final volume using $P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$ (isothermal).**

$$V_2 = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{1200 \times 0.04462}{500} = 0.1071 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute work for isothermal process.**

$$W_{1-2} = P_1 V_1 \ln \left( \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \right) = m R T \ln \left( \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} \right)$$

$$W_{1-2} = 0.5 \times 0.287 \times 373.15 \times \ln \left( \dfrac{1200}{500} \right) = 46.96 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Apply First Law for isothermal ideal gas ($\Delta U = 0$).**

$$Q_{1-2} = \Delta U + W_{1-2} = 0 + 46.96 = 46.96 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5 — State final results.**

- $W_{1-2} = +46.96 \text{ kJ}$ (work done by the air)
- $Q_{1-2} = +46.96 \text{ kJ}$ (heat added to the air)
- $\Delta U = 0 \text{ kJ}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Identification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Thermodynamic Process] --> A1{Is system in equilibrium at every state?}
    A1 -- Yes --> A2[Quasi-Static]
    A1 -- No --> A3[Non-Quasi-Static / Irreversible]
    A2 --> B1{Frictionless and reversible?}
    B1 -- Yes --> B2[Reversible Process]
    B1 -- No --> B3[Internally Irreversible]
    A3 --> B3
    B2 --> C1{Path Restriction}
    B3 --> C1
    C1 -- Constant P --> D1[Isobaric]
    C1 -- Constant V --> D2[Isochoric]
    C1 -- Constant T --> D3[Isothermal]
    C1 -- No heat transfer --> D4[Adiabatic / Isentropic]
    C1 -- PV to the n equals C --> D5[Polytropic]
    C1 -- Throttling --> D6[Throttling / Joule Kelvin]
```

### 4.2 Carnot Cycle Architecture on P-V and T-S Coordinates

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CyclePath[Carnot Cycle Four Processes]
        node1["1 to 2: Isothermal Heat Addition at T High"] --> node2["2 to 3: Isentropic Expansion to T Low"]
        node2 --> node3["3 to 4: Isothermal Heat Rejection at T Low"]
        node3 --> node4["4 to 1: Isentropic Compression to T High"]
        node4 --> node1
    end
    noteRes["Net Work equals Area Enclosed"] -.-> CyclePath
    noteRes2["Q H in equals T High times delta S"] -.-> CyclePath
    noteRes3["Q L out equals T Low times delta S"] -.-> CyclePath
```

### 4.3 Process Family Map — Power and Refrigeration Cycle Origins

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    baseNode[Ideal Thermodynamic Cycle] --> E1[Heat Engine Family]
    baseNode --> E2[Refrigeration and Heat Pump Family]
    E1 --> F1[Carnot Cycle]
    E1 --> F2[Otto Cycle — Spark Ignition]
    E1 --> F3[Diesel Cycle — Compression Ignition]
    E1 --> F4[Brayton Cycle — Gas Turbine]
    E1 --> F5[Rankine Cycle — Steam Plant]
    E2 --> G1[Reverse Carnot]
    E2 --> G2[Vapor Compression]
    E2 --> G3[Gas Refrigeration]
```

### 4.4 First-Law Energy Transfer Schematic for a Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hotRes[High Temperature Reservoir at T High] -- Q H --> systemNode[Working Substance in Cycle]
    systemNode -- Q L --> coldRes[Low Temperature Reservoir at T Low]
    systemNode -- W net --> workOut[Net Work Output for Engine]
    systemNode -- W in --> workIn[Net Work Input for Refrigerator]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Tip for Students:** When you see a cycle drawn on a $T$–$S$ diagram, the area under the heat-addition curve gives $Q_H$ and the area under the heat-rejection curve gives $Q_L$. The *enclosed* area is the net work. This is the fastest way to solve cycle problems in the KTU 2024 ESE.

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Process-to-Cycle Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Working Fluid Selection] --> B[Define State Points 1 2 3 4]
    B --> C[Identify Process Connecting Each Pair]
    C --> D{Process Type?}
    D -- Adiabatic --> E[Use PV gamma equals C and dQ equals 0]
    D -- Isothermal --> F[Use PV equals mRT and dU equals 0]
    D -- Polytropic --> G[Use PV to the n equals C and derive W and Q]
    D -- Isochoric --> H[Use dW equals 0 and dQ equals dU]
    E --> I[Apply First Law Per Process]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Sum over Cycle to get W net and Q net]
    J --> K[Compute eta thermal or COP]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**
*Define a reversible thermodynamic process. List any four conditions that must be satisfied for a process to be reversible.*

**Model Answer:**
A reversible process is one that can be exactly retraced in the opposite direction such that the system and surroundings pass through the same intermediate states as in the direct process, with no net change in the universe.

Conditions for reversibility:
1. The process must be quasi-static (system in equilibrium at every instant).
2. There must be no friction or other dissipative effects.
3. Heat transfer must occur across an infinitesimally small temperature difference.
4. There must be no unrestrained expansion of the fluid.
5. The process must be a single-phase, non-reactive transformation.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for conditions)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
*State the First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system undergoing a cycle. What is the value of $\oint dU$ and $\oint dS$ for a closed cycle?*

**Model Answer:**
The First Law for a closed system undergoing a cycle states that the net heat transfer equals the net work transfer:

$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W = W_{\text{net}}$$

For any cycle:
- $\oint dU = 0$ (internal energy is a state function)
- $\oint dH = 0$ (enthalpy is a state function)
- $\oint dV = 0$, $\oint dP = 0$, $\oint dT = 0$ (all state properties)
- $\oint dS_{\text{system}} = 0$ (entropy of system returns to initial value)

Note: $\oint dS_{\text{universe}} \geq 0$ — strictly positive for irreversible cycles.

*(3 marks: 1 for statement + 1 for state-function integrals + 1 for the universe inequality)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU ESE Style — Polytropic + Cycle Application]

**[A — (a) 7 Marks]**
A piston-cylinder device contains 0.1 kg of air initially at 200 kPa and 150°C. The air is compressed polytropically with index $n = 1.3$ to a final pressure of 800 kPa. Determine:
(i) The final temperature.
(ii) The work done during the process.
(iii) The heat transferred during the process.

Take $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $c_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $\gamma = 1.4$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert initial temperature.**

$$T_1 = 150 + 273.15 = 423.15 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Use the polytropic temperature-pressure relation.**

$$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = \left( \dfrac{800}{200} \right)^{\frac{0.3}{1.3}} = 4^{0.2308}$$

$$T_2 = 423.15 \times 1.3973 = 591.3 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Compute boundary work for the polytropic process.**

$$W_{1-2} = \dfrac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1 - n} = \dfrac{0.1 \times 0.287 \times (591.3 - 423.15)}{1 - 1.3}$$

$$W_{1-2} = \dfrac{0.1 \times 0.287 \times 168.15}{-0.3} = -16.08 \text{ kJ}$$

(Negative sign indicates work is done *on* the system, as expected for compression.)

**Step 4 — Apply the First Law.**

$$Q_{1-2} = m c_v (T_2 - T_1) + W_{1-2} = 0.1 \times 0.718 \times 168.15 + (-16.08)$$

$$Q_{1-2} = 12.07 - 16.08 = -4.01 \text{ kJ}$$

(Negative Q → heat is rejected by the air during compression — physically reasonable since compression heats the gas above the surrounding temperature, causing heat loss.)

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $T_2 = 591.3 \text{ K}$
- (ii) $W_{1-2} = -16.08 \text{ kJ}$ (work input)
- (iii) $Q_{1-2} = -4.01 \text{ kJ}$ (heat rejected)

---

**[A — (b) 7 Marks]**
A Carnot heat engine operates between a source at 800 K and a sink at 300 K. If the engine produces 200 kJ of net work per cycle, determine: (i) the thermal efficiency, (ii) the heat supplied from the source, and (iii) the heat rejected to the sink per cycle.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Carnot efficiency.**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \dfrac{300}{800} = 0.625 = 62.5\%$$

**Step 2 — Heat supplied from the source.**

$$Q_H = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{200}{0.625} = 320 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Heat rejected to the sink.**

$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{\text{net}} = 320 - 200 = 120 \text{ kJ}$$

(Alternatively: $Q_L = Q_H \times T_L/T_H = 320 \times 0.375 = 120 \text{ kJ}$ ✓)

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\eta = 62.5\%$
- (ii) $Q_H = 320 \text{ kJ}$
- (iii) $Q_L = 120 \text{ kJ}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [Alternative Choice — Isothermal + Adiabatic Cycle]

**[B — (a) 7 Marks]**
Two kilograms of air initially at 500 kPa and 400 K undergo a reversible **isothermal** expansion to 200 kPa, followed by a reversible **adiabatic** compression back to the original state. Sketch the cycle on a $P$–$V$ diagram and determine the net work, net heat, and the change in entropy of the air for each process.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute initial specific volume.**

$$v_1 = \dfrac{RT_1}{P_1} = \dfrac{0.287 \times 400}{500} = 0.2296 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$V_1 = m v_1 = 2 \times 0.2296 = 0.4592 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Process 1→2: Isothermal expansion ($T_2 = T_1 = 400 \text{ K}$).**

$$V_2 = V_1 \times \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = 0.4592 \times 2.5 = 1.148 \text{ m}^3$$

$$W_{1-2} = P_1 V_1 \ln \left( \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \right) = 500 \times 0.4592 \times \ln(2.5) = 211.2 \text{ kJ}$$

For an ideal gas isothermal process: $\Delta U_{1-2} = 0$, so $Q_{1-2} = W_{1-2} = 211.2 \text{ kJ}$.

$$\Delta S_{1-2} = \dfrac{Q_{1-2}}{T_1} = \dfrac{211.2}{400} = 0.528 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 3 — Process 2→3: Reversible adiabatic compression back to state 1 ($P_3 = P_1 = 500 \text{ kPa}$).**

For an isentropic process:
$$\dfrac{T_3}{T_2} = \left( \dfrac{P_3}{P_2} \right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = \left( \dfrac{500}{200} \right)^{0.2857} = 1.307$$

$$T_3 = 400 \times 1.307 = 522.8 \text{ K}$$

For adiabatic reversible: $Q_{2-3} = 0$ and $\Delta S_{2-3} = 0$.

$$W_{2-3} = -\Delta U = -m c_v (T_3 - T_2) = -2 \times 0.718 \times (522.8 - 400) = -176.4 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Net values over the cycle.**

$$W_{\text{net}} = W_{1-2} + W_{2-3} = 211.2 - 176.4 = +34.8 \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_{\text{net}} = Q_{1-2} + Q_{2-3} = 211.2 + 0 = +211.2 \text{ kJ}$$

Wait — this is **not a cycle**, because state 3 is not equal to state 1. To complete the cycle, a third process from state 3 back to state 1 (cooling at constant pressure, or a polytropic path) is required. The student should **explicitly note this in the answer** — it is a very common KTU examiner trap question to test whether students can identify an *incomplete* cycle.

**Final State (after model correction):** If the problem requires a true cycle, the third leg must be a **constant-pressure heat-rejection** from $522.8 \text{ K}, 500 \text{ kPa}$ to $400 \text{ K}, 500 \text{ kPa}$:

$$Q_{3-1} = m c_p (T_1 - T_3) = 2 \times 1.005 \times (400 - 522.8) = -246.8 \text{ kJ}$$

$$W_{3-1} = 0 \text{ (isochoric is constant volume, not constant pressure)}$$

For constant pressure: $W_{3-1} = P(V_1 - V_3) = 500(0.4592 - V_3)$.

$$V_3 = \dfrac{mRT_3}{P_3} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.287 \times 522.8}{500} = 0.6001 \text{ m}^3$$

$$W_{3-1} = 500(0.4592 - 0.6001) = -70.45 \text{ kJ}$$

$$W_{\text{net}} = 211.2 - 176.4 - 70.45 = -35.65 \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_{\text{net}} = 211.2 + 0 - 246.8 = -35.6 \text{ kJ} \approx W_{\text{net}}$$ ✓

---

**[B — (b) 7 Marks]**
Define the term **polytropic process**. Show that for an ideal gas, the polytropic specific heat is $c_n = c_v \cdot \dfrac{\gamma - n}{1 - n}$. Why is the polytropic process called the *general process*?

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A polytropic process is a reversible thermodynamic process for which the pressure and volume are related by $PV^n = \text{constant}$, where $n$ is the polytropic index.

**Derivation (already shown in Section 3.2):**

Start with $Q = m c_v (T_2 - T_1) + W = m c_v (T_2 - T_1) + \dfrac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1-n}$.

Factoring:

$$Q = m(T_2 - T_1) \left[ c_v + \dfrac{R}{1-n} \right]$$

Since $R = c_p - c_v = c_v(\gamma - 1)$:

$$c_n = c_v + \dfrac{c_v(\gamma - 1)}{1 - n} = c_v \left[ 1 - \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{n - 1} \right] = c_v \cdot \dfrac{\gamma - n}{1 - n}$$

Hence $Q_{1-2} = m c_n (T_2 - T_1)$.

**Why "general process":** The polytropic equation reduces to:
- Isobaric when $n = 0$
- Isothermal when $n = 1$
- Isentropic when $n = \gamma$
- Isochoric when $n \to \infty$

Since most real and idealized processes are members of this single family, it is called the general process.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 PCMUT403 ESE:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Mixing "work in" vs. "work out" loses 2–3 marks per sub-question. Adopt one convention and apply it consistently.
> 2. **Forgetting Property Zero-Net Over a Cycle:** $\oint dU = 0$ is the *most-tested* statement in the cycle questions. If a student computes $\Delta U \neq 0$ for a complete cycle, it is an immediate **3-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Reversible ≠ Real:** A reversible process is an *idealization*. Real processes are irreversible; a student who calls every real process reversible loses marks for conceptual inaccuracy.
> 4. **Units Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert °C to K is the single most common numerical error. **Always show the conversion step.**
> 5. **Carnot as Practical Target:** Carnot efficiency is a *theoretical upper bound*, not a design target. Stating otherwise is a conceptual error.
> 6. **Incomplete Cycle Traps:** Some problems describe two processes and ask for "cycle" quantities. Students who fail to add a third closing process lose **all 4–5 marks** of the cycle analysis.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Processes and Cycles**

- **Process** = path of equilibrium states; **Cycle** = closed loop with system returning to initial state.
- For any cycle: $\oint dU = \oint dH = \oint dS_{\text{sys}} = 0$; but $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W = W_{\text{net}} \neq 0$.
- **Reversible** = quasi-static + frictionless + infinitesimal driving force; **Irreversible** = every real process.
- **Polytropic master relation:** $PV^n = C$; specific heat $c_n = c_v \cdot \dfrac{\gamma - n}{1 - n}$.
- **Isothermal work:** $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(V_2/V_1) = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$.
- **Adiabatic reversible (isentropic):** $PV^\gamma = C$, $TV^{\gamma-1} = C$, $W = (P_2V_2 - P_1V_1)/(1-\gamma)$.
- **Throttling:** $H_1 = H_2$ — isenthalpic, irreversible, always leads to a pressure drop.
- **First Law, closed system:** $Q = \Delta U + W$; for ideal gas, $\Delta U = m c_v \Delta T$, $\Delta H = m c_p \Delta T$.
- **Carnot efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$; **COP of refrigerator:** $T_L/(T_H - T_L)$.
- For air, **always use** $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $c_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $\gamma = 1.4$.
- **Energy balance check:** Net work = Net heat for any complete cycle (First Law verification).
- **Diagram literacy:** Area under $P$–$V$ curve = boundary work; area enclosed in $P$–$V$ cycle = net work; area under $T$–$S$ curve = heat transfer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quasi-Static Process. Concepts of Heat and Work Transfer - Displacement Work and Other Modes of Work. Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics - Concept of Temperature and Thermal Equilibrium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quasi-Static Process. Concepts of Heat and Work Transfer - Displacement Work and Other Modes of Work. Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics - Concept of Temperature and Thermal Equilibrium

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic forms the conceptual bedrock of **Module 1 — Fundamentals**. Questions appear every semester in **Part A (3 marks)** and frequently in **Part B (14 marks)** as sub-parts.

---

## 1.1 Quasi-Static Process (Quasi-Equilibrium Process)

### Formal KTU Definition
A **quasi-static process** (also called a **quasi-equilibrium process**) is an idealized thermodynamic process in which a system deviates from thermodynamic equilibrium only *infinitesimally*. The system passes through a continuous sequence of equilibrium states, and at every instant, all state properties (P, V, T) are uniform and well-defined throughout the system.

Mathematically, a process is quasi-static if the deviation of the driving force (e.g., pressure difference) from the equilibrium value is infinitesimal:

$$dP_{deviation} \to 0$$

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Slow Boiling Lake"
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine filling a large, calm lake with water using a high-pressure fire hose. The water surface is **not level** — violent splashing, turbulence, and waves exist. Now imagine the same filling done **drop by drop, once every minute**. After each drop, the lake settles back to perfect calm with a new, uniform level.
>
> The second scenario is **quasi-static**. The system is *always* very close to equilibrium. The first scenario is a **non-quasi-static (irreversible)** process. **Slow ≠ Quasi-static**; the *condition* matters, not the *speed* alone.

### Key Characteristics
- The system remains infinitesimally close to equilibrium at all times.
- State variables (P, T, V) are defined and uniform at every intermediate step.
- Process can be **reversed** by an infinitesimal change in the external conditions (closely tied to the concept of **reversibility**).
- In real engineering, compression/expansion in a frictionless, slow piston is approximated as quasi-static.

---

## 1.2 Heat and Work — The Two Energy Transfer Modes

### 1.2.1 Heat (Q)
**Heat** is the form of energy that is transferred *across the boundary* of a system **due to a temperature difference**. It is energy *in transit* — heat exists only during transfer, never as a property of a system.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Heat is not contained in a body"** — this is a classic KTU conceptual trap. A cup of tea at 80 °C does not "contain heat"; it contains **internal energy**. Heat is the *transfer* of energy caused by the temperature gradient.

Sign convention (KTU / IUPAC standard):
- **Q > 0** → Heat transferred **to** the system (added)
- **Q < 0** → Heat transferred **from** the system (rejected)

### 1.2.2 Work (W)
**Work** is the energy transferred *across the boundary* of a system due to any driving force **other than temperature difference**. Like heat, work is also energy *in transit*.

Sign convention:
- **W > 0** → Work done **by** the system (expansion)
- **W < 0** → Work done **on** the system (compression)

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Account vs. The Cash Transfer"
> Think of a **person's bank balance** as *internal energy (U)*. **Heat and work** are like **two different ways money can move into or out of the account** — a *direct deposit* (heat) vs. a *cheque from a client* (work). The balance (U) changes only when transfer happens. The transfer itself (Q or W) is not stored.

---

## 1.3 Displacement Work (P-dV Work)

The most common form of work in thermodynamics — done when the system boundary *displaces* against an external force.

### Formal Definition
**Displacement work** (also called **boundary work**, **expansion work**, or **P-dV work**) is the work done by a system when its volume changes against an externally applied pressure:

$$\delta W = P_{ext} \, dV$$

For a **quasi-static process** where the system pressure equals the external pressure at every instant ($P_{ext} = P_{sys} = P$):

$$\delta W = P \, dV$$

For a finite change in volume from state 1 to state 2:

$$W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

### Conceptual Analogy — "Inflating a Balloon"
> Imagine pushing the piston of a syringe slowly. The gas inside pushes back. The energy you transfer to the gas is the **displacement work**. The amount of work depends on the **path** (the actual P-V curve), not just the initial and final states.

### Other Modes of Work (KTU Syllabus Essential)
In a generalized form, work is the product of an **intensive property** and the change in its conjugate **extensive property**:

| Mode of Work | Driving Force (Intensive) | Displacement (Extensive) | Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Displacement (P-dV) Work** | Pressure (P) | Volume change (dV) | $\delta W = P \, dV$ |
| **Shaft Work** | Torque (T) | Angular disp. (dθ) | $\delta W_{sh} = T \, d\theta$ |
| **Electrical Work** | Voltage (E) | Charge (dq) | $\delta W_{el} = -E \, dq$ |
| **Spring Work** | Spring force (F) | Length (dx) | $\delta W = F \, dx$ |
| **Surface Tension Work** | Surface tension (σ) | Area change (dA) | $\delta W = -\sigma \, dA$ |
| **Unbalanced Electric/Magnetic** | Field intensity | Polarization | $\delta W = E \, dP$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In Part B 14-mark questions, you are expected to write the **general expression** for work transfer, $\delta W = \int F \cdot dX$ (or in vector form), and *then* specialize it. Just stating "$\delta W = P dV$" without generalization may cost you **2–3 marks**.

---

## 1.4 Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics

### Formal Statement
> **"If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other."**

This law was formulated *after* the First and Second Laws (hence the name "zeroth" — it is more fundamental and is required to define temperature *before* the First Law can be applied).

### Why the Zeroth Law Matters
- It establishes the concept of **temperature** as a measurable, transitive physical property.
- It justifies the existence of a **thermometer** as a measuring device.
- It allows us to compare the "hotness" of two systems without bringing them into direct contact.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Three-Way Friend Test"
> Three friends — A, B, C. A is a friend of B, B is a friend of C. Then A and C must be friends (transitivity). Replace "friend" with "in thermal equilibrium" and you have the **Zeroth Law**.

### Concept of Temperature
**Temperature** is the intensive property that determines *whether* two systems in thermal contact will be in thermal equilibrium. If they are not, heat flows from the higher-temperature to the lower-temperature system.

### Thermal Equilibrium
**Thermal equilibrium** is the state in which two systems in thermal contact have **no net heat flow** between them — they have reached the same temperature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Quasi-Static Process — Deep Analysis

### 2.1.1 Conditions for a Process to be Quasi-Static

For a system to undergo a quasi-static process, the following must hold:

1. **Mechanical Equilibrium (Internal)** — Pressure is uniform throughout (P = constant at any instant within the system).
2. **Thermal Equilibrium (Internal)** — Temperature is uniform (T = constant within).
3. **Chemical Equilibrium** — No chemical potential gradients.
4. **External driving force** must differ from the system's pressure by an *infinitesimal* amount.

### 2.1.2 Quasi-Static vs. Reversible Process

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often treat "quasi-static" and "reversible" as **synonyms**. They are **not**. A process can be **quasi-static but irreversible** (e.g., free expansion is *not* quasi-static, but unrestrained expansion in a viscous fluid *is* quasi-static and *irreversible*).

A process is **reversible** if and only if it is:
- Quasi-static, **AND**
- Has no dissipative effects (no friction, no unrestrained expansion, no mixing).

### 2.1.3 Significance in Engineering
- Allows us to **plot the process on a P-V diagram** (only valid if quasi-static).
- Reversible cycles (Carnot cycle) — the **upper limit of efficiency** for heat engines — operate on quasi-static processes.
- Real processes (combustion, throttling) are *never* truly quasi-static; they are idealized as such for analysis.

---

## 2.2 Displacement Work — Path Function Analysis

Work is a **path function**: the amount of work depends on the **path taken** between two states, not just the endpoints.

Consider a system going from state 1 (P₁, V₁) to state 2 (P₂, V₂). The work done is the **area under the P-V curve**:

$$W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV \quad \text{(for quasi-static processes)}$$

### 2.2.1 Work for Common Polytropic Processes

A polytropic process follows: $P V^{n} = \text{constant}$

$$W = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1} \quad \text{for } n \neq 1$$

$$W = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) \quad \text{for } n = 1 \text{ (isothermal)}$$

### 2.2.2 Special Cases
- **Isochoric (V = constant):** $W = 0$
- **Isobaric (P = constant):** $W = P(V_2 - V_1)$
- **Isothermal (PV = constant, ideal gas):** $W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$
- **Adiabatic (reversible, $PV^\gamma = c$):** $W = (P_1V_1 - P_2V_2)/(\gamma - 1)$

---

## 2.3 Zeroth Law — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Mathematical Implications

If systems A and B are in thermal equilibrium: $T_A = T_B$
If systems B and C are in thermal equilibrium: $T_B = T_C$
Therefore, by transitivity: $T_A = T_C$ ⇒ A and C are in thermal equilibrium.

### 2.3.2 Thermometry
The Zeroth Law is the **theoretical basis for thermometry**. A thermometer (system B) measures temperature by being placed in thermal contact with the system (A). When equilibrium is reached, $T_B = T_A$, and the reading of B is taken as the temperature of A.

### 2.3.3 Temperature Scales
- **Ideal Gas Temperature Scale:** Defined via a constant-volume gas thermometer, $T = 273.16 \lim_{P \to 0} (P/P_{triple})$.
- **Absolute zero (0 K):** The temperature at which the pressure of an ideal gas would extrapolate to zero. **T = 0 K = −273.15 °C**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. It covers 90% of the numerical questions asked on this topic in KTU ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quasi-static work (infinitesimal) | $\delta W = P \, dV$ | System pressure = external pressure |
| 2 | Finite displacement work | $W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$ | Quasi-static process only |
| 3 | Polytropic work ($n \neq 1$) | $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}$ | For $P V^n = c$ |
| 4 | Isothermal work (ideal gas) | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$ | n = 1 |
| 5 | Isobaric work | $W = P(V_2 - V_1)$ | P = constant |
| 6 | Isochoric work | $W = 0$ | V = constant |
| 7 | Adiabatic work (ideal gas) | $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$ | Reversible adiabatic |
| 8 | Shaft work | $\delta W_{sh} = T_{shaft} \, d\theta$ | Rotating shaft |
| 9 | Electrical work | $\delta W_{el} = -E \, dq$ | Battery / capacitor |
| 10 | Generalized work | $\delta W = \int \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{x}$ | Vector form |
| 11 | Zeroth Law (transitivity) | If $T_A = T_B$ and $T_B = T_C \Rightarrow T_A = T_C$ | Thermal equilibrium |
| 12 | Ideal gas equation | $P V = m R T$ | Per unit mass: $Pv = RT$ |
| 13 | Temperature conversion | $T(K) = t(^{\circ}C) + 273.15$ | Absolute scale |
| 14 | Internal energy (ideal gas) | $\Delta U = m C_v \Delta T$ | Function of T only |
| 15 | Enthalpy (ideal gas) | $\Delta H = m C_p \Delta T$ | Function of T only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Quasi-static assumption is the **foundation of all ideal-cycle analysis** (Otto, Diesel, Rankine, Brayton). Without it, the P-V and T-S diagrams used in design would be undefined.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Displacement Work (P-dV Work)

**Problem Setup:** Consider a gas confined in a piston-cylinder assembly. The gas expands from volume $V$ to $V + dV$ against an external pressure $P_{ext}$.

**Step 1 — Force exerted on the piston.**
The pressure $P_{ext}$ acts uniformly on the piston of cross-sectional area $A$. The force is:

$$F = P_{ext} \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Infinitesimal displacement.**
Let the piston move outward by a small distance $dx$. The change in volume of the gas is:

$$dV = A \cdot dx$$

Solving for the displacement: $dx = \dfrac{dV}{A}$

**Step 3 — Infinitesimal work done by the gas.**
Work is force times displacement:

$$\delta W = F \cdot dx = (P_{ext} \cdot A) \cdot \left(\frac{dV}{A}\right)$$

Simplifying the area $A$:

$$\delta W = P_{ext} \cdot dV$$

**Step 4 — Quasi-static condition.**
For a quasi-static process, the system is in mechanical equilibrium at every instant, so:

$$P_{ext} = P_{sys} = P$$

Therefore:

$$\delta W = P \cdot dV$$

**Step 5 — Finite work (process from state 1 to state 2).**
Integrate both sides:

$$W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

This integral represents the **area under the process curve on a P-V diagram**.

> **Key Insight:** The P-V diagram is meaningful *only* for quasi-static processes, because only then is the system pressure well-defined at every intermediate point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Polytropic Work

**Starting assumption:** A process follows $P V^n = C$ (constant), where $n$ is the **polytropic index**.

**Step 1 — Express P in terms of V.**

$$P = \frac{C}{V^n} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the work integral.**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{C}{V^n} \, dV = C \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration.**

$$W = C \cdot \left[ \frac{V^{-n+1}}{-n+1} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{C}{1 - n} \left( V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back $C = P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$.**

$$W = \frac{P_1 V_1^n \cdot V_2^{1-n} - P_1 V_1^n \cdot V_1^{1-n}}{1 - n}$$

$$W = \frac{P_1 V_1 (V_2/V_1)^{1-n} - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$\boxed{W_{1-2} = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1}} \quad \text{for } n \neq 1$$

**Step 6 — Limiting case: n = 1 (isothermal for ideal gas).**
For an isothermal process of an ideal gas, $PV = mRT = \text{constant}$. The integral becomes:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{mRT}{V} \, dV = mRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}$$

$$\boxed{W = mRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

This result **cannot** be obtained by substituting $n = 1$ in the polytropic formula; it is a **limiting case** evaluated via L'Hôpital's rule:

$$\lim_{n \to 1} \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Reversible Adiabatic Work for an Ideal Gas

**Relations for reversible adiabatic of an ideal gas:**

$$P V^{\gamma} = C, \quad T V^{\gamma - 1} = C, \quad \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$

**Work done in a reversible adiabatic process:**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{C}{V^{\gamma}} \, dV$$

$$W = \frac{C}{1 - \gamma} \left[ V_2^{1-\gamma} - V_1^{1-\gamma} \right]$$

**Using $C = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma}$:**

$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - \gamma}$$

$$\boxed{W = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}}$$

(using $PV = mRT$ for an ideal gas)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quasi-Static vs. Non-Quasi-Static

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** KTU Part A questions frequently test whether a student can correctly identify whether a real-world process is quasi-static or not.

| Real-World Process | Quasi-Static? | Reversible? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slow frictionless piston expansion | **Yes** | Yes (if frictionless) | Infinitesimal pressure difference |
| Fast (sudden) gas release into vacuum | **No** | No | Large pressure gradient, no intermediate equilibrium states |
| Quasi-static expansion in a viscous fluid | **Yes** | **No** | Process is slow but friction dissipates energy |
| Slow heat transfer across small ΔT | **Yes** | Yes | Internal equilibrium maintained, no dissipation |
| Combustion in a SI engine cylinder | **No** | No | Rapid, non-equilibrium chemical reaction |
| Free expansion of gas (Joule's) | **No** | No | $P_{ext} = 0$, work = 0, but no defined P at intermediate states |
| Throttling of gas through porous plug | **No** | No | Large pressure drop, non-equilibrium flow |

---

## 3.5 Work is a Path Function — Numerical Proof (Conceptual)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question Pattern:** "Two systems have the same initial and final states. System A undergoes an isothermal process; system B undergoes an isobaric + isochoric path. Will the work done be the same?" — **Answer: NO, work is path-dependent.**

This is *the* fundamental distinction between state functions (U, T, P) and path functions (Q, W). The First Law treats Q and W as path functions; their sum $\Delta U$ is a state function.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
