# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define a quasi-static process. Is a quasi-static process always reversible? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **quasi-static process** is an idealized process in which the system remains infinitesimally close to thermodynamic equilibrium at every stage, and all state variables (P, T, V) are uniform and well-defined throughout the system at every instant.

> **Reversibility check:** A quasi-static process is **not always reversible**. Reversibility requires the process to be quasi-static *and* free from dissipative effects (friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing). Example: slow expansion of a gas in a highly viscous fluid is quasi-static but **irreversible** due to viscous dissipation. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. How does it lead to the concept of temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** *"If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other."*

**Concept of temperature:** The Zeroth Law establishes the **transitivity of thermal equilibrium**. This implies the existence of a unique intensive property — **temperature** — that characterizes whether two systems will be in thermal equilibrium when placed in contact. If two systems have the same temperature, they are in thermal equilibrium; if not, heat flows from the higher-temperature to the lower-temperature system. The law also justifies the use of a **thermometer** as a third reference system. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Quasi-Static Process & Displacement Work Focus**

`[KTU University Exam — Model QP, PCMUT403]`

**Q A(a). [7 marks]** Define a quasi-static process with a suitable diagram. Derive the expression for displacement work done by a gas undergoing a quasi-static process. Show that the work done is the area under the P-V curve.

**Q A(b). [7 marks]** A gas in a piston-cylinder assembly undergoes a polytropic process from an initial state of 1 bar, 0.1 m³ to a final state of 4 bar. The polytropic index is $n = 1.3$. Determine:
- (i) The final volume.
- (ii) The work done by the gas.
- (iii) The change in internal energy if the gas is air ($C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $M = 28.97$ kg/kmol).

---

### **Model Solution — Q A(a)** [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Diagram Description [1 mark]**
Refer to the piston-cylinder schematic from SECTION 4.1. State: a gas enclosed by a frictionless piston of cross-sectional area $A$, exerting pressure $P$ on the piston, with external pressure $P_{ext}$ acting downward.

**Step 2 — Force on piston [1 mark]**

$$F = P_{ext} \cdot A$$

**Step 3 — Infinitesimal displacement $dx$, volume change $dV$ [1 mark]**

$$dV = A \cdot dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad dx = \frac{dV}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Work expression [1 mark]**

$$\delta W = F \cdot dx = P_{ext} \cdot A \cdot \frac{dV}{A} = P_{ext} \, dV$$

**Step 5 — Quasi-static condition [1 mark]**
For a quasi-static process, $P_{ext} = P_{sys} = P$, so:

$$\delta W = P \, dV$$

**Step 6 — Integration for finite change [1 mark]**

$$W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

**Step 7 — Geometric interpretation [1 mark]**
This definite integral is the **area under the process curve on a P-V diagram** between $V_1$ and $V_2$. Hence, displacement work is the area enclosed under the P-V path. ✔

---

### **Model Solution — Q A(b)** [7 marks]

**Given:**
$P_1 = 1$ bar $= 100$ kPa, $V_1 = 0.1$ m³, $P_2 = 4$ bar $= 400$ kPa, $n = 1.3$
$C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $M = 28.97$ kg/kmol, $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K

**Step 1 — Final volume using $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$ [2 marks]**

$$V_2 = V_1 \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} = 0.1 \times \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{1/1.3}$$

$$\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{1/1.3} = (0.25)^{0.7692}$$

Computing: $\ln(0.25) = -1.3863$, $0.7692 \times (-1.3863) = -1.0663$, $e^{-1.0663} = 0.3443$

$$V_2 = 0.1 \times 0.3443 = 0.03443 \text{ m}^3 \approx 0.0344 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Work done [2 marks]**

$$W = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1} = \frac{(100)(0.1) - (400)(0.03443)}{1.3 - 1}$$

$$W = \frac{10 - 13.772}{0.3} = \frac{-3.772}{0.3} = -12.57 \text{ kJ}$$

The negative sign indicates **work done on the gas** (compression). Magnitude: **12.57 kJ**.

**Step 3 — Mass and change in internal energy [3 marks]**

$$R = \frac{R_u}{M} = \frac{8.314}{28.97} = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

Using ideal gas: $P_1 V_1 = mRT_1$

$$m = \frac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1}$$

(We need $T_1$. The problem does not give $T_1$ directly, so we use the *change* in internal energy expressed via temperatures.)

$$T_1 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{mR}, \quad T_2 = \frac{P_2 V_2}{mR}$$

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{P_1 V_1} = \frac{400 \times 0.03443}{100 \times 0.1} = \frac{13.772}{10} = 1.3772$$

$$\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1) = m C_v T_1 \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1} - 1\right) = C_v \cdot \frac{P_1 V_1}{R} \left(0.3772\right)$$

$$= \frac{0.718}{0.287} \times 10 \times 0.3772 = 2.502 \times 10 \times 0.3772 = 9.44 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta U \approx +9.44 \text{ kJ}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign convention slip-up (2 marks):** Students frequently forget to mark work *negative* for compression. $P_2 > P_1$ in this problem ⇒ gas compressed ⇒ $V_2 < V_1$ ⇒ $W < 0$.
> - **Polytropic exponent mistake (1 mark):** $1/n$ is the exponent, not $n$. The expression is $V_2 = V_1 (P_1/P_2)^{1/n}$, not $V_1 (P_1/P_2)^n$.
> - **Units check (1 mark):** Pressure must be in kPa, volume in m³ to get work in kJ. Mixing bar with m³ gives a wrong factor of 100.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Zeroth Law & Heat/Work Concepts Focus**

`[KTU University Exam — Model QP, PCMUT403]`

**Q B(a). [7 marks]** Distinguish clearly between heat and work as modes of energy transfer. Define displacement work and derive its expression. List any **four** other modes of work with their mathematical expressions.

**Q B(b). [7 marks]** State and explain the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. How does it form the basis of thermometry? A thermometer is used to measure the temperature of a furnace. The thermometer reads 1500 K when in thermal equilibrium with the furnace. If the furnace is at 1500 K, what is the temperature reading on the Celsius scale? What is the principle of measurement here?

---

### **Model Solution — Q B(a)** [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Heat vs. Work distinction [3 marks]**

| Aspect | Heat (Q) | Work (W) |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving cause** | Temperature difference | Force/pressure difference |
| **Direction** | High T → Low T (spontaneous) | Direction of force × displacement |
| **Sign convention** | + into system, − out | + by system, − on system |
| **Path function?** | Yes | Yes |
| **Property of system?** | No (only in transit) | No (only in transit) |
| **Mechanism** | Random molecular motion transfer | Ordered macroscopic displacement |

**Step 2 — Displacement work definition and derivation [2 marks]**
Already derived in SECTION 3.1. State:

$$\delta W = P_{ext} \, dV, \quad W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV \text{ (quasi-static)}$$

**Step 3 — Four other modes of work [2 marks]**

1. **Shaft work:** $\delta W_{sh} = T_{shaft} \, d\theta$
2. **Electrical work:** $\delta W_{el} = -E \, dq$
3. **Spring work:** $\delta W = -k x \, dx$
4. **Surface tension work:** $\delta W = -\sigma \, dA$

(Any four are acceptable.)

---

### **Model Solution — Q B(b)** [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Statement of Zeroth Law [2 marks]**
*"If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other."*

**Step 2 — Basis of thermometry [2 marks]**
A thermometer (system B) is placed in thermal contact with the system whose temperature is to be measured (system A). When thermal equilibrium is reached, the thermometer reading equals the temperature of A. The Zeroth Law guarantees that two systems at the same temperature reading are in thermal equilibrium — making thermometry a valid, transitive measurement.

**Step 3 — Celsius conversion [2 marks]**

$$t(^{\circ}\text{C}) = T(K) - 273.15 = 1500 - 273.15 = 1226.85 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 1226.85 \,^{\circ}\text{C}}$$

**Step 4 — Principle of measurement [1 mark]**
**Principle:** Thermal equilibrium between the thermometer bulb and the furnace. The thermometer measures temperature by virtue of the **Zeroth Law's transitivity** — the bulb and the furnace reach the same temperature when placed in contact through a diathermic wall.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q B):**
> - **Heat vs. internal energy confusion (2 marks):** A frequent answer reads "the gas *contains* heat" — strictly wrong. Heat and work are *transferred* energy, not *contained* energy.
> - **Forgetting the sign convention (1 mark):** Always specify "+ into" and "− out" for Q; "+ by" and "− on" for W. Examiners deduct a mark for ambiguous sign convention.
> - **Zeroth Law statement must be verbatim (2 marks):** A loose paraphrase may lose marks. Quote the law as the standard statement: *"thermal equilibrium with a third system."*
> - **Celsius conversion requires 273.15, not 273 (1 mark):** KTU examiners have been known to deduct half a mark for using 273.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read before entering the exam hall.**

### Core Definitions
- **Quasi-static process** → System in equilibrium at every instant; properties uniform and defined.
- **Reversible process** → Quasi-static **AND** non-dissipative (no friction, no unrestrained expansion).
- **Heat (Q)** → Energy transfer due to *temperature difference*. **Path function**. In transit.
- **Work (W)** → Energy transfer due to *force × displacement*. **Path function**. In transit.
- **Displacement work** → $\delta W = P \, dV$; $W = \int P \, dV$ for quasi-static.
- **Zeroth Law** → Transitivity of thermal equilibrium ⇒ concept of temperature.
- **Thermal equilibrium** → No net heat flow; equal temperatures.

### Key Formulae to Memorize
- $\delta W = P \, dV$ (displacement work)
- $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}$ (polytropic, $n \neq 1$)
- $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(V_2/V_1)$ (isothermal, ideal gas)
- $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$ (reversible adiabatic)
- $T(K) = t(^{\circ}C) + 273.15$ (temperature conversion)
- $PV = mRT$ (ideal gas law)

### Common Pitfalls
- ❌ Quasi-static ≠ Reversible (always check for dissipation).
- ❌ Heat is **not** stored in a body (only internal energy is).
- ❌ Work and heat are **path functions**, not state functions.
- ❌ Do not use $P_{ext}$ in work formula when assuming quasi-static — use system pressure $P$.
- ❌ Isothermal work formula is a *limit* of polytropic work, not a direct substitution.
- ❌ For $n = 1$, the polytropic formula **blows up** — use the logarithmic form instead.

### Marks Distribution Tip
- **3-mark questions** test definitions, statements, and the "is X the same as Y" conceptual traps.
- **14-mark questions** almost always combine a **derivation** (7 marks) with a **numerical application** (7 marks).
- Always draw the **P-V diagram** in any displacement-work problem — it justifies the area-under-curve interpretation and earns 1–2 easy marks.

### Real-World Engineering Connections
- **Otto / Diesel / Brayton / Rankine cycles** are all modeled as quasi-static for cycle analysis.
- **Thermocouples and RTDs** are practical thermometers operating on the Zeroth Law.
- **Internal combustion engines** — non-quasi-static combustion, so cycles are *approximated* as quasi-static for tractability.
- **Nozzle/Throttle** processes (Rankine cycle) are non-quasi-static — they cannot be plotted on a P-V diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Scales (Description Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Temperature Scales (Description Only) # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Temperature? In the formal language of **KTU 2024 Scheme thermodynamics**, **temperature** is defined as the thermodynamic property that determines the direction of spontaneous heat flow between two bodies placed in thermal contact. Mathematically, it is a scalar state function whose equality is the necessary and sufficient condition for thermal equilibrium (Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics). From a microscopic viewpoint, temperature is directly proportional to the **average translational kinetic energy** of the molecules of a substance: $$\bar{KE}_{trans} = \frac{3}{2} k_B T$$ where $k_B = \textbf{1.380649} \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$ is the Boltzmann constant (exact, post-2019 SI definition). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - Module 1):** > A *temperature scale* is a standardized convention for assigning a unique numerical value to every measurable temperature. A valid scale must be **linear**, **reproducible**, and anchored to **fixed physical reference points**. ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy 🌍 Imagine you and a friend from Germany are describing how hot the weather is. You say *"It's 95 outside"* — your German friend is freezing because they think you mean 95 °C (which would be boiling!). The problem is not the heat — it is the **unit/language** used. Temperature scales are exactly like this: they are different "languages" for describing the same physical phenomenon — how hot or cold something is. Just as we have metric vs. imperial units for length (metres vs. feet), we have multiple scales for temperature: - **Empirical Scales** (based on observable physical properties like expansion of mercury): Celsius, Fahrenheit, Réaumur. - **Absolute / Thermodynamic Scales** (independent of any working substance): Kelvin, Rankine. > [!NOTE] > **Zeroth Law Connection:** Before two scales can be compared, we need a reference. The Zeroth Law guarantees that if body A is in equilibrium with body B, and body B with body C, then A and C are also in equilibrium — this permits the construction of thermometers and scales. ## 1.3 Fundamental Terminology | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Fixed Point** | A reproducible, invariant physical temperature used as a reference (e.g., ice point, steam point). | | **Ice Point** | Temperature at which pure ice melts at standard atmospheric pressure (**1 atm = 101.325 kPa**). | | **Steam Point** | Temperature at which pure water boils at standard atmospheric pressure. | | **Fundamental Interval** | The numerical difference between steam point and ice point on a given scale. | | **Absolute Zero** | Theoretically the lowest attainable temperature (**0 K = −273.15 °C**); point of zero thermal energy. | # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Major Temperature Scales — A Detailed Description ### (A) The Celsius Scale (°C) — Anders Celsius, 1742 - Originally defined with **0 °C** as the steam point and **100 °C** as the ice point (inverted!); later reversed to the modern convention. - Ice point = **0 °C**; Steam point = **100 °C**; Fundamental interval = **100 degrees**. - Adopted as part of the SI system; widely used worldwide for everyday and scientific measurement (except in the United States). ### (B) The Fahrenheit Scale (°F) — Daniel Gabriel Fahrenheit, 1714 - Originally based on three reference points: a mixture of ice, water, and ammonium chloride (0 °F), the freezing point of pure water (32 °F), and the average human body temperature (~96 °F originally, later corrected). - Ice point = **32 °F**; Steam point = **212 °F**; Fundamental interval = **180 degrees**. - Still widely used in the United States, Bahamas, Cayman Islands, and Liberia for weather, cooking, and daily life. ### (C) The Kelvin Scale (K) — Lord Kelvin (William Thomson), 1848 - The **thermodynamic (absolute) scale**, independent of any working substance. - Defined such that the **triple point of water** (where ice, liquid water, and water vapour coexist) is exactly **273.16 K**. - Ice point = **273.15 K**; Steam point = **373.15 K**. - **Unit symbol "K" without the degree sign** — this is a deliberate SI convention. - Used universally in scientific and engineering calculations (especially the **Ideal Gas Law** $PV = nRT$). ### (D) The Rankine Scale (°R) — William Rankine, 1859 - The **absolute English/FPS scale** — what Kelvin is to Celsius, Rankine is to Fahrenheit. - Ice point = **491.67 °R**; Steam point = **671.67 °R**. - Used primarily in **US engineering thermodynamics** (older textbooks and certain aerospace/power-industry standards). - Note: Rankine uses the degree symbol (°R), unlike Kelvin. ### (E) The Réaumur Scale (°Ré) — René Réaumur, 1730 - Historical scale; ice point = **0 °Ré**, steam point = **80 °Ré**. - Used historically in Europe for sugar and syrup industries; now obsolete. - Mentioned here for academic completeness — KTU may include it in Part A short-answer questions. ## 2.2 Reference Points — Master Comparison Below is a **high-yield KTU 2024 reference table** that consolidates all five scales at the critical reference points. **Use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve the markdown table.** | Phenomenon | Celsius (°C) | Fahrenheit (°F) | Kelvin (K) | Rankine (°R) | Réaumur (°Ré) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Absolute Zero** | $\boldsymbol{-273.15}$ | $\boldsymbol{-459.67}$ | $\boldsymbol{0}$ | $\boldsymbol{0}$ | $\boldsymbol{-218.52}$ | | Ice Point (1 atm) | $\boldsymbol{0}$ | $\boldsymbol{32}$ | $\boldsymbol{273.15}$ | $\boldsymbol{491.67}$ | $\boldsymbol{0}$ | | Triple Point of Water | $\boldsymbol{0.01}$ | $\boldsymbol{32.018}$ | $\boldsymbol{273.16}$ | $\boldsymbol{491.688}$ | $\boldsymbol{0.008}$ | | Steam Point (1 atm) | $\boldsymbol{100}$ | $\boldsymbol{212}$ | $\boldsymbol{373.15}$ | $\boldsymbol{671.67}$ | $\boldsymbol{80}$ | | Fundamental Interval | $\boldsymbol{100}$ | $\boldsymbol{180}$ | $\boldsymbol{100}$ | $\boldsymbol{180}$ | $\boldsymbol{80}$ | ## 2.3 The High-Yield Formula Sheet 🧮 > [!NOTE] > **Memorization Tip for KTU Exams:** Master conversions between **Celsius ↔ Kelvin**, **Fahrenheit ↔ Rankine**, and the **cross-conversions Celsius ↔ Fahrenheit**. Every other pair is derived from these three. ### Primary Conversion Formulas $$ \begin{aligned} T(\text{K}) &= T(^\circ \text{C}) + 273.15 \\[4pt] T(^\circ \text{F}) &= \tfrac{9}{5}\, T(^\circ \text{C}) + 32 \\[4pt] T(^\circ \text{R}) &= T(^\circ \text{F}) + 459.67 \\[4pt] T(^\circ \text{R}) &= \tfrac{9}{5}\, T(\text{K}) \end{aligned} $$ ### Inverse / Reverse Conversions $$ \begin{aligned} T(^\circ \text{C}) &= T(\text{K}) - 273.15 \\[4pt] T(^\circ \text{C}) &= \tfrac{5}{9}\bigl[T(^\circ \text{F}) - 32\bigr] \\[4pt] T(^\circ \text{F}) &= T(^\circ \text{R}) - 459.67 \\[4pt] T(\text{K}) &= \tfrac{5}{9}\bigl[T(^\circ \text{F}) - 32\bigr] + 273.15 \end{aligned} $$ ### General Linear Conversion (Any two scales) $$ \frac{T_A - T_{A,\text{ice}}}{T_{A,\text{steam}} - T_{A,\text{ice}}} \;=\; \frac{T_B - T_{B,\text{ice}}}{T_{B,\text{steam}} - T_{B,\text{ice}}} $$ This is the **master relationship** used by examiners to test whether students *understand* why the formulas look the way they do — and is the basis for KTU Part B derivations. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where Each Scale Shines ⚙️ - **Kelvin (K):** Universal for thermodynamics, ideal-gas equations, Carnot efficiency $\eta = 1 - T_C/T_H$ (both $T_C$ and $T_H$ must be in K!). - **Celsius (°C):** HVAC, refrigeration engineering, climate science, weather forecasting. - **Fahrenheit (°F):** US residential/commercial applications, oven controls, weather reporting. - **Rankine (°R):** Legacy US steam-power calculations, aerospace propulsion (some older texts). - **Réaumur (°Ré):** Historical/industrial alcoholometry and sugar refining (now largely obsolete). # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: General Linear Relation Between Any Two Scales **Why this matters:** This derivation is the *most frequently asked* Part B sub-question on Temperature Scales in KTU University Exams. Mastering it guarantees full marks on conversion-formula problems. ### Step 1 — Set up the two scales Consider two linear temperature scales, **Scale A** and **Scale B**, each anchored to the same two physical fixed points (ice and steam): - Scale A: ice point = $A_i$, steam point = $A_s$, fundamental interval = $A_s - A_i$ - Scale B: ice point = $B_i$, steam point = $B_s$, fundamental interval = $B_s - B_i$ ### Step 2 — Define a "fraction of fundamental interval" function For any temperature $T_A$ on Scale A, the fraction of the way from ice to steam is: $$ x \;=\; \frac{T_A - A_i}{A_s - A_i} $$ For the corresponding temperature $T_B$ on Scale B: $$ x \;=\; \frac{T_B - B_i}{B_s - B_i} $$ ### Step 3 — Equate the fractions (since both measure the SAME "hotness") $$ \frac{T_A - A_i}{A_s - A_i} \;=\; \frac{T_B - B_i}{B_s - B_i} $$ This is the **master equation** linking any two linear scales. The rest of the conversions below are algebraic rearrangements. ### Step 4 — Solve for $T_B$ $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{T_B - B_i}{B_s - B_i} &= \frac{T_A - A_i}{A_s - A_i} \\[6pt] T_B - B_i &= (B_s - B_i) \cdot \frac{T_A - A_i}{A_s - A_i} \\[6pt] T_B &= B_i + (B_s - B_i) \cdot \frac{T_A - A_i}{A_s - A_i} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **universal conversion formula**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Celsius to Fahrenheit Conversion Apply the master equation with: - Scale A = Celsius: $A_i = 0$, $A_s = 100$ - Scale B = Fahrenheit: $B_i = 32$, $B_s = 212$ $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{T_F - 32}{212 - 32} &= \frac{T_C - 0}{100 - 0} \\[6pt] \frac{T_F - 32}{180} &= \frac{T_C}{100} \\[6pt] T_F - 32 &= 180 \cdot \frac{T_C}{100} = \tfrac{9}{5} \, T_C \\[6pt] \boxed{\,T_F \;=\; \tfrac{9}{5}\, T_C + 32\,} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification:** $T_C = 100$ °C → $T_F = (9/5)(100) + 32 = 180 + 32 = 212$ °F ✓ (steam point) --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Kelvin ↔ Celsius Apply the master equation with: - Scale A = Celsius: $A_i = 0$, $A_s = 100$ - Scale B = Kelvin: $B_i = 273.15$, $B_s = 373.15$ $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{T_K - 273.15}{373.15 - 273.15} &= \frac{T_C - 0}{100 - 0} \\[6pt] \frac{T_K - 273.15}{100} &= \frac{T_C}{100} \\[6pt] T_K - 273.15 &= T_C \\[6pt] \boxed{\,T_K \;=\; T_C + 273.15\,} \end{aligned} $$ **Why this works:** The Celsius and Kelvin scales have **identical fundamental intervals** (100 units each), so they are simply offset by the location of absolute zero. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Rankine ↔ Fahrenheit Analogous to Kelvin–Celsius, since Rankine and Fahrenheit have identical fundamental intervals (180 units): $$ T_R \;=\; T_F + 459.67 $$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation: Kelvin ↔ Rankine (cross-absolute) Combining the Celsius–Fahrenheit link with both absolute offsets: $$ \begin{aligned} T_R &= T_F + 459.67 \\[4pt] &= \left(\tfrac{9}{5}\, T_C + 32\right) + 459.67 \\[4pt] &= \tfrac{9}{5}\, T_C + 491.67 \\[4pt] &= \tfrac{9}{5}\,(T_C + 273.15) \quad \text{(since } 491.67 = (9/5)(273.15)\text{)} \\[4pt] \boxed{\,T_R \;=\; \tfrac{9}{5}\, T_K\,} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Part B Style) > **Problem:** The temperature of exhaust gas leaving a furnace is measured as **540 °R**. Express this in **(i) °F**, **(ii) K**, **(iii) °C**. ### (i) Conversion to °F $$ T_F = T_R - 459.67 = 540 - 459.67 = 80.33 \,^\circ \text{F} $$ ### (ii) Conversion to K $$ T_K = \tfrac{5}{9}\, T_R = \tfrac{5}{9} \times 540 = 300 \, \text{K} $$ ### (iii) Conversion to °C $$ T_C = T_K - 273.15 = 300 - 273.15 = 26.85 \,^\circ \text{C} $$ ### Cross-Verification Convert °F back to °C directly: $$ T_C = \tfrac{5}{9}(T_F - 32) = \tfrac{5}{9}(80.33 - 32) = \tfrac{5}{9}(48.33) = 26.85 \,^\circ \text{C} \;\; \checkmark $$ # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Taxonomy of Temperature Scales The Mermaid diagram below maps the **empirical vs. absolute** classification and the inter-scale relationships that KTU examiners expect students to sketch in Part B theory answers. ```mermaid graph TD A["TEMPERATURE SCALES"]:::root --> B["EMPIRICAL SCALES"]:::emp A --> C["ABSOLUTE SCALES"]:::abs B --> B1["Celsius deg C"]:::emp B --> B2["Fahrenheit deg F"]:::emp B --> B3["Reaumur deg Re"]:::emp C --> C1["Kelvin K"]:::abs C --> C2["Rankine deg R"]:::abs B1 -- "T_K equals T_C plus 273.15" --> C1 B2 -- "T_R equals T_F plus 459.67" --> C2 B1 -- "T_F equals 9 over 5 times T_C plus 32" --> B2 C1 -- "T_R equals 9 over 5 times T_K" --> C2 B3 -- "T_Re equals 4 over 5 times T_C" --> B1 classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef emp fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#1f2937 classDef abs fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb,color:#1f2937 ``` ## 4.2 Reference Point Anchoring Diagram The following Mermaid block depicts how each scale is anchored to the same two physical fixed points but assigns different numerical values. ```mermaid graph LR P1["ABSOLUTE ZERO
0 K or -273.15 deg C"]:::zero --> P2["ICE POINT
273.15 K or 0 deg C"]:::ice P2 --> P3["TRIPLE POINT
273.16 K or 0.01 deg C"]:::triple P3 --> P4["STEAM POINT
373.15 K or 100 deg C"]:::steam P2 -. "Same physics" .-> P2 P4 -. "Same physics" .-> P4 classDef zero fill:#0f172a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef ice fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1f2937 classDef triple fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#059669,color:#1f2937 classDef steam fill:#fecaca,stroke:#dc2626,color:#1f2937 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Conversion Engine The block diagram below illustrates the *functional architecture* one would use to build a digital temperature-conversion utility — useful as an analogy for understanding how scale transformations are computed. ```mermaid graph TD IN["INPUT TEMPERATURE T_X
plus SCALE IDENTIFIER"]:::in --> DET["SCALE DETECTION
IDENTIFIES SOURCE SCALE"]:::detect DET --> NORM["NORMALIZATION TO KELVIN
Universal Intermediate Form"]:::norm NORM --> SEL["TARGET SCALE SELECTION
User Choice"]:::sel SEL --> CONV["FINAL CONVERSION
Apply Linear Mapping"]:::conv CONV --> OUT["DISPLAY RESULT T_Y"]:::out DET --> ERR["ERROR HANDLER
Unknown Scale Flag"]:::err classDef in fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9 classDef detect fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706 classDef norm fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef sel fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#db2777 classDef conv fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#059669 classDef out fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626 classDef err fill:#1f2937,stroke:#1f2937,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This three-stage architecture (Detect → Normalize to Kelvin → Convert to Target) is exactly how every modern PLC, BMS, and SCADA system handles mixed-unit temperature data. **Kelvin is the universal intermediary** because it is absolute and SI-compliant. # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Define a temperature scale. List any four temperature scales along with their fundamental intervals.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A *temperature scale* is a standardized numerical system for measuring the hotness or coldness of a body, anchored to reproducible fixed physical points and possessing a defined fundamental interval (the numerical difference between the ice point and steam point). Four temperature scales and their fundamental intervals: 1. **Celsius (°C):** Fundamental interval = 100 degrees (0 °C to 100 °C). 2. **Fahrenheit (°F):** Fundamental interval = 180 degrees (32 °F to 212 °F). 3. **Kelvin (K):** Fundamental interval = 100 K (273.15 K to 373.15 K) — absolute scale. 4. **Rankine (°R):** Fundamental interval = 180 °R (491.67 °R to 671.67 °R) — absolute scale. *(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for four scales with intervals = 3 marks)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is absolute zero? Express it in K, °C, °F, and °R.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Absolute zero** is the theoretical lowest possible temperature, at which the kinetic energy of atoms and molecules approaches its minimum value (quantum-mechanical ground state). It is the zero point of the thermodynamic (Kelvin and Rankine) temperature scales. $$ \begin{aligned} 0 \text{ K} &= -273.15 \,^\circ \text{C} \\ &= -459.67 \,^\circ \text{F} \\ &= 0 \,^\circ \text{R} \end{aligned} $$ *(1 mark for concept + 0.5 each for the four values = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice Format ### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified] #### **Part (a)** — Explain the various temperature scales used in engineering, with their ice points, steam points, and fundamental intervals. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** A temperature scale is defined by choosing two fixed reference points and assigning numerical values to them, then dividing the interval between them into equal units. **1. Celsius Scale (Anders Celsius, 1742)** - Ice point = 0 °C; Steam point = 100 °C; Fundamental interval = **100 degrees**. - SI-adopted scale for scientific use; inverted sign convention (°) but not a degree in Kelvin. **2. Fahrenheit Scale (D. G. Fahrenheit, 1714)** - Ice point = 32 °F; Steam point = 212 °F; Fundamental interval = **180 degrees**. - Widely used in the USA for daily life and meteorology. **3. Kelvin Scale (Lord Kelvin, 1848)** - Ice point = 273.15 K; Steam point = 373.15 K; Fundamental interval = **100 K**. - **Thermodynamic / absolute** scale; independent of any working substance. - Unit is the kelvin (no degree sign); defined via the triple point of water (273.16 K exactly). **4. Rankine Scale (William Rankine, 1859)** - Ice point = 491.67 °R; Steam point = 671.67 °R; Fundamental interval = **180 °R**. - The absolute FPS counterpart of Fahrenheit; used in legacy US engineering. **5. Réaumur Scale (R. A. F. Réaumur, 1730)** *(for completeness)* - Ice point = 0 °Ré; Steam point = 80 °Ré; Fundamental interval = **80 degrees**. - Historical scale; now obsolete but academically important. **Conclusion:** Modern engineering thermodynamics universally prefers the **Kelvin scale** because of its absolute nature and absence of negative values, simplifying all gas-law and efficiency calculations. *(7 marks: 1 each per scale's full description + 2 marks for the comparative conclusion)* --- #### **Part (b)** — Derive the conversion formula between the Celsius and Fahrenheit scales using the general linear interpolation principle. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State the Master Principle:** For any two linear temperature scales anchored at the same two physical fixed points (ice and steam), the ratio of the "fraction of fundamental interval traversed" must be equal: $$ \frac{T_C - T_{C,\text{ice}}}{T_{C,\text{steam}} - T_{C,\text{ice}}} \;=\; \frac{T_F - T_{F,\text{ice}}}{T_{F,\text{steam}} - T_{F,\text{ice}}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Reference Values:** Celsius: $T_{C,\text{ice}} = 0$, $T_{C,\text{steam}} = 100$. Fahrenheit: $T_{F,\text{ice}} = 32$, $T_{F,\text{steam}} = 212$. $$ \frac{T_C - 0}{100 - 0} \;=\; \frac{T_F - 32}{212 - 32} $$ **Step 3 — Simplify the Equation:** $$ \frac{T_C}{100} \;=\; \frac{T_F - 32}{180} $$ **Step 4 — Cross-Multiply and Solve for $T_F$:** $$ \begin{aligned} 180 \cdot T_C &= 100 \cdot (T_F - 32) \\[4pt] 1.8 \, T_C &= T_F - 32 \\[4pt] \boxed{\,T_F \;=\; \tfrac{9}{5}\, T_C + 32\,} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Verification:** At $T_C = 100$ °C: $T_F = (9/5)(100) + 32 = 180 + 32 = 212$ °F ✓ At $T_C = 0$ °C: $T_F = 0 + 32 = 32$ °F ✓ **Step 6 — Inverse Formula (for full marks):** $$ \boxed{\,T_C \;=\; \tfrac{5}{9}\bigl(T_F - 32\bigr)\,} $$ *(7 marks: 1 for principle + 1 for substitution + 2 for algebraic manipulation + 1 for final formula + 1 for verification + 1 for inverse)* --- ### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified] #### **Part (a)** — Describe the Kelvin and Rankine temperature scales. Why are they called "absolute" scales? *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Kelvin Scale:** - Proposed by William Thomson (Lord Kelvin) in 1848. - It is the **fundamental thermodynamic temperature scale** in the SI system. - The triple point of pure water is fixed at exactly **273.16 K**. - Therefore, ice point ≈ **273.15 K** and steam point ≈ **373.15 K**. - The unit is written as "K" (no degree sign). - The scale is **independent of the properties of any particular thermometric substance**. **Rankine Scale:** - Proposed by William Rankine in 1859. - The **absolute English (FPS) counterpart** of the Kelvin scale. - Ice point = **491.67 °R**, steam point = **671.67 °R**. - It preserves the Fahrenheit degree size, but the zero is shifted to absolute zero. **Why They Are Called "Absolute":** Both Kelvin and Rankine are called *absolute* temperature scales because: 1. Their **zero point coincides with the theoretical absolute zero** — the temperature at which all molecular motion (classical) ceases. 2. They contain **no negative values** — every physically meaningful temperature is positive. 3. They are based on the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (Carnot cycle efficiency $\eta = 1 - T_C/T_H$), not on the expansion of any specific fluid (mercury, alcohol, etc.). 4. They make the ideal gas law $PV = mRT$ universally valid **without any offset constant**. **Comparison Table:** $$ \begin{aligned} T(\text{K}) &= T(^\circ \text{C}) + 273.15 \\ T(^\circ \text{R}) &= T(^\circ \text{F}) + 459.67 \\ T(^\circ \text{R}) &= \tfrac{9}{5}\, T(\text{K}) \end{aligned} $$ *(7 marks: 2 for Kelvin description + 2 for Rankine description + 3 for "absolute" justification)* --- #### **Part (b)** — A thermocouple in a steam pipe reads a temperature of **140 °F**. Express this temperature in **(i)** °C, **(ii) K, **(iii) °R. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Given:** $T_F = 140 \,^\circ \text{F}$ **(i) Convert to °C:** $$ T_C = \tfrac{5}{9}(T_F - 32) = \tfrac{5}{9}(140 - 32) = \tfrac{5}{9}(108) $$ $$ \boxed{\,T_C = 60 \,^\circ \text{C}\,} $$ **(ii) Convert to K:** $$ T_K = T_C + 273.15 = 60 + 273.15 $$ $$ \boxed{\,T_K = 333.15 \, \text{K}\,} $$ **(iii) Convert to °R (Method A — direct):** $$ T_R = T_F + 459.67 = 140 + 459.67 $$ $$ \boxed{\,T_R = 599.67 \,^\circ \text{R}\,} $$ **(iii) Convert to °R (Method B — verification via Kelvin):** $$ T_R = \tfrac{9}{5}\, T_K = \tfrac{9}{5}(333.15) = 1.8 \times 333.15 $$ $$ T_R = 599.67 \,^\circ \text{R} \;\; \checkmark $$ **Result Summary Table:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | $T_F$ | $\boldsymbol{140 \,^\circ \text{F}}$ (given) | | $T_C$ | $\boldsymbol{60 \,^\circ \text{C}}$ | | $T_K$ | $\boldsymbol{333.15 \, \text{K}}$ | | $T_R$ | $\boldsymbol{599.67 \,^\circ \text{R}}$ | *(7 marks: 2 marks for (i) + 2 marks for (ii) + 3 marks for (iii) with both methods and verification)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting the +273.15 offset:** Students frequently write $T_K = T_C$ (no offset). Always remember Kelvin and Celsius differ ONLY by a constant offset of 273.15. > > 2. **Sign error on inverse conversion:** When going °F → °C, students sometimes compute $\frac{5}{9} \cdot T_F$ instead of $\frac{5}{9}(T_F - 32)$. **Always subtract 32 FIRST, then multiply.** > > 3. **Mixing 273 vs 273.15:** For 3-mark definitions, 273 is acceptable. For 14-mark derivations and numericals, **use 273.15**. KTU examiners penalize truncation to 273 in numerical answers. > > 4. **Using ° symbol with Kelvin:** Writing "°K" instead of "K" is a **mark-deducting formatting error** — Kelvin has no degree sign. > > 5. **Rankine via Kelvin — wrong factor:** Students sometimes write $T_R = \frac{5}{9} T_K$ (Celsius-style factor). Correct is $T_R = \frac{9}{5} T_K$. Always remember Rankine:Fahrenheit :: Kelvin: **but inverted ratio**. > > 6. **Unit omission:** Writing "T = 300" without "K" or "°C" loses a mark. **Always quote the unit explicitly.** --- ## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)** - ✅ **Temperature** = property governing direction of heat flow (Zeroth Law). - ✅ A temperature scale needs **two fixed points** (ice & steam at 1 atm) and a **fundamental interval**. - ✅ **Five scales to know:** Celsius (°C), Fahrenheit (°F), Kelvin (K), Rankine (°R), Réaumur (°Ré). - ✅ **Absolute scales** (Kelvin, Rankine) have their zero at **absolute zero** and contain no negative values. - ✅ **Celsius & Kelvin** share the same fundamental interval (100); differ only by the offset **273.15**. - ✅ **Fahrenheit & Rankine** share the same fundamental interval (180); differ only by the offset **459.67**. - ✅ **Master conversion formula:** $$ \frac{T_A - A_i}{A_s - A_i} = \frac{T_B - B_i}{B_s - B_i} $$ - ✅ **Three direct conversion recipes:** $$ T_K = T_C + 273.15, \quad T_F = \tfrac{9}{5} T_C + 32, \quad T_R = \tfrac{9}{5} T_K $$ - ✅ **Absolute zero values:** $0 \text{ K} = -273.15 \,^\circ \text{C} = -459.67 \,^\circ \text{F} = 0 \,^\circ \text{R}$. - ✅ **Triple point of water** = 273.16 K (exact, SI definition). - ✅ **Universal architecture for conversions:** Detect → Normalize to Kelvin → Convert to target scale. - ✅ **Kelvin is ALWAYS used in:** ideal gas law, Carnot efficiency, entropy calculations, all thermodynamic ratios. --- *End of Module 1 — Temperature Scales (Description Only) Notes.* *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme — PCMUT403 — Thermodynamics and Thermal Systems.*

topics.title: Various Thermometers. First Law of Thermodynamics - Concept of Energy and Various Forms of Energy - Internal Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Various Thermometers. First Law of Thermodynamics - Concept of Energy and Various Forms of Energy - Internal Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Temperature and Thermometers — The Foundation

**Temperature** is a fundamental thermodynamic property that quantitatively represents the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. It is one of the seven base SI quantities and is measured using the unit **Kelvin (K)** in thermodynamic calculations, though **Celsius (°C)** and **Fahrenheit (°F)** are also used in practical applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Temperature is a scalar, intensive thermodynamic property. It is a *point function* and *state function* — meaning it depends only on the state of the system, not on the path taken to reach that state.

A **Thermometer** is a device used to measure the temperature of a body or system. The principle of measurement relies on the **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics**, which states that if two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Money Exchange" of Heat:**
> Think of temperature as the "price level" of thermal energy in a system, while heat (Q) is like the "currency" that flows. Just as money flows from a high-price market to a low-price one until prices equalize, heat flows from a high-temperature body to a low-temperature body until thermal equilibrium is reached. A thermometer is like a "price scanner" that reads the current thermal price level.

## 1.2 Classification of Thermometers

Thermometers are broadly classified into two categories based on their operating principle:

### A. Primary (Primary Standards / Absolute) Thermometers
These establish temperature by direct measurement of fundamental physical quantities, requiring no calibration against a reference. They realize the thermodynamic temperature scale directly.

| Type | Working Principle | Range |
|------|-------------------|-------|
| Gas Thermometer (Constant Volume) | Measures pressure variation of a fixed gas mass | **73 K to 1235 K** |
| Gas Thermometer (Constant Pressure) | Measures volume variation of a gas at constant P | **73 K to 1235 K** |
| Acoustic Thermometer | Uses speed of sound in a gas | Wide range |

### B. Secondary (Practical) Thermometers
These must be calibrated against a primary standard. They use changes in physical properties of materials with temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Liquid-in-Glass Thermometer** uses the thermal expansion of a liquid (mercury or alcohol) confined in a glass capillary. The apparent expansion ∆L is measured against a calibrated scale. Mercury freezes at **−38.83°C** and boils at **356.7°C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)** exploits the principle that the electrical resistance of a pure metal increases linearly with temperature. The most common RTD is the **Platinum Resistance Thermometer (PRT)** with the Callendar-Van Dusen equation as its basis. Standard reference resistance is **R₀ = 100 Ω at 0°C** (PT100).

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermocouple** is based on the **Seebeck Effect** — when two dissimilar metals are joined at two junctions maintained at different temperatures, an electromotive force (EMF) is generated. Types include Type K (Chromel-Alumel), Type J (Iron-Constantan), and Type T (Copper-Constantan).

## 1.3 The First Law of Thermodynamics — Concept of Energy

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** is essentially the law of **Conservation of Energy** applied to thermodynamic systems. It states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another.

### Mathematical Statement

For a closed system undergoing a cyclic process, the First Law is expressed as:

$$\oint (\delta Q - \delta W) = 0$$

This implies that $(\delta Q - \delta W)$ is an exact differential of a property called **Energy (E)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Definition — Energy:**
> Energy is the capacity of a system to produce an effect or do work. It is an **extensive property** of a system, measured in **Joules (J)** in SI units. Energy exists in many forms and is conserved in all processes (per the First Law).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Bank Account" of Physics:**
> Think of energy as money in a bank account. A thermodynamic system is like your account, and the various forms of energy (kinetic, potential, internal) are different currencies (USD, EUR, JPY) stored in that account. The First Law says: *the total balance is always conserved* — money may shift between USD and EUR, but the total worth remains the same. **Heat (Q)** is money deposited, **Work (W)** is money withdrawn, and the change in account balance is the change in total energy (∆E).

## 1.4 Various Forms of Energy

Energy manifests in numerous forms in physical systems. For thermodynamic analysis, the most relevant are:

### A. Macroscopic Forms of Energy
These are associated with the system as a whole and depend on external references.

1. **Kinetic Energy (KE)** — Energy due to motion of the system relative to a reference frame.

   $$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

2. **Potential Energy (PE)** — Energy due to position in a force field (usually gravitational).

   $$PE = mgz$$

### B. Microscopic Forms of Energy
These are associated with the molecular and atomic structure of matter.

1. **Internal Energy (U)** — The sum of all microscopic energies (translational, rotational, vibrational, electronic, and nuclear) of the molecules within a system.
2. **Sensible Energy** — The portion of internal energy associated with kinetic energy of molecules (temperature-dependent).
3. **Latent Energy** — Energy associated with phase change (released or absorbed during transitions).
4. **Chemical Energy** — Energy stored in molecular bonds.
5. **Nuclear Energy** — Energy released from nuclear reactions (fission/fusion).
6. **Electromagnetic Radiation** — Energy transported by photons.

## 1.5 Internal Energy — The Heart of Thermodynamics

**Internal Energy (U)** is the sum of all microscopic energies of all molecules in a system. It is a **thermodynamic property**, an **extensive property**, and a **point function**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (P.K. Nag, Çengel & Boles):** 
> "Internal energy is defined as the sum of all the microscopic forms of energy of a system. It is related to the molecular structure and the degree of molecular activity."

### Microscopic Energy Components
- **Translational Kinetic Energy** — Linear motion of molecules
- **Rotational Kinetic Energy** — Rotational motion of molecules
- **Vibrational Kinetic & Potential Energy** — Vibrational motion of atoms in molecules
- **Intermolecular Potential Energy** — Forces between molecules
- **Electronic Energy** — Energy of electrons in atoms
- **Nuclear Energy** — Energy in atomic nuclei
- **Chemical Bond Energy** — Energy stored in chemical bonds

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** In thermodynamics, we are usually NOT concerned with the absolute value of internal energy, only with its **change (∆U)**. This is because internal energy is a relative property, similar to potential energy in mechanics.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Crowded Train" of Molecules:**
> Imagine a train car packed with people (molecules). The internal energy of the system is the *total microscopic activity* inside the car — the movement of people walking (translational KE), the spinning of people in place (rotational KE), the bouncing of people (vibrational energy), and the people pushing against each other (intermolecular PE). The macroscopic KE of the train (the entire car moving) is NOT part of internal energy — that is the system's kinetic energy. **Internal energy is everything happening INSIDE the molecules, not the bulk motion.**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Various Thermometers

### 1. Liquid-in-Glass Thermometer

**Working Principle:** Utilizes the apparent expansion of a thermometric fluid (mercury, alcohol, or toluene) within a glass stem.

**Key Parameters:**
- Freezing point of mercury: **−38.83°C (234.32 K)**
- Boiling point of mercury: **356.7°C (629.85 K)**
- For temperatures below −38°C, ethyl alcohol is used (freezes at −114°C)

**Limiting Conditions:**
- Above **600°C**: Inert gas (nitrogen) pressurization is used to prevent mercury boiling
- The expansion relationship is approximately **linear**:

$$V = V_0 (1 + \beta \Delta T)$$

where $\beta$ is the volumetric expansion coefficient of the liquid.

### 2. Gas Thermometer (Constant Volume)

**Working Principle:** A fixed mass of gas is maintained at constant volume. The pressure of the gas is measured as temperature changes.

**Ideal Gas Equation Basis:**

$$PV = mRT \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{PV}{mR}$$

At constant V and m:

$$T \propto P \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{P_1}{P_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The gas thermometer is a **primary standard** because thermodynamic temperature can be derived directly from the measurable gas pressure using the ideal gas law. The constant-volume gas thermometer is used to define the International Temperature Scale above the triple point of water (273.16 K).

### 3. Resistance Thermometer (RTD)

**Working Principle:** The electrical resistance of a conductor changes with temperature.

**Platinum RTD (PT100) — Callendar-Van Dusen Equation:**

For $0°C \leq T \leq 850°C$:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + AT + BT^2 - 100\,C\,T^3 + CT^4\right]$$

For $-200°C \leq T \leq 0°C$:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + AT + BT^2 + CT^3 (T - 100)\right]$$

Standard constants: $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\,°C^{-1}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\,°C^{-2}$, $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}\,°C^{-4}$.

**Advantages:** High accuracy, wide range (−200°C to 850°C), excellent stability.

### 4. Thermocouple

**Working Principle:** Based on the **Seebeck Effect** (1821). When two dissimilar metals A and B are joined to form a closed circuit, and the two junctions are at different temperatures, an EMF is generated.

**EMF-Temperature Relation:**

$$E = a(\Delta T) + b(\Delta T)^2 + c(\Delta T)^3$$

where $\Delta T = T_{hot} - T_{cold}$, and a, b, c are Seebeck coefficients.

**Common Thermocouple Types (for KTU reference):**
- **Type K (Chromel-Alumel):** Range −200°C to 1350°C, most common
- **Type J (Iron-Constantan):** Range −40°C to 750°C
- **Type T (Copper-Constantan):** Range −200°C to 350°C

### 5. Radiation Pyrometer

**Working Principle:** Measures temperature by detecting the thermal radiation emitted by a hot body. Based on **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,W/(m^2 \cdot K^4)$ is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**.

**Applications:** Used for very high temperatures (>600°C) where contact thermometers fail — furnaces, molten metals, stars.

### 6. Bimetallic Strip Thermometer

**Working Principle:** Two metals with different thermal expansion coefficients are bonded together. Temperature change causes differential expansion, resulting in mechanical deflection. Used in thermostats.

## 2.2 Concept of Energy — Thermodynamic Perspective

**Energy (E)** of a system is the sum of its **macroscopic energy** and **microscopic (internal) energy**.

$$E_{total} = E_{macro} + U$$

$$E_{total} = KE + PE + U = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgz + U$$

### The Why Behind Energy Conservation

The First Law emerges from a profound empirical observation: in nature, energy is never created or destroyed. Even in nuclear reactions where mass converts to energy (Einstein's $E = mc^2$), the **total energy-mass equivalence is conserved**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The First Law is the foundation for analyzing all energy-conversion devices: heat engines, refrigerators, compressors, turbines, nozzles, and chemical reactors. Without it, we cannot perform energy balances on engineering systems.

## 2.3 Internal Energy — Detailed Properties

### Key Characteristics of Internal Energy
- **State function:** dU is an exact differential
- **Extensive property:** Depends on mass of system
- **Path independent:** Change depends only on initial and final states
- **Related to enthalpy:** $H = U + PV$

### Change in Internal Energy for Ideal Gases

For an ideal gas, internal energy is a function of **temperature only**:

$$U = f(T) \quad \text{(Ideal Gas)}$$

$$\Delta U = m c_v \Delta T \quad \text{(Ideal Gas)}$$

where $c_v$ is the specific heat at constant volume (in **kJ/kg·K** or **kJ/kmol·K**).

### Change in Internal Energy for Real Gases & Incompressible Substances

For solids and liquids, $\Delta U$ is approximately:

$$\Delta U \approx m c_{avg} \Delta T$$

For ideal gases, $c_v$ and $c_p$ are related by **Mayer's relation**:

$$c_p - c_v = R \quad \text{(per unit mass)}$$

For constant specific heats, the relation is:

$$\Delta U = m c_v (T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{[kJ]}$$

For variable specific heats (using tables):

$$\Delta U = m (u_2 - u_1)$$

where $u$ is the specific internal energy (in kJ/kg).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these are the most-tested formulas in KTU exams for this topic.**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------|---------------|
| Internal Energy (Total) | $U = \sum (KE_{mol} + PE_{mol})$ | kJ, extensive property |
| Change in Internal Energy (Ideal Gas) | $\Delta U = m c_v \Delta T$ | $c_v$ in kJ/kg·K |
| Change in Internal Energy (Tables) | $\Delta U = m(u_2 - u_1)$ | $u$ from steam/gas tables |
| Total Energy of System | $E = KE + PE + U = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + mgz + U$ | kJ |
| Specific Internal Energy | $u = U/m$ | kJ/kg, intensive |
| Gas Thermometer (C.V.) | $T = PV/(mR)$ | $R = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K |
| Stefan-Boltzmann (Pyrometer) | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) |
| Mayer's Relation | $c_p - c_v = R$ | Ideal gas, per unit mass |
| Mayer's Relation (Molar) | $\bar{c}_p - \bar{c}_v = \bar{R}$ | $\bar{R} = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K |
| Ratio of Specific Heats | $\gamma = c_p / c_v$ | Dimensionless |
| Enthalpy-Internal Energy Relation | $H = U + PV$ | kJ, $H = U + mRT$ for ideal gas |
| PT100 Resistance | $R_T = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2]$ | $R_0 = 100$ Ω at 0°C |
| Thermocouple EMF | $E = a\Delta T + b(\Delta T)^2$ | Seebeck coefficients |
| Linear Expansion (Bimetallic) | $\Delta L = L_0 \alpha \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = linear expansion coeff. |
| First Law (Closed System) | $Q - W = \Delta E = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$ | kJ |
| First Law (Cycle) | $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$ | Net work = Net heat |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First Law of Thermodynamics for a Closed System

### Statement and Starting Point

The First Law of Thermodynamics states that during any process, the energy of the universe is conserved. For a closed system (no mass transfer), we write a general energy balance.

### Step 1: Net Energy Transfer
The net energy transfer to a closed system equals the change in the total energy of the system.

$$E_{in} - E_{out} = \Delta E_{system}$$

### Step 2: Identify Energy Transfer Modes
For a closed system, energy can be transferred by two mechanisms: **Heat (Q)** and **Work (W)**.

$$Q - W = \Delta E_{system}$$

### Step 3: Express Total Energy
The total energy of the system consists of macroscopic (kinetic and potential) and microscopic (internal) energies.

$$\Delta E = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$$

### Step 4: Substitute

$$\boxed{Q - W = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE}$$

This is the **general First Law equation for a closed system**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used in KTU (and Çengel):** Heat transfer TO the system is **positive (Q > 0)**; Heat transfer FROM the system is **negative (Q < 0)**. Work done BY the system is **positive (W > 0)**; Work done ON the system is **negative (W < 0)**.

### Step 5: For a Stationary Closed System
If the system is stationary ($\Delta KE = \Delta PE = 0$):

$$Q - W = \Delta U$$

This is the simplest form of the First Law.

### Step 6: For a Cyclic Process
A cyclic process returns the system to its initial state, so $\Delta U = 0$:

$$Q_{net} = W_{net}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Change in Internal Energy for an Ideal Gas (Joule's Law)

### Step 1: Definition of Internal Energy
Internal energy is the sum of all microscopic kinetic and potential energies:

$$U = U_{translational} + U_{rotational} + U_{vibrational} + U_{intermolecular}$$

### Step 2: Assumption for Ideal Gas
In an ideal gas, intermolecular forces are **negligible** (no intermolecular PE). Also, for monoatomic gases, only translational KE exists. For diatomic and polyatomic gases, additional rotational and vibrational modes exist.

The average kinetic energy of a molecule is proportional to absolute temperature:

$$\overline{KE}_{molecule} = \frac{3}{2} k_B T$$

where $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is the **Boltzmann constant**.

### Step 3: Total Internal Energy
For N molecules:

$$U = N \cdot \frac{3}{2} k_B T \quad \text{(monatomic ideal gas)}$$

Since $N k_B = n \bar{R}$ (where n is the number of moles, $\bar{R}$ is universal gas constant):

$$U = \frac{3}{2} n \bar{R} T$$

### Step 4: Specific Internal Energy

$$u = \frac{U}{m} = \frac{U}{nM} = \frac{3}{2} \frac{\bar{R}}{M} T = \frac{3}{2} R_{specific} T$$

where $R_{specific} = \bar{R}/M$ is the specific gas constant.

### Step 5: Change in Internal Energy

$$\Delta U = m c_v \Delta T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result (Joule's Law):** For an ideal gas, internal energy is a function of temperature only. This is **Joule's Law**, expressed as $U = U(T)$ — and therefore $\Delta U$ depends only on $\Delta T$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mayer's Relation

### Step 1: Enthalpy Definition
By definition, enthalpy is:

$$H = U + PV$$

### Step 2: For an Ideal Gas
Substitute $PV = mRT$:

$$H = U + mRT$$

### Step 3: Express in Specific Form

$$h = u + RT$$

### Step 4: Take the Differential

$$dh = du + RdT$$

### Step 5: Apply Specific Heat Definitions
At constant pressure: $dh = c_p dT$
At constant volume: $du = c_v dT$

### Step 6: Substitute

$$c_p dT = c_v dT + R dT$$

### Step 7: Final Result

$$\boxed{c_p - c_v = R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Result:** This relation is valid only for **ideal gases** with constant specific heats (or when $c_p$ and $c_v$ are evaluated at the same temperature).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Constant-Volume Gas Thermometer — Temperature Measurement

### Step 1: Starting Point
For a fixed mass of ideal gas at constant volume:

$$PV = mRT \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = \left(\frac{mR}{V}\right)T$$

Since m, R, V are constant, define the gas constant $k = mR/V$:

$$P = kT$$

### Step 2: Apply at Two Temperatures

$$P_1 = k T_1 \quad \text{and} \quad P_2 = k T_2$$

### Step 3: Form the Ratio

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

### Step 4: Define Reference (Triple Point of Water)
The triple point of water is $T_{tp} = 273.16$ K, and the corresponding pressure is $P_{tp}$. The temperature of an unknown system is:

$$\boxed{T = 273.16 \times \frac{P}{P_{tp}}}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Style)

### Example 1: Change in Internal Energy

**Problem:** A piston-cylinder device contains 2 kg of nitrogen (N₂, ideal gas, $c_v = 0.743$ kJ/kg·K) initially at 300 K. The gas is heated to 500 K. Calculate the change in internal energy.

**Step 1: Identify Given Data**
- $m = 2$ kg
- $c_v = 0.743$ kJ/kg·K
- $T_1 = 300$ K
- $T_2 = 500$ K

**Step 2: Apply the Formula**

$$\Delta U = m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$\Delta U = 2 \times 0.743 \times (500 - 300)$$

$$\Delta U = 2 \times 0.743 \times 200$$

$$\Delta U = 297.2 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 4: Conclusion**
The internal energy of the nitrogen increases by **297.2 kJ**.

### Example 2: Total Energy of a System

**Problem:** A 5 kg object is moving at 20 m/s at a height of 10 m. Its internal energy is 50 kJ. Calculate the total energy of the system. (Take g = 9.81 m/s²)

**Step 1: Calculate Kinetic Energy**

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 20^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 400 = 1000 \, \text{J} = 1.0 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Potential Energy**

$$PE = mgh = 5 \times 9.81 \times 10 = 490.5 \, \text{J} = 0.4905 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3: Sum All Energies**

$$E_{total} = KE + PE + U = 1.0 + 0.4905 + 50 = 51.4905 \, \text{kJ}$$

### Example 3: Mayer's Relation Application

**Problem:** For air, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K. Calculate $c_v$.

**Step 1: Apply Mayer's Relation**

$$c_p - c_v = R$$

**Step 2: Solve for $c_v$**

$$c_v = c_p - R = 1.005 - 0.287 = 0.718 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

### Example 4: Gas Thermometer Calibration

**Problem:** A constant-volume gas thermometer shows a pressure of 1.5 × 10⁵ Pa at the triple point of water. What is the temperature when the pressure reads 4.5 × 10⁵ Pa?

**Step 1: Apply the Constant-Volume Relation**

$$T = 273.16 \times \frac{P}{P_{tp}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$T = 273.16 \times \frac{4.5 \times 10^5}{1.5 \times 10^5} = 273.16 \times 3$$

**Step 3: Calculate**

$$T = 819.48 \, \text{K}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Thermometer

| Temperature Range | Best Thermometer Choice | Reason |
|-------------------|-------------------------|--------|
| −200°C to 850°C | **Platinum RTD (PT100)** | High accuracy, excellent stability |
| −200°C to 1350°C | **Thermocouple (Type K)** | Wide range, fast response |
| 600°C to 3000°C | **Radiation Pyrometer** | Non-contact, very high temperatures |
| −39°C to 357°C | **Mercury-in-Glass** | Direct reading, inexpensive |
| −114°C to 50°C | **Alcohol-in-Glass** | Mercury freezes below −39°C |
| 73 K to 1235 K | **Constant-Volume Gas** | Primary standard |
| Cryogenic (below 0°C) | **Thermocouple Type T or E** | Low-temperature stability |
