> This is the *exact* logical template KTU examiners expect for a 7-mark sub-question. Memorize the order.

**Given**: A single-stage, single-acting reciprocating compressor with $D = 0.20\text{ m}$, $L = 0.30\text{ m}$, $c = 0.04$, $N = 300\text{ rpm}$, $P_1 = 100\text{ kPa}$, $P_2 = 800\text{ kPa}$, polytropic index $n = 1.3$. Atmospheric conditions: $101.325\text{ kPa}$, $20\,^\circ\text{C}$.

**Step 1 — Geometric quantities**:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.20)^2}{4} = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2$$

$$V_s = A L = 0.0314 \times 0.30 = 9.425 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{stroke}$$

$$V_c = c \cdot V_s = 0.04 \times 9.425 \times 10^{-3} = 3.77 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric efficiency**:

$$\eta_v = 1 + 0.04 - 0.04(8)^{1/1.3} = 1.04 - 0.04 \times 4.66 = 1.04 - 0.186 = 0.854$$

**Step 3 — Effective suction volume per stroke**:

$$V_1 = \eta_v \times V_s = 0.854 \times 9.425 \times 10^{-3} = 8.05 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Work per cycle (polytropic)**:

$$W = \frac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

$$= \frac{1.3}{0.3} \times 100\,000 \times 8.05 \times 10^{-3} \times \left[8^{0.3/1.3} - 1\right]$$

$$= 4.333 \times 805 \times \left[8^{0.2308} - 1\right] = 3488 \times [1.547 - 1] = 3488 \times 0.547 = 1908 \text{ J/cycle}$$

**Step 5 — Indicated Power (single-acting, k=1)**:

$$\text{IP} = \frac{W \times N}{60} = \frac{1908 \times 300}{60} = 9540 \text{ W} \approx 9.54 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Discharge temperature**:

$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = 293 \times 1.547 = 453.3 \text{ K} \approx 180\,^\circ\text{C}$$

This step-wise structure — **Geometry → ηᵥ → V₁ → W → IP → T₂** — is the KTU examiner's *expected* outline.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Reciprocating Compressor — Working Cycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

> The block diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a single-acting reciprocating compressor's $P\text{-}V$ cycle, with each thermodynamic process represented as a labelled functional block. This representation satisfies the KTU "block-level functional architecture" requirement and renders flawlessly in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Compressor Cylinder Functional Cycle]:::root --> A1[Process 1 to 2: SUCTION]
    A1 --> A2[Process 2 to 3: POLYTROPIC COMPRESSION]
    A2 --> A3[Process 3 to 4: DELIVERY AT CONSTANT P2]
    A3 --> A4[Process 4 to 5: POLYTROPIC RE-EXPANSION OF CLEARANCE GAS]
    A4 --> A1

    A1 -. P equals P1 .-> B1[Inlet Valve Open Outlet Valve Closed]
    A2 -. PV to the n equals C .-> B2[Both Valves Closed Piston Moves Upwards]
    A3 -. P equals P2 .-> B3[Outlet Valve Open Inlet Valve Closed]
    A4 -. PV to the n equals C .-> B4[Both Valves Closed Piston Moves Downwards]
    A4 -. Pressure falls to P1 .-> B5[Inlet Valve Opens at Point 5 Cycle Repeats]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff;
    classDef proc fill:#e0e7ef,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000000;
    class A1,A2,A3,A4 proc;
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, the cycle is drawn as a continuous loop because the suction process 5→1 follows immediately after the re-expansion 4→5. The two polygon blocks (Processes 2→3 and 4→5) represent the **polytropic** work input and the **re-expansion loss** respectively.

## 4.2 Compressor Family — Hierarchical Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Compressors]:::root --> S1[Positive Displacement]
    S0 --> S2[Dynamic Roto Dynamic]
    S1 --> S11[Reciprocating Piston Cylinder]
    S1 --> S12[Rotary Screw]
    S1 --> S13[Rotary Vane]
    S1 --> S14[Scroll]
    S2 --> S21[Centrifugal Radial]
    S2 --> S22[Axial Cascade]
    S2 --> S23[Mixed Flow]
    S11 --> S111[Single Stage]
    S11 --> S112[Multi Stage with Intercooling]
    S111 --> S1111[With Clearance]
    S111 --> S1112[Without Clearance Ideal]
    S112 --> S1121[Two Stage]
    S112 --> S1122[Three Stage]
    classDef root fill:#2d4a1f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff;
    classDef pd fill:#e8f0d8,stroke:#2d4a1f,color:#000000;
    classDef dy fill:#d8e8f0,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000000;
    class S1,S11,S12,S13,S14,S111,S112,S1111,S1112,S1121,S1122 pd;
    class S2,S21,S22,S23 dy;
```

## 4.3 Schematic of a Multi-Stage Compressor with Intercooler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I0[Atmospheric Air] --> I1[Inlet Filter and Silencer]
    I1 --> I2[Stage 1 Cylinder L.P]
    I2 --> I3[Intercooler 1 to near P1 and T1]
    I3 --> I4[Stage 2 Cylinder M.P]
    I4 --> I5[Intercooler 2 if provided]
    I5 --> I6[Stage 3 Cylinder H.P]
    I6 --> I7[After Cooler]
    I7 --> I8[Receiver Tank]
    I8 --> I9[Delivery to Mains at High Pressure]
    classDef comp fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff;
    classDef cool fill:#5f1f1f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff;
    class I2,I4,I6 comp;
    class I3,I5,I7 cool;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note (KTU Hot Topic)**: Intercooling between stages **reduces the total compression work** and **lowers the final delivery temperature**. The optimum intercooling brings the gas back to the *original inlet temperature* $T_1$ before entering the next stage. For a *two-stage* compressor with perfect intercooling, the **optimum pressure ratio** is the square root of the overall pressure ratio: $x_{opt} = \sqrt{P_2/P_1}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define *volumetric efficiency* of a reciprocating compressor. State its expression with clearance. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer**:
Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of the actual volume of fluid (at inlet conditions) handled by the compressor to the swept volume of the piston, expressed as a percentage (or fraction).

$$\eta_v = \frac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_s}$$

With a clearance ratio $c$ and polytropic re-expansion index $n$:

$$\eta_v = 1 + c - c\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$

It accounts for the loss in suction volume due to the re-expansion of the clearance gas that was retained at the end of the delivery stroke.

---

### Q2. Why is isothermal compression the *ideal* case? Mention the practical method used to approach it. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer**:
For a given pressure ratio, isothermal compression requires the **minimum possible work input** because the gas is compressed while heat is continuously removed, keeping its temperature constant. This is the theoretical lower bound on compression work.

In practice, this condition is approached by:
1. **Water-jacketing** the cylinder walls (recirculating cooling water around the cylinder).
2. **Spray cooling** (injecting a fine coolant spray into the cylinder).
3. **Multi-stage compression with intercooling** between stages, which mimics several short near-isothermal steps.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Standard KTU Pattern**: Part (a) for 7 marks (one core concept/derivation) and Part (b) for 7 marks (numerical problem or second concept).

---

### Question A: Derivation + Numerical `[14 Marks]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the work done per cycle in a single-stage reciprocating compressor when the compression follows the polytropic law $PV^n = \text{constant}$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution**:

(Recapitulate the derivation from §3.1, with every step explicitly written.)

Step 1 — Boundary work for a closed compression is given by the first law:

$$W = \int_{V_2}^{V_1} P \, dV$$

(Work *input* is positive during compression.)

Step 2 — The polytropic relation between any two states:

$$P V^n = P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n = C$$

Step 3 — Substitute $P = C V^{-n}$ into the work integral:

$$W = C \int_{V_2}^{V_1} V^{-n} \, dV = C \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_2}^{V_1}$$

Step 4 — Evaluate the bounds:

$$W = \frac{C}{1-n}\left(V_1^{1-n} - V_2^{1-n}\right)$$

Step 5 — Re-express the constant:

$$W = \frac{n}{n-1}\left(P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1\right)$$

Step 6 — Apply $V_2 = V_1 (P_1/P_2)^{1/n}$ to eliminate $V_2$:

$$W = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \quad \text{kJ/cycle} \;\;\blacksquare$$

**Boundary Cases for Full Marks**:
- $n = 1$ (isothermal) → use L'Hôpital or direct integration: $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$.
- $n = \gamma$ (isentropic) → $W = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$.

---

**(b)** A single-stage, single-acting reciprocating air compressor takes in air at $100\text{ kPa}$, $27\,^\circ\text{C}$ and delivers it at $1000\text{ kPa}$. The compression index is $n = 1.25$. The clearance ratio is $5\%$, and the bore and stroke are $150\text{ mm}$ and $200\text{ mm}$ respectively. The compressor runs at $400\text{ rpm}$. Calculate: (i) Volumetric efficiency, (ii) Indicated Power. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Geometry**:

$$A = \frac{\pi (0.150)^2}{4} = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

$$V_s = A L = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.20 = 3.534 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{stroke}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric efficiency** (with $c = 0.05$, $P_2/P_1 = 10$, $n = 1.25$):

$$\eta_v = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05 \times (10)^{1/1.25} = 1.05 - 0.05 \times 10^{0.8}$$

$$= 1.05 - 0.05 \times 6.3096 = 1.05 - 0.3155 = 0.7345 \;(\text{or } 73.45\%)$$

**Step 3 — Effective suction volume per stroke**:

$$V_1 = \eta_v \cdot V_s = 0.7345 \times 3.534 \times 10^{-3} = 2.595 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Work per cycle** (polytropic):

$$W = \frac{1.25}{0.25} \times 100\,000 \times 2.595 \times 10^{-3} \left[10^{0.25/1.25} - 1\right]$$

$$= 5 \times 259.5 \times \left[10^{0.2} - 1\right] = 1297.5 \times [1.5849 - 1] = 1297.5 \times 0.5849$$

$$W = 758.9 \text{ J/cycle}$$

**Step 5 — Indicated Power** (single-acting, $k = 1$):

$$\text{IP} = \frac{W \cdot N}{60} = \frac{758.9 \times 400}{60} = 5059 \text{ W} \approx 5.06 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answers**: (i) $\eta_v = 73.45\%$, (ii) $\text{IP} = 5.06\text{ kW}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B: Concept + Numerical `[14 Marks]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With the help of a labelled $P\text{-}V$ diagram, explain the working of a single-stage reciprocating compressor with clearance. State the formula for volumetric efficiency. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Answer**:

The $P\text{-}V$ diagram of a single-stage reciprocating compressor with clearance shows four distinct processes:

- **1 → 2 (Suction)**: Piston moves from TDC to BDC. Suction valve remains open. Cylinder pressure remains at $P_1$. Gas drawn in has volume $V_s$.
- **2 → 3 (Compression)**: Suction valve closes. Gas is polytropically compressed from $P_1$ to $P_2$ along $PV^n = C$.
- **3 → 4 (Delivery)**: At $P_2$, the delivery valve opens. Piston moves from BDC to TDC, expelling gas at constant pressure $P_2$. A small clearance volume $V_c$ remains occupied by high-pressure gas.
- **4 → 5 (Re-expansion)**: With both valves closed, the trapped gas re-expands polytropically from $P_2$ down to $P_1$. Volume at point 5 is $V_5 = V_c (P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$.

The suction valve reopens only at point 5, not at TDC. The *effective* fresh-gas volume is therefore less than $V_s$.

$$\boxed{\;\eta_v = 1 + c - c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}\;}$$

This loss is fundamental to reciprocating compressors and is the primary reason multi-stage designs (with smaller $P_2/P_1$ per stage) achieve higher overall $\eta_v$. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b)** A two-stage reciprocating compressor with complete intercooling compresses air from $1\text{ bar}$ to $36\text{ bar}$. The intake temperature is $300\text{ K}$ and the compression index is $n = 1.3$ in both stages. Assuming optimum intercooling, determine: (i) the optimum pressure ratio per stage, (ii) the total work of compression per kg of air, and (iii) the temperature at the end of the second stage. Take $R = 0.287\text{ kJ/kg·K}$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Optimum pressure ratio per stage**:

For two stages with perfect intercooling back to $T_1$:

$$x_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{P_2}{P_1}} = \sqrt{36} = 6$$

**Step 2 — Work per stage** (each stage compresses from 1 to 6 bar, then 6 to 36 bar):

$$W_1 = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[x^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right] = \frac{1.3}{0.3} \times 0.287 \times 300 \times \left[6^{0.3/1.3} - 1\right]$$

$$= 4.333 \times 86.1 \times [6^{0.2308} - 1] = 373.0 \times [1.394 - 1] = 373.0 \times 0.394 = 147.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Total work** (intercooling restores $T_1$, so $W_2 = W_1$):

$$W_{total} = 2 \times 147.0 = 294.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Final temperature after stage 2** (intercooled back to $300\text{ K}$ between stages, so same as $T_2$ of stage 2):

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot x_{opt}^{(n-1)/n} = 300 \times 1.394 = 418.2 \text{ K}$$

**Final Answers**: (i) $x_{opt} = 6$, (ii) $W_{total} = 294.0\text{ kJ/kg}$, (iii) $T_2 = 418.2\text{ K}$. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Valuation Warnings)**
> 1. **Mixing up $V_1$ and $V_s$**: In numericals, students often substitute $V_s$ instead of $V_1 = \eta_v \cdot V_s$ into the work formula. **Marks deducted**: 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $k$ factor**: For a *double-acting* compressor, the number of effective working strokes per revolution is $k = 2$, not $1$. Power is *doubled*. **Common slip**: 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention**: In some textbooks $W$ is taken negative (work done *by* the system). Stick to the KTU convention: **work input is positive**.
> 4. **Not stating units in the final answer**: KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per numerical answer if units (kW, kJ/kg, K) are missing.
> 5. **Intercooling misconceptions**: If the problem says "perfect intercooling", you **must** take $T_3 = T_1$ between stages. If it says "intercooled to a specific temperature", use that value instead. Marks are lost for misreading the question.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Compressor = work-consuming device** that raises gas pressure. Pair it with a *turbine* (work-producing) in a gas-turbine system.
- **Isothermal < Polytropic < Isentropic** in terms of work input (for the same $P_2/P_1$, same $T_1$). Always remember this ordering.
- **Polytropic work formula** $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ is the **Swiss Army knife** — every other formula is a special case.
- **Volumetric efficiency** drops as $P_2/P_1$ rises and as $c$ rises. $\eta_v = 1 + c - c(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$.
- **Discharge temperature** $T_2 = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$. If $n = \gamma$ (isentropic) and $P_2/P_1 = 8$, $T_2 \approx 1.667 \cdot T_1$.
- **Multi-stage + intercooling** is used when $P_2/P_1 > 8$ to (a) save work, (b) reduce $T_2$, (c) increase $\eta_v$, (d) reduce moisture condensation in air compressors.
- **Optimum pressure ratio** for $N$ stages with perfect intercooling: $x_{opt} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/N}$.
- **FAD** = $\eta_v \cdot V_s \cdot N \cdot k$ (in m³/min for reciprocating units).
- **MEP** is a *fictitious* uniform pressure that produces the same cycle work — useful for comparing machines.
- **For double-acting cylinder**: two effective strokes per revolution ($k=2$).
- **Important constants to remember**: $R_{\text{air}} = 0.287\text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma_{\text{air}} = 1.4$, $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325\text{ kPa}$.
- **Positive-displacement vs Dynamic**: PD compressors (reciprocating, screw) are suited to **high pressure, low flow**. Dynamic compressors (centrifugal, axial) are suited to **low-to-medium pressure, high flow**.
- **Stagnation effects** and *clearance re-expansion* are the two main reasons real compressors need more work than the ideal isothermal case.

> ✅ **Last-Minute Memory Trigger**: If you remember only **one** equation, remember $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$. Every KTU compressor problem begins here.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nozzles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nozzles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Nozzle** is a carefully shaped duct of **varying cross-sectional area** in which the **kinetic energy (velocity) of a flowing fluid increases** at the expense of its **pressure energy (pressure drop)**. The process is conventionally treated as **adiabatic** (no heat transfer, $Q = 0$), and for ideal analysis, it is **reversible (isentropic)** with no friction or turbulence. The fluid accelerates from a region of high pressure to a region of low pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMUT403 – Module 1):**
> A nozzle converts the *enthalpy* (internal + flow energy) of a high-pressure fluid into *kinetic energy*. The governing principles are the **Continuity Equation** (conservation of mass) and the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** applied under the assumption of steady, adiabatic, and isentropic flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Nozzles by Geometry:**
> 1. **Convergent Nozzle** – Area decreases continuously in the direction of flow.
> 2. **Divergent Nozzle** – Area increases continuously in the direction of flow (used only for subsonic flow or as the second part of a CD nozzle).
> 3. **Convergent-Divergent (De Laval) Nozzle** – First converges to a minimum cross-section (*throat*), then diverges. Mandatory for achieving **supersonic (Mach > 1)** exit velocities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water hose with your thumb partially covering the opening**:
- When you reduce the exit area, the water shoots out faster and travels further.
- This is exactly the principle of a **convergent nozzle** — restricting area increases velocity.

Now think of a **fire hose nozzle or a garden spray gun**:
- The fluid enters at low velocity, gets squeezed through a narrow throat, and exits as a high-speed jet.
- This is the same idea used in **steam turbines, rocket engines, and jet aircraft** where high-pressure steam or gas is converted into a high-velocity exhaust stream to produce **thrust**.

In essence: **Nozzle = Pressure-to-Velocity Converter**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Specific heat ratio for air, $\gamma = 1.4$**
- **Specific heat ratio for superheated steam, $\gamma \approx 1.3$**
- **Specific gas constant, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$**
- **For steam turbines:** inlet pressure is typically **in the range 1 MPa to 25 MPa**, with inlet temperatures **up to 873 K (600 °C)**
- **Mach Number (M)**: defined as $M = V / a$, where $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ is the local speed of sound

## 1.4 Why are Nozzles Important?

- In **steam turbines**: nozzles direct high-velocity steam onto turbine blades.
- In **rocket engines (De Laval nozzle)**: convert combustion gas pressure into supersonic thrust.
- In **jet engines**: propulsive nozzle produces exhaust velocity.
- In **flow measurement devices** (like venturi meters — a related principle).
- In **spray systems and carburetors** for atomization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** A nozzle has *no moving parts* — energy conversion happens purely through **geometry-induced area change**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Governing Principles

The complete theoretical analysis of nozzles rests on **two non-negotiable equations**:

### 2.1.1 Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)
For steady flow, the **mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ is constant** at every cross-section:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \text{constant}$$

For compressible fluids, this is best written using specific volume $v$:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{A_1 V_1}{v_1} = \frac{A_2 V_2}{v_2}$$

**Why this matters:** As the fluid accelerates, both velocity increases and specific volume (due to pressure drop) changes. The area is designed to *channel* this combined effect.

### 2.1.2 Energy Equation (Steady Flow Energy Equation – SFEE)
Applying SFEE between inlet (1) and exit (2) of an adiabatic nozzle (no work, no heat transfer, negligible PE change):

$$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$

Rearranged:

$$V_2^2 - V_1^2 = 2(h_1 - h_2) = 2 \cdot \Delta h_s$$

Where $\Delta h_s$ is the **isentropic enthalpy drop**. This is the **core equation that quantifies how much pressure energy converts into kinetic energy**.

## 2.2 Velocity at Nozzle Exit — Isentropic Compression & Expansion

### 2.2.1 For a Perfect Gas (e.g., air, hot combustion gases)

Starting from $V dV = -v \, dP$ (from Euler's equation for a nozzle) and integrating with $Pv^\gamma = \text{const}$:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{2 \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot R T_1 \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right] + V_1^2}$$

When inlet velocity is negligible ($V_1 \approx 0$):

$$V_2 = \sqrt{2 \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot R T_1 \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]}$$

### 2.2.2 For Steam (using enthalpy from steam tables)

$$V_2 = \sqrt{2 \cdot (h_1 - h_2) \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(m/s, when } h \text{ in kJ/kg)}$$

Here, $h_2$ is the **isentropic enthalpy** at the exit pressure $P_2$ — read from a Mollier chart or steam tables.

## 2.3 The Critical Pressure Ratio & Choked Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The most important theoretical result for KTU:** In a **convergent nozzle**, the mass flow rate **cannot exceed a maximum limit** even if the back pressure is reduced further. This limit corresponds to **sonic conditions (Mach = 1) at the throat**, and the corresponding pressure ratio is called the **Critical Pressure Ratio (CPR)**.

### 2.3.1 Derivation Insight
For a convergent nozzle, mass flow rate as a function of pressure ratio is:

$$\dot{m} = A_2 \cdot P_1 \sqrt{\frac{2\gamma}{(\gamma-1)RT_1} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma} \right]}$$

Differentiating $\dot{m}$ with respect to $P_2/P_1$ and setting $\frac{d\dot{m}}{d(P_2/P_1)} = 0$ gives:

$$\boxed{\frac{P^*}{P_1} = \left( \frac{2}{\gamma + 1} \right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}}$$

For $\gamma = 1.4$ (air): $P^*/P_1 = 0.528$
For $\gamma = 1.3$ (superheated steam): $P^*/P_1 \approx 0.546$
For $\gamma = 1.67$ (monatomic gas): $P^*/P_1 \approx 0.487$

### 2.3.2 Maximum Mass Flow Rate (Choked Flow)

$$\dot{m}_{max} = A^* \cdot P_1 \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{RT_1} \left( \frac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)}}$$

Where $A^*$ is the **throat area**. Beyond this, **no further increase in mass flow is possible** unless back pressure rises.

## 2.4 Super-Saturation (Supercooling) Effect

In **real steam nozzles**, steam sometimes **fails to condense** even when the isentropic state lies in the wet region. This phenomenon is called **Super-Saturation** (or Wilson line effect). The actual entropy increases compared to the isentropic process, leading to a **lower exit velocity** and a **larger nozzle exit area** than predicted by isentropic theory.

**Empirical correction (commonly asked in KTU):** Super-saturation adds approximately **3 to 5%** to the nozzle exit area, computed using the **empirical formula** by C. A. Wilson.

## 2.5 Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)

To account for **friction and turbulence** in real nozzles:

$$C_d = \frac{\text{Actual mass flow rate}}{\text{Ideal (isentropic) mass flow rate}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{actual}}{\dot{m}_{ideal}}$$

Typically, $C_d$ for well-designed nozzles lies between **0.95 and 0.98**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Applicable When** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m} = \rho A V = \frac{AV}{v}$ | Mass flow rate | All nozzles |
| 2 | $V_2 = \sqrt{2(h_1 - h_2) \cdot 1000}$ | Exit velocity (m/s) | Steam nozzle ($h$ in kJ/kg) |
| 3 | $V_2 = \sqrt{2 \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[1 - (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]}$ | Exit velocity (m/s) | Perfect gas, $V_1 \approx 0$ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{P^*}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | Critical pressure ratio | Convergent nozzle, perfect gas |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{max} = A^* P_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{RT_1} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)}}$ | Maximum (choked) mass flow | Sonic condition at throat |
| 6 | $A_2 = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot v_2}{C_d \cdot V_2}$ | Nozzle exit area (actual) | With friction/discharge loss |
| 7 | $M = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{\gamma R T}} = \dfrac{V}{a}$ | Mach number | All compressible flows |
| 8 | $C_d = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{actual}}{\dot{m}_{ideal}}$ | Coefficient of discharge | Real nozzles with friction |
| 9 | $\dfrac{T^*}{T_1} = \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}$ | Stagnation temp. ratio at throat | Critical/sonic condition |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\rho^*}{\rho_1} = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{1/(\gamma-1)}$ | Stagnation density ratio at throat | Critical/sonic condition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In your KTU exam scripts, **never use the vertical bar $\vert$ symbol for absolute value inside inline math** — use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ instead. The above table uses $\vert \cdot \vert$ for column separation only. Always write expressions like $\lvert V_2 - V_1 \rvert$ when needed.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steam power plants:** Nozzle is the *first stage* in impulse turbines — converts high-pressure steam into a high-speed jet that strikes moving blades.
- **Aircraft propulsion:** Afterburner nozzles of jet engines operate as convergent-divergent ducts.
- **Rocketry:** The De Laval nozzle is *the* critical component for converting combustion chamber pressure into supersonic thrust.
- **Industrial cleaning & cutting:** High-pressure water jet cutting uses nozzle principles.
- **Firefighting equipment:** Fire hose nozzles use convergent geometry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Velocity of Steam at Nozzle Exit (using SFEE + Isentropic Relations)

> This derivation is **frequently asked for 7 marks** in KTU ESE. Memorize every step.

### Step 1: Start with the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)

For a nozzle, $W = 0$, $Q = 0$, and potential energy change $\approx 0$. The SFEE between inlet (1) and exit (2) is:

$$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$

### Step 2: Solve for Exit Velocity

Rearranging the SFEE:

$$\frac{V_2^2}{2} = \frac{V_1^2}{2} + (h_1 - h_2)$$

$$V_2^2 = V_1^2 + 2(h_1 - h_2)$$

### Step 3: Introduce Isentropic Enthalpy Drop

For the **ideal isentropic process**, $h_2 = h_{2s}$ — the enthalpy at the exit pressure $P_2$ along the isentrope from the initial state. Define:

$$\Delta h_s = h_1 - h_{2s} \quad \text{(isentropic enthalpy drop, in kJ/kg)}$$

Then:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{V_1^2 + 2 \cdot \Delta h_s \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

The factor of **1000** converts kJ/kg → J/kg.

### Step 4: Special Case — When Inlet Velocity is Negligible

If $V_1 \approx 0$ (large plenum/upstream reservoir):

$$\boxed{V_2 = \sqrt{2000 \cdot \Delta h_s} \quad \text{(m/s, with } \Delta h_s \text{ in kJ/kg)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Velocity from Pressure and Temperature (Perfect Gas)

### Step 1: Apply Euler's Equation for a Nozzle

The differential form of Bernoulli's equation for a frictionless duct is:

$$\frac{dP}{\rho} + V \, dV = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V \, dV = -\frac{dP}{\rho}$$

### Step 2: Substitute Isentropic Relation

For an isentropic process of a perfect gas:

$$\frac{P}{\rho^\gamma} = \text{const} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \rho_1 \left( \frac{P}{P_1} \right)^{1/\gamma}$$

So:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{\rho_1} \left( \frac{P}{P_1} \right)^{-1/\gamma}$$

### Step 3: Integrate from State 1 to State 2

$$V \, dV = -\frac{1}{\rho_1} \left( \frac{P}{P_1} \right)^{-1/\gamma} dP$$

$$\int_{V_1}^{V_2} V \, dV = -\frac{1}{\rho_1} \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \left( \frac{P}{P_1} \right)^{-1/\gamma} dP$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Integrals

The left side:

$$\int_{V_1}^{V_2} V \, dV = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$

The right side, using $P_1^{\,1/\gamma} \cdot \int P^{-1/\gamma} dP = P_1^{\,1/\gamma} \cdot \left[ \frac{P^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]$:

$$-\frac{1}{\rho_1} \cdot P_1^{\,1/\gamma} \cdot \left[ \frac{P_2^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - P_1^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]$$

### Step 5: Simplify Using $P_1 = \rho_1 R T_1$ and $\rho_1 R T_1 / P_1 = 1$

After algebraic manipulation:

$$\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{P_1}{\rho_1} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]$$

### Step 6: Replace $P_1/\rho_1$ with $RT_1$

Since $P_1 = \rho_1 R T_1$:

$$P_1/\rho_1 = R T_1$$

### Step 7: Final Result

$$\boxed{V_2 = \sqrt{V_1^2 + \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]}}$$

For negligible inlet velocity ($V_1 \approx 0$):

$$V_2 = \sqrt{ \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right] }$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Critical Pressure Ratio

> This is the **most tested derivation** in KTU Module 1 — appears almost every exam cycle.

### Step 1: Write Mass Flow Rate as a Function of Exit Pressure

From continuity: $\dot{m} = A \cdot V / v$, and from the velocity equation (with $V_1 = 0$):

$$V_2 = \sqrt{ \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right] }$$

For isentropic expansion: $v_2 = v_1 \cdot (P_1/P_2)^{1/\gamma}$

### Step 2: Combine into a Single Expression for $\dot{m}$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{A_2}{v_1} \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/\gamma} \sqrt{ \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right] }$$

### Step 3: Set $r = P_2/P_1$ for Simplicity

$$\dot{m} = \frac{A_2}{v_1} \cdot r^{1/\gamma} \sqrt{ \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 (1 - r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}) }$$

### Step 4: Maximize $\dot{m}$ by Differentiating with respect to $r$

Let $f(r) = r^{2/\gamma} \cdot (1 - r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma})$. Differentiate:

$$\frac{df}{dr} = \frac{2}{\gamma} r^{(2/\gamma)-1} \cdot (1 - r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}) + r^{2/\gamma} \cdot \left( -\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} r^{(1/\gamma)-1} \right) = 0$$

### Step 5: Simplify

Divide throughout by $r^{(2/\gamma)-1}$:

$$\frac{2}{\gamma} (1 - r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}) - \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 0$$

$$\frac{2}{\gamma} - \frac{2}{\gamma} r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 0$$

$$\frac{2}{\gamma} = r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \left( \frac{2}{\gamma} + \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} \right) = r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \cdot \frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma}$$

### Step 6: Solve for the Critical Pressure Ratio

$$r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \frac{2}{\gamma+1}$$

$$r = \left( \frac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$$

Since $r = P_2/P_1 = P^*/P_1$ at this critical point:

$$\boxed{\frac{P^*}{P_1} = \left( \frac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum (Choked) Mass Flow Rate

### Step 1: Substitute the Critical Pressure Ratio into the Velocity Expression

At the throat (state $^*$), $T^* = T_1 \cdot 2/(\gamma+1)$ (from isentropic relations):

$$V^* = \sqrt{ \gamma R T^* } = \sqrt{ \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1} R T_1 }$$

This is precisely the **local speed of sound at the throat** — confirming the **choked flow / sonic throat** condition.

### Step 2: Compute Critical Density

$$\rho^* = \rho_1 \left( \frac{P^*}{P_1} \right)^{1/\gamma} = \rho_1 \left( \frac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{1/(\gamma-1)}$$

### Step 3: Form the Mass Flow Rate

$$\dot{m}_{max} = A^* \cdot \rho^* \cdot V^*$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\dot{m}_{max} = A^* \cdot \rho_1 \left( \frac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{1/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \sqrt{ \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1} R T_1 }$$

### Step 4: Replace $\rho_1 = P_1 / (RT_1)$

$$\dot{m}_{max} = A^* \cdot \frac{P_1}{R T_1} \cdot \left( \frac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{1/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \sqrt{ \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1} R T_1 }$$

### Step 5: Combine the Exponents of $\frac{2}{\gamma+1}$

$$\dot{m}_{max} = A^* \cdot \frac{P_1}{\sqrt{R T_1}} \cdot \sqrt{ \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1} } \cdot \left( \frac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{1/(\gamma-1)}$$

$$\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{1/(\gamma-1) + 1/2} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/[2(\gamma-1)]} = \sqrt{ \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)} }$$

### Step 6: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{max} = A^* \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{ \frac{\gamma}{R T_1} \left( \frac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)} }}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Three Nozzle Flow Regimes

| **Back Pressure $P_b$ vs $P_1$** | **Nozzle Type Required** | **Exit Mach Number** | **Behavior** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_b > P^*$ (e.g., $P_b/P_1 > 0.528$ for $\gamma=1.4$) | Convergent | Subsonic ($M < 1$) | Flow exits at $P_b$; velocity below sonic. |
| $P_b = P^*$ | Convergent (throat reaches sonic) | $M = 1$ at throat | **Choked flow begins.** |
| $P_b < P^*$ | **Convergent-Divergent (CD)** nozzle required | $M > 1$ at exit | Subsonic in converging section, sonic at throat, supersonic in diverging section. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often assume a simple convergent nozzle always works. **A simple convergent nozzle cannot produce supersonic exit velocity** — you *must* use a convergent-divergent (De Laval) nozzle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Nozzle Geometry Types — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Nozzle Classification by Geometry]
    nodeB[Convergent Nozzle]
    nodeC[Divergent Nozzle]
    nodeD[Convergent-Divergent CD De Laval Nozzle]
    
    nodeE[Area decreases in flow direction]
    nodeF[Area increases in flow direction]
    nodeG[First converges to throat then diverges]
    
    nodeH[Subsonic flow only M less than 1]
    nodeI[Subsonic flow only M less than 1]
    nodeJ[Required for supersonic flow M greater than 1]
    
    nodeK[Used in low pressure turbines and spray systems]
    nodeL[Used as second stage of CD nozzle]
    nodeM[Used in rockets jet engines steam turbines]
    
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeK
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeL
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeD --> nodeM
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow Architecture of a Convergent-Divergent (De Laval) Nozzle

```mermaid
graph LR
    inlet[INLET High Pressure P1 Temperature T1] -->|Subsonic Flow| conv[Converging Section Area Decreases]
    conv -->|Velocity Increases| throat[THROTTLE Minimum Area A asterisk]
    throat -->|Sonic Condition M equals 1| diverg[Diverging Section Area Increases]
    diverg -->|Supersonic Flow| exit[EXIT Low Pressure P2 High Velocity V2]
    
    style inlet fill:#ff9999
    style throat fill:#ffcc00
    style exit fill:#99ccff
```

## 4.3 Pressure–Velocity–Area Block Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Inlet State: P1 high, V1 low, A1 large] -->|Continuity plus Energy| B[Throat State: P star, V sonic, A minimum]
    B -->|Continued expansion| C[Exit State: P2 low, V2 supersonic, A2 large]
    
    A --> A1[Mass m dot constant]
    B --> A1
    C --> A1
    
    A --> A2[SFEE: h1 plus V1 squared by 2 equals h2 plus V2 squared by 2]
    C --> A2
    
    style A fill:#ffcccc
    style B fill:#ffff99
    style C fill:#ccccff
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic for Nozzle Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Given Inlet P1 T1 and Back Pressure Pb] --> q1{Is Pb greater than P star?}
    
    q1 -->|Yes - Subsonic Exit Needed| conc[Use Convergent Nozzle]
    q1 -->|No - Supersonic Exit Needed| cd[Use Convergent Divergent Nozzle]
    
    conc --> conc1[Exit M less than 1]
    cd --> cd1[Throat M equals 1 choked]
    cd --> cd2[Exit M greater than 1 supersonic]
    
    style start fill:#e0e0e0
    style conc fill:#c8e6c9
    style cd fill:#bbdefb
```

## 4.5 Steam Nozzle Process on h-s Diagram (Mollier Chart) — Textual Block

> Since the Mollier diagram is a 2D thermodynamic chart that cannot be drawn directly in Mermaid, here is the **textual flow description** that frequently appears in KTU answers:

```
        h
        ↑
        |         (1) Inlet: P1, T1, h1
        |        .
        |       . \  Isentropic line (s = const)
        |      .   \ 
        |     .     \(2s) Isentropic exit: P2, h2s
        |    .       .
        |   .         .(2) Actual exit: P2, h2 (h2 > h2s)
        |  .          .   ↑
        | .           .   | Loss Δh = h2 - h2s
        |.____________.___.________→ s
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Point 1 = Inlet state (high $h$, given $P_1$, $T_1$).
- Point 2s = Isentropic exit state (along the vertical isentrope $s_1 = s_{2s}$).
- Point 2 = Actual exit (entropy $s_2 > s_{2s}$ due to irreversibilities).
- The isentropic enthalpy drop $\Delta h_s = h_1 - h_{2s}$ is the **ideal kinetic energy available**.
- The actual enthalpy drop $\Delta h = h_1 - h_2 < \Delta h_s$.
- The difference is the **loss** due to friction and turbulence.

## 4.6 Critical Pressure Ratio Computation — Verification Block

```
For Air, gamma = 1.4:
  P star / P1 = (2/2.4)^3.5 = (0.8333)^3.5
  log10(0.8333) = -0.0792
  3.5 × log10(0.8333) = -0.2772
  10^(-0.2772) = 0.5283
  Therefore P star / P1 = 0.5283

For Steam superheated, gamma = 1.3:
  P star / P1 = (2/2.3)^4.333
  log10(2/2.3) = log10(0.8696) = -0.0606
  4.333 × (-0.0606) = -0.2626
  10^(-0.2626) = 0.5457
  Therefore P star / P1 = 0.5457
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — Always convert enthalpy from kJ/kg to J/kg (multiply by 1000). Marks are *specifically docked* for unit errors in the velocity formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $V_1^2$** — When inlet velocity is not negligible (compressor-fed nozzles), many students write $V_2 = \sqrt{2 \Delta h \cdot 1000}$, missing the $V_1^2$ term. Read the problem statement carefully.
> 3. **Wrong $\gamma$ for steam** — Use $\gamma = 1.3$ for **superheated steam** and $\gamma = 1.135$ (approximately) for **saturated/ wet steam** in simplified analysis. Do NOT use $\gamma = 1.4$ for steam.
> 4. **Critical pressure ratio confusion** — Students confuse $P^*/P_1$ with $P_2/P_1$. The critical ratio is a **property of the gas**, not the design.
> 5. **Skipping continuity** — When asked for *throat area*, apply $\dot{m} = \rho^* A^* V^*$ using the critical state properties.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
> Define a nozzle. Differentiate between convergent, divergent, and convergent-divergent nozzles.

**Model Answer:**

A **nozzle** is a duct of varying cross-sectional area used to convert the pressure energy of a flowing fluid into kinetic energy. The process is adiabatic and, ideally, isentropic.

| **Property** | **Convergent** | **Divergent** | **Convergent-Divergent (De Laval)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Area change | Decreases | Increases | Decreases then increases |
| Suitable for | Subsonic ($M<1$) | Subsonic ($M<1$) | Subsonic to supersonic ($M>1$) |
| Velocity at exit | < Sonic | < Sonic | > Sonic possible |
| Typical use | Spray guns, low-pressure steam | Rarely used alone | Rocket engines, jet propulsion, impulse turbines |

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Set B]**
> What is the critical pressure ratio? State its significance in nozzle design.

**Model Answer:**

The **critical pressure ratio** is the ratio of exit (or throat) pressure to inlet pressure at which the **velocity of the fluid at the throat becomes sonic (Mach number = 1)**. It is given by:

$$\frac{P^*}{P_1} = \left( \frac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$$

For air ($\gamma = 1.4$), $P^*/P_1 = 0.528$.

**Significance:**
- It marks the onset of **choked flow** — beyond this, mass flow rate cannot increase further with a further drop in back pressure.
- It determines whether a **simple convergent** nozzle is sufficient, or a **convergent-divergent (CD) nozzle** is required to achieve higher (supersonic) exit velocities.
- For $P_b/P_1 > P^*/P_1$: convergent nozzle is sufficient.
- For $P_b/P_1 \leq P^*/P_1$: a CD nozzle is mandatory.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
> **(a)** Derive an expression for the velocity of steam at the exit of a convergent nozzle from fundamental principles. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Dry saturated steam at a pressure of 1.5 MPa enters a convergent nozzle and expands isentropically to a pressure of 0.1 MPa. Determine:
> (i) The exit velocity, and
> (ii) The exit area of the nozzle for a mass flow rate of 0.5 kg/s.
> Take $C_d = 0.96$.
> Use the following data:
> At 1.5 MPa (dry saturated): $h_1 = 2792.2$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 6.7448$ kJ/(kg·K), $v_1 = 0.13177$ m³/kg
> At 0.1 MPa (s = 6.7448 kJ/(kg·K)): $h_{2s} = 2500.9$ kJ/kg, $v_{2s} = 1.6940$ m³/kg (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Exit Velocity

**Step 1:** Apply the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) between inlet (1) and exit (2) of the nozzle. For a nozzle: $W = 0$, $Q = 0$, and we neglect the change in potential energy.

$$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for $V_2$:

$$\frac{V_2^2}{2} = \frac{V_1^2}{2} + (h_1 - h_2)$$

$$V_2^2 = V_1^2 + 2(h_1 - h_2)$$

**Step 3:** For an isentropic process, $h_2 = h_{2s}$. Define the isentropic enthalpy drop:

$$\Delta h_s = h_1 - h_{2s} \quad \text{(kJ/kg)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and convert kJ to J (multiply by 1000):

$$V_2 = \sqrt{V_1^2 + 2000 \cdot \Delta h_s} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

**Step 5:** If the inlet velocity is negligible ($V_1 \approx 0$):

$$\boxed{V_2 = \sqrt{2000 \cdot \Delta h_s} = 44.72 \sqrt{\Delta h_s} \quad \text{(m/s)}}$$

This is the **fundamental equation for nozzle exit velocity**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1: Compute the isentropic enthalpy drop**

$$\Delta h_s = h_1 - h_{2s} = 2792.2 - 2500.9 = 291.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2: Compute the exit velocity**

Assume inlet velocity $V_1 \approx 0$:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{2000 \times 291.3} = \sqrt{582600} = 763.28 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute the ideal (isentropic) exit area**

From the continuity equation:

$$A_{2,ideal} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v_{2s}}{V_2} = \frac{0.5 \times 1.6940}{763.28} = 1.1097 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Apply the coefficient of discharge for the actual area**

$$A_{2,actual} = \frac{A_{2,ideal}}{C_d} = \frac{1.1097 \times 10^{-3}}{0.96} = 1.1559 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A_{2,actual} \approx 1.156 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 11.56 \text{ cm}^2}$$

**Summary of Results:**
- (i) Exit velocity $V_2 \approx 763.28$ m/s
- (ii) Actual exit area $A_2 \approx 11.56$ cm²

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Set B]**
> **(a)** Air enters a nozzle at stagnation conditions $P_1 = 1$ MPa and $T_1 = 500$ K, with negligible inlet velocity, and expands to a back pressure of 0.2 MPa. Determine:
> (i) The exit velocity,
> (ii) The exit Mach number, and
> (iii) Whether a convergent-divergent nozzle is required.
> Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 287$ J/(kg·K). (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** For the nozzle in part (a), determine the **critical pressure ratio** and the **maximum possible mass flow rate** per unit throat area. If the actual throat area is 5 cm², find the actual mass flow rate. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Exit Velocity, Mach Number, and Nozzle Type

**Step 1: Compute the pressure ratio**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{0.2}{1.0} = 0.2$$

**Step 2: Compute the exit velocity using the isentropic flow equation**

$$V_2 = \sqrt{ \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right] }$$

Compute the exponent term:

$$\left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = (0.2)^{0.4/1.4} = (0.2)^{0.2857}$$

Take logarithm:

$$\log_{10}(0.2) = -0.6990$$

$$0.2857 \times (-0.6990) = -0.1997$$

$$10^{-0.1997} = 0.6314$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the velocity equation**

$$V_2 = \sqrt{ \frac{2 \times 1.4}{0.4} \times 287 \times 500 \times (1 - 0.6314) }$$

$$V_2 = \sqrt{ 7 \times 287 \times 500 \times 0.3686 }$$

$$V_2 = \sqrt{ 7 \times 143500 \times 0.3686 } = \sqrt{ 370,217 }$$

$$\boxed{V_2 \approx 608.5 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the exit temperature**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 0.6314$$

$$T_2 = 500 \times 0.6314 = 315.7 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5: Compute the local speed of sound at the exit**

$$a_2 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_2} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 315.7} = \sqrt{126,839} \approx 356.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6: Compute the exit Mach number**

$$M_2 = \frac{V_2}{a_2} = \frac{608.5}{356.2} = 1.708$$

$$\boxed{M_2 \approx 1.71 \text{ (supersonic)}}$$

**Step 7: Determine the nozzle type**

The critical pressure ratio is:

$$\frac{P^*}{P_1} = \left( \frac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = \left( \frac{2}{2.4} \right)^{3.5} = (0.8333)^{3.5} = 0.5283$$

Since $P_2/P_1 = 0.2 < 0.5283 = P^*/P_1$, the back pressure is **below** the critical pressure ratio.

> **Conclusion:** A **convergent-divergent (De Laval) nozzle** is **required** to achieve supersonic exit flow. A simple convergent nozzle can only accelerate the flow up to $M = 1$ at the throat and cannot produce $M > 1$ at the exit.

---

#### Part (b) — Critical Pressure Ratio and Maximum Mass Flow Rate

**Step 1: State the critical pressure ratio**

$$\boxed{\frac{P^*}{P_1} = 0.5283 \quad \text{(for air, } \gamma = 1.4)}$$

**Step 2: Maximum mass flow rate per unit throat area**

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{max}}{A^*} = P_1 \sqrt{ \frac{\gamma}{R T_1} \left( \frac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)} }$$

Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1} = \frac{2.4}{0.4} = 6$$

Compute the term inside the square root:

$$\left( \frac{2}{2.4} \right)^{6} = (0.8333)^6$$

$$(0.8333)^2 = 0.6944$$

$$(0.8333)^4 = 0.6944^2 = 0.4822$$

$$(0.8333)^6 = 0.4822 \times 0.6944 = 0.3349$$

Now compute:

$$\frac{\gamma}{R T_1} = \frac{1.4}{287 \times 500} = \frac{1.4}{143500} = 9.756 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\frac{\gamma}{R T_1} \times 0.3349 = 9.756 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.3349 = 3.267 \times 10^{-6}$$

Square root:

$$\sqrt{3.267 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.807 \times 10^{-3}$$

Finally:

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{max}}{A^*} = 1 \times 10^{6} \times 1.807 \times 10^{-3} = 1807.5 \text{ kg/(s·m}^2)$$

**Step 3: Actual mass flow rate for $A^* = 5 \text{ cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$**

$$\dot{m}_{max} = 1807.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.9038 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{max} \approx 0.904 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**Note:** This is the *choked* mass flow rate — the maximum that the nozzle can pass regardless of how low the back pressure goes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 1 / Nozzles**

### **Conceptual Foundation**
- A **nozzle** converts **pressure energy → kinetic energy** (adiabatic, ideally isentropic).
- No work is done, no heat transfer occurs; SFEE simplifies to $h_1 + V_1^2/2 = h_2 + V_2^2/2$.
- The **continuity equation** ensures $\dot{m} = \rho A V = \text{const}$ throughout the duct.

### **Key Formulas to Memorize**
- Steam: $V_2 = \sqrt{V_1^2 + 2000(h_1 - h_{2s})}$ (m/s, $h$ in kJ/kg)
- Perfect gas: $V_2 = \sqrt{V_1^2 + \dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ 1 - (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right]}$
- **Critical pressure ratio:** $\dfrac{P^*}{P_1} = \left( \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$
- **Maximum (choked) mass flow:** $\dot{m}_{max} = A^* P_1 \sqrt{ \dfrac{\gamma}{R T_1} \left( \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1} \right)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)} }$
- **Actual nozzle area:** $A_{2,actual} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot v_2}{C_d \cdot V_2}$

### **Numerical Values to Remember**
- $P^*/P_1$ for air ($\gamma = 1.4$) = **0.528**
- $P^*/P_1$ for superheated steam ($\gamma = 1.3$) = **0.546**
- $P^*/P_1$ for combustion gases ($\gamma = 1.33$) = **0.540**
- Typical $C_d$ for nozzles = **0.95 to 0.98**

### **Decision Rules (Frequently Tested)**
- $P_b/P_1 > P^*/P_1$ → **Convergent** nozzle (subsonic exit)
- $P_b/P_1 \leq P^*/P_1$ → **Convergent-Divergent (De Laval)** nozzle (supersonic exit required)
- $V_1 \approx 0$ assumption: valid for large upstream plenum/reservoir

### **Common Exam Pitfalls**
- Forgetting to convert **kJ/kg → J/kg** (factor of 1000)
- Using **wrong $\gamma$** (e.g., 1.4 for steam — incorrect)
- Confusing **actual area with ideal area** (apply $C_d$)
- **Mollier chart reading errors** for steam problems
- Forgetting **super-saturation correction** in long steam nozzles (3–5% area addition)

### **Bonus Tip for KTU 2024 Pattern**
- **Part A (3 marks):** Direct definition/short-derivation
- **Part B (14 marks):** Always has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each
- For numericals, **show all steam table lookups** — examiners reward systematic working
- Always **state assumptions** explicitly: isentropic, adiabatic, steady, $\Delta PE = 0$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Throttling Process - Derivation and Simple Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Throttling Process - Derivation and Simple Problems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Throttling Process:** A throttling process is defined as a **steady-flow, adiabatic, irreversible process** in which a fluid is forced through a constricted passage (such as a **throttle valve, porous plug, orifice plate, capillary tube, or any narrow restriction**) resulting in a significant drop in fluid pressure, while **no work is extracted, no heat is transferred, and changes in kinetic and potential energies are negligible**. The defining thermodynamic identity of a throttling process is: $$h_1 = h_2 = \text{constant}$$ This means the throttling process is an **isenthalpic process** (enthalpy remains constant across the restriction). ### Key Assumptions of a Throttling Process > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board-Standard Assumptions:** > 1. **Steady-state** flow conditions (mass flow rate is constant) > 2. **Adiabatic** boundary (Q = 0) — no heat transfer with surroundings > 3. **No shaft work** (W = 0) — no work is extracted or supplied > 4. **Negligible change in kinetic energy** (ΔKE ≈ 0) > 5. **Negligible change in potential energy** (ΔPE ≈ 0) > 6. **Irreversible** process — entropy of the fluid increases (S₂ > S₁) ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **fire hose with a nozzle squeezed partially shut** at the end. The water pressure drops dramatically on the other side of the nozzle, but the water didn't pass through a turbine (no work), didn't sit on a hotplate (no heat transfer), and didn't shoot out at vastly different height. The fluid simply "suffers" a pressure drop through the restriction, much like a crowd of people being forced through a narrow doorway — they come out the other side at lower "pressure" (slower, more dispersed), having lost energy to friction internally. In engineering, this is exactly what happens in a **refrigerant expansion valve** (the cold side of your AC) or the **steam trap of a Rankine cycle** — fluid loses pressure without doing useful external work. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** The pressure drop across a throttle is typically significant, often in the range of **0.1 MPa to 10 MPa** depending on the application, with the standard SI unit being the **Pascal (Pa)** or **Megapascal (MPa)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Breakdown of the Throttling Process — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — The Physical Setup:** A fluid enters a restriction at **inlet state 1** (high pressure P₁, temperature T₁, specific enthalpy h₁) and exits at **outlet state 2** (low pressure P₂, temperature T₂, specific enthalpy h₂). The pressure drop is **P₁ > P₂**. **Step 2 — Why no work is done:** The fluid passes through a passive constriction (valve, plug). There are no rotating blades, pistons, or any moving boundary that could extract or supply **shaft work**. Hence **W = 0**. **Step 3 — Why the process is adiabatic:** The throttling occurs rapidly. The time of residence inside the restriction is so small that there is negligible time for heat to flow in or out of the fluid. Hence **Q = 0** for all practical engineering purposes. **Step 4 — Why ΔKE and ΔPE are neglected:** The cross-sectional area on both sides of the restriction is large compared to the throat, so velocity changes are not appreciable. The elevation change is small. Hence: $$\Delta KE = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2} \approx 0, \quad \Delta PE = g(z_1 - z_2) \approx 0$$ **Step 5 — The "Why" behind h₁ = h₂:** Since the energy entering the control volume must equal the energy leaving (Steady Flow Energy Equation) and all other terms vanish, the enthalpy must be conserved. The internal energy of friction is converted into enthalpy (a slight temperature change occurs, governed by the Joule-Thomson effect). ### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master These Equations — They Appear in Nearly Every Throttling Question on KTU Exams** | # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Units | |---|----------|------------------|-------| | 1 | $h_1 = h_2$ | Isenthalpic identity (defining equation) | kJ/kg | | 2 | $h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2 + w$ | General Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) | kJ/kg | | 3 | $\Delta s = s_2 - s_1 > 0$ | Entropy increases (irreversibility) | kJ/(kg·K) | | 4 | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$ | Joule-Thomson coefficient | K/Pa | | 5 | $dh = 0$ | For pure substance throttling: differential form | kJ/kg | | 6 | $T_2 - T_1 = \mu_{JT}(P_2 - P_1)$ | Temperature change across throttle | K | | 7 | $c_p \, dT - v \, dP = 0$ | Combined ideal gas throttling relation | — | | 8 | $\mu_{JT} = \frac{T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p - v}{c_p}$ | Exact Joule-Thomson coefficient formula | K/Pa | ### Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where Throttling Is Used in Practice:** > - **Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning:** The **expansion valve** in a vapor-compression cycle is a throttle. It drops high-pressure liquid refrigerant to low pressure, causing partial flash evaporation and the cooling effect. > - **Steam Power Plants:** **Cascade throttling** is used in regenerative Rankine cycles (open and closed feedwater heaters). > - **Gas Liquefaction:** The **Linde-Hampson process** uses Joule-Thomson cooling to liquefy gases like oxygen, nitrogen, and natural gas. > - **Pneumatic Systems:** Pressure regulators in compressed air lines operate on throttling. > - **Cryogenics:** Helium and hydrogen liquefaction systems. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1: Proving h₁ = h₂ from the Steady Flow Energy Equation This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU Module 1 examination. The board expects students to start from the SFEE, apply the assumptions, and reach h₁ = h₂ with full clarity. **Starting Point — The General Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE):** For a control volume under steady-flow conditions, the energy balance per unit mass is: $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2 + w$$ where: - $h_1, h_2$ = specific enthalpy at inlet and outlet (kJ/kg) - $V_1, V_2$ = fluid velocities at inlet and outlet (m/s) - $z_1, z_2$ = elevations at inlet and outlet (m) - $q$ = heat transferred per unit mass (kJ/kg) - $w$ = shaft work per unit mass (kJ/kg) **Step 1 — Apply the Adiabatic Condition:** Since the throttling process is adiabatic (no heat transfer to or from the surroundings): $$q = 0$$ Substituting this into the SFEE: $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2 + w$$ **Step 2 — Apply the No-Work Condition:** The fluid passes through a passive restriction (valve or porous plug). No shaft, electrical, or boundary work is involved: $$w = 0$$ The equation reduces to: $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$ **Step 3 — Neglect Kinetic Energy Changes:** The inlet and outlet cross-sections of the throttle are large, so the fluid velocity does not change appreciably: $$\frac{V_1^2}{2} \approx \frac{V_2^2}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2} \approx 0$$ **Step 4 — Neglect Potential Energy Changes:** The throttle is generally horizontal, or the elevation difference is negligible: $$g(z_1 - z_2) \approx 0$$ **Step 5 — Combine All Conditions:** With KE and PE terms dropping out, the equation becomes: $$h_1 = h_2$$ **Conclusion:** $$\boxed{h_1 = h_2 = \text{constant} \quad (\text{Isenthalpic Process})}$$ This proves that a throttling process is an **isenthalpic process** — enthalpy is conserved while pressure drops. --- ### Derivation 2: Joule-Thomson Coefficient for an Ideal Gas For an ideal gas, enthalpy is a function of temperature only: $h = h(T)$. Therefore, along a constant-h line, dh = 0. **Step 1 — Total Differential of Enthalpy:** Enthalpy $h = u + Pv$. For an ideal gas, $u = u(T)$ and $Pv = RT$. So $h$ depends on $T$ alone: $$h = h(T) \Rightarrow dh = c_p \, dT$$ **Step 2 — Using the Identity for Constant h:** For a throttling process, $dh = 0$: $$c_p \, dT = 0 \Rightarrow dT = 0$$ **Step 3 — Joule-Thomson Coefficient:** $$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h = 0 \quad \text{(for an ideal gas)}$$ This is a famous result: an **ideal gas shows no temperature change** upon throttling. --- ### Derivation 3: Joule-Thomson Coefficient from Maxwell Relations This is a **higher-order derivation** that frequently appears in KTU Part B questions. **Step 1 — Start with enthalpy as a function of T and P:** $$h = h(T, P) \Rightarrow dh = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$ **Step 2 — Identify the partial derivatives:** $$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_P = c_p \quad \text{(specific heat at constant pressure)}$$ **Step 3 — Use the relation:** $$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T = v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$$ **Step 4 — Apply the constant-h condition (dh = 0):** $$0 = c_p \, dT + \left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right] dP$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the Joule-Thomson coefficient:** $$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h = \mu_{JT} = \frac{T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v}{c_p}$$ This is the **exact general formula** for the Joule-Thomson coefficient, valid for any real substance. ### Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU | Property | Throttling Process | Isentropic Process (Nozzle) | |----------|--------------------|------------------------------| | Work (W) | **W = 0** | W may be non-zero (turbine) | | Heat (Q) | **Q = 0** | Q = 0 (adiabatic + reversible) | | Enthalpy | **h₁ = h₂** (constant) | h₁ ≠ h₂ | | Entropy | **S₂ > S₁** (increases) | S₁ = S₂ (constant) | | Pressure | **P₁ > P₂** (drops) | P₁ > P₂ (drops) | | Process type | **Irreversible** | **Reversible adiabatic** | | Energy "loss" | Dispersed as internal fluid friction | None — fully converted to KE | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Mermaid Block Diagram: Throttling Process Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR inlet[INLET STATE 1
High Pressure P1
Temperature T1
Enthalpy h1] throttle[RESTRICTION
Valve or Porous Plug
No Work Done
No Heat Transfer] outlet[OUTLET STATE 2
Low Pressure P2
Temperature T2
Enthalpy h2] enthalpy[ENERGY BALANCE
h1 equals h2
Constant Enthalpy] entropy[ENTROPY CHANGE
S2 greater than S1
Irreversible Process] joule[JOULE THOMSON EFFECT
Temperature Changes
mu JT Coefficient] inlet -->|Flow enters restriction| throttle throttle -->|Flow exits at lower pressure| outlet inlet -.->|Isenthalpic identity| enthalpy outlet -.->|Isenthalpic identity| enthalpy inlet -.->|Irreversibility| entropy throttle -.->|Controls temperature drop| joule joule -.->|Governs T2| outlet ``` ### Mermaid State Transition Diagram: Enthalpy-Pressure-Temperature Path ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Start: Inlet State 1] --> condition1{Adiabatic Q equals 0?} condition1 -->|Yes| condition2{Work W equals 0?} condition1 -->|No| notThrottle[NOT a Throttling Process] condition2 -->|Yes| condition3{Delta KE and PE negligible?} condition2 -->|No| notThrottle condition3 -->|Yes| condition4{Steady Flow?} condition3 -->|No| notThrottle condition4 -->|Yes| throttle[VALID THROTTLING PROCESS
h1 equals h2
P1 greater than P2
S2 greater than S1] condition4 -->|No| notThrottle ``` ### Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ControlVolume[CONTROL VOLUME AROUND THROTTLE] direction LR flow1[Mass flow rate m dot constant] --> energyIn[Energy In equals h1] energyIn --> balance[SFEE Application] energyOut[Energy Out equals h2] --> balance balance --> result[Result: h1 equals h2] end subgraph Environment[SYSTEM BOUNDARY] direction TB adiabatic[Adiabatic Walls Q equals 0] noWork[No Shaft Boundary W equals 0] adiabatic ~~~ noWork end ControlVolume --- Environment ``` ### Schematic Description of a Physical Throttling Setup For readers visualizing a real throttling device: - **Upstream Side (Inlet 1):** Wide-bore pipe carrying fluid at high pressure P₁, with cross-section A₁ and velocity V₁. - **Restriction (Throat):** A narrow constriction with cross-section A_throat much smaller than A₁, causing local pressure drop. - **Downstream Side (Outlet 2):** Pipe resumes wide cross-section A₂, where fluid is at lower pressure P₂ and slightly different temperature T₂. - The **pressure profile** along the flow direction shows a sharp drop exactly at the throat and partial recovery downstream. - The **enthalpy** remains **flat** (constant) throughout — this is the unique signature of throttling. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]** > **Define a throttling process and state the assumptions used in its analysis.** **Model Answer:** A throttling process is a steady-flow, adiabatic, irreversible process in which a fluid passes through a restriction (such as a valve, porous plug, or orifice) resulting in a significant pressure drop. The defining characteristic is that the enthalpy remains constant: **h₁ = h₂**. **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state flow 2. Adiabatic (Q = 0) 3. No shaft work (W = 0) 4. Negligible change in kinetic energy 5. Negligible change in potential energy > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **Why is a throttling process considered isenthalpic? Justify.** **Model Answer:** A throttling process is isenthalpic (h₁ = h₂) because the Steady Flow Energy Equation reduces to h₁ = h₂ once we apply the conditions Q = 0, W = 0, ΔKE ≈ 0, and ΔPE ≈ 0. The energy of the fluid entering the restriction must equal the energy leaving; since no work is extracted and no heat is transferred, enthalpy is conserved across the throttle. For an ideal gas, this further implies that temperature remains constant (T₁ = T₂), but for real substances, a temperature change may occur due to the Joule-Thomson effect. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Throttling Problems** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to convert enthalpy units (kJ/kg vs J/kg). Always use **kJ/kg** consistently. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Using the wrong sign for entropy. Throttling **INCREASES** entropy (S₂ > S₁), it is NOT isentropic. > - **Common Mistake #3:** For ideal gas throttling, students wrongly compute a temperature drop. **μ_JT = 0** for an ideal gas. > - **Common Mistake #4:** In steam table problems, students often use the wrong column or interpolate incorrectly. Practice steam table lookups. > - **Mark Loss Pattern:** Skipping intermediate steps in the SFEE derivation. KTU examiners award step-marks — show every substitution. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]` **(a)** Starting from the Steady Flow Energy Equation, derive the condition for an isenthalpic throttling process. State clearly the assumptions involved. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Steam enters a throttling valve at **1.4 MPa** and **200 °C** and leaves at **100 kPa**. Determine: (i) the temperature of the steam at the exit, and (ii) the change in entropy. Use the steam table data provided. **(7 marks)** **Given Steam Table Data (Approximate):** | State | P (kPa) | T (°C) | h (kJ/kg) | s (kJ/kg·K) | |-------|---------|--------|-----------|-------------| | 1 (inlet) | 1400 | 200 | 2803.3 | 6.4975 | | 2 (saturated vapor at 100 kPa) | 100 | 99.63 | 2675.5 | 7.3594 | **Model Solution for (a):** The derivation is shown in full in **Section 3, Derivation 1** of these notes. The key steps are: $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2 + w$$ Apply: $q = 0$, $w = 0$, $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$: $$\Rightarrow h_1 = h_2 \quad \text{(Isenthalpic)}$$ **Assumptions to be stated:** 1. Steady flow 2. Adiabatic (Q = 0) 3. No work (W = 0) 4. ΔKE ≈ 0 5. ΔPE ≈ 0 **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Apply the isenthalpic condition:** $$h_2 = h_1 = 2803.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the state at exit:** At P₂ = 100 kPa, the saturation temperature is 99.63 °C. We compare h₂ with h_g and h_f at 100 kPa: - $h_g$ at 100 kPa = 2675.5 kJ/kg - $h_f$ at 100 kPa = 417.46 kJ/kg Since $h_2 = 2803.3 > h_g = 2675.5$, the exit state is **superheated vapor**. **Step 3 — Find T₂ by interpolation:** At P = 100 kPa, look up superheated steam tables: - At T = 100 °C: h = 2676.2 kJ/kg - At T = 150 °C: h = 2776.4 kJ/kg We need h = 2803.3 kJ/kg. Interpolating: $$T_2 = 100 + \frac{2803.3 - 2676.2}{2776.4 - 2676.2} \times (150 - 100)$$ $$T_2 = 100 + \frac{127.1}{100.2} \times 50$$ $$T_2 = 100 + 63.42 \approx 163.4 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 4 — Find s₂:** For superheated steam at 100 kPa: - At 100 °C: s = 7.3614 kJ/kg·K - At 150 °C: s = 7.6134 kJ/kg·K Interpolating for h = 2803.3: $$s_2 = 7.3614 + \frac{2803.3 - 2676.2}{2776.4 - 2676.2} \times (7.6134 - 7.3614)$$ $$s_2 = 7.3614 + \frac{127.1}{100.2} \times 0.2520$$ $$s_2 = 7.3614 + 0.3196 \approx 7.681 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ **Step 5 — Compute entropy change:** $$\Delta s = s_2 - s_1 = 7.681 - 6.4975 = 1.1835 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ **Final Answers:** - **(i) T₂ ≈ 163.4 °C** (superheated vapor) - **(ii) Δs ≈ 1.184 kJ/kg·K** (positive, confirming irreversibility) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]` **(a)** Define the Joule-Thomson coefficient. Derive its general expression using Maxwell's relations. Show that for an ideal gas, the Joule-Thomson coefficient is zero. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A refrigerant **R-134a** enters the throttling valve of a refrigeration system at **0.8 MPa** as a saturated liquid and exits at **0.2 MPa**. Determine: (i) the enthalpy at exit, (ii) the quality of the refrigerant at exit, and (iii) the change in entropy. **(7 marks)** **Given R-134a Property Data:** | State | P (MPa) | Phase | h (kJ/kg) | s (kJ/kg·K) | |-------|---------|-------|-----------|-------------| | 1 (saturated liquid) | 0.8 | h_f | 95.47 | 0.3656 | | 2a (saturated liquid at 0.2 MPa) | 0.2 | h_f | 38.43 | 0.1556 | | 2b (saturated vapor at 0.2 MPa) | 0.2 | h_g | 244.46 | 0.9377 | | 2c (saturated liquid at 0.8 MPa) | 0.8 | h_f | 95.47 | 0.3656 | **Model Solution for (a):** **Definition:** $$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h = \text{Joule-Thomson Coefficient (K/Pa)}$$ It represents the rate of change of temperature with respect to pressure during a constant-enthalpy (throttling) process. **Derivation (already shown in Section 3, Derivation 3):** $$dh = c_p \, dT + \left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right] dP$$ For throttling: $dh = 0$: $$0 = c_p \, dT + \left[v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right] dP$$ Solving for $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$: $$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h = \frac{T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v}{c_p}$$ **For an Ideal Gas:** For an ideal gas, $Pv = RT$, so $v = \frac{RT}{P}$. Therefore: $$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$$ Substituting: $$\mu_{JT} = \frac{T \cdot \frac{R}{P} - \frac{RT}{P}}{c_p} = \frac{\frac{RT}{P} - \frac{RT}{P}}{c_p} = 0$$ **Conclusion:** $\mu_{JT} = 0$ for an ideal gas — meaning no temperature change occurs during throttling of an ideal gas. **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Apply isenthalpic condition:** $$h_2 = h_1 = h_{f@0.8\,MPa} = 95.47 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Identify phase at exit:** At P₂ = 0.2 MPa: - $h_f = 38.43$ kJ/kg - $h_g = 244.46$ kJ/kg Since $h_f < h_2 < h_g$, the exit state is a **saturated mixture (two-phase)**. **Step 3 — Calculate quality (x₂):** $$h_2 = h_f + x_2 (h_g - h_f)$$ $$95.47 = 38.43 + x_2 (244.46 - 38.43)$$ $$95.47 - 38.43 = x_2 (206.03)$$ $$57.04 = x_2 \times 206.03$$ $$x_2 = \frac{57.04}{206.03} = 0.2769 \approx 0.277$$ **Step 4 — Calculate entropy change:** $$s_2 = s_f + x_2 (s_g - s_f)$$ $$s_2 = 0.1556 + 0.2769 \times (0.9377 - 0.1556)$$ $$s_2 = 0.1556 + 0.2769 \times 0.7821$$ $$s_2 = 0.1556 + 0.2166 = 0.3722 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ $$\Delta s = s_2 - s_1 = 0.3722 - 0.3656 = 0.0066 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ **Final Answers:** - **(i) h₂ = 95.47 kJ/kg** (conserved) - **(ii) x₂ ≈ 0.277 (27.7% quality)** — two-phase mixture - **(iii) Δs ≈ 0.0066 kJ/kg·K** (small positive value — irreversible) --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Throttling Process** - [x] **Core Identity:** Always start problems with $h_1 = h_2$. This is the **golden key**. - [x] **SFEE Origin:** The isenthalpic condition is derived from SFEE with Q = 0, W = 0, ΔKE ≈ 0, ΔPE ≈ 0. - [x] **Energy Terms Drop:** All kinetic energy, potential energy, heat, and work vanish in the SFEE — leaving only enthalpy. - [x] **Irreversibility:** Entropy **increases** (S₂ > S₁). This is the **opposite** of an isentropic process. - [x] **Ideal Gas Special Case:** T₁ = T₂ for ideal gas throttling (μ_JT = 0). - [x] **Real Gas Behavior:** Joule-Thomson coefficient may be **positive** (cooling) or **negative** (heating), depending on the inversion temperature. - [x] **Joule-Thomson Coefficient Formula:** $\mu_{JT} = \frac{T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v}{c_p}$ - [x] **Application — Refrigeration:** Throttling valve in vapor compression cycle causes the **flash gas + cooling effect**. - [x] **Application — Liquefaction:** Linde-Hampson process relies on repeated throttling to liquefy gases. - [x] **Steam Problems:** In KTU, expect **superheated steam throttling** → find exit state via interpolation in steam tables. - [x] **Refrigerant Problems:** Liquid refrigerant flashes to two-phase mixture upon throttling. - [x] **Sign Convention:** Pressure drop is **P₁ > P₂**. Entropy change is **+ve**. Temperature change depends on substance. - [x] **Most Common Exam Trap:** Confusing throttling (isenthalpic) with nozzle flow (isentropic). Keep them separate! - [x] **Marks Strategy:** Show all SFEE substitution steps clearly. KTU examiners reward step-by-step logic. - [x] **Units Check:** Use kJ/kg, kPa, °C, kJ/(kg·K) — be consistent throughout. - [x] **Final Memory Hook:** **"Throttle = Throttle h constant"** → Enthalpy is conserved. Always.

topics.title: Limitations of First Law of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Limitations of First Law of Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** is the principle of conservation of energy applied to thermodynamic systems. For a closed system undergoing a cyclic or non-cyclic process, it is mathematically expressed as:

$$Q = \Delta U + W$$

where $Q$ is the net heat transfer to the system, $\Delta U$ is the change in internal energy, and $W$ is the net work done by the system. The First Law establishes that energy can be neither created nor destroyed — it can only be **transformed** between heat and work while satisfying overall energy balance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCMUT403):** The First Law is treated as a **quantitative** law. It is a **bookkeeping** law of energy — it balances the energy ledger but does not pass judgment on whether a transformation is *physically permissible* in nature.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Honest Accountant

Imagine a bank accountant who records every rupee that enters and leaves your account. At the end of the month, the accountant proudly declares: *"Money in = Money out + Balance change."* This is the **First Law** — energy accounting is perfectly consistent.

Now imagine you ask the accountant: *"Can I withdraw ₹10,000 from my empty savings account if I simultaneously deposit ₹10,000 into my current account?"* The accountant will happily say **YES** because the books balance. But the bank manager immediately says **NO** because there is no actual cash in the savings account.

This is precisely the limitation of the First Law — it is an **excellent accountant but a poor manager**. It guarantees energy balance but is **blind to direction, feasibility, and quality** of energy transformations.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard SI Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Heat transfer | $Q$ | **Joule (J)** |
| Work transfer | $W$ | **Joule (J)** |
| Internal energy change | $\Delta U$ | **Joule (J)** |
| Entropy change | $\Delta S$ | **Joule per Kelvin (J/K)** |
| Temperature | $T$ | **Kelvin (K)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard absolute thermodynamic temperature scale is the **Kelvin scale**. All entropy and Second-Law analyses in KTU papers require $T$ to be in **Kelvin**, never Celsius or Fahrenheit.

---

### 1.4 The Three Principal Limitations — At a Glance

The First Law of Thermodynamics suffers from the following core shortcomings:

- **Limitation 1 — Directional Blindness:** It cannot predict the *natural direction* of a process. Heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold bodies, but the reverse never occurs spontaneously, even though the First Law is satisfied in both directions.
- **Limitation 2 — Quality of Energy:** It treats all forms of energy as equivalent in quantity, ignoring their *grade* or *availability*. A joule of mechanical work is far more "useful" than a joule of low-temperature heat, yet the First Law assigns them equal status.
- **Limitation 3 — Feasibility & Irreversibility:** It cannot distinguish between a *reversible* and an *irreversible* process. It does not explain why certain processes are spontaneous while others require external intervention.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Limitation

#### Limitation 1: The First Law Cannot Indicate the Direction of a Process

Consider two bodies in thermal contact — one at $500\text{ K}$ and another at $300\text{ K}$.

- **Scenario A (Natural):** Heat flows from the hot body to the cold body. Energy lost by hot body = Energy gained by cold body. The First Law is **satisfied**.
- **Scenario B (Reverse):** Heat flows from the cold body to the hot body. The energy balance is again perfectly maintained, and the First Law is **still satisfied**.

But Scenario B **never occurs spontaneously** in nature. The First Law, being purely a conservation equation, cannot tell us which of the two directions is the natural one. This directional information is the domain of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**.

#### Limitation 2: The First Law Cannot Differentiate the Quality of Energy

Energy exists in many forms — mechanical work, electrical energy, heat at high temperature, heat at low temperature. The First Law treats all of these as **quantitatively equivalent**:

$$1\text{ J of mechanical work} \equiv 1\text{ J of low-temperature heat}$$

But in real engineering practice, this equivalence is **false**. Consider the following two cases:

- **Case 1:** A joule of mechanical work can be **fully and completely** converted into a joule of heat.
- **Case 2:** A joule of low-temperature heat **cannot** be fully converted into a joule of mechanical work — a portion must always be rejected to a low-temperature sink.

Thus, mechanical work is a **higher-grade** energy than heat. The First Law is **silent** on this distinction. The concept of **availability** (or *exergy*) is required to quantify this quality difference.

#### Limitation 3: The First Law Cannot Explain Irreversibility

Many real-world processes are **irreversible** — friction, free expansion, heat transfer across a finite temperature difference, mixing of gases, etc. The First Law is satisfied in all these processes, but it cannot tell us:

- Why friction generates heat but heat cannot spontaneously undo friction.
- Why gases expand freely but never spontaneously compress.
- Why a dropped egg shatters but the fragments never reassemble.

These observations are explained by the Second Law through the concept of **entropy increase** in isolated systems.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all critical relations used in KTU board examinations for Module 2. Memorize the units and the sign conventions carefully — they are the **most common sources of mark deduction**.

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Boundary Condition / Domain | Units |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| First Law (closed system) | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | All processes, all systems | J |
| First Law (steady-flow, SFEE) | $h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2 + w$ | Steady-flow devices | J/kg |
| Clausius Inequality | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ | All cyclic processes | J/K |
| Equality holds for | Reversible cycle only | $\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$ | J/K |
| Entropy definition | $dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | Quasi-static reversible path | J/K |
| Entropy of isolated system | $\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$ | Universal — defines irreversibility | J/K |
| Heat engine efficiency (Carnot) | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Reversible, between two reservoirs | dimensionless |
| COP of Refrigerator (Carnot) | $COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible refrigeration cycle | dimensionless |
| COP of Heat Pump (Carnot) | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_H}$ *(corrected: $\frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$)* | Reversible heat pump cycle | dimensionless |
| Availability (closed system) | $\phi = (u - u_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + p_0(v - v_0)$ | Dead-state referenced | J/kg |

> [!WARNING]
> A very common KTU pitfall: in the **Carnot Heat Pump** formula, students frequently write $T_H$ twice in the denominator. The correct form is $\frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$, **not** $\frac{T_H}{T_H - T_H}$. Board examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this slip.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
