The recognition of First Law limitations is the **conceptual foundation** of every modern energy-conversion device and energy-policy decision:

- **Thermal Power Plants:** Engineers must accept that only a fraction of fuel energy can be converted to work — the rest must be rejected as waste heat. This fraction is governed by the **Carnot efficiency**, a Second-Law outcome.
- **Refrigeration and Air-Conditioning:** The performance of refrigerators is rated by the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, a Second-Law metric that quantifies "useful cooling per unit work input."
- **Energy Auditing and Sustainability:** Modern energy policy uses the concept of **exergy** to identify where energy is being *destroyed* (rendered unavailable) in industrial processes — this directly stems from First Law limitations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the First Law Alone is Insufficient — A Demonstrative Proof

We will now demonstrate, in rigorous algebraic form, that the First Law **cannot** adjudicate the feasibility of a process. Consider two reservoirs:

- A high-temperature reservoir at $T_H = 500\text{ K}$.
- A low-temperature reservoir at $T_L = 300\text{ K}$.

**Step 1 — Define the energy transfers for the natural process (heat flows from hot to cold):**

Let an amount of heat $Q$ be transferred from the hot reservoir to the cold reservoir. By the First Law applied to the *combined* system (hot + cold reservoir), the total energy is conserved:

$$
\Delta U_{total} = -Q + Q = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_{in} = E_{out}
$$

**Step 2 — Now examine the reverse process (heat flows from cold to hot), which the First Law alone cannot rule out:**

The same amount of heat $Q$ is transferred from the cold reservoir at $300\text{ K}$ to the hot reservoir at $500\text{ K}$. Energy balance:

$$
\Delta U_{total} = -Q + Q = 0
$$

The energy equation is **identically satisfied**, and yet:

> **This process is impossible in nature.** It would violate the Clausius statement of the Second Law.

**Step 3 — Apply the Clausius Inequality to discriminate between the two scenarios:**

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0
$$

For the **natural process** (heat $Q$ leaving $T_H$ and entering $T_L$):

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = -\frac{Q}{T_H} + \frac{Q}{T_L} = Q\left(\frac{1}{T_L} - \frac{1}{T_H}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Since } T_H > T_L \Rightarrow \frac{1}{T_L} > \frac{1}{T_H} \Rightarrow \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} > 0
$$

For the **reverse process** (heat $Q$ leaving $T_L$ and entering $T_H$):

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = -\frac{Q}{T_L} + \frac{Q}{T_H} = Q\left(\frac{1}{T_H} - \frac{1}{T_L}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Since } T_H > T_L \Rightarrow \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result:**

The Clausius inequality $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ is **violated** by the natural process and **satisfied** by the reverse... wait — this is precisely the subtlety. Let us restate correctly: the **real** physical process is the natural one (hot to cold), and it gives a **positive** value. The correct statement of the Second Law in entropy form is:

$$
\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0
$$

Computing entropy change of the isolated universe:

For natural process:

$$
\Delta S_{univ} = -\frac{Q}{T_H} + \frac{Q}{T_L} = Q \left( \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H \cdot T_L} \right) > 0
$$

For reverse process:

$$
\Delta S_{univ} = \frac{Q}{T_H} - \frac{Q}{T_L} = -Q \left( \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H \cdot T_L} \right) < 0
$$

Since entropy of an isolated system **cannot decrease**, the reverse process is **forbidden**. The First Law alone gave us no information; the Second Law clearly distinguishes.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — First Law vs. Second Law Capabilities

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **high-yield KTU revision table** for Part A questions. Examiners frequently ask: *"State two limitations of the First Law"* — this table gives the precise wording.

| Aspect of Energy Process | First Law | Second Law |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Conservation of energy | ✔ Validates | ✔ Implicitly satisfied |
| Direction of natural process | ✘ Cannot predict | ✔ Predicts via entropy |
| Quality / grade of energy | ✘ Treats all as equal | ✔ Differentiates via exergy |
| Feasibility of a process | ✘ Cannot determine | ✔ Determines via $\Delta S \geq 0$ |
| Maximum possible efficiency | ✘ No upper bound given | ✔ Provides Carnot bound |
| Conversion of heat to work | ✘ Permits 100 % | ✔ Limits via Carnot efficiency |
| Distinguishes reversible / irreversible | ✘ Cannot | ✔ Yes, via entropy generation |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Illustration: The Quality of Energy

Consider two energy reservoirs, each containing $1000\text{ J}$ of energy:

- **Reservoir A:** Mechanical work reservoir (e.g., a flywheel).
- **Reservoir B:** Low-temperature heat reservoir at $T = 300\text{ K}$.

By the First Law, both contain *equivalent* energy. But the maximum work extractable from each (relative to a dead state at $T_0 = 300\text{ K}$) is:

For **Reservoir A** (work reservoir):

$$
W_{max,A} = 1000\text{ J}
$$

All mechanical work is fully convertible.

For **Reservoir B** (heat reservoir at $T$):

$$
W_{max,B} = Q \left( 1 - \frac{T_0}{T} \right) = 1000 \left( 1 - \frac{300}{300} \right) = 0\text{ J}
$$

At the dead-state temperature, **no work can be extracted** from heat at $T = T_0$. This numerical demonstration makes the First Law's blindness to energy quality **unmistakably evident**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU examinations, when a numerical problem states *"reservoir at $T = T_0$"*, the maximum work extractable is **zero** regardless of the heat quantity. This is a frequently tested edge case.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Energy Analysis Framework

The following diagram illustrates how the **First Law** and **Second Law** relate to each other and what each contributes to a complete thermodynamic analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Energy Analysis of a Process] --> nodeB{First Law Applied}
    nodeA --> nodeC{Second Law Applied}
    
    nodeB --> nodeD[Energy Balance Check: Q equals delta U plus W]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Confirms Quantity of Energy is Conserved]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Cannot Answer: Which Direction is Natural?]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Cannot Answer: Is Process Feasible?]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Cannot Answer: What is Energy Quality?]
    
    nodeC --> nodeI[Entropy Balance: delta S greater than or equal zero]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Determines Direction of Spontaneous Process]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Quantifies Energy Degradation]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Sets Carnot Efficiency Limits]
    
    nodeF --> nodeM[Answer Provided by Second Law]
    nodeG --> nodeM
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeN[Complete Thermodynamic Understanding]
    nodeK --> nodeN
    nodeL --> nodeN
    
    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeN fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeF fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style nodeG fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style nodeH fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Schematic Flowchart: Limitations and Their Remedies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[First Law Limitation Identified] --> node2{Type of Gap}
    
    node2 -->|Direction of Process| node3[Apply Second Law - Clausius Statement]
    node2 -->|Quality of Energy| node4[Apply Availability or Exergy Analysis]
    node2 -->|Feasibility Check| node5[Apply Entropy Principle - delta S greater than or equal zero]
    
    node3 --> node6[Resolves: Direction of Spontaneous Process]
    node4 --> node7[Resolves: Maximum Work Potential]
    node5 --> node8[Resolves: Process Permissibility]
    
    node6 --> node9[Complete Engineering Analysis]
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node9
    
    style node1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style node9 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Energy Degradation Cascade (Conceptual Architecture)

The following block diagram maps how useful energy *degrades* through real processes — a phenomenon entirely invisible to the First Law but central to Second-Law analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[High Grade Energy - Mechanical Work or High T Heat] --> nodeB[Energy Conversion Device - Engine or Turbine]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Process Type}
    
    nodeC -->|Reversible| nodeD[Useful Work Out plus Rejected Heat]
    nodeC -->|Irreversible| nodeE[Useful Work Out plus Larger Rejected Heat plus Internal Losses]
    
    nodeD --> nodeF[Total Energy Conserved - First Law Satisfied]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    
    nodeF --> nodeG[But Available Work Decreased - Second Law Identifies Loss]
    
    nodeG --> nodeH[Low Grade Energy - Heat at Temperature T zero]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Dead State - No Further Work Extraction Possible]
    
    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeI fill:#696969,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style nodeG fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for KTU students:** In all three Mermaid diagrams above, the **yellow nodes** represent the input or starting condition, the **green nodes** represent the final resolved understanding, and the **pink or red nodes** highlight the First Law's blind spots. This colour convention matches the standard KTU textbook pedagogy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

*"State any three limitations of the First Law of Thermodynamics."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The First Law of Thermodynamics, although a rigorous statement of energy conservation, has the following three principal limitations:

1. **No indication of direction:** The First Law cannot tell us whether a process will proceed spontaneously in the forward or reverse direction. For example, it allows both heat flow from hot to cold *and* cold to hot as long as total energy is balanced.
2. **No differentiation of energy quality:** It treats mechanical work and heat as equivalent in magnitude, ignoring that work is a higher-grade, fully convertible form of energy, while heat is a lower-grade, partially convertible form.
3. **No feasibility criterion:** It cannot distinguish a feasible process from an impossible one. For instance, it cannot rule out a 100 %-efficient heat engine, although such a device is impossible by the Second Law.

*(1 mark for each correctly stated limitation — full marks for any three of the above.)*

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

*"Differentiate between the First Law and Second Law of Thermodynamics in terms of what they predict about a thermodynamic process."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | First Law | Second Law |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Primary statement | Energy is conserved | Entropy of isolated system increases |
| Predicts energy quantity | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes |
| Predicts direction of process | ✘ No | ✔ Yes |
| Predicts feasibility | ✘ No | ✔ Yes |
| Differentiates energy quality | ✘ No | ✔ Yes (via exergy) |

*(1 mark for the comparison table; 1 mark for direction-related point; 1 mark for feasibility or quality point.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three major limitations of the First Law of Thermodynamics** with suitable examples for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heat engine operates between a high-temperature reservoir at $T_H = 800\text{ K}$ and a low-temperature reservoir at $T_L = 300\text{ K}$. The engine receives $Q_H = 1000\text{ J}$ of heat from the hot reservoir. **(7 marks)**

Calculate:
  (i) The maximum possible (Carnot) efficiency of the engine.
  (ii) The maximum work output.
  (iii) The heat rejected to the low-temperature reservoir at maximum efficiency.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Detailed Limitations (7 marks)**

The First Law of Thermodynamics is mathematically expressed for a closed system as:

$$
Q = \Delta U + W
$$

It is a **quantitative** conservation principle. Despite being mathematically rigorous, it suffers from three major limitations:

**Limitation 1 — No Indication of Direction (2 marks):**

The First Law does not specify the *natural direction* in which a process will proceed. Consider two bodies at $500\text{ K}$ and $300\text{ K}$ in thermal contact. Heat may flow from hot to cold (the natural direction), or hypothetically from cold to hot — both scenarios satisfy the energy balance. The First Law cannot adjudicate between them.

**Limitation 2 — No Distinction of Energy Quality (2 marks):**

The First Law treats all energy forms as equivalent. But a joule of mechanical work is **fully convertible** to heat, whereas a joule of low-temperature heat cannot be fully converted to work. The First Law ignores the *grade* or *availability* of energy. The concept of **exergy** is required to quantify this.

**Limitation 3 — No Feasibility Criterion (3 marks):**

The First Law cannot identify whether a process is *physically possible*. A hypothetical 100 %-efficient heat engine (one that converts all supplied heat to work with no rejection) satisfies the First Law but is impossible by the Second Law. The Clausius inequality $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ and the entropy principle $\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$ are needed to determine feasibility.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Given data:**
- $T_H = 800\text{ K}$
- $T_L = 300\text{ K}$
- $Q_H = 1000\text{ J}$

**(i) Maximum (Carnot) Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 62.5\%}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Work Output (2.5 marks):**

$$
W_{max} = \eta_{Carnot} \times Q_H
$$

$$
W_{max} = 0.625 \times 1000 = 625\text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{max} = 625\text{ J}}
$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected at Maximum Efficiency (2.5 marks):**

By the First Law applied to the cyclic heat engine:

$$
Q_H = W_{max} + Q_L
$$

$$
Q_L = Q_H - W_{max} = 1000 - 625 = 375\text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_L = 375\text{ J}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** *"The First Law of Thermodynamics is necessary but not sufficient for the complete analysis of a thermodynamic process."* Justify this statement with **at least four arguments**, and explain how the Second Law complements the First Law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reversible refrigeration cycle operates between $T_L = 270\text{ K}$ and $T_H = 300\text{ K}$. The refrigerator removes $Q_L = 1350\text{ J}$ of heat from the cold space per cycle. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
  (i) The Coefficient of Performance (COP) of the refrigerator.
  (ii) The work input required per cycle.
  (iii) The heat rejected to the hot space.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Justification (7 marks)**

The statement is correct — the First Law is *necessary but not sufficient*. Justification:

**Argument 1 — Directional Incompleteness (2 marks):**

The First Law is satisfied in any energy transfer regardless of natural direction. To uniquely specify the *natural* direction, the **Clausius statement** of the Second Law is required: *"Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a colder to a hotter body."*

**Argument 2 — Energy Quality Discrimination (2 marks):**

The First Law treats work and heat as numerical equals. The Second Law, through the **Carnot principle**, establishes that work can be fully converted to heat, but only a *fraction* of heat can be converted to work — this fraction is bounded by $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L / T_H$.

**Argument 3 — Feasibility Criterion (1.5 marks):**

The First Law cannot rule out impossible cycles (e.g., a perpetual motion machine of the second kind). The Second Law, via entropy non-decrease $\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$, eliminates such cycles.

**Argument 4 — Predicting Performance Limits (1.5 marks):**

The First Law provides no upper bound on engine efficiency. The Second Law sets the **maximum possible efficiency** at the Carnot value, which no real engine can exceed.

**Conclusion:** The First Law is *necessary* for energy accounting; the Second Law is *sufficient* to complete the analysis by adding directional, qualitative, and feasibility information.

---

#### **Part (b) — Refrigeration Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given data:**
- $T_L = 270\text{ K}$
- $T_H = 300\text{ K}$
- $Q_L = 1350\text{ J}$ per cycle

**(i) COP of the Refrigerator (2.5 marks):**

For a reversible (Carnot) refrigerator:

$$
COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

Substituting:

$$
COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{270}{300 - 270} = \frac{270}{30} = 9
$$

$$
\boxed{COP_R = 9}
$$

**(ii) Work Input per Cycle (2.5 marks):**

By definition:

$$
COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{in}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
W_{in} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{1350}{9} = 150\text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{in} = 150\text{ J}}
$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected to Hot Space (2 marks):**

By the First Law applied to the cycle:

$$
Q_H = Q_L + W_{in} = 1350 + 150 = 1500\text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_H = 1500\text{ J}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Many students write $W = Q + \Delta U$ instead of $Q = \Delta U + W$. This single error costs **2 full marks** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Temperature Units:** Always convert Celsius to Kelvin before applying any Second-Law or entropy formula. Submitting answers with $T$ in Celsius leads to **incorrect Carnot efficiency** and an immediate **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **First-Law-Only Mistakes:** Students often compute efficiency using only the First Law (e.g., $\eta = W / Q_H$) without checking the Carnot bound. KTU examiners expect you to **compare** the actual efficiency with the Carnot efficiency as a Second-Law consistency check.
> 4. **Work vs. Heat in COP:** Forgetting that the refrigerator COP uses $Q_L / W$ (not $Q_H / W$) is a **classic 1-mark loss** in Part B questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✔ **The First Law is a quantitative conservation law** — it balances the energy books but says nothing about feasibility or direction.
- ✔ **Three principal limitations:** (1) Cannot indicate direction of natural process, (2) Cannot differentiate energy quality, (3) Cannot adjudicate feasibility.
- ✔ **The Second Law complements the First Law** by adding the entropy principle $\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$.
- ✔ **The Clausius inequality** $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ is the formal test that distinguishes feasible from impossible cyclic processes.
- ✔ **Carnot efficiency** $\eta = 1 - T_L / T_H$ is the absolute upper bound for any heat engine operating between two reservoirs — this is a Second-Law result, not a First-Law one.
- ✔ **Exergy / availability** quantifies the *quality* of energy — mechanical work has maximum availability, while heat at the dead-state temperature $T_0$ has zero availability.
- ✔ **Always use Kelvin** for all Second-Law calculations involving $T$ in any formula.
- ✔ **Sign convention:** $Q$ into the system is positive, $W$ done *by* the system is positive (KTU standard).
- ✔ **High-yield KTU keywords** that earn full marks in Part A: *"conservation of energy"*, *"direction of natural process"*, *"quality or grade of energy"*, *"feasibility criterion"*, *"Clausius statement"*, *"entropy principle"*.
- ✔ **Numerical tip:** When a problem gives only $Q_H$ and asks for $W_{max}$ or $Q_L$, the Carnot efficiency must be computed *first* — examiners award **partial credit** for the efficiency step alone even if the subsequent calculation is flawed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kelvin-Planck and Clausius Statements and Their Equivalence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kelvin-Planck and Clausius Statements and Their Equivalence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Second Law of Thermodynamics — Context

The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** governs the **direction** of natural processes and establishes the fundamental limits of energy conversion. While the First Law confirms that energy is conserved (you cannot win), the Second Law states that you cannot even **break even** in a cyclic heat engine because some energy must always be rejected to a low-temperature sink.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Second Law is articulated through two classical statements — **Kelvin-Planck** and **Clausius**. These are **not** independent laws; they are **logically equivalent** statements of the same fundamental physical truth.

---

### 1.2 Kelvin-Planck Statement (KP Statement)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

In simpler engineering terms:

> **No cyclic heat engine can convert all the heat absorbed from a single heat reservoir entirely into work.**

---

### 1.3 Clausius Statement (CS Statement)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

In simpler engineering terms:

> **No refrigeration / heat pump cycle can operate without any external work input**, i.e., heat cannot spontaneously flow from a cold body to a hot body.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Kelvin-Planck Analogy — "The Impossible Single-Source Engine":**
Imagine a ship sailing on the ocean. The Kelvin-Planck statement is equivalent to claiming that you cannot build a ship engine that simply extracts heat from the ocean (single reservoir) and converts 100% of it into useful work to propel the ship. Some heat MUST be dumped back into the ocean (a colder sink) — the ocean itself acts as both source and sink. Without two reservoirs at different temperatures, no net work can be produced.

**Clausius Analogy — "The Self-Refrigerating Cup":**
Imagine placing a hot cup of coffee next to a cold glass of water inside an insulated box. The Clausius statement says heat will **never spontaneously** transfer from the cold water to the hot coffee, making the coffee hotter and water colder, with no external work. Nature **always** requires work input (like a refrigerator's compressor) to push heat "uphill" from cold to hot.

---

### 1.5 PMM2 — Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A device that violates the Kelvin-Planck statement is called a **PMM2 (Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind)**. It would produce continuous work by cooling a single thermal reservoir, **without violating the First Law** (energy is still conserved — it is just being extracted from the reservoir). Such a machine is impossible according to the Second Law.

---

### 1.6 Why Two Statements?

The existence of two equivalent statements arises because the Second Law has **two distinct physical interpretations**:
1. **Energy Quality / Conversion Limit** → Kelvin-Planck
2. **Spontaneous Direction of Heat Flow** → Clausius

Both are manifestations of the same underlying principle: the **entropy of the universe always increases** (or remains constant for reversible processes).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logic Flow of the Kelvin-Planck Statement

Let us decompose the KP statement into its logical constituents:

- **Step 1:** A heat engine operates on a **cycle** (it returns to its initial state, hence $\Delta U = 0$).
- **Step 2:** It interacts with a **single thermal reservoir** at temperature $T_H$.
- **Step 3:** The **only** energy interaction permitted is heat transfer $Q_H$ from the reservoir.
- **Step 4:** The **only** energy output permitted is work $W_{net}$.
- **Step 5:** Energy balance (First Law) demands: $W_{net} = Q_H$ if no other heat interaction occurs.
- **Step 6:** Second Law (KP) **forbids** this. Therefore, an additional heat rejection $Q_L$ to a **second** lower-temperature reservoir is **mandatory**.

Hence: $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$, where $Q_L > 0$.

---

### 2.2 Logic Flow of the Clausius Statement

- **Step 1:** A refrigeration / heat pump cycle operates in a cycle ($\Delta U = 0$).
- **Step 2:** It draws heat $Q_L$ from a low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$.
- **Step 3:** It rejects heat $Q_H$ to a high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$ (where $T_H > T_L$).
- **Step 4:** If **no work input** is allowed ($W_{net,in} = 0$), then by First Law $Q_H = Q_L$.
- **Step 5:** Second Law (Clausius) **forbids** spontaneous heat flow from cold to hot with no work. Hence $W_{net,in} > 0$ is mandatory.
- **Step 6:** Therefore: $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,in}$.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kelvin-Planck violation (PMM2) | $W_{net} = Q_H$ (i.e., $Q_L = 0$) | kJ or J | $Q_L \geq 0$ (must reject some heat) |
| Clausius violation | $Q_H = Q_L$ with $W_{net,in} = 0$ | kJ or J | $W_{net,in} \geq 0$ (must input work) |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | dimensionless | $\eta_{th} < 1$ always for KP |
| COP of Refrigerator | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$ | dimensionless | $COP_R > 0$ requires work |
| COP of Heat Pump | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$ | dimensionless | $COP_{HP} > 1$ |
| Carnot (Reversible) Efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | dimensionless | Maximum possible $\eta_{th}$ |
| Entropy statement (combined) | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} \leq 0$ | kJ/K | Equality for reversible cycle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Board Rule:** When writing either statement in an exam, you **must** explicitly use the phrase *"operates in a cycle"* and *"produces no other effect"*. Omitting these phrases is a guaranteed mark deduction.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plant Design:** The KP statement sets the **theoretical upper limit** of efficiency for any heat engine (Carnot efficiency). Engineers use this benchmark to evaluate real plant performance.
- **Refrigeration and HVAC:** The Clausius statement justifies why every refrigerator **must consume electrical work** to maintain a cold interior.
- **Entropy-Based Design:** The equivalence of the two statements allows engineers to use the **Clausius Inequality** ($\oint \delta Q / T \leq 0$) as a unified analytical tool for any cycle.
- **Feasibility Screening:** Any proposed thermodynamic cycle can be rejected *a priori* if it violates either the KP or CS statement — saving enormous R&D cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Proof of Equivalence: KP Violation $\Leftrightarrow$ CS Violation

We must demonstrate that **a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement implies a violation of the Clausius statement, and vice versa**. We accomplish this using a **composite cycle argument** (a standard KTU board technique).

---

### 3.2 PART A: KP Violation $\Rightarrow$ CS Violation

**Given (Assumption for Proof by Contradiction):**
Suppose there exists a hypothetical engine **E1** that violates the Kelvin-Planck statement. E1 operates in a cycle, exchanges heat $Q_H$ with a single reservoir at $T_H$, and converts it entirely into work $W = Q_H$ (no heat rejection).

**Construction:**
Combine E1 with a **reversible refrigerator R** (which obeys the Second Law) operating between the same two reservoirs at $T_H$ and $T_L$ ($T_H > T_L$). The refrigerator receives work input $W_R$ and transfers heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir and $Q_H'$ to the hot reservoir.

**Step-by-Step Coupling:**

- Step 1: Allow E1 to drive the refrigerator R. So we set $W_R = W = Q_H$ (work output of E1 = work input of R).
- Step 2: Apply energy balance to refrigerator R (First Law, cyclic):

$$
Q_H' = Q_L + W_R
$$

- Step 3: Substitute $W_R = Q_H$:

$$
Q_H' = Q_L + Q_H
$$

- Step 4: For the **composite device** (E1 + R considered together), the net heat interaction with the hot reservoir at $T_H$ is:

$$
Q_{H,net} = Q_H' - Q_H = (Q_L + Q_H) - Q_H = Q_L
$$

- Step 5: The net work interaction of the composite device is:

$$
W_{net,composite} = W - W_R = Q_H - Q_H = 0
$$

- Step 6: **Conclusion:** The composite device:
  - Extracts heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir at $T_L$.
  - Rejects heat $Q_L$ (equal amount) to the hot reservoir at $T_H$.
  - Produces **no net work**.
  - Has **no other effect** on the surroundings.

This composite device is **exactly a Clausius statement violator** — it transfers heat from cold to hot with no work input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic Validated:** If KP is violated, then CS **must** also be violated. Therefore, KP $\Rightarrow$ CS.

---

### 3.3 PART B: CS Violation $\Rightarrow$ KP Violation

**Given (Assumption for Proof by Contradiction):**
Suppose there exists a hypothetical refrigerator **R1** that violates the Clausius statement. R1 transfers heat $Q$ from a cold reservoir at $T_L$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$ **without any work input**.

**Construction:**
Combine R1 with a **reversible heat engine E** (which obeys the Second Law) operating between the same two reservoirs. The engine E absorbs heat $Q_H$ from $T_H$, produces work $W$, and rejects heat $Q_L$ to $T_L$.

**Step-by-Step Coupling:**

- Step 1: Choose the engine E such that it rejects exactly $Q$ to the cold reservoir (matching R1's intake):

$$
Q_L = Q
$$

- Step 2: Work output of engine E (by First Law, cyclic):

$$
W = Q_H - Q_L = Q_H - Q
$$

- Step 3: Net heat interaction of composite device (E + R1) with the hot reservoir:

$$
Q_{H,net} = Q_H - Q \quad \text{(since R1 sends } Q \text{ to hot, engine E extracts } Q_H \text{)}
$$

- Step 4: Net heat interaction with the cold reservoir:

$$
Q_{L,net} = Q - Q = 0
$$

- Step 5: Net work output of the composite device:

$$
W_{net} = W = Q_H - Q
$$

- Step 6: Applying energy balance to the composite cycle:

$$
Q_{H,net} = W_{net} \implies Q_H - Q = Q_H - Q \quad \checkmark
$$

- Step 7: **Conclusion:** The composite device:
  - Interacts with **only one reservoir** — the hot reservoir at $T_H$.
  - Extracts net heat $Q_{H,net} = Q_H - Q$ from it.
  - Converts it **entirely** into net work $W_{net} = Q_H - Q$.
  - Has **no other effect** on the surroundings.

This composite device is **exactly a Kelvin-Planck statement violator** — a PMM2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic Validated:** If CS is violated, then KP **must** also be violated. Therefore, CS $\Rightarrow$ KP.

---

### 3.4 Combined Equivalence Conclusion

Since we have proven:
- KP violation $\Rightarrow$ CS violation
- CS violation $\Rightarrow$ KP violation

By the principle of **logical equivalence**:
$$
\boxed{\text{KP Statement} \iff \text{Clausius Statement}}
$$

Both statements are therefore two faces of the **same underlying physical law** — the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Quick Comparison

| Feature | Kelvin-Planck Statement | Clausius Statement |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Subject | Heat Engine | Refrigerator / Heat Pump |
| Forbidden Action | 100% conversion of $Q_H \to W$ | Spontaneous $Q_L \to Q_H$ with $W=0$ |
| Number of Reservoirs Required | **Two** (source + sink) | **Two** (cold + hot) |
| Violator Machine Name | **PMM2** | Self-acting Refrigerator |
| Associated Engine Metric | $\eta_{th} < 1$ | $COP_R$ finite (requires work) |
| Engineering Relevance | Power cycles, power plants | Refrigeration, cryogenics, heat pumps |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic Block Diagram: Equivalence Proof Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **logical flow** of the equivalence proof — how a violation of one statement, when combined with a compliant device, produces a violation of the other.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Assumption A: KP Violator Exists"] --> B["Engine E1 extracts QH from TH only, produces W = QH"]
    B --> C["Couple E1 with Reversible Refrigerator R"]
    C --> D["Refrigerator R uses work W to pump QL from TL to TH"]
    D --> E["Composite Net Heat at TH = QL"]
    E --> F["Composite Net Work = 0"]
    F --> G["Composite Device: Transfers QL from Cold to Hot with W = 0"]
    G --> H["Result: Clausius Statement Violated"]

    H2["Assumption B: Clausius Violator Exists"] --> I["Refrigerator R1 transfers Q from TL to TH with W = 0"]
    I --> J["Couple R1 with Reversible Heat Engine E"]
    J --> K["Engine E extracts QH from TH, rejects Q to TL, produces W"]
    K --> L["Composite Net Heat at TL = 0"]
    L --> M["Composite Net Work = QH - Q"]
    M --> N["Composite Device: Single reservoir TH yields net work W"]
    N --> O["Result: Kelvin-Planck Statement Violated (PMM2)"]

    H -.Equivalence.-> O
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Devices Implied by Each Statement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KP_Engine["Kelvin-Planck Domain: Heat Engine"]
        TH1["Hot Reservoir TH"] -->|"QH"| Engine1["Cyclic Heat Engine"]
        Engine1 -->|"Wnet"| WorkOut["Work Output"]
        Engine1 -->|"QL"| TL1["Cold Reservoir TL"]
    end

    subgraph CS_Refrig["Clausius Domain: Refrigerator"]
        TL2["Cold Reservoir TL"] -->|"QL"| Refrig["Cyclic Refrigerator"]
        WorkIn["Work Input Wnet"] --> Refrig
        Refrig -->|"QH"| TH2["Hot Reservoir TH"]
    end

    subgraph PMM2["Forbidden Device"]
        TH3["Single Reservoir TH"] -->|"QH"| PMM["PMM2 Hypothetical"]
        PMM -->|"Wnet = QH"| Impossible["100% Conversion IMPOSSIBLE"]
    end
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Identifying Violations in Exam Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Exam Problem: Heat Device Analysis"] --> Q1{"Does the device\noperate in a cycle?"}
    Q1 -->|No| A1["Not a cyclic device - Apply First Law directly"]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Type of device?"}
    Q2 -->|Heat Engine| Q3{"Is QL = 0 ?"}
    Q2 -->|Refrigerator| Q4{"Is Wnet,in = 0 ?"}
    Q3 -->|Yes| V1["KP VIOLATION - PMM2 - Impossible"]
    Q3 -->|No| OK1["Compliant with KP - Check efficiency"]
    Q4 -->|Yes| V2["Clausius VIOLATION - Impossible"]
    Q4 -->|No| OK2["Compliant with Clausius - Check COP"]
    OK1 --> End["Valid Engine - Proceed"]
    OK2 --> End2["Valid Refrigerator - Proceed"]
    V1 --> End3["Reject Hypothesis"]
    V2 --> End4["Reject Hypothesis"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
*State the Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Why is a 100% efficient heat engine impossible?*

**Model Answer:**

The Kelvin-Planck statement states that *"it is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single thermal reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

A 100% efficient heat engine would imply that all heat $Q_H$ absorbed from a single source is fully converted into work $W$ (i.e., $Q_L = 0$ and $\eta_{th} = 1$). This constitutes a PMM2 and is forbidden by the Second Law. Hence, a cyclic heat engine **must reject some heat** $Q_L > 0$ to a lower-temperature sink, making its thermal efficiency strictly less than unity: $\eta_{th} = 1 - Q_L/Q_H < 1$.

*(Self-assessment: 3 marks — 1 mark statement, 1 mark impossibility reason, 1 mark efficiency expression.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*State the Clausius statement. Mention the practical impossibility it conveys.*

**Model Answer:**

The Clausius statement states that *"it is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

Practically, this conveys that **heat cannot spontaneously flow from a cold body to a hot body without external work input**. Therefore, all refrigerators, air-conditioners, and heat pumps require a work input (e.g., compressor work) to operate. A "self-acting" refrigerator that transfers heat from cold to hot with zero work input is a Clausius violator and is impossible.

*(Self-assessment: 3 marks — 1 mark statement, 1 mark practical implication, 1 mark example.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** State both the Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove rigorously that a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement leads to a violation of the Clausius statement. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Kelvin-Planck Statement:**
*"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single thermal reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

In symbolic form, for a cyclic heat engine:
$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} < 1 \quad \text{(equality forbidden)}
$$

**Clausius Statement:**
*"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

In symbolic form, for a cyclic refrigerator:
$$
W_{net,in} > 0 \quad \text{(required to move heat from cold to hot)}
$$

Both statements address different physical aspects of the Second Law but are logically equivalent.

*(Self-assessment: 7 marks — 3 marks for KP, 3 marks for CS, 1 mark symbolic representation.)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Assumption**
Assume a hypothetical engine **E1** violates the Kelvin-Planck statement. Therefore, E1 extracts heat $Q_H$ from a single reservoir at $T_H$ and produces work $W = Q_H$ in a cyclic process.

**Step 2: Construct Composite Device**
Combine E1 with a reversible refrigerator **R** operating between $T_H$ (hot) and $T_L$ (cold), with $T_H > T_L$. Drive R using the work output of E1, so $W_R = W = Q_H$.

**Step 3: Apply First Law to Refrigerator R**
For R operating in a cycle:
$$
Q_H' = Q_L + W_R = Q_L + Q_H
$$

**Step 4: Compute Net Effects of Composite Device**

Net heat rejected to hot reservoir at $T_H$:
$$
Q_{H,net} = Q_H' - Q_H = (Q_L + Q_H) - Q_H = Q_L
$$

Net work interaction of composite device:
$$
W_{net} = W - W_R = Q_H - Q_H = 0
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**
The composite device transfers heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir to the hot reservoir at $T_H$, without any net work input, and produces no other effect on the surroundings.

**Step 6: Conclusion**
This is precisely a Clausius statement violation. Hence, a Kelvin-Planck violation implies a Clausius violation. **Q.E.D.**

*(Self-assessment: 7 marks — 1 mark assumption, 1 mark composite setup, 2 marks energy balance, 1 mark net effect calculation, 1 mark interpretation, 1 mark conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Define a Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2). Explain why it is impossible. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from a Clausius statement violation, prove that a Kelvin-Planck violation must also exist. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

A **PMM2** is a hypothetical cyclic device that:
- Operates in a cycle (satisfies First Law: $\Delta U = 0$).
- Interacts with a **single** thermal reservoir.
- Converts heat extracted from this reservoir **entirely** into work.
- Produces no other effect (no heat rejection, no net entropy creation).

Symbolically, PMM2 would satisfy:
$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1
$$

**Why Impossible:**
- It does **not violate** the First Law (energy is conserved).
- It **does violate** the Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law.
- It would permit 100% conversion of heat into work, which contradicts the empirical observation that heat naturally flows from hot to cold and that work is required to extract useful energy.
- It would also permit the construction of a device that cools a single reservoir indefinitely, producing continuous work — contradicting observed natural direction of processes.

*(Self-assessment: 7 marks — 2 marks definition, 2 marks symbolic representation, 3 marks impossibility reasons.)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Assumption**
Assume a hypothetical refrigerator **R1** violates the Clausius statement. It transfers heat $Q$ from cold reservoir at $T_L$ to hot reservoir at $T_H$ in a cycle, with **no work input** ($W = 0$).

**Step 2: Construct Composite Device**
Combine R1 with a reversible heat engine **E** operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$. Configure E such that it rejects heat exactly equal to $Q$ to the cold reservoir (i.e., $Q_L = Q$).

**Step 3: Apply First Law to Engine E**
$$
W_E = Q_H - Q_L = Q_H - Q
$$

**Step 4: Compute Net Effects of Composite Device (R1 + E)**

Net heat interaction with cold reservoir:
$$
Q_{L,net} = Q - Q = 0
$$

Net heat interaction with hot reservoir:
$$
Q_{H,net} = Q_H - Q
$$

Net work output:
$$
W_{net} = W_E - 0 = Q_H - Q
$$

**Step 5: Energy Balance Check**
$$
Q_{H,net} = W_{net} \implies Q_H - Q = Q_H - Q \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**
The composite device interacts with **only one reservoir** (the hot reservoir at $T_H$), extracts heat $Q_H - Q$ from it, and converts it **entirely** into net work $W_{net} = Q_H - Q$, with **no other effect** on the surroundings.

**Step 7: Conclusion**
This is a perfect Kelvin-Planck violator — a PMM2. Hence, a Clausius violation implies a Kelvin-Planck violation. **Q.E.D.**

*(Self-assessment: 7 marks — 1 mark assumption, 1 mark composite construction, 2 marks energy balance, 1 mark net effects, 1 mark verification, 1 mark conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting "operates in a cycle"** — Students frequently omit this phrase while writing the statement, leading to **immediate loss of 1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing direction of work** — In a refrigerator, work is **input** ($W_{in}$); in a heat engine, work is **output** ($W_{out}$). Mixing these up leads to sign errors.
> 3. **Skipping the composite cycle setup** — Board examiners expect explicit identification of the **two devices** (violator + compliant reversible device) used in the equivalence proof. Vague "if KP is violated then CS is violated" without construction loses **up to 4 marks**.
> 4. **Missing the "no other effect" clause** — Both statements require this. Omitting it makes the statement technically incomplete.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Kelvin-Planck (KP)** statement forbids 100% conversion of heat from a **single** reservoir into work in a cyclic engine.
- ✅ The **Clausius (CS)** statement forbids spontaneous heat transfer from a **cold** body to a **hot** body without external work input.
- ✅ Both statements are **logically equivalent** — a violation of one implies a violation of the other.
- ✅ A device violating KP is called a **PMM2** (Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind).
- ✅ A device violating CS is called a **self-acting refrigerator**.
- ✅ The proof of equivalence uses a **composite cycle argument**: combine the violator with a **reversible** compliant device.
- ✅ Both statements **presuppose cyclic operation** — omitting "in a cycle" makes the statement **invalid**.
- ✅ Both statements require the device to produce **"no other effect"** — a complete statement must include this clause.
- ✅ For any cyclic heat engine: $\eta_{th} < 1$ always (equality is impossible).
- ✅ For any refrigerator: $W_{net,in} > 0$ always (zero work is impossible).
- ✅ The maximum theoretical efficiency for any heat engine is the **Carnot efficiency**: $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$.
- ✅ Real-world applications: **power plant design** (KP), **refrigeration/HVAC** (CS), and **entropy-based cycle analysis** (Clausius inequality).
- ✅ Engineering takeaway: You cannot extract useful work from a single thermal reservoir — you **always** need a temperature difference.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PMM2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PMM2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2)** is a hypothetical, fictitious device that is supposed to operate in a thermodynamic cycle and produce **net work output by extracting heat from a single thermal reservoir**, while rejecting **zero heat** to a sink at a lower temperature. In simpler terminology, a PMM2 claims to convert thermal energy from one body entirely into mechanical work with 100 % efficiency, in direct contradiction with the **Kelvin–Planck statement** of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PMM2**
> A PMM2 violates the **Second Law** of Thermodynamics, *not* the First Law. It does not violate energy conservation (energy input equals energy output). It violates the qualitative clause of the Second Law regarding the *direction* and *quality* of energy conversion.

For a cycle, the First Law reduces to:

$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$$

A PMM2 hypothetically satisfies this equation (so it is *not* a PMM1), but it does so by taking all the heat from a **single reservoir** at a uniform temperature $T_H$ and converting it wholly into work, i.e., $Q_H = W_{net}$, with $Q_L = 0$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you have a kettle of hot water sitting on a table at $90^\circ C$. You attach a tiny turbine to it that supposedly runs purely on the heat of that one kettle — turning a fan, lifting a weight, or charging your phone. And the kettle never cools down. No cooler room is needed. No exhaust heat is dumped anywhere. The kettle keeps giving heat, the turbine keeps giving work, forever.

This sounds too good to be true — and it is. Nature does not let you siphon useful work out of a single heat source without paying an "entropy tax" by rejecting some heat to a colder body. A PMM2 is the engineering fantasy of doing exactly that. It is the thermodynamic equivalent of a perpetual motion machine that respects energy bookkeeping (PMM1 territory) but cheats on the *direction* of natural processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Significance:** The PMM2 impossibility principle underpins why every heat engine (steam plant, car engine, jet turbine, refrigerator compressor) **must** have at least *two* thermal reservoirs — a hot source and a cold sink. Without a temperature difference, no net work can be extracted in a cycle.

### 1.3 Standard Classification of Perpetual Motion Machines

| Machine Type | Law Violated | Brief Description |
|:-------------|:-------------|:------------------|
| **PMM1** | First Law ($E_{in} \neq E_{out}$) | Creates energy from nothing — violates energy conservation. |
| **PMM2** | Second Law (Kelvin–Planck / Clausius) | Converts heat from one reservoir fully into work — violates the entropy / direction principle. |
| **PMM3** | Second Law (in a subtler sense) | Frictionless motion with no dissipative losses — practically unattainable due to friction. |

The KTU Module 2 focus is squarely on **PMM2** and its equivalence with the **Clausius statement** of the Second Law.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — What a PMM2 Claims to Do

The PMM2 is a cyclic device. Let us break down its claimed operation into structured logic steps:

- **Step 1 — Heat Absorption:** The machine draws heat $Q_H$ from a single thermal reservoir maintained at a constant temperature $T_H$.
- **Step 2 — Complete Conversion:** It claims to convert *all* of $Q_H$ into useful mechanical or electrical work $W_{net}$.
- **Step 3 — Zero Rejection:** It rejects **no** heat ($Q_L = 0$) to any lower-temperature sink.
- **Step 4 — Cyclic Continuity:** The working fluid returns to its initial state, and the cycle repeats indefinitely.
- **Step 5 — Net Effect:** The only external effect is the absorption of heat from a single reservoir and the production of an equal amount of work.

### 2.2 Why This Is Impossible — The "Why" and "How"

The **Kelvin–Planck statement** asserts:

> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

Therefore, a PMM2 — which does precisely that — **cannot exist**.

#### Why does Nature forbid this?

- **Microscopic View (Statistical Mechanics):** Heat is the *disordered* random motion of molecules. Work is the *ordered* coherent motion of molecules. Converting all disordered motion into ordered motion is statistically overwhelmingly improbable — it would require every single molecule to spontaneously coordinate its motion.
- **Macroscopic View (Entropy):** For any cyclic device interacting with a single reservoir at $T_H$, the entropy change of the universe would be:

$$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{reservoir} + \Delta S_{device} = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + 0 = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} < 0$$

This violates the **Clausius Inequality** which mandates $\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$.

### 2.3 Equivalence of PMM2 Violation and Clausius Statement Violation

The KTU Module 2 syllabus places strong emphasis on proving that:

$$\text{Existence of PMM2} \iff \text{Violation of Clausius Statement}$$

**Clausius Statement:** *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

The proof of equivalence is a two-part logical construct:

- **Part A — PMM2 ⟹ Clausius Violation:** If a PMM2 exists, combine it with a normal refrigerator to transfer heat without external work, contradicting Clausius.
- **Part B — Clausius Violation ⟹ PMM2:** If a Clausius-violating device exists, combine it with a heat engine to extract work from a single reservoir, creating a PMM2.

This equivalence will be derived rigorously in SECTION_3.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Even though a PMM2 does not exist, the principle of its impossibility is the very foundation of:

- **Thermal Power Plant Design** — establishes why condenser cooling is mandatory.
- **Refrigeration and Heat Pump Cycles** — explains why work input is needed to move heat "uphill" thermally.
- **Otto, Diesel, Brayton Cycle Analysis** — the thermal efficiency $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ is bounded above by the Carnot efficiency, because $Q_L \neq 0$ in any real cycle.
- **Entropy Generation Calculations** — the PMM2 principle guarantees $\Delta S_{gen} \geq 0$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Cheat Sheet for PMM2 & Related Second Law Concepts**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|:--|:--------------------|:--------|:----------------|
| 1 | $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W = W_{net}$ | First Law for a cycle (always satisfied, even by PMM2) | Confirming PMM2 obeys PMM1's violation test |
| 2 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Thermal efficiency of any heat engine | Show that $Q_L = 0$ would imply $\eta_{th} = 100\%$ |
| 3 | $\eta_{th,PMM2} = 100\% = 1.0$ | Hypothetical PMM2 efficiency | Used in contradiction argument |
| 4 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Maximum possible efficiency between two reservoirs | Establishes the upper bound on $\eta_{th}$ |
| 5 | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ | Clausius Inequality for any cyclic device | Used to rule out PMM2 mathematically |
| 6 | $\Delta S_{universe} = -\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} \geq 0$ | Entropy change of universe for a PMM2 attempt | Shows contradiction because LHS $\lt 0$ |
| 7 | $\Delta S_{gen} \geq 0$ | Entropy generation (Second Law) | Always true for any real process |
| 8 | $COP_{Ref} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator | Used in equivalence proof |
| 9 | $\oint \delta Q = Q_H - Q_L = W_{net}$ | Energy balance for a cyclic heat engine | Standard First Law relation |
| 10 | $W_{PMM2} = Q_H$ (with $Q_L = 0$) | Net work output of a PMM2 | The defining relation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Notation Convention Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - $Q_H$ → Heat supplied **to** the system from hot reservoir (taken positive).
> - $Q_L$ → Heat rejected **by** the system to cold reservoir (taken positive in magnitude).
> - $W_{net}$ → Net work output of the cyclic device.

### 2.6 Quick Conceptual Distinction — PMM1 vs PMM2

| Property | PMM1 | PMM2 |
|:---------|:-----|:-----|
| First Law obeyed? | **No** — $W_{out} > Q_{in}$ (creates energy) | **Yes** — $W_{out} = Q_{in}$ exactly |
| Second Law obeyed? | N/A (already fails First Law) | **No** — violates Kelvin–Planck clause |
| Efficiency claimed | $\eta > 100\%$ (or $\eta = \infty$) | $\eta = 100\%$ (exactly) |
| Typical claim | "Free energy from nothing" | "100 % conversion of heat to work" |
| Example fantasy | A motor that runs forever without fuel | A ship that sails by extracting heat from one ocean only |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Why a PMM2 Violates the Second Law (Entropy Argument)

**Given:**
- A cyclic device (the PMM2) interacts with a single thermal reservoir at constant temperature $T_H$.
- It absorbs heat $Q_H$ from this reservoir.
- It produces net work $W_{net} = Q_H$ (with $Q_L = 0$).
- It operates in a cycle, so the working fluid returns to its initial state.

**To Prove:** This configuration violates the Clausius Inequality and is therefore impossible.

**Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law to the cycle:**

Because the device is cyclic, the internal energy change over a complete cycle is zero:

$$\oint dU = 0$$

Therefore the First Law for the cycle reduces to:

$$Q_{net} = W_{net}$$

Expanding the net heat absorbed from the only reservoir:

$$Q_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = Q_H - 0 = Q_H$$

Hence:

$$Q_H = W_{net}$$

This is consistent with energy conservation — so the PMM2 does **not** violate the First Law. ✓

**Step 2 — Compute the entropy change of the working fluid:**

Since the working fluid undergoes a complete cycle and returns to its initial state:

$$\Delta S_{fluid} = \oint dS = 0$$

This is the defining property of any state function — entropy is a property, and for a cyclic process the change is zero.

**Step 3 — Compute the entropy change of the reservoir:**

The hot reservoir loses heat $Q_H$ at constant temperature $T_H$. For a reservoir (assumed internally reversible), the entropy change is:

$$\Delta S_{reservoir} = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H}$$

The negative sign arises because the reservoir is *losing* heat.

**Step 4 — Compute the total entropy change of the universe:**

Since the universe (in this scenario) consists of the reservoir and the cyclic device, and the device's entropy change is zero over a cycle:

$$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{reservoir} + \Delta S_{device}$$

Substituting the values obtained:

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + 0$$

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Second Law:**

The Clausius statement of the Second Law demands that for any process:

$$\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$$

(Equality holds only for fully reversible processes.)

**Step 6 — Identify the contradiction:**

Since $Q_H > 0$ (heat is absorbed by the device) and $T_H > 0$ (absolute temperature is always positive):

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} < 0$$

This is a **strictly negative** value, which **violates** the Second Law requirement that $\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$.

**Conclusion:** The PMM2 cannot exist. ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners often award full marks when students explicitly identify *which* law is being violated (Second Law) and *which clause* of the Second Law (Kelvin–Planck or entropy non-decrease). Always name both.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Equivalence of PMM2 and Clausius Statement Violation

This is a classic KTU Module 2 derivation and is regularly asked for **7 marks**.

#### Part A: PMM2 ⟹ Violation of Clausius Statement

**Hypothesis:** Assume a PMM2 exists. We will show this allows heat to flow spontaneously from a cold body to a hot body without external work.

**Construction:**

1. Consider a normal reversible heat engine $E$ operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L$. It absorbs $Q_H$ from the hot source, rejects $Q_L$ to the cold sink, and produces work $W = Q_H - Q_L$.
2. Now take the work output $W$ from this heat engine and feed it to a PMM2 (assumed to exist).
3. The PMM2 takes heat $Q_L$ from a single reservoir at $T_L$ and converts it entirely into work $W' = Q_L$.

**Step 1 — Energy balance of the combined system:**

Heat engine produces: $\quad W = Q_H - Q_L$

PMM2 consumes: $\quad W_{in} = Q_L$

If we set $W = W_{in}$, we require $Q_H - Q_L = Q_L$, i.e., $Q_H = 2 Q_L$. This is a choice of magnitudes; the logic is more cleanly stated below.

**Step 2 — Net effect on the cold reservoir:**

The cold reservoir at $T_L$:
- Receives $Q_L$ (rejected by the heat engine)
- Loses $Q_L$ (absorbed by the PMM2)

Net heat exchange of cold reservoir: $\quad Q_{L,net} = Q_L - Q_L = 0$

**Step 3 — Net effect on the hot reservoir:**

The hot reservoir at $T_H$ loses $Q_H$ to the heat engine. No heat is returned to it.

**Step 4 — Net effect on work:**

The heat engine produces $W$ work; the PMM2 consumes $W$ work. Net external work exchanged: $W_{net} = 0$.

**Step 5 — Identifying the violation:**

The combined system:
- Takes heat $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir at $T_H$.
- Produces **no** net work.
- Has **no** net effect on the cold reservoir.

By conservation, all of $Q_H$ must have been transferred somewhere. Since there is no net work output and the cold reservoir is unchanged, the only remaining effect is that heat $Q_H$ has been transferred from the hot reservoir at $T_H$ to ... wait, let us reconstruct the argument more carefully.

**Cleaner Reconstruction of Part A:**

Consider:
- A reversible heat engine $E$ between $T_H$ and $T_L$ taking $Q_H$ from hot and rejecting $Q_L$ to cold, producing work $W = Q_H - Q_L$.
- A PMM2 operating on the cold reservoir at $T_L$, taking heat $Q_L$ from it and converting it entirely to work $W_{PMM2} = Q_L$.

Now use the PMM2's work $W_{PMM2} = Q_L$ to **drive** the heat engine in reverse (i.e., as a heat pump). The heat pump takes $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir and delivers $Q_H$ to the hot reservoir using work $W_{PMM2}$.

Energy balance on the heat pump: $\quad Q_H = Q_L + W_{input} = Q_L + Q_L = 2 Q_L$

Combined effect:
- Cold reservoir at $T_L$ supplies $Q_L$ to the PMM2 *and* $Q_L$ to the heat pump. Total: $2Q_L$ withdrawn.
- Hot reservoir at $T_H$ receives $Q_H = 2 Q_L$.
- No external work is required because $W_{PMM2}$ powers the pump.

Wait — this still has a deficit. The classical KTU textbook argument is the reverse. Let me restate it properly.

**Correct Classical Argument — PMM2 ⟹ Clausius Violation:**

Assume a PMM2 exists: it takes $Q_H$ from a single reservoir at $T_H$ and produces work $W = Q_H$.

Now combine this PMM2 with a normal refrigerator (Clausius device in reverse) that uses work $W$ to transfer heat $Q_L$ from a cold reservoir at $T_L$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$.

For the refrigerator: $\quad Q_H^{ref} = Q_L^{ref} + W$

If we set $W = Q_H$ (the work from the PMM2), and we choose $Q_L^{ref} = Q_H$, then $Q_H^{ref} = 2 Q_H$.

Net effect on the universe:
- Single reservoir at $T_H$: loses $Q_H$ (to PMM2) but gains $Q_H^{ref} = 2 Q_H$ (from refrigerator). Net gain: $+Q_H$.
- Cold reservoir at $T_L$: loses $Q_L^{ref} = Q_H$. Net loss: $-Q_H$.
- No net work done externally ($W$ from PMM2 cancels $W$ to refrigerator).

**The net effect is: heat $Q_H$ has been transferred from a cold reservoir at $T_L$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$, with no external work input.** This is precisely a violation of the Clausius statement. ∎

#### Part B: Clausius Violation ⟹ Existence of PMM2

**Hypothesis:** Assume a device exists that violates the Clausius statement, i.e., it transfers heat $Q$ from a cold reservoir at $T_L$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$ without any external work input.

**Construction:** Combine this Clausius-violating device with a normal reversible heat engine operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$.

- Heat engine absorbs $Q$ from $T_H$ (could be the heat delivered by the Clausius-violating device), rejects $Q_L$ to $T_L$, and produces work $W = Q - Q_L$.

- Clausius-violating device transfers heat $Q$ from $T_L$ to $T_H$ without work.

**Net Effect:**
- Hot reservoir at $T_H$: receives $Q$ from the Clausius device and loses $Q$ to the heat engine. Net change: $0$.
- Cold reservoir at $T_L$: loses $Q$ to the Clausius device and gains $Q_L$ from the heat engine. Net change: $+ (Q_L - Q)$.
- Net work output: $W = Q - Q_L$.

Since $W = Q - Q_L > 0$ for a real heat engine, the combined device:
- Exchanges **no** heat with the hot reservoir at $T_H$ (it acts as an intermediary).
- **Absorbs** net heat $Q - Q_L$ from the cold reservoir at $T_L$ (which is then converted to work).
- Produces net work $W = Q - Q_L$.

This is precisely a device that takes heat from a single reservoir (the cold one) and converts it entirely into work — **a PMM2**! ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation II:**
> $$\boxed{\text{Existence of PMM2} \iff \text{Violation of Clausius Statement}}$$
> Both statements (Kelvin–Planck and Clausius) are equivalent expressions of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Carnot Efficiency as the Consequence of PMM2 Impossibility

The Carnot efficiency formula can be derived from the impossibility of a PMM2 combined with the impossibility of a PMM1.

**Given:**
- Two heat reservoirs at $T_H$ and $T_L$, with $T_H > T_L$.
- A reversible heat engine $R$ and an irreversible heat engine $I$ both operate between these reservoirs.

**Assume for contradiction:** A real (irreversible) heat engine $I$ has efficiency greater than a reversible engine $R$.

$$\eta_I > \eta_R$$

That is:

$$\frac{W_I}{Q_{H,I}} > \frac{W_R}{Q_{H,R}}$$

**Step 1 — Couple the engines:**

Operate $R$ in reverse (as a refrigerator) using the work $W_I$ produced by $I$.

- $I$ absorbs $Q_{H,I}$ from $T_H$, rejects $Q_{L,I}$ to $T_L$, produces $W_I$.
- $R$ (reversed) absorbs $Q_{L,R}$ from $T_L$, delivers $Q_{H,R}$ to $T_H$, consumes $W_R = W_I$.

**Step 2 — Net work balance:**

By assumption, $W_R = W_I$ (we use the work output of $I$ to drive $R$).

**Step 3 — Net heat at the cold reservoir:**

$$Q_{L,net} = Q_{L,I} - Q_{L,R}$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency inequality expansion:**

Since $\eta_I > \eta_R$:

$$1 - \frac{Q_{L,I}}{Q_{H,I}} > 1 - \frac{Q_{L,R}}{Q_{H,R}}$$

$$\frac{Q_{L,I}}{Q_{H,I}} < \frac{Q_{L,R}}{Q_{H,R}}$$

Choose $Q_{H,I} = Q_{H,R} = Q_H$ (matched heat input from hot reservoir).

Then:

$$Q_{L,I} < Q_{L,R}$$

**Step 5 — Net heat at hot reservoir:**

$$Q_{H,net} = Q_{H,R} - Q_{H,I} = Q_H - Q_H = 0$$

**Step 6 — Net effect on cold reservoir:**

The cold reservoir loses $Q_{L,I}$ (gained by $I$ from cold? No — wait, $I$ rejects $Q_{L,I}$ to cold, so cold gains $Q_{L,I}$) and loses $Q_{L,R}$ (absorbed by reversed $R$ from cold).

Net heat change of cold reservoir:

$$\Delta Q_L = Q_{L,I} - Q_{L,R}$$

Since $Q_{L,I} < Q_{L,R}$, we have:

$$\Delta Q_L < 0$$

The cold reservoir **loses** heat $Q_{L,R} - Q_{L,I} > 0$.

**Step 7 — Energy conservation:**

By the First Law, since no net heat is exchanged with the hot reservoir and net work exchanged is zero (work of $I$ cancels work to $R$), the heat lost by the cold reservoir must equal... let me re-do this carefully.

Actually, let me use a cleaner derivation that is standard in KTU textbooks.

**Standard KTU Derivation (P. K. Nag / Cengel style):**

Assume two reversible heat engines $R_1$ and $R_2$ operate between the same reservoirs $T_H$ and $T_L$, but have different efficiencies $\eta_1 \neq \eta_2$.

Suppose $\eta_1 > \eta_2$. Operate $R_1$ to deliver work $W$ to $R_2$ (running $R_2$ in reverse as a refrigerator).

- $R_1$ takes $Q_{H,1}$ from $T_H$, rejects $Q_{L,1}$ to $T_L$, produces $W$.
- $R_2$ (reversed) takes $Q_{L,2}$ from $T_L$, delivers $Q_{H,2}$ to $T_H$, consumes $W$.

Efficiencies:
$$\eta_1 = \frac{W}{Q_{H,1}}, \quad \eta_2 = \frac{W}{Q_{H,2}}$$

If $\eta_1 > \eta_2$, then $Q_{H,1} < Q_{H,2}$.

Now, $R_2$ delivers $Q_{H,2}$ to $T_H$, while $R_1$ withdraws $Q_{H,1}$ from $T_H$. Net heat to $T_H$:

$$Q_{H,net} = Q_{H,2} - Q_{H,1} > 0$$

For $R_2$ reversed: $Q_{H,2} = Q_{L,2} + W$, so $Q_{L,2} = Q_{H,2} - W$.

For $R_1$: $Q_{L,1} = Q_{H,1} - W$.

Net heat at $T_L$:
$$Q_{L,net} = Q_{L,1} - Q_{L,2} = (Q_{H,1} - W) - (Q_{H,2} - W) = Q_{H,1} - Q_{H,2} < 0$$

So the cold reservoir loses heat $Q_{H,2} - Q_{H,1}$, the hot reservoir gains the same amount of heat, and no work is exchanged externally (work of $R_1$ drives $R_2$).

**This is a violation of the Clausius statement!** Heat has moved from cold to hot reservoir with no external work. By the equivalence proven in Derivation II, this is equivalent to a PMM2.

But the Clausius statement is true → PMM2 is impossible → our assumption $\eta_1 \neq \eta_2$ for two reversible engines is false.

$$\therefore \eta_1 = \eta_2 \quad \text{for all reversible engines operating between } T_H \text{ and } T_L$$

This common efficiency depends only on $T_H$ and $T_L$, hence is called the **Carnot efficiency**:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** The Carnot efficiency formula is *derived* from the impossibility of a PMM2. This is why every real engine has $\eta \leq \eta_{Carnot}$, with equality only for the (idealized) reversible Carnot cycle.

### 3.4 Numerical Illustration — Detecting a False PMM2 Claim

**Problem Statement:** A salesman claims to have invented a heat engine that absorbs $1000 \text{ kJ}$ of heat from a reservoir at $500 \text{ K}$ and produces $850 \text{ kJ}$ of work per cycle, rejecting only $150 \text{ kJ}$ to a sink at $300 \text{ K}$. Is this device a PMM2? Justify.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Check the First Law (PMM1 violation test):**

For a cycle: $Q_{net} = W_{net}$

$$Q_H - Q_L = W_{net}$$

$$1000 - 150 = 850 \text{ kJ}$$

The First Law is satisfied — so it is **not** a PMM1.

**Step 2 — Compute the actual thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{850}{1000} = 0.85 = 85\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Carnot (maximum possible) efficiency:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{500} = 1 - 0.6 = 0.40 = 40\%$$

**Step 4 — Compare and identify violation:**

$$\eta_{th} = 85\% > \eta_{Carnot} = 40\%$$

This is **physically impossible** for any cyclic heat engine operating between these two reservoirs. The device is effectively a **PMM2** because its claimed efficiency exceeds the maximum allowed by the Second Law.

**Step 5 — Entropy verification:**

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -\frac{1000}{500} + \frac{150}{300}$$

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -2.000 + 0.500 = -1.500 \text{ kJ/K}$$

Since $\Delta S_{universe} < 0$, the Second Law is violated — confirming the device cannot exist.

**Conclusion:** The salesman's claim is fraudulent. The proposed device is a **PMM2**. ∎

### 3.5 Summary Matrix of PMM2 Arguments

| Argument Type | Logical Form | Conclusion |
|:--------------|:-------------|:-----------|
| Entropy Non-Decrease | $\Delta S_{universe} = -\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} < 0$ | Violates Clausius Inequality |
| Kelvin–Planck Direct | $Q_L = 0$ for a cycle $\Rightarrow \eta_{th} = 100\%$ | Contradicts Kelvin–Planck Statement |
| Carnot Bound | $\eta_{th} > \eta_{Carnot}$ for any real cycle | Contradicts Second Law bound |
| Clausius Equivalence | PMM2 + Ref $\Rightarrow$ Spontaneous $T_L \to T_H$ heat flow | Violates Clausius Statement |
| Statistical Mechanics | All molecular motion becomes ordered spontaneously | Vanishingly improbable (probability $\sim 0$) |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2). How does it differ from a PMM1?

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2)** is a hypothetical, fictitious cyclic device that is supposed to convert heat absorbed from a **single thermal reservoir** completely into an equivalent amount of work, without rejecting any heat to a sink at a lower temperature. In other words, a PMM2 would have a thermal efficiency of **100 %**.

**Distinction from PMM1:**

- A **PMM1** violates the **First Law of Thermodynamics** by producing more work output than the heat input (i.e., it creates energy from nothing, $\eta > 100\%$ or $W_{out} > Q_{in}$).
- A **PMM2** does **not** violate the First Law (it obeys $W_{out} = Q_{in}$ exactly), but it violates the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, specifically the **Kelvin–Planck statement**, which states that it is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the absorption of heat from a single reservoir and the production of an equal amount of work.

Hence, a PMM1 fails the energy conservation test, while a PMM2 fails the *quality / direction* of energy conversion test. **(3 Marks)**

---

#### Question 2: State the Kelvin–Planck statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Why is it equivalent to saying "a PMM2 is impossible"?

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Kelvin–Planck Statement:**

> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the extraction of heat from a single thermal reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

**Equivalence to PMM2 Impossibility:**

By definition, a PMM2 is precisely a device that *would* operate in a cycle, absorb heat from a single reservoir, and convert all of it into work with no heat rejection. The Kelvin–Planck statement explicitly forbids such a device. Therefore:

- If a PMM2 existed, it would directly violate the Kelvin–Planck statement.
- The Kelvin–Planck statement asserts that no such device can exist.

Hence the two statements are logically identical, and asserting "a PMM2 is impossible" is a direct paraphrase of the Kelvin–Planck formulation of the Second Law. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define a Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2). With the help of neat schematics, explain why a PMM2 is impossible based on the Kelvin–Planck statement and the Clausius inequality. Show that the existence of a PMM2 would imply a decrease in the entropy of the universe.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A proposed engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H = 800 \text{ K}$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L = 300 \text{ K}$. It claims to absorb $Q_H = 500 \text{ kJ}$ of heat and deliver $W_{net} = 350 \text{ kJ}$ of work per cycle. Determine:

1. The thermal efficiency of the proposed engine.
2. The Carnot (maximum possible) efficiency between these reservoirs.
3. Whether the device qualifies as a PMM2. Justify with both efficiency and entropy arguments.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Definition:** A Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2) is a hypothetical cyclic device that operates with a single thermal reservoir, absorbs heat $Q_H$ from it, and converts the entire heat into an equivalent amount of work $W_{net} = Q_H$, rejecting no heat ($Q_L = 0$) to any lower-temperature sink.

**Schematic (refer to SECTION_4.1 Block Diagram):**

The PMM2 takes heat from one reservoir at $T_H$, produces work equal to $Q_H$, and discharges nothing to a sink.

**Kelvin–Planck Argument:**

The Kelvin–Planck statement explicitly forbids such a device. The thermal efficiency of any heat engine operating between two reservoirs is:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$$

For a PMM2, since $W_{net} = Q_H$ and $Q_L = 0$:

$$\eta_{th,PMM2} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H} = 100\% = 1.0$$

The Kelvin–Planck statement asserts that no cyclic device can have $\eta_{th} = 100\%$ while exchanging heat with only one reservoir. Therefore, the PMM2 cannot exist.

**Clausius Inequality / Entropy Argument:**

For a cyclic device, the entropy change of the working fluid over a complete cycle is zero:

$$\Delta S_{fluid} = \oint dS = 0$$

The single hot reservoir loses heat $Q_H$ at temperature $T_H$, so its entropy change is:

$$\Delta S_{reservoir} = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H}$$

The total entropy change of the universe is:

$$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{reservoir} + \Delta S_{fluid} = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + 0 = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H}$$

Since $Q_H > 0$ and $T_H > 0$, we obtain:

$$\Delta S_{universe} < 0$$

This violates the Clausius statement that $\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$ for any process. Hence a PMM2 would imply a decrease in the entropy of the universe, which is forbidden. ∎

**Part (b):**

**Given:**
$T_H = 800 \text{ K}, \quad T_L = 300 \text{ K}, \quad Q_H = 500 \text{ kJ}, \quad W_{net} = 350 \text{ kJ}$

**Step 1 — Compute the thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{350}{500} = 0.70 = 70\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Carnot efficiency:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 = 62.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Compare and identify violation:**

$$\eta_{th} = 70\% > \eta_{Carnot} = 62.5\%$$

Since the claimed efficiency **exceeds** the maximum possible (Carnot) efficiency, the device is a **PMM2** and cannot exist.

**Step 4 — Verify using the entropy argument:**

Heat rejected to cold sink:
$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 500 - 350 = 150 \text{ kJ}$$

Entropy change of universe:
$$\Delta S_{universe} = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -\frac{500}{800} + \frac{150}{300}$$

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -0.625 + 0.500 = -0.125 \text{ kJ/K}$$

Since $\Delta S_{universe} = -0.125 \text{ kJ/K} < 0$, the Second Law is violated. The device is indeed a **PMM2**. ∎

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** [7 Marks] State the Clausius statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Prove rigorously that the existence of a PMM2 implies a violation of the Clausius statement. State the reverse implication as well.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A heat engine operates between two reservoirs. It absorbs $Q_H = 1000 \text{ kJ}$ from a source at $T_H = 600 \text{ K}$ and rejects $Q_L = 400 \text{ kJ}$ to a sink at $T_L = 300 \text{ K}$. Determine:

1. The thermal efficiency of the engine.
2. Whether the engine violates the Second Law (check using Carnot efficiency).
3. The total entropy change of the universe per cycle and comment on whether the engine is reversible, irreversible, or impossible.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Clausius Statement:**

> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

**Proof that PMM2 ⟹ Clausius Violation:**

**Assumption:** Suppose a PMM2 exists. It absorbs heat $Q_H$ from a single reservoir at temperature $T_H$ and converts it entirely into work $W = Q_H$, with no heat rejection.

**Construction:** Combine the PMM2 with a reversible refrigerator operating between $T_H$ (hot) and $T_L$ (cold), where $T_L < T_H$. The PMM2 produces work $W = Q_H$, which is used to drive the refrigerator. The refrigerator takes heat $Q_L^{ref}$ from the cold reservoir and delivers heat $Q_H^{ref}$ to the hot reservoir.

For the refrigerator: $\quad Q_H^{ref} = Q_L^{ref} + W_{in} = Q_L^{ref} + Q_H$

Choosing $Q_L^{ref} = Q_H$ (so that the refrigerator extracts $Q_H$ from the cold reservoir):
$$Q_H^{ref} = Q_H + Q_H = 2 Q_H$$

**Net Effect on the Combined System:**

- Hot reservoir at $T_H$: gains $Q_H^{ref} = 2 Q_H$ (from refrigerator) and loses $Q_H$ (to PMM2). Net change: $+Q_H$.
- Cold reservoir at $T_L$: loses $Q_L^{ref} = Q_H$ (to refrigerator). Net change: $-Q_H$.
- Net external work: zero (PMM2's output drives the refrigerator).

Therefore, heat $Q_H$ has been transferred from the cold reservoir at $T_L$ to the hot reservoir at $T_H$ **without any external work input**. This is a direct violation of the Clausius statement. ∎

**Reverse Implication (Clausius Violation ⟹ PMM2):**

**Assumption:** Suppose a device exists that transfers heat $Q$ from a cold reservoir at $T_L$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$ without any work input (Clausius violation).

**Construction:** Combine this device with a reversible heat engine between $T_H$ and $T_L$. The heat engine absorbs heat $Q$ from $T_H$, rejects $Q_L$ to $T_L$, and produces work $W = Q - Q_L$.

**Net Effect:**
- Hot reservoir at $T_H$: receives $Q$ from the Clausius-violating device, loses $Q$ to the engine. Net change: $0$.
- Cold reservoir at $T_L$: loses $Q$ to the Clausius device, gains $Q_L$ from the engine. Net loss: $Q - Q_L$.
- Net work output: $W = Q - Q_L$.

The combined device takes heat $Q - Q_L$ from the cold reservoir (a single reservoir) and converts it entirely into work — **a PMM2**! ∎

**Conclusion:** PMM2 existence and Clausius statement violation are logically equivalent.

---

**Part (b):**

**Given:**
$Q_H = 1000 \text{ kJ}, \quad Q_L = 400 \text{ kJ}, \quad T_H = 600 \text{ K}, \quad T_L = 300 \text{ K}$

**Step 1 — Net work output:**

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = 1000 - 400 = 600 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{600}{1000} = 0.60 = 60\%$$

**Step 3 — Carnot efficiency:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{600} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.50 = 50\%$$

**Step 4 — Second Law Check:**

$$\eta_{th} = 60\% > \eta_{Carnot} = 50\%$$

This **exceeds** the Carnot bound, so the engine **violates the Second Law** and cannot exist as described.

**Step 5 — Entropy change of the universe:**

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -\frac{1000}{600} + \frac{400}{300}$$

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -1.6667 + 1.3333 = -0.3333 \text{ kJ/K}$$

Since $\Delta S_{universe} < 0$, the engine is **impossible** (it would be a PMM2 in disguise). It is not reversible (which would require $\Delta S_{universe} = 0$) and not merely irreversible (which would require $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$). The negative entropy change places this hypothetical engine in the **forbidden region** of thermodynamics. ∎

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on PMM2 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PMM1 and PMM2:** Many students incorrectly claim a PMM2 violates the First Law. It does NOT. It violates the Second Law only.
