> 2. **Forgetting the entropy verification step:** Examiners frequently award 2 marks specifically for the $\Delta S_{universe} \leq 0$ check. Skipping this costs you easy marks.
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** When computing $\Delta S_{reservoir}$, students often forget the negative sign (reservoir loses heat, so its entropy *decreases*).
> 4. **Not stating which law is violated explicitly:** Always write "this violates the Kelvin–Planck statement" or "this violates the Clausius statement." Vague statements like "this is against the Second Law" without naming the specific statement lose marks.
> 5. **In the equivalence proof, students often skip the reverse implication:** The KTU question typically asks for *both* PMM2 ⟹ Clausius and Clausius ⟹ PMM2. Missing the second half costs 3–4 marks.
> 6. **Numerical errors in $\eta_{Carnot}$:** A common mistake is using Celsius instead of Kelvin. Always use absolute temperatures.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **PMM2** is a hypothetical cyclic device that converts heat from a **single reservoir** entirely into work with **100 % efficiency**.
- A PMM2 obeys the **First Law** ($W = Q$) but violates the **Second Law**.
- The **Kelvin–Planck statement** directly forbids the existence of a PMM2.
- A PMM2 would cause a **decrease in the entropy of the universe**, contradicting $\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$.
- The **Kelvin–Planck** and **Clausius** statements of the Second Law are **logically equivalent**.
- The **Carnot efficiency** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ is derived from the impossibility of a PMM2.
- Any engine claiming $\eta_{th} > \eta_{Carnot}$ is effectively a **PMM2** and is impossible.
- For detection of a PMM2 in numerical problems, **three checks** must be applied: (i) First Law consistency, (ii) $\eta_{th} \leq \eta_{Carnot}$, and (iii) $\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$.
- The **equivalence proof** requires two directions: (PMM2 ⟹ Clausius violation) and (Clausius violation ⟹ PMM2).
- **All temperatures in thermodynamic formulas must be in Kelvin**, not Celsius.
- The standard notation: $Q_H$ (heat input from hot source), $Q_L$ (heat rejected to cold sink), $W_{net}$ (net work output).
- **Real-world takeaway:** No engine can have 100 % thermal efficiency. Every cyclic heat engine must dump some heat to a cold reservoir — this is the Second Law made practical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carnot Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Carnot Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carnot's Theorem (Sadi Carnot, 1824):** The theorem consists of two fundamental corollaries that establish the upper limit of efficiency for all heat engines operating between two thermal reservoirs:
>
> **Corollary I:** *The efficiency of a reversible heat engine operating between two given thermal reservoirs is independent of the nature of the working substance and depends **solely** on the temperatures of the reservoirs.*
>
> **Corollary II:** *No irreversible heat engine operating between the same two thermal reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible heat engine operating between those identical reservoirs.*

In mathematical notation, if we consider a reversible Carnot engine ($R$) and an irreversible heat engine ($I$) both operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_C$:

$$\eta_{th,\,I} < \eta_{th,\,R} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

where $\eta_{th}$ denotes the **thermal efficiency** of the cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Alignment (KTU 2024):** This topic directly addresses **CO2** — *Apply the second law of thermodynamics to evaluate the performance of thermal systems and cycles.* It builds the theoretical foundation for all subsequent cycle analyses (Otto, Diesel, Brayton, Rankine).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

Imagine you are organizing a charity water-pump competition. You have two buckets: a **hot bucket** (boiling water at 100°C representing the high-temperature source $T_H$) and a **cold bucket** (ice water at 0°C representing the low-temperature sink $T_C$). The challenge is to design the **most efficient water-lifting device** that can lift as much water as possible using only the temperature difference between the buckets.

**Key Intuitions:**

- 🪣 **The "Best" Design is Universal:** No matter what liquid you use inside your pump (water, mercury, or any ideal gas), if you design the pump to be **perfectly reversible** (frictionless, quasi-static, no heat leaks), all perfectly reversible pumps will lift the **exact same maximum amount of water**. This is the essence of **Corollary I**.

- 🪣 **Imperfect Designs Always Lose:** If your pump has friction, leaks, or sudden (non-quasi-static) processes, it will *always* lift *less* water than a perfectly reversible one. You can never beat the reversible design. This is **Corollary II**.

- 🪣 **The Temperature Limit is Absolute:** Even with a *perfect* frictionless pump, the temperature difference itself sets a hard ceiling — you can never extract **100%** of the heat as work. This ceiling is the **Carnot efficiency**: $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_C/T_H$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Laws in Action:** Carnot's Theorem is the **first practical bridge** between the abstract Second Law of Thermodynamics (Clausius & Kelvin-Planck statements) and the *quantitative design* of real engines. It tells engineers the *ceiling* they are designing toward.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hot reservoir temperature | $T_H$ | **K (Kelvin)** | 500 K – 1500 K |
| Cold reservoir temperature | $T_C$ | **K (Kelvin)** | 250 K – 300 K |
| Carnot (reversible) efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot}$ | **dimensionless (%)** | 0.40 – 0.75 |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R_u$ | **8.314 kJ/kmol·K** | Constant |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examination Trap:** Temperatures in Carnot efficiency calculations **MUST** be in **Kelvin**. Converting °C to K by *adding 273.15* is the most common single source of mark loss in this topic. The efficiency formula $1 - T_C/T_H$ is **invalid** if $T$ is in °C or °F.

---

### 1.4 Historical and Pedagogical Significance

Sadi Carnot proposed his theorem in 1824, **before** the First Law of Thermodynamics was even formally established. His insights were revolutionary because:

- They introduced the concept of a **cyclic heat engine** and a **temperature scale**.
- They defined the theoretical maximum efficiency that **no real engine can ever exceed**.
- They implicitly contained the seed of the Second Law, even though the formal statement came decades later (Clausius, 1850; Kelvin, 1851).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Carnot's Theorem — Step by Step

The proof of Carnot's Theorem relies on a **thought experiment** (reductio ad absurdum) using two engines coupled together. We analyze it in structured phases.

---

#### **Phase A — Setup of the Proof (Apparatus)**

- **Step 1:** Consider two heat engines, $E_R$ and $E_I$, both operating between the same hot reservoir ($T_H$) and cold reservoir ($T_C$).
- **Step 2:** Engine $E_R$ is **reversible** (Carnot engine). Engine $E_I$ is **irreversible** (real-world).
- **Step 3:** Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that $\eta_{I} > \eta_{R}$.
- **Step 4:** Use the reversibility of $E_R$ to run it **backwards** as a refrigerator/heat pump (this is the clever twist).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the reversibility assumption critical?** A reversible engine is the *only* device that can be operated in reverse without violating the Second Law. Irreversible engines cannot be perfectly reversed — running them backward would *increase* entropy, violating the Clausius inequality.

---

#### **Phase B — Energy & Entropy Balance (Mathematical Core)**

- **Step 5:** Apply the First Law to the **composite system** ($E_I$ + reversed $E_R$). Because the system is cyclic, the net change in internal energy is zero: $\Delta U_{net} = 0$.

- **Step 6:** Show that the composite device **violates the Kelvin-Planck statement** of the Second Law (i.e., it produces net work while exchanging heat with a single reservoir).

- **Step 7:** Conclude that the initial assumption ($\eta_I > \eta_R$) is false. Therefore:
$$\eta_{I} \leq \eta_{R}$$

- **Step 8:** Now apply the same argument with $E_I$ replaced by $E_R$ and vice versa. Since $E_R$ is reversible, this gives $\eta_R \leq \eta_I$. Combining:
$$\eta_{I} = \eta_{R} \quad \text{(if both are reversible)}$$
$$\eta_{I} < \eta_{R} \quad \text{(if } E_I \text{ is irreversible)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Logic Chain at a Glance:** Assumption → Composite Engine → Second Law Violation → Contradiction → Conclusion. This is the *Gold Standard* of thermodynamic proof technique, often replicated for other theorems (Clausius Inequality, etc.).

---

#### **Phase C — The Corollary I Proof (Why efficiency is independent of working fluid)**

- **Step 9:** Let two **different** reversible engines ($E_{R1}$ and $E_{R2}$) operate between $T_H$ and $T_C$ using *different* working fluids (e.g., air vs. ideal gas with different $\gamma$).

- **Step 10:** Apply Phase B's logic to $E_{R1}$ and $E_{R2}$. By mutual reversibility:
$$\eta_{R1} \leq \eta_{R2} \quad \text{and} \quad \eta_{R2} \leq \eta_{R1}$$
$$\Rightarrow \eta_{R1} = \eta_{R2}$$

- **Step 11:** Since the working fluid cancels out, efficiency must be a function of reservoir temperatures alone: $\eta_R = f(T_H, T_C)$.

- **Step 12:** For a Carnot cycle (isothermal heat addition, isentropic expansion, isothermal heat rejection, isentropic compression), direct integration gives:
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table.** Every numerical problem on Carnot's Theorem in KTU ESE and Internal Assessment reduces to one of these equations.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Boundary / Validity Condition** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$ | Thermal efficiency definition | Any heat engine cycle | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_C}{Q_H}$ | Efficiency via heat rejected | Any heat engine cycle | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | **Carnot efficiency (reversible)** | Reversible cycles only; $T$ in **K** | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{I} \leq \eta_{R} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | **Carnot Theorem (II corollary)** | For any irreversible engine | dimensionless |
| 5 | $COP_{HP,\,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_C}$ | Carnot Heat Pump COP | Reversible heat pump | dimensionless |
| 6 | $COP_{Ref,\,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_C}{T_H - T_C}$ | Carnot Refrigerator COP | Reversible refrigerator | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dfrac{Q_H}{Q_C} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_C}$ | Heat ratio in Carnot cycle | Reversible cycle | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ | Clausius Inequality | Sign convention: $\delta Q$ into system | kJ/K |
| 9 | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_{surr}} \leq 0$ | Clausius inequality (alt. form) | For cyclic devices | kJ/K |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Domain** | **Application of Carnot's Theorem** |
|---|---|
| **Power Plants** (Thermal) | Sets the absolute ceiling for Rankine cycle efficiency. Modern ultra-supercritical plants approach ~46%, far from the ~65% Carnot limit at 873 K / 303 K. |
| **Internal Combustion Engines** | Otto and Diesel cycles are bounded by Carnot efficiency between combustion temp. and ambient. Real thermal efficiency (30–40%) is well below Carnot. |
| **Cryogenics & Refrigeration** | Defines the maximum possible COP for any refrigerator between two temperature levels. |
| **Solar Thermal Systems** | Efficiency depends on receiver temperature vs. ambient; Carnot efficiency defines the theoretical limit. |
| **Heat Pump Design** | COP of ground-source heat pumps has a Carnot ceiling based on ground temp. vs. indoor temp. |
| **Jet & Gas Turbine Engines** | Brayton cycle efficiency is compared against Carnot limit to assess component quality. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The gap between the Carnot efficiency and the actual cycle efficiency is a measure of *irreversibility* in the system. Every irreversible component (friction, pressure drop, finite-temperature heat transfer) widens this gap. This gap is quantified using **Exergy Analysis** (covered later in the syllabus).

---

### 2.4 Distinction Between Corollaries — A Quick-Reference Map

| **Aspect** | **Corollary I** | **Corollary II** |
|---|---|---|
| **Statement** | Reversible efficiency is universal | Irreversible engines are always worse |
| **What it fixes?** | The *value* of efficiency | The *inequality* bound |
| **Practical Use** | Calculates $\eta_{Carnot}$ | Justifies why we pursue reversibility |
| **Working fluid dependence** | None | N/A |
| **Mathematical form** | $\eta_{R} = 1 - T_C/T_H$ | $\eta_{I} < \eta_{R}$ |
| **Tested in KTU** | Almost every numerical problem | Conceptually in 2-mark/3-mark questions |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Corollary I (Carnot Efficiency)

We now derive the Carnot efficiency from first principles, assuming a **reversible Carnot cycle** consisting of four processes:
1. **1→2:** Isothermal heat addition at $T_H$
2. **2→3:** Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) expansion
3. **3→4:** Isothermal heat rejection at $T_C$
4. **4→1:** Isentropic compression

#### **Step 1: First Law for a Cycle**

For any cyclic device, the net work equals the net heat transfer (energy conservation):
$$W_{net} = Q_{net} = Q_H - Q_C$$

where $Q_H$ is the heat absorbed from the hot reservoir during process 1→2, and $Q_C$ is the heat rejected to the cold reservoir during process 3→4.

---

#### **Step 2: Compute $Q_H$ (Isothermal Expansion at $T_H$)**

For an ideal gas undergoing isothermal expansion at $T_H$, the change in internal energy is zero ($\Delta U = 0$ for isothermal ideal gas). Therefore, by the First Law, $Q_H = W_{12}$:

$$Q_H = W_{12} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{mRT_H}{V} \, dV$$

This is a definite integral of the form $\int \frac{1}{V} dV = \ln(V)$. Evaluating the limits:

$$Q_H = mRT_H \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

The sign convention here is that $Q_H > 0$ (heat into the system) when $V_2 > V_1$ (expansion). We have used:
- $PV = mRT$ (Ideal Gas Equation of State)
- $\Delta U = 0$ for isothermal process of an ideal gas

---

#### **Step 3: Compute $Q_C$ (Isothermal Compression at $T_C$)**

By the identical logic, but now for the *rejection* process at the lower temperature $T_C$:

$$Q_C = mRT_C \ln\!\left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)$$

Because this is a *rejection*, and for a Carnot cycle $V_3/V_4 = V_2/V_1$ (by isentropic relations), the heat rejected to the cold reservoir is:

$$Q_C = mRT_C \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Geometric Identity:** In a Carnot cycle on a P-V diagram, the volume ratios $V_2/V_1$ and $V_3/V_4$ are equal. This comes from the isentropic relations $TV^{\gamma-1} = $ const, applied between states 2–3 and 4–1.

---

#### **Step 4: Form the Efficiency Ratio**

By definition, thermal efficiency is:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_C}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_C}{Q_H}$$

Substituting our expressions for $Q_H$ and $Q_C$:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{mRT_C \ln(V_2/V_1)}{mRT_H \ln(V_2/V_1)}$$

The mass $m$, gas constant $R$, and logarithmic term all **cancel out** because they appear identically in numerator and denominator:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The cancellation of $m$, $R$, and $V_2/V_1$ is the mathematical proof of Corollary I.** The efficiency depends *only* on the reservoir temperatures. The choice of working fluid, the mass, the gas constant — none of it matters for a *reversible* cycle.

---

#### **Step 5: Geometric Interpretation**

The ratio $Q_C/Q_H = T_C/T_H$ can be visualized on a **T-s (Temperature-Entropy) diagram** of the Carnot cycle. The cycle appears as a **rectangle**:
- Bottom edge: heat rejection $Q_C = T_C \cdot (s_2 - s_1)$
- Top edge: heat addition $Q_H = T_H \cdot (s_2 - s_1)$
- Vertical edges: isentropic (zero heat transfer)

The efficiency is then:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C \cdot \Delta s}{T_H \cdot \Delta s} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Proof of Corollary II (No Engine Beats Carnot)

This is the proof most often asked in KTU 14-mark questions. We proceed via the **composite-engine contradiction argument**.

#### **Setup**

Let us posit two engines:
- **Engine A:** Reversible Carnot engine with efficiency $\eta_R$
- **Engine B:** An arbitrary (potentially irreversible) engine with efficiency $\eta_I$

Both operate between the same $T_H$ and $T_C$. **Assume for contradiction that $\eta_I > \eta_R$.**

---

#### **Step 1: Adjust the Heat Inputs**

To make the proof clean, we adjust the two engines so that they each deliver the **same amount of work** $W$. This is a standard technique in thermodynamic proofs.

If $\eta_I > \eta_R$, then for the same work output, the irreversible engine requires **less** heat input:
$$Q_{H,\,I} = \frac{W}{\eta_I} < Q_{H,\,R} = \frac{W}{\eta_R}$$

So the irreversible engine draws $Q_{H,\,I}$ from $T_H$, and the reversible engine draws more $Q_{H,\,R}$ from $T_H$.

---

#### **Step 2: Reverse the Reversible Engine**

Since Engine A is reversible, we can run it **backwards** as a refrigerator/heat pump. The reversed engine:
- Receives work input $W$ (same as Engine B's output)
- Absorbs $Q_{C,\,R}$ from the cold reservoir at $T_C$
- Rejects $Q_{H,\,R}$ to the hot reservoir at $T_H$

---

#### **Step 3: Composite System Energy Balance**

The composite system = (Engine B + Reversed Engine A).

| **Reservoir** | **Heat In** | **Heat Out** | **Net** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hot ($T_H$) | $Q_{H,\,I}$ (taken by B) | $Q_{H,\,R}$ (deposited by reversed A) | $Q_{H,\,R} - Q_{H,\,I} > 0$ **(deposited)** |
| Cold ($T_C$) | $Q_{C,\,R}$ (taken by reversed A) | $Q_{C,\,I}$ (deposited by B) | $Q_{C,\,I} - Q_{C,\,R}$ |
| Work | $W$ (into reversed A) | $W$ (out of B) | $0$ **(cancels)** |

By the First Law applied to the *individual* engines, we can find the relationship. For Engine B:
$$W = Q_{H,\,I} - Q_{C,\,I} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{C,\,I} = Q_{H,\,I} - W$$

For Reversed Engine A:
$$W = Q_{H,\,R} - Q_{C,\,R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{C,\,R} = Q_{H,\,R} - W$$

Net heat at cold reservoir:
$$Q_{C,\,I} - Q_{C,\,R} = (Q_{H,\,I} - W) - (Q_{H,\,R} - W) = Q_{H,\,I} - Q_{H,\,R}$$

Since $Q_{H,\,I} < Q_{H,\,R}$, this net is **negative**, meaning the cold reservoir loses $Q_{H,\,R} - Q_{H,\,I}$ amount of heat.

So overall, the composite device **only exchanges heat with the hot reservoir** at $T_H$ — net heat is deposited to the hot reservoir, and **zero net work** is exchanged with the surroundings.

---

#### **Step 4: Detect the Second Law Violation**

The composite device is a **cyclic device** that:
- Exchanges net heat $Q_{H,\,R} - Q_{H,\,I} > 0$ with a **single reservoir** at $T_H$
- Produces **zero net work**
- Operates in a cycle

This is a **direct violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement** of the Second Law, which states:

> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a single body to do work."*

Wait — actually our composite device transfers heat *to* a single body and does *no* net work. The strict Kelvin-Planck statement says no device can produce *positive work* from a single reservoir. Let us carefully state the equivalent Clausius version: 

> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

The composite device is equivalent to this: net heat is moved from $T_C$ (cold) to $T_H$ (hot) without any external work — a spontaneous heat flow from cold to hot, which violates the Clausius statement.

---

#### **Step 5: Conclude the Contradiction**

Since the assumption $\eta_I > \eta_R$ leads to a Second Law violation, the assumption must be **false**. Therefore:
$$\eta_I \leq \eta_R$$

Moreover, if Engine B were also reversible, we could swap the roles and get $\eta_R \leq \eta_B$, hence:
$$\eta_R = \eta_B \quad \text{(both reversible)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The proof is complete.** This is the *Gold Standard* 14-mark question answer for KTU Module 2.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Implications of Carnot's Theorem

| **Implication** | **Mathematical Form** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum possible efficiency | $\eta_{max} = 1 - T_C/T_H$ | No engine can exceed this |
| Required heat ratio | $Q_H/Q_C = T_H/T_C$ | For a Carnot cycle |
| Second Law quantified | $\eta_I < 1 - T_C/T_H$ | Real engines always fall short |
| Reversibility equivalence | $\eta_{R1} = \eta_{R2}$ | Independent of working substance |
| Heat pump COP ceiling | $COP_{HP} \leq T_H/(T_H - T_C)$ | Practical design target |
| Refrigerator COP ceiling | $COP_{Ref} \leq T_C/(T_H - T_C)$ | Practical design target |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A Carnot heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H = 800$ K and a cold reservoir at $T_C = 300$ K. If the engine receives $Q_H = 1000$ kJ of heat, determine:
(i) The thermal efficiency.
(ii) The net work output.
(iii) The heat rejected to the cold reservoir.
(iv) The COP if this engine is reversed and used as a refrigerator.

**Solution:**

**(i) Thermal efficiency:**
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 62.5\%$$

**(ii) Net work output:**
$$W_{net} = \eta_{Carnot} \times Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000 = 625 \;\; \text{kJ}$$

**(iii) Heat rejected:**
$$Q_C = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000 - 625 = 375 \;\; \text{kJ}$$

**Verification using direct ratio:**
$$Q_C = Q_H \times \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1000 \times \frac{300}{800} = 375 \;\; \text{kJ} \;\; \checkmark$$

**(iv) COP as refrigerator:**
$$COP_{Ref} = \frac{T_C}{T_H - T_C} = \frac{300}{800 - 300} = \frac{300}{500} = 0.6$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** For every 1 kJ of work input to this refrigerator, 0.6 kJ of heat is removed from the cold space. To extract 100 kJ of cooling, the refrigerator requires $100/0.6 \approx 166.67$ kJ of work.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Carnot Theorem — Composite-Engine Proof Flow

The following Mermaid diagram traces the logical flow of the **Corollary II proof** (no irreversible engine beats the reversible Carnot engine). This is the most common diagram expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Two engines A and B] --> B{Both operate between TH and TC}
    B --> C[Engine A: Reversible, efficiency etaR]
    B --> D[Engine B: Arbitrary, efficiency etaI]
    C --> E[Assume etaI greater than etaR for contradiction]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Adjust both to deliver equal work W]
    F --> G[Heat required: QHI less than QHR]
    G --> H[Reverse Engine A as a heat pump]
    H --> I[Couple: Engine B drives Reversed A]
    I --> J[Net composite system: cyclic, zero net work]
    J --> K[Calculate net heat exchange with reservoirs]
    K --> L{Net heat flow direction}
    L --> M[Heat extracted from TC and dumped to TH]
    M --> N[Equivalent to Clausius violation]
    N --> O[Second Law is violated]
    O --> P[Contradiction detected]
    P --> Q[Therefore: etaI less than or equal to etaR]
    Q --> R[If B is also reversible: etaI equals etaR]
    R --> S[End: Carnot Theorem II Proved]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style S fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style O fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style P fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style N fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
```

---

### 4.2 Carnot Cycle on T-s and P-V Diagrams (Block Architecture)

Since the physical P-V and T-s diagrams require precise curve drawing, we provide a **block-level functional architecture** describing the energy interactions and state transitions of the Carnot cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph State1[State 1: Low T, Low V]
        S1A[Pressure: P1 High]
        S1B[Temperature: TC]
        S1C[Entropy: s1]
    end

    subgraph Proc12[Process 1-2: Isothermal Expansion at TH]
        P12A[Heat QH absorbed from TH]
        P12B[Work done by gas]
        P12C[Internal energy: constant]
    end

    subgraph State2[State 2: High T, High V]
        S2A[Pressure: P2 lower than P1]
        S2B[Temperature: TH]
        S2C[Entropy: s2 greater than s1]
    end

    subgraph Proc23[Process 2-3: Isentropic Expansion]
        P23A[No heat transfer]
        P23B[Temperature drops TH to TC]
        P23C[Pressure drops P2 to P3]
    end

    subgraph State3[State 3: Low T, High V]
        S3A[Pressure: P3 lowest]
        S3B[Temperature: TC]
        S3C[Entropy: s2 equals s3]
    end

    subgraph Proc34[Process 3-4: Isothermal Compression at TC]
        P34A[Heat QC rejected to TC]
        P34B[Work done on gas]
        P34C[Internal energy: constant]
    end

    subgraph State4[State 4: Low T, Low V]
        S4A[Pressure: P4 high]
        S4B[Temperature: TC]
        S4C[Entropy: s4 equals s1]
    end

    subgraph Proc41[Process 4-1: Isentropic Compression]
        P41A[No heat transfer]
        P41B[Temperature rises TC to TH]
        P41C[Pressure rises P4 to P1]
    end

    S1A --> P12A
    P12A --> S2A
    S2A --> P23A
    P23A --> S3A
    S3A --> P34A
    P34A --> S4A
    S4A --> P41A
    P41A --> S1A

    style State1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style State2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
    style State3 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style State4 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style Proc12 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style Proc23 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style Proc34 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style Proc41 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

---

### 4.3 Heat Flow Architecture in the Composite Proof

This block diagram shows the **energy and entropy flows** when the irreversible engine and the reversed reversible engine are coupled together.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HR[Hot Reservoir at TH]
        HRLabel[Temperature TH Kelvin]
    end

    subgraph CR[Cold Reservoir at TC]
        CRLabel[Temperature TC Kelvin]
    end

    subgraph EI[Engine B - Irreversible]
        EILabel[Efficiency etaI greater than etaR]
    end

    subgraph ERA[Reversed Engine A - Heat Pump]
        ERALabel[Reversible, run backward]
    end

    HR -->|QHI absorbed| EI
    EI -->|Work W produced| ERA
    ERA -->|QHR rejected| HR
    ERA -->|QCR absorbed| CR
    EI -->|QCI rejected| CR

    QHI_Style[QHI less than QHR]:::note
    QCI_Style[QCI less than QCR]:::note

    classDef note fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    classDef violation fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    classDef ok fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32

    HR:::ok
    CR:::ok
    EI:::violation
    ERA:::ok
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The irreversible engine (Engine B) takes $Q_{H,I}$ from the hot reservoir, produces work $W$, and dumps $Q_{C,I}$ to the cold reservoir. The *reversed* reversible engine uses that work $W$ to pump $Q_{C,R}$ from the cold reservoir and dump $Q_{H,R}$ to the hot reservoir. The net effect is that heat is moved from cold to hot with no external work — a Clausius violation if $\eta_I > \eta_R$.

---

### 4.4 Concept Map — Carnot Theorem Hierarchy

```mermaid
mindmap
    root((Carnot Theorem))
        Corollary I
            Reversible efficiency is universal
            Depends only on TH and TC
            Formula etaR equals 1 minus TC over TH
        Corollary II
            No irreversible engine can beat Carnot
            Mathematical form etaI less than etaR
            Proof via composite engine
        Proof Techniques
            Composite engine method
            Clausius inequality application
            Kelvin Planck violation
        Applications
            Rankine cycle analysis
            Otto and Diesel cycles
            Refrigeration COP limits
            Heat pump design
        Key Relations
            QH over QC equals TH over TC
            COP HP equals TH over delta T
            COP Ref equals TC over delta T
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**

**Q1. State and prove Carnot's Theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carnot's Theorem Statement:**
>
> *Corollary I:* The efficiency of a reversible heat engine operating between two given reservoirs is independent of the working substance and depends only on the reservoir temperatures.
>
> *Corollary II:* No irreversible heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible heat engine operating between them.
>
> **Mathematical form:**
> $$\eta_{I} \leq \eta_{R} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$
>
> where $T$ denotes absolute (Kelvin) temperatures. The equality holds if and only if the engine is reversible.

**Proof Sketch (Corollary II):** Consider two engines $E_R$ (reversible) and $E_I$ (irreversible) between $T_H$ and $T_C$. Assume $\eta_I > \eta_R$. Adjust them to deliver equal work $W$. Run $E_R$ in reverse. The composite device transfers heat from $T_C$ to $T_H$ without net work, violating the Clausius statement of the Second Law. Hence $\eta_I \leq \eta_R$. If $E_I$ is also reversible, swapping roles gives $\eta_R \leq \eta_I$, so $\eta_R = \eta_I$.

**(End of Answer — 3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**

**Q2. A Carnot engine operates between 600 K and 300 K. If it receives 500 kJ of heat, calculate the work output and heat rejected.**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $T_H = 600$ K, $T_C = 300$ K, $Q_H = 500$ kJ.

**Step 1:** Compute Carnot efficiency.
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{600} = 0.5 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 50\%$$

**Step 2:** Compute work output.
$$W_{net} = \eta_{Carnot} \times Q_H = 0.5 \times 500 = 250 \;\; \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3:** Compute heat rejected.
$$Q_C = Q_H - W_{net} = 500 - 250 = 250 \;\; \text{kJ}$$

**Verification:** $Q_C = Q_H \times (T_C/T_H) = 500 \times (300/600) = 250$ kJ ✓

**(End of Answer — 3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** State and prove Carnot's theorem with the help of a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Carnot refrigerator operates between a cold space at $-10°C$ and ambient at $35°C$. The refrigerator removes 1500 kJ of heat from the cold space in one hour. Determine:
   (i) The COP of the refrigerator.
   (ii) The power required to drive the refrigerator.
   (iii) The heat rejected to the ambient per hour. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Statement and Proof of Carnot's Theorem (7 marks):**

**Statement:** *No irreversible heat engine operating between two given reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs.* Mathematically:
$$\eta_{I} \leq \eta_{R} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

**Proof by composite engine method:**

1. Consider two engines: $E_R$ (reversible, Carnot) and $E_I$ (irreversible) operating between $T_H$ and $T_C$.
2. Assume $\eta_I > \eta_R$.
3. Adjust both to deliver equal work $W$. Then $Q_{H,I} = W/\eta_I < Q_{H,R} = W/\eta_R$.
4. Run $E_R$ in reverse as a heat pump.
5. Couple $E_I$ and reversed $E_R$. The composite system:
   - Takes $Q_{H,I}$ from hot reservoir
   - Returns $Q_{H,R}$ to hot reservoir (more than it took)
   - Takes $Q_{C,R}$ from cold reservoir (by reversed $E_R$)
   - Returns $Q_{C,I}$ to cold reservoir (less than it took)
6. Net effect: heat is transferred from cold reservoir to hot reservoir with **no external work**.
7. This violates the **Clausius statement** of the Second Law.
8. Contradiction. Therefore $\eta_I \leq \eta_R$.

**Sketch:** (See Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.3 above — the composite engine diagram.)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given:
- $T_C = -10°C = 263.15$ K
- $T_H = 35°C = 308.15$ K
- $Q_C = 1500$ kJ/hr (cooling load)

**(i) COP of refrigerator:**
$$COP_{Ref} = \frac{T_C}{T_H - T_C} = \frac{263.15}{308.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{45} = 5.848$$

**(ii) Power required:**
By definition, $COP_{Ref} = Q_C/W_{in}$, so:
$$W_{in} = \frac{Q_C}{COP_{Ref}} = \frac{1500}{5.848} = 256.5 \;\; \text{kJ/hr}$$

Converting to power (divide by 3600 seconds):
$$\dot{W}_{in} = \frac{256.5}{3600} = 0.0713 \;\; \text{kW} = 71.3 \;\; \text{W}$$

**(iii) Heat rejected to ambient per hour:**
By energy balance:
$$Q_H = Q_C + W_{in} = 1500 + 256.5 = 1756.5 \;\; \text{kJ/hr}$$

**Verification using $Q_H/Q_C = T_H/T_C$:**
$$Q_H = Q_C \times \frac{T_H}{T_C} = 1500 \times \frac{308.15}{263.15} = 1500 \times 1.1710 = 1756.5 \;\; \text{kJ/hr} \;\; \checkmark$$

**(End of Answer A — 14 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the efficiency of a Carnot cycle and show that it depends only on the reservoir temperatures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A power plant operates on a Carnot cycle with a maximum temperature of 800 K and rejects heat to a river at 290 K. If the plant produces 5000 kJ of work per cycle:
   (i) Calculate the cycle efficiency.
   (ii) Find the heat supplied per cycle.
   (iii) Find the heat rejected per cycle.
   (iv) If a real (irreversible) engine operates between the same reservoirs and produces the same work, with a measured efficiency of 30%, comment on its performance relative to the Carnot engine. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Carnot Efficiency (7 marks):**

The Carnot cycle consists of:
- Process 1→2: Isothermal heat addition at $T_H$
- Process 2→3: Isentropic expansion
- Process 3→4: Isothermal heat rejection at $T_C$
- Process 4→1: Isentropic compression

For process 1→2 (isothermal expansion of an ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0$):
$$Q_H = W_{12} = mRT_H \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

For process 3→4 (isothermal compression at $T_C$):
$$Q_C = mRT_C \ln\!\left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right) = mRT_C \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

(The last equality uses the isentropic relations of a Carnot cycle, which give $V_3/V_4 = V_2/V_1$.)

By First Law for the cycle:
$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_C$$

Thermal efficiency:
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_C}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{mRT_C \ln(V_2/V_1)}{mRT_H \ln(V_2/V_1)}$$

After cancellation:
$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}}$$

Since $m$, $R$, and the volume ratio all cancel, the efficiency depends **solely** on $T_H$ and $T_C$ — the working fluid, mass, and gas constant are irrelevant. This proves Corollary I.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $T_H = 800$ K, $T_C = 290$ K, $W_{net} = 5000$ kJ.

**(i) Carnot efficiency:**
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{290}{800} = 1 - 0.3625 = 0.6375 = 63.75\%$$

**(ii) Heat supplied per cycle:**
$$\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_H = \frac{W_{net}}{\eta_{Carnot}} = \frac{5000}{0.6375} = 7843.14 \;\; \text{kJ}$$

**(iii) Heat rejected per cycle:**
$$Q_C = Q_H - W_{net} = 7843.14 - 5000 = 2843.14 \;\; \text{kJ}$$

**Verification:** $Q_C = Q_H \times (T_C/T_H) = 7843.14 \times (290/800) = 2843.14$ kJ ✓

**(iv) Comment on the irreversible engine:**

The irreversible engine has $\eta_I = 30\% = 0.30$, while the Carnot (reversible) engine has $\eta_R = 63.75\%$.

Since $\eta_I < \eta_R$ (i.e., $0.30 < 0.6375$), this is consistent with Carnot's Theorem Corollary II. The irreversible engine is *less efficient* than the Carnot engine, as expected.

**Efficiency deficit:**
$$\Delta\eta = \eta_R - \eta_I = 0.6375 - 0.30 = 0.3375 = 33.75\%$$

For the same work output of 5000 kJ, the irreversible engine would need:
$$Q_{H,\,I} = \frac{W_{net}}{\eta_I} = \frac{5000}{0.30} = 16666.67 \;\; \text{kJ}$$

This is **112% more heat input** than the Carnot engine ($16666.67$ vs $7843.14$ kJ) — illustrating the practical penalty of irreversibilities.

**(End of Answer B — 14 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Temperature in Celsius (–3 to –5 marks):** Using $T_C$ and $T_H$ in °C instead of **Kelvin**. *Always convert*: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
>
> 2. **Confusing heat pump and refrigerator COP (–2 marks):** Heat pump COP uses $T_H$ in the numerator; refrigerator COP uses $T_C$. Memorize both.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the First Law balance (–2 marks):** $Q_H = Q_C + W$ is the energy balance. Many students compute $W$ but forget to compute $Q_C$ (or vice versa).
>
> 4. **Skipping the proof sketch (–2 marks):** In 14-mark questions, the proof of Carnot's Theorem *must* include a labeled diagram of the composite engine setup. Without it, full marks are rarely awarded.
>
> 5. **Assuming the Second Law is "obvious" (–3 marks):** The composite-engine proof is the *rigorous* part. State explicitly which statement of the Second Law is violated (Kelvin-Planck or Clausius) and explain why.
>
> 6. **Ignoring reversibility in Cor. I proof (–1 to –2 marks):** When deriving that efficiency is independent of working fluid, you must explicitly state "the cancellation of the working fluid properties follows from the *reversibility* of the cycle."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Carnot's Theorem**
>
> - ⭐ **Two Corollaries:** I (universality of reversible efficiency) and II (irreversibility penalty).
> - ⭐ **Key Formula:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_C/T_H$ — temperatures in **Kelvin only**.
> - ⭐ **Universal Bound:** For any engine between $T_H$ and $T_C$: $\eta \leq 1 - T_C/T_H$.
> - ⭐ **COP Formulas:** $COP_{HP} = T_H/\Delta T$, $COP_{Ref} = T_C/\Delta T$, where $\Delta T = T_H - T_C$.
> - ⭐ **Heat Ratio Law:** $Q_H/Q_C = T_H/T_C$ (only for reversible/Carnot cycles).
> - ⭐ **Proof Technique:** Composite engine + reverse of reversible engine = Second Law violation.
> - ⭐ **The cancellation** of $m$, $R$, and $V_2/V_1$ in the derivation is the *mathematical heart* of Corollary I.
> - ⭐ **T-s diagram is a rectangle** for a Carnot cycle (a useful visual mnemonic).
> - ⭐ **Carnot efficiency is the upper limit**, never achieved by real engines due to friction, finite $\Delta T$ heat transfer, and non-quasi-static processes.
> - ⭐ **Exergy destruction** in any irreversible engine is $T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$, which is why we quantify entropy generation in subsequent modules.

---

### 5.5 Quick-Recall Memory Aid

> **Mnemonic for COP formulas:** *"Heat Pump is Hot-side, Refrigerator is Cold-side."* — $COP_{HP} = T_H/\Delta T$ and $COP_{Ref} = T_C/\Delta T$.
>
> **Mnemonic for the proof:** *"ASSume, COuple, REverse, OOps (Second Law violated), CONclude."* — ASSUMPTION → COUPLING → REVERSAL → SECOND LAW VIOLATION → CONCLUSION.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corollary of Carnot Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corollary of Carnot Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Corollaries of Carnot's Theorem** are two fundamental logical consequences that follow directly from Carnot's Theorem and the Second Law of Thermodynamics. They form the cornerstone of all thermal efficiency calculations and define the theoretical limits of heat engine performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carnot's Theorem (Pre-requisite):** *No heat engine operating between two given thermal reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs.*

From this theorem, **two corollaries** emerge, which are absolutely essential for KTU board examinations and competitive GATE-level problem solving.

---

### **Corollary 1 — The Irreversibility Inequality**

> **Formal Statement:** *The efficiency of an irreversible heat engine is always strictly less than the efficiency of a reversible heat engine, when both operate between the same two thermal reservoirs (same $T_H$ and $T_L$).*

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\eta_{\text{irrev}} < \eta_{\text{rev}}$$

This corollary establishes the **upper bound** of engine efficiency. It tells engineers that *real* (always irreversible) engines will always fall short of the ideal limit.

---

### **Corollary 2 — Universality of Reversible Efficiency**

> **Formal Statement:** *All reversible heat engines operating between the same two thermal reservoirs have exactly the same efficiency, regardless of the working fluid, cycle architecture, or construction details.*

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\eta_{\text{rev},1} = \eta_{\text{rev},2} = \eta_{\text{rev},3} = \ldots = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

This is a profound result — it means efficiency is a property **only of the reservoir temperatures**, not the engineering design.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like exam grading with two difficulty levels:**

Imagine a university where there are two grading policies:
- **Policy A (Irreversible):** Manual checking by tired professors. Errors, subjective bias, and inconsistency creep in.
- **Policy B (Reversible):** A perfect, ideal, omnipotent grader who never makes mistakes and can reverse any decision.

- **Corollary 1** says: *The perfect grader (reversible) will ALWAYS score the most correct answers for a given amount of time/study. Any real, tired professor (irreversible) will always do worse.*
- **Corollary 2** says: *If you had TWO perfect omnipotent graders, they would BOTH give the exact same score — because perfection is perfection, no matter who the "perfect entity" is.*

In thermal terms: **Perfect reversibility → efficiency is a function of temperatures only**.

A practical engineering analogy is the **waterwheel between two reservoirs** — the maximum work extractable depends only on the height difference (analogue of $T_H - T_L$), not on the wheel's material or design, as long as the process is ideal.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Higher Reservoir Temperature | $T_H$ | Kelvin (K) | Source temperature |
| Lower Reservoir Temperature | $T_L$ | Kelvin (K) | Sink temperature |
| Carnot (Maximum) Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}}$ | Dimensionless (%) | Theoretical upper limit |
| Absolute Zero | $0\,\text{K}$ | $-273.15\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Reference point for $T_L$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Trap:** Temperatures in the Carnot efficiency formula **MUST be in Kelvin (K)**, not Celsius. A $50\,^\circ\text{C}$ mistake in temperature conversion can flip the entire answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### **Proof Logic of Corollary 1 (Irreversibility Inequality)**

The proof follows the classical **proof by contradiction** method, which is the expected KTU valuation pattern.

**Step 1 — Assume the contrary:**
Suppose there exist two engines, Engine $A$ (irreversible) and Engine $R$ (reversible), both operating between the same reservoirs at $T_H$ and $T_L$, such that:

$$\eta_A > \eta_R \quad \text{(assumption to be contradicted)}$$

**Step 2 — Couple them for heat flow analysis:**
Run Engine $R$ **in reverse** (as a refrigerator/heat pump) using the work output of Engine $A$ as input.

**Step 3 — Apply energy balance:**
Since $\eta_A > \eta_R$, for the same heat input $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir, Engine $A$ produces more work than Engine $R$ consumes in reverse.

**Step 4 — Detect a Second Law violation:**
The *net* effect is heat transferred **from the cold reservoir to the hot reservoir** with **no external work input** — this is a direct violation of the **Clausius Statement** of the Second Law.

**Step 5 — Reject the assumption:**
Therefore, the assumption is false. Hence:

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{irrev}} < \eta_{\text{rev}}}$$

---

### **Proof Logic of Corollary 2 (Universality of Reversible Efficiency)**

**Step 1 — Set up the scenario:**
Take two reversible engines, $R_1$ and $R_2$, both between the same $T_H$ and $T_L$.

**Step 2 — Run $R_2$ in reverse** using $R_1$ as the driver.

**Step 3 — Apply the same proof by contradiction:**
If $\eta_{R_1} > \eta_{R_2}$, then by the same logic as Corollary 1, we violate the Second Law. The contradiction forces:

$$\eta_{R_1} \leq \eta_{R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Reverse the roles:**
By symmetry, if we now reverse $R_1$ and drive it with $R_2$:

$$\eta_{R_2} \leq \eta_{R_1}$$

**Step 5 — Combine both inequalities:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{R_1} = \eta_{R_2}}$$

This proves that **all reversible engines have identical efficiency** when operating between the same two thermal reservoirs — the efficiency is purely a function of the reservoir temperatures.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the ONLY formulas you need for this topic in KTU exams.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{rev}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot (maximum) efficiency | $T_H, T_L$ absolute temps | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{irrev}} < \eta_{\text{rev}}$ | Corollary 1 (irreversibility) | — | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{R_1} = \eta_{R_2}$ | Corollary 2 (universality) | Both reversible | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_H}$ | Definition of thermal efficiency | $W, Q_H$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q_L = Q_H \cdot \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Heat rejected (reversible) | $Q_H$ in J or kJ | J or kJ |
| 6 | $W_{\text{net}} = Q_H - Q_L$ | Energy balance (First Law) | $Q$ | J or kJ |
| 7 | $COP_{\text{Carnot,HP}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible Heat Pump COP | $T_H, T_L$ in K | dimensionless |
| 8 | $COP_{\text{Carnot,Ref}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible Refrigerator COP | $T_H, T_L$ in K | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T(\text{K}) = t(^\circ\text{C}) + 273.15$ | Kelvin conversion | temperature | K |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT** use the vertical bar symbol `|` inside any formula in your exam sheet — always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in proper LaTeX notation.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The corollaries have immense practical applications:

1. **Power Plant Design:** Engineers use Corollary 1 to **quantify losses** due to irreversibilities (friction, turbulence, finite temperature difference heat exchange). The gap between actual and Carnot efficiency is the *lost work potential*, also called **exergy destruction**.

2. **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Corollary 2 allows using a *universal benchmark* ($COP_{\text{Carnot}}$) to rate any refrigerator, regardless of refrigerant (R-134a, R-410A, ammonia, CO₂, etc.). The closer a real refrigerator gets to the Carnot COP, the better it is.

3. **Combined Cycle & Cogeneration:** The universal Carnot efficiency sets the *ceiling*, helping engineers decide whether investing in additional stages (reheat, regeneration) is thermodynamically justified.

4. **Cryogenics & LNG:** Establishes the fundamental minimum work required for any cooling process — critical for designing liquefaction cycles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Corollaries of Carnot Theorem via Proof by Contradiction

> This derivation is the **single most expected 14-mark question** pattern in KTU Module 2 of PCMUT403.

### **Setup**

Consider the following idealized arrangement:
- **Engine A:** An *irreversible* heat engine operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$.
- **Engine R:** A *reversible* heat engine operating between the *same* $T_H$ and $T_L$.

Both engines draw heat $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir at $T_H$ and reject $Q_L$ to the cold reservoir at $T_L$.

Their respective thermal efficiencies are:

$$\eta_A = \frac{W_A}{Q_H} \quad \text{(irreversible engine)}$$

$$\eta_R = \frac{W_R}{Q_H} \quad \text{(reversible engine)}$$

---

### **STEP 1 — Assume the contrary (for contradiction)**

To prove Corollary 1, we must show that $\eta_A \not> \eta_R$.

Suppose, for the sake of contradiction, that the irreversible engine is more efficient:

$$\eta_A > \eta_R$$

This implies, for the *same* heat input $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir:

$$W_A > W_R$$

---

### **STEP 2 — Couple the engines (reversible engine in reverse)**

Now we couple the two engines such that the irreversible Engine A **drives** the reversible Engine R backwards (i.e., as a heat pump / refrigerator).

- Engine A (forward): Takes $Q_H$ from $T_H$, produces work $W_A$, rejects $Q_A = Q_H - W_A$ to $T_L$.
- Engine R (reverse): Receives work $W_R$ as input, takes $Q_R$ from $T_L$, and rejects $Q_H' = Q_R + W_R$ to $T_H$.

Since Engine R is reversible, its efficiency running forward equals its $COP$ running in reverse (Carnot equivalence principle):

$$\frac{W_R}{Q_H} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_R}{W_R} \quad \text{(reversible coupling)}$$

---

### **STEP 3 — Energy balance of the combined system**

Let us now examine the *net* exchange of heat with the two reservoirs.

**Net work exchange with surroundings:**
The work $W_R$ consumed by the reversed Engine R is provided by Engine A's output $W_A$.

$$W_{\text{net,external}} = W_A - W_R > 0 \quad \text{(since } W_A > W_R\text{)}$$

This net work leaves the combined system as useful work output.

**Net heat exchange with HOT reservoir at $T_H$:**

$$Q_{H,\text{net}} = Q_H - Q_H' = Q_H - Q_R - W_R$$

Using the reversible relationship $Q_R = W_R \cdot \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ (from COP formula), and $Q_H = \dfrac{W_R}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \dfrac{W_R (T_H - T_L)}{T_H}$:

$$Q_{H,\text{net}} = \frac{W_R (T_H - T_L)}{T_H} - W_R \cdot \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} - W_R$$

**Net heat exchange with COLD reservoir at $T_L$:**

$$Q_{L,\text{net}} = Q_A - Q_R = (Q_H - W_A) - Q_R$$

---

### **STEP 4 — Detect the Second Law violation**

After algebraic simplification (which KTU examiners expect you to show), one finds that:

**The net effect on the cold reservoir is heat being extracted from it, and on the hot reservoir is heat being deposited into it, with net positive work output — equivalent to a spontaneous Clausius violation.**

In formal notation, the combined system acts as a self-running heat pump:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} > 0$$

This violates the **Clausius Inequality**:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

---

### **STEP 5 — Reject the assumption**

The only escape from violating the Second Law is to reject our initial assumption. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\eta_A \not> \eta_R \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \eta_A \leq \eta_R}$$

Since Engine A is **irreversible** (strict inequality in real systems):

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{irrev}} < \eta_{\text{rev}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$$

---

### **STEP 6 — Corollary 2 follows by symmetry**

Replace Engine A with another reversible Engine $R_2$. The same proof gives $\eta_{R_1} \leq \eta_{R_2}$. Reversing the roles gives $\eta_{R_2} \leq \eta_{R_1}$. Combining:

$$\eta_{R_1} = \eta_{R_2} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table for KTU Revisions

| Property | Reversible Engine | Irreversible Engine |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency vs. Carnot | $\eta_{\text{rev}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}}$ | $\eta_{\text{irrev}} < \eta_{\text{Carnot}}$ |
| Magnitude | Maximum possible | Strictly less |
| Achievable in practice? | **No** (idealized) | **Yes** (real engines) |
| Heat transfer mechanism | Quasi-static, infinitesimal $\Delta T$ | Finite $\Delta T$, friction, turbulence |
| Process on $T\text{-}s$ diagram | All process lines are **vertical/horizontal isothermal/isentropic** | Process lines deviate; area = entropy generation |
| Second Law equality | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$ | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$ |
| Working fluid dependence | **None** (Corollary 2) | **Yes** (depends on fluid + cycle) |

---

## 3.3 Sample Numerical Illustration (for conceptual clarity)

> **Problem:** A reversible Carnot engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H = 600\,\text{K}$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L = 300\,\text{K}$. Determine its efficiency.

**Solution using Corollary 2:**

$$\eta_{\text{rev}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{600} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 = 50\%$$

> **Problem:** A real (irreversible) engine operates between the same reservoirs. By Corollary 1, its efficiency is *guaranteed* to be less than $50\%$. This is a *thermodynamic certainty*, not an empirical observation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Logical Structure of the Corollaries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Second Law of Thermodynamics] --> B[Clausius Statement]
    A --> C[Kelvin-Planck Statement]
    B --> D[Carnot Theorem]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Corollary 1: eta_irrev < eta_rev]
    D --> F[Corollary 2: All eta_rev are Equal]
    E --> G[Defines Maximum Possible Efficiency]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Carnot Efficiency Formula: 1 - TL over TH]
    H --> I[Heat Engine Benchmark]
    H --> J[Refrigerator COP Benchmark]
    H --> K[Heat Pump COP Benchmark]
    E --> L[Used to Quantify Irreversibility Losses]
    E --> M[Drives Exergy Destruction Analysis]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Proof-by-Contradiction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Assume eta_A > eta_R] --> S2[Step 2: Couple A forward with R reversed]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Net work leaves combined system]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Heat moves from Cold to Hot reservoir]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Clausius Statement Violated]
    S5 --> S6[Reject Assumption]
    S6 --> R1[Conclusion: eta_irrev < eta_rev]
    S6 --> R2[Corollary 1 Proven]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture — Comparing Engine Performance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RES[Thermal Reservoirs]
        TH[Hot Reservoir TH]
        TL[Cold Reservoir TL]
    end
    subgraph ENG[Engine Block]
        E1[Reversible Engine]
        E2[Irreversible Engine]
        E3[Reversible Engine 2 different fluid]
    end
    TH --> E1
    TH --> E2
    TH --> E3
    E1 --> TL
    E2 --> TL
    E3 --> TL
    E1 --> W1[Work W1]
    E2 --> W2[Work W2]
    E3 --> W3[Work W3]
    W1 --> NOTE[Note: W1 equals W3 since both reversible. W2 is less than W1.]
    W3 --> NOTE
    W2 --> NOTE
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Decision Flow for KTU Problem Solving

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[KTU Problem: Find Efficiency] --> Q{Is the engine reversible?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Use eta = 1 - TL over TH]
    Q -->|No| S[Check if temperatures are given]
    S -->|Yes| T[Compute eta_Carnot and state eta_actual < eta_Carnot]
    S -->|No| U[Use First Law: eta = W over QH]
    R --> V[Apply Corollary 2 if comparing fluids]
    T --> W[Apply Corollary 1 for bound]
    U --> X[Solve for missing variable]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no physical $T\text{-}s$ diagram?** Because corollaries are *theoretical inequalities*, not cyclic processes. The block-level functional architecture above captures the conceptual flow of the proofs more accurately than any physical drawing would.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**State the two corollaries of Carnot's theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Corollary 1:** The efficiency of an irreversible heat engine is always less than the efficiency of a reversible heat engine operating between the same two thermal reservoirs.
> $$\eta_{\text{irrev}} < \eta_{\text{rev}}$$

> **Corollary 2:** All reversible heat engines operating between the same two thermal reservoirs have the same efficiency, irrespective of the working substance used.
> $$\eta_{\text{rev}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

*(1.5 marks each corollary — full statement + formula = 3 marks total)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`
**A reversible heat engine operates between $T_H = 800\,\text{K}$ and $T_L = 400\,\text{K}$. Find its efficiency. If another reversible engine uses a different working fluid between the same reservoirs, what is its efficiency?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

By Corollary 2, both engines must have the same efficiency:

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{400}{800} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$

**Efficiency of both engines = $50\%$** (working fluid is irrelevant — Corollary 2).

*(1.5 marks for formula application + 1.5 marks for stating the universality principle)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Slot]`

**(a)** State and prove the two corollaries of Carnot's theorem using the proof by contradiction method. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A reversible heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at $500\,^\circ\text{C}$ and a cold reservoir at $30\,^\circ\text{C}$. If the engine receives $1000\,\text{kJ}$ of heat from the hot reservoir, calculate:
   (i) The efficiency of the engine.
   (ii) The work output.
   (iii) The heat rejected to the cold reservoir. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Statement of Corollaries:**

**Corollary 1:** $\eta_{\text{irrev}} < \eta_{\text{rev}}$

**Corollary 2:** $\eta_{\text{rev},1} = \eta_{\text{rev},2}$ (same reservoirs, any working fluid)

**Proof by Contradiction:**

*Consider an irreversible engine A and a reversible engine R between the same $T_H$ and $T_L$.*

*Assume $\eta_A > \eta_R$. For the same $Q_H$, this means $W_A > W_R$.*

*Now run R in reverse using the work output of A. The net effect of the combined system is:*
- *Net work output $W_A - W_R > 0$ leaves the system*
- *Net heat is transferred from cold reservoir to hot reservoir*

*This is a direct violation of the Clausius Statement of the Second Law.*

*Hence the assumption is false, proving $\eta_A \leq \eta_R$. Since A is strictly irreversible:*

$$\eta_{\text{irrev}} < \eta_{\text{rev}}$$

*For Corollary 2, replace A with another reversible engine $R_2$ and repeat: $\eta_{R_1} \leq \eta_{R_2}$ and $\eta_{R_2} \leq \eta_{R_1}$, therefore $\eta_{R_1} = \eta_{R_2}$.*

*(3.5 marks for statements, 3.5 marks for proof logic)*

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $T_H = 500 + 273.15 = 773.15\,\text{K}$, $T_L = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15\,\text{K}$, $Q_H = 1000\,\text{kJ}$

**(i) Efficiency (using Corollary 2 — reversible):**

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{303.15}{773.15} = 1 - 0.3921 = 0.6079 \approx 60.79\%$$

**(ii) Work output:**

$$W = \eta \cdot Q_H = 0.6079 \times 1000 = 607.9\,\text{kJ}$$

**(iii) Heat rejected:**

$$Q_L = Q_H - W = 1000 - 607.9 = 392.1\,\text{kJ}$$

*(2 marks part i, 2.5 marks part ii, 2.5 marks part iii)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Main Slot]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain how the coupling of an irreversible and reversible engine leads to a Second Law violation, thus proving Corollary 1. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Two reversible heat engines, one using air and the other using helium, operate independently between $T_H = 900\,\text{K}$ and $T_L = 300\,\text{K}$. Both receive $500\,\text{kJ}$ of heat input. Compare their:
   (i) Efficiencies.
   (ii) Work outputs.
   (iii) Heat rejected. Justify your answer using the appropriate corollary. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Schematic Description (text-based for KTU board exam):**

Draw a block diagram showing:
- Hot reservoir $T_H$ on top
- Cold reservoir $T_L$ on bottom
- Engine A (irreversible) on the left — receives $Q_H$ from $T_H$, produces work $W_A$, rejects $Q_A$ to $T_L$
- Engine R (reversible) on the right — receives work $W_R$ (in reverse), takes $Q_R$ from $T_L$, rejects $Q_H'$ to $T_H$
- Coupling shaft between A and R (work transfer from A → R reversed)

**Logical Flow:**

Assume $\eta_A > \eta_R$ implies $W_A > W_R$ for the same $Q_H$.

Net work output = $W_A - W_R > 0$ (delivered externally)
Net heat to hot reservoir = $Q_H' - Q_H$
Net heat from cold reservoir = $Q_R - Q_A$

By energy balance, the system effectively pumps heat from $T_L$ to $T_H$ while producing net work — **violating Clausius Statement** (no device can transfer heat from cold to hot reservoir without external work input, here net work is *output*, not input).

Hence assumption is invalid → $\eta_A \leq \eta_R$.

*(3 marks schematic + 4 marks logical explanation)*

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $T_H = 900\,\text{K}$, $T_L = 300\,\text{K}$, $Q_H = 500\,\text{kJ}$ (same for both engines)

**Justification (Corollary 2):** All reversible engines between the same two reservoirs have **identical efficiency**, *regardless of working fluid*. Therefore, air and helium engines will perform identically.

**(i) Efficiency (common to both):**

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{900} = 1 - 0.3333 = 0.6667 \approx 66.67\%$$

**(ii) Work outputs (equal for both):**

$$W_{\text{air}} = W_{\text{He}} = \eta \cdot Q_H = 0.6667 \times 500 = 333.33\,\text{kJ}$$

**(iii) Heat rejected (equal for both):**

$$Q_{L,\text{air}} = Q_{L,\text{He}} = Q_H - W = 500 - 333.33 = 166.67\,\text{kJ}$$

> **Conclusion:** The choice of working fluid (air vs helium) makes **no difference** to the performance of a reversible engine. This is the practical engineering impact of Corollary 2.

*(2 marks corollary statement, 2 marks efficiency, 1.5 marks work, 1.5 marks heat rejection)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Temperature Unit Error (–2 marks typical):** Using Celsius directly in $\eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$ is the #1 mistake. Always convert to Kelvin FIRST.
> 2. **Confusing Corollaries (–1 to –3 marks):** Corollary 1 is about *irreversible vs reversible*. Corollary 2 is about *reversible vs reversible*. Students often mix them up. Memorize the keywords: *"Irreversibility"* and *"Universality"*.
> 3. **Skipping the contradiction step (–2 marks):** In 14-mark proofs, KTU expects *explicit assumption + rejection logic*. Do not jump from statement to conclusion.
> 4. **Forgetting the inequality direction:** $\eta_{\text{irrev}} < \eta_{\text{rev}}$, **NOT** $>$. This is a high-frequency sign error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist — read it 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **Carnot Theorem** sets the fundamental efficiency limit; its two corollaries are direct consequences.
- [x] **Corollary 1:** $\eta_{\text{irrev}} < \eta_{\text{rev}}$ — irreversibility always degrades efficiency.
- [x] **Corollary 2:** $\eta_{\text{rev},1} = \eta_{\text{rev},2} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — reversible efficiency depends ONLY on reservoir temperatures.
- [x] **Always convert to Kelvin** before applying the Carnot formula: $T(\text{K}) = t(^\circ\text{C}) + 273.15$.
- [x] **Proof technique:** Always use **proof by contradiction** — assume the opposite, couple a reversible engine in reverse, detect a Second Law (Clausius) violation, reject the assumption.
- [x] **Working fluid does NOT matter** for reversible engines (Corollary 2) — but DOES matter for irreversible ones.
- [x] **Reversible COP formulas** (frequently asked in KTU): 
  - $COP_{\text{HP,rev}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$
  - $COP_{\text{Ref,rev}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$
- [x] **Clausius Inequality** for irreversible cycles: $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$; for reversible: $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$.
- [x] **Engineering takeaway:** The Carnot efficiency is the *theoretical ceiling*; all real engines fall below it. The gap = irreversibility loss = exergy destruction.
- [x] **Common KTU marks-deduction zones:** wrong temperature units, swapped corollary statements, missing contradiction logic, missing final boxed answer.
- [x] **Mnemonic for Corollaries:**
  - *Corollary 1 = "I < R"* (Irreversible < Reversible)
  - *Corollary 2 = "R = R"* (Reversible = Reversible, same $T_H, T_L$)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamic Temperature Scale. Concept of Entropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamic Temperature Scale. Concept of Entropy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Thermodynamic Temperature Scale — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermodynamic Temperature Scale (Kelvin Scale):** A temperature scale defined purely from the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** using a reversible heat engine (Carnot cycle), independent of the physical properties of any thermometric substance. It is the **fundamental (absolute) temperature scale** universally adopted in science and engineering.

Mathematically, the ratio of thermodynamic temperatures of two reservoirs in contact with a reversible heat engine is defined as:

$$ \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} $$

where $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are the heats exchanged reversibly with the two reservoirs (in absolute value).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The triple point of water is fixed at exactly **273.16 K**, which defines the size of one kelvin. Hence the thermodynamic scale is **identical in magnitude** to the ideal gas scale, but its derivation rests on the **Second Law**, not on gas properties.

### 1.2 Concept of Entropy — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Entropy (S):** A thermodynamic property (state function) representing the **quantitative measure of microscopic disorder** or **energy unavailability** in a system. Mathematically, it is defined via a reversible differential heat transfer divided by the absolute thermodynamic temperature.

For a closed system undergoing an internally reversible process:

$$ dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} $$

For a finite change between two equilibrium states:

$$ \Delta S = \int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} $$

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

**Thermodynamic Temperature — The Elevator Analogy**
Imagine a reversible heat engine as an "energy elevator" moving heat between two thermal reservoirs. The temperature of a reservoir is like the **floor number** of a building — the higher the floor (temperature), the more heat "lifts" between floors for the same "work input." The ratio of floors is determined solely by where the elevator moves energy, **not by the building materials (thermometric substance)**.

**Entropy — The Messy Room Analogy**
Entropy is the **"measure of disorder"** in a room. A clean, organized room is **low entropy**; a messy room is **high entropy**. Once a room becomes messy (spontaneous process), it requires **work (energy input)** from a person to restore order. Similarly, in thermodynamic processes, entropy tends to **increase naturally** (Second Law) and cannot spontaneously decrease without external intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant to Remember:** The **Boltzmann constant** $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (exact, since 2019 SI redefinition) links microscopic and macroscopic entropy. Universal gas constant: **$R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)**.

### 1.4 Why These Concepts Matter in Engineering

- The thermodynamic temperature scale is essential for **accurate thermodynamic calculations**, **refrigeration cycles**, **power plant efficiency** (Carnot efficiency), and **cryogenics**.
- Entropy governs the **direction of processes**, **maximum work obtainable**, **irreversibility**, and is the foundation of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** used in designing turbines, compressors, nozzles, and heat exchangers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of the Thermodynamic Temperature Scale

The thermodynamic temperature scale is constructed on the following logical pillars:

- **Step 1 — Carnot Engine Postulate:** Any reversible heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs absorbs heat $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir at $T_H$ and rejects heat $Q_L$ to the cold reservoir at $T_L$. By the First Law, work $W = Q_H - Q_L$.

- **Step 2 — Ratio Definition (Kelvin, 1848):** Define the ratio of thermodynamic temperatures as the ratio of heats exchanged with the reservoirs:

$$ \frac{T_H}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_L} \quad \text{(reversible)} $$

- **Step 3 — Why "Why" Matters:** This definition is **substance-independent** because it relies only on the energy transfer of a reversible cycle — no mercury, alcohol, gas, or resistance thermometer is needed.

- **Step 4 — Reference Point Fixation:** The **triple point of water** is assigned exactly **273.16 K** by international convention. This sets the magnitude of 1 K.

- **Step 5 — Identity with Ideal Gas Scale:** For an ideal gas working substance, the thermodynamic temperature scale **coincides** numerically with the ideal gas temperature scale:

$$ T = \lim_{P \to 0} \left( \frac{PV}{nR} \right) $$

### 2.2 Theoretical Foundation of Entropy

- **Step 1 — Clausius Theorem (1854):** For any **reversible cyclic process**:

$$ \oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0 $$

- **Step 2 — Existence of a State Function:** Since the cyclic integral is zero, the integrand must be the **exact differential** of a property — this property is called **entropy (S)**.

- **Step 3 — Clausius Inequality (for irreversible cycles):** For any **real (irreversible) cycle**:

$$ \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0 $$

- **Step 4 — Entropy Generation:** Combining the two:

$$ \Delta S_{system} = \int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q}{T} + S_{gen} $$

where $S_{gen} \geq 0$ (equality for reversible, greater than zero for irreversible).

- **Step 5 — Second Law Statement (Entropy Form):** The total entropy of the **universe (system + surroundings)** always increases for spontaneous (irreversible) processes and remains constant for reversible processes:

$$ \Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings} \geq 0 $$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` or `\mid` in tables to avoid markdown parsing errors. The following table summarizes all **must-know formulas** for KTU 2024 exams.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thermodynamic temp. ratio | $T_H / T_L = Q_H / Q_L$ | K | Reversible Carnot engine |
| 2 | Carnot Efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L / T_H$ | dimensionless | Independent of working fluid |
| 3 | Entropy (differential) | $dS = \delta Q_{rev} / T$ | J/K | Closed system |
| 4 | Entropy (finite change) | $\Delta S = \int_{1}^{2} (\delta Q_{rev} / T)$ | J/K | Path-independent (state function) |
| 5 | Clausius Theorem | $\oint (\delta Q_{rev} / T) = 0$ | J/K | Reversible cycle |
| 6 | Clausius Inequality | $\oint (\delta Q / T) \leq 0$ | J/K | Any (real) cycle |
| 7 | Entropy Balance | $\Delta S_{sys} = \int (\delta Q / T) + S_{gen}$ | J/K | $S_{gen} \geq 0$ |
| 8 | Specific entropy — ideal gas (a) | $\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_2 / T_1) - R \ln(P_2 / P_1)$ | J/(kg·K) | Most-used form |
| 9 | Specific entropy — ideal gas (b) | $\Delta s = c_v \ln(T_2 / T_1) + R \ln(v_2 / v_1)$ | J/(kg·K) | Volume-based form |
| 10 | Tds Equation 1 (Tds #1) | $T \, ds = du + P \, dv$ | J/kg | Combined 1st & 2nd Law |
| 11 | Tds Equation 2 (Tds #2) | $T \, ds = dh - v \, dP$ | J/kg | Enthalpy form |
| 12 | Isentropic Process (ideal gas) | $T_2 / T_1 = (v_1 / v_2)^{k-1}$ | — | $\Delta s = 0$ |
| 13 | Isentropic Process (ideal gas) | $T_2 / T_1 = (P_2 / P_1)^{(k-1)/k}$ | — | $\Delta s = 0$ |
| 14 | Isentropic Process (ideal gas) | $P_2 / P_1 = (v_1 / v_2)^k$ | — | $\Delta s = 0$ |
| 15 | Boltzmann Relation | $S = k_B \ln \Omega$ | J/K | Microscopic definition |
| 16 | Isothermal $\Delta s$ (ideal gas) | $\Delta s = R \ln(v_2 / v_1) = -R \ln(P_2 / P_1)$ | J/(kg·K) | T = constant |
| 17 | Constant Volume $\Delta s$ | $\Delta s = c_v \ln(T_2 / T_1)$ | J/(kg·K) | V = constant |
| 18 | Constant Pressure $\Delta s$ | $\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_2 / T_1)$ | J/(kg·K) | P = constant |
| 19 | Entropy of water at $T$ (liq.) | $\Delta s = c_{p,water} \ln(T_2 / T_1)$ | J/(kg·K) | Approximation |
| 20 | Carnot Cycle Heat Relation | $Q_H / T_H = Q_L / T_L$ | J/K | Equal entropy transfers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Engineers use $S_{gen}$ to **quantify irreversibility** (lost work). The **exergy destruction** $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ is the true measure of inefficiency in turbines, compressors, throttling valves, and heat exchangers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Thermodynamic Temperature Scale from the Second Law

**Given:** A reversible Carnot heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_H$ (hot) and $T_L$ (cold). Heat $Q_H$ is absorbed at $T_H$, heat $Q_L$ is rejected at $T_L$, and work $W$ is produced.

**First Law (over the complete cycle):**

$$ W = Q_H - Q_L $$

**Reversible Carnot Engine (Kelvin–Planck statement compatibility):** Consider two reversible engines **A** and **B**, both operating between the same reservoirs. Engine **A** produces work $W_A$ from $Q_H^A$, rejecting $Q_L^A$. Engine **B** is reversed — it consumes $W_B$ to pump $Q_L^B$ from cold to hot, delivering $Q_H^B$.

**Combining the Two Engines:** If we couple them so that $W_A$ drives $W_B$, no external work is needed. By the **Second Law (Kelvin–Planck)**, no self-driven cyclic device can produce net work from a single reservoir. Therefore, the **net work must be zero** and **no net heat is exchanged with either reservoir**, leading to:

$$ \frac{Q_H^A}{Q_H^B} = \frac{Q_L^A}{Q_L^B} $$

**Defining the Temperature Ratio:** The above equality is a property of the reservoirs only, independent of the engines. Kelvin defined:

$$ \frac{T_H}{T_L} = \left( \frac{Q_H}{Q_L} \right)_{rev} $$

**Fixing the Reference Point:** Assigning $T = 273.16$ K to the triple point of water uniquely fixes the scale. The **Carnot efficiency** then follows:

$$ \eta_{Carnot} = \frac{W}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The thermodynamic temperature is a **primitive concept** derived solely from the Second Law, requiring no thermometric substance.

### 3.2 Derivation: Entropy as a State Function (Clausius Theorem)

**Starting Point — First Law for a Closed System (differential):**

$$ \delta Q - \delta W = dU $$

For a reversible process, $\delta W = P \, dv$, so:

$$ \delta Q_{rev} = dU + P \, dv $$

**For a Cyclic Process:** Integrating over a complete cycle:

$$ \oint \delta Q_{rev} = \oint dU + \oint P \, dv = 0 + 0 = 0 $$

The first integral uses $dU$ being exact ($\oint dU = 0$); the second uses $P \, dv$ being exact for a reversible path traversed cyclically.

**Dividing by T:**

$$ \oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0 $$

**Existence of a Property:** Since the cyclic integral of $(\delta Q_{rev}/T)$ vanishes, the integrand must be an **exact differential** of some property $S$:

$$ dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \oint dS = 0 \quad \text{(state function confirmed)} $$

### 3.3 Derivation: Clausius Inequality (for Irreversible Cycles)

Consider an irreversible cycle composed of a reversible path **I→II (rev)** and an irreversible path **II→I (irrev)**. Applying the First Law to the whole cycle:

$$ \oint \delta Q = 0 $$

But for the irreversible segment, $\delta Q < \delta Q_{rev}$ (less heat absorbed). Therefore:

$$ 0 = \oint \delta Q < \oint \delta Q_{rev} = \oint T \, dS $$

Hence the **Clausius inequality**:

$$ \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0 $$

### 3.4 Derivation: Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas

**Starting from Tds Equation 1:**

$$ T \, ds = du + P \, dv $$

For an ideal gas, $du = c_v \, dT$ and $P = RT / v$ (per unit mass). Substituting:

$$ T \, ds = c_v \, dT + \frac{RT}{v} dv $$

Dividing by $T$:

$$ ds = c_v \frac{dT}{T} + R \frac{dv}{v} $$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2:

$$ \Delta s = c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) $$

**Tds Equation 2 → Equivalent Form (using $h$):**

$$ T \, ds = dh - v \, dP \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; ds = c_p \frac{dT}{T} - R \frac{dP}{P} $$

Integrating:

$$ \Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) $$

**Equivalence Check:** Subtracting the two expressions and using $c_p - c_v = R$:

$$ c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) = R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) $$

$$ R \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) = R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2 v_2}{P_1 v_1}\right) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 v_2}{P_1 v_1} $$

This is the **ideal gas law**, confirming consistency. $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A piston-cylinder contains 2 kg of air ($R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K) initially at 300 K and 200 kPa. It is heated at constant volume to 600 K. Find:
> (i) the entropy change of the air, and
> (ii) the entropy generated, if the surroundings are at 300 K.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Constant-Volume Process:** $\Delta v = 0$, so no boundary work. Heat added is internal energy change:
$$ Q = m c_v (T_2 - T_1) = 2 \times 0.718 \times (600 - 300) = 430.8 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 2 — Entropy Change of the System (using $c_v$ form):**

$$ \Delta S_{sys} = m \, c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) = 2 \times 0.718 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{600}{300}\right) $$

$$ \Delta S_{sys} = 2 \times 0.718 \times 0.6931 = 0.9951 \text{ kJ/K} $$

**Step 3 — Entropy Change of Surroundings** (heat lost by surroundings at $T_0 = 300$ K):

$$ \Delta S_{surr} = -\frac{Q}{T_0} = -\frac{430.8}{300} = -1.436 \text{ kJ/K} $$

**Step 4 — Entropy Generated:**

$$ S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} = 0.9951 - 1.436 = -0.441 \text{ kJ/K} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Wait — this is negative!** Did we make an error? **No.** The system is heated by an **internal source** (e.g., electric heater) which is not "surroundings" in the conventional sense. For an electrical heating process, the **entropy generation is positive** because electrical work carries negligible entropy. The correct analysis is:
> $$ S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} - \frac{Q}{T_{source}} $$
> If the source is at $T_{source} > T_2$, then $S_{gen} > 0$. KTU students often confuse "surroundings" with the heat source — this is a **classic 2-mark trap** in KTU exams.

### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Process | Heat Transfer $Q$ | $\Delta s$ of ideal gas | Isentropic? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Isothermal | $Q = W = mRT \ln(v_2/v_1)$ | $R \ln(v_2/v_1)$ | Only if $Q=0$ |
| Isochoric (const $v$) | $Q = m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$ | $c_v \ln(T_2/T_1)$ | No |
| Isobaric (const $P$) | $Q = m c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | $c_p \ln(T_2/T_1)$ | No |
| Adiabatic Reversible | $Q = 0$ | $0$ | **Yes** |
| Polytropic $Pv^n$ = const | $Q \neq 0$ | Use generic formula | Only if $n = k$ |
