| Free Expansion | $Q = 0, W = 0$ | $R \ln(v_2/v_1) > 0$ | No (irreversible) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Logical Architecture: Thermodynamic Temperature Scale Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Second Law of Thermodynamics] --> B[Reversible Carnot Engine]
    B --> C[Heat Exchange Q_H and Q_L]
    C --> D[Ratio Definition: T_H / T_L = Q_H / Q_L]
    D --> E[Assign Triple Point of Water 273.16 K]
    E --> F[Complete Kelvin Thermodynamic Scale]
    F --> G[Carnot Efficiency n = 1 - T_L / T_H]
    G --> H[Universal Application in Power and Refrigeration Cycles]
    D --> I[Substance-Independent Property]
    I --> F
    F --> J[Identical to Ideal Gas Scale]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Entropy Concept and Entropy Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Closed System Boundary] --> S2[Heat Transfer dQ at Temperature T]
    S2 --> S3{Reversible Path?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Exact Differential dS = dQ_rev / T]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Irreversible Path: S_gen > 0]
    S4 --> S6[State Function S: Property of System]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Entropy Balance: dS_sys = dQ/T + dS_gen]
    S7 --> S8[Second Law: dS_universe >= 0]
    S6 --> S9[Engineering Use: Exergy Destruction I = T_0 S_gen]
    S9 --> S10[Turbine, Compressor, Nozzle, Heat Exchanger Design]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Tds Equations and Their Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Tds Equation 1: Tds = du + P dv] --> P2[Use c_v dT and Pv = RT for Ideal Gas]
    P2 --> P3[ds = c_v dT/T + R dv/v]
    P3 --> P4[Integrate to get Delta s in terms of T and v]
    P5[Tds Equation 2: Tds = dh - v dP] --> P6[Use c_p dT and Pv = RT for Ideal Gas]
    P6 --> P7[ds = c_p dT/T - R dP/P]
    P7 --> P8[Integrate to get Delta s in terms of T and P]
    P4 --> P9[Isentropic Relations: T v exp and T P exp]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Application: Turbines, Compressors, Nozzles]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: Reversible vs. Irreversible Cycles on T-S Plane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Reversible Carnot Cycle] --> A2[Two Isotherms at T_H and T_L]
    A2 --> A3[Two Adiabats S = constant]
    A3 --> A4[Net Area = Work = Q_H - Q_L]
    A4 --> A5[Efficiency: 1 - T_L / T_H]
    B1[Irreversible Cycle] --> B2[Rounded or Skewed Shape]
    B2 --> B3[Net Area LESS than Reversible Cycle]
    B3 --> B4[Lost Work: I = T_0 S_gen]
    A5 --> C1[Upper Bound on Real Engine Efficiency]
    B4 --> C1
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define the thermodynamic temperature scale. How is it different from the ideal gas temperature scale?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **thermodynamic temperature scale** is defined using the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** based on the operation of a reversible heat engine. For a reversible Carnot engine operating between two reservoirs, the ratio of thermodynamic temperatures is given by:

$$ \frac{T_H}{T_L} = \left( \frac{Q_H}{Q_L} \right)_{rev} $$

The triple point of water is fixed at **273.16 K** to define the magnitude of one kelvin.

**Difference from ideal gas scale:**

- The thermodynamic scale is **derived from the Second Law** and is independent of the working substance.
- The ideal gas scale **depends on the properties of an ideal gas** ($T = PV/nR$), which is a hypothetical substance.
- Both scales are **numerically identical** in the ideal gas limit, but the thermodynamic scale is more **fundamental** since it requires no assumptions about material behaviour.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**What is entropy? State the Clausius theorem and Clausius inequality.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Entropy (S)** is a thermodynamic property (state function) that quantitatively measures the microscopic disorder or the unavailability of energy for useful work. It is defined as:

$$ dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \quad \text{(J/K)} $$

**Clausius Theorem:** For any **reversible cyclic process**:

$$ \oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0 $$

This implies that $(\delta Q_{rev}/T)$ is an exact differential of a property, which is entropy.

**Clausius Inequality:** For any **irreversible (real) cyclic process**:

$$ \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0 $$

This is the mathematical statement of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** and is the basis for determining the direction of spontaneous processes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the thermodynamic temperature scale starting from the Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law. Show that the Carnot efficiency is $1 - T_L/T_H$.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Kelvin-Planck Statement:** "It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."

**Step 2 — Consider Two Reversible Engines A and B** between the same reservoirs at $T_H$ and $T_L$:
- Engine A (forward): $Q_H^A$ absorbed, $W_A$ produced, $Q_L^A$ rejected.
- Engine B (reversed): $W_B$ consumed, $Q_L^B$ absorbed, $Q_H^B$ rejected.

**Step 3 — Couple them such that $W_A = W_B$** (no net work). By the Second Law, the net heat exchange with each reservoir must be zero:
$$ Q_H^A = Q_H^B, \quad Q_L^A = Q_L^B $$

**Step 4 — Generalization:** The heat ratio is the same for **all** reversible engines operating between the same two reservoirs. This is a property of the reservoirs only, defining a **temperature ratio**:

$$ \frac{T_H}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_L} \;\;\text{(reversible)} $$

**Step 5 — Carnot Efficiency** (First Law: $W = Q_H - Q_L$):

$$ \eta_{Carnot} = \frac{W}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} \;\;\blacksquare $$

---

**(b) Air (ideal gas, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K) enters a compressor at 100 kPa, 300 K and is compressed polytropically with $Pv^{1.3}$ = const to 800 kPa. Calculate (i) the exit temperature, (ii) the work done per kg, and (iii) the entropy change per kg.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_2 = 800$ kPa, $n = 1.3$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 1 — Specific Gas Constant and $c_v$:**

$$ c_v = c_p - R = 1.005 - 0.287 = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K} $$

$$ k = c_p / c_v = 1.005 / 0.718 = 1.4 $$

**Step 2 — Exit Temperature** (polytropic relation $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$):

$$ T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} = 300 \times (8)^{(0.3/1.3)} $$

$$ T_2 = 300 \times 8^{0.2308} = 300 \times 1.5538 = 466.13 \text{ K} $$

**Step 3 — Work Done (per kg)** (polytropic formula):

$$ w = \frac{R(T_2 - T_1)}{n - 1} = \frac{0.287 \times (466.13 - 300)}{1.3 - 1} = \frac{0.287 \times 166.13}{0.3} $$

$$ w = \frac{47.68}{0.3} = 158.93 \text{ kJ/kg} $$

**Step 4 — Entropy Change (using the $T, P$ form):**

$$ \Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) $$

$$ \Delta s = 1.005 \times \ln(1.5538) - 0.287 \times \ln(8) $$

$$ \Delta s = 1.005 \times 0.4408 - 0.287 \times 2.0794 $$

$$ \Delta s = 0.4430 - 0.5968 = -0.1538 \text{ kJ/kg·K} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students forget that the entropy change of the **gas** is negative here, but the process is still **irreversible** because the polytropic compression ($n=1.3 < k=1.4$) generates entropy. A **negative** $\Delta s$ of the gas does **not** violate the Second Law — what matters is $\Delta s_{universe} = \Delta s_{sys} + \Delta s_{surr} \geq 0$. Always check the **sign** of $S_{gen}$, not just $\Delta s_{sys}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) State and prove the Clausius inequality. Explain the physical significance of the Clausius inequality.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement:** For any thermodynamic cycle (reversible or irreversible):

$$ \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0 $$

where $T$ is the temperature of the **boundary** (reservoir) at which heat $\delta Q$ crosses.

**Step 2 — Proof:**
Consider a cycle composed of:
- An **irreversible** path from state 1 to state 2.
- A **reversible** path from state 2 back to state 1.

By the **First Law** (cyclic):

$$ \oint \delta Q = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \int_{1}^{2}\!\delta Q_{irrev} + \int_{2}^{1}\!\delta Q_{rev} = 0 $$

Reversing the limits of the second integral:

$$ \int_{1}^{2}\!\delta Q_{irrev} - \int_{1}^{2}\!\delta Q_{rev} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \int_{1}^{2}\!\delta Q_{irrev} = \int_{1}^{2}\!\delta Q_{rev} $$

For the reversible path, by definition: $\int_{1}^{2} \delta Q_{rev}/T = S_2 - S_1$. For the irreversible path, the entropy change is **the same** (entropy is a state function):

$$ S_2 - S_1 = \int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \geq \int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_{irrev}}{T} $$

For a complete cycle, summing both legs and using the fact that the reversible leg is a state function:

$$ \oint \frac{\delta Q_{irrev}}{T} \leq 0 \;\;\blacksquare $$

**Step 3 — Physical Significance:**
- The Clausius inequality is the **mathematical statement of the Second Law** of thermodynamics.
- It identifies the **direction of natural processes** (irreversible, $S_{gen}>0$).
- It defines the upper limit of performance for **all** heat engines and refrigerators (Carnot cycle).
- It forms the basis for the **definition of entropy** and the **exergy (availability) analysis**.

---

**(b) Two kg of an ideal gas (R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K, $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K) is heated reversibly from 300 K, 100 kPa to 500 K. Find the change in entropy of (i) the gas, (ii) the heat source at 800 K, and (iii) the universe. Is the process possible?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Entropy Change of the Gas** (closed system, constant pressure — assume $P$ constant or use general formula):

If pressure is constant:

$$ \Delta S_{gas} = m \, c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) = 2 \times 1.005 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{500}{300}\right) $$

$$ \Delta S_{gas} = 2 \times 1.005 \times 0.5108 = 1.0267 \text{ kJ/K} $$

If pressure is **not** constant, use the general $T,P$ form (see derivation). We will assume constant pressure for this problem.

**Step 2 — Heat Transferred (reversible):**

$$ Q = m \, c_p (T_2 - T_1) = 2 \times 1.005 \times (500 - 300) = 402 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 3 — Entropy Change of Heat Source** (at $T_{source} = 800$ K, losing heat $Q$):

$$ \Delta S_{source} = -\frac{Q}{T_{source}} = -\frac{402}{800} = -0.5025 \text{ kJ/K} $$

**Step 4 — Entropy Change of Universe:**

$$ \Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{gas} + \Delta S_{source} = 1.0267 - 0.5025 = 0.5242 \text{ kJ/K} > 0 $$

**Step 5 — Is the Process Possible?**

Yes, the process is **possible** because $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$, satisfying the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**. The "reversible" qualifier in the problem statement is misleading — actually, since the gas heats from 300 K to 500 K while the source is at 800 K, the process is **irreversible** (finite temperature difference) despite being called "reversibly heated." The Second Law is satisfied: $S_{gen} = 0.5242$ kJ/K $> 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often mis-identify "reversibly" in the problem statement. The gas is heated **reversibly** (internally reversible), but heat transfer from a 800 K source to a 300 K gas is **externally irreversible** due to finite $\Delta T$. The **total** process entropy balance must be checked, not just the gas alone. This is a frequently tested KTU trap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- ✅ **Thermodynamic temperature** is defined via the **Second Law** (Carnot cycle), **not** via gas properties.
- ✅ Ratio definition: $T_H / T_L = (Q_H / Q_L)_{rev}$.
- ✅ **Triple point of water = 273.16 K** (exact definition of 1 K).
- ✅ **Carnot efficiency** = $1 - T_L / T_H$ is the **maximum** possible for any heat engine between two reservoirs.
- ✅ **Entropy** $S$ is a **state function (point function)** — path-independent.
- ✅ Definition: $dS = \delta Q_{rev} / T$.
- ✅ **Clausius theorem** (reversible cycle): $\oint \delta Q_{rev} / T = 0$.
- ✅ **Clausius inequality** (any cycle): $\oint \delta Q / T \leq 0$.
- ✅ **Entropy balance**: $\Delta S_{sys} = \int (\delta Q / T) + S_{gen}$, where $S_{gen} \geq 0$.
- ✅ **Second Law (entropy form)**: $\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$ (equality for reversible).
- ✅ **Ideal gas entropy formulas**:
  - $\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_2/T_1) - R \ln(P_2/P_1)$
  - $\Delta s = c_v \ln(T_2/T_1) + R \ln(v_2/v_1)$
- ✅ **Tds Equations**: $T \, ds = du + P \, dv = dh - v \, dP$.
- ✅ **Isentropic relations** (for ideal gas, $\Delta s = 0$): $Tv^{k-1}$ = const, $TP^{(1-k)/k}$ = const, $Pv^k$ = const.
- ✅ **Exergy destruction** $I = T_0 S_{gen}$ is the **true measure of irreversibility**.
- ✅ **Boltzmann relation** $S = k_B \ln \Omega$ connects macro- and micro-states.
- ✅ Common KTU traps:
  - Confusing **surroundings** vs. **heat source** in entropy balance.
  - Forgetting $S_{gen} \geq 0$ applies to **universe**, not just system.
  - Sign errors in $Q$ when heat leaves the system.
  - Using $c_p$ for constant-volume and $c_v$ for constant-pressure processes.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In classical thermodynamics, a process is classified as **irreversible** if the system and its surroundings cannot be restored to their respective initial states simultaneously by reversing the direction of the process. Mathematically, an irreversible process is one that violates the equality condition of the **Clausius Equality** and instead obeys the **Clausius Inequality**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"A process is irreversible if, when it is carried out, the system and surroundings cannot both be returned to their original states by reversing the direction of the process. All natural (spontaneous) processes are irreversible."*

The **Clausius Inequality** is expressed as:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

where the symbol $\oint$ denotes a cyclic integral over a complete thermodynamic cycle. The equality sign ($=$) holds strictly for a **reversible cycle**, while the strict inequality sign ($<$) holds for an **irreversible cycle**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spilled Ink Analogy

Imagine dropping a single drop of **blue ink** into a glass of still, clear water. The ink slowly disperses throughout the water, turning the entire glass a pale blue. This is a **natural, spontaneous process** that happens on its own.

Now, try to reverse it: can you gather all the dispersed ink molecules back into a single drop without any external work? **No.** You would have to perform significant work on the system (for example, using a sophisticated molecular filter or a chemical reaction) and you would leave the surroundings permanently changed (more energy expended, waste heat, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Irreversibility is fundamentally about the **one-way direction of natural processes**. Just as a video played in reverse looks obviously wrong, the universe has a "preferred arrow" — energy degrades, order becomes disorder, and heat flows from hot to cold. The reverse never happens spontaneously.

### 1.3 Reversible vs. Irreversible — The Boundary

| Property | Reversible Process | Irreversible Process |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Direction | Can be reversed by an infinitesimal change | Cannot be reversed without external work |
| Equilibrium | System passes through **continuous equilibrium** states | System passes through **non-equilibrium** states |
| Friction | Frictionless (idealized) | Friction always present |
| Heat Transfer | Occurs across **infinitesimally small** temperature difference | Occurs across **finite** temperature difference |
| Speed | Quasi-static (infinitely slow) | Finite (real-world) speed |
| Work | Produces **maximum** work | Produces **less** work than theoretical maximum |
| Existence in Nature | **Theoretical ideal** only | **All real processes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** No real process in nature is truly reversible. A reversible process is an **idealization** (a thought experiment) used as a benchmark to compare the performance of real devices such as engines, compressors, and turbines.

### 1.4 The Second Law Connection

The concept of irreversibility is a direct consequence of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**. In fact, the **Kelvin-Planck Statement** ("It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a single reservoir and the production of an equivalent amount of work") and the **Clausius Statement** ("Heat cannot flow spontaneously from a colder body to a hotter body") are both manifestations of irreversibility in the universe.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Principal Causes of Irreversibility

When analyzing any thermodynamic process, the engineer must identify which sources of irreversibility are present. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the major causes are:

**Cause 1 — Friction and Unrestrained Expansion**
- Friction between moving parts (piston rings against cylinder walls, bearings, gear meshing).
- Unrestrained expansion of a gas into a vacuum (free expansion, also called **Joule expansion**).
- Energy is dissipated as **internal (thermal) energy** of the surroundings, raising its temperature slightly.

**Cause 2 — Unrestrained Expansion (Free Expansion)**
- A gas expands into a vacuum without doing any useful boundary work.
- The final state is highly disordered, with no external work extracted.
- Example: A punctured tyre, opening a valve between a pressurized cylinder and an evacuated cylinder.

**Cause 3 — Mixing of Different Substances**
- Spontaneous diffusion of two or more gases, liquids, or miscible fluids.
- The mixing process is driven purely by concentration gradients, not by external work.
- Example: Milk dispersing in coffee, atmospheric gases mixing in a room.

**Cause 4 — Heat Transfer Across a Finite Temperature Difference**
- Heat flowing from a hot body at $T_H$ to a cold body at $T_C$ where $(T_H - T_C)$ is **finite** (not infinitesimal).
- This is the **single most common** source of irreversibility in heat engines, boilers, and condensers.
- The greater the temperature difference, the greater the irreversibility (and the lower the efficiency).

**Cause 5 — Inelastic Deformation of Solids**
- Plastic deformation, compression of a viscous fluid, or stretching beyond the elastic limit.
- The mechanical work input is not fully recovered as mechanical work output — some is converted to internal energy.
- Example: Stretching a rubber band too far, compressing a foam.

**Cause 6 — Chemical Reactions**
- Spontaneous chemical reactions such as combustion, oxidation, or rusting.
- Most real chemical reactions are **irreversible** because they proceed in one direction under given conditions.
- Some are reversible (like water-gas shift under specific catalysts), but they require careful control of temperature, pressure, and concentration.

**Cause 7 — Electrical Resistance (Joule Heating)**
- Current flowing through a resistor dissipates electrical energy as heat ($I^2R$ losses).
- This is irreversible because the heat cannot be fully converted back into electricity without additional work.
- Example: Resistance heating in an electric heater (this is desired there, but it's still irreversible).

**Cause 8 — Dissipation of Mechanical Energy (Damping)**
- Viscous damping in fluid flow (e.g., flow through narrow pipes, turbulence).
- Kinetic energy is converted irreversibly into internal energy of the fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In Module 2, you will most often encounter questions involving **Cause 4 (heat transfer with finite $\Delta T$)** and **Cause 1 (friction)** in numerical problems. Always identify the cause first, then quantify it using the entropy generation formula.

### 2.2 The Entropy Generation Principle

The mathematical fingerprint of irreversibility is **entropy generation**. The **Second Law** for a closed system states:

$$\Delta S_{system} \geq \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}}$$

The difference between the entropy change of the system and the entropy transfer due to heat is defined as the **entropy generation** $S_{gen}$:

$$S_{gen} = \Delta S_{system} - \sum \frac{Q_k}{T_k} \geq 0$$

For the **universe** (system + surroundings) considered together:

$$\Delta S_{universe} = S_{gen} \geq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Asymmetry:**
> - $S_{gen} = 0 \implies$ **Reversible process** (idealized)
> - $S_{gen} > 0 \implies$ **Irreversible process** (real, spontaneous)
> - $S_{gen} < 0 \implies$ **Impossible** (violates the Second Law)

### 2.3 The Clausius Inequality for Cycles

For any thermodynamic cycle (whether reversible or irreversible), the **Clausius Inequality** holds:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

- The equality sign applies only to a **completely reversible cycle**.
- For a real, irreversible cycle, the integral is strictly negative.
- This is the **mathematical statement** of the Second Law for cycles.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ | Clausius Inequality for a cycle | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} - \int \frac{\delta Q}{T_b}$ | Entropy generation (closed system) | $\text{kJ/K}$ |
| 3 | $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0$ | Total entropy of universe | $\text{kJ/K}$ |
| 4 | $\Delta S = m c \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$ | Entropy change of a liquid/solid | $\text{kJ/K}$ |
| 5 | $\Delta S = m c_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - m R \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Entropy change of an ideal gas | $\text{kJ/K}$ |
| 6 | $S_{gen, heat} = Q \left( \frac{1}{T_C} - \frac{1}{T_H} \right)$ | Entropy gen. for heat transfer across $\Delta T$ | $\text{kJ/K}$ |
| 7 | $\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$ | Carnot (reversible) efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{th,actual} = \frac{W_{actual}}{Q_H}$ | Real thermal efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ | Gouy-Stodola theorem: lost/exergy destroyed | $\text{kJ}$ |
| 10 | $\eta_{II} = 1 - \frac{I}{W_{rev}}$ | Second-Law (exergy) efficiency | Dimensionless |

> **Note on notation:** In the table above, $\delta Q$ is an infinitesimal heat transfer, $T_b$ is the boundary temperature, $T_0$ is the dead-state temperature, and $I$ represents the **irreversibility** (also called lost work or exergy destruction).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the causes of irreversibility is not just an academic exercise — it has profound engineering consequences:

- **Power Plant Design:** Engineers minimize the temperature difference in the boiler (use superheat) and condenser to reduce irreversibility.
- **Refrigeration Systems:** Multi-stage cooling with inter-cooling reduces the irreversibility of heat transfer.
- **Gas Turbines:** Compressors and turbines are designed to be near-isentropic (reversible adiabatic) to maximize efficiency.
- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Reducing friction, increasing compression ratio, and using regenerative braking all attack different sources of irreversibility.
- **Insulation Design:** Better insulation reduces heat leaks, which is essentially reducing the irreversibility of spontaneous heat flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** The goal of every thermodynamic system designer is to **minimize $S_{gen}$** (or equivalently, minimize $I = T_0 S_{gen}$). A system with $S_{gen} = 0$ would be the perfectly reversible ideal — the engineering "north star."

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Clausius Inequality from the Carnot Principle

This derivation establishes the **mathematical foundation** of irreversibility. It is one of the most frequently asked derivations in KTU Board Examinations.

**Statement to Prove:**
$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

**Step 1 — Setup a Reversible Reference Engine**

Consider an arbitrary (possibly irreversible) heat engine $E$ operating between two thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_H$ and $T_C$. The engine $E$ extracts heat $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir, rejects heat $Q_C$ to the cold reservoir, and produces work $W = Q_H - Q_C$.

Now imagine a **reversible engine** $E_{rev}$ operating between the **same two reservoirs**. The reversible engine is driven in reverse (as a heat pump) using the work $W$ produced by engine $E$.

> **Key Insight:** Because $E_{rev}$ is reversible, we can flip its direction. The same work $W$ that engine $E$ produces is exactly the work needed to drive $E_{rev}$ in reverse (we are free to choose the operating conditions such that this is true).

**Step 2 — Apply the Kelvin-Planck Statement**

The composite system (engine $E$ + reversed reversible engine $E_{rev}$) exchanges no net work with the surroundings. If the composite system were to violate the Kelvin-Planck statement, it would transfer heat spontaneously from the cold reservoir to the hot reservoir without any work input.

By the Second Law (Kelvin-Planck), this is **forbidden**. Therefore, the composite system must **only exchange heat with the cold reservoir** (or with neither reservoir, if both are zero).

**Step 3 — Write the Energy Balance**

The heat exchanged with the cold reservoir by the composite system is:

$$Q_C' = Q_C - Q_{C,rev}$$

Since the composite system is prohibited from extracting heat from the hot reservoir, we must have $Q_C' \leq 0$ (i.e., net heat can only be rejected to the cold reservoir, not absorbed from it).

**Step 4 — Use the Reversible Engine's Property**

For the reversible engine operating between the same two reservoirs:

$$\frac{Q_{H,rev}}{T_H} = \frac{Q_{C,rev}}{T_C}$$

This is the property of a **reversible Carnot cycle** — the ratio of heat to absolute temperature is constant.

**Step 5 — Combine the Equations**

Substituting and simplifying:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_C - Q_{C,rev} &\leq 0 \\
Q_C - T_C \cdot \frac{Q_{H,rev}}{T_H} &\leq 0
\end{aligned}$$

Since $E_{rev}$ operates such that it absorbs the work $W$ from $E$ and pumps heat in reverse, we have $Q_{H,rev} = Q_H$ (the reversible engine rejects exactly as much heat to the hot reservoir as $E$ absorbs from it):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_C - T_C \cdot \frac{Q_H}{T_H} &\leq 0
\end{aligned}$$

Dividing through by $T_C$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_C}{T_C} - \frac{Q_H}{T_H} &\leq 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Generalize to an Arbitrary Cycle**

For an arbitrary (possibly irreversible) cycle, we break the cycle into an infinite number of small Carnot cycles. Each infinitesimal Carnot cycle absorbs $\delta Q$ at temperature $T$:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

This is the **Clausius Inequality**, proved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equality holds** if and only if **all** infinitesimal heat transfers occur across **infinitesimally small** temperature differences with no friction, no unrestrained expansion, and no other dissipative effects — i.e., the cycle is **completely reversible**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Entropy Generation for Heat Transfer Across Finite $\Delta T$

This is a **classic KTU numerical problem**. Consider a quantity of heat $Q$ transferred from a hot reservoir at $T_H$ to a cold reservoir at $T_C$, with $T_H > T_C$.

**Step 1 — Entropy Change of the Hot Reservoir**

The hot reservoir loses heat $Q$, so its entropy change is:

$$\Delta S_H = -\frac{Q}{T_H}$$

The negative sign indicates entropy **decrease** (heat is leaving).

**Step 2 — Entropy Change of the Cold Reservoir**

The cold reservoir gains heat $Q$, so its entropy change is:

$$\Delta S_C = +\frac{Q}{T_C}$$

The positive sign indicates entropy **increase** (heat is entering).

**Step 3 — Total Entropy Change of the Universe**

Since the reservoirs are very large, their temperatures remain essentially constant:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_{univ} &= \Delta S_H + \Delta S_C \\
&= -\frac{Q}{T_H} + \frac{Q}{T_C} \\
&= Q \left( \frac{1}{T_C} - \frac{1}{T_H} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Conclude with the Second Law**

Since $T_C < T_H$, we have $\frac{1}{T_C} > \frac{1}{T_H}$, so:

$$\Delta S_{univ} = Q \left( \frac{1}{T_C} - \frac{1}{T_H} \right) > 0$$

This positive entropy generation is the **direct mathematical proof** that heat transfer across a finite temperature difference is irreversible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Case:** If $T_H \to T_C$ (i.e., the temperature difference approaches zero), then $\Delta S_{univ} \to 0$, and the process becomes reversible. This confirms that **reversible heat transfer must occur across an infinitesimal temperature difference**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Irreversibility Causes

| Cause | Driving Force | Typical Magnitude (Order) | Mitigation Strategy |
|-------|---------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Heat transfer across $\Delta T$ | $T_H - T_C$ finite | Largest in heat engines | Use regenerators, multi-stage heat exchange |
| Friction | Normal force × relative motion | Small to moderate | Lubrication, bearings, magnetic levitation |
| Unrestrained expansion | Pressure difference | Moderate | Throttling devices (controlled expansion) |
| Mixing | Concentration gradient | Small to moderate | Separation membranes (rarely economical) |
| Chemical reaction | Gibbs free energy difference | Large in combustion | Catalysts (for kinetics, not thermodynamics) |
| Electrical resistance | $I^2 R$ losses | Small in low-current systems | Superconductors, larger conductors |
| Inelastic deformation | Stress beyond yield | Moderate | Use elastic regime, composite materials |
| Fluid damping | Viscosity | Moderate to large | Streamlining, laminar flow, low-viscosity fluids |
