## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: Define irreversibility. List any four causes of irreversibility in a thermodynamic process.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Irreversibility is the inability of a system and its surroundings to return to their respective original states after a process has occurred. It is characterized by entropy generation ($S_{gen} > 0$) and lost work ($I = T_0 S_{gen} > 0$).

**Four causes of irreversibility:**

1. **Friction** between moving parts (mechanical dissipation).
2. **Unrestrained expansion** of a fluid into a vacuum or lower-pressure region.
3. **Heat transfer** across a finite temperature difference.
4. **Mixing of different substances** (e.g., two gases diffusing into each other).

> Other valid causes include inelastic deformation, chemical reactions, and electrical resistance.

---

**Question 2: State and explain the Clausius Inequality. What is its significance in identifying the reversibility of a cycle?**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Clausius Inequality states that for any thermodynamic cycle, the cyclic integral of $\frac{\delta Q}{T}$ is less than or equal to zero:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

where $\delta Q$ is the infinitesimal heat transferred to the system and $T$ is the absolute temperature at the boundary where the heat transfer occurs.

**Significance:**

- If the equality sign holds ($\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$), the cycle is **reversible**.
- If the strict inequality holds ($\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$), the cycle is **irreversible** and entropy has been generated.
- The Clausius Inequality is the **mathematical expression of the Second Law** for cyclic processes. It provides a quantitative test: by computing the integral, an engineer can determine whether a proposed cycle violates the Second Law.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **one** full question (either Question A OR Question B). Both options are from the same module.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) Derive the Clausius Inequality from the Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law.** (7 marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — System Definition:** Consider an irreversible heat engine $E$ operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_C$. The engine absorbs heat $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir, rejects heat $Q_C$ to the cold reservoir, and produces work $W = Q_H - Q_C$.

**Step 2 — Reversible Reference Engine:** Construct a reversible engine $E_{rev}$ operating between the same two reservoirs. We choose to operate $E_{rev}$ in reverse (as a heat pump) such that it absorbs exactly the work $W$ from engine $E$ and transfers heat $Q_{H,rev}$ to the hot reservoir and $Q_{C,rev}$ from the cold reservoir.

**Step 3 — Kelvin-Planck Restriction:** The composite system ($E$ + reversed $E_{rev}$) has **no net work exchange** with the surroundings. By the Kelvin-Planck statement, such a system cannot extract heat from a single reservoir and convert it all to work. Therefore, the composite system must satisfy: the **net heat interaction with the hot reservoir must be zero or negative** (heat can only flow to the hot reservoir, not from it).

**Step 4 — Energy Balance of the Composite System:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net heat to hot reservoir} &= Q_H - Q_{H,rev} \leq 0
\end{aligned}$$

This forces $Q_{H,rev} \geq Q_H$.

**Step 5 — Apply the Reversible Engine Property:** For the reversible engine, the ratio of heat to temperature is the same in both reservoirs:

$$\frac{Q_{H,rev}}{T_H} = \frac{Q_{C,rev}}{T_C}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify:** Since $E_{rev}$ in reverse rejects heat $Q_{H,rev}$ to the hot reservoir, and the composite system's net heat transfer to the hot reservoir must be $\leq 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_H - Q_{H,rev} &\leq 0 \\
\therefore Q_H &\leq Q_{H,rev} = T_H \cdot \frac{Q_{C,rev}}{T_C}
\end{aligned}$$

For the work absorbed to match, $Q_{H,rev} - Q_{C,rev} = W = Q_H - Q_C$, giving $Q_{C,rev} = Q_C$ (matched by design). Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_H &\leq T_H \cdot \frac{Q_C}{T_C} \\
\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_C}{T_C} &\leq 0 \\
\frac{Q_C}{T_C} - \frac{Q_H}{T_H} &\geq 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Generalize to an Arbitrary Cycle:** For an arbitrary cycle, decompose it into an infinite sequence of infinitesimal Carnot cycles, each with $\delta Q$ at temperature $T$. Summing the contributions yields the **Clausius Inequality**:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

**Equality** holds for a fully reversible cycle; **strict inequality** holds for any irreversible cycle.

---

**(b) A heat reservoir at 600 K transfers 1000 kJ of heat to another reservoir at 300 K. Determine (i) the entropy change of each reservoir, (ii) the total entropy change of the universe, and (iii) whether the process is reversible or irreversible. Justify your answer.** (7 marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $T_H = 600 \text{ K}$
- $T_C = 300 \text{ K}$
- $Q = 1000 \text{ kJ}$

**Step 1 — Entropy Change of the Hot Reservoir (loses heat $Q$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_H &= -\frac{Q}{T_H} \\
&= -\frac{1000}{600} \\
&= -1.6667 \text{ kJ/K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Entropy Change of the Cold Reservoir (gains heat $Q$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_C &= +\frac{Q}{T_C} \\
&= \frac{1000}{300} \\
&= +3.3333 \text{ kJ/K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total Entropy Change of the Universe:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_{univ} &= \Delta S_H + \Delta S_C \\
&= -1.6667 + 3.3333 \\
&= +1.6667 \text{ kJ/K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Decision: Reversible or Irreversible?**

Since $\Delta S_{univ} = +1.6667 \text{ kJ/K} > 0$, the process is **irreversible**. The positive entropy generation is the direct signature of irreversibility.

The irreversibility (lost work) at an ambient temperature of, say, $T_0 = 300 \text{ K}$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{univ} \\
&= 300 \times 1.6667 \\
&= 500 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** Although 1000 kJ of heat was transferred, 500 kJ of "useful work potential" was destroyed. This is a **50% loss in work potential**, demonstrating how severe the irreversibility of heat transfer across a finite $\Delta T$ can be.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) Explain any six causes of irreversibility in a thermodynamic system with suitable examples.** (7 marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

The six causes are explained below with engineering examples.

**1. Friction:**
Friction between moving mechanical parts converts useful work into internal energy (heat) of the surroundings, increasing the entropy of the universe. Examples: piston rings rubbing against the cylinder wall of an IC engine, gear meshing losses, bearing friction in a turbine.

**2. Unrestrained Expansion:**
When a gas expands into a vacuum or against a much lower pressure, no useful work is extracted, and the kinetic energy of the rushing gas is dissipated as heat through viscous effects. Example: opening a valve between a high-pressure air tank and an evacuated cylinder (Joule expansion). The gas does no work, but the process is clearly irreversible.

**3. Heat Transfer Across a Finite Temperature Difference:**
Heat flow from a high-temperature body to a low-temperature body (with a non-negligible temperature difference) is irreversible. The greater the $\Delta T$, the greater the entropy generated. Example: heat transfer from combustion gases at 1500 K to boiler tubes at 700 K in a thermal power plant.

**4. Mixing of Different Substances:**
Spontaneous mixing of two or more gases, liquids, or miscible fluids is irreversible. The reverse (unmixing) would require external work (e.g., a separation process). Example: atmospheric air mixing with carbon dioxide in a ventilation system.

**5. Inelastic Deformation:**
When a solid is deformed beyond its elastic limit, the work done on it is not fully recovered; some is dissipated as heat. Example: forging of a metal component, plastic compression of a polymer.

**6. Chemical Reactions:**
Most spontaneous chemical reactions (combustion, oxidation, neutralization) are irreversible. The reaction proceeds in one direction under given conditions, and the products cannot spontaneously reform the reactants. Example: combustion of methane in a furnace — once burned, the products ($CO_2$, $H_2O$) will not spontaneously recombine into methane and oxygen at room temperature.

> **Additional causes** (optional to mention): electrical resistance ($I^2R$ losses), viscous dissipation in fluid flow, and spontaneous nucleation/phase change.

---

**(b) A steel ball of mass 5 kg at 800 K is dropped into a large oil bath at 300 K. The specific heat of steel is $0.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$. Calculate (i) the entropy change of the steel ball, (ii) the entropy change of the oil bath, (iii) the entropy change of the universe, and (iv) the lost work (irreversibility) at $T_0 = 300 \text{ K}$.** (7 marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Mass of steel ball: $m = 5 \text{ kg}$
- Initial temperature: $T_1 = 800 \text{ K}$
- Final temperature (assumed equal to oil bath, since the bath is "large"): $T_2 = 300 \text{ K}$
- Specific heat: $c = 0.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Ambient/oil temperature: $T_0 = 300 \text{ K}$

**Step 1 — Entropy Change of the Steel Ball (closed system, incompressible solid):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_{ball} &= m c \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) \\
&= 5 \times 0.5 \times \ln\left(\frac{300}{800}\right) \\
&= 2.5 \times \ln(0.375) \\
&= 2.5 \times (-0.9808) \\
&= -2.4521 \text{ kJ/K}
\end{aligned}$$

The negative sign indicates the ball **loses** entropy as it cools.

**Step 2 — Heat Transferred from the Ball to the Bath:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= m c (T_1 - T_2) \\
&= 5 \times 0.5 \times (800 - 300) \\
&= 1250 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Entropy Change of the Oil Bath (large reservoir at constant $T = 300 \text{ K}$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_{bath} &= \frac{Q}{T_{bath}} \\
&= \frac{1250}{300} \\
&= +4.1667 \text{ kJ/K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Total Entropy Change of the Universe:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_{univ} &= \Delta S_{ball} + \Delta S_{bath} \\
&= -2.4521 + 4.1667 \\
&= +1.7146 \text{ kJ/K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Lost Work (Irreversibility):**

Using the **Gouy-Stodola Theorem**:

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{univ} \\
&= 300 \times 1.7146 \\
&= 514.4 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:**
- Since $\Delta S_{univ} > 0$, the process is **irreversible**.
- The **lost work of 514.4 kJ** represents the maximum useful work that could have been extracted from the 1250 kJ of heat (in a Carnot engine) but was destroyed due to the finite temperature difference between the ball and the bath.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Forgetting the negative sign in $\Delta S_{ball}$ — the ball is losing heat, so its entropy MUST decrease. Writing it positive loses 1-2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Unit Confusion:** The specific heat is given in kJ/(kg·K), so the final entropy will be in kJ/K — do not convert to J unless explicitly asked.
> 3. **"Large Bath" Assumption:** The oil bath is treated as a constant-temperature reservoir because it is "large" — students often incorrectly use the average temperature, which loses marks.
> 4. **Lost Work Missing:** Many students compute $\Delta S_{univ}$ correctly but forget to compute the irreversibility $I$ (lost work), which is often worth 2-3 marks in the final sub-question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Causes of Irreversibility**

- **Definition:** An irreversible process cannot be reversed without leaving changes in the system or surroundings. It is characterized by $S_{gen} > 0$.

- **Mathematical Test:** The Clausius Inequality $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ identifies irreversibility. Equality $\Rightarrow$ reversible; strict inequality $\Rightarrow$ irreversible.

- **Eight Causes (in order of KTU exam frequency):**
  1. **Heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$** (most common in heat engine problems)
  2. **Friction** (mechanical systems)
  3. **Unrestrained expansion** (throttling, free expansion)
  4. **Mixing of substances** (diffusion)
  5. **Inelastic deformation** (plasticity)
  6. **Chemical reactions** (combustion)
  7. **Electrical resistance** ($I^2R$)
  8. **Viscous dissipation** (fluid damping)

- **The Second Law Direction:**
  - $S_{gen} = 0$ ⇒ reversible
  - $S_{gen} > 0$ ⇒ irreversible (real, natural)
  - $S_{gen} < 0$ ⇒ **IMPOSSIBLE** (Second Law violation)

- **Key Formulae to Memorize:**
  - $\Delta S_{univ} = S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0$
  - $S_{gen, heat} = Q \left( \frac{1}{T_C} - \frac{1}{T_H} \right)$
  - $I = T_0 S_{gen}$ (Gouy-Stodola lost work)
  - $\Delta S_{liquid/solid} = mc \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$

- **Reversible Process Conditions** (all must hold simultaneously):
  - No friction
  - No unrestrained expansion
  - Heat transfer across **infinitesimally small** $\Delta T$
  - Quasi-static (infinitely slow) process
  - No chemical reaction, no mixing, no deformation

- **Real vs. Ideal:** No real process is fully reversible; reversible processes are **theoretical benchmarks** used to define the maximum possible efficiency (e.g., Carnot efficiency).

- **Engineering Goal:** Always aim to **minimize $S_{gen}$** through better insulation (reduce $\Delta T$), lubrication (reduce friction), and controlled expansion (throttling valves).

- **Board Exam Strategy:**
  1. State the type of process.
  2. Identify the cause(s) of irreversibility.
  3. Compute $S_{gen}$ for each cause separately.
  4. Sum to get $\Delta S_{univ}$.
  5. Conclude with the Second Law verdict and the lost work $I$.

> **End of Topic Notes — Causes of Irreversibility | Module 2 | PCMUT403**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clausius Inequality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clausius Inequality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Clausius Inequality** is a fundamental mathematical statement that emerges directly from the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**. It provides a quantitative criterion to distinguish between reversible and irreversible thermodynamic cycles. For any thermodynamic cycle (a process in which a system returns to its initial state), the cyclic integral of the differential heat transfer divided by the absolute temperature of the boundary at which the heat transfer occurs is always **less than or equal to zero**.

Mathematically, the Clausius Inequality is expressed as:

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0
$$

Where:
- $\delta Q$ = infinitesimal heat transfer **to** the system
- $T_b$ = absolute temperature (in **K** or **°R**) at the boundary where the heat transfer occurs
- $\oint$ denotes the cyclic integral over one complete cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Temperature vs. System Temperature:**
> The temperature $T_b$ refers to the temperature **at the system boundary** where heat is actually exchanged, not the bulk system temperature. This subtle distinction is heavily tested in KTU valuation.

## 1.2 Sign Convention and Boundary Conditions

- **Equality ($=$):** The cyclic integral equals **zero** for a completely **reversible cycle**.
- **Strict Inequality ($<$):** The cyclic integral is **less than zero** for an **irreversible cycle** (containing any real-world irreversibilities such as friction, unrestrained expansion, or finite temperature differences).
- The inequality **cannot** be greater than zero for any real cycle. This is a direct consequence of the Second Law.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "River of Lost Heat"

Imagine a hiker walking in a circle and returning to the starting point. The hiker can gain elevation easily (receiving heat at high temperature is "useful"), but losing elevation through a bumpy, irreversible path is harder than the smooth reversible descent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation:**
> Think of $\frac{\delta Q}{T}$ as a "quality-weighted" measure of heat. High-temperature heat is "premium" energy. The Clausius Inequality states that the net sum of all such quality-weighted heat transfers over a complete cycle can never be positive — the universe always "charges a tax" (entropy generation) on any real process. Only in the ideal, frictionless, infinitely-slow reversible limit does this tax become zero.

## 1.4 Physical Significance in Engineering

The Clausius Inequality is the **mathematical bridge** between the Second Law and the property of **entropy**. Without it, entropy could not be defined as a thermodynamic property. It is the foundation upon which all subsequent thermal system analyses (Carnot cycle, Rankine cycle, refrigeration, heat pumps) are built.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context (KTU Board Favourite):**
> Named after the German physicist **Rudolf Clausius** (1822–1888), who formulated it in **1854** as part of his work on the mechanical theory of heat. Clausius is also credited with first coining the term "entropy."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Inequality

The Clausius Inequality is built upon a chain of rigorous deductions from the Second Law:

1. **Step 1 — Foundation (Kelvin-Planck Statement):** It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the absorption of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work.
2. **Step 2 — Combination Principle:** Any actual cycle can be mentally combined with a **reversible Carnot heat engine** to form a composite system. The composite system exchanges heat only with a single reservoir.
3. **Step 3 — Application of Kelvin-Planck:** The composite system cannot violate the Kelvin-Planck statement, so its net work output cannot be positive.
4. **Step 4 — Algebraic Rearrangement:** Setting up the energy balance and rearranging yields the inequality $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Inequality

- **For a Reversible Cycle:** $\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$. Since the cycle is reversible, no entropy is generated, and the "entropy tax" is zero.
- **For an Irreversible Cycle:** $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$. Irreversibilities generate entropy ($S_{gen} > 0$), and this positive entropy generation is mathematically equivalent to a negative value of the cyclic integral.

## 2.3 The Deep Connection to Entropy

For an internally reversible process between two states 1 and 2:
$$
\Delta S = S_2 - S_1 = \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}
$$

This is the **Clausius Definition of Entropy**, which is a direct corollary of the Clausius Inequality. The inequality guarantees that entropy is a **point function** (a property) and not a path function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corollary — Clausius Theorem:**
> For a reversible cycle, $\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$. This implies that $\int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ is independent of the path, which is the defining characteristic of a thermodynamic property (entropy).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Conditions | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0$ | Clausius Inequality (General) | Any cycle | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$ | Reversible Cycle | Internally & externally reversible | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$ | Irreversible Cycle | Contains any irreversibility | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\Delta S = \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | Entropy Change Definition | Reversible path between states 1 & 2 | J/K or kJ/K |
| 5 | $S_{gen} = \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} + \Delta S_{system}$ | Entropy Generation (for cycle $\Delta S_{sys}=0$) | $S_{gen} \geq 0$ | J/K |
| 6 | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq \oint \frac{\delta Q_{T_0}}{T_0}$ | Comparison with reservoir at $T_0$ | Combined system analysis | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Reminder for Tables:** The absolute value bars around $T_b$ are not used in Clausius Inequality notation — $T_b$ is always absolute temperature, and the integral is the algebraic sum of $\frac{\delta Q}{T_b}$, not the magnitude sum.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Power Cycle Analysis:** Determines the maximum possible efficiency of heat engines (Carnot efficiency) and quantifies how much actual cycles fall short.
2. **Refrigeration & Heat Pumps:** Establishes the theoretical limits of Coefficient of Performance (COP) and identifies irreversibility sources.
3. **Entropy Balance for Cycles:** Provides the basis for the useful formula $S_{gen,cycle} = -\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \geq 0$.
4. **Exergy (Availability) Analysis:** Direct predecessor to the concept of lost work or irreversibility: $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Clausius Inequality (Classical Proof)

**Given:**
- A thermodynamic system undergoing a cycle while exchanging heat $\delta Q_i$ with $n$ thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_i$ (where $i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$).
- The temperatures $T_i$ may or may not be equal to the system boundary temperature at the point of heat exchange.

**To Prove:**
$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0
$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Construct a Composite System:**
We combine the given cyclic system with a **reversible (Carnot) heat engine** that operates between a single auxiliary reservoir at temperature $T_0$ and the $n$ reservoirs at $T_i$. The reversible engine exchanges heat $Q_{0,rev}$ with reservoir at $T_0$ and heat $\delta Q_{i,rev}$ with reservoir at $T_i$.

For the reversible engine operating between $T_0$ and $T_i$, the Carnot efficiency gives:

$$
\frac{\delta Q_{i,rev}}{T_i} = \frac{\delta Q_{0,rev}}{T_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy Balance of the Combined System:**
The net heat exchange of the combined system with each reservoir $i$ is:
$$
\delta Q_{i,combined} = \delta Q_i + \delta Q_{i,rev}
$$

The combined system exchanges net heat with the reservoir at $T_0$ equal to:
$$
Q_0 = \delta Q_{0,rev} \quad (\text{since the original system has no contact with } T_0)
$$

For the combined cycle to be valid, the original cycle must be modified such that the reversible engine compensates exactly — meaning the system exchanges heat $\delta Q_i$ at temperatures $T_i$ while the auxiliary reversible engine does the rest.

**Step 3 — Apply the Kelvin-Planck Statement:**
The Kelvin-Planck statement requires that the **net work of the combined cycle cannot be positive** if it exchanges heat with a single reservoir at $T_0$. The net work of the combined system is:

$$
W_{net,combined} = Q_0 - \sum_i \delta Q_{i,combined}
$$

Since the reversible engine's work can be calculated via Carnot relations, and applying the Second Law, the only consistent outcome is:

$$
Q_0 \leq 0
$$

with equality only if all processes are reversible.

**Step 4 — Relate $Q_0$ to the Clausius Integral:**
For the reversible auxiliary engine:

$$
\frac{Q_0}{T_0} = \sum_i \frac{\delta Q_{i,rev}}{T_i}
$$

The reversible engine is designed so that its heat exchange with each reservoir **cancels** the corresponding heat exchange of the original system (a standard technique — the engine is run in reverse to "undo" non-reversible heat transfers). This requires:

$$
\delta Q_{i,rev} = -\delta Q_i \quad \text{(for each } i \text{)}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\frac{Q_0}{T_0} = -\sum_i \frac{\delta Q_i}{T_i} = -\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_i}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Inequality:**
Substituting $Q_0 \leq 0$:

$$
-\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_i} \leq 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0}
$$

This is the **Clausius Inequality**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Equality vs. Inequality Cases

| Aspect | Reversible Cycle | Irreversible Cycle |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| Clausius Integral | $\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$ | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$ |
| Entropy Generation | $S_{gen} = 0$ | $S_{gen} > 0$ |
| System Entropy Change | $\oint dS_{sys} = 0$ | $\oint dS_{sys} = 0$ |
| Universe Entropy Change | $\Delta S_{univ} = 0$ | $\Delta S_{univ} > 0$ |
| Direction of Inequality | N/A (equality) | Strictly less than zero |
| Practical Realizability | Ideal (limiting case) | All real cycles |
| Examples | Carnot cycle, idealized Otto cycle | Actual Otto cycle, friction processes |

## 3.3 Corollary Derivation: Clausius Theorem

**Given:** A reversible cycle between two states 1 and 2 traversed via two different reversible paths A and B.

**Proof Sketch:**

Path 1 → 2 via A, then Path 2 → 1 via B forms a complete reversible cycle. By Clausius Inequality:

$$
\int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev,A}}{T} + \int_2^1 \frac{\delta Q_{rev,B}}{T} = 0
$$

Rearranging:
$$
\int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev,A}}{T} = \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev,B}}{T}
$$

This shows the integral is **path-independent**, which is the mathematical signature of a thermodynamic property. We define this property as **entropy**:

$$
S_2 - S_1 = \int_1^2 \left( \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \right)_{int.rev}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Verification of the Inequality

**Problem:** A system exchanges the following heat quantities over one cycle:
- $Q_1 = +1000$ kJ at $T_1 = 800$ K
- $Q_2 = -600$ kJ at $T_2 = 500$ K
- $Q_3 = -400$ kJ at $T_3 = 300$ K

Determine if the cycle is reversible, irreversible, or impossible.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate the cyclic integral:

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{Q_1}{T_1} + \frac{Q_2}{T_2} + \frac{Q_3}{T_3}
$$

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{1000}{800} + \frac{-600}{500} + \frac{-400}{300}
$$

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 1.250 - 1.200 - 1.333 = -1.283 \; \text{kJ/K}
$$

Step 2: Apply the Clausius Inequality:
Since $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = -1.283 \; \text{kJ/K} < 0$, the inequality is satisfied.

Step 3: Conclusion:
The cycle is **irreversible** but **possible** (it does not violate the Second Law). The negative value indicates entropy generation within the system.

$$
\boxed{S_{gen,cycle} = -\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = +1.283 \; \text{kJ/K} > 0}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logical Flow: From Second Law to Clausius Inequality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Kelvin Planck Statement of Second Law] --> B[Combine Actual Cycle with Reversible Carnot Engine]
    B --> C[Composite System Exchanges Heat with Single Reservoir at T0]
    C --> D[Apply Second Law: W net less than or equal to 0]
    D --> E[Express Q0 in terms of Clausius Integral]
    E --> F[Algebraic Rearrangement]
    F --> G[Result Clausius Inequality]
    G --> H{Equality or Inequality}
    H --> I[Equality Reversible Cycle]
    H --> J[Inequality Irreversible Cycle]
    I --> K[Foundation for Entropy Definition]
    J --> L[Entropy Generation Greater than Zero]
    K --> M[Carnot Efficiency Limits]
    L --> N[Real Cycle Performance Analysis]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Clausius Inequality Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Reservoir T1] -->|Q1| SYS[Thermodynamic System]
    R2[Reservoir T2] -->|Q2| SYS
    R3[Reservoir T3] -->|Q3| SYS
    SYS -->|W net| OUT[Work Output Boundary]
    SYS --> CL[Compute Clausius Integral]
    CL --> DEC{Value of Integral}
    DEC -->|Equal Zero| REV[Reversible Cycle Identified]
    DEC -->|Less than Zero| IRR[Irreversible Cycle Identified]
    DEC -->|Greater than Zero| IMP[Impossible Cycle Violates Second Law]
    REV --> S1[Entropy Gen equals 0]
    IRR --> S2[Entropy Gen greater than 0]
    IMP --> S3[Reject Cycle Design]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy: Clausius Inequality Within Thermodynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ZL[Zeroth Law Temperature Definition] --> FL1[First Law Energy Conservation]
    FL1 --> SL[Second Law of Thermodynamics]
    SL --> KP[Kelvin Planck Statement]
    SL --> CC[Clausius Statement]
    KP --> CI[Clausius Inequality]
    CC --> CI
    CI --> ST[Entropy Definition S equals integral dQ rev over T]
    CI --> RE[Reversible Cycle Criterion]
    CI --> IE[Irreversible Cycle Criterion]
    ST --> ET[Entropy Tables and Property Relations]
    ST --> EB[Entropy Balance for Open and Closed Systems]
    RE --> CR[Carnot Cycle Maximum Efficiency]
    IE --> RC[Real Cycle Irreversibility Quantification]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Clausius Inequality and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The Clausius Inequality states that for any thermodynamic cycle:

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0
$$

where $\delta Q$ is the heat transfer to the system and $T_b$ is the absolute temperature at the system boundary.

**Significance:**
- It provides a **mathematical criterion** to distinguish reversible cycles (equality) from irreversible cycles (strict inequality).
- It is the **foundation for defining entropy** as a thermodynamic property.
- It is a direct mathematical consequence of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**.
- No real cycle can have a value of $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b}$ greater than zero.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for statement, 2 marks for significance)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**Why is the equality in Clausius Inequality applicable only to reversible cycles?**

**Model Answer:**

For a reversible cycle, all processes within the cycle (and the heat transfer across the boundary) occur without any irreversibility. This means:
- No entropy is generated internally.
- The system can be restored to its original state without leaving any net change in the universe.
- The cyclic integral of $\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ evaluates to exactly **zero**.

If even a single irreversibility is present (e.g., friction, finite temperature difference, unrestrained expansion), entropy is generated ($S_{gen} > 0$), and the integral becomes strictly **less than zero**. Hence, equality is a **limiting ideal** achievable only in the absence of all irreversibilities.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 2 marks reasoning)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Clausius Inequality from the Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heat engine operates in a cycle and exchanges heat with three reservoirs:
   - $Q_1 = +500$ kJ at $T_1 = 1000$ K
   - $Q_2 = -300$ kJ at $T_2 = 600$ K
   - $Q_3 = -250$ kJ at $T_3 = 400$ K

Determine whether the cycle is reversible, irreversible, or impossible. If irreversible, calculate the entropy generated per cycle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Consider a system undergoing a cycle, exchanging heat $\delta Q_i$ with $n$ reservoirs at temperatures $T_i$ ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$).

**Step 2:** Combine the given system with a **reversible Carnot engine** operating between a reference reservoir at $T_0$ and each of the $n$ reservoirs.

**Step 3:** For the reversible engine, the Carnot relation gives:

$$
\frac{\delta Q_{i,rev}}{T_i} = \frac{\delta Q_{0,rev}}{T_0}
$$

**Step 4:** Design the reversible engine to absorb the heat $-\delta Q_i$ from each reservoir $i$:

$$
\delta Q_{i,rev} = -\delta Q_i
$$

**Step 5:** The combined system exchanges heat $Q_0$ with the single reservoir at $T_0$:

$$
\frac{Q_0}{T_0} = \sum_i \frac{\delta Q_{i,rev}}{T_i} = -\sum_i \frac{\delta Q_i}{T_i} = -\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T}
$$

**Step 6:** By the Kelvin-Planck statement, the combined system cannot produce net work from a single reservoir, so $W_{net} \leq 0$. Energy balance gives $Q_0 = W_{net} \leq 0$.

**Step 7:** Combining results:

$$
-\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0
$$

This is the **Clausius Inequality**. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $Q_1 = 500$ kJ at $T_1 = 1000$ K; $Q_2 = -300$ kJ at $T_2 = 600$ K; $Q_3 = -250$ kJ at $T_3 = 400$ K.

**Step 1:** Compute the Clausius integral:

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{Q_1}{T_1} + \frac{Q_2}{T_2} + \frac{Q_3}{T_3}
$$

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{500}{1000} + \frac{-300}{600} + \frac{-250}{400}
$$

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0.500 - 0.500 - 0.625 = -0.625 \; \text{kJ/K}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the inequality:
Since $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = -0.625 \; \text{kJ/K} < 0$, the cycle **satisfies** the Clausius Inequality.

**Step 3:** Determine cycle nature:
The integral is **strictly negative**, hence the cycle is **irreversible**.

**Step 4:** Calculate entropy generation:
For a cyclic process, the system entropy change is zero. From the entropy balance:

$$
S_{gen} = \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \cdot (-1) = 0.625 \; \text{kJ/K}
$$

Equivalently, since for a cycle $\oint dS_{sys} = 0$:

$$
S_{gen} = -\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0.625 \; \text{kJ/K} > 0
$$

**Conclusion:** The cycle is **irreversible and feasible**, with entropy generation of $0.625$ kJ/K per cycle.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the Clausius Theorem and establish that entropy is a thermodynamic property. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A refrigeration cycle operates between a cold reservoir at $-10°C$ and a hot reservoir at $40°C$. The cycle absorbs $200$ kJ from the cold reservoir and rejects $350$ kJ to the hot reservoir per cycle. Verify the cycle using the Clausius Inequality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Clausius Theorem:** For any reversible cycle, the cyclic integral of $\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ is zero.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0
$$

**Proof that Entropy is a Property:**

**Step 1:** Consider a reversible cycle consisting of two reversible paths A and B connecting states 1 and 2. The cycle is: 1 → 2 (via path A) → 1 (via path B).

**Step 2:** Apply the Clausius Theorem:

$$
\int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev,A}}{T} + \int_2^1 \frac{\delta Q_{rev,B}}{T} = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Reverse the limits of the second integral:

$$
\int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev,A}}{T} - \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev,B}}{T} = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$
\int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev,A}}{T} = \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev,B}}{T}
$$

**Step 5:** Since this equality holds for **any** two reversible paths between 1 and 2, the integral is **path-independent**. A path-independent integral defines a thermodynamic property.

**Step 6:** This property is defined as **entropy (S)**:

$$
S_2 - S_1 = \int_1^2 \left( \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \right)_{int.rev}
$$

Entropy is therefore an **exact differential** and a **point function**. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $T_C = -10°C = 263.15$ K; $T_H = 40°C = 313.15$ K; $Q_C = +200$ kJ (absorbed from cold reservoir); $Q_H = -350$ kJ (rejected to hot reservoir).

**Step 1:** Check energy balance (First Law):
Net work input: $W_{in} = Q_H + Q_C = -350 + 200 = -150$ kJ (input of 150 kJ, consistent with refrigeration).

**Step 2:** Apply the Clausius Inequality:

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} = \frac{Q_C}{T_C} + \frac{Q_H}{T_H}
$$

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} = \frac{+200}{263.15} + \frac{-350}{313.15}
$$

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} = 0.7601 - 1.1177 = -0.3576 \; \text{kJ/K}
$$

**Step 3:** Verify against the inequality:
Since $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} = -0.3576 \; \text{kJ/K} < 0$, the cycle **satisfies** the Clausius Inequality.

**Step 4:** Conclusion:
The refrigeration cycle is **feasible and irreversible**. The entropy generated per cycle is:

$$
S_{gen} = -\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} = 0.3576 \; \text{kJ/K}
$$

**Note:** The actual COP of this cycle is:

$$
COP_{actual} = \frac{Q_C}{W_{in}} = \frac{200}{150} = 1.333
$$

The Carnot (reversible) COP is:

$$
COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_C}{T_H - T_C} = \frac{263.15}{50} = 5.263
$$

Since $COP_{actual} < COP_{Carnot}$, irreversibility is confirmed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Clausius Inequality Problems:**
> 1. **Temperature Units:** Students frequently forget to convert Celsius to Kelvin. Always use $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign Convention:** The sign of $Q$ depends on the direction of heat flow *to* the system. Heat *absorbed* is positive; heat *rejected* is negative. Confusing signs flips the conclusion. Loss: **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Cyclic Nature:** For a cycle, $\oint dS_{sys} = 0$. Many students wrongly add a non-zero system entropy change. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Boundary vs. Bulk Temperature:** The inequality strictly uses boundary temperature $T_b$ for the heat transfer. Loss: **1 mark** if used incorrectly.
> 5. **Concluding "Impossible" Prematurely:** A negative result is **not impossible**; it is **irreversible**. Only a positive result indicates impossibility.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Clausius Inequality:**

- **Core Statement:** $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0$ for **any** cycle.
- **Reversible Cycle:** Equality holds ($= 0$).
- **Irreversible Cycle:** Strict inequality holds ($< 0$).
- **Impossible Cycle:** Positive integral ($> 0$) violates the Second Law.
- **Temperature must be in absolute units (K or °R)** at the system boundary.
- **Heat sign convention:** $Q > 0$ if heat enters the system.
- **Direct consequence of Kelvin-Planck** statement of the Second Law.
- **Foundation of entropy:** Clausius Inequality → Clausius Theorem → Entropy definition.
- **Entropy Generation:** $S_{gen} = -\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \geq 0$ (equality for reversible, positive for irreversible).
- **For a cycle:** $\oint dU = 0$, $\oint dS_{sys} = 0$ (state properties return to initial values).
- **Real-world application:** Distinguishes feasible cycles from infeasible designs; sets the theoretical limit (Carnot) for all thermal cycles.
- **Must-know equation:** $S_2 - S_1 = \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ (entropy as a property).
- **Key insight:** The inequality is the **mathematical link** between the Second Law and the concept of entropy as a thermodynamic state function.
- **Examiner favourite question types:** Numerical verification, derivation proofs, identifying reversibility, and COP comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Increase of Entropy. Availability and Irreversibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principle of Increase of Entropy. Availability and Irreversibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Principle of Increase of Entropy — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> The **Principle of Increase of Entropy** is the most fundamental corollary of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**. It states that *the entropy of an isolated system (or the universe, treated as an isolated system) can never decrease; it can only increase for an irreversible process or remain constant for a reversible process*.

Mathematically, the principle is expressed as:

$$S_{gen} \ge 0$$

where $S_{gen}$ is the **entropy generated** within the system boundaries during a process. The equality holds for a **reversible** process, and the strict inequality holds for an **irreversible** process.

The principle is essentially a restatement of the **Clausius Inequality** for a cyclic process applied to a fixed mass of fluid:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Entropy is *not* conserved like energy. The principle does **not** say "entropy is constant" — it says entropy of the universe is **non-decreasing**. This is the deepest philosophical distinction between the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics.

---

### 1.2 Availability (Exergy) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Availability** (also called **Exergy** or **Available Energy**) is defined as the *maximum useful work that can be extracted from a system as it is brought reversibly from its initial state to the dead state (the state of thermodynamic equilibrium with the environment at temperature $T_0$ and pressure $P_0$)*.

For a **closed system**, the availability $\phi$ is:

$$\phi = (u - u_0) + P_0(v - v_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$$

where subscript $0$ denotes properties at the **dead state** (environment), and $V$, $z$ are velocity and elevation relative to the environment.

---

### 1.3 Irreversibility — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Irreversibility** $I$ is defined as the *difference between the reversible work and the actual work for a process occurring between two specified states*. It is a direct measure of the lost potential to do useful work due to the irreversible nature of real processes.

$$I = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Gouy–Stodola Theorem:** Irreversibility is directly proportional to the total entropy generated during the process, scaled by the absolute temperature of the environment:
> $$I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$
> This is one of the **highest-weightage topics** in KTU Module 2 numerical problems.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

#### The Spilled Coffee Analogy
Imagine pouring a fresh hot cup of coffee (high-grade energy, low entropy) into the room environment. The coffee cools down, the room warms up by an imperceptible amount, and the **total entropy of the universe has increased**. The thermal energy is *still there* (First Law is satisfied), but it is now **unavailable** to do useful work because it is at the same temperature as the surroundings. The energy has not been destroyed — its **availability has been destroyed**.

#### The "Spending Money" Analogy for Entropy
Think of energy as **money** and entropy as **tax**. Every real (irreversible) transaction charges a tax. The total money in the world (energy) is conserved, but your *spending power* (availability) decreases with every transaction. The tax collected is the **irreversibility** — energy still exists, but it can never be recovered as useful work.

#### The Dam Analogy for Availability
Picture a mountain lake held back by a dam. The water has **potential energy** (analogous to *availability*) because of the height difference from the sea. As the water falls and reaches the sea (dead state), it can no longer generate electricity. The lost potential is the **irreversibility** of the falling process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants to Remember (KTU Board Exam):**
> - Standard environment temperature: $T_0 = 298.15$ K (or **25 °C**)
> - Standard environment pressure: $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa (or **1 atm**)
> - Universal gas constant: $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Clausius Inequality — Foundation of Entropy Principle

The **Clausius Inequality** is the mathematical backbone of the Principle of Increase of Entropy. For any thermodynamic cycle, the cyclic integral of $\delta Q / T$ is bounded:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$

**Equality** → Reversible cycle (Carnot cycle is the unique maximum-efficiency cycle between two reservoirs).
**Strict Inequality** → Irreversible cycle (all real cycles).

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

- For a **reversible** cycle, all processes can be reversed, so the cyclic integral equals zero.
- For an **irreversible** cycle, internal dissipation (friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing, heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$) generates entropy, and the integral is strictly less than zero.
- Since entropy $S$ is a **point function** (state property), the cyclic integral being $\le 0$ implies that *for any process between two states, the change in entropy is greater than or equal to the integral of $\delta Q / T$*:

$$dS \ge \frac{\delta Q}{T} \quad \text{or} \quad S_2 - S_1 \ge \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T}$$

---

### 2.2 Entropy Balance for a Closed System

The **entropy balance** is the Second Law expressed as an equality by introducing $S_{gen}$:

$$\Delta S_{system} = \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} + S_{gen}$$

Equivalently:

$$S_{gen} = \Delta S_{system} - \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} \ge 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The entropy balance is used in KTU problems to determine whether a proposed process is *feasible* (i.e., $S_{gen} \ge 0$). If a student calculates $S_{gen} < 0$, the proposed process **violates the Second Law** and is impossible.

---

### 2.3 Reversibility vs. Irreversibility — Classification of Causes

**Reversible Process:** A process that can be reversed without leaving any net change in either the system or the surroundings. Requires:
1. Quasi-equilibrium (infinitely slow) process.
2. No friction, no unrestrained expansion, no mixing of dissimilar fluids.
3. No heat transfer across a finite temperature difference.

**Irreversibilities** (causes of entropy generation):

| Type | Source |
|---|---|
| **External Irreversibility** | Heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$ |
| **Internal Irreversibility** | Friction, viscous dissipation, unrestrained expansion |
| **Internal + External** | Combustion, chemical reaction |

---

### 2.4 Availability (Exergy) for a Closed System — Full Development

Starting from the **First Law** for a closed system undergoing a reversible process from state 1 to dead state 0:

$$W_{rev} = (U_1 - U_0) - T_0(S_1 - S_0) + P_0(V_1 - V_0) + \frac{mV_1^2}{2} + mgz_1$$

For a **non-flow (closed) system** (ignoring KE and PE for simplicity):

$$\phi = (u_1 - u_0) - T_0(s_1 - s_0) + P_0(v_1 - v_0)$$

If KE and PE are **non-negligible** (KTU problems on compressed gas in a tank moving upward):

$$\phi = (u_1 - u_0) + P_0(v_1 - v_0) - T_0(s_1 - s_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$$

For a **flow system** (steady-flow exergy per unit mass):

$$\psi = (h_1 - h_0) - T_0(s_1 - s_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The $-T_0(s_1 - s_0)$ term represents the *unavailable energy* (the energy that is bound up in the form of disorder and cannot be converted to work even by an ideal reversible engine).

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the master reference for KTU 2024 ESE numericals on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clausius Inequality | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$ | kJ/K | Cyclic integral |
| 2 | Entropy Principle | $S_{gen} \ge 0$ | kJ/K | For any process |
| 3 | Entropy Generation (closed) | $S_{gen} = m(s_2 - s_1) - \int \frac{\delta Q}{T_b}$ | kJ/K | Boundary at $T_b$ |
| 4 | Closed System Availability | $\phi = (u - u_0) + P_0(v - v_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \tfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$ | kJ | Per unit mass: kJ/kg |
| 5 | Flow Exergy (per kg) | $\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \tfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$ | kJ/kg | Open system |
| 6 | Irreversibility | $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ | kJ | Gouy–Stodola |
| 7 | Irreversibility (alternative) | $I = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$ | kJ | Definition |
| 8 | Decrease in Availability | $\phi_1 - \phi_2 = W_{rev,out} - W_{actual,out}$ | kJ | Closed system |
| 9 | Second Law Efficiency | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{\eta_{th}}{\eta_{th,rev}} = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{rev}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le \eta_{II} \le 1$ |
| 10 | Isentropic Process | $s_2 = s_1$ | — | Reversible adiabatic |
| 11 | Dead State | $T = T_0,\; P = P_0$ | — | Zero availability |
| 12 | Carnot Efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | dimensionless | Upper bound |
| 13 | Isochoric Heat | $Q = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ | Constant $V$ |
| 14 | Isobaric Heat | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ | Constant $P$ |
| 15 | Ideal Gas $s$ change | $\Delta s = c_p \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} - R \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | kJ/(kg·K) | $\gamma = c_p/c_v$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** When applying the entropy balance, the heat transfer $\delta Q$ follows the **First-Law sign convention** — $Q_{in}$ is *positive* and $Q_{out}$ is *negative*. Mixing these conventions is the most common cause of lost marks in Part B problems.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Entropy / Availability / Irreversibility |
|---|---|
| **Power Plant Design** | Steam Rankine cycle optimization; exergy analysis pinpoints largest irreversibility (typically boiler) |
| **Refrigeration & HVAC** | Second-law efficiency compares actual COP with Carnot COP |
| **Internal Combustion Engines** | Availability of fuel-air mixture; irreversibility in combustion and exhaust |
| **Chemical Process Industries** | Exergy analysis of distillation, reactors, heat exchangers |
| **Cryogenics** | Minimum work of liquefaction derived from availability function |
| **Renewable Energy Systems** | Geothermal, OTEC use ambient $T_0$ as reference dead-state reservoir |
| **Waste Heat Recovery** | Quantifies how much exhaust energy is still *available* (exergy) vs. *total* (energy) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Availability of a Closed System (from First + Second Law)

**Step 1 — Start with the First Law for a closed system undergoing a reversible process:**

For a closed system exchanging heat $\delta Q$ at boundary temperature $T_b$ and doing work $\delta W$:

$$\delta Q - \delta W = dU$$

Since the process is **reversible** and the system is in thermal equilibrium with the surroundings at $T_b$:

$$T_b = T_{system} = T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta Q_{rev} = T \cdot dS$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the reversible heat:**

$$T \cdot dS - \delta W_{rev} = dU$$

Rearranging for the reversible work output:

$$\delta W_{rev} = -dU + T \cdot dS$$

**Step 3 — Introduce a heat reservoir at $T_0$ (the environment).**

Replace $T$ with $T_0$ (since the reversible engine operates between the system temperature and the dead-state temperature):

$$\delta W_{rev} = -dU + T_0 \cdot dS$$

**Step 4 — Account for work done against the environment (boundary work at $P_0$):**

The actual useful work excludes the work done in displacing the atmosphere. Subtract $P_0 \, dV$:

$$\delta W_{useful,rev} = -dU + T_0 \cdot dS - P_0 \cdot dV$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from initial state (1) to dead state (0):**

$$W_{rev,max} = (U_1 - U_0) - T_0(S_1 - S_0) + P_0(V_1 - V_0)$$

**Step 6 — Per unit mass and including kinetic + potential energy:**

Dividing by mass $m$ and adding $\tfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$:

$$\boxed{\phi = (u - u_0) + P_0(v - v_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz}$$

This is the **closed system availability** as required by KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Gouy–Stodola Theorem ($I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$)

**Step 1 — Write the entropy balance for the system + surroundings (universe):**

$$S_{gen} = (S_2 - S_1)_{system} + (S_2 - S_1)_{surr}$$

**Step 2 — For surroundings exchanging heat $Q$ at constant $T_0$:**

$$(S_2 - S_1)_{surr} = -\frac{Q}{T_0}$$

(Negative because heat *leaving* surroundings decreases its entropy; if $Q > 0$ enters the system, surroundings lose entropy.)

**Step 3 — Apply the First Law to the system (closed, with work $W$):**

$$U_2 - U_1 = Q - W$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$Q = (U_2 - U_1) + W$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Q$ into the surroundings entropy change:**

$$(S_2 - S_1)_{surr} = -\frac{(U_2 - U_1) + W}{T_0}$$

**Step 5 — Total entropy generation:**

$$S_{gen} = (S_2 - S_1)_{system} - \frac{(U_2 - U_1) + W}{T_0}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to isolate $W$:**

$$W = (U_1 - U_2) - T_0(S_1 - S_2) - T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$

**Step 7 — Compare with the reversible work $W_{rev}$ (where $S_{gen} = 0$):**

$$W_{rev} = (U_1 - U_2) - T_0(S_1 - S_2)$$

**Step 8 — Take the difference $W_{rev} - W_{actual}$:**

$$W_{rev} - W = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$

By definition, this difference is the **irreversibility** $I$:

$$\boxed{I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}}$$

This is the **Gouy–Stodola Theorem** — a guaranteed 7-mark derivation question in KTU 2024 ESE.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Standard)

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern Problem]**
> A rigid, insulated tank of volume **$V = 0.5 \text{ m}^3$** contains air at **$P_1 = 1.5 \text{ MPa}$** and **$T_1 = 600 \text{ K}$**. A valve is opened and the air escapes into the atmosphere at **$P_0 = 100 \text{ kPa}$** and **$T_0 = 300 \text{ K}$** until the pressure inside equals atmospheric. Treat air as an ideal gas with $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K). Determine:
> (a) The change in availability of the air remaining in the tank.
> (b) The irreversibility of the process.
> Take $g = 0$, $V = 0$.

**Given:**
- $V = 0.5$ m³, $P_1 = 1500$ kPa, $T_1 = 600$ K
- $P_2 = P_0 = 100$ kPa, $T_0 = 300$ K
- $c_p = 1.005$, $c_v = 0.718$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Adiabatic ($Q = 0$), rigid tank, air ideal gas

**Step 1 — Find initial mass $m_1$:**

$$m_1 = \frac{P_1 V}{R T_1} = \frac{1500 \times 0.5}{0.287 \times 600} = \frac{750}{172.2} = 4.355 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — For an adiabatic rigid container with gas escaping, the temperature of remaining gas remains at $T_1$ (since $u_2 = u_1$ for the gas that stays, in a fixed-volume adiabatic discharge — the classical result):**

$$T_2 = T_1 = 600 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Find final mass $m_2$:**

$$m_2 = \frac{P_2 V}{R T_2} = \frac{100 \times 0.5}{0.287 \times 600} = \frac{50}{172.2} = 0.2904 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Entropy change of the gas remaining in the tank (state 1 → 2, ideal gas, same T):**

For an ideal gas:
$$s_2 - s_1 = c_p \ln \frac{T_2}{T_1} - R \ln \frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

$$s_2 - s_1 = 1.005 \ln\left(\frac{600}{600}\right) - 0.287 \ln\left(\frac{100}{1500}\right)$$

$$s_2 - s_1 = 0 - 0.287 \times \ln(0.06667) = -0.287 \times (-2.7081) = 0.7772 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Change in availability of the air remaining:**

$$\Delta \phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = m_2 (u_2 - u_0) - m_1(u_1 - u_0) + P_0(m_2 v_2 - m_1 v_1) - T_0[m_2 s_2 - m_1 s_1]$$

**Compute term-by-term:**

- $u_2 - u_0 = c_v (T_2 - T_0) = 0.718 \times (600 - 300) = 215.4$ kJ/kg
- $u_1 - u_0 = c_v (T_1 - T_0) = 0.718 \times (600 - 300) = 215.4$ kJ/kg
- $v_1 = R T_1 / P_1 = 0.287 \times 600 / 1500 = 0.1148$ m³/kg
- $v_2 = R T_2 / P_2 = 0.287 \times 600 / 100 = 1.722$ m³/kg
- $s_0$ is the entropy at dead state; compute $s$ relative to dead state using $T_0 = 300$ K, $P_0 = 100$ kPa as reference:

For state 1:
$$s_1 - s_0 = c_p \ln \frac{T_1}{T_0} - R \ln \frac{P_1}{P_0} = 1.005 \ln(2) - 0.287 \ln(15)$$
$$= 1.005 \times 0.6931 - 0.287 \times 2.7081 = 0.6966 - 0.7772 = -0.0806 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

For state 2:
$$s_2 - s_0 = c_p \ln \frac{T_2}{T_0} - R \ln \frac{P_2}{P_0} = 1.005 \ln(2) - 0.287 \ln(1) = 0.6966 - 0 = 0.6966 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Compute $\phi_1$ for the initial mass:**

$$\phi_1 = m_1[(u_1 - u_0) + P_0(v_1 - v_0) - T_0(s_1 - s_0)]$$

$v_0 = R T_0 / P_0 = 0.287 \times 300 / 100 = 0.861$ m³/kg

$$\phi_1 = 4.355[215.4 + 100(0.1148 - 0.861) - 300(-0.0806)]$$

$$\phi_1 = 4.355[215.4 + 100(-0.7462) + 24.18]$$

$$\phi_1 = 4.355[215.4 - 74.62 + 24.18] = 4.355 \times 164.96 = 718.4 \text{ kJ}$$

**Compute $\phi_2$ for the final mass:**

$$\phi_2 = m_2[(u_2 - u_0) + P_0(v_2 - v_0) - T_0(s_2 - s_0)]$$

$$\phi_2 = 0.2904[215.4 + 100(1.722 - 0.861) - 300(0.6966)]$$

$$\phi_2 = 0.2904[215.4 + 100(0.861) - 208.98]$$

$$\phi_2 = 0.2904[215.4 + 86.1 - 208.98] = 0.2904 \times 92.52 = 26.86 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 6 — Decrease in availability (Part a answer):**

$$\Delta \phi = \phi_1 - \phi_2 = 718.4 - 26.86 = 691.5 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 7 — Entropy generation for the process (Part b).**

The escaped air carries entropy to the surroundings. Total entropy generation = entropy change of remaining gas + entropy change of atmosphere (which gained the escaped mass with entropy $s_2$ at its initial state, ending at dead state $s_0$).

A cleaner approach: For the **universe** = tank + surroundings:
$$S_{gen} = m_2(s_2 - s_0) - m_1(s_1 - s_0) + (m_1 - m_2) \cdot 0 \quad \text{(adiabatic, no heat to surroundings)}$$

Actually, with the proper formulation including the mass that left:

$$S_{gen} = m_2 s_2 - m_1 s_1 + (m_1 - m_2) s_{atm,exit}$$

For a simpler closed-form, use the difference in availability and the relation:

$$I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen} = \Delta \phi - W_{useful}$$

Since no work is extracted in this uncontrolled discharge, $W_{useful} = 0$:

$$I = \Delta \phi = 691.5 \text{ kJ}$$

Therefore:
$$S_{gen} = \frac{I}{T_0} = \frac{691.5}{300} = 2.305 \text{ kJ/K}$$

> **Final Answers:**
> (a) Decrease in availability of air remaining in the tank: $\Delta \phi \approx 691.5$ kJ
> (b) Irreversibility: $I \approx 691.5$ kJ; $S_{gen} \approx 2.305$ kJ/K

---

### 3.4 Compact Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for KTU Numericals

| Given Process Type | First Identify | Then Apply |
|---|---|---|
| Constant volume heating | $W = 0$, $Q = mc_v \Delta T$ | $\Delta S = mc_v \ln(T_2/T_1)$ |
| Constant pressure heating | $W = P \Delta V$, $Q = mc_p \Delta T$ | $\Delta S = mc_p \ln(T_2/T_1)$ |
| Isothermal expansion/compression | $\Delta U = 0$, $Q = W$ | $\Delta S = mR \ln(V_2/V_1)$ |
| Adiabatic (reversible) | Isentropic, $s_2 = s_1$ | $S_{gen} = 0$ |
| Adiabatic (irreversible) | $Q = 0$, $s_2 > s_1$ | $S_{gen} = m(s_2 - s_1) > 0$ |
| Heat transfer at constant $T$ | Reservoir problem | $\Delta S = Q/T$ |
| Tank discharge to atmosphere | $T_2 = T_1$ (rigid adiabatic) | $S_{gen}$ from initial–final mass balance |
| Work producing device | $W_{actual}$ given, compute $W_{rev}$ | $I = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$ |
| Availability question | Compute $\phi_1$, $\phi_2$ | $\eta_{II} = (\phi_1 - \phi_2)/(\text{input availability})$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Principle of Increase of Entropy — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Second Law of Thermodynamics] --> B[Clausius Inequality]
    A --> C[Principle of Increase of Entropy]
    B --> D[Cyclic Integral less than or equal to zero]
    C --> E[Entropy of Universe is non-decreasing]
    C --> F[Reversible Process: Sgen equals zero]
    C --> G[Irreversible Process: Sgen greater than zero]
    G --> H1[External Irreversibility: Finite Delta T]
    G --> H2[Internal Irreversibility: Friction, Unrestrained Expansion]
    G --> H3[Chemical Irreversibility: Combustion, Reaction]
    F --> I1[Carnot Cycle maximum efficiency]
    I1 --> I2[Entropy Generation equals zero]
    I2 --> I3[No degradation of energy quality]
    G --> J[Availability decreases]
    J --> K[Gouy Stodola: I equals T0 times Sgen]
    K --> L[Second Law Efficiency: Actual Work over Reversible Work]
    L --> M[Design Improvement: Reduce Irreversibilities]
```

### 4.2 Availability Analysis — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SYS1[System at Initial State 1] -->|Boundary at T and P| PROC[Process: Real Irreversible]
    PROC -->|Actual Work Wactual| SYS2[System at Final State 2]
    PROC -.->|Sgen greater than zero| SURROUNDINGS[Environment T0, P0]
    SURROUNDINGS -->|Heat Rejection Q| PROC
    SYS1 -->|Reversible Path| REVPROC[Ideal Reversible Engine]
    REVPROC -->|Maximum Work Wrev| DEAD[Dead State T0, P0]
    REVPROC -.->|Sgen equals zero| DEAD
    PROC -->|Energy Loss Boundary| IBLOCK[Irreversibility Block I equals T0 Sgen]
    IBLOCK --> OUT1[Work Lost]
    IBLOCK --> OUT2[Exergy Destroyed]
    IBLOCK --> OUT3[Second Law Efficiency Reduced]
    DEAD --> OUT4[Zero Availability]
```

### 4.3 Entropy Generation and Available Energy Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Total Energy Input Q in or W in] --> USABLE[Available Energy A or Exergy]
    INPUT --> UNAVAIL[Unavailable Energy B equals T0 times S]
    USABLE --> USED[Useful Work Output W actual]
    USABLE --> LOST[Lost Work equals T0 Sgen]
    LOST --> DESTROY[Exergy Destroyed equals Irreversibility]
    DESTROY --> IRR1[Friction]
    DESTROY --> IRR2[Heat Transfer across Finite Delta T]
    DESTROY --> IRR3[Mixing of Fluids]
    DESTROY --> IRR4[Chemical Reaction]
    USED --> EFF[Second Law Efficiency equals W actual over W rev]
    LOST --> EFF
    EFF --> TARGET[Design Target: Minimize I, Maximize eta_II]
```

### 4.4 Reversible vs. Irreversible Process — Side-by-Side Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REV[Reversible Process Block]
        R1[State 1] -->|Quasi static, no Delta T, no friction| R2[State 2]
        R2 -.->|Sgen equals zero| RNOTE[All Work Recoverable]
    end
    subgraph IRREV[Irreversible Process Block]
        I1[State 1] -->|Finite driving force, friction present| I2[State 2]
        I2 -.->|Sgen greater than zero| INOTE[Lost Work equals T0 Sgen]
        I2 -->|Heat rejected to T0| QATMO[Q to atmosphere]
    end
    REV --> COMPARE[Comparison Block]
    IRREV --> COMPARE
    COMPARE --> RES[Reversible Work greater than Irreversible Work]
    RES --> DEGRAD[Energy Quality Degraded]
    DEGRAD --> ENTROPY[Entropy Increased]
```
