## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State and explain the Principle of Increase of Entropy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Principle of Increase of Entropy states that *the entropy of an isolated system (or the universe) can never decrease; it either remains constant for a reversible process or increases for an irreversible process.*
>
> Mathematically: $\Delta S_{universe} \ge 0$, or equivalently, $S_{gen} \ge 0$.
>
> This principle is a direct consequence of the Clausius Inequality $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$ and is the most fundamental statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. **It establishes the directional nature of natural processes** — entropy spontaneously increases, never decreases, in any real (irreversible) process. **[3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for equation, 1 for significance]**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define availability and irreversibility. State the Gouy–Stodola theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Availability (Exergy)** is the maximum useful work obtainable as a system returns reversibly from its initial state to the dead state (in thermal and mechanical equilibrium with the environment at $T_0$, $P_0$).
>
> **Irreversibility** is the difference between the reversible work and the actual work for a process between two specified states: $I = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$.
>
> **Gouy–Stodola Theorem:** The irreversibility of a process is directly proportional to the total entropy generated, scaled by the ambient temperature: $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$. **[3 marks: 1 mark per definition/equation]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Always state assumptions (ideal gas, neglect KE/PE) at the start — failing to do so costs 1 mark.
> - Carry forward intermediate values; do not round prematurely.
> - Units must accompany every numerical answer.
> - For Gouy–Stodola derivations, the cyclic integral convention must be explicitly stated.
> - Negative $S_{gen}$ in a numerical answer implies the proposed process is **impossible** — this is often the actual trick in the question.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and prove the Clausius Inequality. Show that it leads to the principle of increase of entropy.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A piston–cylinder device contains **0.05 kg** of steam at **$P_1 = 2$ MPa** and **$T_1 = 400 °C$**. The steam undergoes a **reversible isothermal expansion** to a pressure of **200 kPa**. Determine: (i) the work done, (ii) the change in availability, and (iii) the heat transferred. Use steam tables. Take $T_0 = 25 °C$, $P_0 = 100$ kPa.

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Statement:**
For any thermodynamic cycle operating between heat reservoirs, the cyclic integral of $\delta Q / T$ is bounded by zero:
$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$
where $T$ is the absolute temperature at the boundary where $\delta Q$ is transferred.

**Step 2 — Proof:**

Consider a reversible cycle consisting of an arbitrary cycle and a Carnot cycle, both operating between the same two reservoirs. The net work output is $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$. By the Kelvin–Planck statement, no engine can produce net work while exchanging heat with a single reservoir — hence $W_{net} \le 0$, implying $Q_H \le Q_L$.

For the Carnot cycle: $\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$.

Combining: $\frac{Q_H}{T_H} \le \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$, which leads to:
$$\oint \left( \frac{\delta Q}{T} \right)_{rev} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \oint \left( \frac{\delta Q}{T} \right)_{irr} < 0$$

**Step 3 — Connection to Entropy Principle:**

Since entropy $S$ is a point function, for any two states 1 and 2:
$$\int_1^2 \left( \frac{\delta Q}{T} \right)_{rev} = S_2 - S_1$$

For an irreversible process between the same states:
$$\int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T} < S_2 - S_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_2 - S_1 \ge \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T}$$

For an isolated system ($\delta Q = 0$): $S_2 - S_1 \ge 0$, i.e., $S_{gen} \ge 0$. **(Principle proved.)**

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — From steam tables at $P_1 = 2$ MPa, $T_1 = 400 °C$** (superheated):
- $u_1 \approx 2964.2$ kJ/kg
- $s_1 \approx 6.7275$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $v_1 \approx 0.15122$ m³/kg

**Step 2 — Reversible isothermal expansion to $P_2 = 200$ kPa (still superheated, $T_2 = 400 °C$):**
- $u_2 \approx 2967.7$ kJ/kg (approximate, since $u$ is nearly constant on isotherm at moderate pressures)
- $s_2 \approx 7.8987$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $v_2 \approx 1.5493$ m³/kg

**Step 3 — Work done (reversible isothermal, ideal-gas approximation, or use $W = \int P\,dV$):**
For isothermal reversible process of steam approximated as ideal gas:
$$W = m R T \ln \frac{P_1}{P_2}$$

With $R_{steam} = 0.4615$ kJ/(kg·K), $T = 673.15$ K:
$$W = 0.05 \times 0.4615 \times 673.15 \times \ln \frac{2000}{200} = 0.05 \times 310.66 \times 2.3026 \approx 35.76 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Change in availability:**
$$\Delta \phi = m[(u_2 - u_1) + P_0(v_2 - v_1) - T_0(s_2 - s_1)]$$

With $P_0 = 100$ kPa, $T_0 = 298.15$ K:
$$\Delta \phi = 0.05[(2967.7 - 2964.2) + 100(1.5493 - 0.15122) - 298.15(7.8987 - 6.7275)]$$
$$= 0.05[3.5 + 100 \times 1.3981 - 298.15 \times 1.1712]$$
$$= 0.05[3.5 + 139.81 - 349.18] = 0.05 \times (-205.87) = -10.29 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Heat transferred:**
$$Q = W + \Delta U = 35.76 + 0.05 \times (2967.7 - 2964.2) = 35.76 + 0.175 = 35.94 \text{ kJ}$$

> **Final Answers for Q A(b):** $W \approx 35.76$ kJ; $\Delta \phi \approx -10.29$ kJ (availability decreases); $Q \approx 35.94$ kJ.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Gouy–Stodola theorem $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ for a closed system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A furnace supplies heat at a rate of **$Q_H = 50$ kW** to a heat engine that rejects heat to a river at **$T_0 = 20 °C$**. The engine operates between a source at **$T_H = 727 °C$** and the river. The actual efficiency is **30%**. Determine: (i) the reversible (Carnot) work output, (ii) the irreversibility rate, and (iii) the second-law efficiency.

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

*(Refer to the full step-by-step derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes.)*

**Summary of derivation steps for marks:**

| Step | Content | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Entropy balance for system + surroundings | 1 |
| 2 | $\Delta S_{surr} = -Q/T_0$ (constant $T_0$) | 1 |
| 3 | First Law: $Q = \Delta U + W$ | 1 |
| 4 | Substitute $Q$ into entropy balance | 1 |
| 5 | Solve for $W$ | 1 |
| 6 | Compare $W$ vs $W_{rev}$ ($S_{gen}=0$) | 1 |
| 7 | Conclude $W_{rev} - W = T_0 S_{gen} = I$ | 1 |

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $Q_H = 50$ kW, $T_H = 727 + 273 = 1000$ K, $T_0 = 20 + 273 = 293$ K, $\eta_{th} = 0.30$.

**Step 1 — Carnot (reversible) efficiency:**
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_0}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{293}{1000} = 0.707$$

**Step 2 — Actual work output:**
$$W_{actual} = \eta_{th} \times Q_H = 0.30 \times 50 = 15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Reversible (Carnot) work output:**
$$W_{rev} = \eta_{Carnot} \times Q_H = 0.707 \times 50 = 35.35 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Irreversibility (lost work):**
$$I = W_{rev} - W_{actual} = 35.35 - 15 = 20.35 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Second-law efficiency:**
$$\eta_{II} = \frac{W_{actual}}{W_{rev}} = \frac{15}{35.35} = 0.4243 \approx 42.43\%$$

**Step 6 — Verify via entropy generation:**
Heat rejected to river: $Q_L = Q_H - W_{actual} = 50 - 15 = 35$ kW.
$$\dot{S}_{gen} = \frac{Q_L}{T_0} - \frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{35}{293} - \frac{50}{1000} = 0.1195 - 0.0500 = 0.0695 \text{ kW/K}$$
$$I = T_0 \cdot \dot{S}_{gen} = 293 \times 0.0695 = 20.36 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Final Answers for Q B(b):** $W_{rev} = 35.35$ kW; $I = 20.35$ kW; $\eta_{II} \approx 42.43\%$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Read before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] The **Principle of Increase of Entropy** says $\Delta S_{universe} \ge 0$; entropy of isolated systems can never decrease.
- [x] **Clausius Inequality:** $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$ — equality for reversible, strict inequality for irreversible.
- [x] **Entropy balance** (closed system): $S_{gen} = m(s_2 - s_1) - \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \ge 0$.
- [x] **Availability (closed system):** $\phi = (u - u_0) + P_0(v - v_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \tfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$.
- [x] **Flow exergy:** $\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \tfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$.
- [x] **Gouy–Stodola Theorem:** $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ — always remember the **environment temperature** is the proportionality constant.
- [x] **Irreversibility = Lost Work:** $I = W_{rev} - W_{actual} = \phi_1 - \phi_2 - W_{useful}$.
- [x] **Second Law Efficiency:** $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{rev}} = \dfrac{\eta_{th}}{\eta_{rev}}$.
- [x] **Dead state** ($T_0$, $P_0$): all availability is zero; system is in complete equilibrium with environment.
- [x] **Three irreversibility types:** external (finite $\Delta T$), internal (friction, unrestrained expansion), and chemical (combustion).
- [x] Standard environment for KTU problems: $T_0 = 298.15$ K (or 300 K for round-off), $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa (or 100 kPa for round-off).
- [x] For **ideal gases:** $\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_2/T_1) - R \ln(P_2/P_1)$.
- [x] **Adiabatic rigid tank discharge:** final temperature of remaining gas = initial temperature ($T_2 = T_1$); mass decreases.
- [x] **Sketches to draw in exams:** (1) $T$-$s$ diagram with the area under the curve representing heat transfer; (2) block diagram of system + surroundings + dead state for availability problems.
- [x] **Common mistakes:** (i) Forgetting the $P_0 \Delta V$ term in availability; (ii) using $T_{system}$ instead of $T_{boundary}$ in entropy balance; (iii) sign errors in $\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{Q}/T_0 - \dot{Q}/T_H$ for heat engines.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** In any KTU problem where you are asked to "show that the process satisfies the Second Law," compute $S_{gen}$ explicitly and verify $S_{gen} \ge 0$. A negative value = **process impossible** = full marks for demonstrating the violation, but only if you state it explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Third Law of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Third Law of Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Third Law of Thermodynamics (Planck's Statement):**
> The entropy of a **perfectly crystalline, pure substance** is **zero** at the temperature of **absolute zero (0 K)**.
>
> $$\lim_{T \to 0\,\text{K}} S = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Nernst Heat Theorem (Nernst–Simon Statement):**
> For any isothermal process involving substances in **internal equilibrium**, the entropy change approaches zero as the temperature approaches absolute zero:
>
> $$\lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S = 0$$

The Third Law provides an **absolute reference point** for entropy — unlike internal energy or enthalpy, which are defined only up to an arbitrary constant. With this law, the entropy of any substance can be computed as an **absolute value** at any state by integrating from 0 K upward.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine entropy as a measure of **"messiness" or disorder** of a system.

- **At room temperature:** A crystal lattice is vibrating vigorously — atoms wiggle, bonds stretch. The system is "messy" → entropy > 0.
- **At absolute zero (0 K):** All atomic motion ceases. A perfectly ordered crystal settles into its **single, unique ground state** — there is **only one** microscopic arrangement consistent with the macroscopic state. There is **no messiness left** → entropy = **exactly zero**.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it like a tidy room:** If only one specific arrangement of objects is allowed (a perfectly ordered crystal), then there is only **one way** to arrange them. Boltzmann's equation $S = k \ln(W)$ gives $S = k \ln(1) = 0$, because $W$ (the number of microstates) equals 1.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = \mathbf{1.380649 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = \mathbf{8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}}$
- **Absolute Zero:** $T = \mathbf{0\ \text{K}} = \mathbf{-273.15\ ^\circ\text{C}}$
- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = \mathbf{6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}}$
- **Entropy Unit (SI):** $\text{J/(mol·K)}$

---

## 1.4 Limitations / Scope of the Law

> [!WARNING]
> The Third Law applies **only** to substances that are:
> 1. In **internal thermodynamic equilibrium** at 0 K.
> 2. Form **perfect crystals** (single, unique ground state).
> 3. Are **chemically pure**.
>
> Substances like **CO**, **NO**, **H₂O (ice)** have residual entropy at 0 K because their crystals are not perfectly ordered — their entropy is **not zero** at 0 K.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The "Why" and "How"

### 2.1.1 Evolution of the Third Law

The Third Law did not emerge from a single experiment. It was the culmination of two major insights:

- **Nernst (1906)** observed that for galvanic cells, the Gibbs energy $G$ and enthalpy $H$ curves become **tangent to each other** as $T \to 0$. This implies their temperature derivatives (entropy) coincide and vanish:
  $$\lim_{T \to 0} \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = \lim_{T \to 0} \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P = 0$$

- **Planck (1911)** extended this to **absolute entropy**, assigning a unique reference value (zero) at 0 K.

- **Simon (1937)** refined it to demand that the substance be in **internal equilibrium**.

### 2.1.2 Logical Structure of the Law

1. **Step 1 — Zero Point:** At $T = 0$ K, a perfect crystal occupies its single lowest quantum state, so $W = 1$ and $S = k_B \ln(1) = 0$.
2. **Step 2 — Behavior of Heat Capacity:** The law forces all heat capacities to approach zero as $T \to 0$:
   $$\lim_{T \to 0} C_P = \lim_{T \to 0} C_V = 0$$
   (Confirmed experimentally for all known substances.)
3. **Step 3 — Zero-Order Coeff. of Thermal Expansion:** The volume coefficient of expansion $\alpha$ also vanishes at 0 K:
   $$\lim_{T \to 0} \alpha = 0$$
4. **Step 4 — Unattainability:** It is **impossible** to reach 0 K in a finite number of thermodynamic operations.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** — these equations appear repeatedly in KTU ESE, MSE, and numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Planck Statement | $S_{0\,\text{K}} = 0$ | Absolute entropy reference |
| 2 | Nernst Heat Theorem | $\lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S = 0$ | Predicts entropy change vanishes |
| 3 | Boltzmann Relation | $S = k_B \ln(W)$ | Links microstates to entropy |
| 4 | Absolute Entropy Integral | $S(T) = \int_{0}^{T} \dfrac{C_P}{T}\, dT$ | Calculates $S$ from 0 K upward |
| 5 | Heat Capacity Limit | $\lim_{T \to 0} C_P = 0$ | Required for integral convergence |
| 6 | Expansion Coeff. Limit | $\lim_{T \to 0} \alpha = 0$ | Volume stabilizes at 0 K |
| 7 | Pressure Coeff. Limit | $\lim_{T \to 0} \beta = 0$ | Pressure stabilizes at 0 K |
| 8 | Helmholtz / Gibbs derivative | $\lim_{T \to 0} \left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V = \lim_{T \to 0} \left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = 0$ | Consequence of Nernst theorem |
| 9 | Debye $T^3$ Law (low-T $C_P$) | $C_P \approx a\, T^{3}$ | Used in absolute entropy integrals |
| 10 | Unattainability (qualitative) | Cannot reach 0 K in finite steps | Guides cryogenic engineering limits |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** Use `\vert` (not the pipe character) when writing absolute values inside formulas in any KTU answer book to avoid markdown rendering issues.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Third Law |
|---|---|
| **Cryogenics** (LNG, LHe storage) | Defines minimum cooling limit for magnetic refrigerators, Joule–Thomson cycles |
| **Materials Science** | Predicts residual entropy in glassy / disordered solids (e.g., polymers) |
| **Chemical Thermodynamics** | Enables computation of absolute equilibrium constants via $K = e^{-\Delta G^\circ / RT}$ |
| **Quantum Computing** | Qubit readout requires operation near 0 K; Third Law sets fundamental limits |
| **Superconductivity Research** | BCS theory depends on phonon behavior at low temperatures governed by Third Law |

---

## 2.4 Statement Equivalence Table

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"State and explain any two equivalent statements of the Third Law."* Memorize the three equivalent forms below.

| Form | Statement | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Planck's Statement** | $S = 0$ for a perfect crystal at 0 K | Absolute entropy reference |
| **Nernst–Simon Statement** | $\Delta S \to 0$ as $T \to 0$ for processes between equilibrium states | Focuses on entropy change |
| **Unattainability Statement** | No finite sequence of operations can cool a system to 0 K | Practical / operational form |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Entropy Change Vanishes at 0 K (Nernst Theorem → Planck Statement)

We begin with the **Maxwell relation** linking Gibbs energy to entropy:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S
$$

For a chemical reaction or any process, the entropy change is:

$$
\Delta S = -\left[\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]
$$

Nernst postulated that **as $T \to 0$, $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$ become tangent**, meaning their slopes coincide:

$$
\lim_{T \to 0} \left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_P = \lim_{T \to 0} \left(\frac{\partial \Delta H}{\partial T}\right)_P
$$

Using $\Delta H = \Delta G + T \Delta S$, differentiate with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \Delta H}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_P + \Delta S + T \left(\frac{\partial \Delta S}{\partial T}\right)_P
$$

Substitute the limit condition (slopes equal at $T=0$):

$$
\lim_{T \to 0} \left[ \Delta S + T \left(\frac{\partial \Delta S}{\partial T}\right)_P \right] = 0
$$

Since both $T \to 0$ and $\Delta S \to 0$ must satisfy the equation **independently of the derivative term**, we deduce:

$$
\lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S = 0
$$

This is the **Nernst Heat Theorem**. By further imposing that entropy itself — not just its change — is zero at 0 K, we obtain **Planck's stronger form**:

$$
\lim_{T \to 0} S = 0
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Absolute Entropy Expression (KTU High-Yield Result)

Starting from the thermodynamic definition of entropy for a single-phase substance:

$$
dS = \frac{C_P}{T}\, dT \quad \text{(at constant } P\text{)}
$$

Integrate from a reference state (0 K, $S=0$) to the state of interest $(T, P)$:

$$
S(T) - S(0) = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{C_P}{T}\, dT
$$

Apply the Third Law $S(0) = 0$:

$$
\boxed{S(T) = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{C_P}{T}\, dT}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For this integral to **converge** at the lower limit, we must have $\lim_{T \to 0} C_P / T < \infty$, which requires $C_P \to 0$ at least as fast as $T$. The **Debye $T^3$ law** ($C_P = a T^3$) confirms this for real solids at low temperature.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style, 7-Mark Standard)

> [!TIP]
> This is the *type* of problem asked in Part B. Master this template — substitute new numbers in the exam.

**Problem:**
A solid obeys the Debye law $C_P = a\, T^{3}$ with $a = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{J/(mol·K}^4\text{)}$ between 0 K and 20 K. Compute the absolute entropy at 20 K.

**Solution (Full Step-by-Step):**

Apply the Third Law integral:

$$
S(T) = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{a\, T^{3}}{T}\, dT = \int_{0}^{T} a\, T^{2}\, dT
$$

Integrate:

$$
S(T) = a \left[ \frac{T^{3}}{3} \right]_{0}^{T} = \frac{a\, T^{3}}{3}
$$

Substitute $a = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ and $T = 20$:

$$
S(20) = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-5}) \times (20)^{3}}{3}
$$

Compute $(20)^3 = 8000$:

$$
S(20) = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-5}) \times 8000}{3} = \frac{0.2}{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{S(20\,\text{K}) \approx 0.0667\ \text{J/(mol·K)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The result is very small, consistent with the Third Law: entropy rises gradually from zero as temperature increases from absolute zero.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — First, Second, Third Law

| Aspect | First Law | Second Law | Third Law |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Core Statement** | Energy is conserved | Entropy of universe increases for irreversible processes | Entropy of perfect crystal is zero at 0 K |
| **Key Quantity** | Internal Energy $U$ | Entropy $S$ | Absolute entropy reference |
| **Defines?** | Energy balance | Direction of processes | Absolute scale of entropy |
| **Reference Point** | Arbitrary for $U$ | Arbitrary for $S$ | $S = 0$ at $T = 0$ K |
| **Domain** | All processes | All natural processes | Pure crystalline solids at equilibrium |
| **KTU Exam Weight** | High | High | **Moderate (Module 2 focus)** |

---

## 3.5 Derivation Sketch: Unattainability of Absolute Zero

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks for a *qualitative* proof. Below is a rigorous 6-line version.

Consider an isentropic process connecting state 1 (warmer) to state 2 (cooler). For any real substance:

$$
\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_P}{T}\, dT = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 \to T_1 > 0
$$

To achieve $T_2 = 0$, the integral must vanish over an infinite temperature drop — requiring **infinite time / infinite operations**. Therefore, **0 K is unattainable in a finite number of steps.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formally (Nernst's theorem corollary):** If $T_2 = 0$ were reachable, then $\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{0} C_P / T\, dT$ would have to be both **zero** (isentropic) and **non-zero** (since $C_P > 0$ for $T > 0$). Contradiction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Logical Hierarchy of the Third Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Third Law of Thermodynamics"] --> B["Planck Statement"]
    A --> C["Nernst Simon Statement"]
    A --> D["Unattainability Statement"]
    A --> E["Consequences"]

    B --> B1["Entropy of perfect crystal at 0 K equals zero"]
    B --> B2["Provides absolute entropy reference"]

    C --> C1["Entropy change vanishes at 0 K"]
    C --> C2["Applies to equilibrium states only"]

    D --> D1["Cannot reach 0 K in finite steps"]
    D --> D2["Sets cryogenic engineering limits"]

    E --> E1["Heat capacity C P tends to zero"]
    E --> E2["Expansion coefficient alpha tends to zero"]
    E --> E3["Debye T cubed law at low T"]
    E --> E4["Absolute entropy calculable from 0 K"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Absolute Entropy Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput["Low T Heat Capacity Data C P of T"] --> nodeDebye["Debye T cubed fit region"]
    nodeDebye --> nodeIntegrate["Integrate C P over T from 0 to T"]
    nodeInput --> nodeIntegrate
    nodeThirdLaw["Third Law: S at 0 K equals 0"] --> nodeIntegrate
    nodeIntegrate --> nodeOutput["Absolute Entropy S at temperature T"]
    nodeOutput --> nodeApp1["Equilibrium constant K calculation"]
    nodeOutput --> nodeApp2["Residual entropy detection"]
    nodeOutput --> nodeApp3["Cryogenic design limits"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Comparison: Perfect Crystal vs Imperfect Crystal at 0 K

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodePerfect["Perfect Crystal at 0 K"] --> nodeW1["Single ground state W equals 1"]
    nodeW1 --> nodeSzero["S equals k ln 1 equals 0"]
    nodeImperfect["Imperfect Crystal e.g. CO NO Ice at 0 K"] --> nodeW2["Multiple ground states W greater than 1"]
    nodeW2 --> nodeSres["S residual greater than zero"]
    nodeSres --> nodeApp["Used to compute residual entropy of glasses and disordered solids"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to draw or explain the Third Law, you may reproduce the block diagram from Section 4.2 in your answer script. Examiners reward structural clarity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State and explain Planck's statement of the Third Law of Thermodynamics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Planck's Statement:**
> The entropy of a perfectly crystalline, pure substance is **zero** at the absolute zero of temperature (0 K).

**Explanation (1 mark):** At 0 K, a perfect crystal occupies a unique ground quantum state. By Boltzmann's relation $S = k_B \ln W$, with only one microstate ($W = 1$), entropy becomes $S = 0$.

**Significance (1 mark):** This provides an **absolute reference** for entropy, enabling calculation of absolute (not just relative) entropy values using $S(T) = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{C_P}{T}\, dT$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why is absolute zero unattainable? Explain with the Nernst Heat Theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unattainability Principle:** It is impossible to reduce the temperature of any system to absolute zero in a finite number of thermodynamic operations.

**Reasoning (2 marks):** According to the Nernst Heat Theorem, $\lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S = 0$. To reach 0 K via an isentropic process, the integral $\int_{T_i}^{0} \frac{C_P}{T}\, dT = 0$ must vanish — which would require an infinite sequence of cooling steps or infinite time. Any finite process leaves $T > 0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** in Part B. **Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.**

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for **absolute entropy** of a solid obeying the Debye $T^3$ law. State clearly where the Third Law is invoked in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solid has low-temperature heat capacity described by:
$$C_P = a T + b T^{3}$$
where $a = 4.2 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{J/(mol·K}^2\text{)}$ and $b = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{J/(mol·K}^4\text{)}$.

Calculate the **absolute entropy at 15 K** using the Third Law. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

##### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition of entropy differential** (1 mark):
$$
dS = \frac{C_P}{T}\, dT \quad \text{at constant } P
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from 0 K reference** (2 marks):
$$
S(T) - S(0) = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{C_P}{T}\, dT
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Third Law** (2 marks):
By Planck's statement, $S(0) = 0$ for a perfect crystal. Thus:
$$
S(T) = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{C_P}{T}\, dT
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Debye law** $C_P = a\, T^3$ **(1 mark)**:
$$
S(T) = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{a\, T^{3}}{T}\, dT = \int_{0}^{T} a\, T^{2}\, dT
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate** (1 mark):
$$
\boxed{S(T) = \frac{a\, T^{3}}{3}}
$$

---

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$$C_P = a T + b T^{3}$$
$$a = 4.2 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{J/(mol·K}^2\text{)}, \quad b = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{J/(mol·K}^4\text{)}, \quad T = 15\ \text{K}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Third Law integral** (1 mark):
$$
S(T) = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{C_P}{T}\, dT = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{a T + b T^{3}}{T}\, dT
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify integrand** (1 mark):
$$
S(T) = \int_{0}^{T} \left(a + b T^{2}\right) dT
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate term-by-term** (2 marks):
$$
S(T) = a \left[ T \right]_{0}^{T} + b \left[ \frac{T^{3}}{3} \right]_{0}^{T} = aT + \frac{b T^{3}}{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values** (1 mark):
$$
S(15) = (4.2 \times 10^{-4})(15) + \frac{(2.0 \times 10^{-5})(15)^{3}}{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate term-by-term** (2 marks):
- First term: $(4.2 \times 10^{-4})(15) = 6.3 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- Second term: $(15)^3 = 3375$, so $\frac{(2.0 \times 10^{-5})(3375)}{3} = \frac{0.0675}{3} = 2.25 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$

**Final result:**
$$
\boxed{S(15\,\text{K}) = 6.3 \times 10^{-3} + 2.25 \times 10^{-2} = 0.0288\ \text{J/(mol·K)}}
$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Adapted]`

**(a)** State the **Nernst Heat Theorem** and the **Unattainability Principle**. Explain how they are mathematically related. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical reaction has $\Delta H^\circ = -50\ \text{kJ/mol}$ and $\Delta S^\circ = -100\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$ at 298 K. Using the **Third Law**, justify whether the standard Gibbs energy $\Delta G^\circ$ can be computed **absolutely** (i.e., without empirical entropy data). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

##### **Part (a) — Statements and Relationship (7 marks)**

**Nernst Heat Theorem (3 marks):**
> For any isothermal process between states in internal equilibrium, the entropy change approaches zero as the temperature approaches absolute zero:
> $$\lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S = 0$$

**Unattainability Principle (2 marks):**
> It is impossible to cool any system to absolute zero in a finite number of thermodynamic operations.

**Mathematical Relationship (2 marks):**
> The Unattainability Principle is a **direct corollary** of the Nernst Theorem. If we attempt an isentropic cooling process from $T_1 > 0$ to $T_2$:
> $$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_P}{T}\, dT = 0$$
> For $T_2 = 0$, the integral demands $\lim_{T \to 0} C_P = 0$ and infinite operations — proving unattainability.

---

##### **Part (b) — Justification (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Recall Gibbs–Helmholtz framework** (2 marks):
$$
\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Third Law enables absolute $\Delta S^\circ$** (3 marks):
> Without the Third Law, $\Delta S^\circ$ would only be known relative to an arbitrary zero. The Third Law assigns $S = 0$ at 0 K for a perfect crystal, allowing absolute measurement of $S^\circ$ via:
> $$S^\circ = \int_{0}^{298} \frac{C_P}{T}\, dT$$
> Thus $\Delta S^\circ$ for the reaction becomes **absolute**, and so does $\Delta G^\circ$.

**Step 3 — Compute** (2 marks):
$$
\Delta G^\circ = -50\,000 - (298)(-100) = -50\,000 + 29\,800 = -20\,200\ \text{J/mol}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta G^\circ \approx -20.2\ \text{kJ/mol}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Yes, $\Delta G^\circ$ can be computed absolutely because the Third Law provides an unambiguous entropy reference at 0 K.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to set $S(0) = 0$** before integrating — examiner deducts **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Nernst Theorem with Planck Statement** — Nernst says $\Delta S \to 0$; Planck says $S \to 0$. Many students write one when asked for the other. Deduct **2 marks** if swapped.
> 3. **Using relative instead of absolute entropy** in chemical equilibrium problems — examiner will not award the "absolute" interpretation mark.
> 4. **Not stating the perfect-crystal assumption** — examiners at KTU specifically look for this qualifier; omitting it loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — always write $\text{J/(mol·K)}$ for entropy values.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Third Law of Thermodynamics**

- ⭐ **Planck's Statement:** $S_{0\,\text{K}} = 0$ for a **perfect, pure, crystalline** substance.
- ⭐ **Nernst–Simon Statement:** $\lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S = 0$ for processes between equilibrium states.
- ⭐ **Unattainability Statement:** 0 K cannot be reached in a **finite** number of steps.
- ⭐ **Boltzmann Justification:** $S = k_B \ln W$, with $W = 1$ at ground state.
- ⭐ **Consequence 1:** $\lim_{T \to 0} C_P = 0$ (required for integral convergence).
- ⭐ **Consequence 2:** $\lim_{T \to 0} \alpha = 0$ and $\lim_{T \to 0} \beta = 0$.
- ⭐ **Absolute Entropy Formula:** $S(T) = \int_{0}^{T} \dfrac{C_P}{T}\, dT$.
- ⭐ **Debye Law (low-T):** $C_P \approx a\, T^{3}$.
- ⭐ **Exception (Residual Entropy):** CO, NO, ice, and glasses have $S_{0\,\text{K}} > 0$ due to disorder.
- ⭐ **KTU Must-Write Qualifier:** Always mention *perfect crystal* and *internal equilibrium*.
- ⭐ **Engineering Significance:** Foundation of cryogenics, superconductivity, absolute equilibrium constants.
- ⭐ **Examiner Trap:** Never claim $S = 0$ for **all** substances at 0 K — only for perfect crystals.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"P-N-U"** → **P**lanck, **N**ernst–Simon, **U**nattainability — the three faces of the Third Law.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Exergy. Air Standard Cycles: Otto and Diesel Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Exergy. Air Standard Cycles: Otto and Diesel Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — EXERGY, OTTO & DIESEL AIR-STANDARD CYCLES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Concept of Exergy (Availability)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXERGY (ϕ)** is defined as the *maximum useful work* that can be obtained as a system is brought reversibly from a given initial state to a state of equilibrium with the surroundings (the **Dead State**). The terms *Exergy*, *Availability*, and *Available Energy* are synonymous in KTU textbooks.

**Dead State (Subscript `0`):**
A system is said to be at the *dead state* when it is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with the environment (same T, P, chemical potential, velocity, and elevation). At this state, the exergy is defined to be **zero** because no further useful work can be extracted.

**Environment Reference:**
The exergy of a system is always measured *relative to* a specified environment:
- $T_0$ = ambient temperature (K)
- $P_0$ = ambient pressure (kPa)
- $C_{p_0}, C_{v_0}$ = specific heats evaluated at $T_0$

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference between Energy and Exergy:**
> - **Energy** is *conserved* (1st Law). It is a quantitative property.
> - **Exergy** is *destroyed* due to irreversibilities (2nd Law). It is a *quality* indicator.
> - Exergy is the *useful* part of energy; the rest is *anergy*.

### 1.2 Closed-System (Non-Flow) Exergy

For a closed system undergoing a process, the non-flow exergy change is:

$$\phi_2 - \phi_1 = (U_2 - U_1) + P_0(V_2 - V_1) - T_0(S_2 - S_1)$$

**On a unit-mass basis:**

$$\phi_2 - \phi_1 = (u_2 - u_1) + P_0(v_2 - v_1) - T_0(s_2 - s_1)$$

### 1.3 Flow Exergy (Stream Exergy)

When mass crosses the boundary of a control volume, the **flow exergy** (also called *availability of a flowing fluid*) is:

$$\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$$

The terms $\frac{V^2}{2}$ and $gz$ are the kinetic and potential exergy contributions, respectively.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy: "A Hot Cup of Tea"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a cup of hot tea at $90^\circ \text{C}$ in a $25^\circ \text{C}$ room.
> - The **energy** inside the tea is high (lots of molecular motion).
> - The **exergy** is the *work-producing potential* of that energy — i.e., how much useful work you could extract if you ran a small heat engine between the tea and the room *reversibly*.
> - As the tea cools, its exergy drops. When it finally reaches $25^\circ \text{C}$ (dead state), its temperature equals the room's — **exergy = 0**, even though the tea still contains thermal *energy*.
> - The energy is never lost, but the exergy (ability to do work) is **destroyed by irreversibility** — it took the "high-grade" energy down to "low-grade" energy.

### 1.5 Air-Standard Cycles — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air-Standard Assumptions (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> 1. The working fluid is **air**, which behaves as an **ideal gas**.
> 2. The **combustion process** is replaced by **heat addition from an external source** ($Q_{in}$).
> 3. The **exhaust process** is replaced by **heat rejection to the surroundings** ($Q_{out}$).
> 4. All processes are **internally reversible**; cycle is analyzed as **closed** (the air returns to its initial state).
> 5. Specific heats $C_p$ and $C_v$ are **constant** (cold-air-standard analysis).

Two principal volumetric cycles are studied:
- **Otto Cycle** — spark-ignition (petrol) engines
- **Diesel Cycle** — compression-ignition (diesel) engines

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Exergy

A system's total exergy can be split into four components:

| Component | Formula | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Internal Exergy** | $(U - U_0) + P_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)$ | Useful work from internal state change |
| **Kinetic Exergy** | $\frac{1}{2}mV^2$ | Useful work from macroscopic velocity |
| **Potential Exergy** | $mgz$ | Useful work from elevation above datum |
| **Chemical Exergy** | $\mu_0 N_i$ (advanced topic) | Useful work from chemical composition difference |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board examinations, the **chemical exergy** is rarely tested unless specifically asked. Focus is on **thermal (internal)**, **kinetic**, and **potential** exergy.

## 2.2 Irreversibility and Gouy-Stodola Theorem

For any actual process:

$$I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$

where $I$ is the **irreversibility** (exergy destroyed) and $S_{gen}$ is the entropy generated. The **Gouy-Stodola Theorem** states that the lost work (exergy destruction) equals $T_0$ times the entropy generation of the universe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Second-Law Efficiency (Exergy Efficiency):**
> $$\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{Exergy recovered}}{\text{Exergy supplied}} = 1 - \frac{I}{\text{Exergy supplied}}$$

## 2.3 The OTTO Cycle (Ideal Cycle for SI Engines)

The Otto cycle consists of **four internally reversible processes**:

| Process | Description | Constant Property | Ideal Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1 → 2** | **Isentropic compression** | $s = \text{const}$ | Adiabatic + Reversible |
| **2 → 3** | **Constant volume heat addition** | $v = \text{const}$ | $Q_{in}$ added at $V_2 = V_3$ |
| **3 → 4** | **Isentropic expansion** | $s = \text{const}$ | Adiabatic + Reversible |
| **4 → 1** | **Constant volume heat rejection** | $v = \text{const}$ | $Q_{out}$ rejected at $V_4 = V_1$ |

**Compression Ratio (r):**
$$r = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{V_4}{V_3}$$

**Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency (Air-Standard):**
$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

**Cut-off Ratio / Pressure Ratio (β):** For Otto, $\beta = \dfrac{P_3}{P_2}$ (peak pressure ratio).

## 2.4 The DIESEL Cycle (Ideal Cycle for CI Engines)

| Process | Description | Constant Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1 → 2** | Isentropic compression | $s = \text{const}$ |
| **2 → 3** | **Constant pressure heat addition** | $P = \text{const}$ |
| **3 → 4** | Isentropic expansion | $s = \text{const}$ |
| **4 → 1** | Constant volume heat rejection | $v = \text{const}$ |

**Cut-off Ratio ($r_c$):**
$$r_c = \frac{V_3}{V_2}$$

**Diesel Cycle Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(r_c - 1)}$$

**Compression Ratio (r):** $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the following high-yield table — these equations are the most frequently asked in KTU University Exams and model tests.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Closed-system exergy change | $\phi_2 - \phi_1 = (U_2-U_1) + P_0(V_2-V_1) - T_0(S_2-S_1)$ | kJ |
| 2 | Flow exergy (specific) | $\psi = (h-h_0) - T_0(s-s_0) + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$ | kJ/kg |
| 3 | Irreversibility | $I = T_0 S_{gen}$ | kJ |
| 4 | Otto efficiency | $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | — |
| 5 | Diesel efficiency | $\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_c^{\gamma}-1}{\gamma(r_c-1)}$ | — |
| 6 | Isentropic relation (ideal gas) | $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$ | — |
| 7 | Isentropic relation (ideal gas) | $TP^{\frac{1-\gamma}{\gamma}} = \text{const}$ | — |
| 8 | Specific heat ratio (air) | $\gamma = 1.4$ | — |
| 9 | Gas constant for air | $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ | kJ/kg·K |
| 10 | Mean Effective Pressure (Otto) | $P_m = \dfrac{P_1 \, r \, (r^{\gamma-1}-1)}{(\gamma-1)(r-1)} \left[ \dfrac{r^{\gamma}-1}{r-1} \right]$ | kPa |

> **Engineering Utility (Real-World):**
> - **Exergy analysis** is used in power plant design (combined-cycle, cogeneration), refrigeration, and process industries to pinpoint *where* energy quality is destroyed.
> - **Otto & Diesel cycle efficiencies** guide the design of automobile engines — modern petrol engines aim for higher $r$ (efficiency), while diesel engines optimize the **trade-off** between $r$ and $r_c$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-System Non-Flow Exergy

Consider a closed system undergoing a process from State 1 to State 2. It exchanges heat $Q$ with a reservoir at $T_0$ and produces work $W$.

**Step 1 — Maximum Useful Work Concept**
The *maximum* useful work is delivered by a **reversible** process between the system and the dead state.

**Step 2 — Apply the First Law (for a closed system)**

$$Q - W = U_2 - U_1$$

Solving for work:

$$W = Q - (U_2 - U_1)$$

**Step 3 — Express Q in terms of Entropy Change (reversible)**
For the reversible heat exchange with the environment at $T_0$:

$$Q_{rev} = T_0 (S_2 - S_1)$$

But the environment itself does volume work $P_0(V_2 - V_1)$ as the system changes volume. To get the *useful* work, we subtract this:

$$W_{max,useful} = (U_2 - U_1) + P_0(V_2 - V_1) - T_0(S_2 - S_1)$$

**Step 4 — Per Unit Mass**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_2 - \phi_1 &= (u_2 - u_1) + P_0(v_2 - v_1) - T_0(s_2 - s_1) \\
&= (u_2 - u_1) - T_0(s_2 - s_1) + P_0(v_2 - v_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

> The term $P_0(v_2-v_1)$ accounts for the work the environment performs on the system during reversible expansion/compression — it is the boundary work *by* the surroundings.

**Step 5 — Introduce Enthalpy (for Flow Exergy)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi &= (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0) \\
    &= (u + Pv) - (u_0 + P_0 v_0) - T_0(s - s_0) \\
    &= (u - u_0) + P v - P_0 v_0 - T_0(s - s_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a closed stationary tank (no flow), $P = P_0$ and $v = v_0$ at dead state, simplifying back to the closed-system exergy.

## 3.2 Derivation: Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency

**Step 1 — Apply 1st Law to the cycle** (steady cyclic, so $\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$):

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

**Step 2 — Heat addition at constant volume (Process 2 → 3):**

$$Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 3 — Heat rejection at constant volume (Process 4 → 1):**

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 4 — Isentropic relations for processes 1→2 and 3→4:**

For process 1→2 (isentropic compression):
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$

For process 3→4 (isentropic expansion, $V_3 = V_4/r$ ratio is same):
$$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{Otto} &= 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} \\
&= 1 - \frac{\frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} - T_1}{T_3 - T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{T_1\left(\frac{T_3}{T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}} - 1\right)}{T_1\left(\frac{T_3}{T_1} - r^{\gamma-1}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the fact that for an Otto cycle, $\dfrac{T_3}{T_1} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} \cdot \dfrac{T_3}{T_2}$ and canceling yields:

$$\boxed{\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Diesel Cycle Thermal Efficiency

**Step 1 — Heat addition at constant pressure (Process 2 → 3):**

$$Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 2 — Heat rejection at constant volume (Process 4 → 1):**

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 3 — Isentropic relations:**

- Process 1→2: $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$
- Process 2→3 (constant $P$): $\dfrac{V_3}{V_2} = \dfrac{T_3}{T_2} = r_c$ ⟹ $T_3 = r_c \cdot T_2$
- Process 3→4 (isentropic, $V_4/V_3 = r/r_c$):
$$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma-1} = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{Diesel} &= 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{C_v(T_4 - T_1)}{C_p(T_3 - T_2)} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $T_4 = T_3 (r_c/r)^{\gamma-1} = r_c \cdot T_2 \cdot (r_c/r)^{\gamma-1} = T_1 r^{\gamma-1} \cdot r_c \cdot (r_c/r)^{\gamma-1}$:

$$T_4 = T_1 \cdot r_c^{\gamma}$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{Diesel} &= 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_1 r_c^{\gamma} - T_1}{r_c \cdot T_1 r^{\gamma-1} - T_1 r^{\gamma-1}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (r_c - 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (r_c - 1)}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Otto vs. Diesel at same $r$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A classic 14-mark KTU question: *"Compare Otto and Diesel cycles for the same compression ratio and heat input."* The following matrix is the expected answer.

| Property | Otto Cycle | Diesel Cycle |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heat addition | At **constant volume** | At **constant pressure** |
| Fuel injection | Spark-ignition | Compression-ignition |
| Compression ratio | Lower (8–12) | Higher (14–25) |
| Efficiency at same $r$ | **Higher** | Lower |
| Peak temperature | Higher | Lower |
| Cut-off ratio | Not defined ($r_c = 1$) | $r_c > 1$ |
| $\eta$ formula | $1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$ | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_c^{\gamma}-1}{\gamma(r_c-1)}$ |
| Real engine analog | Petrol / SI engine | Diesel / CI engine |

**Limiting Case:** As $r_c \to 1$, the Diesel cycle degenerates to the Otto cycle:
$$\lim_{r_c \to 1} \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(r_c - 1)} = 1 \quad \text{(by L'Hôpital's rule)}$$

This confirms **internal consistency** of the two formulas.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Exergy Concept — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how exergy flows through a generic thermal system, identifying where destruction occurs:

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Energy Source] --> convA[Conversion Device]
    suppA[Exergy Supply] --> convA
    convA --> outA[Useful Work Output]
    convA --> wastedA[Exergy Destroyed]
    wastedA --> ambA[Environment Dead State]
    outA --> sinkA[Exergy Recovered]
    sinkA --> ambA
    lostA[Anergy to Environment] --> ambA
    ambA -.-> ctxA[Reference Environment T0 P0]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Exergy enters with the energy source.
- A fraction is converted to **useful work** (exergy recovered).
- A fraction is **destroyed** by irreversibility (friction, mixing, unrestrained expansion).
- All "low-grade" energy and the destroyed exergy end up in the **environment at the dead state**.

## 4.2 Otto Cycle — P-V and T-S Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    state1[State 1: T1 P1 V1] --> proc12[Process 1-2 Isentropic Compression]
    proc12 --> state2[State 2: T2 P2 V2 Higher T Higher P]
    state2 --> proc23[Process 2-3 Constant Volume Heat Addition]
    proc23 --> state3[State 3: T3 P3 V3 Highest T Highest P]
    state3 --> proc34[Process 3-4 Isentropic Expansion]
    proc34 --> state4[State 4: T4 P4 V4 Lower T Lower P]
    state4 --> proc41[Process 4-1 Constant Volume Heat Rejection]
    proc41 --> state1
```

**Key Geometric Insights on P-V diagram:**
- Processes 1→2 and 3→4 are steeper (isentropic, $\gamma > 1$).
- Processes 2→3 and 4→1 are vertical lines (constant volume).
- The enclosed area = **net work** output of the cycle.

## 4.3 Diesel Cycle — P-V Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    ds1[State 1: T1 P1 V1] --> dproc12[Process 1-2 Isentropic Compression]
    dproc12 --> ds2[State 2: T2 P2 V2]
    ds2 --> dproc23[Process 2-3 Constant Pressure Heat Addition]
    dproc23 --> ds3[State 3: T3 P3 V3 Maximum V]
    ds3 --> dproc34[Process 3-4 Isentropic Expansion]
    dproc34 --> ds4[State 4: T4 P4 V4]
    ds4 --> dproc41[Process 4-1 Constant Volume Heat Rejection]
    dproc41 --> ds1
```

**Key Geometric Insights on P-V diagram:**
- Process 2→3 is a **horizontal line** (constant pressure, length = $V_3 - V_2$).
- The **cut-off ratio** $r_c = V_3/V_2$ is geometrically visible as the horizontal length ratio.
- Compared to Otto, the expansion stroke of Diesel is *longer* (V₃ > V₂ means more expansion work).

## 4.4 Energy vs. Exergy Degradation Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    fuelA[Fuel Chemical Energy] --> |100 percent| combA[Combustion High T Gases]
    combA --> |Exergy drops| mechA[Mechanical Shaft Work]
    mechA --> |Exergy drops| elecA[Electrical Output]
    elecA --> |Exergy drops| usefulA[Useful End Use]
    combA -.-> |Heat lost to coolant| deadA[Anergy to Environment]
    mechA -.-> |Friction losses| deadA
    elecA -.-> |Resistance heating| deadA
```

> This illustrates the **degradation cascade**: at every conversion step, the exergy (work potential) is reduced, even when the *energy* is conserved.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper]
> **Define the term 'Exergy' and explain the concept of 'Dead State'.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - (1 mark) **Exergy** is defined as the *maximum useful work* that can be extracted as a system is brought reversibly from a given state to the *dead state* — a state of complete thermodynamic equilibrium with the environment.
> - (1 mark) The **Dead State** is characterized by the system being at the same temperature ($T_0$), pressure ($P_0$), chemical potential, velocity, and elevation as the environment. At this state, exergy is **zero** by definition.
> - (1 mark) **Key Example:** A block of hot metal cooled to ambient $T_0$ has *no exergy* remaining, even though it still contains thermal *energy*.
> - **Keywords for full marks:** reversible process, equilibrium, environment reference, $T_0$, $P_0$.

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2]
> **List the four processes of an ideal Otto cycle and identify the heat addition/rejection processes.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. Process **1→2**: Isentropic compression (reversible adiabatic).
> 2. Process **2→3**: **Constant volume heat addition** ($Q_{in}$ supplied).
> 3. Process **3→4**: Isentropic expansion (reversible adiabatic).
> 4. Process **4→1**: **Constant volume heat rejection** ($Q_{out}$ rejected).
> - (Bonus ½ mark) Used as ideal model for **spark-ignition (petrol) engines** with compression ratio $r = V_1/V_2$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Air-Standard Otto Cycle

> [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the air-standard efficiency of the Otto cycle in terms of the compression ratio $r$ and specific heat ratio $\gamma$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An ideal Otto cycle has a compression ratio of 8 and operates with air at the beginning of compression at $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$ and $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$. The maximum temperature in the cycle is $T_3 = 2000 \text{ K}$. Calculate the thermal efficiency, the heat added, the heat rejected, and the mean effective pressure. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

The Otto cycle efficiency is derived from $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$, where:

- $Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$
- $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$

Using the isentropic relations $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$ and $T_4 = T_3 / r^{\gamma-1}$, and substituting (full step-by-step algebra shown in **Section 3.2** above), we obtain:

$$\boxed{\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $r = 8$, $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $T_3 = 2000 \text{ K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.

**Step 1 — Find $T_2$:**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 300 \times 8^{0.4} = 300 \times 2.2974 = 689.2 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Find $T_4$:**

$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{2000}{2.2974} = 870.5 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.2974} = 0.5647 = 56.47\%$$

**Step 4 — Heat Added:**

$$Q_{in} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.718 \times (2000 - 689.2) = 0.718 \times 1310.8 = 941.15 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Heat Rejected:**

$$Q_{out} = C_v (T_4 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (870.5 - 300) = 0.718 \times 570.5 = 409.62 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6 — Net Work:**

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = 941.15 - 409.62 = 531.53 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7 — Mean Effective Pressure:**

First, $V_1 = \dfrac{RT_1}{P_1} = \dfrac{0.287 \times 300}{100} = 0.861 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$, and $V_2 = V_1/r = 0.1076 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$.

$$P_m = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{531.53}{0.861 - 0.1076} = \frac{531.53}{0.7534} = 705.5 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\eta = 56.47\%$
- $Q_{in} = 941.15 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $Q_{out} = 409.62 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $P_m = 705.5 \text{ kPa}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative — Diesel Cycle & Exergy**

> [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the four processes of an ideal Diesel cycle on a P-V diagram. Derive an expression for its air-standard efficiency. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Air enters the compressor of a gas turbine plant at $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$. It is compressed to $P_2 = 1.0 \text{ MPa}$ isentropically. The air is then heated at constant pressure to $T_3 = 1000 \text{ K}$. Determine the **change in specific flow exergy** of the air as it passes through the compressor and the heater. Take $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $T_0 = 300 \text{ K}$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Sketch + Derivation (7 marks)**

- (1 mark) **Sketch of P-V diagram** — Refer to Section 4.3 above for the Mermaid schematic; on P-V coordinates show:
  - Curve 1→2: steep isentropic compression
  - Line 2→3: horizontal constant-pressure heat addition (length = $V_3 - V_2$)
  - Curve 3→4: isentropic expansion to $V_4 = V_1$
  - Line 4→1: vertical constant-volume heat rejection
- (6 marks) **Derivation** — Follow Section 3.3 step-by-step derivation above. Final result:

$$\boxed{\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (r_c - 1)}}$$

**Part (b) — Exergy Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $P_2 = 1000 \text{ kPa}$ (10×), $T_3 = 1000 \text{ K}$, $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $T_0 = 300 \text{ K}$.

**Step 1 — Find $T_2$ (isentropic compression):**

$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = 300 \times 10^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \times 10^{0.2857}$$

$$10^{0.2857} = 1.9307 \implies T_2 = 300 \times 1.9307 = 579.2 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Change in Flow Exergy Across Compressor (1→2):**

Flow exergy: $\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$

For ideal gas, $h - h_0 = C_p(T - T_0)$ and $s - s_0 = C_p \ln(T/T_0) - R \ln(P/P_0)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\psi_{comp} &= C_p(T_2 - T_0) - T_0\left[C_p \ln\frac{T_2}{T_0} - R \ln\frac{P_2}{P_0}\right] \\
&= 1.005 \times (579.2 - 300) - 300 \times \left[1.005 \ln\frac{579.2}{300} - 0.287 \ln\frac{1000}{100}\right] \\
&= 280.59 - 300 \times [1.005 \times 0.6575 - 0.287 \times 2.3026] \\
&= 280.59 - 300 \times [0.6608 - 0.6608] \\
&= 280.59 - 0 \\
&= 280.59 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Note: For an isentropic ideal-gas process with $T_0 = T_1$, the isentropic exergy change simplifies to $C_p(T_2 - T_1)$ — a useful KTU shortcut.)*

**Step 3 — Change in Flow Exergy Across Heater (2→3):**

Constant pressure: $P_3 = P_2 = 1000 \text{ kPa}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\psi_{heater} &= C_p(T_3 - T_2) - T_0\left[C_p \ln\frac{T_3}{T_2} - R \ln\frac{P_3}{P_2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $P_3 = P_2$, the $R \ln(P_3/P_2)$ term is **zero**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\psi_{heater} &= C_p(T_3 - T_2) - T_0 C_p \ln\frac{T_3}{T_2} \\
&= 1.005 \times (1000 - 579.2) - 300 \times 1.005 \times \ln\frac{1000}{579.2} \\
&= 423.11 - 301.5 \times 0.5461 \\
&= 423.11 - 164.62 \\
&= 258.49 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\Delta\psi_{compressor} = 280.59 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $\Delta\psi_{heater} = 258.49 \text{ kJ/kg}$

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that cost marks in Exergy & Cycle problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use absolute temperatures** — Always convert °C to K before any $\ln$ or power calculation. *Marks lost per slip: 1–2.*
> 2. **Mixing up dead-state pressure and system pressure** — $P_0$ is the *environment* pressure; it is *not* necessarily $P_1$ of the cycle. For an isolated process, set $P_0 = P_1$ only if explicitly stated.
> 3. **Wrong sign in exergy change equation** — Students often write $\phi_2 - \phi_1 = (U_2 - U_1) - P_0(V_2-V_1) - T_0(S_2-S_1)$ (wrong sign on $P_0$ term). The correct sign is **+** because the environment *does* work on the system during expansion.
> 4. **Forgetting the cut-off ratio $r_c$ in Diesel cycle** — A common error is to apply Otto's formula to a Diesel cycle. Always check whether heat is added at constant $V$ (Otto) or constant $P$ (Diesel).
> 5. **Using $C_p$ instead of $C_v$ (or vice versa) for constant-volume processes** — Use $C_v$ for Otto's $Q_{in}/Q_{out}$, but $C_p$ for Diesel's $Q_{in}$. *A 1-mark deduction is typical.*
> 6. **Skipping the P-V or T-S diagram** — In 7-mark derivation questions, a **neat labelled diagram is mandatory** (1 mark reserved by KTU examiners).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this section for last-minute exam prep):**

- **Exergy is NOT energy.** Exergy measures *work potential*; energy is conserved, exergy is destroyed.
- The **Dead State** is the unique state where exergy = 0, defined by $T_0, P_0, V_0, S_0$ equal to the environment.
- **Closed-system exergy:** $\phi_2 - \phi_1 = (U_2-U_1) + P_0(V_2-V_1) - T_0(S_2-S_1)$
- **Flow exergy:** $\psi = (h-h_0) - T_0(s-s_0) + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$
- **Irreversibility** $I = T_0 S_{gen}$ — a *positive* quantity in every real process.
- **Otto cycle** = constant-volume heat addition; efficiency depends *only* on $r$ and $\gamma$.
- **Diesel cycle** = constant-pressure heat addition; efficiency depends on $r$, $r_c$, and $\gamma$.
- **Otto vs Diesel at same $r$:** Otto is *more efficient* because constant-$V$ heat addition peaks the cycle temperature.
- **Cut-off ratio** $r_c = V_3/V_2$: as $r_c \to 1$, Diesel $\to$ Otto.
- **Air-standard assumptions:** ideal gas, constant $C_p, C_v$, closed cycle, replacement of combustion by $Q_{in}$.
- For air, always use: $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$.
- **MNEMONIC for Otto processes:** **"S V S V"** — *S*ientropic, *V*olume const, *S*ientropic, *V*olume const.
- **MNEMONIC for Diesel processes:** **"S P S V"** — *S*ientropic, *P*ressure const, *S*ientropic, *V*olume const.
- Remember the **Gouy-Stodola theorem**: lost work = $T_0$ × entropy generation — this appears in 3-mark short questions frequently.
- For **isentropic ideal-gas processes** with $T_0 = T_{initial}$, exergy change simplifies to $C_p(T_{final} - T_{initial})$ — a powerful shortcut.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dual Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — DUAL CYCLE (LIMITED PRESSURE / MIXED CYCLE)

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dual Cycle** (also called the **Limited Pressure Cycle** or **Mixed Cycle** or **Sabathe Cycle**) is an idealized thermodynamic air-standard cycle that combines the constant-volume heat addition of the **Otto cycle** with the constant-pressure heat addition of the **Diesel cycle**. It serves as a more realistic model for modern high-speed diesel engines and dual-fuel engines, where combustion occurs in two distinct phases — first rapidly (near-constant volume) and then at a controlled rate (near-constant pressure).

**Standard State Points of the Dual Cycle:**
| Point | State Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **1** | Beginning of compression (TDC after exhaust) |
| **2** | End of isentropic compression (just before combustion) |
| **3** | End of constant-volume heat addition |
| **4** | End of constant-pressure heat addition |
| **5** | End of isentropic expansion (just before exhaust) |

The cycle consists of **five processes** (excluding the regenerative process sometimes considered):
- **1 → 2**: Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression
- **2 → 3**: Heat addition at **constant volume**
- **3 → 4**: Heat addition at **constant pressure**
- **4 → 5**: Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) expansion
- **5 → 1**: Heat rejection at **constant volume**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Two-Stage Chef's Oven":**
> Imagine a chef preparing bread in a high-power commercial oven. First, the chef blasts the oven at maximum heat (like instant high temperature, ≈ constant-volume heating — fuel burns rapidly). Then, to avoid burning the crust, the chef lowers the heat but keeps the temperature steady (≈ constant-pressure heating — fuel burns gradually while piston moves out). This **two-stage heating** protects the engine from excessive peak pressures (which would require a heavier, more expensive engine block) while still extracting maximum energy from the fuel.
>
> In a **Diesel engine** at high speeds, fuel injection takes a finite amount of time. Initially, the fuel ignites almost instantaneously (a sharp pressure rise ≈ constant volume), and the remainder burns as the piston descends (≈ constant pressure). The Dual Cycle mathematically models exactly this behavior.

### Key Performance Parameters (Governing Ratios)

| Symbol | Parameter | Definition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $r$ | **Compression Ratio** | $V_1 / V_2$ (ratio of maximum to minimum volume) |
| $r_c$ | **Cut-off Ratio** | $V_4 / V_3$ (ratio of volumes at start and end of constant-pressure heat addition) |
| $r_p$ | **Pressure Ratio (β)** | $P_3 / P_2$ (ratio of pressures across constant-volume heat addition) |
| $\gamma$ | **Isentropic Index** | $C_p / C_v$ for air ($\approx \mathbf{1.4}$) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why three ratios?** The Dual Cycle is more flexible than Otto or Diesel because combustion is split. Each ratio independently controls one aspect: $r$ governs compression, $r_p$ controls the "Otto-like" fraction, and $r_c$ controls the "Diesel-like" fraction. This is why the Dual Cycle can model real engines more faithfully.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process-by-Process Theoretical Breakdown

### Process 1 → 2 : Isentropic Compression
- The piston compresses the air-fuel mixture from Bottom Dead Centre (BDC) to Top Dead Centre (TDC) without any heat transfer (Q = 0).
- Process is **reversible and adiabatic**, hence isentropic.
- Both pressure and temperature rise sharply.

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma} = r^{\gamma}
$$

**Why?** Adiabatic compression concentrates the gas's internal energy into a smaller volume, raising temperature proportionally to volume reduction raised to the $\gamma - 1$ power.

### Process 2 → 3 : Constant-Volume Heat Addition
- A fraction of fuel is burned **almost instantaneously** at TDC.
- Volume is held constant (V₂ = V₃).
- Pressure rises from P₂ to P₃ by the **pressure ratio** $r_p$.

$$
\frac{P_3}{P_2} = r_p
$$

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_2} = \frac{P_3}{P_2} = r_p
$$

**Why?** At constant V, the ideal gas law gives $P/T =$ constant, hence $T_3/T_2 = P_3/P_2 = r_p$.

### Process 3 → 4 : Constant-Pressure Heat Addition
- The remaining fuel is injected and burns at a controlled rate.
- Pressure remains constant (P₃ = P₄), but volume expands from V₃ to V₄.
- Volume increases by the **cut-off ratio** $r_c$.

$$
\frac{V_4}{V_3} = r_c
$$

$$
\frac{T_4}{T_3} = \frac{V_4}{V_3} = r_c
$$

**Why?** At constant P, the ideal gas law gives $V/T =$ constant, hence $T_4/T_3 = V_4/V_3 = r_c$.

### Process 4 → 5 : Isentropic Expansion
- The hot, high-pressure gases expand doing work on the piston (power stroke).
- Process is isentropic.
- Volume expands from V₄ back to V₅ = V₁.

$$
\frac{T_5}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_5}\right)^{\gamma - 1}
$$

Since $V_5 = V_1$ and $V_4 = r_c \cdot V_3 = r_c \cdot V_2$, and $V_2 = V_1 / r$:

$$
\frac{V_4}{V_5} = \frac{r_c \cdot V_2}{V_1} = \frac{r_c}{r}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{T_5}{T_4} = \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1}
$$

### Process 5 → 1 : Constant-Volume Heat Rejection
- Exhaust valve opens, gases reject heat to surroundings at constant volume V₅ = V₁.
- Temperature drops from T₅ back to T₁.
- Heat rejected per unit mass: $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_5 - T_1)$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T_2$ | $T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$ | K |
| 2 | $P_2$ | $P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma}$ | kPa |
| 3 | $T_3$ | $T_2 \cdot r_p$ | K |
| 4 | $T_4$ | $T_3 \cdot r_c$ | K |
| 5 | $T_5$ | $T_4 \cdot (r_c / r)^{\gamma-1}$ | K |
| 6 | Heat Supplied $Q_{in}$ | $C_v (T_3 - T_2) + C_p (T_4 - T_3)$ | kJ/kg |
| 7 | Heat Rejected $Q_{out}$ | $C_v (T_5 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg |
| 8 | Net Work $W_{net}$ | $Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ | kJ/kg |
| 9 | Thermal Efficiency $\eta_{th}$ | $1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_{th}$ (expanded) | $1 - \dfrac{1}{\gamma \, r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_p \, r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma \, r_p (r_c - 1)}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) | $W_{net} / (V_1 - V_2)$ | kPa |
| 12 | Air-standard Efficiency | Same as Otto if $r_c = 1$ ; same as Diesel if $r_p = 1$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for Special Cases:**
> - If $r_p = 1$ (no constant-V heat addition) → Dual Cycle **reduces to Diesel Cycle**.
> - If $r_c = 1$ (no constant-P heat addition) → Dual Cycle **reduces to Otto Cycle**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Dual Cycle is the most practical idealization for:
- **High-speed Diesel engines** (commercial vehicles, marine propulsion)
- **Dual-fuel engines** (gas + diesel pilot)
- **HCCI (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition)** research engines
- **Gas turbine modeling** (where combustion occurs at roughly constant pressure but is approximated as two-stage)

Engineers use the Dual Cycle to predict peak cylinder pressure (which determines engine block strength requirements), thermal efficiency (which determines fuel economy), and MEP (which determines engine specific output).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Air-Standard Thermal Efficiency of the Dual Cycle

**Starting Principle:** For a cycle, by the First Law of Thermodynamics:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}
$$

**Step 1:** Identify the heat transfer processes.

Heat is supplied during **Process 2-3 (constant V)** and **Process 3-4 (constant P)**:

$$
Q_{in} = Q_{2-3} + Q_{3-4} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2) + m C_p (T_4 - T_3)
$$

Heat is rejected during **Process 5-1 (constant V)**:

$$
Q_{out} = Q_{5-1} = m C_v (T_5 - T_1)
$$

**Step 2:** Express all temperatures in terms of $T_1$, $r$, $r_p$, $r_c$, and $\gamma$.

From the isentropic relations derived earlier:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

$$
T_3 = T_2 \, r_p = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} \, r_p
$$

$$
T_4 = T_3 \, r_c = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} \, r_p \, r_c
$$

$$
T_5 = T_4 \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_1 \, r_p \, r_c^{\gamma}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Algebraic Verification for T₅:**
> $T_5 = T_4 \cdot (r_c/r)^{\gamma-1}$
> $\quad = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \cdot r_p \cdot r_c \cdot r_c^{\gamma-1} \cdot r^{-(\gamma-1)}$
> $\quad = T_1 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c \cdot r_c^{\gamma-1} = T_1 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma}$

**Step 3:** Substitute into $Q_{in}$.

$$
Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2) + m C_p (T_4 - T_3)
$$

Factor out $m C_v T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$ from the first term and $m C_p T_1 r^{\gamma-1} r_p$ from the second:

$$
Q_{in} = m C_v T_1 r^{\gamma - 1} \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \, r_p (r_c - 1) \right]
$$

> We used the fact that $C_p = \gamma C_v$.

**Step 4:** Substitute into $Q_{out}$.

$$
Q_{out} = m C_v (T_5 - T_1) = m C_v T_1 (r_p \, r_c^{\gamma} - 1)
$$

**Step 5:** Form the ratio $Q_{out}/Q_{in}$.

$$
\frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{m C_v T_1 (r_p \, r_c^{\gamma} - 1)}{m C_v T_1 r^{\gamma - 1} \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \, r_p (r_c - 1) \right]}
$$

The $m$, $C_v$, and $T_1$ cancel cleanly:

$$
\frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{r_p \, r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r^{\gamma - 1} \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \, r_p (r_c - 1) \right]}
$$

**Step 6:** Write the final efficiency expression.

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{r_p \, r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma \, r_p (r_c - 1)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th,\,Dual} = 1 - \frac{r_p \, r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r^{\gamma - 1} \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \, r_p (r_c - 1) \right]}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Dual vs. Otto vs. Diesel)

| Feature | Otto Cycle | Diesel Cycle | Dual Cycle |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Heat Addition | Constant V only | Constant P only | **Constant V + Constant P** |
| Governing Ratios | $r$ | $r$, $r_c$ | $r$, $r_p$, $r_c$ |
| Peak Pressure (for given r) | Moderate | Low | **Highest** |
| Peak Temperature | High | Moderate | **Highest** |
| Efficiency at low r | Lower | Higher | Intermediate |
| Efficiency at high r | Higher | Lower | **Highest** |
| Best Suited For | SI Engines | Slow CI Engines | **High-Speed CI / Dual-Fuel** |
| Formula reduces to Dual if | $r_c = 1$ | $r_p = 1$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Trend (KTU favourite question):**
> For a **fixed compression ratio $r$**, increasing the **pressure ratio $r_p$** (i.e., shifting heat addition more toward constant volume) **increases** thermal efficiency. This is why modern high-speed diesels aim to make combustion as rapid as possible at TDC, despite higher peak pressures.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** An air-standard Dual Cycle has a compression ratio $r = 18$, pressure ratio $r_p = 1.5$, and cut-off ratio $r_c = 1.6$. The temperature at the beginning of compression is $T_1 = 320$ K. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K. Calculate:
> (a) The temperatures at all five state points.
> (b) The net work done per kg of air.
> (c) The thermal efficiency.
> (d) The mean effective pressure (MEP) if $V_1 = 0.004$ m³/kg.

**Solution:**

**(a) Temperatures:**

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 320 \cdot (18)^{0.4}
$$

$$
18^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 18} = e^{0.4 \times 2.8904} = e^{1.1562} \approx 3.178
$$

$$
T_2 = 320 \times 3.178 \approx 1016.96 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_3 = T_2 \cdot r_p = 1016.96 \times 1.5 \approx 1525.44 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_c = 1525.44 \times 1.6 \approx 2440.70 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_5 = T_4 \cdot \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1} = 2440.70 \times \left(\frac{1.6}{18}\right)^{0.4}
$$

$$
(0.0889)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln(0.0889)} = e^{0.4 \times (-2.420)} = e^{-0.968} \approx 0.380
$$

$$
T_5 = 2440.70 \times 0.380 \approx 927.47 \text{ K}
$$

**Summary:** $T_1 = 320$ K, $T_2 \approx 1017$ K, $T_3 \approx 1525$ K, $T_4 \approx 2441$ K, $T_5 \approx 927$ K.

**(b) Net Work:**

$$
Q_{in} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) + C_p (T_4 - T_3)
$$

With $C_p = \gamma C_v = 1.4 \times 0.718 = 1.0052$ kJ/kg·K:

$$
Q_{2\text{-}3} = 0.718 \times (1525.44 - 1016.96) = 0.718 \times 508.48 = 365.09 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{3\text{-}4} = 1.0052 \times (2440.70 - 1525.44) = 1.0052 \times 915.26 = 920.02 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 365.09 + 920.02 = 1285.11 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{out} = C_v (T_5 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (927.47 - 320) = 0.718 \times 607.47 = 436.16 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = 1285.11 - 436.16 = 848.95 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**(c) Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{848.95}{1285.11} \approx 0.6606 \text{ or } 66.06\%
$$

Cross-check using the master formula:

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{r_p r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r^{\gamma-1}[(r_p-1) + \gamma r_p(r_c-1)]}
$$

$$
r_c^{\gamma} = 1.6^{1.4} = e^{1.4 \ln 1.6} = e^{1.4 \times 0.4700} = e^{0.658} \approx 1.931
$$

$$
r_p r_c^{\gamma} - 1 = 1.5 \times 1.931 - 1 = 2.897 - 1 = 1.897
$$

$$
(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1) = 0.5 + 1.4 \times 1.5 \times 0.6 = 0.5 + 1.26 = 1.76
$$

$$
r^{\gamma-1} = 3.178
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1.897}{3.178 \times 1.76} = 1 - \frac{1.897}{5.593} = 1 - 0.3391 = 0.6609 \approx 66.09\%
$$

> The two methods agree (small differences due to rounding), confirming correctness. ✓

**(d) Mean Effective Pressure:**

$$
MEP = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 (1 - 1/r)}
$$

$$
V_1 - V_2 = 0.004 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{18}\right) = 0.004 \times 0.9444 = 0.003778 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
MEP = \frac{848.95 \text{ kJ/kg}}{0.003778 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}} = \frac{848.95 \times 10^3 \text{ J/kg}}{0.003778 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}
$$

$$
MEP = 224,711 \text{ Pa} \approx 224.7 \text{ kPa} \approx 2.247 \text{ bar}
$$
