# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the Dual Cycle. Why is it also called a "limited pressure cycle"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Dual Cycle is an air-standard cycle consisting of two heat addition processes — one at constant volume and one at constant pressure — sandwiched between isentropic compression and expansion, with heat rejection at constant volume. It is called a **"limited pressure cycle"** because, compared to the Otto cycle, the peak (maximum) cylinder pressure is limited by shifting part of the heat addition to a constant-pressure process. In a pure constant-volume cycle, all the heat is added at TDC, causing an extremely high peak pressure; the Dual Cycle deliberately limits this peak by allowing some heat to be added at constant pressure, which is more representative of high-speed diesel engine combustion where fuel injection takes a finite duration. **(3 marks)**

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: State the air-standard efficiency expression for the Dual Cycle and explain the role of the pressure ratio.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{r_p \, r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r^{\gamma - 1} \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \, r_p (r_c - 1) \right]}
$$

**Role of pressure ratio $r_p$:** It represents the magnitude of the constant-volume heat addition phase. As $r_p$ increases, more heat is added at constant volume, raising peak temperature and hence thermal efficiency. If $r_p = 1$, the cycle degenerates into the Diesel cycle (only constant-pressure heat addition). Higher $r_p$ therefore enables higher efficiency but at the cost of higher peak cylinder pressure and mechanical stress on engine components. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### 📘 **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the air-standard efficiency of the Dual Cycle starting from first principles. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An air-standard Dual Cycle has compression ratio $r = 17$, pressure ratio $r_p = 1.6$, and cut-off ratio $r_c = 1.5$. The temperature and pressure at the start of compression are $T_1 = 330$ K and $P_1 = 100$ kPa. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_v = 0.717$ kJ/kg·K. Calculate:
   (i) The pressures and temperatures at all five principal state points. **(4 marks)**
   (ii) The thermal efficiency and the net work done per kg of air. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Air is the working fluid and behaves as an ideal gas.
2. All processes are internally reversible.
3. Compression and expansion are isentropic (adiabatic + reversible).
4. Constant-volume and constant-pressure processes replace actual combustion.
5. No heat loss, no friction, no leakage; intake and exhaust at constant volume.

**Derivation Steps (6 marks):**

The four key temperatures in terms of $T_1$ are:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

$$
T_3 = T_2 \, r_p = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} \, r_p
$$

$$
T_4 = T_3 \, r_c = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} \, r_p \, r_c
$$

$$
T_5 = T_4 \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_1 \, r_p \, r_c^{\gamma}
$$

Heat supplied:

$$
Q_{in} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) + C_p (T_4 - T_3)
$$

$$
Q_{in} = C_v \, T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \, r_p (r_c - 1) \right]
$$

Heat rejected:

$$
Q_{out} = C_v (T_5 - T_1) = C_v \, T_1 \left( r_p \, r_c^{\gamma} - 1 \right)
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{r_p \, r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r^{\gamma - 1} \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \, r_p (r_c - 1) \right]}
$$

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $r = 17$, $r_p = 1.6$, $r_c = 1.5$, $T_1 = 330$ K, $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.717$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 1: Compute $r^{\gamma-1}$.**

$$
17^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 17} = e^{0.4 \times 2.8332} = e^{1.1333} \approx 3.105
$$

**Step 2: Compute temperatures.**

$$
T_2 = 330 \times 3.105 = 1024.7 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_3 = 1024.7 \times 1.6 = 1639.5 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_4 = 1639.5 \times 1.5 = 2459.3 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_5 = 330 \times 1.6 \times (1.5)^{1.4} = 528 \times 1.738 = 917.7 \text{ K}
$$

(where $1.5^{1.4} = e^{1.4 \ln 1.5} = e^{1.4 \times 0.4055} = e^{0.5677} \approx 1.764$, using more precision: $T_5 \approx 528 \times 1.764 \approx 931.4$ K — recompute $1.5^{1.4}$: $1.5^{1.4} = 1.5^1 \cdot 1.5^{0.4} = 1.5 \times 1.176 = 1.764$, so $T_5 \approx 528 \times 1.764 \approx 931.4$ K)

**Step 3: Compute pressures.**

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 100 \times (17)^{1.4} = 100 \times 17 \times 17^{0.4} = 100 \times 17 \times 3.105 = 5278.5 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
P_3 = P_2 \cdot r_p = 5278.5 \times 1.6 = 8445.6 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
P_4 = P_3 = 8445.6 \text{ kPa} \quad (\text{constant pressure process})
$$

$$
P_5 = P_4 \cdot \left(\frac{V_4}{V_5}\right)^{\gamma} = P_4 \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma} = 8445.6 \times \left(\frac{1.5}{17}\right)^{1.4}
$$

$$
(0.0882)^{1.4} = e^{1.4 \ln 0.0882} = e^{1.4 \times (-2.428)} = e^{-3.399} \approx 0.0334
$$

$$
P_5 = 8445.6 \times 0.0334 \approx 282.1 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $Q_{in}$, $Q_{out}$, $W_{net}$, and $\eta_{th}$.**

With $C_p = 1.4 \times 0.717 = 1.0038$ kJ/kg·K:

$$
Q_{in} = 0.717 (1639.5 - 1024.7) + 1.0038 (2459.3 - 1639.5)
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 0.717 \times 614.8 + 1.0038 \times 819.8 = 440.81 + 822.92 = 1263.73 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{out} = 0.717 \times (931.4 - 330) = 0.717 \times 601.4 = 431.20 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{net} = 1263.73 - 431.20 = 832.53 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{832.53}{1263.73} = 0.6588 \approx 65.88\%
$$

**Verification using the master formula:**

$$
r_c^{\gamma} = 1.5^{1.4} = 1.764
$$

$$
r_p \, r_c^{\gamma} - 1 = 1.6 \times 1.764 - 1 = 2.822 - 1 = 1.822
$$

$$
(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1) = 0.6 + 1.4 \times 1.6 \times 0.5 = 0.6 + 1.12 = 1.72
$$

$$
r^{\gamma-1} \times 1.72 = 3.105 \times 1.72 = 5.341
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1.822}{5.341} = 1 - 0.3411 = 0.6589 \approx 65.89\% \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### 📘 **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the Dual Cycle with Otto and Diesel cycles in terms of processes, governing ratios, peak pressure, and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An engine operates on an air-standard Dual Cycle with $T_1 = 350$ K, $P_1 = 1$ bar, $r = 16$, $r_p = 1.4$, and $r_c = 1.7$. Determine:
   (i) The net heat supplied and net work done per kg of air. **(4 marks)**
   (ii) The mean effective pressure if the displaced volume per kg is $V_1 - V_2 = 0.0035$ m³/kg. **(3 marks)**

Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Comparison (7 marks)

| Aspect | Otto Cycle | Diesel Cycle | Dual Cycle |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heat Addition | Constant V only (1-2) | Constant P only (1-2) | **Constant V + Constant P (2 stages)** |
| Governing Ratios | $r$ | $r$, $r_c$ | **$r$, $r_p$, $r_c$** |
| Peak Pressure (same $r$) | Moderate | Lowest | **Highest** |
| Peak Temperature | High | Moderate | **Highest** |
| Approximate Application | SI engines, spark ignition | Slow-speed CI engines | **High-speed CI, dual-fuel engines** |
| Limiting Case | $r_c = 1$ in Dual | $r_p = 1$ in Dual | — |
| Efficiency at low r | Lower | Higher | **Between Otto & Diesel** |
| Efficiency at high r | Higher | Lower | **Highest** |

**Key insights (2 marks):**
1. The Dual Cycle is the **most general** form encompassing both Otto ($r_c = 1$) and Diesel ($r_p = 1$) as special cases.
2. The Dual Cycle achieves **higher efficiency at high compression ratios** compared to the Diesel cycle because of the rapid (constant-V) part of combustion.
3. The penalty is **higher peak pressure and temperature**, demanding stronger engine components.

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute the exponent.**

$$
r^{\gamma-1} = 16^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 16} = e^{0.4 \times 2.7726} = e^{1.1090} \approx 3.031
$$

**Step 2: Compute temperatures.**

$$
T_2 = 350 \times 3.031 = 1060.9 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_3 = 1060.9 \times 1.4 = 1485.3 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_4 = 1485.3 \times 1.7 = 2525.0 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_5 = 350 \times 1.4 \times (1.7)^{1.4} = 490 \times 2.105 \approx 1031.5 \text{ K}
$$

(where $1.7^{1.4} = e^{1.4 \ln 1.7} = e^{1.4 \times 0.5306} = e^{0.7428} \approx 2.102$)

**Step 3: Compute $Q_{in}$.**

With $C_p = 1.4 \times 0.718 = 1.0052$ kJ/kg·K:

$$
Q_{in} = 0.718 (1485.3 - 1060.9) + 1.0052 (2525.0 - 1485.3)
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 0.718 \times 424.4 + 1.0052 \times 1039.7
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 304.72 + 1045.10 = 1349.82 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $Q_{out}$ and $W_{net}$.**

$$
Q_{out} = 0.718 \times (1031.5 - 350) = 0.718 \times 681.5 = 489.32 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = 1349.82 - 489.32 = 860.50 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5: Compute MEP.**

$$
MEP = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{860.50 \text{ kJ/kg}}{0.0035 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}} = \frac{860,500 \text{ J/kg}}{0.0035 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}
$$

$$
MEP = 245,857,143 \text{ Pa} \approx 245.86 \text{ bar} \approx 24.59 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign and Direction Errors:** Students frequently forget that the constant-V heat addition comes **first** (2→3) and constant-P comes **second** (3→4). Reversing the order gives $T_4 < T_3$, which is physically impossible.
> 2. **Forgetting $C_p = \gamma C_v$:** The heat supplied at constant pressure uses $C_p$, not $C_v$. This is the most common mistake — losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency in MEP:** Ensure $W_{net}$ is in **J/kg** (multiply kJ/kg by 1000) and volume is in **m³/kg**, so MEP comes out in **Pa**.
> 4. **Cut-off ratio vs. pressure ratio confusion:** Students sometimes use $r_p$ where $r_c$ is required (or vice versa). Remember: $r_p$ governs constant-V (pressure rise at constant V), $r_c$ governs constant-P (volume expansion at constant P).
> 5. **Not verifying boundary cases:** The examiner may award bonus marks if a student mentions the Otto ($r_c=1$) and Diesel ($r_p=1$) reductions explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 High-Yield Quick Facts for Last-Minute Revision:**

✅ **Cycle Composition:** Dual = Otto's constant-V + Diesel's constant-P heat addition.

✅ **Five State Points:** $1 \to 2$ (isentropic comp.) $\to 3$ (const. V heat in) $\to 4$ (const. P heat in) $\to 5$ (isentropic exp.) $\to 1$ (const. V heat out).

✅ **Three Governing Ratios:**
   - $r = V_1/V_2$ → compression ratio
   - $r_p = P_3/P_2$ → pressure ratio
   - $r_c = V_4/V_3$ → cut-off ratio

✅ **Temperature Formulae (MUST memorize):**
   - $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$
   - $T_3 = T_2 r_p$
   - $T_4 = T_3 r_c$
   - $T_5 = T_1 r_p r_c^{\gamma}$

✅ **Master Efficiency Formula:**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{r_p \, r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r^{\gamma - 1} \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \, r_p (r_c - 1) \right]}
$$

✅ **Limiting Cases (very high-yield for KTU):**
   - $r_c = 1 \Rightarrow$ **Otto Cycle**
   - $r_p = 1 \Rightarrow$ **Diesel Cycle**

✅ **Trends:**
   - ↑ $r$ → ↑ $\eta_{th}$ (efficiency increases with compression ratio)
   - ↑ $r_p$ → ↑ $\eta_{th}$ (more constant-V heating is better for efficiency)
   - ↑ $r_c$ → ↓ $\eta_{th}$ (more constant-P heating lowers efficiency)

✅ **Peak Pressure:** Dual > Otto > Diesel (for same compression ratio $r$).

✅ **Standard Values:** $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

✅ **MEP Formula:** $MEP = W_{net} / (V_1 - V_2)$ — indicates engine's specific output capability.

✅ **Real-World Application:** High-speed diesel engines, dual-fuel engines, HCCI research.

✅ **P-V Diagram Shape:** A "tilted trapezoid with a vertical left side and a slanted right side" — distinguish from Otto (rectangle) and Diesel (trapezoid only).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Standard Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Air Standard Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Air Standard Efficiency** is defined as the ratio of the net work output to the heat input for an ideal air-standard cycle, evaluated under the idealized *air-standard assumptions*. Mathematically, it is the **thermal efficiency of the cycle** when air (treated as an ideal gas with constant specific heats) is the working fluid.

$$
\eta_{\text{air}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 2):** Students must be able to derive and apply the air-standard efficiency expressions for the **Otto, Diesel, Dual (Limited Pressure), and Brayton (Joule) cycles**, evaluate **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)**, and compare cycle performance for Part-B (14-mark) problems.

## 1.2 The Air-Standard Assumptions (Working Fluid Model)

Since real engines operate with combustion products (a complex mixture of CO₂, H₂O, N₂, etc.), KTU/IEGRE standard analysis replaces this mixture with **air** for analytical tractability. The governing assumptions are:

1. The working fluid is a **fixed mass of air** (a perfect gas) that remains perfectly sealed within the system — it is never replaced, exhausted, or replenished.
2. The specific heats **$C_p$** and **$C_v$** remain **constant** throughout the cycle, evaluated at room temperature ($C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$).
3. All processes (compression, heat addition, expansion, heat rejection) are **internally reversible** (quasi-equilibrium / quasi-static).
4. The **combustion process is replaced by heat addition** from an external source.
5. The **exhaust process is replaced by heat rejection** to a sink that restores the working fluid to its initial state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these assumptions?** Real combustion chemistry is intractable for hand analysis. Air-standard assumptions yield closed-form efficiency expressions that are accurate to within 2–5% for SI and CI engines, which is excellent for design-stage evaluation — the **exact purpose of Module 2 analysis**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge Bucket"

Imagine you have a **sponge** (the air) inside a sealed **piston-cylinder bucket** (the engine). You **squeeze** the sponge (compression — work done ON the system), then pour **hot water** over it (heat addition — $Q_{in}$), it expands and pushes the bucket lid up (expansion — work done BY the system), then you **cool it** with cold water (heat rejection — $Q_{out}$), and finally release the bucket so the sponge returns to its original size (the cyclic reset).

The **Air Standard Efficiency** is essentially asking: *"Of all the hot water (fuel energy) you poured in, what fraction did you manage to convert into useful mechanical work (lifting the lid), after accounting for the unavoidable cold water you had to pour out?"* The compression ratio and the cycle's thermodynamic "shape" determine how efficient this conversion is.

## 1.4 The Four KTU-Relevant Air-Standard Cycles

| Cycle | Heat Addition Type | Real Engine Analog |
|---|---|---|
| **Otto Cycle** | Constant Volume (Isochoric) | Spark Ignition (Petrol) Engine — ideal |
| **Diesel Cycle** | Constant Pressure (Isobaric) | Compression Ignition (Diesel) Engine — ideal |
| **Dual (Sabathe) Cycle** | Constant Volume + Constant Pressure | Modern High-Speed CI & SI Engines (realistic) |
| **Brayton (Joule) Cycle** | Constant Pressure | Gas Turbine / Jet Engine |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Structured Logic — Why Each Efficiency Has Its Form

The thermal efficiency of *any* air-standard cycle depends on **only two thermodynamic parameters**:
- **The compression ratio** ($r$ or $r_p$)
- **The cutoff/pressure ratio** ($r_c$, $\beta$, or $r_p$)
- The ratio of specific heats $\gamma$ (= **1.4** for air in KTU problems)

### 2.1.1 The Otto Cycle — Logic Chain

The Otto cycle has **two adiabatic (isentropic) processes** (1→2 and 3→4) and **two constant-volume processes** (2→3 heat addition, 4→1 heat rejection).

- **Step 1:** Compression 1→2: Adiabatic, so $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$ where $r = V_1/V_2$.
- **Step 2:** Heat addition 2→3: Constant volume, so $Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$.
- **Step 3:** Expansion 3→4: Adiabatic, $T_4 = T_3 \cdot (1/r)^{\gamma-1}$.
- **Step 4:** Heat rejection 4→1: $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$.
- **Step 5:** Apply first law: $\eta = 1 - Q_{out}/Q_{in}$ → **efficiency depends ONLY on $r$ and $\gamma$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight (Examiner's favorite):** In the Otto cycle, efficiency **increases with compression ratio $r$** and **decreases with $\gamma$**. For $r=8$, $\eta \approx 56.5\%$. This is the *only* way to improve SI engine efficiency without changing fuel.

### 2.1.2 The Diesel Cycle — Logic Chain

Same adiabatic compression/expansion, but heat addition is **at constant pressure** (2→3).

- Heat addition: $Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$.
- **Cutoff ratio** $\beta = V_3/V_2$ (an additional geometric parameter).
- The expansion is from $V_3$ back to $V_1$, so the effective expansion ratio differs from $r$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Many students confuse **cutoff ratio ($\beta$)** with **compression ratio ($r$)**. They are different! $r = V_1/V_2$ (initial volume / clearance volume), while $\beta = V_3/V_2$ (volume at end of injection / clearance volume). Always $\beta > 1$, and typically $1 < \beta < 2$ in real diesels.

### 2.1.3 The Dual (Limited Pressure / Sabathe) Cycle — Logic Chain

Combines both: heat is added **partly at constant volume** (2→3) and **partly at constant pressure** (3→4). This is the most realistic model for modern high-speed diesel engines.

- Two extra parameters: **pressure ratio $r_p = p_3/p_2$** and **cutoff ratio $\beta = V_4/V_3$**.
- This cycle "sits between" Otto and Diesel on the efficiency map.

### 2.1.4 The Brayton (Joule) Cycle — Logic Chain

Used for **gas turbines**. All four processes: 2 adiabatics, 1 isobaric heat addition, 1 isobaric heat rejection.

- Key parameter: **pressure ratio $r_p = p_2/p_1$** (across compressor).
- Unlike reciprocating engines, the working fluid flows *through* the system, but the cycle is analyzed closed-loop using air-standard assumptions.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Cycle | Air-Standard Efficiency Formula $\eta$ | Key Geometric Parameter(s) | Defining Equations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Otto** | $\eta_O = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$, $T_4 = T_3 \left(\dfrac{1}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1}$ |
| 2 | **Diesel** | $\eta_D = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \left[ \dfrac{\beta^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\beta - 1)} \right]$ | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$, $\beta = \dfrac{V_3}{V_2}$ | $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$, $T_3 = T_2 \cdot \beta$ |
| 3 | **Dual (Sabathe)** | $\eta_{du} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_p \beta^{\gamma} - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (\beta - 1)}$ | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$, $r_p = \dfrac{p_3}{p_2}$, $\beta = \dfrac{V_4}{V_3}$ | $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$, $\dfrac{T_3}{T_2} = r_p$, $\dfrac{T_4}{T_3} = \beta$ |
| 4 | **Brayton (Joule)** | $\eta_B = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | $r_p = \dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = \dfrac{p_3}{p_4}$ | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, $T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_p^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ |
| 5 | **MEP (Otto)** | $\text{MEP} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_1 - V_2} = \dfrac{P_1 r \left[ r^{\gamma-1}(\lambda - 1) - (r\lambda - 1)/r^{\gamma-1} \right]}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)}$ | $\lambda = T_3/T_2$ (explosion ratio) | Used to size the engine cylinder |
| 6 | **MEP (Diesel)** | $\text{MEP} = \dfrac{P_1 r \gamma \left[ r^{\gamma-1}(\beta - 1) - (\beta^{\gamma} - 1)/\gamma \right]}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)}$ | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Alert for KTU Exams:** Some textbooks use $r_c$ for cutoff ratio, while the official KTU Module 2 (P.K. Nag, Chapter on Air-Standard Cycles) uses $\beta$. Both are acceptable, but be consistent within a single derivation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Cycle Used | Why Efficiency Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive (Petrol)** | Otto / Dual | Determines BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) and fuel economy rating (km/L) |
| **Trucks, Generators, Marine** | Diesel / Dual | Higher efficiency → lower fuel cost per km/ton |
| **Aviation Jet Engines** | Brayton | Determines specific thrust (N per kg/s of air) and TSFC |
| **Power Plants (Gas)** | Brayton with Reheat/Regen | Sets the LCOE (Levelized Cost of Electricity) |
| **Rocket Turbopumps** | Brayton (closed) | Determines payload mass fraction |

The **compression ratio** and **peak cycle temperature** are the two design knobs an engineer has. The efficiency formulas tell us exactly how sensitive performance is to these knobs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Otto Cycle Air-Standard Efficiency (Full Derivation)

**Process Map:**
- 1 → 2: **Isentropic compression** (adiabatic + reversible)
- 2 → 3: **Constant volume heat addition** $Q_{in}$
- 3 → 4: **Isentropic expansion**
- 4 → 1: **Constant volume heat rejection** $Q_{out}$

### Step 1: Isentropic Relations (Process 1-2)

For an isentropic process of an ideal gas with constant specific heats:

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}
$$

### Step 2: Isentropic Relations (Process 3-4)

By the symmetry of the cycle (same isentropic relations, same volumes in reverse):

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}}
$$

### Step 3: Heat Addition (Process 2-3, Constant Volume)

For a constant volume process, $Q = m C_v \Delta T$. Thus:

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)
$$

### Step 4: Heat Rejection (Process 4-1, Constant Volume)

$$
Q_{\text{out}} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)
$$

### Step 5: Apply the Definition of Thermal Efficiency

The thermal efficiency of a cycle is defined as:

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}
$$

Substituting Steps 3 and 4:

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{m C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m C_v (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

### Step 6: Substitute $T_2$ and $T_4$ from Steps 1 and 2

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{\dfrac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} - T_1}{T_3 - T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}}
$$

### Step 7: Factor Out $T_1$ and Apply the Trick

Multiply numerator and denominator by $1/T_1$. Let $T_3/T_1 = \tau$ (a temperature ratio for compactness):

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{\dfrac{\tau}{r^{\gamma-1}} - 1}{\tau - r^{\gamma-1}}
$$

Multiply both numerator and denominator of the inner fraction by $r^{\gamma-1}$:

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{\tau - r^{\gamma-1}}{r^{\gamma-1}(\tau - r^{\gamma-1})} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}
$$

### ✅ **Final Result: Otto Cycle Efficiency**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Diesel Cycle Air-Standard Efficiency (Full Derivation)

**Process Map:**
- 1 → 2: Isentropic compression
- 2 → 3: **Constant pressure heat addition** $Q_{in}$
- 3 → 4: Isentropic expansion (note: $V_4 = V_1$)
- 4 → 1: Constant volume heat rejection

### Step 1: Compression (1-2) — Same as Otto

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}
$$

### Step 2: Constant Pressure Heat Addition (2-3)

For an isobaric process: $V/T = \text{const}$, so $T_3/T_2 = V_3/V_2 = \beta$ (the cutoff ratio).

$$
T_3 = \beta \cdot T_2 = \beta \cdot T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}
$$

### Step 3: Heat Added (2-3, Constant Pressure)

For a constant pressure process, $Q = m C_p \Delta T$:

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2) = m C_p T_2 (\beta - 1) = m C_p T_1 r^{\gamma-1} (\beta - 1)
$$

### Step 4: Isentropic Expansion (3-4)

The expansion goes from $V_3$ to $V_4 = V_1$. The expansion ratio is $V_3/V_1 = \beta/r$.

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \left(\frac{r}{\beta}\right)^{\gamma-1}
$$

Solving for $T_4$:

$$
T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{\beta}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1}
$$

Substituting $T_3 = \beta T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$:

$$
T_4 = \beta T_1 r^{\gamma-1} \cdot \frac{\beta^{\gamma-1}}{r^{\gamma-1}} = T_1 \cdot \beta^{\gamma}
$$

### Step 5: Heat Rejected (4-1, Constant Volume)

$$
Q_{\text{out}} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1) = m C_v T_1 (\beta^{\gamma} - 1)
$$

### Step 6: Apply Thermal Efficiency Definition

$$
\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{m C_v T_1 (\beta^{\gamma} - 1)}{m C_p T_1 r^{\gamma-1} (\beta - 1)}
$$

The $T_1$ and $m$ cancel. Using $C_p/C_v = \gamma$, we get $C_v/C_p = 1/\gamma$:

$$
\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{\beta^{\gamma} - 1}{\beta - 1}
$$

### ✅ **Final Result: Diesel Cycle Efficiency**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \left[ \frac{\beta^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\beta - 1)} \right]}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Dual (Sabathe / Limited Pressure) Cycle Efficiency

**Process Map:**
- 1 → 2: Isentropic compression
- 2 → 3: **Constant volume heat addition** (partial $Q_{in}$)
- 3 → 4: **Constant pressure heat addition** (remaining $Q_{in}$)
- 4 → 5: Isentropic expansion
- 5 → 1: Constant volume heat rejection

### Step 1: Compression (1-2)

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}
$$

### Step 2: Constant Volume Heat Addition (2-3)

Pressure ratio $r_p = p_3/p_2$. At constant volume, $T \propto p$:

$$
T_3 = T_2 \cdot r_p = T_1 r^{\gamma-1} r_p
$$

### Step 3: Constant Pressure Heat Addition (3-4)

Cutoff ratio $\beta = V_4/V_3$. At constant pressure, $T \propto V$:

$$
T_4 = T_3 \cdot \beta = T_1 r^{\gamma-1} r_p \beta
$$

### Step 4: Total Heat Input

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2) + m C_p (T_4 - T_3)
$$

Substituting and factoring $T_1$:

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = m T_1 r^{\gamma-1} \left[ C_v (r_p - 1) + C_p r_p (\beta - 1) \right]
$$

### Step 5: Isentropic Expansion (4-5)

Expanding from $V_4$ to $V_5 = V_1$. Expansion ratio $= V_1/V_4 = r/(r_p \beta)$ ... wait, careful: $V_4/V_1 = \beta r_p / r$, so $V_1/V_4 = r/(\beta r_p)$.

$$
T_5 = T_4 \left(\frac{V_1}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma-1} = T_4 \left(\frac{r}{\beta r_p}\right)^{\gamma-1}
$$

Substituting $T_4$:

$$
T_5 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1} r_p \beta \cdot \frac{r^{\gamma-1}}{(\beta r_p)^{\gamma-1}} = T_1 \cdot \frac{r^{2(\gamma-1)} r_p \beta}{(\beta r_p)^{\gamma-1}}
$$

This simplifies (after careful algebra) to:

$$
T_5 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \cdot \frac{r_p \beta^{\gamma} - (r_p - 1)(\beta - 1) \gamma - 1}{r_p - 1 + \gamma r_p (\beta - 1)} \cdot \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \quad \text{(intermediate form)}
$$

### Step 6: Standard Final Form (Direct Reference)

Using the standard textbook result (P.K. Nag, Chapter 7), the dual cycle efficiency reduces to:

### ✅ **Final Result: Dual Cycle Efficiency**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{Dual}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_p \beta^{\gamma} - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (\beta - 1)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Behavior Check (KTU Examiner Loves This!):**
> - If $\beta = 1$ (no constant-pressure part), the dual cycle **reduces to the Otto cycle** ✓
> - If $r_p = 1$ (no constant-volume part), the dual cycle **reduces to the Diesel cycle** ✓
> Students who verify these limits in their answer scripts gain **extra credit** with KTU examiners.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Brayton (Joule) Cycle Efficiency

**Process Map:**
- 1 → 2: Isentropic compression
- 2 → 3: **Constant pressure heat addition** (combustion chamber)
- 3 → 4: Isentropic expansion (turbine)
- 4 → 1: Constant pressure heat rejection

### Step 1: Compressor (1-2, Isentropic)

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

### Step 2: Turbine (3-4, Isentropic)

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{p_3}{p_4}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

By process 2-3 isobaric, $p_2 = p_3$. By process 4-1 isobaric, $p_4 = p_1$. So $p_3/p_4 = p_2/p_1 = r_p$. ✓

### Step 3: Heat Addition and Rejection (Constant Pressure)

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2), \qquad Q_{\text{out}} = m C_p (T_4 - T_1)
$$

### Step 4: Apply Efficiency Definition

$$
\eta_B = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

### Step 5: Express in Terms of $r_p$ Only

Using $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ and $T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$:

Let $x = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. Then $T_2 = x T_1$ and $T_4 = T_3/x$.

$$
\eta_B = 1 - \frac{T_3/x - T_1}{T_3 - x T_1}
$$

Factor $T_1$ (let $\tau = T_3/T_1$):

$$
\eta_B = 1 - \frac{\tau/x - 1}{\tau - x}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $x$:

$$
\eta_B = 1 - \frac{\tau - x}{x(\tau - x)} = 1 - \frac{1}{x} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}
$$

### ✅ **Final Result: Brayton Cycle Efficiency**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{Brayton}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) — Otto Cycle

**Definition:** MEP is a fictitious constant pressure which, if acting on the piston throughout the power stroke, would produce the same net work as the actual cycle.

$$
\text{MEP} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_{\text{swept}}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_1 - V_2}
$$

### Step 1: Net Work for Otto Cycle

Using the standard result from the Otto derivation (3.1):

$$
W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}} = m C_v \left[(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)\right]
$$

In terms of $T_1$ and ratios:

$$
W_{\text{net}} = m C_v T_1 \left[(\lambda r^{\gamma-1} - r^{\gamma-1}) - \left(\frac{\lambda}{r^{\gamma-1}} - 1\right)\right]
$$

where $\lambda = T_3/T_2$ is the **explosion ratio**.

Simplifying:

$$
W_{\text{net}} = m C_v T_1 \left[r^{\gamma-1}(\lambda - 1) - \frac{(\lambda - 1)}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot r + (r-1)\right] \quad \text{(intermediate)}
$$

After full algebraic simplification:

$$
W_{\text{net}} = \frac{m R T_1 r \left[r^{\gamma-1}(\lambda - 1) - (\lambda r - 1)/r^{\gamma-1}\right]}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)} \quad \text{(final form using } PV = mRT\text{)}
$$

### Step 2: Express in Terms of MEP

Using $P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$, so $m R T_1 = P_1 V_1$. Substitute $V_1 = V_2 \cdot r$ and $V_1 - V_2 = V_2(r-1)$:

$$
\text{MEP} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{P_1 r \left[ r^{\gamma-1}(\lambda - 1) - (\lambda r - 1)/r^{\gamma-1} \right]}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)}
$$

### ✅ **Final Result: MEP for Otto Cycle**

$$
\boxed{\text{MEP}_{\text{Otto}} = \frac{P_1 r}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)} \left[ r^{\gamma-1}(\lambda - 1) - \frac{\lambda r - 1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \right]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** MEP is essentially "specific work output" per unit displacement. A higher MEP for a given cylinder size means a more powerful, more compact engine — critical for automotive and aerospace applications where weight and volume are constrained.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

**Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2024 type):** An Otto cycle has a compression ratio of 8:1. The temperature and pressure at the start of compression are 17°C and 1 bar. The maximum temperature reached during the cycle is 2000 K. Determine:
1. The temperature and pressure at the end of each process.
2. The air-standard efficiency.
3. The MEP. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

**Solution:**

**Given:** $T_1 = 290$ K, $P_1 = 1$ bar = 100 kPa, $r = 8$, $T_3 = 2000$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$

**Step 1: Find $T_2$ (after isentropic compression)**

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 290 \times 8^{0.4} = 290 \times 2.2974 = 666.2 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2: Find $P_2$ (isentropic: $P_2/P_1 = r^\gamma$)**

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 100 \times 8^{1.4} = 100 \times 18.379 = 1837.9 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3: Find $P_3$ (constant volume: $P_3/P_2 = T_3/T_2$)**

$$
P_3 = P_2 \cdot \frac{T_3}{T_2} = 1837.9 \times \frac{2000}{666.2} = 5517 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4: Find $T_4$ (isentropic expansion)**

$$
T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \frac{2000}{8^{0.4}} = \frac{2000}{2.2974} = 870.6 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 5: Air-Standard Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.2974} = 1 - 0.4353 = 0.5647 = 56.47\%
$$

**Step 6: Net Work and MEP**

Heat input: $Q_{in} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.718 \times (2000 - 666.2) = 957.65$ kJ/kg
Heat rejected: $Q_{out} = C_v (T_4 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (870.6 - 290) = 416.66$ kJ/kg
Net work: $W_{net} = 957.65 - 416.66 = 540.99$ kJ/kg

Specific volumes using $P_1 V_1 = R T_1$:
$v_1 = RT_1/P_1 = 0.287 \times 290 / 100 = 0.8323$ m³/kg
$v_2 = v_1/r = 0.1040$ m³/kg
$v_1 - v_2 = 0.7283$ m³/kg

$$
\text{MEP} = \frac{W_{net}}{v_1 - v_2} = \frac{540.99}{0.7283} = 742.8 \text{ kPa}
$$

**✅ Final Answers:** $\eta = 56.47\%$, MEP = 742.8 kPa, $T_2 = 666.2$ K, $P_2 = 1837.9$ kPa, $P_3 = 5517$ kPa, $T_4 = 870.6$ K.
