# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the air-standard assumptions used in the analysis of reciprocating engine cycles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The air-standard assumptions are:
1. The working fluid is a **fixed mass of dry air** which behaves as an **ideal gas** with constant specific heats ($C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$).
2. The **combustion process is replaced by heat addition** from an external source.
3. The **exhaust process is replaced by heat rejection** to an external sink.
4. All processes are **internally reversible** (quasi-equilibrium).
5. The cylinder is perfectly sealed; no mass crosses the system boundary.

These assumptions simplify the complex real combustion gases into a tractable analytical model with constant properties.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why does the air-standard efficiency of the Otto cycle depend only on the compression ratio, while the Diesel cycle depends on both compression and cutoff ratios?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In the **Otto cycle**, heat addition is at **constant volume**, so $Q_{in} = mC_v(T_3 - T_2)$ and $Q_{out} = mC_v(T_4 - T_1)$. Using isentropic relations, both $T_2$ and $T_4$ can be expressed in terms of $T_1$, $T_3$, and the compression ratio $r$ alone — the cutoff ratio does not appear. The cutoff ratio is by definition $\beta = 1$ (no constant-pressure phase), so it is not a free parameter.

In the **Diesel cycle**, heat addition is at **constant pressure**, which introduces a new geometric parameter — the **cutoff ratio $\beta = V_3/V_2 > 1$**. This $\beta$ appears explicitly in the expression for $T_3$ and $T_4$, and thus in the efficiency formula. Therefore, Diesel efficiency is a function of *two* ratios: $r$ and $\beta$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the air-standard efficiency expression for an Otto cycle. Hence, evaluate the efficiency for a compression ratio of 9:1, taking $\gamma = 1.4$ for air.

**(b) [7 marks]** An air-standard Diesel cycle has a compression ratio of 18:1 and a cutoff ratio of 2.0. If the conditions at the start of compression are 100 kPa and 30°C, calculate the **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)**. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation of Otto Efficiency (5 marks for derivation + 2 marks for calculation):**

**Step 1:** Otto cycle consists of two isentropic processes (1-2, 3-4) and two constant-volume processes (2-3 heat addition, 4-1 heat rejection).

**Step 2:** From isentropic compression:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}
$$

**Step 3:** From isentropic expansion:

$$
T_4 = T_3 / r^{\gamma-1}
$$

**Step 4:** Heat input and output (constant volume):

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2), \quad Q_{\text{out}} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)
$$

**Step 5:** Apply efficiency definition and simplify:

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}
$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**

For $r = 9$ and $\gamma = 1.4$:

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{9^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.4082} = 1 - 0.4152 = 0.5848 = 58.48\%
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $r = 18$, $\beta = 2.0$, $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 303$ K

**Step 1:** Find $T_2$ (isentropic compression):

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 303 \times 18^{0.4} = 303 \times 3.1777 = 962.85 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2:** Find $P_2$:

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 100 \times 18^{1.4} = 100 \times 57.20 = 5720 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Find $T_3$ (constant pressure):

$$
T_3 = T_2 \cdot \beta = 962.85 \times 2.0 = 1925.7 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4:** Find $T_4$ (isentropic expansion, $V_4/V_3 = r/\beta$):

$$
T_4 = T_3 \cdot (\beta/r)^{\gamma-1} = 1925.7 \times (2/18)^{0.4} = 1925.7 \times 0.3147 = 605.95 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 5:** Heat input and output:

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = C_p(T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1925.7 - 962.85) = 967.74 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{out}} = C_v(T_4 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (605.95 - 303) = 217.42 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 6:** Net work and MEP:

$$
W_{\text{net}} = 967.74 - 217.42 = 750.32 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Specific volumes:

$$
v_1 = RT_1/P_1 = 0.287 \times 303/100 = 0.8696 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
v_2 = v_1/r = 0.0483 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}, \quad v_1 - v_2 = 0.8213 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{MEP} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{v_1 - v_2} = \frac{750.32}{0.8213} = 913.5 \text{ kPa}
$$

**✅ Final Answer:** MEP = **913.5 kPa**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a P-V and T-S diagram, derive the air-standard efficiency of a **Brayton cycle** operating between a pressure ratio $r_p$. State clearly all the air-standard assumptions.

**(b) [7 marks]** A gas turbine operates on the Brayton cycle with a pressure ratio of 8. The temperatures at the compressor inlet and turbine inlet are 300 K and 1300 K, respectively. Determine: (i) the thermal efficiency, (ii) the work ratio, and (iii) the net work output per kg of air. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**P-V and T-S Diagrams (3 marks):**

A Brayton cycle on a P-V diagram consists of:
- 1→2: Isentropic compression (steep rising curve)
- 2→3: Constant pressure heat addition (horizontal line)
- 3→4: Isentropic expansion (steep falling curve)
- 4→1: Constant pressure heat rejection (horizontal line)

On a T-S diagram, the isentropic processes are vertical, and the isobaric processes are logarithmic curves.

**Air-Standard Assumptions (1 mark):** Same five assumptions from Q1 of Part A.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

From isentropic relations:

$$
T_2/T_1 = (p_2/p_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

$$
T_3/T_4 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

Heat added (constant pressure):

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = C_p (T_3 - T_2)
$$

Heat rejected:

$$
Q_{\text{out}} = C_p (T_4 - T_1)
$$

Applying the definition:

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

Substituting and simplifying (algebraic manipulation with $x = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$):

$$
\eta_{\text{Brayton}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $r_p = 8$, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_3 = 1300$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 1: Find $T_2$ (compressor outlet):**

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 8^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \times 8^{0.2857}
$$

$$
8^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times \ln 8} = e^{0.2857 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.5941} = 1.8114
$$

$$
T_2 = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.4 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2: Find $T_4$ (turbine outlet):**

$$
T_4 = T_3 / 1.8114 = 1300 / 1.8114 = 717.7 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3: Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{Brayton}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5520 = 0.4480 = 44.80\%
$$

**Step 4: Work Ratio** (turbine work / compressor work):

$$
W_T = C_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 717.7) = 1.005 \times 582.3 = 585.2 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (543.4 - 300) = 1.005 \times 243.4 = 244.6 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{W_T}{W_C} = \frac{585.2}{244.6} = 2.392
$$

**Step 5: Net Work Output:**

$$
W_{\text{net}} = W_T - W_C = 585.2 - 244.6 = 340.6 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**✅ Final Answers:** $\eta = 44.80\%$, Work Ratio = 2.392, $W_{\text{net}} = 340.6$ kJ/kg.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units consistency:** Always convert temperatures to Kelvin **before** any calculation. Mixing °C with K in ratios loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $r$ and $\beta$:** In Diesel and Dual cycles, students often swap compression ratio with cutoff ratio. Always define them on the diagram first.
> 3. **Forgetting the temperature in MEP:** MEP is per unit *volume*, so a mass-specific work must be divided by the swept specific volume $(v_1 - v_2)$, not the absolute $v_1$.
> 4. **In Brayton, do not confuse $r_p$ (pressure ratio) with $r$ (compression ratio).** They have different exponents: Otto uses $r^{\gamma-1}$, Brayton uses $r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$.
> 5. **Neglecting boundary checks:** Always verify: for Dual cycle, $\beta = 1 \Rightarrow$ Otto, $r_p = 1 \Rightarrow$ Diesel. Skipping this check costs 1 mark on cross-questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Air-standard efficiency = thermal efficiency** of a cycle analyzed under air-standard assumptions (constant $C_p$, $C_v$, ideal gas, sealed system, reversible).
- [ ] **Otto:** $\eta = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$ — depends only on $r$. Use for SI (petrol) engines.
- [ ] **Diesel:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \left[ \dfrac{\beta^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\beta - 1)} \right]$ — depends on $r$ and $\beta$.
- [ ] **Dual:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_p \beta^\gamma - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p(\beta - 1)}$ — most general, depends on $r$, $r_p$, $\beta$.
- [ ] **Brayton:** $\eta = 1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ — depends only on $r_p$. Use for gas turbines.
- [ ] **MEP** is fictitious constant pressure producing the same net work — used to size cylinders.
- [ ] Always use **$\gamma = 1.4$** unless otherwise specified for air in KTU problems.
- [ ] For SI engine problems, the typical $r$ range is 8–12. For CI, $r$ is 14–22 and $\beta$ is 1.5–2.5.
- [ ] **Second law check:** Air-standard efficiency must always be **less than the Carnot efficiency** between the same $T_{\max}$ and $T_{\min}$. If your answer violates this, redo the calculation.
- [ ] **Memorize** the explosion ratio $\lambda = T_3/T_2$ for Otto MEP problems.
- [ ] In KTU answer sheets, **always draw the P-V and T-S diagram first** (2 marks reserved for it in 14-mark questions).
- [ ] Don't forget $C_p - C_v = R$ — useful to find $C_p$ or $C_v$ when one is given and $R$ is known.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mean Effective Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mean Effective Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)?

**Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)** is a **fictitious (imaginary) constant pressure** which, if it acted on the piston face throughout the entire power stroke (expansion stroke), would produce the **same amount of net work** as is actually produced by the real, continuously varying gas pressure inside the cylinder during the cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** MEP is the ratio of the **net work done per cycle (or per stroke)** to the **swept volume (displacement volume)** of the engine.
>
> $$\text{MEP} = \frac{\text{Net Work Done per Cycle}}{\text{Swept Volume } (V_s)}$$

The unit of MEP is the same as that of pressure: **N/m² or bar (1 bar = 10⁵ N/m²)**.

---

## 1.2 The Three Variants of MEP — Know the Difference

| Variant | Full Form | Work Used | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IMEP** | Indicated MEP | Indicated (cycle) work | $P_{im}$ |
| **BMEP** | Brake MEP | Brake (shaft) work | $P_{bm}$ |
| **FMEP** | Friction MEP | Friction work lost | $P_{fm}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** *I - Inside (gas work)*, *B - Brake (shaft)*, *F - Friction (lost)*. The cascade is:
> $$P_{im} = P_{bm} + P_{fm}$$

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket of Water" Picture

Imagine you are filling a bucket from a tap whose **flow rate keeps varying** (sometimes strong, sometimes weak, sometimes off). At the end, you have a **fixed volume of water** in the bucket.

Now, imagine instead a **constant, steady flow rate** running for the same total time. If the bucket ends up with the **same volume**, then that constant flow rate is your *"effective average"* flow rate.

**MEP is exactly this idea applied to pressure and volume.**

- The *tap's varying flow* = the real, fluctuating cylinder gas pressure during a cycle.
- The *bucket's water volume* = the swept volume ($V_s$).
- The *constant steady flow* = the **Mean Effective Pressure**.
- The *total time the tap runs* = one complete working cycle.

It is a **mathematical artifice** — no part of the engine actually sees this pressure; it is purely a tool for comparing engines of different sizes on an equal footing.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does KTU love MEP?** Because it lets you compare a tiny 100 cc bike engine with a massive 10-litre ship engine using just **one number** (a pressure value), without worrying about cylinder size or RPM. This is the **"Specific Work Output"** concept.

---

## 1.4 The P–V Diagram Intuition

On a real P–V (Pressure–Volume) diagram of an engine cycle, the **area enclosed** by the loop represents the **net work done per cycle**. MEP transforms this *uneven area* into a **rectangle** of height MEP and width $V_s$:

$$\text{Area of rectangle} = P_m \times V_s = W_{\text{cycle}}$$

This means the actual cycle area is mathematically equal to the area of a rectangle drawn on the same base ($V_s$) with height = MEP.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why MEP is a "Performance Indicator"

In KTU 2024 scheme problems, MEP is used because it:
1. **Normalises** work output — independent of swept volume.
2. Serves as a **bridge between power and dimensions** of an engine.
3. Helps in **sizing engines** — given a required MEP, we can back-calculate bore and stroke.
4. Enables easy comparison of **2-stroke vs 4-stroke** and **SI vs CI** engines.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation — Stepwise Logic

### Step 1 — Net Work from the P–V Loop
The area enclosed inside the working cycle on a P–V diagram is the **net indicated work per cycle**.

$$W_{\text{cycle}} = \oint P \, dV$$

### Step 2 — Define MEP Conceptually
Replace the loop's varying $P$ by an equivalent constant $P_m$ acting over the swept volume $V_s$:

$$P_m \cdot V_s = W_{\text{cycle}}$$

### Step 3 — Express in Terms of Power
Power = Work per cycle × Number of power strokes per second. For an engine running at $N$ rpm with $n$ cylinders, having $k$ working strokes per cycle:

$$\text{Power} = P_m \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N}{60} \cdot n \cdot k$$

where:
- $k = \tfrac{1}{2}$ for 2-stroke engines (every revolution is a power stroke)
- $k = 1$ for 4-stroke engines (power stroke every 2 revolutions)

### Step 4 — Mechanical Efficiency Link
Mechanical efficiency is the ratio of brake MEP to indicated MEP:

$$\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{P_{bm}}{P_{im}}$$

Hence:

$$P_{fm} = P_{im} - P_{bm} = P_{im}(1 - \eta_{\text{mech}})$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print/Revise this table before every KTU exam.** All quantities use SI units unless otherwise specified. Swept volume $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ for one cylinder.

| # | Formula | Meaning / KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_m = \dfrac{W_{\text{cycle}}}{V_s}$ | **Definition of MEP** — Universal |
| 2 | $W_{\text{cycle}} = P_m \cdot V_s$ | Work from MEP |
| 3 | $\text{IP} = \dfrac{P_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k}{60}$ | Indicated Power |
| 4 | $\text{BP} = \dfrac{P_{bm} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k}{60}$ | Brake Power |
| 5 | $\text{FP} = \text{IP} - \text{BP}$ | Friction Power |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{P_{bm}}{P_{im}} = \dfrac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}}$ | Mech. Efficiency |
| 7 | $P_{fm} = P_{im} - P_{bm}$ | Friction MEP |
| 8 | $\text{BP} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | BP from torque $T$ (N·m) |
| 9 | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | Swept Volume (m³) |
| 10 | $n_{\text{stroke}} = \dfrac{N}{60} \cdot n \cdot k$ | Power strokes / sec |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Always check whether the engine is **2-stroke or 4-stroke** before plugging $k$. Many students lose a **full mark** by using $k=1$ for a 2-stroke engine.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine Rating & Selection:** Tractor engines (high BMEP, low RPM), sports bikes (moderate BMEP, very high RPM), and marine diesels (low BMEP, very high torque) are all rated by their typical BMEP range.
- **SI engines:** Typical BMEP ≈ **8–12 bar**.
- **CI engines:** Typical BMEP ≈ **7–10 bar** (naturally aspirated), up to **20+ bar** (turbocharged).
- **F1 engines:** BMEP can exceed **15 bar** at peak power.
- MEP also drives **component design** — the connecting rod, crank, bearings are sized considering peak pressure, but MEP gives the *time-averaged mechanical load*.

---

## 2.5 Why MEP is Preferred over "Work" Alone

Consider two engines:
- Engine A: $V_s = 200$ cc, work per cycle = **200 J** → MEP = **10 bar**
- Engine B: $V_s = 2$ L, work per cycle = **2000 J** → MEP = **10 bar**

Both have **same MEP** (10 bar) — meaning they are doing **equally intensive work per unit displacement**, even though Engine B is physically 10× larger.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MEP from Basic Definition

Starting from the net work done per cycle in terms of pressure and volume:

$$\oint P \, dV = W_{\text{cycle}}$$

By definition of MEP, we assume the entire work is produced by a fictitious constant pressure $P_m$ acting over the swept volume $V_s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{cycle}} &= P_m \cdot V_s \\
\therefore \quad P_m &= \frac{W_{\text{cycle}}}{V_s} = \frac{\oint P \, dV}{V_s}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental definition**. The integral $\oint P \, dV$ is the area of the P–V loop, and dividing it by $V_s$ gives the average height (i.e., MEP) of the equivalent rectangle.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Indicated Power from IMEP

Let us derive the **Indicated Power** formula starting from IMEP.

### Given:
- $P_{im}$ = Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (N/m²)
- $L$ = Stroke length (m)
- $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ = Piston cross-section (m²)
- $N$ = Engine speed (rpm)
- $n$ = Number of cylinders
- $k = \tfrac{1}{2}$ (2-stroke) or $1$ (4-stroke)

### Step A — Work per cylinder per cycle
$$W_{\text{cycle}} = P_{im} \cdot V_s = P_{im} \cdot A \cdot L$$

### Step B — Number of power strokes per second
For $n$ cylinders running at $N$ rpm, with $k$ power strokes per cycle:
$$n_{\text{ps}} = \frac{N}{60} \cdot n \cdot k$$

### Step C — Indicated Power
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IP} &= W_{\text{cycle}} \times n_{\text{ps}} \\
&= \left(P_{im} \cdot A \cdot L\right) \cdot \frac{N \cdot n \cdot k}{60} \\
\text{IP} &= \frac{P_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k}{60} \quad \text{(Watts)}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Note (for KTU):** 1 kW = 1.341 HP. To convert IP from W to kW, divide by 1000.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mechanical Efficiency in Terms of MEPs

Mechanical efficiency is defined as the ratio of brake power (useful shaft output) to indicated power (total gas work):

$$\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}}$$

Substituting the MEP-based power expressions (from Step C above) and cancelling identical terms $\dfrac{L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k}{60}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{mech}} &= \frac{P_{bm} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k / 60}{P_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k / 60} \\
&= \frac{P_{bm}}{P_{im}}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{bm} = \eta_{\text{mech}} \cdot P_{im}$$

And the friction MEP follows directly from energy balance:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FP} &= \text{IP} - \text{BP} \\
P_{fm} \cdot \text{common factor} &= P_{im} \cdot \text{common factor} - P_{bm} \cdot \text{common factor} \\
P_{fm} &= P_{im} - P_{bm} = P_{im}(1 - \eta_{\text{mech}})
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Linking BMEP to Torque and Engine Geometry

Brake Power is also expressible through torque:
$$\text{BP} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$$

Equating both expressions for BP:

$$\frac{P_{bm} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k}{60} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$$

Cancelling $N/60$ from both sides and solving for torque:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{bm} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot k &= 2\pi T \\
T &= \frac{P_{bm} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot k}{2\pi}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **This is a KTU favourite formula** — when torque $T$ is given and we need to find BMEP (or vice-versa), this relation is used.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Setup

| Given | Find | Equation to Use |
|---|---|---|
| Work/cycle + Swept volume | MEP | $P_m = W/V_s$ |
| IP + $V_s$ + $N$ + $n$ + $k$ | $P_{im}$ | $P_{im} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \text{IP}}{V_s \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k}$ |
| BP + torque + $N$ | $P_{bm}$ | First find $V_s$ from $T$ equation, then back-solve |
| $P_{im}$ + $\eta_{\text{mech}}$ | $P_{bm}$ | $P_{bm} = \eta_{\text{mech}} \cdot P_{im}$ |
| IP + BP | FP, $P_{fm}$ | FP = IP − BP; then $P_{fm} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \text{FP}}{V_s \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — P–V Loop vs MEP Rectangle

This diagram illustrates how MEP converts the irregular P–V cycle into a rectangle of equivalent area (work).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Real P-V Cycle Area] -->|Equal Area| B[Fictitious MEP Rectangle]
    A2[Loop Shape: Varying P] --> A
    B2[Constant Height Pm] --> B
    B3[Base = Swept Volume Vs] --> B
    B --> C[Area = Pm x Vs = W_cycle]
    C --> D[Used to Compute IP, BP, FP]
    D --> E[IP / BP gives mechanical efficiency]
    D --> F[Friction Power FP = IP - BP]
    style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style C fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    style E fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207
    style F fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchy of MEP Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0[IMEP Pim] --> P1[Total indicated gas work per cycle per unit volume]
    P0 --> P2[From indicator diagram area Vs]
    P0 --> P3[IMEP = Wcycle / Vs]
    P3 --> Q1[Indicator Diagram Method]
    P3 --> Q2[Computed from P-V cycle area]

    P4[BMEP Pbm] --> P5[Brake work per cycle per unit volume]
    P4 --> P6[Pbm = Pm x eta_mech]
    P4 --> P7[From dynamometer BP and torque]

    P8[FMEP Pfm] --> P9[Friction loss per cycle per unit volume]
    P8 --> P10[Pfm = Pim - Pbm]
    P8 --> P11[Pfm = Pim x 1 minus eta_mech]

    P3 --> R1[Performance Metric]
    P4 --> R1
    P8 --> R1
    R1 --> R2[Compare engines of different sizes]

    style P0 fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1
    style P4 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    style P8 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style R1 fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Work-Energy Cascade of an Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GAS[Chemical Energy of Fuel] --> COMB[Combustion in Cylinder]
    COMB --> IP[Indicated Power IP]
    IP -->|Loss FP| BP[Brake Power BP]
    BP --> SHAFT[Output Shaft Work]
    IP --> IMEP[IMEP Pim]
    BP --> BMEP[BMEP Pbm]
    FP --> FMEP[FMEP Pfm]
    IMEP --> EQ[Pim = Pbm + Pfm]
    BMEP --> EQ
    FMEP --> EQ
    style IP fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e
    style BP fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style FP fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d
    style IMEP fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e
    style BMEP fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style FMEP fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Notice that the three MEPs flow **parallel** to their corresponding powers (IMEP↔IP, BMEP↔BP, FMEP↔FP). They are just **specific forms** (per unit swept volume) of the same quantities.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Mean Effective Pressure. Explain its significance as a performance parameter of an I.C. engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) is the **fictitious constant pressure** which, if maintained throughout the entire power stroke, would produce the same work as the actual variable pressure during the cycle. Mathematically:

$$P_m = \frac{\text{Net work done per cycle}}{\text{Swept volume } (V_s)}$$

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
1. It is a measure of the **work output per unit displacement volume**, allowing direct comparison of engines of different sizes.
2. It eliminates the effect of engine dimensions (bore, stroke, number of cylinders), making it a true **performance metric**.
3. It is the basis for calculating **indicated, brake, and friction power** of an engine.
4. Useful in **engine rating and design selection** for specific applications (e.g., aviation, marine, automotive).

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between IMEP, BMEP, and FMEP. Write the relation between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Full Form | Work Used | Relation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IMEP** | Indicated MEP | Gas/indicated work inside cylinder | $P_{im}$ |
| **BMEP** | Brake MEP | Brake (shaft) work | $P_{bm}$ |
| **FMEP** | Friction MEP | Friction loss work | $P_{fm}$ |

**Relation (2 marks):**

$$P_{im} = P_{bm} + P_{fm}$$

Also: $\eta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{P_{bm}}{P_{im}}$ and $P_{fm} = P_{im}(1 - \eta_{\text{mech}})$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes students make in MEP problems:
> 1. **Wrong stroke constant $k$** — using $k=1$ for 2-stroke engines. Verify by reading the question carefully.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — bore in mm, stroke in cm, pressure in bar — must convert **everything to SI** before substituting.
> 3. **Forgetting to divide by $n$** for multi-cylinder engines when MEP is asked *per cylinder*.
> 4. **Confusing kW with kJ/s** — both equal numerically, but $N \cdot m/s$ is W (Watts).
> 5. **Indicator Diagram sign** — area is *net* (work + pumping), not gross.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine has bore 80 mm and stroke 100 mm. The engine develops **20 kW** of brake power at **3000 rpm**. The indicated mean effective pressure is **8 bar**. Calculate:
- **(a)** The **brake mean effective pressure (BMEP)**, and
- **(b)** The **friction mean effective pressure (FMEP)** and **friction power**.

---

### **Solution to Question B-A:**

#### **Given Data**
- Type: 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine → $k = 1$
- Bore $D = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m
- Stroke $L = 100$ mm $= 0.10$ m
- Brake Power BP $= 20$ kW $= 20{,}000$ W
- Speed $N = 3000$ rpm
- $P_{im} = 8$ bar $= 8 \times 10^5$ N/m²
- $n = 4$ cylinders

#### **Part (a) — BMEP** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Swept volume per cylinder:**
$$V_s = A \cdot L = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.10 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange BP formula for BMEP:**

Starting from:

$$\text{BP} = \frac{P_{bm} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k}{60}$$

Solving for $P_{bm}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{bm} &= \frac{60 \cdot \text{BP}}{L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 20{,}000}{0.10 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 3000 \times 4 \times 1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator and Denominator:**
- Numerator $= 1{,}200{,}000$
- Denominator $= 0.10 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 12000 = 6.0318$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{bm} &= \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{6.0318} \\
&= 1.989 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
&\approx 1.989 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer (a): $P_{bm} \approx 1.99$ bar**

---

#### **Part (b) — FMEP and Friction Power** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Indicated Power first:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IP} &= \frac{P_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k}{60} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 10^5 \times 0.10 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 3000 \times 4 \times 1}{60} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 10^5 \times 6.0318}{60} \\
&= \frac{4.8254 \times 10^6}{60} \\
&= 80{,}424 \text{ W} = 80.42 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Friction Power:**
$$\text{FP} = \text{IP} - \text{BP} = 80.42 - 20 = 60.42 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — FMEP** (using same relation):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{fm} &= \frac{60 \cdot \text{FP}}{L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 60{,}424}{6.0318} \\
&= 6.011 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
&\approx 6.01 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:** $P_{im} = P_{bm} + P_{fm} = 1.99 + 6.01 = 8.00$ bar ✓

**Answers (b):** $P_{fm} \approx 6.01$ bar; Friction Power $\approx 60.42$ kW

**Mechanical Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}} = \frac{20}{80.42} = 0.2487 = 24.87\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** $\eta_{\text{mech}} \approx 25\%$ is unusually low — typical values are 80–90%. This means the IMEP given is for a **heavily loaded/fictitious** example purely for numerical practice.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Alternative]`

A 2-stroke, single-cylinder oil engine develops **7.5 kW** of indicated power at **1500 rpm**. The bore is **120 mm**, stroke is **140 mm**, and the mechanical efficiency is **80%**.
- **(a)** Determine the **Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP)** and **Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP)**.
- **(b)** Determine the **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption** if the engine consumes **2.5 kg/hr** of fuel. *(Bonus extension integrated with MEP-based BP derivation.)*

---

### **Solution to Question B-B:**

#### **Given Data**
- 2-stroke, single-cylinder → $k = \tfrac{1}{2}$, $n = 1$
- IP $= 7.5$ kW $= 7500$ W
- $N = 1500$ rpm
- Bore $D = 0.12$ m, Stroke $L = 0.14$ m
- $\eta_{\text{mech}} = 0.80$
- Fuel consumption $m_f = 2.5$ kg/hr

#### **Part (a) — IMEP and BMEP** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Piston area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.12)^2 = 0.011310 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Swept volume:**
$$V_s = A \cdot L = 0.011310 \times 0.14 = 1.5834 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging IP formula to solve for IMEP:**

$$\text{IP} = \frac{P_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k}{60}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{im} &= \frac{60 \cdot \text{IP}}{L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 7500}{0.14 \times 0.011310 \times 1500 \times 1 \times 0.5} \\
&= \frac{450{,}000}{1.1876} \\
&= 3.789 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
&\approx 3.79 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — BMEP from mechanical efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{bm} &= \eta_{\text{mech}} \cdot P_{im} \\
&= 0.80 \times 3.789 \\
&= 3.031 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answers (a):** $P_{im} \approx 3.79$ bar, $P_{bm} \approx 3.03$ bar

---

#### **Part (b) — Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Brake Power:**
$$\text{BP} = \eta_{\text{mech}} \times \text{IP} = 0.80 \times 7.5 = 6.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — BSFC formula:**
$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{m_f}{\text{BP}} \quad \text{(units: kg/kWh)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{BSFC} &= \frac{2.5 \text{ kg/hr}}{6.0 \text{ kW}} \\
&= 0.4167 \text{ kg/kWh} \\
&\approx 416.7 \text{ g/kWh}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer (b): BSFC $\approx 0.417$ kg/kWh or 416.7 g/kWh**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A well-designed 2-stroke oil engine has BSFC around 0.30–0.40 kg/kWh. Our value (0.417) is in the reasonable range — slightly higher, possibly indicating a smaller/low-speed engine.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise these 12 bullets before entering the KTU exam hall:**

- ☐ **MEP = W_cycle / V_s** — always the starting definition.
- ☐ MEP has the **same unit as pressure**: N/m² or **bar** (1 bar = 10⁵ N/m²).
- ☐ Three variants: **IMEP, BMEP, FMEP** — I for *inside*, B for *brake*, F for *friction*.
- ☐ **Cascade rule:** $P_{im} = P_{bm} + P_{fm}$.
- ☐ **Mechanical efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{mech}} = P_{bm} / P_{im} = \text{BP} / \text{IP}$.
- ☐ **Stroke constant $k$:** $k = 1$ for 4-stroke, $k = 1/2$ for 2-stroke.
- ☐ **Power formula:** $\text{Power} = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n \cdot k}{60}$ (in Watts).
- ☐ **Torque formula:** $T = \dfrac{P_{bm} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot k}{2\pi}$ (in N·m).
- ☐ **Swept volume:** $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ — *per cylinder*.
- ☐ **Conversion factors:** 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa; 1 kW = 1.341 HP; 1 N·m = 1 J.
- ☐ **Realistic MEP ranges:** SI engines ~8–12 bar; CI engines ~7–10 bar (NA), 15–20+ bar (turbo); F1 > 15 bar.
- ☐ **MEP is "fictitious"** — no part of the engine actually sees this constant pressure; it is purely a performance comparison tool.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Relative Efficiency ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Relative Efficiency** (also termed **Thermal Efficiency Ratio** or **Efficiency Ratio**) is a dimensionless performance metric in engineering thermodynamics that quantifies how closely an *actual* thermodynamic cycle or heat engine performs compared to a *theoretical ideal* (reversible) cycle operating between the same temperature limits. Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio of the **actual thermal efficiency** of a real engine/cycle to the **Carnot (reversible) thermal efficiency** operating between identical thermal reservoirs: $$\eta_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{rev}}}$$ where the **Carnot efficiency** is the maximum possible efficiency any heat engine can achieve between two given temperatures: $$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_2}{T_1} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Relative Efficiency is the standard benchmark used by examiners to evaluate the performance of all practical thermodynamic cycles — Otto, Diesel, Dual, Brayton, and Rankine — against the reversible Carnot standard. Always appearing in numerical problems where the student must compute cycle efficiency and compare it to Carnot. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two students taking the same exam. - **Student A (Carnot Engine)** is a perfect, idealized student who scores **100 out of 100** with no careless mistakes, no wasted energy, and works under perfectly reversible conditions. - **Student B (Actual Engine)** is a real student who scores, say, **35 out of 100**. The *absolute* score of Student B (35) tells you his knowledge. But the **Relative Efficiency** (35 / 100 = 0.35 or 35%) tells you **how close he came to perfection**. It answers the question: *"Out of the maximum possible marks, what fraction did he actually secure?"* In a thermodynamic engine: - **Friction, heat loss, incomplete combustion, finite-time processes, and irreversibilities** are the "careless mistakes" that lower real performance. - **Relative Efficiency** tells the engineer: *"Your engine is achieving X% of the theoretical perfection."* A well-designed modern gasoline engine might have: - $\eta_{\text{actual}} \approx 35\%$ - $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \approx 60\%$ - $\eta_{\text{rel}} \approx 58.3\%$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Bounds:** > - $0 \leq \eta_{\text{rel}} \leq 1$ (or $0\%$ to $100\%$) > - A value of **1.0 (100%)** would mean the actual engine IS the Carnot engine — impossible in reality due to irreversibilities. > - The closer $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ is to 1, the better the engine design. --- ### 1.3 Physical Significance in Engineering | Domain | Why Relative Efficiency Matters | |---|---| | **Internal Combustion Engines** | Benchmarks Otto/Diesel cycles against Carnot to assess design quality | | **Steam Power Plants** | Compares Rankine cycle performance to reversible Rankine | | **Gas Turbines** | Evaluates Brayton cycle losses relative to ideal Brayton and Carnot | | **Refrigeration & HVAC** | Compares actual COP to Carnot COP | | **Combined Cycle Plants** | Used to assess overall plant efficiency vs. theoretical maximum | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Does It Exist? The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (Kelvin-Planck statement) dictates that *no heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible (Carnot) engine operating between the same reservoirs*. This establishes Carnot efficiency as the **universal upper bound**. Real engines suffer from multiple irreversibilities: - **Heat transfer across finite temperature differences** - **Friction in moving parts** (piston rings, bearings) - **Unburned fuel losses** - **Pressure drop in pipelines and valves** - **Throttling and mixing losses** Relative Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{rel}}$) is therefore a **design quality index** — it tells the engineer how much of the theoretical maximum has been "captured" by the actual machine. ### 2.2 The Logic Chain of Computation **Step 1 — Compute the actual thermal efficiency** of the given cycle using the first law and cycle analysis: $$\eta_{\text{actual}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{supplied}}} = \frac{Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Carnot efficiency** using the source ($T_H$) and sink ($T_L$) temperatures of the cycle: $$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ > [!NOTE] > For KTU problems, $T_H$ and $T_L$ are usually given directly, OR they must be inferred from the highest and lowest cycle temperatures. Always use **absolute (Kelvin)** scale. **Step 3 — Compute the relative efficiency**: $$\eta_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret**: $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ closer to 1 = better design. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$ | Core definition of relative efficiency | Dimensionless ($\vert 0,1 \mid$) | | 2 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Reversible (ideal) efficiency between two reservoirs | Temperatures in **Kelvin (K)** | | 3 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{min}}}{T_{\text{max}}}$ | Same as above using min/max cycle temperatures | K only | | 4 | $\eta_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | First-law efficiency of any cycle | Dimensionless | | 5 | $\eta_{\text{rel}}(\%) = \eta_{\text{rel}} \times 100$ | Percentage form for reporting | Percentage | | 6 | $\text{Efficiency Deficit} = 1 - \eta_{\text{rel}}$ | Fraction of "lost" potential | Indicates irreversibility | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Rule:** Never confuse **Relative Efficiency** with **Isentropic Efficiency** or **Mechanical Efficiency**. They are distinct concepts: > - **Relative Efficiency**: actual cycle vs. Carnot cycle > - **Isentropic Efficiency**: actual device vs. ideal (frictionless) device > - **Mechanical Efficiency**: indicated work vs. brake work ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Engine Development**: Carmakers use $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ to track R&D progress year-over-year. - **Power Plant Audit**: Regulatory bodies require relative efficiency reporting. - **Educational Benchmark**: KTU examiners use it as the single most-tested performance metric. - **Design Optimization**: Higher $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ means less fuel burnt per kWh → lower emissions, lower cost. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: General Form of Relative Efficiency Starting from the **First Law for a cyclic heat engine**: $$W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}$$ Dividing both sides by $Q_{\text{in}}$: $$\frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$ This defines the **actual thermal efficiency**: $$\eta_{\text{actual}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$ For a **reversible (Carnot) engine** operating between the same two reservoirs, the heat ratio equals the temperature ratio: $$\left(\frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}\right)_{\text{rev}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ Therefore, the Carnot efficiency becomes: $$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ Now, dividing the two: $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{rel}} &= \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}}{1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}} \end{aligned}$$ This is the **master equation** the examiner expects in derivations. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 (Standard KTU Style) **Problem:** An engine operates between a source at **800 K** and a sink at **300 K**. It receives **1000 kJ** of heat and rejects **600 kJ**. Calculate: 1. Actual thermal efficiency 2. Carnot efficiency 3. Relative efficiency 4. Efficiency deficit **Solution:** **Step 1 — Actual efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{actual}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{600}{1000} = 0.40 = 40\%$$ **Step 2 — Carnot efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 = 62.5\%$$ **Step 3 — Relative efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{0.40}{0.625} = 0.64 = 64\%$$ **Step 4 — Efficiency deficit:** $$\text{Deficit} = 1 - \eta_{\text{rel}} = 1 - 0.64 = 0.36 = 36\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The actual engine captures only **64%** of the theoretically achievable efficiency. The remaining **36%** is destroyed by irreversibilities — this is the room available for engineering improvement. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 (Otto Cycle Context) **Problem:** An Otto cycle has compression ratio $r = 8$, with $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $T_2 = 700 \text{ K}$. Take $\gamma = 1.4$. For the air-standard cycle: - $T_3 = 1500 \text{ K}$, $T_4 = 700 \text{ K}$ (incorrect — recompute) - Actual: $T_3 = 1500 \text{ K}$, $T_4 = 750 \text{ K}$ Find relative efficiency if $T_H = 1500 \text{ K}$ and $T_L = 300 \text{ K}$. **Solution:** $$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.2974} = 1 - 0.4353 = 0.5647$$ $$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{300}{1500} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$$ $$\eta_{\text{rel}} = \frac{0.5647}{0.80} = 0.7059 = 70.59\%$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Patterns | Pattern | Given | Find | Key Formula | |---|---|---|---| | **Pattern 1** | $Q_{\text{in}}$, $Q_{\text{out}}$, $T_H$, $T_L$ | $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ | $\dfrac{1 - Q_{\text{out}}/Q_{\text{in}}}{1 - T_L/T_H}$ | | **Pattern 2** | Cycle-specific $\eta_{\text{actual}}$ formula + $T_H$, $T_L$ | $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ | $\dfrac{\eta_{\text{cycle}}}{1 - T_L/T_H}$ | | **Pattern 3** | $\eta_{\text{rel}}$, $T_H$, $T_L$ | $\eta_{\text{actual}}$ | $\eta_{\text{rel}} \times \eta_{\text{Carnot}}$ | | **Pattern 4** | $\eta_{\text{actual}}$ & $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ | New $T_L$ required | $T_L = T_H(1 - \eta_{\text{actual}}/\eta_{\text{rel}})$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Position of Relative Efficiency in the Performance Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A["THERMODYNAMIC PERFORMANCE METRICS"]:::title --> B["THEORETICAL UPPER BOUND"] A --> C["ACTUAL CYCLE METRICS"] A --> D["COMPARATIVE METRICS"] B --> B1["Carnot Efficiency
eta_C = 1 - TL/TH"] B --> B2["Reversible Cycle
Isentropic Devices"] C --> C1["Actual Thermal Efficiency
eta_act = W_net / Q_in"] C --> C2["Indicated / Brake Efficiency"] C --> C3["Volumetric / Combustion Efficiency"] D --> D1["Relative Efficiency
eta_rel = eta_act / eta_Carnot"] D --> D2["Isentropic Efficiency
actual / ideal device"] D --> D3["Overall / Plant Efficiency"] D1 --> E1["If eta_rel close to 1
Engine approaches perfection"] D1 --> E2["If eta_rel much less than 1
High irreversibility loss"] D1 --> E3["If eta_rel exceeds 1
IMPOSSIBLE - second law violated"] B1 -.provides upper bound.-> D1 C1 -.actual performance.-> D1 classDef title fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Computation Pipeline for $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ ```mermaid flowchart LR IN1["Cycle Data
Q_in, Q_out OR
Otto/Diesel/Brayton parameters"]:::input --> P1["Step 1: Compute
eta_actual = W_net / Q_in"] IN2["Temperature Limits
T_H, T_L in Kelvin"]:::input --> P2["Step 2: Compute
eta_Carnot = 1 - TL/TH"] P1 --> P3["Step 3: Divide
eta_rel = eta_actual / eta_Carnot"] P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4["Step 4: Check
0 less than eta_rel less than 1"] P4 --> P5["Step 5: Interpret
Design quality index"]:::output P5 --> OUT1["eta_rel near 1
Excellent design"] P5 --> OUT2["eta_rel moderate
Typical real engine"] P5 --> OUT3["eta_rel far from 1
High improvement potential"] classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef output fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Schematic: Real Engine vs. Carnot Engine ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CARNOT["CARNOT ENGINE (IDEAL)"] direction TB C1["Hot Reservoir
T_H = 1500 K"]:::hot -->|Q_in_carnot| C2["Reversible
Heat Engine"]:::ideal C2 -->|W_net_carnot| C3["Shaft Work
Maximum Possible"]:::work C2 -->|Q_out_carnot| C4["Cold Reservoir
T_L = 300 K"]:::cold C2 -.eta_Carnot = 80%.-> CMETA["eta_Carnot = 1 - TL/TH"] end subgraph ACTUAL["ACTUAL ENGINE (REAL)"] direction TB A1["Hot Source
T_H = 1500 K"]:::hot -->|Q_in_actual| A2["Real Engine
WITH FRICTION, HEAT LOSS,
FINITE TIME PROCESSES"]:::real A2 -->|W_net_actual| A3["Shaft Work
Less than Carnot"]:::work A2 -->|Q_out_actual
MORE than Carnot| A4["Cold Sink
T_L = 300 K"]:::cold A2 -.eta_actual = 35%.-> AMETA["eta_actual = W_net / Q_in"] end CMETA --> RATIO["eta_rel = eta_actual / eta_Carnot = 35 / 80 = 0.4375 = 43.75%"] AMETA --> RATIO classDef hot fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000 classDef cold fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef ideal fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000 classDef real fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef work fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define the term Relative Efficiency of a heat engine. What is its upper theoretical limit?* **Model Answer:** Relative efficiency is defined as the ratio of the actual thermal efficiency of a heat engine to the thermal efficiency of a reversible (Carnot) engine operating between the same temperature limits: $$\eta_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$$ Its upper theoretical limit is **unity (1.0 or 100%)**, which is approached only when the actual engine operates without any irreversibility (i.e., behaves like a Carnot engine). Since real engines always have irreversibilities such as friction and heat loss, $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ is always **less than 1**. **[3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for Carnot expression, 1 for limit]** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *A heat engine operates between temperature limits of 900 K and 300 K. If its actual efficiency is 30%, determine the relative efficiency of the engine.* **Model Answer:** Step 1 — Compute Carnot efficiency: $$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{900} = 0.6667 = 66.67\%$$ Step 2 — Compute relative efficiency: $$\eta_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{0.30}{0.6667} = 0.45 = 45\%$$ **Answer:** $\eta_{\text{rel}} = 45\%$ **[3 marks: 1 for Carnot, 1 for ratio, 1 for final answer with units]** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **(a)** Define Relative Efficiency. Explain its significance in evaluating the performance of a thermodynamic cycle. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A Carnot cycle operates between 1000 K and 300 K. An actual engine operating between the same temperature limits has an efficiency of 40%. Calculate the relative efficiency and the lost work potential per kJ of heat supplied. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution for (a):** **Definition:** Relative Efficiency is the ratio of actual thermal efficiency to Carnot (reversible) efficiency operating between the same two thermal reservoirs: $$\eta_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$$ **Significance:** 1. **Benchmarking tool:** It provides a normalized measure to compare engines of different sizes and types against the universal Carnot standard. 2. **Design quality indicator:** A high $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ (close to 1) indicates that the engine has minimal irreversibilities and is well-designed. 3. **Improvement potential:** The quantity $(1 - \eta_{\text{rel}})$ quantifies the fraction of available work that is being destroyed by irreversibilities — this directly points to the scope for R&D. 4. **Second-Law compliance:** Any $\eta_{\text{rel}} > 1$ would violate the Second Law; thus, $\eta_{\text{rel}} \leq 1$ acts as a self-check on calculations. 5. **Engineering decision-making:** Carmakers, power-plant designers, and aerospace engineers use $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ to select cycles, working fluids, and operating conditions. **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - $T_H = 1000 \text{ K}$, $T_L = 300 \text{ K}$ - $\eta_{\text{actual}} = 0.40$ - $Q_{\text{in}} = 1 \text{ kJ}$ (per unit basis) **Step 1 — Carnot efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{300}{1000} = 0.70 = 70\%$$ **Step 2 — Relative efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{rel}} = \frac{0.40}{0.70} = 0.5714 = 57.14\%$$ **Step 3 — Actual work output per kJ:** $$W_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{\text{actual}} \times Q_{\text{in}} = 0.40 \times 1 = 0.40 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum (Carnot) work output per kJ:** $$W_{\text{Carnot}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \times Q_{\text{in}} = 0.70 \times 1 = 0.70 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 5 — Lost work potential:** $$W_{\text{lost}} = W_{\text{Carnot}} - W_{\text{actual}} = 0.70 - 0.40 = 0.30 \text{ kJ}$$ **Final Answers:** $\eta_{\text{rel}} = 57.14\%$, Lost work = $0.30 \text{ kJ/kJ of heat supplied}$. **[14 marks distributed: 2 for definition, 3 for significance points, 3 for numerical setup, 2 for calculation, 2 for lost work interpretation]** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the concept of Relative Efficiency. Compare Relative Efficiency with Isentropic Efficiency. **[7 marks]** **(b)** An air-standard Otto cycle has a compression ratio of 9 and operates with $\gamma = 1.4$. The minimum and maximum cycle temperatures are 320 K and 1800 K respectively. Calculate: (i) Otto cycle efficiency, (ii) Carnot efficiency, (iii) Relative efficiency. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution for (a):** **Concept (with reference to schematic):** A heat engine receives heat $Q_H$ from a high-temperature source at $T_H$, produces work $W_{\text{net}}$, and rejects heat $Q_L$ to a sink at $T_L$. For the *ideal reversible* (Carnot) engine, the efficiency is the maximum possible. For the *actual* engine, efficiency is lower due to irreversibilities. **Relative Efficiency** is the ratio of the actual efficiency to this Carnot benchmark. **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Relative Efficiency | Isentropic Efficiency | |---|---|---| | **Compares** | Actual cycle vs. Carnot cycle | Actual device vs. ideal (isentropic) device | | **Applicable to** | Whole thermodynamic cycle | Individual device (turbine, compressor, nozzle) | | **Accounts for** | All cycle irreversibilities | Only internal irreversibilities of the device | | **Formula** | $\eta_{\text{act}} / \eta_{\text{Carnot}}$ | $\eta_{\text{actual-device}} / \eta_{\text{isentropic-device}}$ | | **Typical Use** | Otto/Diesel/Rankine cycle assessment | Turbine stage, compressor stage | **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - $r = 9$, $\gamma = 1.4$ - $T_{\text{min}} = T_L = 320 \text{ K}$ - $T_{\text{max}} = T_H = 1800 \text{ K}$ **(i) Otto cycle efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{9^{0.4}}$$ Compute $9^{0.4}$: $$\begin{aligned} 9^{0.4} &= e^{0.4 \ln 9} = e^{0.4 \times 2.1972} = e^{0.8789} = 2.4082 \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.4082} = 1 - 0.4152 = 0.5848 = 58.48\%$$ **(ii) Carnot efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{320}{1800} = 1 - 0.1778 = 0.8222 = 82.22\%$$ **(iii) Relative efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{Otto}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{0.5848}{0.8222} = 0.7112 = 71.12\%$$ **Final Answers:** $\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 58.48\%$, $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 82.22\%$, $\eta_{\text{rel}} = 71.12\%$. **[14 marks: 3 for sketch+concept, 2 for comparison table, 3 for Otto calc, 1 for Carnot, 1 for ratio, 1 for final answer]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Temperature Scale Trap (–3 marks):** Students often plug in Celsius values into $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - T_L/T_H$. This gives a completely wrong answer. **ALWAYS convert to Kelvin** ($T_K = T_C + 273.15$). > 2. **Reversed Ratio Trap (–2 marks):** A common mistake is $\eta_{\text{rel}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} / \eta_{\text{actual}}$, which gives values $> 1$ and violates the Second Law. Remember: actual is ALWAYS less than Carnot. > 3. **Unit Omission (–1 mark):** Always state $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ as a percentage (%) in the final answer for clarity. > 4. **Confusion with Isentropic Efficiency (–2 marks):** Do not use the isentropic efficiency formula ($\eta_{\text{isen}} = h_{\text{actual}} / h_{\text{isentropic}}$ for turbines) where relative efficiency is required. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Relative Efficiency = $\eta_{\text{actual}} / \eta_{\text{Carnot}}$** — the single most important formula in this topic. - Always use **Kelvin** temperatures when computing $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - T_L/T_H$. - $\eta_{\text{rel}}$ is a **dimensionless ratio**; report in percentage for clarity. - Bound: $0 < \eta_{\text{rel}} \leq 1$. A value $> 1$ indicates a calculation error. - It is **NOT** the same as Isentropic Efficiency, Mechanical Efficiency, or Combustion Efficiency. - Lost work potential = $W_{\text{Carnot}} - W_{\text{actual}} = (1 - \eta_{\text{rel}}) \times \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \times Q_{\text{in}}$. - The quantity $(1 - \eta_{\text{rel}})$ is called the **Efficiency Deficit** and represents the fraction of available work destroyed by irreversibilities. - Typical real-world values: IC engines $\eta_{\text{rel}} \approx 50-70\%$, large steam plants $\eta_{\text{rel}} \approx 60-80\%$. - In KTU exams, expect 14-mark Part B questions pairing relative efficiency with Otto/Diesel/Brayton cycle efficiency formulas.

topics.title: Refrigeration Systems - Unit of Refrigeration
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Refrigeration Systems - Unit of Refrigeration

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Refrigeration?

**Refrigeration** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the process of extracting heat from a low-temperature reservoir (cold body / refrigerated space) and rejecting it to a high-temperature reservoir (surroundings) with the help of an external work input, usually a compressor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Refrigeration is the process of removing heat from a substance or space to lower its temperature below that of the surrounding environment and maintaining it at a temperature lower than the surroundings.* — A continuous heat transfer from a lower-temperature body to a higher-temperature body requires external work, since heat does not naturally flow uphill (Clausius statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics).

The working substance used to absorb and transport this heat is called the **Refrigerant**. Common refrigerants include R-134a, R-410A, R-22 (legacy), ammonia (R-717), and CO₂ (R-744).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Brigade of Heat"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students (Plain English):**
> Imagine a fire fighter on top of a hill trying to send a bucket of water *up* to a tank on a higher hill. Water (heat) never flows up by itself. So we hire workers (the compressor) who carry the buckets of water (heat) uphill. Each worker consumes energy (work) to lift one bucket. The amount of heat removed per unit time is what we measure as the "**capacity of the refrigeration system**." That capacity, expressed in standard units, is called the **Unit of Refrigeration**.

In simple words:
- **Cold body** → releases heat → into the **refrigerant** (the "bucket").
- The refrigerant carries this heat → the **compressor** pumps it → the **condenser** dumps it → into the **atmosphere**.
- The amount of heat removed per minute, when expressed in a standard reference, gives the **unit of refrigeration**.

---

### 1.3 The Concept of "Unit of Refrigeration"

Since heat is invisible and cannot be weighed, engineers needed a **standard, universally agreed numerical value** to express "how much cooling" a refrigerator or an air-conditioner can do. This standard measure is the **Unit of Refrigeration**.

The standard unit, adopted internationally, is the **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Ton of Refrigeration (TR):**
> *The unit of refrigeration is defined as the rate of heat extraction (cooling effect) required to freeze ONE metric ton (1000 kg) of pure water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours.*

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Mass of water | **1 tonne = 1000 kg** |
| Time period | **24 hours = 1440 minutes** |
| Latent heat of fusion of water (at 0 °C) | **h_fg = 335 kJ/kg** (or 79.7 kcal/kg) |

Since this definition is based on how much heat a tonne of ice can absorb while melting (and a refrigeration system essentially reverses this melting by *removing* that much heat), the unit has historical ties to the use of natural ice in the 19th century.

---

### 1.4 Types of "Ton" — US Ton vs Metric Ton

There are **two commonly used definitions** depending on the geographic and academic convention:

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard / US / Commercial Ton of Refrigeration (TR):**
> $$1 \text{ TR} = 50 \, \frac{\text{kcal}}{\text{min}} = 3000 \, \frac{\text{kcal}}{\text{hr}} = 200 \, \frac{\text{BTU}}{\text{min}} = 12{,}000 \, \frac{\text{BTU}}{\text{hr}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Imperial / British Ton of Refrigeration:**
> $$1 \text{ Imperial TR} = 56 \, \frac{\text{BTU}}{\text{min}} = 3360 \, \frac{\text{BTU}}{\text{hr}}$$

The **SI / SI-derived value** of 1 TR in **kilowatts** is therefore:

$$1 \text{ TR} = 3.51685 \, \text{kW} \approx 3.517 \, \text{kW}$$

This conversion is obtained because $1 \text{ kcal} = 3.968 \text{ BTU}$ and $1 \text{ kW} = 860 \text{ kcal/hr}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Working Principle — Reverse Heat Engine

A refrigeration system is the **reverse of a heat engine**:

| Heat Engine | Refrigerator / Heat Pump |
|---|---|
| Heat taken from HOT source | Heat taken from COLD space |
| Converts part to WORK | WORK is INPUT |
| Rejects rest to COLD sink | Rejects (Heat + Work) to HOT sink |
| η = W/Q_H = 1 − Q_L/Q_H | COP = Q_L/W (refrigerator); COP = Q_H/W (heat pump) |

The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** mandates that work input is non-negotiable — you cannot move heat from cold to hot without external work (Clausius / Kelvin-Planck statements).

---

### 2.2 The Ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRS)

The most commonly analysed system in KTU 2024 examinations is the **Standard Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)**, consisting of four processes:

1. **Process 1 → 2 :** Isentropic compression (low-pressure vapour → high-pressure superheated vapour) in the **compressor**.
2. **Process 2 → 3 :** Isobaric heat rejection (high-pressure superheated vapour → saturated liquid) in the **condenser**.
3. **Process 3 → 4 :** Isenthalpic expansion (high-pressure liquid → low-pressure two-phase mixture) in the **throttling valve**.
4. **Process 4 → 1 :** Isobaric heat absorption (low-pressure two-phase mixture → low-pressure vapour) in the **evaporator**.

The **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** is the heat absorbed per kilogram of refrigerant in the evaporator:

$$\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$

The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is defined as:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{refrigerator}} = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

For a **Heat Pump**, COP is defined as:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{heat pump}} = \frac{Q_H}{W} = \frac{h_2 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key relationship to remember:**
> $$\text{COP}_{\text{heat pump}} = \text{COP}_{\text{refrigerator}} + 1$$
> This is derived directly from the First Law: $Q_H = Q_L + W$.

---

### 2.3 Refrigerant Mass Flow Rate (ṁ)

The mass of refrigerant flowing per second is determined by the load (in kW or TR) and the refrigerating effect:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{\text{Load (kW)}}{\text{RE (kJ/kg)}}$$

---

### 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Notes / KTU Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** | $h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed per kg of refrigerant in evaporator |
| 2 | **Compressor Work (W)** | $h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Specific work input (isentropic) |
| 3 | **Heat Rejected (Q_H)** | $h_2 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Condenser load per kg |
| 4 | **COP of Refrigerator** | $(h_1 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **COP of Heat Pump** | $(h_2 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$ | — | Always = COP_ref + 1 |
| 6 | **Refrigerant Mass Flow Rate** | $\dot{m} = \dot{Q}_L / (h_1 - h_4)$ | kg/s | Direct from load |
| 7 | **1 TR (Standard)** | $50 \text{ kcal/min} = 200 \text{ BTU/min} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | — | Most commonly used in KTU |
| 8 | **1 Imperial TR** | $56 \text{ BTU/min} = 3360 \text{ BTU/hr}$ | — | Less common, UK legacy |
| 9 | **1 TR in kW** | $\frac{200 \text{ BTU/min} \times 1055.06 \text{ J/BTU}}{60 \text{ s}} = 3516.85 \text{ W}$ | W | $\approx 3.517 \text{ kW}$ |
| 10 | **Heat load (Sensible)** | $\dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | kW | For cooling air/water |
| 11 | **Heat load (Latent)** | $\dot{m} \, h_{fg}$ | kW | For freezing / dehumidification |
| 12 | **Power Input to Compressor** | $P = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) / \eta_{\text{mech}}$ | kW | If isentropic efficiency given |
| 13 | **1 Horsepower (HP)** | $2545 \text{ BTU/hr} = 0.746 \text{ kW}$ | kW | Mechanical HP |
| 14 | **Carnot COP (refrigerator)** | $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ | — | Theoretical maximum |
| 15 | **Carnot COP (heat pump)** | $T_H / (T_H - T_L)$ | — | Theoretical maximum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for Vapour Compression Cycle:**
> - Compressor inlet: **saturated vapour** (or slightly superheated) → $s_1 = s_2$.
> - Throttle outlet: **two-phase mixture** → $h_3 = h_4$ (isenthalpic).
> - Evaporator & condenser: **constant pressure** processes.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Air-conditioning & HVAC systems**: capacity is quoted directly in TR (e.g., a "2-ton split AC" = 2 TR cooling).
- **Cold storage / food preservation**: tonnes of refrigeration determines how many tonnes of produce can be stored.
- **Industrial process cooling**: plastic injection moulding, chemical reactors.
- **Cryogenics & medical**: blood banks, vaccine storage.
- **Cascade / multi-stage systems**: used when temperature differences exceed what a single stage can handle.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why 1 TR = 3.517 kW

This is the single most important numerical derivation in the unit. The KTU board repeatedly asks students to prove or compute this value.

**Starting point — the definition of 1 TR:**

1 tonne of water (1000 kg) at 0 °C is to be converted to ice at 0 °C in 24 hours. The total heat to be removed is the latent heat of freezing.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total heat to be removed} &= m \times h_{fg} \\
&= 1000 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
&= 335{,}000 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

Next, the time period is converted to seconds so the rate is in kJ/s (= kW):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time} &= 24 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} \times 60 \text{ s/min} \\
&= 24 \times 3600 = 86{,}400 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the rate of heat removal (in kW) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_L &= \frac{335{,}000 \text{ kJ}}{86{,}400 \text{ s}} \\
&= 3.876 \text{ kW (using } h_{fg} = 335 \text{ kJ/kg)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on $h_{fg}$:** The exact latent heat of fusion of water at 0 °C is **335 kJ/kg** (≈ 79.7 kcal/kg). The 3.517 kW value arises when we instead use the older **1 TR = 50 kcal/min** definition (see derivation 3.2 below). The two definitions converge because the older definition was historically calibrated using $h_{fg} \approx 80$ kcal/kg with a 24-hour period and a mass of 1 tonne.

**Therefore, in standard engineering practice (using the kcal convention):**

$$1 \text{ TR} = 50 \text{ kcal/min} = 3.5169 \text{ kW} \approx 3.517 \text{ kW}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: 1 TR = 50 kcal/min and the SI conversion to kW

The **older but universally adopted** definition is:

$$1 \text{ TR} = \frac{m \cdot h_{fg}}{t} = \frac{1000 \times 80}{24 \times 60} \text{ kcal/min}$$

This assumes $h_{fg} = 80$ kcal/kg (the older thermodynamic value, still used for quick textbook calculations).

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ TR} &= \frac{80{,}000 \text{ kcal}}{1440 \text{ min}} \\
&= 55.555 \text{ kcal/min} \;\;\;\; \text{(using } h_{fg} = 80 \text{ kcal/kg)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **A common point of confusion in KTU answers:** The value of $h_{fg}$ used in the calculation changes the final result. Most KTU model solutions accept either 50 kcal/min (using $h_{fg} = 72$ kcal/kg or rounded) or 55.5 kcal/min (using $h_{fg} = 80$ kcal/kg). The **standard figure universally accepted in Indian engineering textbooks (P.K. Nag, R.K. Rajput)** is **1 TR = 50 kcal/min**, with the implicit $h_{fg}$ value of about **72 kcal/kg** used to fit the 24-hour ton-of-ice definition.

**Converting 50 kcal/min to kW:**

We know that:
$$1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ kJ} \quad ; \quad 1 \text{ min} = 60 \text{ s}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ TR} &= 50 \, \frac{\text{kcal}}{\text{min}} \times \frac{4.186 \text{ kJ}}{1 \text{ kcal}} \times \frac{1 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ s}} \\
&= \frac{50 \times 4.186}{60} \text{ kJ/s} \\
&= \frac{209.3}{60} \text{ kJ/s} \\
&= 3.488 \text{ kJ/s} \\
&= 3.488 \text{ kW} \;\;\;\; \text{(using } 1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ kJ)}
\end{aligned}
$$

If we instead use the more precise $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.1868 \text{ kJ}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ TR} &= \frac{50 \times 4.1868}{60} \\
&= \frac{209.34}{60} \\
&= 3.489 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Gold Standard" KTU Conversion:**
> The most common KTU-board-accepted conversion comes via the **BTU route**, which uses the precise international BTU definition:
> $$1 \text{ TR} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr} = 200 \text{ BTU/min}$$
> Using $1 \text{ BTU} = 1055.06 \text{ J}$:
> $$1 \text{ TR} = \frac{200 \times 1055.06}{60} = \frac{211{,}012}{60} = 3516.87 \text{ W} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$$

This is the value the KTU board uses in its official answer keys.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: COP Relationship Between Refrigerator and Heat Pump

Starting from the First Law applied to the cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_H &= Q_L + W \\
\Rightarrow W &= Q_H - Q_L
\end{aligned}
$$

The COP of a refrigerator is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_R &= \frac{Q_L}{W} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

The COP of a heat pump is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{HP} &= \frac{Q_H}{W} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding them:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_R + 1 &= \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} + 1 \\
&= \frac{Q_L + (Q_H - Q_L)}{Q_H - Q_L} \\
&= \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L} = \text{COP}_{HP}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Carnot COP for Refrigerator

For a **reversible (Carnot) refrigeration cycle** operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$ (absolute temperatures in Kelvin):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H} \\
\Rightarrow Q_H &= Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Work input:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= Q_H - Q_L = Q_L \left(\frac{T_H}{T_L} - 1\right) = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, R}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}
$$

Similarly:

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, HP}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical — Standard KTU Pattern

**Problem:** A vapour compression refrigeration system uses R-134a as refrigerant. The enthalpies at various points are:
- $h_1 = 247.23$ kJ/kg (saturated vapour at evaporator exit)
- $h_2 = 271.22$ kJ/kg (superheated vapour at compressor exit, isentropic)
- $h_3 = h_4 = 113.42$ kJ/kg (saturated liquid / throttled mixture)

The system has a cooling capacity of **10 TR**. Calculate:
1. Refrigerating Effect (RE)
2. COP of the refrigerator
3. Mass flow rate of refrigerant
4. Power required by the compressor

---

**Solution (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1: Refrigerating Effect (RE)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RE} &= h_1 - h_4 \\
&= 247.23 - 113.42 \\
&= 133.81 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: COP of the refrigerator**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP} &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \\
&= \frac{133.81}{271.22 - 247.23} \\
&= \frac{133.81}{23.99} \\
&= 5.578
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total cooling load in kW**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_L &= 10 \text{ TR} \times 3.517 \text{ kW/TR} \\
&= 35.17 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Mass flow rate of refrigerant**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{h_1 - h_4} \\
&= \frac{35.17 \text{ kW}}{133.81 \text{ kJ/kg}} \\
&= 0.2628 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compressor power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{W} &= \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) \\
&= 0.2628 \times 23.99 \\
&= 6.305 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (alternate route):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{W} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\text{COP}} = \frac{35.17}{5.578} = 6.305 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.6 Heat Load Calculation — Air-Conditioning Type Problem

**Problem:** A room contains 600 kg of air. The air is to be cooled from 35 °C to 25 °C. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. Calculate the heat to be removed in kJ and in TR·minutes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_L &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 600 \times 1.005 \times (35 - 25) \\
&= 600 \times 1.005 \times 10 \\
&= 6030 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to TR·minutes (1 TR·min = 50 kcal = 209.3 kJ):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TR·min} &= \frac{6030 \text{ kJ}}{209.3 \text{ kJ per TR·min}} \\
&= 28.81 \text{ TR·minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Architecture — Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (Block & P-H View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EVAP["Evaporator\nLow Pressure\nHeat Absorbed: QL"] -->|h1 saturated vapour| COMP["Compressor\nIsentropic Compression\nWork Input: W"]
    COMP -->|h2 superheated vapour| COND["Condenser\nHigh Pressure\nHeat Rejected: QH"]
    COND -->|h3 saturated liquid| THROTTLE["Throttle / Expansion Valve\nIsenthalpic\nh3 = h4"]
    THROTTLE -->|h4 two-phase mixture| EVAP
    COLD["Cold Space\n(Refrigerated Zone)"] -.->|QL pulled in| EVAP
    COND -.->|QH dumped out| HOT["Hot Surroundings\n(Atmosphere)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this schematic:**
> - The **Evaporator (EVAP)** pulls heat $Q_L$ from the cold space (left side).
> - The **Compressor (COMP)** consumes work $W$ to push the refrigerant to high pressure.
> - The **Condenser (COND)** dumps the combined heat $(Q_L + W)$ to the surroundings (right side).
> - The **Throttle (THROTTLE)** drops the pressure without any work exchange.

---

### 4.2 P-h Diagram of the Ideal Vapour Compression Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["State 1\nSat. Vapour\nP = Pevap\nh = h1, s = s1"] -->|Process 1 to 2\nIsentropic Compression\ns constant| B["State 2\nSuperheated Vapour\nP = Pcond\nh = h2"]
    B -->|Process 2 to 3\nIsobaric Heat Rejection\nP constant| C["State 3\nSat. Liquid\nP = Pcond\nh = h3"]
    C -->|Process 3 to 4\nIsenthalpic Throttling\nh constant| D["State 4\nTwo-Phase Mixture\nP = Pevap\nh = h4 = h3"]
    D -->|Process 4 to 1\nIsobaric Heat Absorption\nP constant| A
    subgraph Dome["Saturation Dome (P-h Chart)"]
        A
        B
        C
        D
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the P-h Chart (Mollier Diagram):**
> - The horizontal axis is **enthalpy (h) in kJ/kg**.
> - The vertical axis is **pressure (P)**, plotted on a logarithmic scale.
> - The dome is the saturation curve: left branch = saturated liquid, right branch = saturated vapour.
> - **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** is the horizontal distance $h_1 - h_4$ under the dome (inside the wet region).
> - **Compressor work** is the vertical rise from 1 to 2 (constant entropy).

---

### 4.3 Energy Flow Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT["Electrical / Mechanical Work Input\nW (kW)"] --> COMPnode["Compressor"]
    QLnode["Refrigeration Effect\nQL (kW) = 3.517 × TR"] --> OUT["Useful Cooling Output\nto Cold Space"]
    COMPnode -->|Energy added| QHnode["Heat Rejected to Surroundings\nQH = QL + W"]
    OUT --> QLnode
    QLnode -->|Sum with W| QHnode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Audit (First Law Compliance):**
> The First Law demands $Q_H = Q_L + W$ at all times. The refrigeration effect (cooling) cannot exceed the heat dumped at the condenser by more than the work input — this is a useful sanity check in numerical problems.

---

### 4.4 Hierarchy of Units — Conversion Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K["kW (SI base)"] -->|"divide by 3.517"| TR["Ton of Refrigeration (TR)"]
    TR -->|"multiply by 12,000"| BTU["BTU per hour"]
    TR -->|"multiply by 200"| BTUmin["BTU per minute"]
    TR -->|"multiply by 50"| KCAL["kcal per minute"]
    TR -->|"multiply by 3024"| KCALh["kcal per hour"]
    BTU -->|"divide by 3412.14"| HP["Mechanical Horsepower (HP)"]
    K -->|"multiply by 1.341"| HP
```
