## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
*Define the term "Ton of Refrigeration" and explain its significance.*

**Model Answer:**

> The **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** is the standard commercial unit used to express the cooling capacity of a refrigeration system. By definition, 1 TR is the rate of heat extraction required to freeze **one metric tonne (1000 kg) of pure water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**.

> Numerically,
> $$1 \text{ TR} = 50 \text{ kcal/min} = 200 \text{ BTU/min} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$$

> **Significance:**  
> (i) Provides a standardised way to compare cooling capacities of different refrigeration systems (e.g., a "1.5 TR split AC" delivers 5.275 kW of cooling).  
> (ii) Historically tied to the use of natural ice, it remains the universal industry benchmark for HVAC and cold-storage load ratings.  
> (iii) Simplifies the calculation of compressor sizing and refrigerant flow rates.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
*Differentiate between COP of a refrigerator and COP of a heat pump. Show the relationship between them.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Refrigerator | Heat Pump |
|---|---|---|
| **Desired Output** | Heat removed from cold space ($Q_L$) | Heat delivered to hot space ($Q_H$) |
| **Input** | Work ($W$) | Work ($W$) |
| **COP Formula** | $Q_L / W$ | $Q_H / W$ |
| **Application** | Air-conditioning, cold storage, food preservation | Space heating, water heating |
| **Range of Values** | Typically 2 to 6 | Typically 3 to 7 (always 1 higher than refrigerator) |

> **Relationship:** From the First Law, $Q_H = Q_L + W$, so:
> $$\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \text{COP}_{\text{R}} + 1$$

---

### 📘 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

## ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**  
*(a)* Derive an expression to show that **1 Ton of Refrigeration = 3.517 kW**. State clearly the assumption regarding the latent heat of water. **  [7 Marks]**

*(b)* A vapour compression refrigeration cycle uses R-134a. The enthalpies at the four cardinal points are: $h_1 = 240$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 265$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = h_4 = 110$ kJ/kg. If the system is rated at 5 TR, determine:  
 (i) Refrigerating Effect (RE) in kJ/kg  
 (ii) COP of the system  
 (iii) Mass flow rate of the refrigerant in kg/s  
 (iv) Power required by the compressor in kW **  [7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a): Derivation of 1 TR = 3.517 kW

**Step 1 — State the definition of 1 TR:**

> 1 TR is the heat removed to freeze 1 tonne (1000 kg) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours.

**Step 2 — Write the heat removed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_L &= m \times h_{fg} \\
&= 1000 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad (h_{fg} = 335 \text{ kJ/kg at 0°C}) \\
&= 3.35 \times 10^5 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Time conversion:**

$$
t = 24 \text{ hr} = 24 \times 3600 \text{ s} = 86{,}400 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Cooling rate (in kW):**

$$
\dot{Q}_L = \frac{Q_L}{t} = \frac{3.35 \times 10^5}{86{,}400} = 3.877 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — BTU-based definition (used in textbooks):**

> The standard textbook definition of 1 TR is:
> $$1 \text{ TR} = 200 \text{ BTU/min}$$

Using $1 \text{ BTU} = 1055.06 \text{ J}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ TR} &= \frac{200 \times 1055.06}{60} \text{ W} \\
&= \frac{2{,}11{,}012}{60} \text{ W} \\
&= 3516.87 \text{ W} \\
&\approx 3.517 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\therefore 1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** Latent heat of fusion of water at 0 °C is $h_{fg} = 335$ kJ/kg (equivalently, 1 TR is taken as 50 kcal/min, an empirical standard that yields the same 3.517 kW value).

---

#### Part (b): Refrigeration System Performance

**Given:** $h_1 = 240$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 265$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = h_4 = 110$ kJ/kg, Load = 5 TR

**(i) Refrigerating Effect:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RE} &= h_1 - h_4 \\
&= 240 - 110 \\
&= 130 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) COP of the system:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP} &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \\
&= \frac{130}{265 - 240} \\
&= \frac{130}{25} \\
&= 5.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate of refrigerant:**

First convert load: $\dot{Q}_L = 5 \times 3.517 = 17.585$ kW

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{17.585}{130} \\
&= 0.1353 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Compressor power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{W} &= \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) \\
&= 0.1353 \times 25 \\
&= 3.382 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $\dot{W} = \dot{Q}_L / \text{COP} = 17.585 / 5.2 = 3.382$ kW ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert TR to kW before computing ṁ. Always do this FIRST, otherwise the answer will be off by a factor of 3.5.
> - **Mistake 2:** Using $h_3$ in the throttling step instead of $h_4$. Remember: $h_3 = h_4$ (isenthalpic throttle). Writing $h_3 - h_4$ for RE is WRONG.
> - **Mistake 3:** Confusing COP_R and COP_HP. Use $Q_L / W$ for refrigerators, $Q_H / W$ for heat pumps.
> - **Mistake 4:** Failing to write units (kJ/kg, kW, kg/s) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 to 1 mark for missing units.

---

## ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**  
*(a)* A cold storage is required to maintain a temperature of $-10$ °C inside when the ambient temperature is $35$ °C. Assuming a Carnot refrigeration cycle, calculate the **COP of the refrigerator** and the **COP of the heat pump** that would operate between these limits. **  [7 Marks]**

*(b)* A refrigeration system of 20 TR capacity operates between an evaporator temperature of $-15$ °C and a condenser temperature of $40$ °C. The refrigerant used has the following properties (per kg):
- Refrigerating Effect: $120$ kJ/kg
- Compressor work input: $30$ kJ/kg
- Heat rejected: $150$ kJ/kg

Calculate:  
 (i) Mass flow rate of refrigerant  
 (ii) Power required by the compressor  
 (iii) Heat rejected by the condenser  
 (iv) COP and compare it with the theoretical Carnot COP **  [7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a): Carnot COP Calculations

**Given:** $T_L = -10 + 273 = 263$ K, $T_H = 35 + 273 = 308$ K

**COP of Carnot Refrigerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, R}} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \\
&= \frac{263}{308 - 263} \\
&= \frac{263}{45} \\
&= 5.844
\end{aligned}
$$

**COP of Carnot Heat Pump:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, HP}} &= \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} \\
&= \frac{308}{45} \\
&= 6.844
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \text{COP}_{\text{R}} + 1 = 5.844 + 1 = 6.844$ ✓

---

#### Part (b): Practical Refrigeration System Performance

**Given:** Capacity = 20 TR, RE = 120 kJ/kg, $W = 30$ kJ/kg, $Q_H = 150$ kJ/kg, $T_L = -15$ °C = 258 K, $T_H = 40$ °C = 313 K

**Step 1: Total cooling load:**

$$
\dot{Q}_L = 20 \times 3.517 = 70.34 \text{ kW}
$$

**(i) Mass flow rate of refrigerant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\text{RE}} = \frac{70.34}{120} \\
&= 0.5862 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Power required by the compressor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{W} &= \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1) \\
&= 0.5862 \times 30 \\
&= 17.59 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Heat rejected by the condenser:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_H &= \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_4) \\
&= 0.5862 \times 150 \\
&= 87.93 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (First Law):**

$$
\dot{Q}_L + \dot{W} = 70.34 + 17.59 = 87.93 \text{ kW} = \dot{Q}_H \quad \checkmark
$$

**(iv) Actual COP and Carnot COP comparison:**

Actual COP:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} &= \frac{\text{RE}}{W} = \frac{120}{30} = 4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Carnot COP:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, R}} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{258}{313 - 258} = \frac{258}{55} = 4.691
\end{aligned}
$$

**Relative efficiency (Second-Law efficiency):**

$$
\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{4.0}{4.691} = 0.8527 = 85.27\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to add 273 to convert °C → K. The Carnot COP formula is invalid with Celsius temperatures. This is the **#1 most common mistake** in KTU scripts and costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting the factor 3.517 in the TR-to-kW conversion. A 20 TR system is 70.34 kW, NOT 20 kW.
> - **Mistake 3:** Not stating the **Second Law efficiency** in part (b)(iv). KTU's revised Bloom's Taxonomy map rewards students who comment on *irreversibility* and the gap between actual and Carnot performance.
> - **Mistake 4:** Using $\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m} \times W$ (multiplied) instead of dividing. Read the question carefully — capacity is cooling, so the relevant energy is RE = $h_1 - h_4$.

---

## ⚡ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition of 1 TR:** Heat removed to freeze 1 tonne of water at 0 °C in 24 hours.
- **Master Conversion:** $1 \text{ TR} = 50 \text{ kcal/min} = 200 \text{ BTU/min} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$.
- **Alternative Conversions:** $1 \text{ HP} = 2545 \text{ BTU/hr} = 0.746 \text{ kW}$; $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ kJ}$; $1 \text{ BTU} = 1055.06 \text{ J}$.
- **VCRS Four Processes:** Compression (isentropic, 1→2), Condensation (isobaric, 2→3), Throttling (isenthalpic, 3→4), Evaporation (isobaric, 4→1).
- **Refrigerating Effect (RE):** Always $h_1 - h_4$ (NOT $h_1 - h_3$).
- **Compressor work:** $h_2 - h_1$ per kg; multiplies by ṁ to get total power.
- **COP_R = $Q_L / W$** and **COP_HP = $Q_H / W$**.
- **Master Relationship:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ (direct from $Q_H = Q_L + W$).
- **Carnot COP_R = $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$** — always use **absolute temperatures in Kelvin**.
- **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \dot{Q}_L / (h_1 - h_4)$. Always express $\dot{Q}_L$ in kW and RE in kJ/kg.
- **First Law check:** $\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}$ — use this as a verification step in every problem.
- **Two definitions of TR:** Metric/Standard TR (most common, 50 kcal/min) vs Imperial TR (UK, 56 BTU/min). KTU uses the Standard TR.
- **Second Law efficiency (Relative COP):** $\eta_{II} = \text{COP}_{actual} / \text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}$ — useful for design comparison.
- **P-h Diagram:** Know the location of the four cardinal points inside the saturation dome.
- **Refrigerants:** R-134a (current), R-410A, R-22 (legacy), ammonia (industrial), CO₂ (eco-friendly, transcritical).
- **Practical COP range:** Vapour compression systems usually 2.5 to 6; Carnot COP is the theoretical maximum.
- **Units discipline:** Always write kJ/kg, kW, kg/s; KTU deducts 0.5 mark for missing units in numericals.
- **Heat load equations:** Sensible: $m c_p \Delta T$; Latent: $m h_{fg}$.
- **Compressor isentropic efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{isen}} = (h_{2s} - h_1) / (h_{2a} - h_1)$ — a *favourite* KTU 14-mark variant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refrigeration Effect & C.O.P. Refrigerants - Classification of Refrigerants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Refrigeration Effect & C.O.P. Refrigerants - Classification of Refrigerants ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Refrigeration Effect (RE) > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *Refrigeration Effect (RE)* is the quantity of heat absorbed by the refrigerant from the cold space (evaporator) per unit mass during the evaporation process. It represents the actual cooling output produced by the refrigeration system and is the most important performance parameter for any vapor compression refrigeration cycle (VCRS). Mathematically, for a simple saturated vapor compression cycle operating on **1 kg of refrigerant**: $$RE = h_1 - h_4 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$ where: - **$h_1$** = Enthalpy of refrigerant vapor leaving the evaporator (saturated/superheated vapor) - **$h_4$** = Enthalpy of refrigerant leaving the expansion valve (saturated liquid, before evaporation) > [!IMPORTANT] > The Refrigeration Effect is **per-unit-mass**. To obtain the total cooling capacity, multiply by the mass flow rate of the refrigerant: > $$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m} \times (h_1 - h_4) \quad [\text{kW}]$$ --- ### 1.2 Ton of Refrigeration (TR) The commercial unit of refrigeration capacity is the **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)**. > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** 1 Ton of Refrigeration is the rate of heat removal required to freeze **1 ton (2000 lb = 907.185 kg)** of water at **0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**. $$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} = 210 \text{ kJ/min} = \frac{210}{60} \text{ kW} = 3.517 \text{ kW}}$$ --- ### 1.3 Coefficient of Performance (C.O.P.) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** The *Coefficient of Performance* is defined as the ratio of the desired effect (cooling or heating) to the work input supplied to the system. Unlike a heat engine's thermal efficiency ($\eta < 1$), the C.O.P. of a refrigerator or heat pump is always **greater than unity**. For a **Refrigerator (Cooling purpose):** $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ For a **Heat Pump (Heating purpose):** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$$ **Key Relationship:** $$COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1$$ This follows directly from the First Law applied to the cyclic device: $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$. --- ### 1.4 Refrigerant > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition:** A *refrigerant* is a working fluid that undergoes a phase-change cycle (typically liquid $\rightarrow$ vapor $\rightarrow$ liquid) to absorb heat at a low temperature from the refrigerated space and reject it at a higher temperature to the surroundings. The refrigerant is the *lifeblood* of any VCRS. --- ### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!TIP] > **The "Sweat Towel" Analogy:** Imagine a hot summer day. You soak a towel in cold water and hang it on a railing. The water in the towel absorbs heat from the air and evaporates, making the surrounding air feel cooler. The towel has essentially acted as a "refrigerant" — it absorbed heat (cooling effect) and changed phase (liquid $\rightarrow$ vapor). A mechanical refrigeration system does the **same thing**, but in a **closed loop** using a specialized fluid (refrigerant) like **R-134a** or **Ammonia (NH₃)** inside copper tubes, with a compressor forcing the cycle. Think of the C.O.P. like the **miles-per-gallon (kmpl)** of a car. Just as a higher kmpl means better fuel economy, a higher C.O.P. means more cooling per unit of electrical energy consumed. For a typical domestic refrigerator, $COP \approx 2$ to $3$, meaning for every **1 kW** of electrical input, you get **2–3 kW** of cooling. --- ### 1.6 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Remark | |---|---|---| | **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** | **3.517 kW** | Commercial cooling unit | | **1 kcal/hr** | **1.163 W** | CGS cooling unit | | **1 BTU/hr** | **0.293 W** | Imperial cooling unit | | **Latent heat of fusion of ice** | **335 kJ/kg** (≈ 80 kcal/kg) | Used in TR definition | | **Universal Gas Constant (R)** | **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** | For refrigerant gas calcs | | **Specific heat of water (c_p)** | **4.187 kJ/(kg·K)** | For secondary refrigerant calcs | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRS) The ideal saturated vapor compression cycle consists of **four processes**, forming a closed loop on a **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram** and a **Temperature-Entropy (T-s) diagram**: 1. **Process 1→2: Compression (Isentropic)** - Saturated vapor at evaporator pressure is compressed to condenser pressure. - Work input: $W_{comp} = h_2 - h_1$ - Entropy constant: $s_1 = s_2$ 2. **Process 2→3: Condensation (Isobaric Heat Rejection)** - Superheated vapor at point 2 rejects heat to the surroundings at constant pressure. - Heat rejected: $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ - Exit at point 3: Saturated liquid 3. **Process 3→4: Expansion (Throttling / Isenthalpic)** - High-pressure liquid is throttled to low evaporator pressure. - **Throttling law:** $h_3 = h_4$ (no work, no heat transfer) - This is **irreversible** (entropy increases: $s_4 > s_3$) 4. **Process 4→1: Evaporation (Isobaric Heat Absorption)** - Liquid-vapor mixture absorbs heat from the cold space. - **Refrigeration Effect:** $RE = h_1 - h_4$ - Exit at point 1: Saturated vapor > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why throttling and not an expander?** A throttling valve (capillary tube or expansion valve) is used because it is cheap, reliable, and handles two-phase flow well. An isentropic expander could theoretically produce work and improve C.O.P. by 5–10%, but is rarely used in practice due to cost and complexity with wet vapor. --- ### 2.2 Why C.O.P. > 1 is Possible (No Violation of Second Law) A student often wonders: *"How can we get more cooling than the work input?"* This is **not** a violation of the Second Law because: - The First Law for a cyclic device says: $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ - The C.O.P. is **not** an efficiency — it is a **performance ratio**. - Energy is still conserved ($Q_H$ rejected is greater than $W$ input because $Q_L$ is also added to the rejection side). - A heat pump delivering $COP = 4$ does **not** mean "free energy" — it means 1 kW of work plus 3 kW of absorbed ambient heat are delivered as 4 kW of heating. > [!TIP] > **Heat Pump vs. Refrigerator:** Same hardware, different purpose. A refrigerator's goal is to *cool* a refrigerated space (delivers $Q_L$). A heat pump's goal is to *heat* a building (delivers $Q_H = Q_L + W$). The hardware components are identical — only the location of the "useful" heat exchange is changed. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **complete formula set** required for Module 3 refrigeration problems. Memorize these for ESE. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Refrigeration Effect (per kg)** | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Cooling produced per kg refrigerant | | 2 | **Compressor Work (per kg)** | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Actual isentropic work input | | 3 | **Heat Rejected (per kg)** | $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | At condenser | | 4 | **C.O.P. of Refrigerator** | $COP_R = \dfrac{RE}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Dimensionless | Always $\geq 1$ | | 5 | **C.O.P. of Heat Pump** | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Dimensionless | Always $> 1$ | | 6 | **Relation** | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | — | Derives from $Q_H = Q_L + W$ | | 7 | **Tons of Refrigeration** | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | kW | Commercial unit | | 8 | **Total Cooling Load** | $\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m} \times (h_1 - h_4)$ | kW | Where $\dot{m}$ in kg/s | | 9 | **Mass Flow Rate** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_L}{RE}$ | kg/s | From capacity & RE | | 10 | **Power Required** | $\dot{W} = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Compressor shaft power | | 11 | **Carnot C.O.P. (Ref.)** | $COP_{Carnot,R} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless | Upper limit; T in K | | 12 | **Carnot C.O.P. (H.P.)** | $COP_{Carnot,HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless | Upper limit; T in K | | 13 | **Volumetric Capacity** | $q_v = v_1 \times (h_1 - h_4)$ | kJ/m³ | Important for compressor sizing | | 14 | **Throttling Process** | $h_3 = h_4$ | kJ/kg | Isenthalpic, irreversible | --- ### 2.4 Classification of Refrigerants > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Refrigerants are classified into **Primary** and **Secondary** refrigerants based on their role in the refrigeration system. #### A. Primary Refrigerants These fluids **directly** undergo the phase-change cycle in the evaporator and condenser. | Category | Examples | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Halogenated Compounds (CFCs)** | R-11, R-12, R-113 | Legacy systems (phased out) | | **Hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs)** | R-22 | Window ACs (being phased out) | | **Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)** | R-134a, R-404A, R-410A, R-407C | Modern domestic & commercial AC | | **Hydrocarbons (Natural)** | R-600a (Isobutane), R-290 (Propane) | Eco-friendly domestic fridges | | **Inorganic Compounds** | Ammonia (R-717), CO₂ (R-744), Water (R-718) | Industrial cold storage, ice rinks | | **Zeotropic Blends** | R-401A, R-401B | Replacement refrigerants | | **Azeotropic Blends** | R-500, R-502 | Legacy low-temp systems | > [!NOTE] > **The "R" Numbering System:** Refrigerants are designated with the letter "R" followed by a number. For example: > - **R-12**: $CCl_2F_2$ (Dichlorodifluoromethane) — a CFC > - **R-22**: $CHClF_2$ (Chlorodifluoromethane) — an HCFC > - **R-134a**: $CH_2FCF_3$ (Tetrafluoroethane) — an HFC > - **R-717**: Ammonia ($NH_3$) — inorganic > - **R-744**: Carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) — natural #### B. Secondary Refrigerants (Brines / Heat Transfer Fluids) These fluids **do not** undergo phase change. They are first cooled by the primary refrigerant in the evaporator and then circulated to absorb heat from the cold space. | Secondary Refrigerant | Composition | Freezing Point | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Water** | H₂O | 0 °C | Above-freezing cooling (chilled water AC) | | **Sodium Chloride Brine** | NaCl + Water | −21.1 °C | Cold storage, ice plants | | **Calcium Chloride Brine** | CaCl₂ + Water | −51 °C | Low-temp industrial refrigeration | | **Ethylene Glycol** | $C_2H_6O_2$ + Water | −51 °C (pure) | Chilled water, secondary loops | | **Propylene Glycol** | $C_3H_8O_2$ + Water | −60 °C | Food industry (non-toxic) | | **Methanol / Glycerol blends** | Alcohol + Water | Variable | Specialty low-temp | > [!TIP] > **Real-world use:** In a large cold storage warehouse, you wouldn't run ammonia lines into every aisle. Instead, ammonia cools a calcium chloride brine in a central chiller, and the cold brine is pumped to fan-coil units in the storage rooms. This is safer, more flexible, and isolates the toxic primary refrigerant in a single location. --- ### 2.5 Desirable Properties of an Ideal Refrigerant > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 syllabus: Student must be able to list and explain the desirable properties of an ideal refrigerant and classify them.** This is a high-yield topic for Part A (3-mark) questions. **Thermodynamic Properties:** - Low boiling point (lower than the desired refrigerated space temperature) - High latent heat of vaporization (more cooling per kg) - Low specific heat of liquid (less flash gas formation during throttling) - High specific heat of vapor (better heat transfer) - Low specific volume of vapor (smaller compressor displacement) - Low condensing pressure (lighter, cheaper compressor) - High evaporating pressure (above atmospheric to prevent air ingress) **Chemical/Physical Properties:** - Non-toxic and non-irritating - Non-flammable and non-explosive - Chemically stable (no decomposition in the cycle) - Non-corrosive to metals (especially copper and steel) - Miscible with lubricating oil (or separates easily — design-specific) - High thermal conductivity (better heat transfer) **Environmental Properties (Modern Priority):** - **Zero ODP** (Ozone Depletion Potential) — no chlorine or bromine - **Low GWP** (Global Warming Potential) — preferably below 150 - **Zero or low TEWI** (Total Equivalent Warming Impact) - **Long atmospheric lifetime** **Economic Properties:** - Cheap and readily available - Easy to detect leaks - Easy to recycle and reclaim --- ### 2.6 Environmental Impact Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential):** Measures a refrigerant's ability to destroy stratospheric ozone. **CFC-11 = 1.0** (reference). R-12 has ODP ≈ 0.82, R-22 has ODP ≈ 0.055, R-134a has ODP = 0. Ammonia has ODP = 0. > > **GWP (Global Warming Potential):** Measures contribution to global warming over 100 years relative to $CO_2$ (= 1.0). R-12 ≈ 10,900, R-22 ≈ 1,810, R-134a ≈ 1,430, Ammonia ≈ 0, $CO_2$ = 1. This led to the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** and **Kigali Amendment (2016)**, phasing out CFCs and HCFCs in favor of HFCs, HFOs (hydrofluoroolefins), and natural refrigerants. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: C.O.P. of a Refrigerator from First Principles **Starting Principle:** For a cyclic device operating in steady state, the **First Law of Thermodynamics** requires that the net energy in equals the net energy out. **Step 1: Energy balance on the cycle as a whole (control volume)** The cycle receives work $W_{net}$ from the compressor, absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir (at $T_L$), and rejects heat $Q_H$ to the hot reservoir (at $T_H$). $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$$ > **Logic:** Energy is conserved. Whatever the system absorbs ($Q_L$) plus whatever is pumped in ($W_{net}$) must be rejected ($Q_H$) — nothing accumulates in a cyclic steady-state device. **Step 2: Define the C.O.P. of a refrigerator** By definition, C.O.P. is the ratio of desired effect to work input. For a refrigerator, the desired effect is the heat removed from the cold space: $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$ **Step 3: Eliminate $Q_H$ from the energy balance** We do not need $Q_H$ in the C.O.P. expression for a refrigerator, so we leave it as is. **Step 4: Introduce the Second Law constraint (Carnot limit)** For a **reversible (Carnot) cycle** operating between two thermal reservoirs at $T_L$ and $T_H$ (absolute temperatures in Kelvin): $$\frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H}$$ This is the Clausius statement of the Second Law for a reversible cycle: total entropy change is zero. **Step 5: Express $W_{net}$ in terms of $Q_L$ and the temperatures** From the First Law: $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ Substituting the Second Law relation $Q_H = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}$: $$W_{net} = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L = Q_L \left(\frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}\right)$$ **Step 6: Substitute into the C.O.P. expression** $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \left(\frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}\right)}$$ $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ > **Logic:** This is the **maximum theoretical C.O.P.** — the Carnot limit. No real refrigerator can exceed this value. The smaller the temperature difference $(T_H - T_L)$, the higher the C.O.P. **Step 7: C.O.P. of Heat Pump by analogous derivation** By definition: $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}}{Q_L \left(\frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}\right)} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$ **Step 8: Relationship between the two C.O.P.s** $$\frac{COP_{HP}}{COP_{Ref}} = \frac{T_H / (T_H - T_L)}{T_L / (T_H - T_L)} = \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$ $$COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1$$ **Algebraic verification:** $$COP_{HP} - COP_{Ref} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} - \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H - T_L} = 1 \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Relation between C.O.P. and Refrigeration Effect for a VCRS **Given the four state points of an ideal VCRS:** | State | Location | Property | |---|---|---| | 1 | Evaporator exit / Compressor inlet | Saturated vapor | | 2 | Compressor exit / Condenser inlet | Superheated vapor | | 3 | Condenser exit / Expansion valve inlet | Saturated liquid | | 4 | Expansion valve exit / Evaporator inlet | Liquid + vapor mixture | **Step 1: Apply the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) to the compressor (1→2)** $$\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$$ Per unit mass: $$w_c = h_2 - h_1$$ **Step 2: Apply SFEE to the condenser (2→3)** $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3)$$ **Step 3: Apply SFEE to the expansion valve (3→4)** For a throttling device: no work, no heat transfer, negligible kinetic and potential energy changes: $$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{(Isenthalpic)}$$ **Step 4: Apply SFEE to the evaporator (4→1)** $$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$$ **Step 5: Substitute into the C.O.P. expression** $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L / \dot{m}}{\dot{W}_{comp} / \dot{m}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{RE}{w_c} \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** To maximize C.O.P., the designer should: > 1. **Maximize** $(h_1 - h_4)$ — choose a refrigerant with high latent heat and operate the evaporator at the highest feasible temperature. > 2. **Minimize** $(h_2 - h_1)$ — use a refrigerant with low specific heat of vapor, and reduce the compression ratio by selecting appropriate evaporating and condensing pressures. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Common Refrigerants | Refrigerant | Chemical Formula | Type | Boiling Pt (°C) | ODP | GWP (100-yr) | Toxicity | Flammability | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **R-12** | $CCl_2F_2$ | CFC | −29.8 | 0.82 | 10,900 | Low | None | Legacy (banned) | | **R-22** | $CHClF_2$ | HCFC | −40.8 | 0.055 | 1,810 | Low | None | Window AC (phasing out) | | **R-134a** | $CH_2FCF_3$ | HFC | −26.1 | 0 | 1,430 | Low | None | Domestic AC, car AC | | **R-410A** | R-32/R-125 blend | HFC blend | −51.4 | 0 | 2,088 | Low | None | Modern split AC | | **R-404A** | R-125/143a/134a blend | HFC blend | −46.5 | 0 | 3,922 | Low | None | Commercial low-temp refrigeration | | **R-600a** | Isobutane | Hydrocarbon | −11.7 | 0 | ~3 | Low | **Yes** | Eco-friendly domestic fridges | | **R-290** | Propane | Hydrocarbon | −42.1 | 0 | ~3 | Low | **Yes** | Commercial refrigeration | | **R-717** | Ammonia ($NH_3$) | Inorganic | −33.3 | 0 | 0 | **High** | **Yes** | Industrial cold storage, ice rinks | | **R-744** | $CO_2$ | Natural | −78.4 (sublimes) | 0 | 1 | Low | None | Transcritical systems, heat pumps | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Take-away:** There is **no perfect refrigerant**. Every choice involves trade-offs: > - **Ammonia** has the best thermodynamic performance and zero GWP/ODP, but is **toxic**. > - **HFCs** like R-134a are safe but have high GWP. > - **Hydrocarbons** like R-600a are eco-friendly and efficient, but are **flammable**. > - **$CO_2$ (R-744)** is making a comeback in transcritical systems for hot climates. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Ideal Vapor Compression Refrigeration System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Compressor
1 to 2
Isentropic Compression
W input] -->|Superheated Vapor
h2 high P| B[Condenser
2 to 3
Isobaric Heat Rejection
QH to surroundings] B -->|Saturated Liquid
h3 high P| C[Expansion Valve
3 to 4
Throttling Process
h3 equals h4] C -->|Two Phase Mixture
h4 low P| D[Evaporator
4 to 1
Isobaric Heat Absorption
QL from cold space] D -->|Saturated Vapor
h1 low P| A QL[QL Refrigeration Effect
h1 minus h4] -.-> D WN[Wnet Compressor Work
h2 minus h1] -.-> A QH[QH Heat Rejected
h2 minus h3] -.-> B ``` ### 4.2 P-h Diagram Schematic (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHD["PRESSURE ENTHALPY P-h DIAGRAM"] direction TB P1["State 1: Saturated Vapor at Low Pressure
h1 = 591 kJ/kg"] P2["State 2: Superheated Vapor at High Pressure
h2 = 638 kJ/kg"] P3["State 3: Saturated Liquid at High Pressure
h3 = 247 kJ/kg"] P4["State 4: Liquid Vapor Mix at Low Pressure
h4 = h3 = 247 kJ/kg"] P1 -->|1 to 2 Isentropic s constant| P2 P2 -->|2 to 3 Isobaric Condensation| P3 P3 -->|3 to 4 Throttling h constant| P4 P4 -->|4 to 1 Isobaric Evaporation| P1 end ``` ### 4.3 Classification Hierarchy of Refrigerants ```mermaid flowchart TB ROOT[REFRIGERANTS] --> PRIM[Primary Refrigerants] ROOT --> SEC[Secondary Refrigerants] PRIM --> HALO[Halogenated Compounds] PRIM --> NAT[Natural Compounds] PRIM --> BLEND[Blends] HALO --> CFC[CFC: R11 R12 R113] HALO --> HCFC[HCFC: R22] HALO --> HFC[HFC: R134a R410A R404A] HALO --> HFO[HFO: R1234yf R1234ze] NAT --> NH3[Ammonia R717] NAT --> CO2R[Carbon Dioxide R744] NAT --> H2O[Water R718] NAT --> PROP[Propane R290] NAT --> ISOB[Isobutane R600a] BLEND --> ZEO[Zeotropic: R401A R407C] BLEND --> AZEO[Azeotropic: R500 R502] SEC --> BRINE[Brines: NaCl CaCl2] SEC --> GLYCOL[Glycols: Ethylene Propylene] SEC --> WATER[Water Chilled] SEC --> ALC[Alcohol based: Methanol Glycerol] ``` ### 4.4 C.O.P. Comparison: Refrigerator vs. Heat Pump ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph REFRIG["REFRIGERATOR MODE"] R1[QL absorbed from cold space] --> R2[QL plus W equals QH] R2 --> R3[QH rejected to surroundings] R4[COP_R = QL over W] -.-> R1 end subgraph HEATP["HEAT PUMP MODE"] H1[QL absorbed from outdoors] --> H2[QL plus W equals QH] H2 --> H3[QH delivered to indoor space] H4[COP_HP = QH over W] -.-> H3 end REFRIG --- HEATP REL["COP_HP = COP_R + 1
QH equals QL + W by First Law"] -.-> REFRIG REL -.-> HEATP ``` ### 4.5 Schematic: Mass and Energy Flows in VCRS ```mermaid flowchart LR EVAP[EVAPORATOR
Pressure P_low
Temperature T_low] --|"RE = h1 - h4
QL = m_dot x RE"|--> COLD[Cold Space
T_L] COMP[COMPRESSOR
W_input
Shaft Power] --|"w = h2 - h1
Isentropic"|--> EVAP COND[CONDENSER
Pressure P_high
Temperature T_high] --|"QH = h2 - h3
Heat Out"|--> HOT[Hot Reservoir
Ambient T_H] EVAP -->|Saturated Vapor h1| COMP COMP -->|Superheated Vapor h2| COND COND -->|Saturated Liquid h3| VALVE[EXPANSION VALVE
h3 equals h4
Throttling] VALVE -->|Two Phase Mix h4| EVAP ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCMUT403]** > Define *Coefficient of Performance* of a refrigerator. Why is its value always greater than unity? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator** ($COP_R$) is defined as the ratio of the heat extracted from the cold space (refrigeration effect) to the work input supplied to the compressor: $$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$ It is always greater than unity because the heat extracted $Q_L$ is the **sum** of the work input and the heat absorbed from a colder external source (per the First Law: $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$). Numerically, the desired output $Q_L$ exceeds the work input $W$ alone, so the ratio $Q_L / W > 1$. A typical domestic refrigerator has a C.O.P. of 2 to 4. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCMUT403]** > Distinguish between **primary** and **secondary** refrigerants. Give two examples of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Primary Refrigerant | Secondary Refrigerant | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Undergoes phase change (evaporation/condensation) in the cycle | Does **not** undergo phase change; acts as a heat carrier | | **Role** | Absorbs heat directly from the space to be cooled | First cooled by primary refrigerant; then absorbs heat from the cold space | | **Examples** | Ammonia (R-717), R-12, R-134a, R-22 | Brine (NaCl solution), Ethylene glycol, Water | Primary refrigerants circulate through the compressor, condenser, and expansion valve. Secondary refrigerants (brines) are pumped through chilled coils in the cold space. --- ### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Attempt Either A or B** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCMUT403 Module 3]** > **(a)** A vapor compression refrigeration cycle uses **R-134a** as the refrigerant. The saturated vapor enters the compressor at **−10 °C** and is compressed isentropically to a condensing pressure of **1.0 MPa**. The refrigerant leaves the condenser as a saturated liquid. Determine: > 1. Refrigeration Effect (kJ/kg) > 2. Compressor Work (kJ/kg) > 3. C.O.P. of the refrigerator > 4. C.O.P. of the heat pump > 5. Mass flow rate of refrigerant for **5 TR** cooling capacity > > **(7 marks)** > **(b)** With the help of a **P-h diagram**, explain the working of an ideal vapor compression refrigeration cycle. Derive the expression for the **C.O.P. of a refrigerator** in terms of enthalpies. > **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1: Identify state points (from R-134a saturation tables)** **State 1** (Saturated vapor at $T_1 = -10°C$): - $P_1 = P_{sat} \text{ at } -10°C = 0.2006 \text{ MPa}$ - $h_1 = h_g \text{ at } -10°C = 392.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - $s_1 = s_g \text{ at } -10°C = 1.7334 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ **State 2** (Superheated vapor at $P_2 = 1.0 \text{ MPa}$, $s_2 = s_1 = 1.7334 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$): - From R-134a superheated tables at 1.0 MPa, $s = 1.7334$ corresponds to approximately $T_2 \approx 40°C$ - $h_2 \approx 422.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$ **State 3** (Saturated liquid at $P_3 = 1.0 \text{ MPa}$, $T_3 \approx 39.4°C$): - $h_3 = h_f \text{ at } 1.0 \text{ MPa} = 255.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$ **State 4** (After throttling, $h_4 = h_3$): - $h_4 = 255.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$ --- **Step 2: Calculate Refrigeration Effect (Part 1)** $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 392.7 - 255.55 = 137.15 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\boxed{RE \approx 137.15 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ --- **Step 3: Calculate Compressor Work (Part 2)** $$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 422.5 - 392.7 = 29.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\boxed{w_c \approx 29.8 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ --- **Step 4: Calculate C.O.P. of Refrigerator (Part 3)** $$COP_R = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{137.15}{29.8} = 4.603$$ $$\boxed{COP_R \approx 4.60}$$ --- **Step 5: Calculate C.O.P. of Heat Pump (Part 4)** $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 4.603 + 1 = 5.603$$ $$\boxed{COP_{HP} \approx 5.60}$$ **Verification using $Q_H$:** $$Q_H = h_2 - h_3 = 422.5 - 255.55 = 166.95 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{w_c} = \frac{166.95}{29.8} = 5.60 \quad \checkmark$$ --- **Step 6: Calculate Mass Flow Rate (Part 5)** Cooling capacity = 5 TR = $5 \times 3.517 = 17.585 \text{ kW}$ $$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{RE} = \frac{17.585}{137.15} = 0.1282 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 0.128 \text{ kg/s}}$$ --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Working of Ideal VCRS (with P-h diagram):** Refer to the P-h diagram in Section 4.2. The cycle has four processes: 1. **Compression (1→2):** Saturated vapor from evaporator is compressed isentropically in the compressor. Pressure rises from $P_L$ to $P_H$. The vapor becomes superheated. Work is consumed: $w_c = h_2 - h_1$. 2. **Condensation (2→3):** The superheated vapor at high pressure rejects heat to the surroundings (ambient at $T_H$) at constant pressure. It first desuperheats, then condenses to saturated liquid at state 3. Heat rejected: $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$. 3. **Expansion (3→4):** The saturated liquid is throttled through an expansion valve to the low evaporator pressure $P_L$. The process is isenthalpic: $h_4 = h_3$. Some liquid flashes into vapor (flash gas), producing a two-phase mixture. 4. **Evaporation (4→1):** The two-phase mixture absorbs heat from the cold space (at $T_L$) at constant low pressure. The liquid fully evaporates to saturated vapor at state 1. Heat absorbed: $Q_L = h_1 - h_4 = RE$. **Derivation of C.O.P.:** Applying the steady-flow energy equation to each component and using $h_3 = h_4$ (throttling): $$w_c = h_2 - h_1, \quad RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3$$ $$COP_R = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \quad \blacksquare$$ --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCMUT403 Module 3]** > **(a)** Define *refrigerant*. List the **desirable properties** of an ideal refrigerant. Classify refrigerants into **primary** and **secondary** categories, giving two examples of each. Discuss the environmental concerns associated with conventional refrigerants (CFCs and HCFCs) and the modern alternatives. > **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A refrigeration system has a **C.O.P. of 3.5** and operates between an evaporator temperature of **−5 °C** and a condenser temperature of **45 °C**. Determine: > 1. The **Carnot C.O.P.** for the same temperature limits. > 2. The **relative C.O.P.** (ratio of actual to Carnot). > 3. Comment on the **second-law efficiency** of the cycle. > **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer for (a):** **Definition of Refrigerant:** A refrigerant is a heat-transfer fluid that absorbs heat at low temperature by evaporating and rejects heat at high temperature by condensing, undergoing a cyclic phase change. It is the working fluid of a refrigeration system. **Desirable Properties of an Ideal Refrigerant:** *Thermodynamic:* - Low boiling point at atmospheric pressure - High latent heat of vaporization - Low specific heat of liquid - Low specific volume of vapor - High thermal conductivity - Low condensing pressure (reduces compressor cost) *Chemical/Physical:* - Non-toxic, non-flammable, non-explosive - Chemically stable (no decomposition) - Non-corrosive to metals - Miscible with lubricating oil (or easily separable) - Easy leak detection *Environmental:* - Zero ODP, low GWP - Long-term atmospheric stability - Easy to recycle **Classification:** | Type | Definition | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Primary** | Undergoes phase change; acts as the working fluid | R-12, R-22, R-134a, Ammonia (R-717), R-744 ($CO_2$) | | **Secondary** | Does not undergo phase change; absorbs heat from primary refrigerant and carries it to the cold space | Sodium chloride brine, Calcium chloride brine, Ethylene glycol | **Environmental Concerns:** **CFCs (e.g., R-12, R-11)** contain chlorine, which upon reaching the stratosphere breaks down the ozone layer. R-12 has an ODP of 0.82 and GWP of 10,900. Their production was banned under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)**. **HCFCs (e.g., R-22)** have a lower ODP (0.055) but still harm ozone. They are being phased out under the **Montreal Protocol** schedule (developed countries completed phase-out in 2020, developing countries by 2030–2040). **Modern Alternatives:** - **HFCs** (R-134a, R-410A): Zero ODP but high GWP (1,430 and 2,088 respectively) — regulated under the **Kigali Amendment (2016)**. - **HFOs** (R-1234yf, R-1234ze): Zero ODP and very low GWP (<1) — next-generation automotive AC. - **Natural refrigerants:** Ammonia (R-717), $CO_2$ (R-744), Hydrocarbons (R-290 propane, R-600a isobutane) — zero ODP and negligible GWP, but flammability and toxicity remain issues. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin** $$T_L = -5 + 273 = 268 \text{ K}$$ $$T_H = 45 + 273 = 318 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: Calculate Carnot C.O.P. (Refrigerator)** $$COP_{Carnot,R} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{268}{318 - 268} = \frac{268}{50}$$ $$\boxed{COP_{Carnot,R} = 5.36}$$ **Step 3: Calculate Relative C.O.P. (Second-Law Efficiency)** $$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} = \frac{3.5}{5.36} = 0.653$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{II} \approx 65.3\%}$$ **Step 4: Comment on Second-Law Efficiency** The actual cycle achieves **65.3% of the ideal Carnot performance**. This is a reasonable but not exceptional value — typical real-world VCRS systems operate at 50–70% of Carnot C.O.P. The remaining 34.7% represents losses due to: - Isentropic compression inefficiency (real compressor is not ideal) - Pressure drops in the condenser and evaporator - Heat transfer to/from surroundings through pipe insulation - Sub-cooling/superheating not at design points - Throttling irreversibility (replacing the throttle with an expander would gain 5–10% more). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** when applying the Carnot formula — this is the **#1 mistake** in refrigeration problems. $T_L$ and $T_H$ must be in **absolute temperature (K)**, not Celsius. > 2. **Mixing up states 1, 2, 3, 4** — always sketch the P-h or T-s diagram first and label the states. $h_1$ is **vapor leaving evaporator**, $h_2$ is **vapor leaving compressor**, $h_3$ is **liquid leaving condenser**, $h_4$ is **mixture leaving expansion valve** ($h_3 = h_4$). > 3. **Confusing Refrigeration Effect with Heat Rejected** — RE is $Q_L$ (cold side), not $Q_H$ (hot side). > 4. **Forgetting the "1 TR = 3.517 kW"** conversion when computing mass flow rate. > 5. **Not writing units** in the final answer — KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks for missing units. > 6. **Confusing C.O.P. of refrigerator and heat pump** — remember the +1 relation: $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Refrigeration Effect & C.O.P. + Classification of Refrigerants** - **Refrigeration Effect (RE)** = $h_1 - h_4$ — the cooling produced per kg of refrigerant in the evaporator. - **C.O.P. of Refrigerator** = $\dfrac{RE}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ — always $\geq 1$. - **C.O.P. of Heat Pump** = $\dfrac{Q_H}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1} = COP_R + 1$. - **Ton of Refrigeration**: $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 210 \text{ kJ/min}$ — the standard commercial cooling unit. - **Throttling process**: $h_3 = h_4$ (isenthalpic, irreversible). - **Carnot C.O.P. (Ref.)**: $\dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ — the upper theoretical limit; **always use Kelvin**. - **Mass flow rate**: $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_L}{RE}$ — needed to size compressors and piping. - **Volumetric capacity**: $q_v = v_1 \times (h_1 - h_4)$ — important for compressor displacement. - **Primary refrigerants** undergo phase change (e.g., R-134a, R-717, R-744). - **Secondary refrigerants** (brines, glycols) do not undergo phase change — they are heat carriers. - **CFC refrigerants** (R-12, R-11) have **high ODP** — banned under the Montreal Protocol. - **HCFC refrigerants** (R-22) have **low but non-zero ODP** — being phased out. - **HFC refrigerants** (R-134a, R-410A) have **zero ODP** but **high GWP** — regulated by the Kigali Amendment. - **Natural refrigerants** (NH₃, CO₂, hydrocarbons) have **zero ODP and negligible GWP** — the future of refrigeration. - **Ideal refrigerant properties**: low boiling point, high latent heat, non-toxic, non-flammable, non-corrosive, zero ODP, low GWP, cheap, chemically stable. - **Refrigeration cycle (VCRS) processes**: Compression (1→2, isentropic) → Condensation (2→3, isobaric) → Throttling (3→4, isenthalpic) → Evaporation (4→1, isobaric). - **Second-Law Efficiency** = $\dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}}$ — typically 50–70% for real VCRS. - **To increase C.O.P.**: raise $T_L$, lower $T_H$, use refrigerant with higher RE, lower compression ratio. - **Heat pump vs refrigerator**: same hardware; heat pump delivers $Q_H$ (heating), refrigerator delivers $Q_L$ (cooling). - **Key refrigerants to remember**: R-12 (CFC, legacy), R-22 (HCFC, phasing out), R-134a (HFC, current), R-410A (HFC, modern AC), R-717 (ammonia, industrial), R-744 (CO₂, transcritical), R-600a (isobutane, eco-friendly). > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU ESE, refrigeration problems nearly always give you **state-point enthalpies** (or ask you to read them from tables for a specified refrigerant). Always **sketch the P-h diagram first**, label the four states, identify the throttling relation ($h_3 = h_4$), and then apply the formulas from the cheat sheet in Section 2.3. Carry at least 4 decimal places in table lookups; rounding errors can lose 1–2 marks per part.

topics.title: Desirable Properties of Refrigerants. Refrigeration Cycles - Reversed Carnot Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Desirable Properties of Refrigerants. Refrigeration Cycles - Reversed Carnot Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Refrigerant?

A **refrigerant** is a working fluid that absorbs heat from a low-temperature region (the cold space) at low pressure and rejects that heat to a high-temperature region (the surroundings) at high pressure, while undergoing a cyclic phase change, typically liquid $\leftrightarrow$ vapor. The refrigerant is the *lifeblood* of any vapor-compression refrigeration system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Strict):** A refrigerant is a substance, usually a fluid, used in a heat pump and refrigeration cycle that undergoes a phase transition from liquid to gas and back, absorbing heat during evaporation (in the evaporator) and releasing it during condensation (in the condenser).

### 1.2 What is a Reversed Carnot Cycle?

The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is a *theoretical, ideal, completely reversible thermodynamic cycle* in which a working fluid operates between two thermal reservoirs. All four processes are internally and externally reversible. It is the most efficient refrigeration (or heat pump) cycle operating between two specified temperature limits and serves as the **theoretical benchmark** against which all real refrigeration cycles are compared.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Reversed"?** In the standard (power) Carnot cycle, heat flows from a hot source to a cold sink and work is *produced*. In the Reversed Carnot Cycle, the direction is opposite — work is *consumed* to move heat from a cold region to a hot region (against its natural gradient).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Refrigerant as a Water Bucket on a Rope"

Imagine a bucket of cold water tied to a rope, passing over a pulley above a hot ceiling.

- **Evaporator (Cold Space):** The cold bucket descends into a cold room and "soaks up" heat (just like refrigerant *evaporates* by absorbing latent heat).
- **Compressor (Pulling the rope up):** You pull the rope, doing **work**, lifting the now-warm bucket upward against gravity.
- **Condenser (Hot Space):** At the top, near a hot ceiling, the bucket releases the absorbed heat to the surroundings (refrigerant *condenses*, rejecting latent heat).
- **Expansion Valve (Lowering the bucket gently back down):** The bucket is lowered back, but its temperature is *reduced* (throttling effect), ready to absorb more heat.

The "refrigerant" is the bucket. The "work input" is the person pulling the rope. The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is the *most efficient possible way* to do this — no friction on the pulley, no splashing, no leakage.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Normal Boiling Point (NBP) | $^\circ$C @ 1 atm | Temperature at which refrigerant boils at atmospheric pressure |
| Critical Temperature ($T_c$) | $^\circ$C | Above this, gas cannot be liquefied by pressure alone |
| Critical Pressure ($P_c$) | bar or MPa | Pressure required to liquefy gas at $T_c$ |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization ($h_{fg}$) | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed per kg during evaporation |
| ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential) | dimensionless (R-11 = 1.0) | Relative ozone-layer damage |
| GWP (Global Warming Potential) | dimensionless (CO$_2$ = 1.0) | Relative global warming impact |
| COP | dimensionless | Coefficient of Performance |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Desirable (Ideal) Properties of Refrigerants — Complete Taxonomy

Refrigerant properties are grouped into **five major categories** for KTU exam purposes.

#### A. Thermodynamic Properties (Most Critical for KTU)

- **Low boiling point and low freezing point:** Evaporator pressure should be above atmospheric (to prevent air ingress) and freezing point must be below evaporator temperature.
- **High critical temperature and pressure:** Critical temperature must be *well above* the condenser temperature to allow condensation by ordinary cooling (air/water).
- **High latent heat of vaporization ($h_{fg}$):** More heat absorbed per kg $\rightarrow$ lower mass flow rate, smaller compressor.
- **Low specific heat of liquid ($c_{p,\text{liq}}$):** Reduces the amount of flash gas formed during throttling (improves cycle efficiency).
- **Low specific volume of vapor ($v_g$):** Reduces compressor displacement (smaller cylinder size).
- **High thermal conductivity of liquid:** Better heat transfer in evaporator and condenser.
- **Low viscosity (both liquid and vapor):** Reduces pumping work and pressure drops.

#### B. Chemical Properties

- **Chemical stability:** Must not decompose at high condenser temperatures or with lubricant.
- **Non-corrosive** to metals (copper, steel, aluminum) used in the system.
- **Non-flammable and non-explosive:** Critical for safety in domestic/industrial use.
- **Non-toxic:** Safe for humans, food-preservation, and occupied spaces.
- **Miscibility with lubricating oil:** Oil returns easily to compressor; no oil logging in evaporator.
- **No chemical reaction with moisture:** Moisture must not form acids or sludges.

#### C. Physical Properties

- **Low viscosity** (already mentioned) — affects heat transfer and pressure drop.
- **High dielectric strength** — important in hermetic compressors.
- **Low freezing point** — must remain liquid in the evaporator.
- **Small leakage tendency** — small molecular size, large molecular weight (paradox, but the molecular structure should resist leakage through joints).

#### D. Environmental Properties (Kigali Amendment / Montreal Protocol)

- **Zero ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential):** Required for all modern refrigerants.
- **Low GWP (Global Warming Potential):** Especially critical for direct-emission systems.
- **Short atmospheric lifetime** to limit long-term warming impact.
- **Zero or low TEWI** (Total Equivalent Warming Impact, including indirect $\text{CO}_2$ from energy use).

#### E. Operational / Practical Properties

- **Easy leak detection** (for toxic or flammable fluids).
- **Low cost** and wide availability.
- **No unpleasant odor.**
- **Non-hygroscopic** (does not absorb moisture readily).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question — "No single refrigerant is ideal":** In nature, **no fluid possesses all desirable properties simultaneously**. Hence, selection is always a *compromise* between thermodynamic performance, safety, and environmental impact. (e.g., Ammonia is excellent thermodynamically but toxic; $\text{CO}_2$ is environmentally friendly but has very high operating pressures.)

### 2.2 Classification of Refrigerants (Brief)

| Class | Examples | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CFCs** (Chlorofluorocarbons) | R-11, R-12 | Phased out (Montreal Protocol) — high ODP |
| **HCFCs** (Hydrochlorofluorocarbons) | R-22 | Being phased out — moderate ODP |
| **HFCs** (Hydrofluorocarbons) | R-134a, R-410A, R-404A | Zero ODP, but high GWP — transitional |
| **HFOs** (Hydrofluoroolefins) | R-1234yf, R-1234ze | Low GWP, future refrigerants |
| **Natural Refrigerants** | $\text{NH}_3$ (R-717), $\text{CO}_2$ (R-744), Propane (R-290), Water (R-718) | Zero ODP, low GWP; natural limitations |
| **Inorganic Compounds** | $\text{NH}_3$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{CO}_2$ | Brine/ice systems, industrial refrigeration |

### 2.3 Reversed Carnot Cycle — Process-by-Process Analysis

The cycle consists of **four reversible processes** operating between a low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$ and a high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$ (both in K).

**Process 1-2: Isentropic Compression** (Reversible Adiabatic)
- Working fluid in saturated vapor state is compressed isentropically.
- Pressure and temperature rise from $P_1, T_L$ to $P_2, T_2$.
- **No heat transfer** ($Q = 0$).
- Work done **on** the system (compressor work input): positive.

**Process 2-3: Isothermal Heat Rejection at $T_H$** (Reversible)
- Saturated vapor condenses to saturated liquid at constant high temperature $T_H$.
- Heat $Q_H$ is rejected to the hot reservoir.
- Most heat is rejected as latent heat of condensation.

**Process 3-4: Isentropic Expansion** (Reversible Adiabatic)
- Saturated liquid is expanded isentropically to the low pressure $P_1$.
- Temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$.
- **No heat transfer** ($Q = 0$).
- This is a *hypothetical isentropic turbine* (NOT a real throttling valve, which is irreversible).

**Process 4-1: Isothermal Heat Absorption at $T_L$** (Reversible)
- Liquid-vapor mixture absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir at constant $T_L$.
- Saturated mixture becomes saturated vapor — evaporation is complete.
- This is the **useful refrigeration effect**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Refrigeration Effect | $RE = Q_L = T_L (S_1 - S_4)$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed per kg at $T_L$ |
| 2 | Heat Rejected | $Q_H = T_H (S_2 - S_3)$ | kJ/kg | Heat rejected per kg at $T_H$ |
| 3 | Net Work Input | $W_{\text{net}} = Q_H - Q_L$ | kJ/kg | From 1st Law over cycle |
| 4 | Work (Compressor) | $W_C = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Isentropic process |
| 5 | Work (Turbine) | $W_T = h_3 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Isentropic expansion |
| 6 | **COP of Refrigerator** | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | – | Desired/Work |
| 7 | **COP of Heat Pump** | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | – | Desired/Work |
| 8 | Relationship | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ | – | Always true for any cycle |
| 9 | C.O.P. Carnot Heat Engine | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_H}$ | – | For comparison |
| 10 | Combined Identity | $\text{COP}_{HP} \cdot \text{COP}_R = \dfrac{T_H T_L}{(T_H - T_L)^2}$ | – | Useful identity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark this in your KTU register:** Temperatures in $\text{COP} = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ are **always in Kelvin (K)**. Forgetting this conversion is the #1 mark-loser in KTU exams.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Benchmark for real cycles:** The Reversed Carnot COP is the **upper theoretical limit**. Any real vapor-compression cycle is compared against it. (No real cycle can ever exceed Reversed Carnot COP operating between the same two reservoirs.)
- **Guides compressor and heat-exchanger design:** Sizing, selection, and energy efficiency ratings (EER, SEER) of refrigeration equipment are referenced to Carnot limits.
- **Drives the search for better refrigerants:** A refrigerant with a smaller temperature glide or higher $h_{fg}$ brings the actual cycle *closer* to the Carnot ideal.
- **Basis of thermodynamic optimization:** Used in pinch analysis, exergy analysis, and pinch-point studies of cryogenic, HVAC, and industrial refrigeration systems.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Coefficient of Performance of a Reversed Carnot Refrigerator

**Step 1 — Statement of the cycle (definition).**

A Reversed Carnot cycle operating between a low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$ and a high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$ consists of:
- Two isothermal processes (heat transfer at constant temperature).
- Two isentropic processes (reversible adiabatic — no entropy change).

**Step 2 — Heat transfer during isothermal processes (1-4 and 2-3).**

For a reversible isothermal process, the heat transferred is related to the change in entropy by:

$$
Q = T \cdot \Delta S
$$

For Process 4-1 (isothermal heat absorption at $T_L$):

$$
Q_L = T_L \cdot (S_1 - S_4)
$$

For Process 2-3 (isothermal heat rejection at $T_H$):

$$
Q_H = T_H \cdot (S_2 - S_3)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply entropy conservation (isentropic processes).**

For Process 1-2 (isentropic compression): $S_1 = S_2$.

For Process 3-4 (isentropic expansion): $S_3 = S_4$.

Therefore, the entropy changes are equal in magnitude:

$$
S_1 - S_4 = S_2 - S_3 = \Delta S \quad \text{(say)}
$$

**Step 4 — Express heat transfers in terms of $\Delta S$.**

$$
Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta S
$$

$$
Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics (steady-state cycle).**

The net work input equals the net heat transfer (with sign convention: work input is positive, $Q_L$ in, $Q_H$ out):

$$
W_{\text{net}} = Q_H - Q_L
$$

Substituting the expressions from Step 4:

$$
W_{\text{net}} = T_H \cdot \Delta S - T_L \cdot \Delta S = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S
$$

**Step 6 — Define COP of refrigerator.**

By definition, the Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator is the ratio of desired effect (heat removed) to the work input:

$$
\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute.**

$$
\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta S}{(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S}
$$

The $\Delta S$ cancels:

$$
\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

**Final Result (Box It):**

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coefficient of Performance of a Reversed Carnot Heat Pump

**Step 1 — Definition of heat-pump COP.**

The desired effect for a heat pump is the heat rejected at the high temperature (to warm a space):

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $Q_H$ and $W_{\text{net}}$ from above.**

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{T_H \cdot \Delta S}{(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$

**Step 1 — Write both expressions.**

$$
\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}
$$

**Step 2 — Add $1$ to $\text{COP}_R$.**

$$
\text{COP}_R + 1 = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} + 1 = \frac{T_L + (T_H - T_L)}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the right-hand side.**

$$
\text{COP}_R + 1 = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \text{COP}_{HP}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This identity is **universal** for *any* refrigeration cycle (not just Carnot), as long as the same two reservoir temperatures and the same work input are considered. It comes directly from the First Law and the definition of COP.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Input Expression for a Given Cooling Load

**Step 1 — Required refrigeration capacity.**

Let $\dot{Q}_L$ be the required rate of heat removal (kW). The refrigerant mass flow rate is:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{q_L} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{h_1 - h_4}
$$

where $h_1 - h_4$ is the specific refrigeration effect (kJ/kg).

**Step 2 — Power input to compressor.**

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{in}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \dot{Q}_L \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat rejected rate.**

$$
\dot{Q}_H = \dot{W}_{\text{in}} + \dot{Q}_L = \dot{Q}_L \left(1 + \frac{1}{\text{COP}_R}\right) = \dot{Q}_L \cdot \frac{\text{COP}_{HP}}{\text{COP}_R}
$$

### 3.5 Solved Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam style):** A Reversed Carnot refrigeration cycle operates between a cold reservoir at $-10^\circ$C and a hot reservoir at $40^\circ$C. The refrigeration load is 5 kW. Determine:
> (a) COP of the refrigerator
> (b) COP of the heat pump
> (c) Power input to the compressor
> (d) Rate of heat rejected to the hot reservoir

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**

$$
T_L = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_H = 40 + 273 = 313 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute COP of refrigerator.**

$$
\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50} = 5.26
$$

**Step 3 — COP of heat pump.**

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1 = 5.26 + 1 = 6.26
$$

Or directly:

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{313}{50} = 6.26
$$

**Step 4 — Power input to compressor.**

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{in}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \frac{5}{5.26} = 0.9506 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Rate of heat rejected.**

$$
\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{\text{in}} = 5 + 0.9506 = 5.9506 \text{ kW}
$$

Verification using $\text{COP}_{HP}$:

$$
\dot{Q}_H = \dot{W}_{\text{in}} \times \text{COP}_{HP} = 0.9506 \times 6.26 = 5.951 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Reversed Carnot vs. Real Vapour Compression Cycle

| Property | Reversed Carnot Cycle | Real Vapour Compression Cycle |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Compression | Isentropic (1-2) | Isentropic (practically achievable) |
| Condensation | Isothermal at $T_H$ (2-3) | At variable $T$ from $T_2$ to $T_3$ (real gas) |
| Expansion | Isentropic in turbine (3-4) | Throttling valve (irreversible) |
| Evaporation | Isothermal at $T_L$ (4-1) | At variable $T$ in evaporator |
| Refrigerant inside | Wet mixture (2-phase throughout) | Superheated vapor, subcooled liquid |
| Internal reversibility | 100% | < 100% (friction, pressure drops) |
| COP | Maximum possible ($\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}$) | Always lower than $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}$ |
| Practical use | Theoretical benchmark only | Used in actual systems |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Punchline:** The Reversed Carnot cycle is **impossible to build in practice** because (1) isentropic expansion in a turbine is impractical (work output is tiny) and (2) two-phase compression causes compressor damage. Hence the *throttling valve* replaces the turbine, and the *saturated vapor* is superheated slightly before compression.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List any six desirable thermodynamic properties of an ideal refrigerant.**

**Model Answer (6 points for 3 marks):**

1. **Low boiling point** at atmospheric pressure (so that evaporation occurs at desired low temperature).
2. **High critical temperature** (well above the condenser temperature) so that the refrigerant can be condensed.
3. **High latent heat of vaporization** ($h_{fg}$), so that a large amount of heat is absorbed per kg of refrigerant.
4. **Low specific heat of liquid** ($c_p$), so that the amount of flash gas formed during throttling is minimized.
5. **Low specific volume of saturated vapor** ($v_g$), to reduce the size of the compressor for a given capacity.
6. **High thermal conductivity** in both phases for better heat transfer in evaporator and condenser.

*(Any six from the full list given in Section 2.1 will be awarded full marks.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator and a heat pump. Show that $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
- **COP of a refrigerator:** $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}}$
- **COP of a heat pump:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net}}}$

**Proof:**

From the First Law for a cyclic device: $Q_H = Q_L + W_{\text{net}}$.

Therefore:

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net}}} = \frac{Q_L + W_{\text{net}}}{W_{\text{net}}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}} + 1 = \text{COP}_R + 1
$$

Hence proved. ∎

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **COP of a Reversed Carnot refrigeration cycle** operating between two thermal reservoirs at $T_L$ and $T_H$. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Reversed Carnot refrigerator is used to maintain a space at $-15^\circ$C when the ambient temperature is $35^\circ$C. The heat leakage into the cold space is 4 kW. Calculate: (i) the COP of the refrigerator, (ii) the power required by the compressor, and (iii) the rate of heat rejected to the surroundings. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. All four processes are internally and externally reversible.
2. Compression and expansion are isentropic (reversible adiabatic).
3. Heat addition and rejection occur isothermally (at constant $T_L$ and $T_H$ respectively).
4. The working fluid behaves ideally.
5. There is no change in kinetic or potential energy.
6. The cycle operates in steady state.

**Derivation:**

The four processes of the Reversed Carnot cycle on a T-S diagram are 1-2 (isentropic compression), 2-3 (isothermal heat rejection at $T_H$), 3-4 (isentropic expansion), and 4-1 (isothermal heat absorption at $T_L$).

Heat absorbed from the cold reservoir at $T_L$:

$$
Q_L = T_L (S_1 - S_4)
$$

Heat rejected to the hot reservoir at $T_H$:

$$
Q_H = T_H (S_2 - S_3)
$$

Since processes 1-2 and 3-4 are isentropic: $S_1 = S_2$ and $S_3 = S_4$.

Therefore, $S_1 - S_4 = S_2 - S_3 = \Delta S$ (say).

Net work input from First Law:

$$
W_{\text{net}} = Q_H - Q_L = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S
$$

COP of refrigerator:

$$
\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}} = \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta S}{(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}
$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures.**

$$
T_L = -15 + 273 = 258 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_H = 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — COP of the refrigerator.**

$$
\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{258}{308 - 258} = \frac{258}{50} = 5.16
$$

**Step 3 — Power required by the compressor.**

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{in}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \frac{4}{5.16} = 0.7752 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Rate of heat rejected to surroundings.**

$$
\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{\text{in}} = 4 + 0.7752 = 4.7752 \text{ kW}
$$

**Summary of Answers:**
- (i) $\text{COP}_R = 5.16$
- (ii) Power = $0.7752$ kW
- (iii) Heat rejected = $4.7752$ kW

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **desirable properties of refrigerants** under the categories: thermodynamic, chemical, physical, and environmental. Why is it impossible to find a single ideal refrigerant? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Reversed Carnot heat pump is used to heat a building. The indoor temperature is $22^\circ$C and the outdoor temperature is $2^\circ$C. The heat loss from the building is 8 kW. Determine: (i) the COP of the heat pump, (ii) the power consumed, and (iii) the total heat supplied to the building. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**1. Thermodynamic Properties (2 marks):**
- Low boiling point (at atm. pressure), low freezing point.
- High critical temperature and pressure.
- High latent heat of vaporization.
- Low specific heat of liquid.
- Low specific volume of vapor.
- High thermal conductivity.

**2. Chemical Properties (1.5 marks):**
- Chemical stability, non-corrosive to metals, non-flammable, non-toxic, miscible with lubricating oil, no reaction with moisture.

**3. Physical Properties (1.5 marks):**
- Low viscosity (both liquid and vapor), small leakage tendency, high dielectric strength, no unpleasant odor.

**4. Environmental Properties (1 mark):**
- Zero ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential), low GWP (Global Warming Potential), short atmospheric lifetime.

**5. Why no single ideal refrigerant (1 mark):**
- Trade-offs exist: refrigerants with excellent thermodynamic performance (e.g., $\text{NH}_3$) are toxic; environmentally friendly refrigerants (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$) operate at very high pressures. Hence selection is a *compromise* among thermodynamics, safety, cost, and environment.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**

$$
T_L = 2 + 273 = 275 \text{ K} \quad \text{(cold outdoor side)}
$$

$$
T_H = 22 + 273 = 295 \text{ K} \quad \text{(warm indoor side)}
$$

**Step 2 — COP of the heat pump.**

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{295}{295 - 275} = \frac{295}{20} = 14.75
$$

**Step 3 — Power consumed.**

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{in}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_H}{\text{COP}_{HP}} = \frac{8}{14.75} = 0.5424 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify using First Law and check rate of heat extracted from outside.**

$$
\dot{Q}_L = \dot{Q}_H - \dot{W}_{\text{in}} = 8 - 0.5424 = 7.4576 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_R = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{W}_{\text{in}}} = \frac{7.4576}{0.5424} = 13.75
$$

Check: $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1 = 13.75 + 1 = 14.75$ ✓

**Step 5 — Total heat supplied to the building (given as $\dot{Q}_H$):**

$$
\dot{Q}_H = 8 \text{ kW}
$$

**Summary of Answers:**
- (i) $\text{COP}_{HP} = 14.75$
- (ii) Power = $0.5424$ kW
- (iii) Heat supplied to building = $8$ kW

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Reversed Carnot Problems:**
>
> 1. **Temperature in °C instead of Kelvin** — *This alone costs 1 mark per question* in the final numerical answer. Always convert: $T(\text{K}) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273$.
> 2. **Confusing $\text{COP}_{HP}$ and $\text{COP}_R$** — If the question says "heat pump" the desired effect is $Q_H$ (heat delivered), not $Q_L$.
> 3. **Using $T_L = 0$ without conversion** when the cold space is at sub-zero temperatures.
> 4. **Forgetting that COP is dimensionless** — never write units for COP. (EER, on the other hand, has units of Btu/hr·W.)
> 5. **Drawing the cycle in the wrong direction on T-S diagram** — Reversed Carnot on T-S goes *counter-clockwise* (clockwise for power Carnot). The Examiner often gives marks for the T-S direction.
> 6. **Stating "isentropic turbine" is real** — it is purely hypothetical. Mention this to score the "comment" marks.
> 7. **Forgetting the assumptions** in the derivation question — the 1-mark assumption section can be the difference between full and partial credit.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Refrigerant** = working fluid that absorbs heat at low T/pressure and rejects it at high T/pressure via a phase change.
- ✅ **Desirable Properties** are grouped into **5 buckets**: Thermodynamic, Chemical, Physical, Environmental, Operational.
- ✅ **No single ideal refrigerant exists** — selection is always a *compromise*.
- ✅ **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is the **theoretical benchmark** (100% internally & externally reversible) for all refrigeration and heat-pump cycles.
- ✅ **Four Processes**: 1-2 Isentropic Compression, 2-3 Isothermal Heat Rejection ($T_H$), 3-4 Isentropic Expansion (hypothetical turbine), 4-1 Isothermal Heat Absorption ($T_L$).
- ✅ **Key Formula (Refrigerator):** $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$
- ✅ **Key Formula (Heat Pump):** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$
- ✅ **Universal Identity:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ (valid for *any* cycle, not just Carnot).
- ✅ **Temperatures must always be in Kelvin (K)** for all COP equations.
- ✅ **Work Input:** $W_{\text{net}} = Q_H - Q_L = \dot{Q}_L / \text{COP}_R = \dot{Q}_H / \text{COP}_{HP}$
- ✅ **Practical limitation:** The isentropic expansion turbine is replaced by a *throttling valve* in real cycles (irreversible), and the *saturated vapor* is superheated slightly before compression to avoid liquid droplets damaging the compressor.
- ✅ **Real cycles** have COP *lower* than the Reversed Carnot COP between the same two reservoirs — Reversed Carnot sets the *upper bound*.
- ✅ **Diagram must be drawn counter-clockwise on T-S** and **clockwise on P-V** for the Reversed Carnot cycle.
- ✅ **CFC → HCFC → HFC → HFO** is the historical evolution under the **Montreal Protocol** (1987) and **Kigali Amendment** (2016).
- ✅ **Modern refrigerants** (R-1234yf, R-1234ze, R-744, R-717, R-290) prioritize **low GWP** and **zero ODP**.
- ✅ **KTU Examiner Priority:** Be ready to (a) derive the COP, (b) list 6+ desirable properties, (c) solve a numerical problem with temperature conversion, and (d) draw the cycle on T-S and P-V.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reversed Joule Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reversed Joule Cycle ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reversed Joule Cycle (Reversed Brayton / Gas Refrigeration Cycle):** > A thermodynamic cycle in which a *gaseous working fluid* (typically air) undergoes two **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic) processes — compression and expansion — sandwiched between two **isobaric** (constant pressure) processes — heat rejection and heat absorption — to produce a refrigerating effect. It is the reverse of the Joule/Brayton power cycle and forms the theoretical foundation of all **gas refrigeration systems**, including aircraft air-conditioning plants. The four sequential states of the cycle are denoted **1 → 2 → 3 → 4**: | State | Description | Pressure Level | |-------|-------------|----------------| | **1** | Entry to compressor | Low | | **2** | Exit of compressor / Entry to cooler | High | | **3** | Exit of cooler / Entry to expander | High | | **4** | Exit of expander / Entry to cold space | Low | ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Air-Conditioned Blower Engine" > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy (Plain English):** > Picture the *refrigerator inside a jet aircraft*. Instead of using a chemical refrigerant (like in your home AC), the airplane simply uses **air itself** as the coolant. The system *squeezes* the air very hard (compressor), then lets it *cool down* against the outside air (cooler), then *lets it expand* rapidly (turbine) — and that expansion makes it **bitterly cold**, just like the cold air you feel when you spray an aerosol can. This chilled air is blown into the cabin, absorbs heat from the passengers, and the cycle repeats. That is exactly what a Reversed Joule Cycle does — it pumps *heat uphill* using only a gas (no phase change). ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Specific heat of air at constant pressure:** $c_p = 1.005 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$ *(standard atmospheric air)* - **Ratio of specific heats for air:** $\gamma = 1.4$ - **Gas constant for air:** $R = 0.287 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$ - **Pressure ratio range (typical aircraft AC):** $r_p = 3$ to $6$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Process-by-Process Breakdown The reversed Joule cycle consists of **four ideal processes**, traversed in the sequence 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 1: #### **Process 1 → 2 : Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic) Compression** - Working principle: The working fluid is compressed in a **compressor** from low pressure $P_1$ to high pressure $P_2$. - Why: To raise both temperature and pressure so the gas can later be cooled efficiently. - Energy equation: $q_{1-2} = 0$, hence $w_{c,\text{in}} = c_p(T_2 - T_1) > 0$. - Governing relation: $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$. #### **Process 2 → 3 : Constant Pressure (Isobaric) Heat Rejection** - Working principle: The hot, high-pressure gas flows through a **cooler/heat exchanger**, rejecting heat $q_{out}$ to the surroundings at ambient temperature. - Why: To remove the heat of compression *before* expansion, ensuring the gas is cold enough after expansion. - Pressure condition: $P_2 = P_3$. - Energy equation: $q_{2-3} = c_p(T_2 - T_3)$ (rejected to sink). #### **Process 3 → 4 : Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic) Expansion** - Working principle: The cooled, high-pressure gas expands in an **expander** (turbine or reciprocating expansion cylinder) to the low pressure $P_4$. - Why: Expansion produces a *very low* temperature $T_4$, lower than the refrigerated space temperature — this is the source of the refrigerating effect. - Energy equation: $q_{3-4} = 0$, hence work recovered $w_{t,\text{out}} = c_p(T_3 - T_4) > 0$. - Governing relation: $\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\dfrac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$. #### **Process 4 → 1 : Constant Pressure (Isobaric) Heat Absorption** - Working principle: The cold, low-pressure gas passes through the **refrigerated space (cold chamber)**, absorbing heat $q_{in}$ from it. - Why: This absorbed heat IS the *refrigerating effect* (RE). - Pressure condition: $P_4 = P_1$. - Energy equation: $q_{4-1} = c_p(T_1 - T_4) > 0$ = Refrigerating Effect. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below is the **complete closed-book reference** for any KTU 2024 problem on this topic. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in place of vertical pipe symbols if required by your note-taking software. | # | Quantity | Governing Equation | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|--------------------|-------|---------| | 1 | Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_3}{P_4}$ | dimensionless | Always greater than 1 | | 2 | Compression Temp. Ratio | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | dimensionless | Isentropic relation | | 3 | Expansion Temp.
