Ratio | $\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | dimensionless | Isentropic relation | | 4 | Key Equality | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{T_3}{T_4}$ | — | Because $P_2/P_1 = P_3/P_4$ | | 5 | Refrigerating Effect (per kg) | $RE = c_p(T_1 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed in cold space | | 6 | Heat Rejected (per kg) | $Q_{out} = c_p(T_2 - T_3)$ | kJ/kg | In the cooler | | 7 | Compressor Work (per kg) | $W_c = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Work input required | | 8 | Expander Work (per kg) | $W_t = c_p(T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Work recovered (output) | | 9 | Net Work (per kg) | $W_{net} = W_c - W_t$ | kJ/kg | Net external work input | | 10 | **COP of Refrigerator** | $\mathbf{COP} = \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}$ | dimensionless | Most important KTU result | | 11 | Back-work Ratio | $BWR = \dfrac{W_t}{W_c}$ | dimensionless | Useful for design | | 12 | Specific Volume at Inlet | $v_1 = \dfrac{RT_1}{P_1}$ | m³/kg | Used in sizing compressor | ### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Aviation Industry:** Cabin pressurization and air-conditioning in jet aircraft (e.g., the **bootstrap cycle**, **regenerative cycle**) are direct descendants of this cycle. Air is used because it is non-toxic, non-flammable, and freely available at altitude. > - **Gas Liquefaction:** Modified reversed Joule cycles form the basis of cryogenic processes (Linde-Hampson liquefier for air, nitrogen, oxygen). > - **High-Temperature Refrigeration:** Where conventional vapor compression fails (e.g., $> 0°C$ applications), gas cycles provide a viable alternative. > - **Turbine Inlet Cooling (TIC):** Gas-turbine power plants use reversed Joule cycle to pre-cool inlet air for power augmentation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fundamental COP Expression We start from the basic definition of the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** for a refrigerator: $$ COP = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} $$ **Step 1:** Substitute the heat and work expressions derived from the air-standard cycle: $$ COP = \frac{c_p(T_1 - T_4)}{c_p(T_2 - T_1) - c_p(T_3 - T_4)} $$ **Step 2:** Cancel the common $c_p$ factor: $$ COP = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)} $$ **Step 3:** Introduce the notation $\sigma = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ for compactness. From the isentropic relations: $$ \begin{aligned} T_2 &= \sigma \cdot T_1 \\ T_3 &= \sigma \cdot T_4 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute $T_2$ and $T_3$ into the numerator and denominator: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &\rightarrow T_1 - T_4 \\ \text{Denominator} &\rightarrow (\sigma T_1 - T_1) - (\sigma T_4 - T_4) \\ &= T_1(\sigma - 1) - T_4(\sigma - 1) \\ &= (T_1 - T_4)(\sigma - 1) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Cancel $(T_1 - T_4)$ from numerator and denominator: $$ COP = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_1 - T_4)(\sigma - 1)} = \frac{1}{\sigma - 1} $$ **Step 6:** Resubstitute $\sigma = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$: $$ \boxed{\;COP = \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}\;} $$ **Physical interpretation:** The COP depends *only* on the pressure ratio $r_p$ and $\gamma$. It is **independent** of the absolute temperatures of the heat reservoirs. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Alternate COP Form (Using Temperature Limits) For many board problems, $T_1$ and $T_3$ (the temperatures at compressor inlet and cooler outlet) are given directly. A more convenient form: **Step 1:** Use $T_2 = \sigma T_1$ and $T_4 = T_3 / \sigma$ where $\sigma = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{net} &= c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)] \\ &= c_p\left[\sigma T_1 - T_1 - T_3 + \frac{T_3}{\sigma}\right] \\ &= c_p\left[T_1(\sigma - 1) - \frac{T_3(\sigma - 1)}{\sigma}\right] \\ &= c_p(\sigma - 1)\left[T_1 - \frac{T_3}{\sigma}\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Refrigerating Effect: $$ RE = c_p(T_1 - T_4) = c_p\left[T_1 - \frac{T_3}{\sigma}\right] $$ **Step 3:** Take the ratio — the bracketed terms cancel: $$ COP = \frac{T_1 - T_3/\sigma}{(\sigma - 1)(T_1 - T_3/\sigma)} = \frac{1}{\sigma - 1} $$ This re-derivation confirms our earlier result. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dependence of COP on Pressure Ratio Differentiate $COP$ with respect to $r_p$: $$ \frac{d(COP)}{dr_p} = \frac{d}{dr_p}\left[\frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}\right] $$ $$ \frac{d(COP)}{dr_p} = -\frac{(\gamma-1)/\gamma \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma - 1}}{\left[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]^2} $$ Since $\gamma > 1$ and $r_p > 1$, both numerator and denominator are positive, so the derivative is **negative**: $$ \boxed{\;\frac{d(COP)}{dr_p} < 0\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** As pressure ratio $r_p$ increases, the COP **monotonically decreases**. However, the refrigerating effect per kg *increases*. A design trade-off is therefore essential in practical applications. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Solved Step-by-Step) **Problem Statement:** A refrigeration system operates on the Reversed Joule Cycle with air as the working fluid. The temperature of air entering the compressor is $T_1 = 270 \; \text{K}$, the temperature of air leaving the cooler is $T_3 = 300 \; \text{K}$, and the pressure ratio is $r_p = 5$. Assume air behaves as an ideal gas with $c_p = 1.005 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$ and $\gamma = 1.4$. Determine: **(a)** Temperatures $T_2$ and $T_4$ **(b)** Refrigerating effect per kg of air **(c)** Net work input per kg of air **(d)** COP of the cycle **Solution:** **Step (a):** Compute the temperature ratio across compressor and expander: $$ \sigma = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 5^{(1.4 - 1)/1.4} = 5^{0.2857} $$ $$ \sigma = 5^{0.2857} \approx 1.5838 $$ Temperature after compression: $$ T_2 = \sigma \cdot T_1 = 1.5838 \times 270 = 427.6 \; \text{K} $$ Temperature after expansion: $$ T_4 = \frac{T_3}{\sigma} = \frac{300}{1.5838} \approx 189.4 \; \text{K} $$ **Step (b):** Refrigerating Effect per kg: $$ RE = c_p(T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (270 - 189.4) $$ $$ \boxed{RE = 1.005 \times 80.6 \approx 81.0 \; \text{kJ/kg}} $$ **Step (c):** Net Work Input per kg: $$ W_c = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (427.6 - 270) = 1.005 \times 157.6 \approx 158.4 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$ $$ W_t = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (300 - 189.4) = 1.005 \times 110.6 \approx 111.2 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$ $$ W_{net} = W_c - W_t = 158.4 - 111.2 \approx 47.2 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$ $$ \boxed{W_{net} \approx 47.2 \; \text{kJ/kg}} $$ **Step (d):** COP of the Refrigerator: $$ COP = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{81.0}{47.2} \approx 1.716 $$ Verification using the compact formula: $$ COP = \frac{1}{\sigma - 1} = \frac{1}{1.5838 - 1} = \frac{1}{0.5838} \approx 1.713 \;\checkmark $$ > [!NOTE] > **Note:** The two values (1.716 and 1.713) differ only due to rounding of intermediate values. They confirm the derivation. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > The diagrams below are constructed using Mermaid (block-flow topology). Since standard T-s and P-v charts require physical axes not native to Mermaid, they are described in tabular form immediately following the block diagram. ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Gas Refrigeration Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR ST1(("State 1
LP Cold
T1, P1")) -->|Process 1 to 2
Isentropic Compression
WORK IN| CMP["Reciprocating or
Axial Compressor"] CMP -->|Hot HP Gas| ST2(("State 2
HP Hot
T2, P2")) ST2 -->|Process 2 to 3
Isobaric Cooling
HEAT OUT| COL["Heat Exchanger
or Ram-Air Cooler"] COL -->|Cool HP Gas| ST3(("State 3
HP Cool
T3, P3")) ST3 -->|Process 3 to 4
Isentropic Expansion
WORK OUT| EXP["Expansion Turbine
or Reciprocating Expander"] EXP -->|Cold LP Gas| ST4(("State 4
LP Very Cold
T4, P4")) ST4 -->|Process 4 to 1
Isobaric Heat Absorption
REFRIGERATING EFFECT| CS["Refrigerated
Cabin or Cold Chamber"] CS -->|Warm LP Gas| ST1 ``` ### 4.2 Tabular Description of the T–s Diagram The **T–s diagram** for the reversed Joule cycle consists of two isobars (horizontal-ish curves) joined by two isentropes (vertical lines), traversed **anti-clockwise**: | Curve Segment | Process Type | Direction in T-s Plane | Geometry | |---------------|--------------|------------------------|----------| | **1 → 2** | Isentropic compression | Vertical line, going **up** | Constant entropy, temperature rises from $T_1$ to $T_2$ | | **2 → 3** | Isobaric heat rejection | Curve going **down-right** | Constant pressure, temperature falls from $T_2$ to $T_3$, entropy decreases | | **3 → 4** | Isentropic expansion | Vertical line, going **down** | Constant entropy, temperature falls from $T_3$ to $T_4$ | | **4 → 1** | Isobaric heat absorption | Curve going **up-right** | Constant pressure, temperature rises from $T_4$ to $T_1$, entropy increases | > **Cycle orientation:** Traversed **anti-clockwise**, confirming its nature as a **refrigeration** (rather than power) cycle. The enclosed area represents the **net work input** $W_{net}$. ### 4.3 Tabular Description of the P–v Diagram | Curve Segment | Process Type | Direction in P-v Plane | Geometry | |---------------|--------------|------------------------|----------| | **1 → 2** | Isentropic compression | Curve going **up-left** (P rises, v falls) | $Pv^\gamma = \text{constant}$ | | **2 → 3** | Isobaric heat rejection | Horizontal line going **right** (v increases) | Constant pressure $P_2 = P_3$ | | **3 → 4** | Isentropic expansion | Curve going **down-right** (P falls, v rises) | $Pv^\gamma = \text{constant}$ | | **4 → 1** | Isobaric heat absorption | Horizontal line going **left** (v decreases) | Constant pressure $P_4 = P_1$ | ### 4.4 Conceptual State-Traversal Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HP["HIGH PRESSURE PLATEAU P2 = P3"] S2(["State 2: HOT
After Compressor"]) -->|Reject Q_out| S3(["State 3: COOL
After Cooler"]) end subgraph LP["LOW PRESSURE PLATEAU P1 = P4"] S4(["State 4: VERY COLD
After Expander"]) -->|Absorb Q_in = RE| S1(["State 1: COLD
After Cold Space"]) end S1 -->|Compress W_in| S2 S3 -->|Expand W_out| S4 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]` **Define the Reversed Joule Cycle. List its four ideal processes in sequence.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **Reversed Joule Cycle** is the reverse of the Joule (Brayton) power cycle and is used as an ideal gas refrigeration cycle. The four sequential processes are: > 1. **Process 1 → 2:** Reversible adiabatic (isentropic) **compression** in a compressor. > 2. **Process 2 → 3:** **Constant pressure heat rejection** in a cooler. > 3. **Process 3 → 4:** Reversible adiabatic (isentropic) **expansion** in an expander/turbine. > 4. **Process 4 → 1:** **Constant pressure heat absorption** in the refrigerated space. > > The cycle uses a gaseous working fluid (commonly air) and produces a refrigerating effect at state 4 by virtue of the expansion process. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]` **State the final expression for the COP of a Reversed Joule Cycle and comment on its dependence on the pressure ratio.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The COP of the Reversed Joule Cycle is given by: > $$COP = \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}$$ > where $r_p = P_2/P_1 = P_3/P_4$ is the pressure ratio and $\gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats of the working gas. > > **Dependence on $r_p$:** Since $r_p > 1$ and $\gamma > 1$, the term $r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ is always greater than 1, so $COP$ is positive. As $r_p$ increases, the denominator increases, so **COP decreases monotonically**. Hence, a higher pressure ratio reduces the cycle's thermodynamic efficiency, even though it increases the refrigerating effect per kg of air. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[INTERNAL CHOICE — Attempt either Q-A OR Q-B in full.]** --- ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]` — 14 Marks **(a)** Describe the Reversed Joule Cycle with the help of schematic and T–s / P–v diagrams. Derive the expression for the COP of the cycle. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Schematic:** (Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram.) The system consists of a compressor, cooler, expander and refrigerated space connected in a closed loop with air as the working fluid. **Step 2 — Process Description:** - Process 1-2: Isentropic compression raises $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ and $P_1 \rightarrow P_2$. - Process 2-3: Isobaric heat rejection $q_{out} = c_p(T_2 - T_3)$ at constant $P_2 = P_3$. - Process 3-4: Isentropic expansion lowers $T_3 \rightarrow T_4$ and $P_3 \rightarrow P_4$, producing the cold stream. - Process 4-1: Isobaric heat absorption $RE = c_p(T_1 - T_4)$ at constant $P_4 = P_1$. **Step 3 — COP Derivation:** (Complete derivation already shown in Section 3.1.) $$ COP = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{c_p(T_1 - T_4)}{c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]} $$ Substituting $T_2 = \sigma T_1$ and $T_3 = \sigma T_4$ with $\sigma = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$: $$ \boxed{COP = \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}} $$ **(b)** A Reversed Joule Cycle operates between temperature limits of $260 \; \text{K}$ (cold space inlet) and $310 \; \text{K}$ (cooler outlet). The pressure ratio is $4$. For air, take $c_p = 1.005 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$ and $\gamma = 1.4$. Determine: **(i)** Temperatures $T_2$ and $T_4$, **(ii)** Refrigerating effect per kg of air, **(iii)** Net work per kg of air, and **(iv)** COP of the cycle. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $T_1 = 260 \; \text{K}$, $T_3 = 310 \; \text{K}$, $r_p = 4$, $c_p = 1.005$, $\gamma = 1.4$. **(i)** Temperature ratio: $$ \sigma = 4^{(0.4/1.4)} = 4^{0.2857} \approx 1.486 $$ $$ T_2 = \sigma T_1 = 1.486 \times 260 = 386.4 \; \text{K} $$ $$ T_4 = \frac{T_3}{\sigma} = \frac{310}{1.486} \approx 208.6 \; \text{K} $$ **(ii)** Refrigerating Effect: $$ RE = c_p(T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (260 - 208.6) = 1.005 \times 51.4 \approx 51.66 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$ **(iii)** Net Work: $$ W_{net} = c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)] = 1.005 \times [(386.4 - 260) - (310 - 208.6)] $$ $$ W_{net} = 1.005 \times [126.4 - 101.4] = 1.005 \times 25.0 = 25.13 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$ **(iv)** COP: $$ COP = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{51.66}{25.13} \approx 2.056 $$ Verification: $COP = 1/(1.486 - 1) = 1/0.486 \approx 2.058$ ✓ --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]` — 14 Marks **(a)** Compare the Reversed Joule (Gas Refrigeration) Cycle with the Vapour Compression Refrigeration (VCR) Cycle. State any **four** advantages and **three** limitations of the former. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Basis | Reversed Joule Cycle | Vapour Compression Cycle | |-------|----------------------|---------------------------| | Working fluid | Air or any gas | Refrigerant undergoing phase change | | Heat absorption | Sensible heat only | Latent heat (dominant) + sensible heat | | COP | Low (typically 1.5–2.5) | High (typically 2.5–5.0) | | Operating temperature | Suitable for moderate and very low temperatures | Best near the refrigerant's boiling point | | Weight & bulk | Bulky for same RE (high mass flow) | Compact (high RE per kg) | | Safety | Non-toxic, non-flammable | Some refrigerants are toxic/flammable (e.g., NH₃) | **Four Advantages of Reversed Joule Cycle:** 1. **Safety:** Air is non-toxic, non-flammable, and non-corrosive. 2. **Temperature flexibility:** Cycle can operate at *any* temperature without being constrained by a phase-change boiling point. 3. **No evaporator/condenser phase change issues:** Avoids problems like oil return, frosting, and moisture ingress. 4. **Open system possibility:** In aircraft, air can be bled directly from the engine and discharged overboard — no need for a fully closed loop. **Three Limitations of Reversed Joule Cycle:** 1. **Low COP:** Theoretically and practically lower than VCR for the same temperature lift. 2. **High mass flow rate:** Required because air's specific heat is small (~1 kJ/kg·K) versus refrigerant latent heat (~150 kJ/kg). 3. **Bulky hardware:** Large compressor and expander for given capacity; cycle components are heavy and large. **(b)** An aircraft refrigeration system operates on a Reversed Joule Cycle. The temperature of air entering the compressor is $-10°C$ and the temperature of air leaving the cooler is $25°C$. The pressure ratio is $5$. For air, take $c_p = 1.005 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$ and $\gamma = 1.4$. Determine: **(i)** Maximum and minimum temperatures in the cycle, **(ii)** COP of the cycle, and **(iii)** Net work per kg of air. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $T_1 = -10°C = 263 \; \text{K}$, $T_3 = 25°C = 298 \; \text{K}$, $r_p = 5$. **(i)** Temperature ratio: $$ \sigma = 5^{0.4/1.4} = 5^{0.2857} \approx 1.5838 $$ Maximum temperature (after compression): $$ T_2 = \sigma T_1 = 1.5838 \times 263 \approx 416.5 \; \text{K} \;(\approx 143.5°C) $$ Minimum temperature (after expansion): $$ T_4 = \frac{T_3}{\sigma} = \frac{298}{1.5838} \approx 188.1 \; \text{K} \;(\approx -85.0°C) $$ **(ii)** COP: $$ COP = \frac{1}{\sigma - 1} = \frac{1}{0.5838} \approx 1.713 $$ **(iii)** Net Work: $$ W_{net} = c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)] = 1.005 \times [(416.5 - 263) - (298 - 188.1)] $$ $$ W_{net} = 1.005 \times [153.5 - 109.9] = 1.005 \times 43.6 \approx 43.82 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Wrong direction of cycle:** Students often mistakenly traverse the cycle in the power direction (clockwise on T-s). For a *refrigeration* cycle it is **anti-clockwise**. Reversed polarity will cost full marks. > 2. **Using $P$ instead of $r_p$ in the COP formula:** Always express COP as a function of the *pressure ratio* $r_p = P_2/P_1$, not absolute pressure. > 3. **Confusing T1 and T4:** State 1 is the *leaving* temperature from the cold space; State 4 is the *entering* temperature. Many students reverse these, getting negative RE. > 4. **Forgetting units:** Always use K (Kelvin) for temperatures in isentropic relations. Celsius will give wrong answers. > 5. **Not verifying with the formula:** Always cross-check your COP result using the compact formula $COP = 1/(r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1)$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Reversed Joule Cycle** > > - [x] Cycle is **reversed Joule/Brayton** — gas refrigeration cycle, *no phase change*. > - [x] Working fluid: **air** (or any ideal gas); assume $c_p$ constant. > - [x] **Four processes:** 1-2 Isentropic Compression → 2-3 Isobaric Heat Rejection → 3-4 Isentropic Expansion → 4-1 Isobaric Heat Absorption. > - [x] **Direction on T-s:** **Anti-clockwise** → refrigeration cycle. > - [x] Pressure ratio definition: $r_p = P_2/P_1 = P_3/P_4$. > - [x] **Isentropic relations:** $T_2/T_1 = T_3/T_4 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. > - [x] **Refrigerating Effect:** $RE = c_p(T_1 - T_4)$. > - [x] **Net Work:** $W_{net} = c_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]$. > - [x] **Master formula for COP:** $COP = \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}$ — independent of $T_1$ and $T_3$. > - [x] **Higher $r_p$ ⇒ Lower COP** (verified by negative derivative). > - [x] **Higher $r_p$ ⇒ Higher RE per kg** (trade-off). > - [x] **Practical limitation:** Low COP and high mass flow → bulky plant. > - [x] **Key applications:** Aircraft air-conditioning, gas liquefaction, turbine inlet cooling. > - [x] **Common exam trick:** "Reverse the cycle direction in your mind before applying any energy equation." > - [x] **Standard air values to memorize:** $c_p = 1.005 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$.

topics.title: Vapour Compression Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Vapour Compression Cycle ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC):** A closed thermodynamic cycle in which a working fluid (refrigerant) undergoes phase change (evaporation and condensation) to absorb heat from a low-temperature reservoir (cold space) and reject it to a high-temperature reservoir (surroundings). The cycle operates on the **Reverse Rankine Cycle** principle and is the most widely employed refrigeration mechanism in practical domestic, commercial, and industrial applications. The cycle is termed "**vapour compression**" because the low-pressure vapour is mechanically **compressed** to a high-pressure, high-temperature vapour before condensation. The four essential hardware components are: 1. **Compressor** (pressure booster & work input device) 2. **Condenser** (heat rejection unit at constant pressure) 3. **Expansion Device / Throttle Valve** (pressure drop & flow controller) 4. **Evaporator** (heat absorption unit at constant pressure) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Sponge and Bucket System":** > Imagine you are sweating on a hot day. Your body releases sweat, and as the sweat **evaporates** from your skin, it absorbs heat (cooling effect). The water vapour is the "refrigerant." Now imagine a mechanical system: > - A **sponge (evaporator)** soaks up heat from a cold room by evaporating a liquid. > - A **pump (compressor)** squeezes the vapour, raising its pressure and temperature. > - The hot, compressed vapour is dumped into the **outdoor air via a radiator (condenser)**, where it condenses back to liquid and releases heat. > - The liquid then passes through a **narrow nozzle (expansion valve)**, drops in pressure, becomes cold, and returns to the sponge. > > The liquid essentially "carries" heat from inside the fridge to outside, just like a sponge carrying water from one bucket to another. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Standard COP (Coefficient of Performance):** Defined as the ratio of desired effect (refrigeration effect) to work input. - **Saturated Refrigerant Temperatures:** Function of operating pressure (e.g., R-134a at 0.14 MPa → $-10^\circ$C, at 0.8 MPa → $30^\circ$C). - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ kJ/kmol}\cdot\text{K}$ - **Latent Heat of Vaporization ($h_{fg}$):** Pressure-dependent; falls as pressure rises. ### 1.4 Schematic Functional Block Overview | Hardware Block | Thermodynamic State Change | Energy Interaction | |---|---|---| | **Compressor** | Low-P Vapour $\rightarrow$ High-P Vapour | Work Input ($W_c$) | | **Condenser** | High-P Vapour $\rightarrow$ High-P Liquid | Heat Rejection ($Q_R$) | | **Expansion Valve** | High-P Liquid $\rightarrow$ Low-P Liq.-Vap. Mix | Throttling (No work, No heat) | | **Evaporator** | Low-P Mix $\rightarrow$ Low-P Vapour | Heat Absorption ($Q_E$) | ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Analysis of the Four Thermodynamic Processes (Theoretical Logic Steps) The **Ideal Vapour Compression Cycle** assumes: - Compression is **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic). - Pressure drops in connecting lines are negligible. - Refrigerant leaves the condenser as a **saturated liquid** (no subcooling considered in basic ideal cycle). - Refrigerant leaves the evaporator as **saturated vapour** (no superheating considered in basic ideal cycle). - Throttling is an **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy) process. #### Process 1$\rightarrow$2: **Isentropic Compression in Compressor** - **Why?** To raise the refrigerant pressure above the ambient/condenser pressure so that it can reject heat at a higher temperature. - **How?** The compressor consumes external work ($W_c$) and increases enthalpy from $h_1$ to $h_2$, with entropy remaining constant ($s_1 = s_2$). - **State 1:** Saturated vapour at evaporator pressure ($P_e$). - **State 2:** Superheated vapour at condenser pressure ($P_c$). #### Process 2$\rightarrow$3: **Constant-Pressure Heat Rejection in Condenser** - **Why?** The hot superheated vapour must give up its latent heat to the surroundings to condense. - **How?** The refrigerant first **desuperheats** (state 2 to 2'), then **condenses** to a saturated liquid (state 3). - **Condition:** $P_2 = P_3 = P_c$, heat is rejected at constant pressure. #### Process 3$\rightarrow$4: **Throttling / Isenthalpic Expansion** - **Why?** To drop the refrigerant pressure from condenser pressure to evaporator pressure, enabling low-temperature evaporation. - **How?** The refrigerant passes through an expansion valve or capillary tube. The process is highly irreversible but assumed to be adiabatic. - **Key Result:** $h_3 = h_4$ (enthalpy is conserved). - **State 4:** A low-quality, two-phase liquid-vapour mixture. #### Process 4$\rightarrow$1: **Constant-Pressure Heat Absorption in Evaporator** - **Why?** The cold liquid-vapour mixture absorbs heat from the refrigerated space, fully evaporating into saturated vapour. - **How?** Heat flows from the cold space into the refrigerant at constant low pressure ($P_e$). - **Condition:** $P_4 = P_1 = P_e$. ### 2.2 The Why Behind Refrigerant Selection > [!IMPORTANT] > An ideal refrigerant should have a high latent heat of vaporization, low boiling point at evaporator conditions, high condensing pressure (but not too high), be non-toxic, non-flammable, non-corrosive, and have zero Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP). Common choices include **R-134a**, **R-410A**, **R-22** (legacy), and natural refrigerants like **R-717 (Ammonia)** and **R-744 (CO$_2$)**. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Domestic refrigeration:** Household refrigerators and freezers. - **Air-conditioning systems:** Window ACs, split ACs, central HVAC. - **Cold chain logistics:** Cold storage warehouses, refrigerated transport. - **Industrial processes:** Food preservation, pharmaceutical storage, cryogenics, LNG processing. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Refrigeration Effect (RE)** | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed per kg of refrigerant in evaporator | | 2 | **Compressor Work ($W_c$)** | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Per kg of refrigerant | | 3 | **Heat Rejected in Condenser ($Q_R$)** | $Q_R = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | Per kg of refrigerant | | 4 | **COP of Refrigerator** | $COP = \dfrac{RE}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | Always $> 1$ for a practical refrigerator | | 5 | **Mass Flow Rate ($\dot{m}$)** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_E}{(h_1 - h_4)}$ | kg/s | $Q_E$ = Total cooling load (kW) | | 6 | **Compressor Power ($P$)** | $P = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Brake power includes mechanical efficiency | | 7 | **COP (Heat Pump Mode)** | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_R}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | $COP_{HP} = COP_{ref} + 1$ | | 8 | **Throttling Relation** | $h_3 = h_4$ | kJ/kg | Isenthalpic process | | 9 | **Isentropic Compression** | $s_1 = s_2$ | kJ/kg·K | Used to find $h_2$ from p-h chart | | 10 | **Volumetric Capacity** | $V = \dot{m} \cdot v_1$ | m³/s | Used to size the compressor displacement | | 11 | **Carnot COP (Ideal Limit)** | $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | The theoretical maximum COP | | 12 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\text{Actual}}{\text{Swept Volume}}$ | dimensionless | Compressor-specific | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on Tables:** Vertical pipes $\vert$ are not used inside table cells. For absolute values, use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ in formulas. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation of Coefficient of Performance (COP) — Ideal Vapour Compression Cycle #### Step 1: Energy Balance on the Evaporator (Control Volume) The evaporator absorbs heat $Q_E$ from the cold space, and the refrigerant experiences an enthalpy rise from $h_4$ to $h_1$. The **Refrigeration Effect per kg** of refrigerant is: $$RE = q_E = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ **Logic:** This is a steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) with no work and negligible kinetic/potential energy changes, so the heat absorbed equals the enthalpy rise of the refrigerant. #### Step 2: Energy Balance on the Compressor The compressor consumes work $W_c$ per kg and increases the refrigerant enthalpy from $h_1$ to $h_2$: $$w_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ **Logic:** The compressor is adiabatic (no heat transfer), so all work input goes into raising the enthalpy. Since compression is isentropic ($s_1 = s_2$), the state at point 2 can be uniquely determined on the p-h diagram. #### Step 3: Energy Balance on the Condenser The refrigerant releases heat to the surroundings, decreasing in enthalpy from $h_2$ to $h_3$: $$q_R = h_2 - h_3 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ **Logic:** Heat rejection occurs at constant pressure. The vapour first desuperheats, then condenses to saturated liquid, releasing both sensible and latent heat. #### Step 4: Energy Balance on the Expansion Valve The expansion process is isenthalpic (throttling): $$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ **Logic:** The valve has no moving parts, no work transfer, and the process is fast enough to be considered adiabatic. A throttling process always conserves enthalpy, even though entropy increases due to irreversibility. #### Step 5: Definition of COP The **Coefficient of Performance** is the ratio of the desired effect (refrigeration) to the work input: $$ \begin{aligned} COP_{ref} &= \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{RE}{W_c} \\[6pt] &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** This dimensionless parameter is a measure of the cycle's efficiency. Unlike thermal efficiency, COP can be greater than unity. #### Step 6: Relation to Heat Pump COP The heat rejected in the condenser is the useful output for a heat pump: $$ \begin{aligned} COP_{HP} &= \frac{q_R}{w_c} = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1} \end{aligned} $$ By the first law applied to the complete cycle, $q_R = w_c + RE$, therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} COP_{HP} &= \frac{w_c + RE}{w_c} = 1 + \frac{RE}{w_c} \\[6pt] COP_{HP} &= 1 + COP_{ref} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Subcooling and Superheating | Modification | Effect on RE | Effect on $W_c$ | Effect on COP | Practical Reason | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Subcooling (state 3 below sat. liquid)** | Increases | No change | **Increases** | More enthalpy drop in evaporator | | **Superheating (state 1 above sat. vapour)** | Increases slightly | Increases | Slight increase/decrease | Ensures no liquid at compressor inlet | | **Increased Condenser Pressure** | Decreases | Increases | **Decreases** | Higher compression ratio | | **Decreased Evaporator Pressure** | Decreases | Increases | **Decreases** | Lower $h_1$, higher compression work | | **Use of Multi-Stage with Intercooling** | Increases | Decreases | **Increases** | Reduces compression work | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Superheating vs. Subcooling Effect on COP:** Subcooling *always* improves COP because it does not require any additional work. Superheating, if done *inside* the evaporator (useful cooling), improves COP; if done *outside* (e.g., in suction line), it slightly *degrades* COP since $W_c$ rises more than $RE$. ### 3.3 Numerical Example: Standard KTU-Style Problem > **Problem:** A refrigerant R-134a vapour compression cycle operates between an evaporator pressure of $0.14$ MPa (saturation temperature $-10^\circ$C) and a condenser pressure of $0.8$ MPa (saturation temperature $30^\circ$C). The refrigerant enters the compressor as saturated vapour and leaves the condenser as saturated liquid. > Enthalpies (from R-134a tables): > $h_1 = 244.50$ kJ/kg (sat. vapour at $0.14$ MPa) > $h_2 = 271.50$ kJ/kg (superheated vapour at $0.8$ MPa, $s = s_1$) > $h_3 = 93.58$ kJ/kg (sat. liquid at $0.8$ MPa) > $h_4 = h_3 = 93.58$ kJ/kg (throttling) > > **Find:** (a) Refrigeration Effect, (b) Compressor Work, (c) COP of the refrigerator, (d) COP of heat pump. #### Solution (Part a): Refrigeration Effect $$ \begin{aligned} RE &= h_1 - h_4 \\[4pt] RE &= 244.50 - 93.58 \\[4pt] RE &= 150.92 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The enthalpy difference between the inlet and outlet of the evaporator equals the cooling produced per kg of refrigerant. #### Solution (Part b): Compressor Work $$ \begin{aligned} W_c &= h_2 - h_1 \\[4pt] W_c &= 271.50 - 244.50 \\[4pt] W_c &= 27.00 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The work input equals the enthalpy rise of the refrigerant across the compressor. #### Solution (Part c): COP of Refrigerator $$ \begin{aligned} COP_{ref} &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \\[4pt] COP_{ref} &= \frac{150.92}{27.00} \\[4pt] COP_{ref} &= 5.589 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** For every 1 kJ of work input, 5.589 kJ of heat is removed from the cold space. #### Solution (Part d): COP of Heat Pump $$ \begin{aligned} COP_{HP} &= COP_{ref} + 1 \\[4pt] COP_{HP} &= 5.589 + 1 \\[4pt] COP_{HP} &= 6.589 \end{aligned} $$ **Verification via Direct Formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} COP_{HP} &= \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1} \\[4pt] &= \frac{271.50 - 93.58}{27.00} \\[4pt] &= \frac{177.92}{27.00} \\[4pt] &= 6.589 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC) The following **Mermaid block-level functional architecture flow** maps the interactions of the four primary components, with state points clearly marked. It also indicates the energy and mass interactions at each stage. ```mermaid flowchart LR Start([Start of Cycle]) --> EVAP EVAP["EVAPORATOR
P_e, T_L
State 4 to State 1
h4 to h1
Heat IN Q_E"] --> COMP COMP["COMPRESSOR
Isentropic Compression
State 1 to State 2
h1 to h2
Work IN W_c"] --> COND COND["CONDENSER
P_c, T_H
State 2 to State 3
h2 to h3
Heat OUT Q_R"] --> EXV EXV["EXPANSION VALVE
Throttling
h3 equals h4
No Work, No Heat"] --> EVAP EXV --> EndLoop((Cycle Repeats)) EVAP --> EndLoop COMP --> EndLoop COND --> EndLoop subgraph ColdSpace["COLD RESERVOIR T_L"] EVAP end subgraph HotSpace["HOT RESERVOIR T_H"] COND end ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid p-h Diagram: Schematic State Point Mapping The p-h (Pressure-Enthalpy) diagram is the **most critical** schematic for VCRC analysis. Below is a Mermaid representation of the cycle on a pressure-enthalpy plane, with state points labeled. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PH_Diagram["P-H DIAGRAM SCHEMATIC FOR VCRC"] direction TB SatCurve["Saturation Dome
Critical Point at Top"] DomeL["Saturated Liquid Line"] DomeR["Saturated Vapour Line"] IsoP_Low["Constant Pressure Line P_e Low"] IsoP_High["Constant Pressure Line P_c High"] S1["STATE 1
Sat. Vapour
P_e, h1, s1"] S2["STATE 2
Superheated Vapour
P_c, h2, s2 equals s1"] S3["STATE 3
Sat. Liquid
P_c, h3"] S4["STATE 4
Two-Phase Mix
P_e, h4 equals h3"] Path12["Process 1 to 2: Isentropic Compression
Entropy Constant"] Path23["Process 2 to 3: Constant Pressure Condensation
Desuperheat then Condense"] Path34["Process 3 to 4: Throttling
Vertical Drop on p-h Chart"] Path41["Process 4 to 1: Constant Pressure Evaporation
Horizontal Line at P_e"] S1 -->|Path12| S2 S2 -->|Path23| S3 S3 -->|Path34| S4 S4 -->|Path41| S1 end ``` ### 4.3 T-s Diagram Schematic Description For completeness, the **T-s (Temperature-Entropy)** diagram of an ideal VCRC is described structurally: - The **saturation dome** is a closed curve with the critical point at the top. - The **evaporator process (4$\rightarrow$1)** is a horizontal line inside the dome at $T_L$. - The **compressor process (1$\rightarrow$2)** is a **vertical line** (isentropic, $s$ constant) going upward, exiting the dome into the superheated region. - The **condenser process (2$\rightarrow$3)** is a curve that first descends through the superheated region (desuperheating), then follows the saturated vapour line down to the saturated liquid line at $T_H$. - The **expansion process (3$\rightarrow$4)** is a downward curve to the left (irreversible, entropy increases). ### 4.4 Functional Architecture Mapping Table | Component | Input State | Output State | Process Type | Energy Form | |---|---|---|---|---| | Compressor | State 1 (Low-P Vapour) | State 2 (High-P Vapour) | Isentropic | Work In | | Condenser | State 2 (High-P Vapour) | State 3 (High-P Liquid) | Constant Pressure | Heat Out | | Expansion Valve | State 3 (High-P Liquid) | State 4 (Low-P Mixture) | Throttling | None | | Evaporator | State 4 (Low-P Mixture) | State 1 (Low-P Vapour) | Constant Pressure | Heat In | ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** **Q1. Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. How is it different from thermal efficiency?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired cooling effect (heat extracted from the cold space) to the work input required to achieve it: $$COP_{ref} = \frac{\text{Refrigeration Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_E}{W_c}$$ It is a **dimensionless quantity** and can be **greater than 1**, whereas thermal efficiency is always less than 1. COP compares the useful refrigeration output to the work input, while thermal efficiency compares useful work output to heat input in a heat engine. --- **Q2. Why is the throttling process in a vapour compression cycle considered isenthalpic?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The expansion valve is a **constant enthalpy device** because: 1. The valve has **no moving parts**, so no work is transferred ($\delta W = 0$). 2. The process is rapid, with negligible heat transfer ($\delta Q = 0$). 3. Changes in kinetic and potential energy are negligible. Applying the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE):** $h_3 + \frac{V_3^2}{2} + gZ_3 + q = h_4 + \frac{V_4^2}{2} + gZ_4 + w$, with the above assumptions, simplifies to $h_3 = h_4$. Hence, the process is **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy), though irreversible with entropy increasing. --- ### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]** --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a neat schematic and a p-h diagram, explain the working of an ideal vapour compression refrigeration cycle. List the assumptions made. **Model Solution:** The **ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)** consists of four main components: **compressor, condenser, expansion valve, and evaporator**. The refrigerant circulates through these components in a closed loop, undergoing phase changes to transfer heat from a low-temperature region to a high-temperature region. **Working Principle:** 1. **Compression (1$\rightarrow$2):** The low-pressure saturated vapour from the evaporator is drawn into the compressor, where it is compressed isentropically to a high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour. Work $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ is supplied to the compressor. 2. **Condensation (2$\rightarrow$3):** The hot, high-pressure vapour enters the condenser, where it rejects heat $Q_R = h_2 - h_3$ to the surroundings at constant pressure. The refrigerant first desuperheats, then condenses into a saturated liquid. 3. **Expansion (3$\rightarrow$4):** The saturated liquid passes through the expansion valve, undergoing a throttling process where its pressure and temperature drop sharply. Since the process is isenthalpic, $h_3 = h_4$. The refrigerant becomes a low-quality, two-phase mixture. 4. **Evaporation (4$\rightarrow$1):** The cold liquid-vapour mixture flows through the evaporator, absorbing heat $Q_E = h_1 - h_4$ from the refrigerated space at constant low pressure, and fully evaporates back to saturated vapour. **Schematic Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid schematic. **p-h Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the Mermaid p-h diagram. **Key Assumptions of the Ideal VCRC:** 1. Compression is isentropic (reversible, adiabatic): $s_1 = s_2$. 2. No pressure drop in the connecting pipelines. 3. Refrigerant leaves the condenser as a saturated liquid (no subcooling). 4. Refrigerant leaves the evaporator as a saturated vapour (no superheating). 5. Throttling is an isenthalpic process: $h_3 = h_4$. 6. Heat transfer in the evaporator and condenser occurs at constant pressure. 7. Kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible. --- **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A vapour compression refrigeration cycle uses R-134a as the working fluid. The refrigerant enters the compressor as saturated vapour at $0.18$ MPa and leaves the condenser as saturated liquid at $1.0$ MPa. The isentropic compression gives $h_2 = 273.66$ kJ/kg. Given: $h_1 = 241.30$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = 101.61$ kJ/kg. Calculate: (i) Refrigeration Effect (RE) (ii) Compressor Work ($W_c$) (iii) COP of the refrigerator (iv) Mass flow rate of refrigerant for a cooling load of $10$ kW. **Model Solution:** **(i) Refrigeration Effect:** Throttling gives $h_4 = h_3 = 101.61$ kJ/kg. $$ \begin{aligned} RE &= h_1 - h_4 \\[4pt] &= 241.30 - 101.61 \\[4pt] &= 139.69 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Compressor Work:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_c &= h_2 - h_1 \\[4pt] &= 273.66 - 241.30 \\[4pt] &= 32.36 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) COP of the Refrigerator:** $$ \begin{aligned} COP_{ref} &= \frac{RE}{W_c} \\[4pt] &= \frac{139.69}{32.36} \\[4pt] &= 4.317 \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant:** For a total cooling load $Q_E = 10$ kW: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m} &= \frac{Q_E}{RE} \\[4pt] &= \frac{10}{139.69} \\[4pt] &= 0.0716 \text{ kg/s} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to kg/h: $\dot{m} = 0.0716 \times 3600 = 257.66$ kg/h. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare the Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC) and the Vapour Absorption Refrigeration Cycle (VARC) in terms of working principle, components, energy input, COP, and applications. **Model Solution (Tabular Comparison):** | S.No. | Parameter | VCRC | VARC | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Working Principle** | Mechanical compression of refrigerant vapour | Heat-induced absorption of refrigerant in a secondary fluid (absorbent) | | 2 | **Energy Input** | Mechanical work (shaft work to compressor) | Heat energy (to generator) + small pump work | | 3 | **Main Components** | Compressor, Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator | Generator, Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator, Absorber, Pump, Rectifier | | 4 | **Working Fluid** | Single refrigerant (e.g., R-134a) | Binary mixture: Refrigerant (e.g., NH$_3$) + Absorbent (e.g., H$_2$O) | | 5 | **Moving Parts** | Compressor (high-speed rotating) | Liquid pump only (low-speed) | | 6 | **COP** | Higher (typically 3–5) | Lower (typically 0.3–0.7) | | 7 | **Noise & Vibration** | Higher (due to compressor) | Very low (no compressor) | | 8 | **Maintenance** | Higher (compressor servicing) | Lower | | 9 | **Applications** | Domestic refrigeration, AC, cold storage | Industrial cooling, large-scale refrigeration, rural electrification | | 10 | **Sensitivity to Capacity Reduction** | Poor (compressor cannot run efficiently at part load without VFD) | Better (input heat can be modulated) | | 11 | **Use of Waste Heat** | Not directly | Can utilize low-grade waste heat (advantage) | **Conclusion:** VCRC is preferred where mechanical power is readily available, while VARC is favored in locations with abundant low-grade waste heat and where quiet operation is desired. --- **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** An actual VCRC using R-12 operates between an evaporator temperature of $-10^\circ$C and a condenser temperature of $40^\circ$C. The refrigerant is subcooled by $5^\circ$C below the condenser temperature and superheated by $5^\circ$C above the evaporator temperature. From the property tables, the following enthalpies are obtained: $h_1 = 183.20$ kJ/kg (superheated vapour at $-5^\circ$C) $h_2 = 209.00$ kJ/kg (superheated vapour at $40^\circ$C, $s = s_1$) $h_3 = 64.59$ kJ/kg (subcooled liquid at $35^\circ$C) Calculate: (i) Refrigeration Effect (ii) Compressor Work (iii) COP of the refrigerator (iv) Compare with the COP of a Carnot refrigerator operating between the same temperature limits ($T_L = 263$ K, $T_H = 313$ K). **Model Solution:** **Given:** $h_4 = h_3 = 64.59$ kJ/kg (throttling). **(i) Refrigeration Effect:** $$ \begin{aligned} RE &= h_1 - h_4 \\[4pt] &= 183.20 - 64.59 \\[4pt] &= 118.61 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Compressor Work:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_c &= h_2 - h_1 \\[4pt] &= 209.00 - 183.20 \\[4pt] &= 25.80 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) COP of the Refrigerator:** $$ \begin{aligned} COP_{ref} &= \frac{118.61}{25.80} \\[4pt] &= 4.597 \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Carnot COP for Comparison:** $$ \begin{aligned} COP_{Carnot} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \\[4pt] &= \frac{263}{313 - 263} \\[4pt] &= \frac{263}{50} \\[4pt] &= 5.260 \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison:** The VCRC COP (4.597) is **less than the Carnot COP** (5.260), which is consistent with the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**. The Carnot cycle represents the theoretical maximum; real cycles always have lower COP due to irreversibilities (non-isentropic compression, throttling losses, finite temperature differences in heat exchangers). **Relative COP:** $\frac{COP_{ref}}{COP_{Carnot}} = \frac{4.597}{5.260} = 0.874$, or **87.4%** of Carnot efficiency. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting throttling assumption:** Students often use the wrong value for $h_4$. Remember, $h_3 = h_4$ *always* (throttling is isenthalpic). Marks lost: 1–2. > 2. **Mixing up COP$_{ref}$ and COP$_{HP}$:** Ensure the correct output is in the numerator. $COP_{ref} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ and $COP_{HP} = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$. They differ by exactly 1. > 3. **Unit errors in mass flow rate:** When computing $\dot{m}$ for a load given in kW (kJ/s), the result is in kg/s. Converting to kg/h requires multiplying by 3600. Marks lost: 1. > 4. **Confusing superheating inside vs. outside the evaporator:** Superheating inside the evaporator (sensible heat absorbed from cold space) is *useful* and increases COP. Superheating in the suction line (from ambient heat) is *wasteful* and decreases COP. > 5. **Not drawing or referencing the p-h diagram:** KTU examiners *expect* a labelled p-h or T-s diagram for full marks in descriptive questions. Drawing it correctly (with the saturation dome, isobars, and process directions) is worth 2–3 marks alone. > 6. **Sign convention slip-ups:** Heat absorbed is positive ($Q_E > 0$), heat rejected is positive ($Q_R > 0$), work input is positive ($W_c > 0$). Do not introduce negative signs unless explicitly asked. --- ### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **VCRC** = **V**apour **C**ompression **R**efrigeration **C**ycle. The most commercially dominant refrigeration cycle. - **Four Components:** Compressor $\rightarrow$ Condenser $\rightarrow$ Expansion Valve $\rightarrow$ Evaporator. - **Four State Points:** - State 1: Saturated vapour at $P_e$ (compressor inlet) - State 2: Superheated vapour at $P_c$ (compressor outlet / condenser inlet) - State 3: Saturated liquid at $P_c$ (condenser outlet / valve inlet) - State 4: Two-phase mixture at $P_e$ (valve outlet / evaporator inlet) - **Key Equations (Memorize These):** - $RE = h_1 - h_4$ - $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ - $Q_R = h_2 - h_3$ - $h_3 = h_4$ (throttling) - $s_1 = s_2$ (isentropic compression) - $COP_{ref} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ - $COP_{HP} = COP_{ref} + 1 = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$ - **Carnot COP (Ideal Limit):** $COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ — must be **greater** than actual VCRC COP. - **Subcooling** $\uparrow$ RE, $\uparrow$ COP (no extra work). - **Superheating inside evaporator** $\uparrow$ RE, $\uparrow$ COP. - **Superheating in suction line** $\uparrow$ RE marginally, $\uparrow$ $W_c$ more, so $\downarrow$ COP. - **Higher condenser pressure** $\downarrow$ COP. - **Lower evaporator pressure** $\downarrow$ COP. - **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \frac{Q_E}{h_1 - h_4}$. - **Compressor power:** $P = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$. - **Common Refrigerants:** R-134a (modern), R-22 (legacy, ozone-depleting), R-410A, R-717 (Ammonia), R-744 (CO$_2$). - **p-h Diagram is KING:** Always draw or refer to it. The cycle appears as a "distorted rectangle" inside and outside the saturation dome. - **Heat Pump vs. Refrigerator:** Same hardware, different purpose. The heat pump delivers $Q_R$ to a useful space, while the refrigerator extracts $Q_E$ from a cold space.

topics.title: Vapor-Absorption Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vapor-Absorption Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Vapor Absorption Cycle (VAC)** is a thermally-activated refrigeration cycle that produces cooling effect by utilizing a heat input (thermal energy) instead of mechanical work as the primary energy source. Unlike the Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC), which uses a mechanical compressor, the absorption cycle uses a **thermochemical compression process** — achieved by the *absorption* of refrigerant vapor into an absorbent liquid and subsequent *desorption (boiling out)* of the vapor using external heat.

The cycle is governed by two pressure levels: a **low pressure** (evaporator and absorber) and a **high pressure** (generator and condenser), with the working fluid being a **binary mixture** (refrigerant + absorbent).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Key Terms:** *Absorbent*, *Refrigerant*, *Generator (Boiler)*, *Absorber*, *Pump*, *Expansion Valve*, *COP*, *Maximum COP*, *Three-Fluid System*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the comparison between **Vapor Absorption and Vapor Compression** cycles, the **maximum COP derivation**, and **practical modifications** like the use of a rectifier, heat exchanger, analyzer, and pump.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the absorption cycle like a **sponge-and-bucket system** to understand it intuitively:

- **The Sponge (Absorbent)**: Imagine a sponge that can soak up water vapor (refrigerant vapor). After soaking, if you heat the sponge, it releases the water back as vapor.
- **The Squeezing (Heat)**: To get the refrigerant out of the absorbent in the *generator*, you "squeeze" it using **heat** instead of mechanical force.
- **Mechanical Work vs. Thermal Work**: In a vapor compression system, a motor (compressor) physically pushes the refrigerant. In an absorption system, **heat does the pushing** — turning liquid absorbent into a hot vapor mixture, which is then separated and condensed.

**Real-World Analogy**: Picture two towels — a *dry* towel and a *wet* towel. If you put a wet towel near a heater (generator), the water evaporates. The remaining dry towel can then absorb more water (from the absorber side). The absorption cycle cleverly exploits this "absorption-desorption" loop using two fluids.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Use Absorption?** Absorption systems can run on **waste heat**, **solar energy**, or **natural gas**, making them ideal where electricity is scarce or expensive — like in large industrial cooling, cold storage, and solar-powered refrigerators.

## 1.3 Working Fluid Pairs (Key for KTU)

| Pair | Refrigerant | Absorbent | Application |
|------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| **NH₃–H₂O** | Ammonia | Water | Industrial refrigeration (below 0°C) |
| **H₂O–LiBr** | Water | Lithium Bromide | Air conditioning (above 0°C) |
| **R-22–DMETEG** | R-22 | Organic fluid | Solar cooling |

**Physical Constants to Remember:**
- **1 TR (Ton of Refrigeration) = 3.517 kW** of cooling effect
- **Latent heat of NH₃ at -33.3°C ≈ 1117 kJ/kg**
- **COP theoretical maximum** (Carnot refrigeration) = $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Simple Vapor Absorption System (VAS)

A basic single-stage vapor absorption refrigeration system consists of **four main heat exchangers** and **one pump**:

1. **Absorber**: Low-pressure refrigerant vapor (from evaporator) is absorbed by the weak (dilute) absorbent solution, forming a strong (rich) solution. Heat is rejected here at low temperature.
2. **Pump**: A small liquid pump raises the pressure of the strong solution from low to high pressure. This is the **only mechanical work input** — which is very small compared to the heat input.
3. **Generator (Boiler)**: External heat (Q_G) drives off the refrigerant vapor from the strong solution, leaving behind a weak solution.
4. **Condenser**: High-pressure refrigerant vapor is condensed by rejecting heat (Q_C) to the surroundings.
5. **Expansion Valve (Throttle)**: High-pressure liquid refrigerant is throttled to low pressure, partially flashing into a cool liquid-vapor mixture.
6. **Evaporator**: The low-pressure refrigerant absorbs heat (Q_E — the desired cooling effect) from the refrigerated space and vaporizes.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why an Absorber?** The absorber mimics the suction side of a compressor by creating a low-pressure region. By chemically absorbing the vapor, it maintains low pressure without mechanical work.
- **Why a Pump?** Although the pump provides mechanical work, the energy required is **negligible (typically < 1%)** compared to the heat input at the generator. This is the key advantage.
- **Why a Generator?** Heat input raises the temperature of the strong solution, boiling off the more volatile refrigerant (e.g., NH₃ in NH₃-H2O system). This is where the bulk of energy enters the system.
- **Why a Rectifier (in practical systems)?** Some absorbent vapor may carry over with the refrigerant from the generator. A rectifier separates this carryover to ensure pure refrigerant reaches the condenser.

## 2.3 COP of Vapor Absorption Cycle

The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** for an ideal vapor absorption cycle is:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{VAC,ideal}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_{\text{pump}}}$$

For a simplified analysis (neglecting pump work, $W_p \approx 0$):

$$\text{COP}_{\text{VAC,ideal}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G}$$

Applying an **energy balance** to the entire cycle (steady-state, neglecting kinetic and potential energy):

$$Q_E + Q_G = Q_C + Q_A$$

where $Q_A$ is the heat rejected at the absorber.

For a **maximum COP** analysis (assuming the absorption process is *reversible* and treating the solution as a single fluid with average temperature $T_G$, absorber at $T_A$, evaporator at $T_E$, condenser at $T_C$):

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \left(\frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}\right) \times \left(\frac{T_C - T_A}{T_G - T_A}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The first factor $(T_E / (T_C - T_E))$ is the **Carnot COP** for refrigeration. The second factor is the **efficiency penalty** of the absorption process, which is always less than 1. This means the practical COP of a vapor absorption system is **always less than the Carnot COP**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{COP} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_p}$ | General COP of absorption cycle | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_E (T_C - T_A)}{(T_C - T_E)(T_G - T_A)}$ | Maximum COP of ideal VAC | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Q_E + Q_G = Q_C + Q_A$ | Energy balance for complete cycle | kW |
| 4 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot refrigeration COP | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q_E = m_r \cdot h_{fg}$ | Cooling effect at evaporator | kW |
| 6 | $Q_G = m_a \cdot (h_{\text{strong}} - h_{\text{weak}})$ | Heat input at generator | kW |
| 7 | $\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{\text{cycle}} \cdot \text{COP}_{\text{max}}$ | Actual COP (with cycle efficiency) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | Standard refrigeration ton | kW |
| 9 | $\text{COP}_{\text{VAC}} < \text{COP}_{\text{VCRC}}$ | Practical comparison | dimensionless |
| 10 | $W_p = \dot{m}_s \cdot v_f \cdot (P_{\text{high}} - P_{\text{low}})$ | Pump work for solution | kW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In numerical problems, you are usually given evaporator temperature $T_E$, condenser temperature $T_C$, absorber temperature $T_A$, and generator temperature $T_G$. Always convert to **Kelvin** (add 273.15 to °C) before applying the COP formula.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Vapor absorption systems are extensively used in:
- **Industrial process cooling** (chemical, pharmaceutical)
- **Large-scale air conditioning** (LiBr-H₂O chillers)
- **Solar refrigeration** (no electricity needed, only sun heat)
- **Cold storage warehouses** in rural areas
- **Heat recovery systems** (using waste heat from engines/turbines)
- **Domestic refrigerators** (Electrolux model — silent, no moving parts)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum COP of Vapor Absorption Cycle

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider an **ideal vapor absorption cycle** operating between four temperature reservoirs:
- $T_E$ = Evaporator temperature (cold space)
- $T_A$ = Absorber temperature 
- $T_C$ = Condenser temperature
- $T_G$ = Generator temperature (heat source)

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-flow, steady-state operation
2. All processes internally reversible
3. Negligible kinetic and potential energy changes
4. Solution behaves as a single fluid in the absorber and generator
5. Refrigerant is pure (saturated vapor at evaporator exit, saturated liquid at condenser exit)
6. Throttling is isenthalpic
7. Heat rejection in absorber and condenser occurs at constant temperatures $T_A$ and $T_C$

### 3.1.2 Energy Balance (First Law)

Applying the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) to each component:

**Evaporator (1→2):** $Q_E = m_r (h_2 - h_1)$

**Condenser (3→4):** $Q_C = m_r (h_3 - h_4)$

**Generator (8→9):** $Q_G = m_a h_8 - m_a h_9 - m_r h_3$ (with appropriate sign convention)

**Absorber (6→7):** $Q_A = m_a h_7 - m_a h_6 - m_r h_2$

For the **overall cycle** (steady-state, cyclic):

$$Q_E + Q_G = Q_C + Q_A$$

### 3.1.3 Entropy Balance (Second Law)

For a **reversible** cycle, the total entropy generation is zero. The total entropy change of all reservoirs must equal zero:

$$\frac{Q_E}{T_E} - \frac{Q_G}{T_G} - \frac{Q_C}{T_C} - \frac{Q_A}{T_A} = 0$$

where the signs follow: heat absorbed is positive, heat rejected is negative.

### 3.1.4 Solving for COP

We have two equations (energy and entropy balances) with four unknowns ($Q_E, Q_G, Q_C, Q_A$). To find COP, we eliminate $Q_C$ and $Q_A$.

**From energy balance:**
$$Q_C + Q_A = Q_E + Q_G \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**From entropy balance:**
$$\frac{Q_C}{T_C} + \frac{Q_A}{T_A} = \frac{Q_E}{T_E} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Multiply equation (1) by $\frac{1}{T_A}$:
$$\frac{Q_C}{T_A} + \frac{Q_A}{T_A} = \frac{Q_E + Q_G}{T_A}$$

Subtract from (2):

$$\frac{Q_C}{T_C} - \frac{Q_C}{T_A} = \frac{Q_E}{T_E} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G} - \frac{Q_E + Q_G}{T_A}$$

$$Q_C \left(\frac{1}{T_C} - \frac{1}{T_A}\right) = \frac{Q_E}{T_E} - \frac{Q_E}{T_A} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G} - \frac{Q_G}{T_A}$$

$$Q_C \cdot \frac{T_A - T_C}{T_C T_A} = Q_E \cdot \frac{T_A - T_E}{T_E T_A} + Q_G \cdot \frac{T_A - T_G}{T_G T_A}$$

Solving for $Q_C$:

$$Q_C = \frac{T_C}{T_A - T_C} \left[ Q_E \cdot \frac{T_A - T_E}{T_E} + Q_G \cdot \frac{T_A - T_G}{T_G} \right]$$

Substituting back into equation (1) and solving for the COP $\left(\frac{Q_E}{Q_G}\right)$ yields the classical result:

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_E (T_C - T_A)}{(T_C - T_E)(T_G - T_A)}}$$

### 3.1.5 Verification

**Check (1):** If the absorption process is *perfect* (no temperature penalty), i.e., $T_A = T_E$ and $T_G = T_C$, then:

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_E (T_C - T_E)}{(T_C - T_E)(T_C - T_E)} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} = \text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}$$

This confirms that the maximum theoretical COP of VAC reduces to the Carnot COP when absorption is ideal. ✓

**Check (2):** If we set $T_A = T_E$ and $T_G = T_C$, the cycle approaches the Carnot refrigeration cycle, validating the derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation of COP for Practical (Simple) Absorption Cycle

For the **simple practical cycle** (neglecting pump work and heat exchanger):

$$\text{COP}_{\text{simple}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{m_r \cdot h_{fg,E}}{m_a \cdot \Delta h_{\text{gen}}}$$

where $m_r$ = mass flow rate of refrigerant, $m_a$ = mass flow rate of strong solution, $\Delta h_{\text{gen}}$ = enthalpy difference in generator.

The **concentration ratio** (CR) is defined as:

$$\text{CR} = \frac{x_{\text{strong}} - x_{\text{weak}}}{x_{\text{strong}}(1 - x_{\text{weak}})}$$

where $x$ represents the mass fraction of refrigerant in the solution.

The mass flow rate ratio is:

$$\frac{m_a}{m_r} = \frac{1}{x_{\text{strong}} - x_{\text{weak}}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Cycles

| Parameter | Vapor Compression (VCRC) | Vapor Absorption (VAC) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Energy Input | Mechanical work (compressor) | Thermal energy (heat) + small pump work |
| Moving Parts | Many (compressor) | Few or none (Electrolux) |
| COP | Higher (3–5 typically) | Lower (0.4–1.2 typically) |
| Noise | High | Very low (silent operation) |
| Electricity Required | Yes (mandatory) | Minimal (only pump) |
| Heat Source | N/A | Boiler, solar, waste heat |
| Maintenance | Moderate to high | Low |
| Capacity Range | Small to large | Medium to very large |
| Cost | Lower for small capacities | Higher initial cost |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of Simple Vapor Absorption System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Evaporator\nLow Pressure\nT_E] -->|Refrigerant Vapor| A2[Absorber\nLow Pressure\nT_A]
    A2 -->|Strong Solution| A3[Pump\nPressure Increase\nW_p small]
    A3 -->|High P Strong Solution| A4[Generator\nHigh Pressure\nQ_G Input\nT_G]
    A4 -->|Refrigerant Vapor| A5[Condenser\nHigh Pressure\nQ_C Reject\nT_C]
    A5 -->|Liquid Refrigerant| A6[Expansion Valve\nThrottling\nH constant]
    A6 -->|Low P Mixture| A1
    A4 -->|Weak Solution| A7[Expansion Valve 2\nH constant]
    A7 -->|Weak Solution| A2
    A2 -->|Q_A Reject| EXT1[To Cooling Tower]
    A5 -->|Q_C Reject| EXT2[To Cooling Tower]
    A4 -->|Q_G Input| EXT3[From Heat Source]
    A1 -->|Q_E Absorbed| EXT4[From Cold Space]
```

## 4.2 P-h Diagram Representation (T-s Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LP["LOW PRESSURE REGION (Evaporator + Absorber)"]
        direction TB
        LP1["State 1: Saturated Vapor at T_E\nExit of Evaporator"]
        LP2["State 2: After Absorber\nStrong Liquid Solution"]
        LP3["State 6: Weak Solution from Generator\nvia Throttle"]
    end
    
    subgraph HP["HIGH PRESSURE REGION (Generator + Condenser)"]
        direction TB
        HP1["State 3: Saturated Vapor at T_G\nExit of Generator"]
        HP2["State 4: Saturated Liquid at T_C\nExit of Condenser"]
        HP3["State 5: After Expansion Valve 1\nLow P Liquid-Vapor Mix"]
        HP4["State 8: Strong Solution from Pump\nHigh P Liquid"]
    end
    
    LP1 -->|"Absorption Process (6 to 7)"| LP2
    LP2 -->|"Pump (7 to 8)"| HP4
    HP4 -->|"Generator Heat Input (8 to 9)"| HP1
    HP1 -->|"Condenser (3 to 4)"| HP2
    HP2 -->|"Throttling (4 to 5)"| LP1
    HP4 -.->|"Heat Exchange (Modified)"| LP2
    HP1 -.->|"Rectification (Modified)"| HP1
```

## 4.3 Practical Modified Vapor Absorption System (with Rectifier, Heat Exchanger, Analyzer, Pump)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Generator\nQ_G in] -->|Vapor + Carryover| B[Rectifier\nSeparates Absorbent]
    B -->|Pure Refrigerant Vapor| C[Condenser\nQ_C out]
    C -->|Liquid Refrigerant| D[Expansion Valve 1]
    D -->|Low P Liquid| E[Evaporator\nQ_E in]
    E -->|Low P Vapor| F[Absorber\nQ_A out]
    F -->|Strong Solution| G[Solution Pump\nW_p in]
    G -->|High P Solution| H[Solution Heat Exchanger\nHX]
    H -->|Heated Solution| A
    A -->|Weak Solution| I[Expansion Valve 2]
    I -->|Low P Weak Solution| H
    H -->|Cooled Weak Solution| F
    F -->|Analyzer\nChecks Concentration| F
    A -->|To Analyzer| A
```

## 4.4 Component Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENERGY_FLOW["ENERGY FLOW DIAGRAM"]
        QG["Q_G = Heat Input\nat Generator\n(Largest Energy)"]
        QE["Q_E = Cooling Effect\nat Evaporator\n(Desired Output)"]
        QC["Q_C = Heat Reject\nat Condenser"]
        QA["Q_A = Heat Reject\nat Absorber"]
    end
    
    QG --> SUM["Energy Balance:\nQ_E + Q_G = Q_C + Q_A"]
    QE --> SUM
    SUM --> QC
    SUM --> QA
    
    COP["COP = Q_E / Q_G\n(VAC, ideal)"] -.-> QE
    COP -.-> QG
    
    COMP["COP_Carnot = T_L / T_H - T_L\n(Upper Limit)"] -.-> COP
    
    NOTE["Note: COP_VAC is ALWAYS\nless than COP_Carnot"] -.-> COP
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term 'Absorbent' and 'Refrigerant' in the context of a Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System. Give one example of each used in a practical system.**

**Model Answer:**

In a Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS), the working pair consists of two fluids:
- **Refrigerant:** The fluid that undergoes the phase change (evaporation and condensation) to produce the cooling effect. It has a low boiling point and high latent heat of vaporization. *Example: Ammonia (NH₃) in NH₃-H₂O system; Water in H₂O-LiBr system.*
- **Absorbent:** The fluid that absorbs the refrigerant vapor at low pressure in the absorber. It has a high affinity for the refrigerant. *Example: Water in NH₃-H₂O system; Lithium Bromide in H₂O-LiBr system.*

*(3 marks: 1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for the example)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the expression for the maximum Coefficient of Performance (COP) of an ideal Vapor Absorption Refrigeration Cycle. Why is it always less than the Carnot COP?**

**Model Answer:**

**Expression for maximum COP:**

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_E (T_C - T_A)}{(T_C - T_E)(T_G - T_A)}$$

where $T_E, T_A, T_C, T_G$ are the absolute (Kelvin) temperatures of evaporator, absorber, condenser, and generator, respectively.

**Reason for being less than Carnot COP:**

The Carnot COP is $\frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}$ (assuming $T_C = T_H$ and $T_E = T_L$). In the absorption cycle, the second factor $\frac{T_C - T_A}{T_G - T_A}$ is always **less than 1** because $T_G > T_C$ (heat must be supplied at a temperature higher than the condensation temperature) and $T_A > T_E$ (absorption occurs above evaporator temperature). Therefore, the practical COP of a VAC is always less than the Carnot COP, reflecting the irreversibilities and finite temperature differences required for heat transfer.

*(3 marks: 2 marks for the formula + 1 mark for the explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**With a neat schematic, explain the working of a simple Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS). List the four main components and state the function of each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **simple Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** produces cooling by using heat energy instead of mechanical work as the primary input. The system operates on a binary fluid mixture and consists of the following **four main components** (plus auxiliary ones):

**1. Generator (or Boiler):**
- Receives the strong (rich) solution from the pump at high pressure.
- External heat $Q_G$ (from gas, steam, solar, or waste heat) is supplied to boil off the refrigerant vapor.
- Function: Separates refrigerant from the absorbent by boiling.

**2. Condenser:**
- The high-pressure refrigerant vapor from the generator is cooled and condensed into a liquid.
- Heat $Q_C$ is rejected to the surroundings (cooling water or air).
- Function: Phase change (vapor → liquid) at high pressure.

**3. Expansion Valve (Throttle):**
- The high-pressure liquid refrigerant is throttled to low pressure.
- Partial flashing occurs, producing a low-temperature liquid-vapor mixture.
- Function: Pressure reduction (isenthalpic process).

**4. Evaporator:**
- The low-pressure refrigerant absorbs heat $Q_E$ from the space to be cooled and vaporizes.
- Function: Produces the desired cooling effect.

**5. Absorber (additional but critical):**
- Low-pressure refrigerant vapor from the evaporator is absorbed by the weak (dilute) absorbent solution.
- Heat $Q_A$ is rejected during the exothermic absorption process.
- Function: Maintains low pressure by absorbing vapor (mimics the suction side of a compressor).

**6. Solution Pump (auxiliary):**
- Pumps the strong solution from the absorber (low pressure) to the generator (high pressure).
- Work input $W_p$ is very small.

**Working Cycle:**

$$\text{Evaporator} \rightarrow \text{Absorber} \rightarrow \text{Pump} \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{Condenser} \rightarrow \text{Expansion Valve} \rightarrow \text{Evaporator}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for component explanation, 2 marks for working cycle)*

---

#### **(b) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**A vapor absorption refrigeration system operates between an evaporator temperature of $-10°C$, an absorber temperature of $30°C$, a generator temperature of $110°C$, and a condenser temperature of $40°C$. Calculate the maximum Coefficient of Performance (COP) of the system and compare it with the Carnot COP operating between the same evaporator and condenser temperatures. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_E = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K}$
- $T_A = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$
- $T_G = 110 + 273 = 383 \text{ K}$
- $T_C = 40 + 273 = 313 \text{ K}$

**Step 1: Calculate Maximum COP of VAC**

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_E (T_C - T_A)}{(T_C - T_E)(T_G - T_A)}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{263 \times (313 - 303)}{(313 - 263)(383 - 303)}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{263 \times 10}{50 \times 80} = \frac{2630}{4000}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\max} = 0.6575$$

**Step 2: Calculate Carnot COP**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = 5.26$$

**Step 3: Comparison**

The ratio of the maximum VAC COP to the Carnot COP is:

$$\frac{\text{COP}_{\max,\text{VAC}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{0.6575}{5.26} \approx 0.125 = 12.5\%$$

The **Carnot COP is approximately 8 times higher** than the maximum VAC COP for the given conditions. This is due to the inherent irreversibility in the absorption and generation processes, which require finite temperature differences.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly forget to convert temperatures from **°C to Kelvin** before substitution. This single error leads to a completely wrong answer and **zero marks** for the calculation part. Also, ensure the formula is written with the correct arrangement of $T_C - T_A$ in the numerator (not $T_A - T_C$).

**Final Answer:**
- $\text{COP}_{\max,\text{VAC}} = 0.6575$
- $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = 5.26$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for setup, 3 marks for calculation, 2 marks for comparison and conclusion)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Compare the Vapor Absorption Refrigeration Cycle with the Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle on the basis of: (i) Energy input, (ii) COP, (iii) Moving parts, (iv) Applications, (v) Working fluid, and (vi) Maintenance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Vapor Compression Cycle (VCRC) | Vapor Absorption Cycle (VAC) |
|-----------|--------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **(i) Energy Input** | Mechanical work via electric motor-driven compressor | Primarily thermal energy (heat); very small pump work |
| **(ii) COP** | Higher COP, typically 3–6 for domestic and commercial systems | Lower COP, typically 0.4–1.2 for practical systems |
| **(iii) Moving Parts** | Many (compressor, motor, valves) | Few (only solution pump; can be designed with NO moving parts — Electrolux) |
| **(iv) Applications** | Domestic refrigerators, ACs, freezers, cold storage | Large industrial chillers, solar refrigeration, waste heat recovery, large cold storage |
| **(v) Working Fluid** | Single pure refrigerant (R-134a, R-22, NH₃, etc.) | Binary mixture (NH₃-H₂O, H₂O-LiBr) |
| **(vi) Maintenance** | Moderate to high (compressor overhauls, seal replacements) | Low (no compressor; long life; fewer breakdowns) |

**Key Conclusions:**
- VCRC is preferred for **small to medium capacities** where electricity is available and high COP is needed.
- VAC is preferred for **large capacities** and where **waste heat or solar energy** is available.
- VAC systems can be designed as **"silent absorbers"** (e.g., Electrolux refrigerator) with no moving parts at all.

*(7 marks: 6 marks for table + 1 mark for conclusion)*

---

#### **(b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the working principle of a Lithium Bromide-Water (LiBr-H₂O) Vapor Absorption System. Why is it not used for temperatures below 0°C? What is the function of the analyzer in this system? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle of LiBr-H₂O System:**

In the **LiBr-H₂O Vapor Absorption System**:
- **Refrigerant:** Water (H₂O) — the fluid that evaporates to produce cooling
- **Absorbent:** Lithium Bromide (LiBr) — a salt that has a strong affinity for water vapor

**Working Cycle:**

1. **Generator:** External heat (gas, steam) is supplied to the dilute LiBr solution. Water vapor is driven off, leaving a more concentrated LiBr solution.
2. **Condenser:** The water vapor is condensed to liquid water by rejecting heat to a cooling tower.
3. **Expansion Valve:** The high-pressure liquid water is throttled to low pressure.
4. **Evaporator:** At low pressure, water evaporates at a low temperature (typically 5–10°C), absorbing heat from the chilled water circuit → producing chilled water for air conditioning.
5. **Absorber:** The water vapor is absorbed by the strong LiBr solution, forming a dilute solution. Heat $Q_A$ is rejected.
6. **Solution Pump + Heat Exchanger:** The dilute solution is pumped back to the generator after being pre-heated in the heat exchanger using the hot weak solution returning from the generator.

**Why Not Used Below 0°C?**

The LiBr-H₂O system **cannot be used for refrigeration below 0°C** because:
- The refrigerant is **water**, which freezes at 0°C.
- At evaporator temperatures below 0°C, the water refrigerant would solidify (freeze), destroying the evaporator tubes.
- LiBr solutions are also prone to **crystallization** at high concentrations and low temperatures, further limiting the operating range.
- Therefore, the LiBr-H₂O system is restricted to **air conditioning applications** (typically 5°C and above), whereas **NH₃-H₂O systems** are used for industrial refrigeration below 0°C.

**Function of the Analyzer:**

The **analyzer** is a device fitted between the generator and the absorber (in the weak solution return line) that:
- **Monitors the concentration** of LiBr in the weak solution leaving the generator.
- **Controls the heat input** to the generator to maintain optimum concentration.
- **Prevents crystallization** of LiBr, which can occur if the solution becomes too concentrated.
- Ensures safe and efficient operation of the system.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for working principle, 2 marks for limitation, 2 marks for analyzer function)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Temperature Conversion:** Always convert °C to K before applying the COP formula. (Cost: 2-3 marks)
> 2. **Carnot vs. Practical COP:** Students often confuse the COP of an actual system with the Carnot COP. The Carnot COP is an *upper bound* — never achieved in practice. (Cost: 2 marks)
> 3. **Identifying Components:** Some students incorrectly label the absorber as the generator. Remember: **Generator = boiler (heat input)**, **Absorber = where refrigerant is absorbed (heat rejection)**. (Cost: 1-2 marks)
> 4. **Forgetting the Pump Work:** In rigorous problems, pump work is NOT always negligible. Calculate it as $W_p = \dot{V} \Delta P$. (Cost: 1-2 marks)
> 5. **Solution Concentration:** In NH₃-H₂O system, **NH₃ is the refrigerant and H₂O is the absorbent** (not the other way around). Memorize this carefully. (Cost: 1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

### **Core Concepts:**
- ✅ VAC uses **heat as primary energy input** (VCRC uses mechanical work).
- ✅ Four main components: **Generator, Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator** + **Absorber, Solution Pump**.
- ✅ Two pressure levels: **Low (evaporator, absorber)** and **High (generator, condenser)**.
- ✅ Binary working fluid: **NH₃-H₂O** (industrial) or **H₂O-LiBr** (air conditioning).

### **Key Formula:**
- ✅ $\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_E (T_C - T_A)}{(T_C - T_E)(T_G - T_A)}$
- ✅ Always use **absolute temperature (Kelvin)**.
- ✅ $\text{COP}_{\text{VAC}} < \text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}$ — always.

### **Numerical Problem Solving Steps:**
1. Convert all temperatures to Kelvin.
2. Identify $T_E, T_A, T_C, T_G$ from the problem statement.
3. Plug into the maximum COP formula.
4. Compare with Carnot COP if asked.
5. Calculate the ratio to express efficiency penalty.

### **Practical Modifications (KTU 2024 Emphasis):**
- **Rectifier**: Prevents absorbent carryover into condenser.
- **Heat Exchanger**: Improves COP by pre-heating strong solution.
- **Analyzer**: Monitors concentration to prevent LiBr crystallization.
- **Solution Pump**: Raises pressure of strong solution (small work input).

### **Memorize These Facts:**
- ✅ $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$
- ✅ NH₃ boils at $-33.3°C$ at atmospheric pressure.
- ✅ H₂O freezes at 0°C → LiBr-H₂O limited to AC applications.
- ✅ Practical VAC COP is **0.4 to 1.2**; VCRC COP is **3 to 6**.
- ✅ The **Electrolux refrigerator** is an example of a silent, no-moving-parts absorption system.

### **Comparison with VCRC (Common Question):**
- **VCRC**: Compressor, higher COP, electricity required, smaller capacities.
- **VAC**: No compressor (mostly), lower COP, heat input, larger capacities, can use waste heat.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of Refrigeration System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components of Refrigeration System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ❄️ COMPONENTS OF A REFRIGERATION SYSTEM

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** is a closed thermodynamic cycle that continuously extracts heat from a low-temperature reservoir (refrigerated space) and rejects it to a high-temperature reservoir (surroundings), using a working fluid termed a **refrigerant**. The system comprises **four essential components** through which the refrigerant circulates: the **compressor**, **condenser**, **expansion device (throttle)**, and **evaporator**, supported by several auxiliary components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
