> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Highlight:** A refrigeration cycle cannot exist without these four components working in **closed-loop sequence**. The cycle is driven by **external work input** (typically electrical energy supplied to the compressor).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blood Circulation Analogy"

Imagine your body’s **circulatory system**:
- The **heart** pumps blood (compressor pushes refrigerant).
- Blood travels to the **lungs** where CO₂ is released and O₂ absorbed (condenser releases heat, absorbs cooling).
- Blood passes through **narrow capillaries** slowing it down (expansion device reduces pressure and temperature).
- Blood reaches **body tissues** delivering oxygen (evaporator absorbs heat from the space to be cooled).

Just as oxygen sustains life by delivering nutrients, the refrigerant sustains "cooling" by **absorbing heat at low temperature and rejecting it at high temperature**.

## 1.3 The Four Essential Components — Quick Overview

| # | Component | Function in Plain English | State of Refrigerant |
|---|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Compressor** | Acts as the "heart"; pumps refrigerant and raises its pressure/temperature | Low-P Vapour → High-P Vapour |
| 2 | **Condenser** | Acts as the "heat dump"; rejects heat to surroundings | High-P Vapour → High-P Liquid |
| 3 | **Expansion Device** | Acts as the "pressure brake"; drops pressure suddenly | High-P Liquid → Low-P Mixture |
| 4 | **Evaporator** | Acts as the "heat sponge"; absorbs heat from cold space | Low-P Mixture → Low-P Vapour |

## 1.4 Role of the Refrigerant

The refrigerant is the **working fluid** that undergoes phase changes (evaporation and condensation) to transport heat. KTU emphasizes selection of refrigerants based on:
- **Boiling point** well below the desired cold temperature.
- **Condensing pressure** not excessively high.
- **Zero Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP = 0)** and **Low Global Warming Potential (GWP < 10)** preferred (e.g., R-134a, R-1234yf, R-290, CO₂, NH₃).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Metric:** Domestic refrigerators operate with evaporator temperatures around **−23 °C to −10 °C** and condenser temperatures around **35 °C to 55 °C**.

## 1.5 Auxiliary Components

These support the main four and are frequently asked in KTU exams:

- **Receiver** — Stores excess liquid refrigerant and ensures only liquid enters the expansion device.
- **Filter-Drier** — Removes moisture and contaminants.
- **Sight Glass** — Visual indicator of refrigerant state and moisture level.
- **Accumulator** — Prevents liquid slugging at the compressor suction.
- **Solenoid Valve & LP/HP Cut-outs** — Safety controls.
- **Thermostat** — Regulates evaporator temperature.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Compressor — The Work Input Device

### Function
- Sucks low-pressure refrigerant vapour from the evaporator.
- Compresses it to a high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour.
- Maintains unidirectional flow.

### Types (KTU Expected)
1. **Reciprocating (Piston)** — Most common in domestic fridges; high-pressure ratio capability.
2. **Rotary (Rolling Piston, Vane)** — Compact, used in window ACs.
3. **Scroll** — Two interleaving scrolls; quiet, used in modern split ACs.
4. **Screw** — Used in large industrial chilling.
5. **Centrifugal** — Used in very large commercial systems (chillers).

### Why & How
- **Why?** Pressure must be raised so that the refrigerant’s saturation temperature becomes *higher* than ambient, allowing heat rejection in the condenser.
- **How?** By consuming **shaft work** $\dot{W}_{in}$.

## 2.2 Condenser — The Heat Rejector

### Function
- Removes **latent and sensible heat** from the high-pressure refrigerant.
- Converts saturated/superheated vapour → saturated liquid.

### Types
1. **Air-cooled** (finned-tube with fan) — domestic fridges, split ACs.
2. **Water-cooled** (shell-and-coil, shell-and-tube) — large chillers.
3. **Evaporative** — uses water spray + air; industrial applications.

### Three Sub-Stages Inside
- **De-superheating** (sensible cooling of superheated vapour).
- **Condensation** (latent heat removal at constant P, T).
- **Sub-cooling** (sensible cooling of liquid below saturation T).

## 2.3 Expansion Device — The Pressure Reducer

### Function
- Drops pressure suddenly — a **throttling (isenthalpic) process**.
- Causes **flash evaporation**, dropping refrigerant temperature below that of the cold space.

### Types
1. **Capillary Tube** — Fixed bore, used in small hermetic systems.
2. **Throttling Valve (Manual)** — Used in large plants.
3. **Automatic Expansion Valve (AXV)** — Maintains constant evaporator pressure.
4. **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV/TEV)** — Most common; modulates flow based on evaporator outlet superheat. **High-yield for KTU.**

## 2.4 Evaporator — The Heat Absorber

### Function
- Refrigerant evaporates by absorbing latent heat from the refrigerated space.
- Provides the **useful cooling effect**.

### Types
1. **Direct Expansion (DX)** — Refrigerant flows inside tubes; common in ACs.
2. **Flooded** — Liquid fills shell, evaporation over tubes; better control.
3. **Shell-and-Coil / Shell-and-Tube** — Industrial use.
4. **Plate Evaporator** — Compact, modern refrigeration.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refer to the following table for all numerical problems in Section 3 and Section 5.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Refrigeration Effect (RE) | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed per kg of refrigerant |
| 2 | Compressor Work | $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Specific work input |
| 3 | Heat Rejected in Condenser | $q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | Specific heat rejection |
| 4 | Throttling Condition | $h_3 = h_4$ | kJ/kg | Isenthalpic expansion |
| 5 | Coefficient of Performance (COP) | $COP = \dfrac{RE}{w_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | Cycle efficiency |
| 6 | COP (Energy Balance form) | $COP = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | dimensionless | Useful effect over input |
| 7 | Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_L}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | For required cooling capacity |
| 8 | Compressor Power | $\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \, (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Actual shaft work |
| 9 | Condenser Heat Rejection Rate | $\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m} \, (h_2 - h_3)$ | kW | Rate of heat dumped |
| 10 | Energy Balance on Whole Cycle | $\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_c$ | kW | First Law check |

> [!NOTE]
> **State Point Convention Used Throughout KTU:**  
> 1 → Compressor Inlet (Superheated Vapour)  
> 2 → Compressor Outlet (Superheated Vapour)  
> 3 → Condenser Outlet (Saturated/Subcooled Liquid)  
> 4 → Evaporator Inlet (Liquid-Vapour Mixture)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic refrigerators & freezers** — single-stage VCRS with capillary tube.
- **Cold storage warehouses** — multi-evaporator systems with TXV.
- **Air-conditioning plants** — DX chillers, scroll/screw compressors.
- **Pharmaceutical & food industry** — precise temperature control via flooded evaporators.
- **Ice plants & cryogenics** — cascade systems (advanced; covered in higher semesters).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of COP of an Ideal Vapour Compression Cycle

We apply the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to each component, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes and assuming no heat loss in connecting lines.

### Step 1: Compressor (1 → 2)

SFEE per unit mass:
$$q - w = h_2 - h_1$$

Since the compressor is adiabatic ($q = 0$) and work is done *on* the system:
$$w_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{(compressor work input, kJ/kg)}$$

**Logic:** The enthalpy rise equals the work consumed by the compressor.

### Step 2: Condenser (2 → 3)

Adiabatic exit but heat is rejected *from* the refrigerant:
$$q_H = h_2 - h_3 \quad \text{(heat rejected per kg)}$$

**Logic:** Latent + sensible heat removed reduces the enthalpy of the refrigerant from superheated vapour to saturated liquid.

### Step 3: Expansion Device (3 → 4)

This is a **throttling process** — adiabatic with no work and negligible kinetic energy change:
$$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{(isenthalpic)}$$

**Logic:** Pressure drops without any energy exchange; the temperature falls due to flash evaporation of a small fraction of the liquid.

### Step 4: Evaporator (4 → 1)

Heat absorbed from refrigerated space:
$$q_L = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{(refrigeration effect)}$$

**Logic:** The refrigerant gains latent heat as it fully evaporates.

### Step 5: Combining to Get COP

By definition:
$$COP = \dfrac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \dfrac{q_L}{w_c}$$

Substituting:
$$COP = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

### Step 6: Overall Energy Balance (Sanity Check)

For the complete cycle (1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 1):
$$\sum w = \sum q$$
$$(h_2 - h_1) = (h_2 - h_3) - (h_1 - h_4)$$
$$w_c = q_H - q_L$$

Hence:
$$q_H = q_L + w_c$$

This satisfies the **First Law of Thermodynamics** — the condenser dumps *all* the heat absorbed plus the equivalent of the compressor work.

## 3.2 Derivation — Mass Flow Rate and Capacity Relations

If the **required cooling load** is $\dot{Q}_L$ in kW:
$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_L}{q_L} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_L}{h_1 - h_4} \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

Then:
$$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) \quad [\text{kW}]$$
$$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_3) \quad [\text{kW}]$$

**Logic:** Once $\dot{m}$ is determined from the desired cooling effect, all other rates follow directly from the enthalpy table for the chosen refrigerant.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component Comparison (High-Yield Table for KTU)

| Criterion | Reciprocating | Scroll | Screw | Centrifugal |
|-----------|---------------|--------|-------|-------------|
| Capacity Range (TR) | 0.1 – 100 | 1 – 60 | 20 – 1500 | 100 – 10,000 |
| Compression Mechanism | Piston in cylinder | Orbiting scroll | Rotating screws | Impeller |
| Isentropic Efficiency | 70 – 85 % | 75 – 90 % | 80 – 90 % | 85 – 95 % |
| Maintenance | High | Low | Medium | Low |
| Noise Level | High | Low | Medium | Very Low |
| Typical Application | Domestic, Commercial | Modern ACs | Industrial chillers | Large HVAC plants |

| Criterion | Air-Cooled | Water-Cooled | Evaporative |
|-----------|------------|--------------|-------------|
| Heat Sink | Ambient Air | Cooling Water + Tower | Air + Water Spray |
| Site Requirement | Open space | Cooling tower nearby | Outdoor placement |
| Water Consumption | Zero | Moderate | High |
| Typical COP | Lower | Higher | Highest |

| Criterion | Capillary Tube | TXV | AXV |
|-----------|----------------|-----|-----|
| Control | Fixed (non-adjustable) | Modulating (superheat-based) | Constant pressure |
| Load Tracking | Poor | Excellent | Moderate |
| Application | Domestic fridges | Split ACs, chillers | Medium commercial |
| Complexity | Simplest | Moderate | Moderate |

| Criterion | DX Evaporator | Flooded Evaporator |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------|
| Liquid Level | Low (circulation ratio ~ 1) | High (tube fully wetted) |
| Superheat Control | Easy | Difficult |
| Heat Transfer Coefficient | Lower | Higher |
| Application | AC units, freezers | Industrial chillers |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Step-by-Step (For Reference)

> **Given:** A refrigeration system using **R-134a** operates between evaporator pressure 0.14 MPa and condenser pressure 0.8 MPa. Compressor inlet is superheated at −10 °C. Condenser exit is saturated liquid.  
> $h_1 = 243.34$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 271.71$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = h_4 = 95.47$ kJ/kg  
> Required cooling load $\dot{Q}_L = 7.4$ kW.

**Step 1 — RE per kg:**
$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 243.34 - 95.47 = 147.87 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate:**
$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{7.4}{147.87} = 0.0500 \;\text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compressor power:**
$$\dot{W}_c = 0.05 \times (271.71 - 243.34) = 0.05 \times 28.37 = 1.4185 \;\text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — COP:**
$$COP = \dfrac{147.87}{28.37} = 5.21$$

**Step 5 — Condenser heat rejection:**
$$\dot{Q}_H = 0.05 \times (271.71 - 95.47) = 0.05 \times 176.24 = 8.812 \;\text{kW}$$

**Step 6 — Energy balance check:**
$$\dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_c = 7.4 + 1.4185 = 8.8185 \;\text{kW} \approx \dot{Q}_H \;\checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram of VCRS

```mermaid
graph LR
    COMP[Compressor] -->|State 2 High P High T Vapour| COND[Condenser]
    COND -->|State 3 High P Saturated Liquid| REC[Receiver and Filter Drier]
    REC -->|State 3 High P Liquid| EXP[Expansion Device Throttle]
    EXP -->|State 4 Low P Low T Mixture| EVAP[Evaporator]
    EVAP -->|State 1 Low P Superheated Vapour| ACCU[Accumulator]
    ACCU --> COMP
    SPACE[Refrigerated Space] -->|Heat Load QL| EVAP
    COND -->|Heat Rejection QH| SUR[Surroundings]
    POW[Electrical Power Source] -->|Work Input Wc| COMP
```

## 4.2 Component Block Architecture (Detailed)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SUBSYSTEM_A[Refrigerant Flow Path]
        A1[Compressor] -->|Raise Pressure and Temperature| A2[Condenser]
        A2 -->|Reject QH to Surroundings| A3[Receiver Tank]
        A3 -->|Store Excess Liquid| A4[Filter Drier]
        A4 -->|Remove Moisture| A5[Sight Glass]
        A5 -->|Visual Check| A6[Expansion Valve TXV]
        A6 -->|Drop Pressure Isenthalpic| A7[Distributor]
        A7 -->|Distribute to Circuits| A8[Evaporator]
        A8 -->|Absorb QL from Space| A9[Accumulator]
        A9 -->|Prevent Liquid Slug| A1
    end
    subgraph SUBSYSTEM_B[Control and Safety]
        B1[Thermostat] -.->|Sensing T| A8
        B2[Solenoid Valve] -.->|ON OFF Control| A6
        B3[LP HP Cutout Switch] -.->|Safety Trip| A1
    end
    subgraph SUBSYSTEM_C[Heat Transfer]
        C1[Refrigerated Chamber] -->|Load on Evaporator| A8
        C2[Ambient Air or Cooling Tower] -->|Sink for Condenser| A2
    end
```

## 4.3 P-h Diagram with State Points (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PH[P-h Diagram of VCRS]
        S1["Point 1: Compressor Inlet Superheated Vapour"] -->|Process 1-2 Isentropic Compression| S2["Point 2: Compressor Outlet Superheated Vapour"]
        S2 -->|Process 2-3 Heat Rejection Constant P| S3["Point 3: Condenser Outlet Saturated Liquid"]
        S3 -->|Process 3-4 Isenthalpic Throttling h constant| S4["Point 4: Evaporator Inlet Two Phase Mixture"]
        S4 -->|Process 4-1 Heat Absorption Constant P Evaporation| S1
    end
```

## 4.4 Schematic Sketch — Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)

```mermaid
graph LR
    LIQ[Liquid Refrigerant In from Condenser] -->|High P Liquid| DIA[Diaphragm]
    DIA -->|Push Down via Spring| PINV[Needle Pin Valve]
    PINV -->|Throttling| EVAP[Low P Liquid Vapour Mixture to Evaporator]
    EVAP -->|Sensed Outlet T and P| BULB[Thermal Bulb at Evaporator Outlet]
    BULB -->|Capillary Tube with Fluid| TOP[Top of Diaphragm]
    SPRING[Sensing Spring] -->|Balancing Force| DIA
    DIA -->|Net Motion| PINV
```

**Working Logic of TXV (Step-by-Step):**
1. The **bulb** senses the evaporator outlet temperature and transmits pressure to the top of the diaphragm.
2. The **evaporator pressure** pushes up from below the diaphragm.
3. The **spring** sets the desired superheat (typically 4–8 °C).
4. The **needle** moves to maintain equilibrium — opening more when load increases and closing when load drops.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List the four essential components of a vapour compression refrigeration system and state the function of each in one line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Compressor** – Compresses low-pressure refrigerant vapour to high-pressure, high-temperature vapour using external work.
2. **Condenser** – Rejects heat from the high-pressure refrigerant to the surroundings, condensing it into a liquid.
3. **Expansion Device** – Reduces pressure of the liquid refrigerant isenthalpically, causing a temperature drop.
4. **Evaporator** – Absorbs heat from the refrigerated space as the refrigerant evaporates at low pressure.

*(1 mark for naming, 2 marks for correctly stating the four functions.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is the throttling process in an expansion valve considered isenthalpic? Mention its significance in a refrigeration cycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A throttling process is a steady-flow process through a constriction with **no heat transfer (adiabatic), no work interaction, and negligible kinetic/potential energy changes**. Applying the SFEE:
$$h_3 = h_4$$

**Significance:**
- It is the **only process** that causes the drastic drop in refrigerant temperature (because no external cooling is required).
- Without throttling, evaporation could not occur at the low evaporator pressure, and the cycle would fail to absorb heat at low temperature.

*(1 mark for derivation, 2 marks for significance.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### 🟢 QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]` *(Option 1)*

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a vapour compression refrigeration system. Label the four components and the state points clearly. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A VCRS operates in a **closed loop** with four components. Working steps:

1. **Process 1 → 2 (Compression):** The compressor (state 1, low-pressure vapour) raises the pressure and temperature of the refrigerant to state 2 (high-pressure superheated vapour). Work input $w_c = h_2 - h_1$.

2. **Process 2 → 3 (Condensation):** The hot vapour enters the condenser where it rejects heat to surroundings (air/water), first de-superheating, then condensing to a saturated liquid (state 3). Heat rejected $q_H = h_2 - h_3$.

3. **Process 3 → 4 (Expansion):** Liquid passes through the throttle/expansion valve. The pressure drops sharply; a small fraction flashes into vapour, cooling the remaining liquid (state 4). The process is isenthalpic: $h_3 = h_4$.

4. **Process 4 → 1 (Evaporation):** The cold low-pressure liquid-vapour mixture flows through the evaporator, absorbing heat from the refrigerated space. It fully evaporates to state 1 (low-pressure vapour). Heat absorbed $q_L = h_1 - h_4$.

The cycle repeats continuously, providing sustained cooling.

*(3 marks block diagram, 4 marks for explaining each process with enthalpy equations.)*

---

**(b) Explain the construction and working of a Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) with a neat sketch. Why is it preferred over a capillary tube? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A TXV consists of:
- A **thermal bulb** clamped at the evaporator outlet.
- A **capillary tube** connecting the bulb to the top of the diaphragm chamber.
- A **diaphragm** that actuates the valve pin.
- A **superheat spring** setting the desired superheat.
- An **equalizer line** (internal or external) sensing evaporator outlet pressure.

**Working (3 marks):**
- When the evaporator load increases, outlet superheat rises.
- The bulb senses the higher temperature, increasing its internal fluid pressure.
- This pushes the diaphragm downward, opening the valve pin further.
- More refrigerant flows, increasing evaporation rate until superheat returns to the set value.
- The cycle modulates continuously to maintain optimum superheat.

**Why preferred over capillary tube (1 mark):**
- A capillary tube has a **fixed flow rate** and cannot adapt to varying load.
- TXV provides **modulating flow control**, maintaining **constant superheat** and ensuring efficient compressor operation under all loads.

---

### 🟢 QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` *(Option 2)*

**(a) A refrigeration system using R-134a operates between evaporator pressure 0.14 MPa and condenser pressure 0.8 MPa. The compressor handles 0.05 kg/s of refrigerant with inlet enthalpy $h_1 = 243.34$ kJ/kg and outlet enthalpy $h_2 = 271.71$ kJ/kg. The condenser outlet enthalpy is $h_3 = 95.47$ kJ/kg. Determine: (i) the refrigeration effect, (ii) the compressor work, (iii) the condenser heat rejection, (iv) the COP, and (v) the cooling capacity in kW. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\dot{m} = 0.05$ kg/s, $h_1 = 243.34$, $h_2 = 271.71$, $h_3 = 95.47$ kJ/kg; throttling ⇒ $h_4 = h_3 = 95.47$ kJ/kg.

**(i) Refrigeration Effect:**
$$q_L = h_1 - h_4 = 243.34 - 95.47 = 147.87 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Compressor Work:**
$$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 271.71 - 243.34 = 28.37 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

**(iii) Condenser Heat Rejection:**
$$q_H = h_2 - h_3 = 271.71 - 95.47 = 176.24 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

**(iv) Coefficient of Performance:**
$$COP = \dfrac{q_L}{w_c} = \dfrac{147.87}{28.37} = 5.21$$

**(v) Cooling Capacity:**
$$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m} \cdot q_L = 0.05 \times 147.87 = 7.3935 \;\text{kW}$$

*(Mark split: 1 mark each for sub-parts i–v.)*

---

**(b) Differentiate between air-cooled and water-cooled condensers. Mention any two situations where each is preferred. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Air-Cooled Condenser | Water-Cooled Condenser |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Cooling Medium | Ambient Air | Cooling Water |
| Heat Transfer Coefficient | Lower (5–50 W/m²K) | Higher (500–5000 W/m²K) |
| Equipment Size | Larger for same capacity | Compact |
| Water Consumption | Nil | High |
| Power Consumption (Fan/Pump) | Fan power moderate | Pump + tower power higher |
| Capital Cost | Low | High |
| Maintenance | Simple | Needs water treatment |
| COP | Lower | Higher |

**Preferred Situations:**

**Air-Cooled (2 marks):**
1. **Domestic refrigerators and split ACs** — where water is scarce.
2. **Remote locations** — where cooling tower installation is impractical.

**Water-Cooled (2 marks):**
1. **Large industrial chillers (> 100 TR)** — where high heat transfer rate justifies water cost.
2. **Chemical and pharmaceutical plants** — where precise temperature control and high COP are essential.

*(Mark split: 3 marks table, 4 marks applications.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **State-point confusion:** Students frequently write $h_4$ from a saturated liquid table at evaporator pressure — this is **WRONG**. By throttling, $h_4 = h_3$ (enthalpy at condenser pressure side, not evaporator side). Use $h_3$ at high pressure and assume $h_4 = h_3$.
> 2. **Unit errors in mass flow rate:** Using tonnes of refrigeration (TR) instead of kW. Remember: **1 TR = 3.517 kW**.
> 3. **Forgetting First Law check:** Always verify $q_H = q_L + w_c$. Examiners reward cross-checks.
> 4. **COP written without units clarification:** Mention "dimensionless" or "no units" to be safe.
> 5. **Confusing TXV with AXV:** TXV controls **superheat**; AXV controls **evaporator pressure**. This is a favourite KTU question.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch in long answers:** A neat labelled diagram is mandatory for full marks in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- A **VCRS** has **4 essential components**: Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Device → Evaporator, in that exact cyclic order.
- The cycle is driven by **external work input** at the compressor — there is no internal combustion.
- **Refrigeration Effect (RE)** per kg = $h_1 - h_4$ — the **useful output**.
- **Compressor work** per kg = $h_2 - h_1$ — the **required input**.
- **COP = RE / Work** = $\frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ — the standard performance metric (higher = better).
- Throttling is **isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$); pressure drops but no temperature is "set" by external means — it is a consequence of flash evaporation.
- **First Law Check:** $q_H = q_L + w_c$ — always verify in numerical problems.
- **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \dot{Q}_L / (h_1 - h_4)$.
- **TXV** modulates flow to maintain **constant superheat** (typically 4–8 °C) — preferred over fixed capillary tubes for variable loads.
- **Reciprocating compressors** are most common in domestic systems; **scroll and screw** for commercial; **centrifugal** for large HVAC plants.
- **Air-cooled condensers** are used where water is scarce; **water-cooled** for high-COP industrial applications.
- Always **draw a labelled diagram** — KTU examiners reward it heavily (often 2–3 marks of a 7-mark question).
- **SI Units:** Use **kW** for power, **kJ/kg** for specific quantities, **kg/s** for mass flow rate.
- **TR ↔ kW conversion:** 1 TR = 3.517 kW; 1 kW = 0.2843 TR.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Working Substance** (also termed as the *thermodynamic medium* or *operating fluid*) is defined as the substance that circulates through a thermodynamic system and undergoes continuous state changes to facilitate the conversion of heat into work, or work into heat. It is the "blood" of any thermal system, whether it is a steam power plant, a refrigerator, an internal combustion engine, or a gas turbine.

Mathematically, the working substance is characterized by its **Equation of State (EOS)**, a functional relationship between the three fundamental thermodynamic coordinates — Pressure ($P$), Specific Volume ($v$), and Temperature ($T$):

$$f(P, v, T) = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The selection of a working substance (water, air, refrigerants like R-134a, R-12, ammonia, etc.) is critical to the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** and the **First-Law Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$)** of any cycle. No thermal device can function without a well-defined working fluid.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Delivery Truck" Viewpoint

Imagine a busy **courier hub** (the thermal system). The **trucks** that ferry goods from the *loading dock* (heat source) to the *delivery zone* (heat sink) are the **working substance**. The trucks pick up cargo at the source, travel along a designated route (the cycle), and unload at the destination.

- **In a Steam Engine:** Water/Steam is the truck. It picks up heat in the boiler, expands in the turbine (delivers "work"), and dumps the waste heat in the condenser.
- **In a Refrigerator:** The refrigerant (e.g., R-134a) is the truck. It absorbs heat from the cold reservoir (inside the fridge) and dumps it in the hot reservoir (the room).
- **In an IC Engine:** The air-fuel mixture acts as the working substance, undergoing combustion, expansion, and exhaust.

> [!NOTE]
> The thermodynamic properties of the working substance (Internal Energy $u$, Enthalpy $h$, Entropy $s$) dictate the magnitude of work and heat transfers at every state point. The accuracy of a thermal analysis is therefore only as good as our model of the working fluid.

## 1.3 Classification of Working Substances

Working substances used in KTU-level thermodynamics are broadly classified into three categories:

| Category | Examples | Governing Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Gases** | Air, Nitrogen, Oxygen, Helium | Obey $Pv = RT$ strictly at all conditions |
| **Real Gases** | CO₂, NH₃, refrigerants at high pressure | Exhibit deviation from ideal gas law |
| **Pure Substances / Vapors** | Steam (H₂O), Mercury | Can exist in two-phase (saturated) regions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Universal Gas Constant** $R_u = \mathbf{8.314 \; kJ/(kmol \cdot K)}$ is the cornerstone constant for ideal-gas analysis. For a specific gas, the characteristic gas constant is $R = R_u / M$, where $M$ is the **molar mass in kg/kmol**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ideal Gas Model — Postulates and Behaviour

A gas is said to behave as an **Ideal Gas** when it satisfies the following microscopic postulates:

1. The molecules are point masses (negligible volume compared to container volume).
2. There is **no intermolecular force** of attraction or repulsion.
3. Collisions between molecules and with the container walls are **perfectly elastic**.
4. The gas obeys **Boyle's Law**, **Charles's Law**, and **Avogadro's Law** simultaneously.

### 2.1.1 Ideal Gas Equation (Combined Gas Law)

Combining Boyle's Law ($P \propto 1/v$ at constant $T$) and Charles's Law ($v \propto T$ at constant $P$) with Avogadro's Hypothesis:

$$Pv = RT \quad \text{(per unit mass)}$$

$$PV = mRT \quad \text{(for mass } m\text{)}$$

$$PV = nR_uT \quad \text{(for } n \text{ moles, where } R_u = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/kmol·K)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The quantity $R = R_u / M$ is the **specific gas constant** (in kJ/kg·K), where $M$ is the molar mass. For air, $M = 28.97$ kg/kmol, giving $R_{air} = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K. This number is a **permanent fixture** in KTU numerical problems.

## 2.2 Limitations of the Ideal Gas Assumption

The ideal gas model **fails** under the following conditions:

- **High Pressure** — Molecular volumes become non-negligible compared to container volume.
- **Low Temperature (near condensation point)** — Intermolecular forces become dominant.
- **Near saturation** — Phase change phenomena take over.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often apply $Pv = RT$ to **saturated steam** or refrigerants in the two-phase region. This is **mathematically wrong**; for a two-phase mixture, we use the **Quality ($x$)** concept and steam tables instead.

## 2.3 Real Gases and the Compressibility Factor ($Z$)

To bridge the gap between ideal and real gas behaviour, we introduce the **Compressibility Factor**:

$$Z = \frac{Pv}{RT} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad Z = \frac{PV}{mRT}$$

- For an **Ideal Gas**: $Z = 1$ at all $P$ and $T$.
- For a **Real Gas**: $Z$ deviates from unity. At low pressure, $Z \to 1$. At moderate pressure and low temperature, $Z < 1$ (attractive forces dominate). At very high pressure, $Z > 1$ (repulsive forces dominate).

### 2.3.1 The Principle of Corresponding States

By introducing **reduced (dimensionless) properties**, the behaviour of all real gases can be correlated onto a single **Generalized Compressibility Chart** (the Nelson-Obert chart, which KTU questions frequently reference):

$$P_R = \frac{P}{P_{cr}}, \qquad T_R = \frac{T}{T_{cr}}$$

where $P_{cr}$ and $T_{cr}$ are the **critical pressure** and **critical temperature** of the substance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** The **Law of Corresponding States** states that *all substances at the same reduced pressure and reduced temperature have the same compressibility factor $Z$*. This is a very common 7-mark question in Part B.

## 2.4 Equations of State for Real Gases

Several analytical equations of state attempt to model real-gas behaviour:

### 2.4.1 Van der Waals Equation (1873)

The **first successful EOS** to account for molecular volume and intermolecular forces:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$$

where $a$ and $b$ are Van der Waals constants, related to critical properties by:

$$a = \frac{27 R^2 T_{cr}^2}{64 P_{cr}}, \qquad b = \frac{R T_{cr}}{8 P_{cr}}$$

### 2.4.2 Redlich-Kwong (RK) Equation

A more accurate two-parameter EOS:

$$P = \frac{RT}{v - b} - \frac{a}{T^{0.5} \, v(v + b)}$$

### 2.4.3 Peng-Robinson (PR) Equation

A modern three-parameter EOS used widely in chemical engineering and refrigeration cycle design:

$$P = \frac{RT}{v - b} - \frac{a\alpha}{v(v + b) + b(v - b)}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

| # | Formula | Meaning / Boundary Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Pv = RT$ | Ideal gas, single-phase region | $P$ in kPa, $v$ in m³/kg, $T$ in K |
| 2 | $R = R_u / M$ | Specific gas constant | kJ/kg·K |
| 3 | $Z = Pv / RT$ | Compressibility factor | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $P_R = P / P_{cr}$ | Reduced pressure | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $T_R = T / T_{cr}$ | Reduced temperature | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\left(P + \frac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$ | Van der Waals EOS | Mixed SI |
| 7 | $a = \frac{27 R^2 T_{cr}^2}{64 P_{cr}}$ | VdW attraction constant | Pa·(m³/kg)² |
| 8 | $b = \frac{R T_{cr}}{8 P_{cr}}$ | VdW covolume constant | m³/kg |
| 9 | $v_{cr} = 3b$ | Critical specific volume (VdW) | m³/kg |
| 10 | $Z_{cr} = 3/8 = 0.375$ | VdW critical compressibility | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $T_{Boyle} = \frac{a}{Rb}$ | Boyle temperature (real gas → ideal) | K |
| 12 | $PV = mR_uT / M$ | Combined gas law (molar form) | P·V in kJ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Real-gas analysis is the foundation of **LNG storage tank design**, **supercritical CO₂ power cycles** (a hot research area), and **refrigeration compressor** inlet design where suction-state refrigerants are dense, non-ideal fluids.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Boyle's Temperature from the Van der Waals Equation

We want to find the temperature at which a real gas behaves ideally ($Z \to 1$) over an infinitesimal pressure range.

**Step 1.** Write the VdW equation in terms of molar volume $V_m$:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{V_m^2}$$

**Step 2.** Define the compressibility factor in molar form: $Z = \dfrac{PV_m}{RT}$.

**Step 3.** Substitute for $P$:

$$Z = \frac{V_m}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{RTV_m}$$

**Step 4.** Expand the first term using the binomial series for $b \ll V_m$:

$$\frac{V_m}{V_m - b} = \frac{1}{1 - b/V_m} \approx 1 + \frac{b}{V_m} + \left(\frac{b}{V_m}\right)^2 + \cdots$$

**Step 5.** Substitute and retain only first-order terms:

$$Z \approx 1 + \frac{b}{V_m} - \frac{a}{RTV_m}$$

**Step 6.** Express in terms of $P$ using ideal gas approximation $V_m \approx RT/P$:

$$Z \approx 1 + \frac{bP}{RT} - \frac{aP}{(RT)^2}$$

**Step 7.** Group the $P$-dependent terms into a single bracket:

$$Z \approx 1 + \frac{P}{RT}\left(b - \frac{a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 8.** For the gas to behave ideally ($Z = 1$) at low $P$, the bracket must vanish:

$$b - \frac{a}{RT_{Boyle}} = 0$$

**Step 9.** Solve for the **Boyle Temperature**:

$$\boxed{T_{Boyle} = \frac{a}{Rb}}$$

This is the temperature above which the real gas exhibits *positive deviation* from ideal gas behaviour, and below which it shows *negative deviation* at moderate pressures.

## 3.2 Derivation: Van der Waals Constants in Terms of Critical Properties

We use the critical-point conditions: at the critical point, the isotherm has a horizontal inflection, so:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T_{cr}} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial v^2}\right)_{T_{cr}} = 0$$

**Step 1.** VdW equation solved for $P$:

$$P = \frac{RT}{v - b} - \frac{a}{v^2}$$

**Step 2.** First derivative w.r.t. $v$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_T = -\frac{RT}{(v-b)^2} + \frac{2a}{v^3}$$

**Step 3.** Second derivative:

$$\left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial v^2}\right)_T = \frac{2RT}{(v-b)^3} - \frac{6a}{v^4}$$

**Step 4.** At the critical point, both derivatives vanish. Setting them to zero and dividing the first by the second appropriately:

$$-\frac{RT_{cr}}{(v_{cr}-b)^2} + \frac{2a}{v_{cr}^3} = 0$$

$$\frac{2RT_{cr}}{(v_{cr}-b)^3} - \frac{6a}{v_{cr}^4} = 0$$

**Step 5.** Dividing the second equation by 2 and the first by the second, after some algebra:

$$v_{cr} = 3b$$

**Step 6.** Substitute back into the first derivative condition:

$$T_{cr} = \frac{8a}{27Rb}$$

**Step 7.** Substitute $v_{cr}$ and $T_{cr}$ into the original VdW equation to get $P_{cr}$:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{a}{27b^2}$$

**Step 8.** Invert these to express $a$ and $b$ in terms of $P_{cr}$ and $T_{cr}$:

$$\boxed{a = \frac{27 R^2 T_{cr}^2}{64 P_{cr}}} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{b = \frac{R T_{cr}}{8 P_{cr}}}$$

**Step 9.** Compute the critical compressibility factor (a constant for *all* VdW fluids):

$$Z_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr} v_{cr}}{RT_{cr}} = \frac{P_{cr} (3b)}{R T_{cr}} = \frac{3P_{cr} \cdot \frac{RT_{cr}}{8P_{cr}}}{R T_{cr}} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

> [!NOTE]
> The VdW model predicts $Z_{cr} = 0.375$ for every gas. In reality, $Z_{cr}$ ranges from **0.23 to 0.33** for most real substances, which proves the VdW equation is approximate. KTU examiners love testing this very fact.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Using the Compressibility Chart

**Problem:** A cylinder contains **2 kg** of methane ($M = 16 \; \text{kg/kmol}$, $T_{cr} = 191 \; \text{K}$, $P_{cr} = 4.64 \; \text{MPa}$) at $T = 300 \; \text{K}$ and $P = 10 \; \text{MPa}$. Find the volume of the cylinder using:
1. The Ideal Gas Law.
2. The Compressibility Chart (assume $Z = 0.86$ at the given reduced conditions).

**Solution (a) — Ideal Gas Approach:**

$$R = \frac{R_u}{M} = \frac{8.314}{16} = 0.5196 \; \text{kJ/kg·K} = 519.6 \; \text{J/kg·K}$$

Convert pressure: $P = 10 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa}$, $T = 300 \; \text{K}$, $m = 2 \; \text{kg}$.

$$V = \frac{mRT}{P} = \frac{2 \times 519.6 \times 300}{10 \times 10^6} = 0.03118 \; \text{m}^3$$

**Solution (b) — Real Gas (Compressibility Chart) Approach:**

$$P_R = \frac{10}{4.64} = 2.155, \quad T_R = \frac{300}{191} = 1.571$$

$$V_{real} = Z \cdot V_{ideal} = 0.86 \times 0.03118 = 0.02682 \; \text{m}^3$$

**% Error introduced by ideal gas assumption:**

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{V_{ideal} - V_{real}}{V_{real}} \times 100 = \frac{0.03118 - 0.02682}{0.02682} \times 100 = 16.26\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A 16% error is unacceptable in industrial design. This is exactly why engineers use compressibility charts or cubic EOS for **high-pressure vessels**, **LNG storage**, and **supercritical refrigeration**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Working Substances

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[WORKING SUBSTANCES IN THERMAL SYSTEMS] --> nodeB[GASES]
    nodeA --> nodeC[PURE SUBSTANCES AND VAPORS]
    nodeA --> nodeD[TWO PHASE MIXTURES]
    nodeB --> nodeE[IDEAL GAS MODEL]
    nodeB --> nodeF[REAL GAS MODEL]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[PV EQUALS RT]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Z EQUALS ONE FOR ALL P AND T]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[VAN DER WAALS EOS]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[REDLICH KWONG EOS]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[PENG ROBINSON EOS]
    nodeF --> nodeF4[GENERALIZED COMPRESSIBILITY CHART]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[STEAM WATER CYCLE]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[REFRIGERANTS R134A R12 NH3]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[SATURATED LIQUID VAPOR MIXTURE]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2[QUALITY X FRACTION]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF5[CRITICAL PROPERTIES PCR TCR VCR]
    nodeF5 --> nodeF6[LAW OF CORRESPONDING STATES]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Flow: How a Working Substance Operates in a Heat Engine

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS1[HEAT SOURCE QIN] --> nodeS2[BOILER OR COMBUSTOR]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[WORKING SUBSTANCE AT HIGH T AND P]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[EXPANDER TURBINE OR PISTON]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[SHAFT WORK WOUT]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS6[LOW PRESSURE WORKING FLUID]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[CONDENSER OR HEAT REJECTION]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8[HEAT SINK QOUT]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS9[PUMP OR COMPRESSOR]
    nodeS9 --> nodeS1
```

## 4.3 P–V Diagram — Isotherm Comparison (Ideal vs Real Gas)

The following schematic captures the qualitative difference between an **ideal gas isotherm** and a **real gas isotherm** below the critical temperature:

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP1["P V DIAGRAM ISOTHERM COMPARISON"] --> nodeP2["IDEAL GAS ISOTHERM HYPERBOLA PV EQUALS CONSTANT"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP3["REAL GAS ISOTHERM BELOW TCR"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4["REGION 1 LOW P NEARLY IDEAL Z CLOSE TO 1"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP5["REGION 2 MODERATE P Z LESS THAN 1 ATTRACTIVE FORCES DOMINATE"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP6["REGION 3 HIGH P Z GREATER THAN 1 REPULSIVE FORCES DOMINATE"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP7["CRITICAL ISOTHERM INFLECTION POINT AT PCR TCR"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The critical isotherm is the boundary case — it has an **inflection point** where both $\partial P/\partial v$ and $\partial^2 P/\partial v^2$ vanish simultaneously. Below this isotherm, the real gas can be liquefied by pressure alone; above it, no amount of pressure can liquefy the gas (the supercritical regime).

## 4.4 Compressibility Factor Behaviour — A Schematic Trend

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeZ1["Z COMPRESSIBILITY FACTOR AXIS"] --> nodeZ2["Z EQUALS 1 IDEAL GAS BASELINE"]
    nodeZ2 --> nodeZ3["Z LESS THAN 1 ATTRACTION DOMINANT T LOW OR T MODERATE"]
    nodeZ2 --> nodeZ4["Z GREATER THAN 1 REPULSION DOMINANT P HIGH"]
    nodeZ2 --> nodeZ5["Z CROSSES UNITY AT BOYLE TEMPERATURE FOR EACH P"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a working substance. Give two examples each for an ideal and a real working fluid used in thermal systems.**

**Model Answer:**
A working substance is the thermodynamic medium that circulates through a thermal system, undergoing cyclic state changes to enable the conversion of heat into work or vice versa. It is characterized by its equation of state $f(P, v, T) = 0$.

- **Ideal working fluids:** Air, Nitrogen (N₂), Oxygen (O₂), Helium.
- **Real working fluids:** Steam (H₂O), Carbon dioxide (CO₂), Ammonia (NH₃), Refrigerant R-134a.

*(3 marks awarded for: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for ideal examples, 1 mark for real examples.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Law of Corresponding States. What is the significance of the compressibility factor $Z$?**

**Model Answer:**
**Law of Corresponding States:** All real gases at the **same reduced pressure ($P_R$) and reduced temperature ($T_R$)** have approximately the **same compressibility factor ($Z$)**, regardless of their chemical identity. This allows a single **Generalized Compressibility Chart** (Nelson-Obert) to be used for all substances.

**Significance of $Z$:**
$$Z = \frac{Pv}{RT}$$
- $Z = 1 \Rightarrow$ Ideal gas behaviour.
- $Z \neq 1 \Rightarrow$ Deviation from ideality; indicates the magnitude of intermolecular forces and molecular volume effects.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for the law, 1.5 for the significance and equation.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### QUESTION A (Module Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Starting from the Van der Waals equation, derive the expressions for the Van der Waals constants $a$ and $b$ in terms of the critical properties $P_{cr}$ and $T_{cr}$. Show that the critical compressibility factor $Z_{cr} = 3/8$ for any fluid obeying the VdW EOS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rigid vessel of volume **0.05 m³** contains **1.2 kg** of carbon dioxide ($M = 44 \; \text{kg/kmol}$, $P_{cr} = 7.38 \; \text{MPa}$, $T_{cr} = 304 \; \text{K}$). The temperature is **350 K** and the pressure is **8 MPa**. Using the compressibility chart method, determine:
(i) The actual pressure in the vessel.
(ii) The percentage deviation from the ideal gas law. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Refer to the **exhaustive derivation in Section 3.2** of these notes. The key results are:

- From the inflection point conditions $\partial P/\partial v = 0$ and $\partial^2 P/\partial v^2 = 0$ at the critical state, we obtain $v_{cr} = 3b$, $T_{cr} = 8a/(27Rb)$, and $P_{cr} = a/(27b^2)$.
- Inverting these gives $a = \dfrac{27 R^2 T_{cr}^2}{64 P_{cr}}$ and $b = \dfrac{R T_{cr}}{8 P_{cr}}$.
- Substituting into $Z_{cr} = P_{cr} v_{cr}/(R T_{cr})$:

$$Z_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr} \cdot 3b}{R T_{cr}} = \frac{3 P_{cr}}{R T_{cr}} \cdot \frac{R T_{cr}}{8 P_{cr}} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

*Mark split: 2 marks for setting up derivatives, 2 marks for solving, 2 marks for $a$ and $b$, 1 mark for $Z_{cr}$ result.*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute specific gas constant:**

$$R = \frac{R_u}{M} = \frac{8.314}{44} = 0.18895 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute reduced properties:**

$$T_R = \frac{T}{T_{cr}} = \frac{350}{304} = 1.151$$

**Step 3 — Use the generalized compressibility chart.** For $T_R = 1.15$ and $P_R$ to be determined iteratively:

$$P_R = \frac{P}{P_{cr}} = \frac{8}{7.38} = 1.084$$

From the standard Nelson-Obert compressibility chart, at $T_R = 1.15$ and $P_R = 1.08$, we read off:

$$Z \approx 0.62 \;\; \text{(typical chart value at this point)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute actual specific volume using real gas law:**

$$v = \frac{ZRT}{P} = \frac{0.62 \times 0.18895 \times 350}{8000} = 0.005125 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Verify consistency** (since vessel is rigid with V = 0.05 m³ and m = 1.2 kg):

$$v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{0.05}{1.2} = 0.04167 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Since this does not match the calculated $v$ at $P = 8 \; \text{MPa}$, the actual pressure is solved iteratively. The actual pressure satisfies:

$$P = \frac{Z R T}{v} = \frac{Z \times 0.18895 \times 350}{0.04167} = Z \times 1587 \; \text{kPa}$$

Iterating with the chart at $T_R = 1.15$, the converged value of $P$ is approximately **$P \approx 4.5 \; \text{MPa}$** (with $Z \approx 0.30$ at $P_R = 0.61$).

**Step 6 — Percentage deviation from ideal gas law:**

$$P_{ideal} = \frac{RT}{v} = \frac{0.18895 \times 350}{0.04167} = 1587 \; \text{kPa} = 1.587 \; \text{MPa}$$

$$\% \text{Deviation} = \frac{1.587 - 4.5}{4.5} \times 100 \approx -65\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B Numericals):**
> 1. **Do not forget to convert units.** Temperature must be in **Kelvin**, pressure in **kPa** (or MPa consistently), and mass in **kg**. Marks are deducted for unit mismatches.
> 2. **Don't assume the given pressure is correct.** In rigid-tank problems, the given $P$ is often a *trial* value; you must iterate with the compressibility chart.
> 3. **Always compute $T_R$ first** before reading the chart. The order of operations is: $R \to T_R \to P_R \to Z \to P_{actual}$.
> 4. **Forgetting to use $R = R_u / M$** is the most common 1-mark loss in Part (a)-type derivations.

---

### QUESTION B (Module Choice 2 — Alternative Path) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the Boyle temperature expression $T_{Boyle} = a/(Rb)$ from the Van der Waals equation. Explain its physical significance with the help of a $Z$ vs $P$ schematic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Principle of Corresponding States**, the following critical data are given for nitrogen: $T_{cr} = 126 \; \text{K}$, $P_{cr} = 3.39 \; \text{MPa}$. Calculate the Van der Waals constants $a$ and $b$ for nitrogen. If 5 kg of N₂ is stored at 200 K and 5 MPa, find: (i) the volume occupied assuming ideal gas behaviour, and (ii) the volume corrected for real-gas effects using $Z = 0.95$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Refer to the **full derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. The key results are:

$$T_{Boyle} = \frac{a}{Rb}$$

**Physical significance:**
- $T_{Boyle}$ is the temperature at which a real gas behaves like an ideal gas over an **infinitesimal pressure range** (slope of $Z$ vs $P$ is zero at $P \to 0$).
- For $T > T_{Boyle}$: $Z > 1$ (positive deviation — repulsion dominates).
- For $T < T_{Boyle}$: $Z < 1$ (negative deviation — attraction dominates) at moderate pressures.
- Above the Boyle temperature, no liquefaction is possible by isothermal compression alone.

*Mark split: 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for $T_{Boyle}$ expression, 2 marks for physical interpretation and schematic.*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute specific gas constant for N₂** ($M = 28 \; \text{kg/kmol}$):

$$R = \frac{8.314}{28} = 0.2969 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 2 — Van der Waals constants:**

$$a = \frac{27 R^2 T_{cr}^2}{64 P_{cr}} = \frac{27 \times (0.2969)^2 \times (126)^2}{64 \times 3.39 \times 10^3}$$

Computing the numerator: $27 \times 0.08815 \times 15876 = 37{,}789$ (kJ)²·K²/(kmol)².

Computing the denominator: $64 \times 3390 = 216{,}960$ kPa.

$$a = \frac{37{,}789}{216{,}960} = 0.1742 \; \text{kPa·(m³/kg)²}$$

$$b = \frac{R T_{cr}}{8 P_{cr}} = \frac{0.2969 \times 126}{8 \times 3390} = \frac{37.41}{27{,}120} = 1.379 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Ideal gas volume:**

$$V_{ideal} = \frac{mRT}{P} = \frac{5 \times 0.2969 \times 200}{5000} = \frac{296.9}{5000} = 0.05938 \; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Real gas volume with $Z = 0.95$:**

$$V_{real} = Z \times V_{ideal} = 0.95 \times 0.05938 = 0.05641 \; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Difference:**

$$\Delta V = 0.05938 - 0.05641 = 0.00297 \; \text{m}^3 \quad (5\% \text{ smaller than ideal})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B — Numerical):**
> 1. **Unit trap:** $a$ comes out in kPa·(m³/kg)², $b$ in m³/kg. Many students forget the $10^3$ factor when $P_{cr}$ is given in MPa. **Always convert MPa to kPa** before substituting.
> 2. **Chart-reading errors:** In $Z$ chart problems, students often confuse $P_R$ and $T_R$ axes. Recall that $P_R$ is the **vertical axis** and $T_R$ is the **horizontal axis** in the standard Nelson-Obert chart.
> 3. **Rounding:** Do not round intermediate values. Carry at least **4 significant digits** until the final step.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Working substance** is the medium that carries heat and work through a thermal cycle. Selection of the right fluid (water, air, R-134a, NH₃) is dictated by the desired cycle performance.
- The **ideal gas equation** $Pv = RT$ is the foundation of low-pressure, high-temperature analysis. The **specific gas constant** is $R = R_u / M$ with $R_u = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/kmol·K}$.
- The **Van der Waals equation** $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$ is the simplest EOS that captures both molecular volume ($b$) and intermolecular attraction ($a$).
- Critical-point analysis gives the **VdW constants** in terms of $P_{cr}$ and $T_{cr}$: $a = \dfrac{27 R^2 T_{cr}^2}{64 P_{cr}}$, $b = \dfrac{R T_{cr}}{8 P_{cr}}$.
- The **critical compressibility factor** for VdW fluids is universally $Z_{cr} = 3/8 = 0.375$, which is a known limitation of the VdW model.
- The **Boyle temperature** $T_{Boyle} = a/(Rb)$ marks the threshold between attractive- and repulsive-dominated behaviour.
- The **Principle of Corresponding States** unifies all real-gas behaviour through reduced coordinates $P_R$ and $T_R$, enabling the use of a single compressibility chart.
- The **Compressibility Factor** $Z = Pv/(RT)$ quantifies deviation from ideality: $Z = 1$ for ideal gases, $Z \neq 1$ for real gases.
- Always check whether the problem operates in the **single-phase region** (use $Pv = RT$ or compressibility chart) or the **two-phase region** (use quality $x$ and steam/refrigerant tables). Mixing these two approaches is the most common error.
- **Industrial applications** of real-gas analysis include LNG storage, supercritical CO₂ cycles, refrigeration compressor design, and high-pressure chemical reactors.
- **Unit conversions** are the silent killer in KTU numericals: kPa ↔ MPa, °C ↔ K, kg ↔ kmol — get these right first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation on T-S and P-H Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Representation on T-S and P-H Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In steam power plant engineering, a **Steam Power Cycle** is a thermodynamic closed loop in which working fluid (water/steam) undergoes a series of processes to convert heat energy into mechanical work (and subsequently electrical energy). The most fundamental ideal cycle is the **Carnot Cycle**, but practical steam plants operate on the **Rankine Cycle** and its modifications (Reheat and Regenerative).

Under the KTU 2024 scheme for *Thermodynamics and Thermal Systems*, representing these cycles on the **Temperature–Entropy (T-s) Diagram** and the **Pressure–Enthalpy (P-h) Diagram** is mandatory because the area under the curve on these diagrams directly yields the **heat transfer (Q)** and **work done (W)**, which are the primary outputs needed to calculate thermal efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Standardized):**
> - **Rankine Cycle:** The ideal vapor-compression cycle used for converting heat into work using a condensable working fluid (water). Components: Pump, Boiler, Turbine, Condenser.
> - **T-s Diagram:** A thermodynamic chart plotting Temperature (T) on the Y-axis against Specific Entropy (s) on the X-axis.
> - **P-h Diagram:** A thermodynamic chart plotting Pressure (P) on the Y-axis (usually logarithmic) against Specific Enthalpy (h) on the X-axis.
> - **Reheat Cycle:** A modified Rankine cycle where steam is expanded in stages, re-heated between stages to avoid excessive moisture.
> - **Regenerative Cycle:** A modified Rankine cycle where a portion of steam is bled from the turbine to preheat the feed water, improving efficiency.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a child blowing soap bubbles. The bubble expands (work output) and contracts. Now imagine a *controlled* bubble system where the child blows a bubble (steam expands in a turbine), then breathes air back in to prepare for the next blow (condenser + pump). 

A **T-s diagram** is like mapping the *temperature and disorder* (entropy) of the child's breath. When the bubble is being blown, temperature and disorder change in a specific way. The *area under the curve* on the T-s diagram represents the actual energy (heat) transferred to or from the breath.

A **P-h diagram** is like mapping the *pressure and total heat content* (enthalpy) of the breath. It is extremely useful for engineers because just like an electrician uses a circuit map, a thermal engineer uses the P-h chart to instantly read the energy content (enthalpy) at any point in the cycle without doing complex math. Water's "saturation dome" — the bell-shaped curve — is like a phase-change map: inside the dome is a wet mixture of liquid and steam, outside is pure liquid (left) or pure superheated steam (right).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why T-s and P-h Diagrams are Critical for KTU Exams:**
> 1. Area under T-s curve = Heat Transfer (Q = ∫T ds)
> 2. Vertical drop on h-axis in P-h diagram = Turbine Work / Pump Work
> 3. They visually depict the **saturation dome** (phase change boundaries).
> 4. Efficiency is easily seen as a ratio of areas.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## A. The Carnot Vapor Cycle (Theoretical Benchmark)
The Carnot cycle on a T-s diagram is a rectangle. It consists of:
1. **Isothermal Heat Addition (1-2):** Saturated liquid to saturated vapor at temperature $T_H$.
2. **Isentropic Expansion (2-3):** Expansion in turbine from $T_H$ to $T_L$.
3. **Isothermal Heat Rejection (3-4):** Wet steam condensed to saturated liquid at $T_L$.
4. **Isentropic Compression (4-1):** Compression in pump from $T_L$ to $T_H$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Carnot Cycle is NOT used practically?** Because Process 3-4 requires compressing a wet mixture (two-phase fluid), which causes mechanical damage (blade erosion) to the pump. Furthermore, Process 2-3 requires expanding steam into the wet region (high moisture content), which destroys turbine blades. Hence, the **Rankine Cycle** was adopted as the practical alternative.

## B. The Ideal Rankine Cycle (The Practical Workhorse)
The Rankine cycle modifies the Carnot cycle by replacing the wet compression with **pump work on saturated liquid** and expands steam only to the condenser pressure (not all the way to the lowest temperature $T_L$).

The four processes are:
- **Process 1-2 (Pump):** Isentropic compression of saturated liquid from condenser pressure $P_L$ to boiler pressure $P_H$.
- **Process 2-3 (Boiler):** Constant pressure heat addition, taking sub-cooled liquid to saturated liquid, then to saturated vapor, and finally to superheated steam.
- **Process 3-4 (Turbine):** Isentropic expansion of steam from $P_H$ to $P_L$.
- **Process 4-1 (Condenser):** Constant pressure heat rejection, condensing wet steam to saturated liquid.

## C. The Reheat Rankine Cycle
To improve efficiency and reduce moisture content in the low-pressure turbine, steam is expanded in stages. It is expanded to an intermediate pressure, then sent back to the boiler to be re-heated (usually to the original turbine inlet temperature) before further expansion.

- **Process 1-2:** Pump
- **Process 2-3:** Primary Boiler Heating
- **Process 3-4:** HP Turbine (Isentropic Expansion)
- **Process 4-5:** Reheater (Constant Pressure Reheating)
- **Process 5-6:** LP Turbine (Isentropic Expansion)
- **Process 6-1:** Condenser

## D. The Regenerative Rankine Cycle (Open Feedwater Heater)
In a regenerative cycle, a fraction of steam ($y$) is extracted (bled) from the turbine at an intermediate pressure. This bled steam is used to preheat the feedwater before it enters the main boiler.

- **Process 1-2:** Pump 1 (Condensate pump)
- **Process 2-5:** Open Feedwater Heater (Mixing of bled steam and condensate)
- **Process 5-6:** Pump 2 (Boiler feed pump)
- **Process 6-3:** Boiler
- **Process 3-4:** Turbine (Expansion with Bleed at State 7)
- **Process 4-7:** Bleed Extraction
- **Process 7-1:** Remaining Expansion to Condenser

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Process / Concept | Governing Equation | Units (SI) | Key Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heat Transfer (T-s area) | $Q = \int T \, ds$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ or $\text{kW}$ | Applicable for any process |
| Turbine Work (Ideal) | $W_T = h_3 - h_4$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | Isentropic: $s_3 = s_4$ |
| Pump Work (Ideal) | $W_P = h_2 - h_1$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | Isentropic: $s_1 = s_2$ |
| Boiler Heat Added | $Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | Constant Pressure process |
| Condenser Heat Rejected | $Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | Constant Pressure process |
| Rankine Efficiency | $\eta_{Rankine} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$ | Dimensionless | Ratio of areas on T-s |
| Reheat Cycle Work | $W_{net} = (h_3 - h_4) + (h_5 - h_6)$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | $s_3 = s_4$ and $s_5 = s_6$ |
| Reheat Boiler Heat | $Q_{in} = (h_3 - h_2) + (h_5 - h_4)$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | Two constant P additions |
| Regenerative Bleed Fraction | $y = \dfrac{h_5 - h_2}{h_7 - h_2}$ | Dimensionless | Energy balance at FWH |
| Specific Steam Consumption | $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}$ | $\text{kg/kWh}$ | Inverse of net work |
| Work Ratio (Back Work) | $WR = \dfrac{W_P}{W_T}$ | Dimensionless | Indicates pump penalty |
| Carnot Efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Dimensionless | Function of reservoir temps |
| Enthalpy of Vaporization | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | At saturation conditions |
| Steam Quality (Dryness) | $x = \dfrac{s - s_f}{s_{fg}}$ | Dimensionless | Determines turbine exit state |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Utility:**
> These cycle modifications are the backbone of **thermal power plants** (NTPC, Tata Power), **nuclear power plants** (where entire cycles are kept at low pressures to use water as a safer moderator), and **solar thermal plants**. The regenerative cycle in particular is universally used because it virtually eliminates the irreversible heat transfer losses associated with heating subcooled water in the boiler.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Derivation of the Regenerative Bleed Fraction ($y$)

In an **Open Feedwater Heater (OFWH)**, the bled steam (State 7) mixes directly with the condensate (State 2) to produce a mixed feedwater state (State 5). To find the mass fraction of steam that must be bled, we apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (Conservation of Energy) to the mixing chamber.

**Step 1: Define the Mass Flow Rates**
Let the total mass flow rate entering the turbine be $m_{total}$ (State 3). The fraction $y$ is bled at State 7. Therefore:
- Mass flow of bled steam = $y \cdot m_{total}$
- Mass flow remaining in turbine = $(1 - y) \cdot m_{total}$

**Step 2: Apply Conservation of Mass to the Mixing Chamber**
The total mass entering the mixing chamber must equal the total mass leaving.
- Mass entering via condensate line (State 2) = $(1 - y) \cdot m_{total}$
- Mass entering via bleed line (State 7) = $y \cdot m_{total}$
- Mass leaving (State 5) = $m_{total}$

**Step 3: Apply Conservation of Energy (First Law)**
Assuming the feedwater heater is perfectly insulated (adiabatic) and there is negligible change in kinetic and potential energy:
$$\text{Energy In} = \text{Energy Out}$$
$$(1 - y) \cdot h_2 + y \cdot h_7 = 1 \cdot h_5$$

**Step 4: Expand the Equation**
We distribute the terms across the parentheses:
$$h_2 - y \cdot h_2 + y \cdot h_7 = h_5$$

**Step 5: Group the Bleed Fraction ($y$) Terms**
We move all terms containing $y$ to one side of the equation:
$$h_2 - h_5 = y \cdot h_2 - y \cdot h_7$$
$$h_2 - h_5 = y \cdot (h_2 - h_7)$$

**Step 6: Isolate $y$**
By dividing both sides by $(h_2 - h_7)$, we obtain the standard KTU formula for the bleed fraction:
$$y = \frac{h_2 - h_5}{h_2 - h_7}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the original equation $(1 - y)h_2 + y h_7 = h_5$ is fundamentally an energy balance. The state 5 enthalpy is a *weighted average* of states 2 and 7 enthalpies, weighted by their respective mass fractions. This is a classic lever rule in thermodynamics.

## Derivation 2: Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Reheat Rankine Cycle

**Step 1: Define the Net Work Output**
The net work is the sum of the work produced by the two turbine stages minus the work consumed by the pump.
$$W_{net} = W_{T(HP)} + W_{T(LP)} - W_P$$
$$W_{net} = (h_3 - h_4) + (h_5 - h_6) - (h_2 - h_1)$$

**Step 2: Define the Total Heat Input**
Heat is added in the primary boiler (Process 2-3) and in the reheater (Process 4-5).
$$Q_{in} = Q_{boiler} + Q_{reheater} = (h_3 - h_2) + (h_5 - h_4)$$

**Step 3: Formulate the Efficiency Ratio**
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the Expressions**
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) + (h_5 - h_6) - (h_2 - h_1)}{(h_3 - h_2) + (h_5 - h_4)}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Pump Work Approximation**
In a typical Rankine cycle, the pump work $W_P = h_2 - h_1$ is very small compared to the turbine work (usually less than 1%). For KTU examinations, if the pump work is ignored:
$$h_1 \approx h_2$$
$$\eta_{th} \approx \frac{(h_3 - h_4) + (h_5 - h_6)}{(h_3 - h_2) + (h_5 - h_4)}$$

## Derivation 3: Determination of State 5 Enthalpy (Pump Exit) in Rankine Cycle

State 1 is saturated liquid at the condenser pressure $P_1$. The pump compresses it isentropically to the boiler pressure $P_2$.

**Step 1: Define Pump Work in terms of Specific Volume**
For an incompressible liquid undergoing isentropic compression, the work is:
$$W_P = \int_1^2 v \, dP \approx v_1 (P_2 - P_1)$$

**Step 2: Relate Work to Enthalpy Change**
By the steady flow energy equation (neglecting kinetic and potential energy):
$$W_P = h_2 - h_1$$

**Step 3: Equate the Two Expressions**
$$h_2 - h_1 = v_1 (P_2 - P_1)$$

**Step 4: Isolate $h_2$**
$$h_2 = h_1 + v_1 (P_2 - P_1)$$

**Step 5: Apply to Numerical Context**
If $P_1 = 10 \text{ kPa}$, then $h_1 \approx h_f \text{ at } 10 \text{ kPa} = 191.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$, and $v_1 \approx 0.00101 \text{ m}^3\text{/kg}$. If $P_2 = 10 \text{ MPa}$:
$$h_2 = 191.8 + 0.00101 \times (10000 - 10) \approx 201.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Carnot Vapor Cycle on T-s Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid

    B -->|Isothermal Heat Addition 1 to 2| C["State 3: Saturated Vapor at T_H, P_H"]
    C -->|Isentropic Expansion 2 to 3| D["State 4: Wet Steam at T_L, P_L"]
    D -->|Isothermal Heat Rejection 3 to 4| A
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style B fill:#fff3e1,stroke:#ff6600
    style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style D fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900
```

    B -->|Constant P Heat Addition 2 to 3| C["State 3: Superheated Steam at P_H, T_3"]
    C -->|Isentropic Expansion 3 to 4| D["State 4: Wet Steam at P_L, T_4"]
    D -->|Constant P Heat Rejection 4 to 1| A

    end
