# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Compare the Carnot cycle and the Rankine cycle for a steam power plant. Why is the Rankine cycle preferred in practice?**

**Model Answer:**
The Carnot cycle operates entirely within the wet steam region for both compression and expansion, with isothermal heat addition and rejection. The Rankine cycle, however, completes compression in the liquid region (saturated liquid is compressed by the pump) and completes heat addition in the superheated region (steam is superheated in the boiler before entering the turbine).

**Why Rankine is preferred:**
1. **Pump protection:** In the Carnot cycle, the pump has to compress a wet two-phase mixture, which causes severe mechanical erosion of the impeller. In the Rankine cycle, the pump handles only saturated liquid.
2. **Turbine protection:** The Rankine cycle limits expansion so that moisture content at the turbine exit is manageable (typically above 88% dryness), whereas the Carnot cycle expands deep into the wet region, causing blade erosion.
3. **Practical superheating:** The Rankine cycle allows steam to be superheated, which improves thermal efficiency and reduces the moisture content in the low-pressure stages.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is the purpose of reheating steam in a Rankine cycle? Show the reheat process on a T-s diagram.**

**Model Answer:**
The purpose of reheating is to **increase the thermal efficiency** of the Rankine cycle and **reduce the moisture content** of the steam at the exit of the low-pressure turbine. Excessive moisture (below 88% dryness fraction) causes erosion of the turbine blades.

**T-s Diagram Representation:**
On the T-s diagram, the reheat process appears as a vertical drop (isentropic expansion in HP turbine from State 3 to 4), followed by a rightward movement at constant pressure (heat addition in the reheater from State 4 to 5), and then another vertical drop (isentropic expansion in the LP turbine from State 5 to 6). This shifts the final expansion to a higher entropy region, resulting in a higher average temperature of heat addition.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**(a) [7 Marks]** In an ideal reheat Rankine cycle, steam enters the HP turbine at 15 MPa and 600°C. After expanding to 3 MPa, it is reheated to 600°C and then expanded in the LP turbine to 10 kPa. Assuming pump work is negligible, determine:
1. The enthalpy at the exit of the HP turbine (State 4)
2. The enthalpy at the exit of the LP turbine (State 6)
3. The thermal efficiency of the cycle

**(b) [7 Marks]** Sketch the T-s diagram for this reheat cycle and show all the processes. Compare the efficiency of this reheat cycle with a simple Rankine cycle operating between the same pressure limits.

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Given Data:**
- Boiler pressure $P_3 = 15 \text{ MPa}$
- Boiler exit temperature $T_3 = 600°\text{C}$
- Reheat pressure $P_4 = 3 \text{ MPa}$
- Reheat temperature $T_5 = 600°\text{C}$
- Condenser pressure $P_6 = 10 \text{ kPa}$
- Pump work is negligible

**State 1 (Condenser Exit – Saturated Liquid at 10 kPa):**
From steam tables at 10 kPa:
- $h_1 = h_f = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $s_1 = s_f = 0.6492 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

Since pump work is negligible: $h_2 \approx h_1 = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**State 3 (HP Turbine Inlet – Superheated at 15 MPa, 600°C):**
From superheated steam tables at 15 MPa and 600°C:
- $h_3 = 3583.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $s_3 = 6.6796 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**State 4 (HP Turbine Exit at 3 MPa, Isentropic):**
The process 3-4 is isentropic, so $s_4 = s_3 = 6.6796 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.
At $P_4 = 3 \text{ MPa}$, the saturation entropy values are:
- $s_f = 2.6457 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $s_g = 6.1856 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

Since $s_4 > s_g$, the steam is **superheated** at State 4.
From superheated steam tables at 3 MPa and $s = 6.6796$:
- $h_4 \approx 3045.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**State 5 (Reheater Exit at 3 MPa, 600°C):**
- $h_5 = 3682.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $s_5 = 7.5085 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**State 6 (LP Turbine Exit at 10 kPa, Isentropic):**
$s_6 = s_5 = 7.5085 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.
At 10 kPa: $s_f = 0.6492$, $s_g = 8.1488 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.
Since $s_f < s_6 < s_g$, the steam is **wet** at State 6.
The dryness fraction $x_6$ is:
$$x_6 = \frac{s_6 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{7.5085 - 0.6492}{8.1488 - 0.6492} = \frac{6.8593}{7.4996} = 0.9146$$
The enthalpy $h_6$ is:
$$h_6 = h_f + x_6 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.9146 \times (2583.9 - 191.81) = 191.81 + 0.9146 \times 2392.09 = 2379.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(a) Results:**
1. $h_4 = 3045.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (superheated)
2. $h_6 = 2379.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$ with dryness fraction $x_6 = 0.9146$ (above 88%, so safe for turbine)
3. Thermal efficiency:
   - HP turbine work: $W_{T(HP)} = h_3 - h_4 = 3583.1 - 3045.8 = 537.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$
   - LP turbine work: $W_{T(LP)} = h_5 - h_6 = 3682.3 - 2379.1 = 1303.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$
   - Net work: $W_{net} = 537.3 + 1303.2 - 0 = 1840.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$
   - Total heat input: $Q_{in} = (h_3 - h_2) + (h_5 - h_4) = (3583.1 - 191.81) + (3682.3 - 3045.8) = 3391.3 + 636.5 = 4027.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$
   - Efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{1840.5}{4027.8} = 0.457 = 45.7\%$

**(b) T-s Diagram Sketch:**
The T-s diagram for the reheat cycle shows:
- A vertical line from 1 to 2 (pump, very small)
- A curved line from 2 to 3 crossing the saturation dome into the superheated region (boiler heating)
- A vertical line from 3 to 4 dropping from 15 MPa to 3 MPa (HP turbine)
- A rightward curve at constant 3 MPa from 4 to 5 returning to 600°C (reheater)
- A vertical line from 5 to 6 dropping from 3 MPa to 10 kPa (LP turbine)
- A horizontal line from 6 to 1 at constant 10 kPa (condenser)

**Comparison with Simple Rankine Cycle:**
For a simple Rankine cycle operating between 15 MPa (at 600°C) and 10 kPa:
- $h_4'$ at 10 kPa and $s = 6.6796$ gives $x = 0.8028$, which is **below 88%** (unsafe).
- The thermal efficiency of a simple Rankine cycle at the same conditions would be approximately **40–41%**.
- The reheat cycle improves efficiency by **4–5%** and raises the turbine exit dryness above 88%, making it both more efficient and safer.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**(a) [7 Marks]** A regenerative Rankine cycle uses one open feedwater heater. Steam enters the turbine at 8 MPa and 500°C, and the condenser operates at 20 kPa. Steam is extracted at 1 MPa for the feedwater heater. Determine the bleed fraction and the thermal efficiency of the cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Sketch the T-s diagram and the layout of the cycle. Explain how the regenerative cycle improves efficiency over the simple Rankine cycle.

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Given Data:**
- Boiler pressure: $P_6 = 8 \text{ MPa}$, $T_6 = 500°\text{C}$
- Condenser pressure: $P_1 = 20 \text{ kPa}$
- Bleed pressure: $P_7 = 1 \text{ MPa}$

**State 1 (Condenser Exit):** Saturated liquid at 20 kPa:
- $h_1 = 251.42 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $v_1 = 0.001017 \text{ m}^3\text{/kg}$, $s_1 = 0.8320 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**State 2 (Condensate Pump Exit at 1 MPa):**
$$h_2 = h_1 + v_1(P_7 - P_1) = 251.42 + 0.001017 \times (1000 - 20) = 252.42 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**State 6 (Turbine Inlet):** Superheated at 8 MPa, 500°C:
- $h_6 = 3398.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $s_6 = 6.7240 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**State 7 (Bleed Point at 1 MPa, Isentropic):**
$s_7 = s_6 = 6.7240 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. At 1 MPa: $s_f = 2.1381$, $s_g = 6.5865$. Since $s_7 > s_g$, steam is superheated.
- $h_7 \approx 3076.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**State 5 (Feedwater Heater Exit – Saturated Liquid at 1 MPa):**
- $h_5 = h_f = 762.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**State 4 (Boiler Feed Pump Exit at 8 MPa):**
$h_4 = h_5 + v_5(P_6 - P_7) = 762.81 + 0.001127 \times (8000 - 1000) = 762.81 + 7.89 = 770.70 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**State 3 (Turbine Exit to Condenser at 20 kPa, Isentropic):**
$s_3 = s_6 = 6.7240 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. At 20 kPa: $s_f = 0.8320$, $s_g = 7.9073$.
- $x_3 = (6.7240 - 0.8320) / (7.9073 - 0.8320) = 0.8316$
- $h_3 = 251.42 + 0.8316 \times (2606.3 - 251.42) = 2205.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**(a) Results:**
1. **Bleed fraction:** $y = \dfrac{h_2 - h_5}{h_2 - h_7} = \dfrac{252.42 - 762.81}{252.42 - 3076.2} = \dfrac{-510.39}{-2823.78} = 0.1807$
2. **Thermal efficiency:**
   - Turbine work: $W_T = (h_6 - h_7) + (1 - y)(h_7 - h_3) = (3398.3 - 3076.2) + (1 - 0.1807)(3076.2 - 2205.4) = 322.1 + 0.8193 \times 870.8 = 322.1 + 713.4 = 1035.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$
   - Pump work: $W_P = (1 - y)(h_2 - h_1) + (h_4 - h_5) = 0.8193 \times 1.0 + 7.89 = 8.71 \text{ kJ/kg}$
   - Net work: $W_{net} = 1035.5 - 8.71 = 1026.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$
   - Heat input: $Q_{in} = h_6 - h_4 = 3398.3 - 770.70 = 2627.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$
   - Efficiency: $\eta = 1026.8 / 2627.6 = 0.391 = 39.1\%$

**(b) Efficiency Improvement:**
A simple Rankine cycle operating between 8 MPa (500°C) and 20 kPa would have an efficiency of approximately **33–34%**. The regenerative cycle improves efficiency by **5–6%** because:
- The bleed steam preheats the feedwater to the saturation temperature of the bleed pressure (1 MPa).
- This **eliminates the irreversible heat transfer** that would otherwise occur in the boiler when heating subcooled water from ~45°C (condenser exit) to ~295°C (boiler exit saturation).
- The mean temperature of heat addition in the boiler is significantly increased.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Pump work is NOT zero:** Students frequently write $h_2 = h_1$ without using the $v_1 \Delta P$ formula. This loses **1–2 marks** because the pump work, though small, is explicitly required in the energy balance.
> 2. **State 4 vs State 5 confusion:** Many students confuse the boiler feed pump exit (State 4) with the feedwater heater exit (State 5). State 5 is saturated liquid at the bleed pressure; State 4 is compressed liquid at the boiler pressure.
> 3. **Skipping dryness fraction calculation:** In reheat and regenerative problems, students must always check whether the turbine exit is wet or superheated. If wet, calculate $x$ and then $h$. Failure to do so loses **2 marks** and gives physically unrealistic answers.
> 4. **Bleed fraction sign error:** The numerator $h_2 - h_5$ and denominator $h_2 - h_7$ are both negative, but the ratio is positive. Students sometimes drop the negative signs and get a negative bleed fraction, which is physically meaningless.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Carnot Cycle** is the theoretical maximum, but **Rankine Cycle** is the practical standard because it handles phase changes safely.
- **T-s Diagram:** Area under the curve = Heat ($Q = \int T \, ds$). Vertical lines represent isentropic processes (pump, turbine).
- **P-h Diagram:** Vertical distance = Work. Highly convenient for reading enthalpies without calculation.
- **Saturation Dome:** Inside = wet steam (quality $0 < x < 1$); Left = subcooled/saturated liquid; Right = superheated steam.
- **Reheat Cycle** uses two turbines (HP and LP) with a reheater in between. Goal: Increase efficiency AND keep exit dryness above 88%.
- **Regenerative Cycle** uses bled steam to preheat feedwater. Goal: Increase mean temperature of heat addition in the boiler.
- **Bleed fraction** $y$ is found from energy balance at the feedwater heater: $(1 - y)h_2 + yh_7 = h_5$.
- **Pump work** $W_P = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ is small but non-zero; ignoring it loses marks.
- **Dryness fraction** must be calculated for every turbine exit state to confirm it is in the wet region.
- **Rankine Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$.
- **Reheat Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) + (h_5 - h_6) - (h_2 - h_1)}{(h_3 - h_2) + (h_5 - h_4)}$.
- **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ (upper bound; no steam cycle achieves this).
- **Back Work Ratio:** $BWR = W_P / W_T$ (typically 0.01–0.04 for Rankine cycles).
- **Specific Steam Consumption:** $SSC = 3600 / W_{net}$ (in $\text{kg/kWh}$).
- **Typical Exam Trick:** KTU often provides a reheat cycle problem with a regeneration twist. Practice both modifications.
- **Always draw the cycle** on the T-s diagram with **labeled states and saturation dome** to secure full marks in the 7-mark drawing questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Problems. Compressors - Reciprocating Compressors - Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Simple Problems. Compressors - Reciprocating Compressors - Principle of Operation # Module 3 — Reciprocating Compressors: Principle of Operation & Simple Problems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) A **Compressor** is a positive-displacement or rotodynamic machine that converts mechanical work (shaft work) supplied by a prime-mover into fluid (gas/vapour) potential energy in the form of **elevated static pressure**. The working fluid emerges at a discharge pressure **substantially higher** than the suction pressure. A **Reciprocating Compressor** is a *positive-displacement* compressor in which the compression of the working fluid is achieved by the linear back-and-forth motion of a piston inside a closed cylinder, driven through a connecting rod and crankshaft mechanism. Compression occurs in **discrete, cyclic batches** (one batch per stroke). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Reciprocating compressors fall under the "positive displacement" class — every revolution (or stroke) delivers a *fixed geometric volume* of fluid; pressure rise is a *consequence* of this delivery against a downstream resistance, not the *cause* of the intake. This is the fundamental distinction from rotodynamic machines like centrifugal compressors. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Pump" Intuition Imagine pushing down the handle of a **bicycle pump** with your thumb firmly sealing the outlet: 1. Pull handle **up** → fresh air rushes in (suction stroke). 2. Push handle **down** → air gets squeezed into a smaller space → its pressure rises sharply (compression stroke). 3. Once pressure exceeds the resistance (atmospheric + tire valve spring), air bursts through the valve into the tire (discharge). A reciprocating compressor is essentially a **large, precisely-engineered, multi-cylinder bicycle pump** that does this hundreds of times per minute, with automatic spring-loaded suction and delivery (reed or poppet) valves. The piston *forces* the gas into a higher pressure state by physically *reducing its volume* — this is **why compressors are work-consuming devices** (unlike turbines, which produce work). ### 1.3 Classification Snapshot | Family | Type | Example | |---|---|---| | **Positive Displacement** | **Reciprocating** (this topic) | Single-acting, Double-acting | | | Rotary | Screw, Vane, Lobe | | **Rotodynamic** | Centrifugal / Axial | Turbo-compressors | ### 1.4 Standard Operating Metrics (used throughout KTU problems) - **Free Air Delivery (FAD)** = volume of air, measured at *inlet* (atmospheric) conditions, actually compressed per unit time. Units: **m³/min** or **m³/s**. - **Swept (Stroke) Volume, Vs = (π/4)·D²·L** — geometric volume displaced by piston. - **Compression Ratio, rp = P2 / P1** (dimensionless). - **Indicated Power (IP)** — power developed inside the cylinder from gas-side p–V work. - **Shaft Power / Brake Power (BP)** — power actually drawn at the crankshaft (IP + mechanical losses). - **Mechanical Efficiency, ηm = IP / BP**. - **Isothermal Efficiency, ηiso = W_ideal_isothermal / W_actual**. - **Adiabatic (Isentropic) Efficiency, ηad = W_isentropic / W_actual**. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** In KTU problems, the term *air* typically means an ideal gas with **R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K** and **γ (Cp/Cv) = 1.4** unless stated otherwise. Polytropic index **n** is given (commonly n = 1.25 to 1.35 for air compressors with cooling). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Principle of Operation — Stroke-by-Stroke Logic A single-acting reciprocating compressor completes **one compression cycle per crankshaft revolution**. The cycle has four phases captured perfectly in the **Pressure–Volume (p–V) diagram**: | Process | Piston Motion | Valve Status | What Happens | |---|---|---|---| | **1 → 2** Suction | Moves from TDC to BDC (downward) | Suction valve OPEN, Delivery valve CLOSED | Gas at P1 drawn in | | **2 → 3** Compression | Moves from BDC to TDC (upward) | Both valves CLOSED | Gas compressed from P1 to P2 | | **3 → 4** Delivery | Near TDC | Suction valve CLOSED, Delivery valve OPEN | Gas expelled at P2 | | **4 → 1** Re-expansion (clearance) | Piston moves away from TDC | Both CLOSED initially, then suction opens | Trapped clearance gas expands | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Clearance Volume (Vc)** is the small volume that remains *between the piston face at TDC and the cylinder head*. It is *unavoidable* for mechanical clearance, lubrication, and valve clearance. Its presence is the single biggest reason reciprocating compressors **lose volumetric efficiency** at high pressure ratios. ### 2.2 Indicator (p–V) Diagram — The Heart of the Topic For an **ideal compressor with zero clearance**, the p–V diagram is a simple closed loop: - **Left side** (suction line 1→2): horizontal line at P1 - **Top** (compression 2→3): follows the chosen polytropic/adiabatic/isothermal path - **Right side** (delivery 3→4): horizontal line at P2 - **Bottom** (expansion 4→1): same path as compression, but reversed The **enclosed area of the loop = Net Work Done per cycle (Wc)**. This is the *thermodynamic* work — same value whether computed via $\int V\,dP$ or $\int P\,dV$ over a closed cycle (Green's theorem for closed paths). For **real compressors with clearance**, the loop becomes a **figure-eight-like shape**: the suction line no longer starts at BDC volume (Vs + Vc) but at a smaller re-expanded volume because clearance gas must re-expand down to P1 first. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **textbook-grade, board-verifiable, and KTU-approved**. Units are **SI** unless stated. Use $\vert$ in lieu of `|` to avoid markdown breakage. | # | Quantity | Formula | Assumptions / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Swept volume per cylinder | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | D = bore, L = stroke | | 2 | Clearance ratio | $C = \dfrac{V_c}{V_s}$ | Typical: **2 % to 6 %** | | 3 | Volumetric efficiency (with clearance) | $\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$ | Drops as P2/P1 rises | | 4 | Volumetric efficiency (no clearance) | $\eta_v = 1$ | Ideal case | | 5 | Isothermal work per cycle | $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Ideal gas, T = constant | | 6 | Adiabatic work per cycle | $W_{ad} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ | With n = γ = 1.4 for air | | 7 | Polytropic work per cycle | $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ | 1 < n < γ for cooled compression | | 8 | Indicated Power (per cylinder) | $IP = \dfrac{W_c \cdot N \cdot k}{60}$ | N = rpm, k = number of cylinders | | 9 | Indicated Power (from FAD) | $IP_{iso} = P_1 \cdot \dot{V}_a \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | $\dot{V}_a$ = free air delivery m³/s | | 10 | Mean Effective Pressure | $MEP = \dfrac{W_c}{V_s}$ | Pa or N/m² | | 11 | Isothermal Efficiency | $\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{W_{iso,actual\,input}}{W_{iso,ideal}}$ | < 1 for adiabatic/polytropic | | 12 | Adiabatic / Isentropic Efficiency | $\eta_{ad} = \dfrac{W_{ad,ideal}}{W_{ad,actual}}$ | Used for after-cooled / uncooled | | 13 | Minimum work (any process) | $W_{iso} < W_{poly} < W_{ad}$ | Cooling always reduces work | | 14 | FAD relation | $\dot{V}_a = \eta_v \cdot V_s \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot k}{60}$ | m³/s at inlet conditions | | 15 | Pressure ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | Dimensionless | ### 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice - **Air-conditioning & refrigeration**: Reciprocating compressors (hermetic, semi-hermetic) are the workhorse of small-to-medium cooling systems (≤ 200 kW). - **Gas pipelines (CNG, NG transmission)**: Multi-stage reciprocating compressors pressurize natural gas for long-distance transport. - **Pneumatic tools, braking systems, paint spraying**: All depend on compressed air from reciprocating units. - **Petrochemical & process industries**: Specialty gas (H₂, N₂, O₂) compression. The **key engineering trade-off** is *compression ratio per stage*: going beyond **rp ≈ 6** per stage causes excessive discharge temperatures (T₂ can exceed 200 °C → lubricant failure, safety risk). Hence multi-staging with **inter-cooling** is used industrially. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation A — Work of Isothermal Compression (Per Cycle) **Starting Premise:** A perfect gas compressed **isothermally** from state 1 (P1, V1) to state 2 (P2, V2). The gas obeys **Boyle's Law**: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2 = \text{const}$. **Step 1** — Write the elementary work done *on* the gas during an infinitesimal compression: $$ dW = P \, dV $$ **Step 2** — Substitute the ideal gas isothermal relation $P = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{V}$: $$ dW = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{V} \, dV $$ **Step 3** — Integrate between the **suction volume V1** and the **delivery volume V2**: $$ W_{iso} = \int_{V_2}^{V_1} \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{V} \, dV = P_1 V_1 \int_{V_2}^{V_1} \dfrac{dV}{V} $$ **Step 4** — Evaluate the logarithmic integral: $$ W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \left[ \ln V \,\Big|_{V_2}^{V_1} \right] = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right) $$ **Step 5** — Using Boyle's Law to convert V-ratio into P-ratio: $$ \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)} $$ This is the **lower bound** on compressor work — physically the *most efficient* possible compression. ### 3.2 Derivation B — Work of Polytropic (or Adiabatic) Compression **Starting Premise:** Process follows **PVⁿ = constant**, where **n = γ** gives adiabatic, **n = 1** gives isothermal, **1 < n < γ** gives polytropic with cooling. **Step 1** — Elementary work: $$ dW = P \, dV $$ **Step 2** — Polytropic relation: $P V^n = P_1 V_1^n \Rightarrow P = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n}$ $$ dW = P_1 V_1^n \, V^{-n} \, dV $$ **Step 3** — Integrate: $$ W_{poly} = P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_2}^{V_1} V^{-n} \, dV = P_1 V_1^n \left[ \dfrac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_2}^{V_1} $$ **Step 4** — Expand the limits: $$ W_{poly} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{1-n} \left[ V_1^{1-n} - V_2^{1-n} \right] $$ **Step 5** — Pull out common factor $V_1^{1-n}$ and use $V_1/V_2 = (P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$: $$ W_{poly} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n V_1^{1-n}}{1-n} \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1-n} \right] $$ $$ W_{poly} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{1-n} \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} \right] $$ **Step 6** — Multiply numerator and denominator by $-1$ to flip the inequality and clean the form: $$ \boxed{W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \, P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]} $$ When **n = γ**, this is the adiabatic (isentropic) work; when **n = 1**, the limit $W_{iso}$ is recovered via L'Hôpital's rule. ### 3.3 Derivation C — Volumetric Efficiency with Clearance **Why it matters:** Without clearance, every swept volume Vs is filled at suction. *With* clearance, a portion of the stroke is "wasted" re-expanding the trapped clearance gas from P2 down to P1 before new gas can enter. **Step 1** — Volume at point 3 (start of delivery) = Vc + Vs (geometric). At point 3: pressure = P2, volume = Vc. After adiabatic re-expansion to P1 (point 4): volume becomes **V4**. $$ P_2 V_c^{n} = P_1 V_4^{n} \Rightarrow V_4 = V_c \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} $$ **Step 2** — Volume of *fresh* gas drawn in per cycle (point 1 to point 2): $$ V_a = V_s + V_c - V_4 = V_s + V_c - V_c \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} $$ **Step 3** — Divide by swept volume Vs and use C = Vc/Vs: $$ \eta_v = \dfrac{V_a}{V_s} = 1 + C - C\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_v = 1 + C - C\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}} $$ **Insight:** As $P_2 \to P_1$, $\eta_v \to 1$ (no work done, full filling). As $P_2/P_1$ rises, $\eta_v$ drops sharply — this is the **physical reason** multi-staging with inter-cooling is used industrially: splitting a high ratio into 2 or 3 stages keeps each stage's $\eta_v$ acceptably high. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Solved Illustrative Problem (Worked-Through Model) > [!NOTE] > This is a **fully solved KTU-pattern numerical**. Every line of reasoning is shown so you can replicate it in the exam hall. **Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):** A single-stage, single-acting reciprocating compressor runs at **300 rpm** and has cylinder dimensions **bore = 200 mm**, **stroke = 300 mm**. It compresses air from **1.05 bar, 27 °C** to **7 bar**. The compression process is **polytropic with n = 1.30**. Calculate: 1. Swept volume per cycle. 2. Free air delivery (FAD) per minute (assume clearance is negligible). 3. Indicated power developed. **Given Data** $$ D = 0.200\;\text{m},\quad L = 0.300\;\text{m},\quad N = 300\;\text{rpm},\quad k = 1 $$ $$ P_1 = 1.05 \times 10^5\;\text{Pa},\quad P_2 = 7 \times 10^5\;\text{Pa},\quad n = 1.30 $$ **Part (a) — Swept Volume** $$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.200)^2 (0.300) $$ $$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 0.04 \times 0.300 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 0.012 = 9.4248 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3 $$ $$ \boxed{V_s = 9.425 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3 \approx 9.425\;\text{litres/cycle}} $$ **Part (b) — Free Air Delivery** For negligible clearance, $\eta_v = 1$, so every swept volume of air at inlet conditions is compressed. $$ \dot{V}_a = V_s \times \dfrac{N \times k}{60} = 9.425 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{300 \times 1}{60} $$ $$ \dot{V}_a = 9.425 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 = 47.12 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s} $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{V}_a = 0.04712\;\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 2.827\;\text{m}^3/\text{min}} $$ **Part (c) — Indicated Power** Step 1 — Compute work per cycle using the polytropic formula: $$ W_c = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \, P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(n-1)/n} - 1\right] $$ Step 2 — Compute pressure ratio: $$ \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{7}{1.05} = 6.6667 $$ Step 3 — Compute exponent: $$ \dfrac{n-1}{n} = \dfrac{0.30}{1.30} = 0.2308 $$ Step 4 — Raise ratio to exponent: $$ (6.6667)^{0.2308} $$ We compute via logarithms: $\ln(6.6667) = 1.8971$, then $0.2308 \times 1.8971 = 0.4378$, then $e^{0.4378} = 1.5494$. Step 5 — Compute the bracketed term: $$ 1.5494 - 1 = 0.5494 $$ Step 6 — Compute the multiplier: $$ \dfrac{n}{n-1} = \dfrac{1.30}{0.30} = 4.3333 $$ Step 7 — Substitute. With $V_1 = V_s$ per cycle (no clearance): $$ W_c = 4.3333 \times (1.05 \times 10^5) \times (9.425 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.5494 $$ $$ W_c = 4.3333 \times 1.05 \times 10^5 \times 9.425 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5494 $$ $$ W_c = 4.3333 \times 989.625 \times 0.5494 = 4.3333 \times 543.69 $$ $$ W_c \approx 2356.0\;\text{J/cycle} $$ Step 8 — Convert to indicated power: $$ IP = \dfrac{W_c \times N \times k}{60} = \dfrac{2356.0 \times 300 \times 1}{60} $$ $$ IP = 11\,780\;\text{W} \approx 11.78\;\text{kW} $$ $$ \boxed{IP \approx 11.78\;\text{kW}} $$ **Cross-Verification via FAD method:** Using $IP_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \, P_1 \dot{V}_a \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$: $$ IP = 4.3333 \times (1.05 \times 10^5) \times 0.04712 \times 0.5494 $$ $$ IP = 4.3333 \times 4944.6 \times 0.5494 = 4.3333 \times 2717.0 \approx 11\,774\;\text{W} $$ (Minor rounding difference of < 0.1 %, **self-checks pass**.) ✓ --- ## 4. P2 V4"] V4 --- V1 V2 -.->|Polytropic path 2 to 3| V3 V4 -.->|Expansion path 4 to 1 if clearance exists| V1 style V1 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#000,color:#000 style V2 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#000,color:#000 style V3 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#000,color:#000 style V4 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation of the Loop:** > - The **left edge** (vertical segment V1 → V2) is the suction stroke — gas at constant low pressure P1 is drawn in. > - The **top curve** (V2 → V3) is the actual compression — its curvature is dictated by the process (isothermal, polytropic, adiabatic). > - The **right edge** (V3 → V4) is the delivery stroke at constant high pressure P2. > - The **bottom curve** (V4 → V1) is the re-expansion of clearance gas (degenerates to a single point if clearance = 0). > - **Enclosed area = Work done per cycle.** ### 4.3 Single-Acting vs. Double-Acting — Schematic Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SA["SINGLE-ACTING COMPRESSOR"] SA_CYL["CYLINDER

KTU problems often specify "*single-acting*" or "*double-acting*" via the factor **k** = 1 or k = 2 respectively. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Pattern, Dec 2024 Scheme]** **Q.A1.** *Define a reciprocating compressor. With the help of a labelled sketch, explain its principle of operation.* **Model Answer:** A reciprocating compressor is a positive-displacement machine that raises the pressure of a gas by physically reducing its volume using a piston reciprocating inside a closed cylinder. The principle of operation involves four processes per cycle: 1. **Suction stroke** — piston moves from TDC to BDC, drawing in gas at low pressure P1 through an open suction valve. 2. **Compression stroke** — piston moves from BDC to TDC; both valves remain closed; gas pressure rises from P1 to P2 following the chosen compression process (isothermal/polytropic/adiabatic). 3. **Delivery stroke** — once cylinder pressure exceeds P2, the delivery valve opens and compressed gas is expelled at constant pressure P2. 4. **Re-expansion of clearance** — the small clearance volume Vc retains some gas which expands back to P1 before fresh intake resumes. The net work per cycle equals the area enclosed by the indicator p–V diagram. > **[Marks Distribution Hint]:** Definition (1) + labelled diagram (1) + cycle explanation (1) = 3 marks. --- **Q.A2.** *What is meant by volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor? State the expression for it including clearance.* **Model Answer:** **Volumetric efficiency (ηv)** is defined as the ratio of the *actual volume of gas drawn in at suction conditions* to the *swept (displacement) volume of the piston*, both expressed per cycle. It quantifies how effectively the geometric capacity of the cylinder is utilized for fresh gas intake. For a compressor with clearance ratio C = Vc/Vs and polytropic index n: $$ \eta_v = 1 + C - C\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!1/n} $$ For an *ideal clearance-free* compressor, ηv = 1. > **[Marks Distribution Hint]:** Definition (1.5) + formula with symbols explained (1.5) = 3 marks. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) #### **QUESTION A — (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style, Module 3]* **Q.A(a)** A single-stage, single-acting reciprocating compressor has a bore of **150 mm** and a stroke of **200 mm**. It runs at **360 rpm** and compresses air from **1 bar to 6 bar** following a **polytropic process with n = 1.25**. **[7 Marks]** Calculate: 1. The swept volume of the cylinder. 2. The free air delivery per minute (assume clearance is negligible). 3. The indicated power required to drive the compressor. **Q.A(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the effect of **clearance volume** on the volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor. **[7 Marks]** --- ##### **Solution to Q.A(a):** **Given:** $$ D = 0.150\;\text{m},\quad L = 0.200\;\text{m},\quad N = 360\;\text{rpm},\quad k = 1 $$ $$ P_1 = 1 \times 10^5\;\text{Pa},\quad P_2 = 6 \times 10^5\;\text{Pa},\quad n = 1.25 $$ **(i) Swept Volume:** $$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.150)^2 (0.200) $$ $$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0225 \times 0.200 = 0.7854 \times 0.0045 $$ $$ \boxed{V_s = 3.534 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3 = 3.534\;\text{litres}} $$ **(ii) Free Air Delivery:** $$ \dot{V}_a = V_s \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot k}{60} = 3.534 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{360}{60} = 3.534 \times 10^{-3} \times 6 $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{V}_a = 21.21 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 1.272\;\text{m}^3/\text{min}} $$ **(iii) Indicated Power:** Compute pressure ratio: $$ r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{6}{1} = 6 $$ Compute exponent: $$ \dfrac{n-1}{n} = \dfrac{0.25}{1.25} = 0.20 $$ Compute the bracketed term: $$ (6)^{0.20} = e^{0.20 \times \ln 6} = e^{0.20 \times 1.7918} = e^{0.3584} = 1.4310 $$ So bracket $= 1.4310 - 1 = 0.4310$. Compute multiplier: $$ \dfrac{n}{n-1} = \dfrac{1.25}{0.25} = 5.0 $$ Work per cycle: $$ W_c = 5.0 \times (1 \times 10^5) \times (3.534 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.4310 $$ $$ W_c = 5.0 \times 353.4 \times 0.4310 = 5.0 \times 152.31 = 761.55\;\text{J/cycle} $$ Indicated Power: $$ IP = \dfrac{W_c \times N \times k}{60} = \dfrac{761.55 \times 360}{60} = 761.55 \times 6 $$ $$ \boxed{IP = 4569.3\;\text{W} \approx 4.57\;\text{kW}} $$ --- ##### **Solution to Q.A(b) — Effect of Clearance on Volumetric Efficiency:** **Definition:** Clearance volume (Vc) is the small unavoidable volume remaining between the piston face at TDC and the cylinder head (including valve pockets). **Effect on Cycle (p–V Diagram):** 1. At TDC, the cylinder still contains gas at pressure P2 occupying volume Vc. 2. On the return (suction) stroke, this gas **must re-expand** down to pressure P1 before the suction valve can open. 3. Only the volume **Vs + Vc − V4** is filled with fresh gas per cycle, where: $$ V_4 = V_c \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} $$ 4. Hence the **effective intake volume Va is less than Vs**, and: $$ \eta_v = \dfrac{V_a}{V_s} = 1 + C - C\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} < 1 $$ **Key Observations:** - $\eta_v$ **decreases** as compression ratio $P_2/P_1$ increases. - $\eta_v$ **decreases** as clearance ratio $C$ increases. - $\eta_v \to 1$ when $P_2 \to P_1$ (no compression needed) or $C \to 0$ (no clearance). **Practical Consequences:** - Higher pressure ratios require **multi-staging with inter-cooling** to keep each stage's ηv acceptable. - Designers minimise mechanical clearance but cannot eliminate it (for thermal expansion, valve clearance, lubrication). - For **very high ratios (rp > 8)**, single-stage reciprocating compressors become impractical — centrifugal or multi-stage reciprocating units are preferred. > **[Marks Distribution Hint]:** Diagram (2) + definition of clearance (1) + derivation of ηv formula (2) + observations and consequences (2) = 7 marks. --- #### **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice) — (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam — Model Pattern, Module 3]* **Q.B(a)** A two-cylinder, single-acting reciprocating compressor compresses air from **1.013 bar to 8 bar**. The compressor runs at **240 rpm**. Each cylinder has bore **120 mm** and stroke **150 mm**. The compression follows **PV^1.3 = constant**. **[7 Marks]** Calculate: 1. The swept volume per cylinder. 2. The indicated power required. **Q.B(b)** Compare isothermal, polytropic, and adiabatic compression processes in a reciprocating compressor with respect to: (i) work input, (ii) heat transfer, (iii) discharge temperature, (iv) practical feasibility. **[7 Marks]** --- ##### **Solution to Q.B(a):** **Given:** $$ k = 2,\quad N = 240\;\text{rpm},\quad P_1 = 1.013 \times 10^5\;\text{Pa},\quad P_2 = 8 \times 10^5\;\text{Pa} $$ $$ D = 0.120\;\text{m},\quad L = 0.150\;\text{m},\quad n = 1.3 $$ **(i) Swept Volume per Cylinder:** $$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.120)^2 (0.150) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0144 \times 0.150 $$ $$ V_s = 0.7854 \times 0.00216 = 1.6965 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3 $$ $$ \boxed{V_s = 1.696 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3 \approx 1.696\;\text{litres/cycle/cylinder}} $$ **(ii) Indicated Power:** Compute pressure ratio: $$ r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{8}{1.013} = 7.898 $$ Compute exponent: $$ \dfrac{n-1}{n} = \dfrac{0.3}{1.3} = 0.2308 $$ Compute bracket: $$ (7.898)^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times \ln 7.898} = e^{0.2308 \times 2.0666} = e^{0.4770} = 1.6113 $$ Bracket $= 1.6113 - 1 = 0.6113$. Multiplier: $$ \dfrac{n}{n-1} = \dfrac{1.3}{0.3} = 4.3333 $$ Work per cycle **per cylinder**: $$ W_c = 4.3333 \times (1.013 \times 10^5) \times (1.6965 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.6113 $$ $$ W_c = 4.3333 \times 101300 \times 1.6965 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6113 $$ $$ W_c = 4.3333 \times 171.85 \times 0.6113 = 4.3333 \times 105.06 $$ $$ W_c \approx 455.3\;\text{J/cycle/cylinder} $$ Indicated Power (both cylinders): $$ IP = \dfrac{W_c \times N \times k}{60} = \dfrac{455.3 \times 240 \times 2}{60} = \dfrac{455.3 \times 480}{60} $$ $$ IP = 455.3 \times 8 = 3642.4\;\text{W} $$ $$ \boxed{IP \approx 3.64\;\text{kW}} $$ --- ##### **Solution to Q.B(b) — Comparative Analysis:** | Criterion | Isothermal (n=1) | Polytropic (1 **[Marks Distribution Hint]:** Tabular comparison (3) + ordering of work (1) + discharge temperature derivations (2) + practical insight (1) = 7 marks. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks Every Semester:** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor `k`** (number of cylinders × single/double-acting = 1 or 2) in the IP formula. Always state `k` explicitly in your *Given Data*. > > 2. **Mixing up $n$ and $\gamma$.** For air, $\gamma = 1.4$ is *adiabatic*. Polytropic index **n** is *given* in the problem (typically 1.25–1.35 with cooling). Don't substitute $\gamma$ when **n** is provided — even though the *form* of the polytropic and adiabatic work formulas are identical, the *numerical answer changes*. > > 3. **Unit mismatch in $P_1 V_1$.** If $P_1$ is in **bar**, convert to **Pa** ($1\;\text{bar} = 10^5\;\text{Pa}$) *before* multiplying by $V_1$ in m³. Many students write $P_1 = 1$, forget the conversion, and end up with IP 100 000× too small. > > 4. **Volume per cycle vs. per minute.** When the problem says "compressor delivers X m³/min," that's the FAD $\dot{V}_a$ at *inlet* conditions. Work per cycle uses $V_1 = V_s$ (or = $\eta_v V_s$ with clearance), not $\dot{V}_a$. > > 5. **Neglecting the volumetric efficiency check.** If the problem gives clearance and rp, you *must* compute $\eta_v$ first, otherwise $V_s$ will be wrong by 30–50 % at high ratios. > > 6. **Not stating the assumption of ideal gas.** KTU examiners want you to write: *"Assuming air behaves as an ideal gas, with $R = 0.287\;\text{kJ/kg·K}$ and $\gamma = 1.4$..."* in the first step. Omitting it costs at least half a mark. > > 7. **Sign convention for work.** The work of compression is *positive* (energy input to gas). Don't write negative values. > > 8. **Drawing the p–V diagram incorrectly.** For a compressor, the cycle is traversed **clockwise** (not anticlockwise as in a heat engine). The enclosed area is *work input*, not work output. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — keep this in your notebook:** - ✅ A **reciprocating compressor** is a *positive-displacement*, *work-consuming* device that raises gas pressure by physically reducing gas volume in a cylinder via a reciprocating piston. - ✅ One cycle = **4 processes**: suction (P = P1), compression (P1 → P2), delivery (P = P2), and clearance re-expansion (P2 → P1). - ✅ The **p–V indicator diagram** is a clockwise loop whose enclosed area = work input per cycle. - ✅ **Clearance volume (Vc)** is unavoidable — it reduces volumetric efficiency via $\eta_v = 1 + C - C \cdot r_p^{1/n}$. - ✅ **Isothermal work** $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln r_p$ — minimum possible work (theoretical ideal). - ✅ **Polytropic / Adiabatic work** $W = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 [r_p^{(n-1)/n} - 1]$ — use given **n**. - ✅ **Ordering**: $W_{iso} < W_{poly} < W_{ad}$ for the same compression ratio. - ✅ **Indicated Power** $IP = \dfrac{W_c \cdot N \cdot k}{60}$ with N in rpm and k = number of effective working faces. - ✅ **Swept volume** $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$. - ✅ **FAD** $\dot{V}_a = \eta_v \cdot V_s \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot k}{60}$ — at *inlet* conditions. - ✅ **Single-acting** = 1 compression per revolution per cylinder; **double-acting** = 2 compressions per revolution per cylinder. - ✅ **Air properties** to memorise: $R = 0.287\;\text{kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005\;\text{kJ/kg·K}$, $C_v = 0.718\;\text{kJ/kg·K}$. - ✅ **High compression ratio** (> 6 per stage) → unmanageable discharge temperatures → **use multi-staging with inter-cooling**. - ✅ For *every* numerical problem: state given data, state assumptions, convert units (Pa from bar!), show every algebraic step, box the final answer, and mention the validity of the result. - ✅ Always draw a **labelled p–V diagram** for compressor questions — it carries 1–2 marks even if numerical part is incomplete. - ✅ **Polytropic curve** in p–V lies **between** isothermal (flatter) and adiabatic (steeper) curves.

topics.title: Work Required
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Work Required

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Required (in Compressible Flow / Compression Processes)** is defined as the *minimum useful mechanical energy that must be supplied by an external agent (shaft work) to a working fluid in order to achieve a desired change of state* — typically raising its pressure from an initial value $P_1$ to a higher final value $P_2$ — while simultaneously satisfying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** and the relevant process equation (polytropic, isothermal, isentropic, or isochoric).

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, for a **closed system (non-flow)**, the work required is obtained from:

$$W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

For an **open system (steady-flow / flow work)** such as a reciprocating compressor, the work required per unit mass is given by the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** with $\Delta KE = \Delta PE = 0$:

$$w_{comp} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} v \, dP = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{(for adiabatic, no kinetic energy change)}$$

The **negative sign** arises because work is done *on* the system (work input), making it positive on the input side.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bicycle Pump Story"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine pumping air into a bicycle tyre.**
> 
> - You **push the handle inward** — this is the *work required* you supply from your muscles.
> - The air inside **resists** — this resistance grows stronger as the pressure inside the tyre rises.
> - If you push *very slowly* (isothermal compression), the heat generated has time to escape through the cylinder walls → **less work needed**.
> - If you push *very fast* (adiabatic compression), the air heats up → **more work needed**.
> 
> This is exactly why a two-stage pump with an intercooler (used in diesel engines, gas turbines, and air compressors) requires **less total work** than a single-stage pump for the same final pressure.

The same principle governs every compressor, from the small inflator at a petrol bunk to the massive axial compressors used in jet engines.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants (used throughout this module):**
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314 \text{ kJ/kmol·K}$
> - **Specific Gas Constant for Air:** $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
> - **Ratio of Specific Heats for Air:** $\gamma = 1.4$
> - **Mechanical Equivalent of Heat:** $1 \text{ kW} = 1 \text{ kJ/s}$
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCMUT403 — Module 3):**
> Students are expected to derive the expression for work required in:
> 1. **Polytropic compression** $PV^n = C$
> 2. **Isothermal compression** $PV = C$
> 3. **Adiabatic (Isentropic) compression** $PV^\gamma = C$
> 4. **Multistage compression with intercooling** and the concept of **optimum intermediate pressure for minimum work**.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Compression Processes

| Process | Governing Equation | Heat Transfer | Work Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Isothermal** | $PV = C$ | Heat rejected (maximum) | **Minimum** |
| **Polytropic** | $PV^n = C$, $1 < n < \gamma$ | Partial heat rejection | Intermediate |
| **Adiabatic / Isentropic** | $PV^\gamma = C$ | No heat transfer (reversible) | **Maximum** |
| **Multistage + Intercooled** | Combined | Heat rejected between stages | **Greatly reduced** |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Memory Trick:** Remember the ordering of work for the same pressure ratio:
> $$W_{iso} < W_{poly} < W_{adiab} \quad \text{(for same } P_1 \rightarrow P_2\text{)}$$
> This single inequality is a high-yield point in KTU 2024 ESE papers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why Work Is Required in Compression

Compression of a gas requires work because the gas resists volume reduction. The fundamental law governing this is:

$$P \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$$

As the volume is reduced by a small amount $dV$ at pressure $P$, the infinitesimal boundary work done **on** the gas is:

$$dW = -P \, dV$$

> The **negative sign** is crucial: when $dV < 0$ (compression), work done on the system is **positive** (energy input).

### Logical Steps for Solving Work-Required Problems:

1. **Identify the process** (isothermal, polytropic, adiabatic) from the given data.
2. **Establish the relationship** between $P$ and $V$ using the process equation.
3. **Eliminate one variable** (either $P$ or $V$) from the integral.
4. **Evaluate the integral** $\int P \, dV$ between state 1 and state 2.
5. **Apply the gas equation** $PV = mRT$ to express the result in convenient form (using $P_1, V_1$ or in terms of temperature).
6. **For flow work**, additionally use the SFEE: $w = -\int v \, dP = h_2 - h_1$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for solving all work-required problems in PCMUT403 Module 3.**

| # | Process | Governing Law | Work Required Formula (Closed System) | Work Required per kg (Flow) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Isothermal** | $PV = C$ | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right) = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | $w = R T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ |
| 2 | **Polytropic** ($PV^n = C$) | $n \neq 1$ | $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n-1}\!\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(n-1)/n}\right]$ | $w = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\, R T_1 \!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ |
| 3 | **Adiabatic / Isentropic** | $PV^\gamma = C$ | $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$ | $w = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\, R T_1 \!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ |
| 4 | **Multistage with Intercooling** (Optimum) | $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ | $W_{multi,\,opt} = \dfrac{2n}{n-1}\, P_1 V_1 \!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(n-1)/(2n)} - 1\right]$ | $w_{multi} = \dfrac{2n}{n-1}\, R T_1 \!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!(n-1)/(2n)} - 1\right]$ |

**Key auxiliary formulas used with the above:**

$$P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$$

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} \quad \text{(Polytropic)}$$

$$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} \quad \text{(Polytropic)}$$

**Work Saved in Multistage Compression:**

$$W_{saved} = W_{single} - W_{multi}$$

$$\%\, \text{Saving} = \frac{W_{single} - W_{multi}}{W_{single}} \times 100$$

**Optimum Intermediate Pressure (for minimum work in two-stage compression):**

$$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}$$

**Optimum Pressure Ratio per stage (for $N$ stages):**

$$r_{opt} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/N}$$

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is "Work Required" actually applied in industry?**
> 
> 1. **Reciprocating Compressors** — chemical plants, CNG stations, refrigeration systems.
> 2. **Gas Turbine Compressors** — aviation (turbofan engines), power generation.
> 3. **Hydraulic & Pneumatic Systems** — automated manufacturing, brakes (EBD systems in cars).
> 4. **Supercritical CO₂ Systems** — modern refrigeration and power cycles (emerging in 2024+).
> 5. **Diesel Engine Turbochargers** — multistage compression with intercooling to achieve high pressure ratios (up to 5:1).
> 6. **Scuba Diving Air Tanks** — multistage compression to safely fill high-pressure cylinders (200–300 bar).

The **minimum work principle** (isothermal compression) is the *theoretical lower bound* — no real machine can ever reach it, but it is the benchmark against which all real machines are measured. This is why every compressor manufacturer publishes a *polytropic or isentropic efficiency* — a metric that compares actual work required to this ideal.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 1: Work Required for Polytropic Compression ($PV^n = C$, $n \neq 1$)**

### Starting Point

For a closed system undergoing quasi-static compression from state 1 to state 2, the boundary work done **on** the gas is:

$$W_{1-2} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

### Step 1 — Express Pressure in terms of Volume

From the polytropic relation $PV^n = C = P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$:

$$P = \frac{C}{V^n} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into the Integral

$$W_{1-2} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n} \, dV = -P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral

$$\int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV = \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}}{1-n}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back

$$W_{1-2} = -P_1 V_1^n \cdot \frac{V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}}{1-n} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{n-1} \left( V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right)$$

### Step 5 — Factor Out $V_1^{1-n}$ to Use Initial State

$$W_{1-2} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n \, V_1^{1-n}}{n-1} \left[ \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1-n} - 1 \right] = \frac{P_1 V_1}{n-1} \left[ \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n-1} - 1 \right]$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Pressure Ratio Using Polytropic Relation

From $PV^n = C$: $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\,W_{1-2} = \frac{P_1 V_1}{n-1} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right] \,}$$

### Step 7 — Express per unit mass (work per kg)

Divide by mass $m$ and use $P_1 V_1 / m = R T_1$:

$$\boxed{\,w_{comp} = \frac{n}{n-1} \, R \, T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right] \,}$$

> This is the **master equation** for all compression work calculations in KTU Module 3.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 2: Special Case — Isothermal Compression ($n = 1$)**

### Step 1 — Start with Polytropic Formula and Take the Limit

$$\lim_{n \to 1} \frac{1}{n-1} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Standard Limit $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{a^x - 1}{x} = \ln a$

Let $x = (n-1)/n$. As $n \to 1$, $x \to 0$. Then:

$$\lim_{n \to 1} \frac{(P_2/P_1)^x - 1}{(n-1)/n} = \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Hence the Isothermal Work Formula

$$\boxed{\,W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = m R T_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = m R T \ln\!\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) \,}$$

> Note: For isothermal compression, $T_1 = T_2 = T$ (temperature constant).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 3: Special Case — Adiabatic (Isentropic) Compression ($n = \gamma$)**

By direct substitution of $n = \gamma$ in the polytropic formula:

$$\boxed{\,W_{adi} = \frac{P_1 V_1}{\gamma - 1} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right] = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1} \,}$$

Or per unit mass:

$$w_{adi} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right] = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 4: Work Required for Two-Stage Compression with Intercooling (Optimum Case)**

### Setup

A two-stage compressor compresses gas from $P_1$ to $P_2$ via an intermediate pressure $P_x$. Between the two stages, the gas is cooled back to its initial temperature $T_1$ (perfect intercooling).

### Step 1 — Work Done in Stage 1 ($P_1 \to P_x$)

$$W_1 = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

### Step 2 — Work Done in Stage 2 ($P_x \to P_2$)

After intercooling, the gas returns to $T_1$ and the new volume is $V_x = V_1$ (since $P_x V_x = mRT_1 = P_1 V_1$):

$$W_2 = \frac{n}{n-1} P_x V_x \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right] = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

### Step 3 — Total Work

$$W_{total} = W_1 + W_2 = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} + \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 2 \right]$$

### Step 4 — Minimize Work w.r.t. $P_x$

For minimum work, $\dfrac{dW_{total}}{dP_x} = 0$:

$$\frac{dW_{total}}{dP_x} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \frac{n-1}{n} \left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{-1/n} \cdot \frac{1}{P_1} + \frac{n-1}{n} \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{-1/n} \cdot \left(-\frac{P_2}{P_x^2}\right) \right] = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$\left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{-1/n} \cdot \frac{1}{P_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{-1/n} \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_x^2}$$

$$\frac{P_x^{-1/n}}{P_1^{(n-1)/n}} = \frac{P_2 \cdot P_x^{1/n}}{P_x^2 \cdot P_2^{1/n}} = \frac{P_2^{(n-1)/n}}{P_x^{(2n-1)/n}}$$

### Step 5 — Solve the Resulting Equation

After cross-multiplying and simplifying, we arrive at:

$$\boxed{\,P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}\,} \quad \text{(Optimum Intermediate Pressure)}$$

> This is a beautiful result: the geometric mean of the suction and delivery pressures gives the minimum compression work.

### Step 6 — Minimum Total Work in Two-Stage Compression

Substituting $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ into the total work expression:

$$\boxed{\,W_{multi,\,min} = \frac{2n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/(2n)} - 1 \right] \,}$$

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 5: Generalization to N-Stage Compression**

By identical reasoning (using AM-GM inequality), for $N$ stages of compression with perfect intercooling, the work required is minimum when **all stage pressure ratios are equal**:

$$\boxed{\,r_{opt} = \frac{P_2}{P_1} \text{ raised to } \frac{1}{N}\,}$$

And the total work becomes:

$$W_{N,\,min} = \frac{N \cdot n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/(N \cdot n)} - 1 \right]$$

---

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Work-Required Comparison** (for $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $P_2 = 900$ kPa, $V_1 = 0.5$ m³, $m = 1$ kg of air, $T_1 = 300$ K):

| Process | $n$ | Work (kJ) | $T_2$ (K) | Heat Rejected (kJ) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Isothermal | 1 | 98.96 | 300.0 | 98.96 |
| Polytropic | 1.25 | 121.4 | 387.3 | 41.0 |
| Polytropic | 1.35 | 130.7 | 421.5 | 18.7 |
| Adiabatic | 1.4 | 136.3 | 444.8 | 0 |
| **Two-Stage (Optimum, $n=1.25$)** | 1.25 | **108.1** | 300 (then up) | 61.7 |

> The **two-stage with intercooling** dramatically reduces work compared to single-stage adiabatic compression, while also keeping the **final temperature low** — a critical factor for mechanical safety and lubrication.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PV Diagram — Comparison of Compression Processes

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders a **functional architecture flow** mapping the relationship between pressure, volume, and work in different compression paths. This serves as a high-fidelity substitute for a hand-drawn PV diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Initial State P1 V1]:::startNode
    B[Final State P2 V2]:::endNode
    C[Isothermal Path PV equals C]:::isoNode
    D[Polytropic Path PV to power n]:::polyNode
    E[Adiabatic Path PV to power gamma]:::adiNode
    F[Area under curve equals Work Done]:::areaNode
    G[Smaller area: Iso - 98.96 kJ]:::resultNode
    H[Medium area: Poly - 121.4 kJ]:::resultNode
    I[Largest area: Adi - 136.3 kJ]:::resultNode

    A --> C --> B
    A --> D --> B
    A --> E --> B
    C -.-> F
    D -.-> F
    E -.-> F
    F --> G
    F --> H
    F --> I

    classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef isoNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef polyNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef adiNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef areaNode fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resultNode fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Interpretation:** The area enclosed under each curve in the PV diagram represents the magnitude of work done during compression. The isothermal path is the **flattest** (lowest work), and the adiabatic path is the **steepest** (highest work).

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Two-Stage Compression with Intercooling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Atmospheric Air Suction P1 T1]:::inputNode
    CYL1[Stage 1 Cylinder P1 to Px]:::cylNode
    IC[Intercooler Px and T1]:::coolNode
    CYL2[Stage 2 Cylinder Px to P2]:::cylNode
    OUT[Delivered Air P2 T2]:::outputNode
    PX[Optimum Pressure Px equals sqrt P1 times P2]:::optNode
    WS[Work Saved versus single stage]:::saveNode

    IN --> CYL1 --> IC --> CYL2 --> OUT
    PX -.->|governs| CYL1
    PX -.->|governs| CYL2
    CYL1 -.-> WS
    CYL2 -.-> WS

    classDef inputNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cylNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef coolNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef optNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef saveNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Selecting the Correct Compression Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{What process is given?}:::decisionNode
    Q2{Is it a flow or non flow problem?}:::decisionNode
    R1[Use Polytropic Formula with n]:::resultNode
    R2[Use Isothermal Formula with ln]:::resultNode
    R3[Use Adiabatic Formula with gamma]:::resultNode
    R4[For multistage use Optimum Px]:::resultNode
    R5[Apply SFEE for work per kg]:::resultNode

    Q1 -->|PV to power n equals C| Q2
    Q1 -->|PV equals C| R2
    Q1 -->|PV to power gamma equals C| R3
    Q1 -->|Two or more stages| R4
    Q2 -->|Closed system| R1
    Q2 -->|Open system steady flow| R5

    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resultNode fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Work Done as a Function of Intermediate Pressure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AXIS[Horizontal axis: Px varies from P1 to P2]:::axisNode
    CURVE[Bell shaped curve: Total work Wt]:::curveNode
    MIN[Minimum point at Px equals sqrt P1 times P2]:::minNode
    LEFT[Left side: Stage 1 work dominates]:::sideNode
    RIGHT[Right side: Stage 2 work dominates]:::sideNode
    NOTE[Total work is sum of W1 and W2: minimum at geometric mean]:::noteNode

    AXIS --> CURVE
    CURVE --> MIN
    LEFT -.->|under intercooling| CURVE
    RIGHT -.->|over intercooling| CURVE
    MIN --> NOTE

    classDef axisNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef curveNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef minNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef sideNode fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef noteNode fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**"Define the term 'work required' in the context of compression of a gas. State the expression for work required in a polytropic process."** (3 Marks)

> **Model Answer:**
> 
> **Definition (2 Marks):** The *work required* in compression is the minimum useful mechanical energy that must be supplied externally (via shaft work) to a gas in order to raise its pressure from a lower value $P_1$ to a higher value $P_2$. It is calculated as the area under the process curve on a PV diagram and is given by the integral $W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$ (closed system) or $w = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} v \, dP$ (open system).
> 
> **Expression (1 Mark):** For a polytropic process $PV^n = C$:
> 
> $$W_{1-2} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

---

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**"Why is isothermal compression considered to require the minimum work? What is the role of intercooling in multistage compression?"** (3 Marks)

> **Model Answer:**
> 
> **Isothermal Minimum Work (2 Marks):** In isothermal compression, the temperature is maintained constant by continuously rejecting heat to the surroundings. Since gas pressure at any given volume is lower when temperature is lower (from $PV = mRT$), the area under the isothermal curve on a PV diagram is the smallest among all reversible processes connecting the same two pressure limits. Mathematically, $W_{iso} = mRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$, and the function $\ln(x) < (x^k - 1)/k$ for $k > 0$ and $x > 1$, ensuring $W_{iso}$ is the theoretical lower bound.
> 
> **Role of Intercooling (1 Mark):** Intercooling between compression stages reduces the temperature of the gas back to (or near) the suction temperature, thereby lowering the specific volume entering the next stage. This **reduces the work required** in subsequent stages and also keeps delivery temperatures safe for valves, lubricants, and cylinder materials.

---

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, OR a single integrated problem worth 14 marks. The student is given an **internal choice** between two questions (A or B). Model solutions are provided below for both choices.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
### **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Set A]**

**A reciprocating compressor compresses $0.5 \text{ kg}$ of air per stroke from $1 \text{ bar}$, $300 \text{ K}$ to $9 \text{ bar}$ following a polytropic process with index $n = 1.25$.**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the work required in a polytropic compression process. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Calculate the work required per cycle, the temperature at the end of compression, and the heat rejected during the process. Take $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ and $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Solution:**

> **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**
> 
> Refer to **Derivation 1 in SECTION 3** above. The full derivation is:
> 
> Step 1: Work done on the gas: $W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$
> 
> Step 2: From $PV^n = C$: $P = C/V^n = P_1 V_1^n / V^n$
> 
> Step 3: Substituting and integrating:
> 
> $$W = -P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV = \frac{P_1 V_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$
> 
> **(Full step-by-step expansion as shown in Section 3, Derivation 1, must be written in the answer book for full marks.)**

---

> **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**
> 
> **Given:** $m = 0.5$ kg, $P_1 = 1$ bar $= 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_2 = 9$ bar $= 900$ kPa, $n = 1.25$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K
> 
> **Step 1: Find $T_2$ (Temperature after compression)**
> 
> For a polytropic process:
> 
> $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n}$$
> 
> $$\frac{T_2}{300} = (9)^{0.25/1.25} = (9)^{0.2}$$
> 
> $$9^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \ln 9} = e^{0.2 \times 2.1972} = e^{0.4394} = 1.552$$
> 
> $$T_2 = 300 \times 1.552 = 465.6 \text{ K}$$
> 
> **Step 2: Calculate Work Required**
> 
> $$W = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot m \cdot R \cdot T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$
> 
> $$W = \frac{1.25}{0.25} \times 0.5 \times 0.287 \times 300 \times [1.552 - 1]$$
> 
> $$W = 5 \times 0.5 \times 0.287 \times 300 \times 0.552$$
> 
> $$W = 5 \times 43.05 \times 0.552 = 118.8 \text{ kJ}$$
> 
> **Step 3: Calculate Heat Rejected**
> 
> From the First Law for a closed system: $Q = \Delta U + W$
> 
> $$\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1) = 0.5 \times 0.718 \times (465.6 - 300) = 0.5 \times 0.718 \times 165.6 = 59.45 \text{ kJ}$$
> 
> $$Q = 59.45 + 118.8 = 178.25 \text{ kJ (heat supplied)}$$
> 
> **Wait — sign check:** The convention here is work done ON the system. Re-arranging:
> 
> $$Q = \Delta U - W_{by\,gas} = \Delta U - (-W) = \Delta U + W$$
> 
> Since $T_2 > T_1$, heat has been **rejected** during compression (the gas must lose heat to remain on a polytropic with $n < \gamma$):
> 
> $$\boxed{W_{required} = 118.8 \text{ kJ}, \quad T_2 = 465.6 \text{ K}, \quad Q_{rejected} = -59.4 \text{ kJ}}$$
> 
> (Negative sign indicates heat loss to surroundings.)

---

### **PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**
### **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Set B]**

**A two-stage reciprocating air compressor with perfect intercooling compresses air from $1 \text{ bar}$, $300 \text{ K}$ to $81 \text{ bar}$. The compression in each stage follows a polytropic law with index $n = 1.3$.**

**(a)** Show that the optimum intermediate pressure for minimum compression work is the geometric mean of the suction and delivery pressures. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Calculate the optimum intermediate pressure, the work required per kg of air, the work saved compared to a single-stage compressor, and the percentage saving. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Solution:**

> **Part (a) — Proof of Optimum Pressure (7 Marks):**
> 
> Refer to **Derivation 4 in SECTION 3** above. The key steps are:
> 
> 1. Total work in two-stage compression with intercooling:
> 
> $$W_{total} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} + \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 2 \right]$$
> 
> 2. Differentiate w.r.t. $P_x$ and set to zero:
> 
> $$\frac{dW_{total}}{dP_x} = 0$$
> 
> 3. After simplification, this yields:
> 
> $$\boxed{P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}}$$
> 
> (Full derivation as in Section 3 must be reproduced in the answer script.)

---

> **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**
> 
> **Given:** $P_1 = 1$ bar, $P_2 = 81$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K, $n = 1.3$, $m = 1$ kg (per kg basis)
> 
> **Step 1: Optimum Intermediate Pressure**
> 
> $$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2} = \sqrt{1 \times 81} = 9 \text{ bar}$$
> 
> **Step 2: Single-Stage Work**
> 
> Pressure ratio $r = P_2/P_1 = 81$
> 
> $$w_{single} = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ r^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right] = \frac{1.3}{0.3} \times 0.287 \times 300 \times \left[ 81^{0.3/1.3} - 1 \right]$$
> 
> Calculate exponent: $0.3/1.3 = 0.2308$
> 
> $81^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times \ln 81} = e^{0.2308 \times 4.3944} = e^{1.0143} = 2.758$
> 
> $$w_{single} = 4.333 \times 86.1 \times (2.758 - 1) = 4.333 \times 86.1 \times 1.758 = 655.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
> 
> **Step 3: Two-Stage Work (Optimum)**
> 
> Each stage has pressure ratio $r_s = \sqrt{81} = 9$
> 
> $$w_{two-stage} = \frac{2n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ r^{(n-1)/(2n)} - 1 \right]$$
> 
> Exponent: $(n-1)/(2n) = 0.3/2.6 = 0.1154$
> 
> $81^{0.1154} = e^{0.1154 \times 4.3944} = e^{0.5071} = 1.660$
> 
> $$w_{two-stage} = 2 \times 4.333 \times 86.1 \times (1.660 - 1) = 8.667 \times 86.1 \times 0.660 = 492.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
> 
> **Step 4: Work Saved**
> 
> $$W_{saved} = 655.9 - 492.6 = 163.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
> 
> **Step 5: Percentage Saving**
> 
> $$\%\, \text{Saving} = \frac{163.3}{655.9} \times 100 = 24.9\%$$
> 
> $$\boxed{P_x = 9 \text{ bar}, \quad w_{two-stage} = 492.6 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad W_{saved} = 163.3 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad \%\, \text{Saving} = 24.9\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (lose 2–3 marks each):**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Confusion:** Many students write $W = +\int P\, dV$ for compression, getting a *negative* answer. The convention in KTU 2024 is **work done ON the system is positive** for compression. Use $W = -\int P\, dV$.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Negative Sign in $dW = -P\, dV$:** The minus sign is a part of the formula, not a "tweak" you can drop.
> 
> 3. **Exponent Mistake in Polytropic Formula:** The exponent is $(n-1)/n$ — not $1/n$, not $(n-1)$, not $1/(n-1)$. Memorize the pattern.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ applies only at the OPTIMUM:** If the problem states a specific $P_x$ (e.g., $P_x = 5$ bar), do **not** use the geometric mean — use the given value directly.
> 
> 5. **Heat Rejected Sign Error:** In polytropic compression with $1 < n < \gamma$, heat is **rejected** (negative Q). Students often report it as positive, losing 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Unit Inconsistency:** Always convert bar to kPa (multiply by 100) and °C to K (add 273.15). $R = 0.287$ requires $P$ in kPa, $V$ in m³, $T$ in K.
> 
> 7. **Misapplying SFEE vs Closed-System Integral:** Reciprocating compressor problems during the **compression stroke** are closed-system; only the *steady-flow* $w = -\int v\, dP$ form is needed if stated as a flow process. Read the wording carefully.
> 
> 8. **Single-Stage vs Multistage Comparison:** When asked "work saved compared to single stage," compute BOTH $w_{single}$ and $w_{multi}$ from the polytropic formula — do not assume the multistage formula alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — "Work Required" (Module 3)**

### **Core Formulas (Memorize These!)**

- **Polytropic work (closed system):** $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n-1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$
- **Polytropic work per kg:** $w = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$
- **Isothermal work per kg:** $w = R T \ln(P_2/P_1) = R T \ln(V_1/V_2)$
- **Adiabatic work per kg:** $w = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = \dfrac{\gamma R T_1}{\gamma-1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$
- **Optimum intermediate pressure:** $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}$
- **Optimum stage pressure ratio (N stages):** $r_{opt} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$
- **Two-stage minimum work:** $W_{2,min} = \dfrac{2n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/(2n)} - 1\right]$

### **Key Ordering (High-Yield for Theory)**

$$W_{isothermal} < W_{polytropic} < W_{adiabatic} \quad \text{(same } P_1, P_2, V_1\text{)}$$

### **Key Constants for Air**

- $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $\gamma = 1.4$
- $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

### **Critical Distinctions**

- **Closed system** (boundary work): $W = -\int P\, dV$
- **Open system** (steady flow): $w = -\int v\, dP = h_2 - h_1$ (adiabatic)
- **Polytropic index $n$:** $n = 1$ → isothermal; $n = \gamma$ → adiabatic; $1 < n < \gamma$ → real compression

### **Intercooling Benefits (Recall All 4)**

1. Reduces work of compression.
2. Reduces delivery temperature (safer for valves & lubrication).
3. Improves volumetric efficiency.
4. Brings specific volume back to original → smaller cylinder sizes in subsequent stages.

### **Units to Watch**

- $P$ in **kPa** (not bar) when $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K
- $T$ in **Kelvin** (K = °C + 273)
- $W$ in **kJ**; $w$ (per kg) in **kJ/kg**

### **One-Line Final Truth**

> *"For the same pressure rise, the closer your compression is to isothermal, the less work you need — and multistage compression with perfect intercooling is the engineering way to approach this theoretical minimum."*

---
