topics.title: Isothermal and Volumetric Efficiencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Isothermal and Volumetric Efficiencies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Isothermal Efficiency (η_isothermal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Isothermal Efficiency** of a compressor is defined as the ratio of the **isothermal work input** (the minimum possible work required for compression) to the **actual work input** supplied to the compressor for the same mass of gas and same pressure ratio.

$$\eta_{isothermal} = \frac{W_{isothermal}}{W_{actual}} = \frac{\text{Minimum (ideal) work input}}{\text{Actual work input}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used:**
> - For air as the working fluid: **R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K**
> - Specific heat ratio for air: **γ = 1.4**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **P_atm = 1.01325 bar ≈ 1 bar = 100 kPa**

**Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Pump:**
Imagine pumping air into a bicycle tire. If you press the handle **very slowly**, the air inside the cylinder has time to exchange heat with the surroundings and remains nearly at ambient temperature — this is **isothermal compression**, which requires the **least work**. But if you pump **rapidly**, the air cannot lose heat fast enough, its temperature rises sharply, and you must expend **more work** to achieve the same tire pressure. The isothermal efficiency essentially asks: *"How close did your actual pumping process come to the ideal slow (isothermal) process?"* A value close to 100% means your compression is nearly ideal.

---

## 1.2 Volumetric Efficiency (η_volumetric)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Volumetric Efficiency** of a reciprocating compressor is defined as the ratio of the **actual volume of gas drawn in (at inlet conditions)** to the **swept (displacement) volume of the piston** per cycle.

$$\eta_{volumetric} = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}} = \frac{\text{Actual intake volume per cycle}}{\text{Swept volume of the piston}}$$

**Conceptual Analogy — The Syringe with Dead Space:**
Consider a medical syringe where the needle hub contains a small trapped volume of air (the "clearance volume"). When you pull the plunger back fully, the syringe does not draw in a full plunger-volume of fresh fluid — some of that motion is wasted in re-expanding the trapped air inside the hub. The **clearance volume** in a reciprocating compressor plays exactly the same role: the residual compressed gas in the cylinder re-expands during the intake stroke, occupying space that fresh gas would otherwise fill. Volumetric efficiency quantifies this "wasted" swept volume.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Higher volumetric efficiency ⇒ higher mass flow rate for a given physical compressor size ⇒ smaller, cheaper compressor for the same duty. This is why multi-stage compressors with intercooling (which lower the effective pressure ratio per stage) are preferred for high-pressure applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why These Efficiencies Exist

The ideal **isothermal** process delivers the **theoretical minimum work** for a given compression duty (since work done = ∫V dP at constant T is the smallest possible area under the P–V curve). Any real compression — adiabatic, polytropic, or with friction — requires **more work**. The isothermal efficiency captures this **thermodynamic irreversibility**.

The **volumetric efficiency** captures a different, **geometric** limitation: no reciprocating compressor can have zero clearance volume, and the re-expansion of clearance gas from the high discharge pressure back to the intake pressure **robs** part of the swept volume from useful intake.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

**A. Isothermal Efficiency Derivation Logic:**
1. Start with the ideal isothermal work: $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(r)$
2. Express actual polytropic work: $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$
3. Form the ratio and simplify.
4. The compression index $n$ is the key variable: $n \to 1$ ⇒ polytropic → isothermal ⇒ efficiency → 100%.

**B. Volumetric Efficiency Derivation Logic:**
1. Define clearance ratio: $C = V_c / V_s$
2. Apply polytropic expansion law on trapped gas: $P_2 V_4^n = P_1 V_3^n$
3. Identify $V_3 = V_c \cdot r^{1/n}$ (expanded clearance volume at intake pressure)
4. The actual intake volume = $V_s - (V_3 - V_c)$ (swept volume minus the re-expansion loss)
5. Form the ratio and simplify.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used below:**
> $P_1$ = inlet pressure, $P_2$ = discharge pressure, $r = P_2/P_1$ (pressure ratio),
> $V_1$ = volume at start of compression, $V_s$ = swept volume, $V_c$ = clearance volume,
> $C = V_c/V_s$ = clearance ratio, $n$ = polytropic index, $\eta$ = efficiency.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Validity / Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Isothermal work input | $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(r)$ | J (per cycle) | Valid for ideal gas, isothermal reversible compression |
| 2 | Polytropic work input | $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\, P_1 V_1 \left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ | J (per cycle) | Valid for $n \neq 1$; same $n$ for compression and expansion |
| 3 | **Isothermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{(n-1)/n \cdot \ln(r)}{r^{(n-1)/n} - 1}$ | dimensionless (×100 = %) | $0 < \eta_{iso} \leq 1$; approaches 1 as $n \to 1$ |
| 4 | Clearance Ratio | $C = V_c / V_s$ | dimensionless | Typical range: 0.02 – 0.10 for industrial compressors |
| 5 | Expanded clearance volume (at $P_1$) | $V_3 = V_c \cdot r^{1/n}$ | m³ | From $P_2 V_c^n = P_1 V_3^n$ |
| 6 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \cdot r^{1/n}$ | dimensionless (×100 = %) | Also written as $\eta_{vol} = 1 - C \left(r^{1/n} - 1\right)$ |
| 7 | Actual intake volume | $V_{actual} = \eta_{vol} \cdot V_s$ | m³ | Used in mass flow rate $\dot{m} = P_1 \dot{V}_{actual}/(RT_1)$ |
| 8 | Net work per cycle (with clearance) | $W_{net} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\, P_1 (V_1 - V_3) \left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ | J | $V_1 - V_3$ is the actual fresh-gas volume per cycle |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Isothermal efficiency** is used while **designing and rating** compressors (e.g., specifying motor power).
> - **Volumetric efficiency** is used for **sizing** the compressor displacement to deliver a required free air delivery (FAD).
> - Both efficiencies are critical inputs to the **performance curves** published by compressor manufacturers (e.g., Atlas Copco, Ingersoll-Rand).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Efficiency

**Given:** Single-stage, single-acting reciprocating compressor with clearance. Compression and expansion both follow the polytropic law $PV^n = C_1$ (same index $n$).

**Notation of States (refer to P–V diagram):**
- **State 1:** Start of compression stroke; pressure = $P_1$, volume = $V_1$
- **State 2:** End of compression (start of discharge); pressure = $P_2$, volume = $V_2 = V_c$
- **State 3:** End of expansion (start of fresh intake); pressure = $P_1$, volume = $V_3$
- **State 4:** End of intake (start of compression in next cycle); pressure = $P_1$, volume = $V_1$

**Geometry:**
$$V_s = V_1 - V_4 = V_1 - V_c \quad ; \quad C = \frac{V_c}{V_s}$$

**Step 1 — Expansion of Clearance Gas (State 4 → State 3):**
Apply polytropic relation between states 4 and 3 (expansion from $P_2$ to $P_1$):
$$P_2 \, V_4^n = P_1 \, V_3^n$$

Substitute $V_4 = V_c$ and rearrange:
$$V_3^n = \frac{P_2}{P_1} V_c^n = r \cdot V_c^n$$

Taking the n-th root:
$$V_3 = V_c \cdot r^{1/n} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 — Fresh Gas Drawn In:**
The piston sweeps volume $V_s$ from state 4 to state 1. However, the first portion of this sweep — from $V_4$ to $V_3$ — is occupied by the re-expanding clearance gas, **not** by fresh intake. Therefore:
$$V_{actual} = V_1 - V_3 = (V_4 + V_s) - V_3 = V_c + V_s - V_c \cdot r^{1/n}$$

**Step 3 — Form the Ratio:**
$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_s} = \frac{V_c + V_s - V_c \cdot r^{1/n}}{V_s}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $V_s$:
$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{V_c}{V_s} + 1 - \frac{V_c}{V_s} \cdot r^{1/n} = C + 1 - C \cdot r^{1/n}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}}$$

**Step 4 — Limit Checks:**
- If $C = 0$ (no clearance): $\eta_{vol} = 1$ (100% — ideal case).
- If $r = 1$ (no compression): $\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C = 1$ (100% — trivially true).
- $\eta_{vol}$ **decreases** as $r$ or $C$ increases.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Isothermal Efficiency

**Step 1 — Isothermal Work (the ideal reference):**
For a reversible isothermal compression of an ideal gas:
$$W_{iso} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{P_1 V_1}{V} \, dV = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

$$W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(r) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2 — Actual Polytropic Work Input:**
For a polytropic process $PV^n = C_2$ with $n \neq 1$:
$$W_{poly} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} C_2 V^{-n} dV = \frac{C_2}{1-n} \left[V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}\right]$$

Using $C_2 = P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$ and $V_2 = V_1 \cdot r^{-1/n}$:
$$W_{poly} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{1-n} \left[V_1^{1-n} r^{-(1-n)/n} - V_1^{1-n}\right] = \frac{P_1 V_1}{1-n} \left[r^{-(1-n)/n} - 1\right]$$

Simplifying (and noting $1-n = -(n-1)$):
$$W_{poly} = \frac{P_1 V_1}{n-1} \left[1 - r^{-(n-1)/n}\right] = \frac{P_1 V_1}{n-1} \left[\frac{r^{(n-1)/n} - 1}{r^{(n-1)/n}}\right]$$

$$W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1} \, P_1 V_1 \left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \cdot r^{-(n-1)/n}$$

But the more direct and universally used form (rearranging via $P_1V_1 = P_2V_2 \cdot r^{(n-1)/n}$ or by direct integration in P–V form) is:
$$W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1} \, P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

*(Note: This is the standard textbook form and is the form KTU expects; it can be re-derived by integrating in pressure form $W = \int P \, dV$ or via the alternate identity $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1}(P_2V_2 - P_1V_1)$ after substituting $P_2V_2 = P_1V_1 r^{(n-1)/n}$.)*

**Step 3 — Form the Ratio:**
$$\eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{iso}}{W_{poly}} = \frac{P_1 V_1 \ln(r)}{\dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]}$$

Cancel $P_1 V_1$:
$$\eta_{iso} = \frac{(n-1) \ln(r)}{n \left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{iso} = \frac{\dfrac{n-1}{n} \cdot \ln(r)}{r^{(n-1)/n} - 1}}$$

**Step 4 — Limit Checks:**
- If $n = 1$ (isothermal limit): The denominator $\to \ln(r)$ (by L'Hôpital), and the ratio $\to 1$. So $\eta_{iso} \to 100\%$. ✓
- If $n = \gamma = 1.4$ (adiabatic): $\eta_{iso} < 100\%$, showing irreversibility loss.
- $\eta_{iso}$ **decreases** as $r$ increases or as $n$ moves away from 1.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Design Parameters

| Parameter | Effect on $\eta_{vol}$ | Effect on $\eta_{iso}$ | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| Increase pressure ratio $r$ | **Decreases** (loss grows as $r^{1/n}$) | **Decreases** | Favours multi-stage compression with intercooling |
| Increase clearance $C$ | **Decreases** linearly with $C$ | No direct effect (only via $n$) | Minimise mechanical clearance; use poppet valves |
| Increase polytropic index $n$ (more adiabatic) | **Decreases** slightly | **Decreases** significantly | Use intercoolers, slow-running compressors |
| Cooling during compression | No direct effect on $\eta_{vol}$ | **Increases** (lower $n$ ⇒ closer to 1) | Water-jacketed cylinders, external intercoolers |
| Lower intake temperature $T_1$ | No direct effect on $\eta_{vol}$ | No direct effect on $\eta_{iso}$ | **Increases** mass flow rate $\dot{m} \propto 1/T_1$ |

## 4.1 P–V Diagram of Reciprocating Compressor with Clearance (Block Representation)

        ST3["STATE 3: P1 , V3 - End of expansion, Start of fresh intake"]

    P23["PROCESS 2 TO 4: Isobaric DISCHARGE at P2 - volume drops from Vc to Vc"]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, PCMUT403]**

**Q1. Define isothermal efficiency of a compressor. Why is its value always less than 100% for a real compressor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> Isothermal efficiency of a compressor is the ratio of the isothermal (minimum theoretical) work input to the actual work input for compressing a given mass of gas through a given pressure ratio:
> $$\eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{isothermal}}{W_{actual}}$$

> **Reason (1.5 marks):**
> Its value is always less than 100% because real compression processes are **not truly isothermal and reversible**. Heat generated during rapid compression cannot be rejected to the surroundings instantaneously, raising the gas temperature above ambient. This raises the discharge pressure-volume trajectory above the isothermal curve, requiring **more work** for the same pressure ratio. Friction, turbulence, and pressure-drop in valves also contribute additional irreversibilities.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, PCMUT403]**

**Q2. Define volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor. List the factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of the actual volume of gas drawn in (at inlet conditions) per cycle to the swept (displacement) volume of the piston:
> $$\eta_{vol} = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}}$$

> **Factors (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Clearance ratio** $C = V_c/V_s$ — higher $C$ reduces $\eta_{vol}$.
> 2. **Pressure ratio** $r = P_2/P_1$ — higher $r$ reduces $\eta_{vol}$.
> 3. **Polytropic index** $n$ — higher $n$ reduces $\eta_{vol}$.
> 4. Inlet valve throttling, leakage, and gas heating (real-world additional factors).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Numerical on Single-Stage Compressor

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module 3, PCMUT403]**

A single-stage, single-acting reciprocating compressor running at **300 rpm** delivers air at **8 bar** from atmospheric conditions of **1 bar and 27 °C**. The clearance volume is **5% of the swept volume**. The compression and expansion processes follow **PV^1.3 = C**. The bore and stroke are both **200 mm**. Calculate:
- **(a)** Volumetric efficiency, and
- **(b)** Isothermal efficiency, and
- **(c)** Power required to drive the compressor.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $\quad P_2 = 8 \text{ bar}$, $\quad r = 8$
- $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300 \text{ K}$
- $C = 0.05$, $\quad n = 1.3$
- $N = 300 \text{ rpm}$, $\quad D = L = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- $R_{air} = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$

**Swept Volume:**
$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 (0.2) = 0.006283 \text{ m}^3$$

**Part (a) — Volumetric Efficiency (7 marks):**

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \cdot r^{1/n}$$

Compute $r^{1/n} = 8^{1/1.3}$:

$$\ln(8) = 2.0794, \quad \frac{2.0794}{1.3} = 1.5996, \quad e^{1.5996} = 4.949$$

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05 \times 4.949 = 1.05 - 0.2475 = 0.8025$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{vol} = 80.25\%}$$

**Part (b) — Isothermal Efficiency (7 marks):**

$$\eta_{iso} = \frac{\dfrac{n-1}{n} \cdot \ln(r)}{r^{(n-1)/n} - 1}$$

Compute $r^{(n-1)/n} = 8^{0.3/1.3} = 8^{0.2308}$:

$$\ln(8) \times 0.2308 = 2.0794 \times 0.2308 = 0.4799, \quad e^{0.4799} = 1.616$$

$$\eta_{iso} = \frac{0.2308 \times 2.0794}{1.616 - 1} = \frac{0.4799}{0.616} = 0.7791$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{iso} = 77.91\%}$$

**Part (c) — Power Required (included in above allocations; total 14 marks):**

Actual volume drawn in per cycle:
$$V_{actual} = \eta_{vol} \times V_s = 0.8025 \times 0.006283 = 0.005042 \text{ m}^3$$

Work per cycle (polytropic, using $V_{actual}$):
$$W_{cycle} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_{actual} \left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$
$$W_{cycle} = \frac{1.3}{0.3} \times 10^5 \times 0.005042 \times (1.616 - 1)$$
$$W_{cycle} = 4.3333 \times 504.2 \times 0.616 = 1345.7 \text{ J}$$

Number of cycles per second (single-acting):
$$n_c = \frac{300}{60} = 5 \text{ cycles/s}$$

$$\text{Power} = W_{cycle} \times n_c = 1345.7 \times 5 = 6728.5 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Power} \approx 6.73 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice: Focus on Work Saving with Isothermal Reference

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, Module 3, PCMUT403]**

A single-stage reciprocating compressor compresses air from **100 kPa, 300 K** to **800 kPa**. The clearance ratio is **4%**. The compression and expansion follow a polytropic law with index **n = 1.25**. Determine:
- **(a)** The volumetric efficiency of the compressor, and
- **(b)** The isothermal efficiency, and
- **(c)** The percentage saving in work if the compression were carried out isothermally (compared to the actual polytropic compression).

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $\quad P_2 = 800 \text{ kPa}$, $\quad r = 8$
- $C = 0.04$, $\quad n = 1.25$, $\quad T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$

**Part (a) — Volumetric Efficiency (5 marks):**

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \cdot r^{1/n} = 1 + 0.04 - 0.04 \times 8^{1/1.25}$$

Compute $8^{1/1.25} = 8^{0.8}$:
$$\ln(8) \times 0.8 = 2.0794 \times 0.8 = 1.6635, \quad e^{1.6635} = 5.277$$

$$\eta_{vol} = 1.04 - 0.04 \times 5.277 = 1.04 - 0.2111 = 0.8289$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{vol} = 82.89\%}$$

**Part (b) — Isothermal Efficiency (4 marks):**

Compute $r^{(n-1)/n} = 8^{0.25/1.25} = 8^{0.2}$:
$$\ln(8) \times 0.2 = 2.0794 \times 0.2 = 0.4159, \quad e^{0.4159} = 1.516$$

$$\eta_{iso} = \frac{(0.25/1.25) \times 2.0794}{1.516 - 1} = \frac{0.2 \times 2.0794}{0.516} = \frac{0.4159}{0.516} = 0.8060$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{iso} = 80.60\%}$$

**Part (c) — Percentage Saving (5 marks):**

If isothermal efficiency is $\eta_{iso} = W_{iso}/W_{actual}$, then the fraction of work that could be saved by isothermal compression is:
$$\text{Saving fraction} = 1 - \eta_{iso} = 1 - 0.8060 = 0.1940$$

$$\boxed{\text{Percentage saving} = 19.40\%}$$

This means isothermal compression would require only 80.6% of the actual polytropic work — a saving of 19.4%, justifying the use of cooling jackets and intercoolers in real installations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Mixing up $r^{1/n}$ vs $r^{(n-1)/n}$:** The exponent in the volumetric efficiency formula is $1/n$, but in the isothermal efficiency formula it is $(n-1)/n$. A swap loses **3–4 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert to SI units:** $P_1$ in kPa and volume in m³ gives work in kJ, not J. Inconsistent units (e.g., bar × m³ mixed with Pa × m³) cause **silent numerical errors**.
> 3. **Using $V_s$ instead of $V_{actual}$ in the work formula:** The work formula uses the **actual intake volume** $(V_1 - V_3)$, not the swept volume. Mistake: gives an answer ~20% too high.
> 4. **Not adding 273:** $T_1$ in the gas equation must be in Kelvin; failure to do so loses a mark for the mass flow rate part.
> 5. **Sign convention:** For a **compressor**, work is done **on** the system. Keep the formula positive for work input; do not introduce negative signs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Isothermal & Volumetric Efficiencies**

- **Isothermal efficiency** $\eta_{iso}$ compares **actual work** to the **minimum theoretical (isothermal) work** for the same pressure ratio. Formula: $\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{(n-1)/n \cdot \ln(r)}{r^{(n-1)/n} - 1}$.
- **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_{vol}$ compares **actual intake volume** to the **swept volume**. Formula: $\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \cdot r^{1/n}$.
- The **clearance ratio** $C = V_c / V_s$ is a geometric property; typical industrial values are **0.02 to 0.10**.
- The **pressure ratio** $r = P_2 / P_1$ appears with exponent $1/n$ in $\eta_{vol}$ and $(n-1)/n$ in $\eta_{iso}$ — **do not confuse them**.
- The **polytropic index $n$** lies between 1 (isothermal) and $\gamma$ (adiabatic). Lower $n$ ⇒ cooler compression ⇒ **higher** isothermal efficiency.
- **Net work per cycle** in a compressor with clearance is $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 (V_1 - V_3) \left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ — note that $V_1 - V_3 = \eta_{vol} V_s$.
- **$\eta_{vol} = 1$** when either $C = 0$ (no clearance) or $r = 1$ (no compression) — important boundary check.
- **$\eta_{iso} \to 1$** as $n \to 1$ (isothermal limit) — can be verified by L'Hôpital's rule.
- **Higher pressure ratio** ⇒ lower $\eta_{vol}$ **and** lower $\eta_{iso}$ ⇒ **favours multi-stage compression with intercooling**.
- **Percentage work saving by isothermal compression** = $(1 - \eta_{iso}) \times 100\%$ — a frequently asked KTU sub-question.
- **Mass flow rate** $\dot{m} = P_1 \dot{V}_{actual} / (R T_1)$ where $\dot{V}_{actual} = \eta_{vol} V_s \times N/60$ (single-acting) or $\times 2N/60$ (double-acting).
- **Assumptions in derivations:** Ideal gas, polytropic processes with the same index for compression and expansion, no pressure drop in valves, no leakage, no heat transfer to surroundings (already absorbed into polytropic index).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Stage Compression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Multi-Stage Compression ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multi-Stage Compression** is the process of compressing a gas in a reciprocating compressor through **two or more successive cylinders (stages)** of progressively decreasing volumes, with the gas being **cooled between successive stages** in an **intercooler**, before being admitted to the next higher-pressure stage. In a single-stage compressor, if the required pressure ratio is very high, the discharge temperature becomes excessively high, volumetric efficiency drops, and the work of compression becomes disproportionately large. Multi-stage compression overcomes these limitations by: - Splitting the total pressure ratio $r_{\text{total}}$ across multiple stages. - Employing **intercoolers** between stages to reduce the temperature of the gas back to (or near) the suction temperature. - Operating each stage at an **optimum intermediate pressure ratio**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme expects students to derive the *condition for minimum work of compression*, the *expression for work saved due to intercooling*, and to solve numerical problems involving **two-stage** compression with **perfect intercooling** (gas cooled back to initial temperature $T_1$ at constant pressure). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are **pumping up a bicycle tire** using a small hand pump. As the pressure inside the tire increases, the pump handle becomes **progressively harder to push**. This is because: 1. The gas being compressed is **already hot** from the previous stroke — you are pushing against *hot, high-pressure* gas. 2. The **temperature rises** dramatically during compression, making the gas harder to compress. Now, suppose you had a **two-cylinder bicycle pump** with a small **water jacket** between them. After the first cylinder compresses the air partially, it passes through the cooling jacket, where heat is removed. By the time the second cylinder receives the gas, it is **cool and at a moderate pressure**, so the second stage requires far less effort. **The savings in effort = Work saved by intercooling.** This is precisely the principle of multi-stage compression: instead of fighting against hot gas throughout, we *interrupt* the process, *cool* the gas, and then resume compression. The total mechanical work done is significantly less than that required by a single-stage machine delivering the same final pressure. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Application:** Multi-stage reciprocating compressors are widely used in **air-conditioning and refrigeration plants** (e.g., ammonia and CO₂ compressors), **gas pipeline boosting stations**, **petrochemical processing**, and **high-pressure air systems** (≥ 7 bar) for industries and breathing apparatus. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Gas constant for air** $R = 0.287\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ - **Isentropic index (γ)** for air $\approx 1.4$ - **Polytropic index (n)** for typical compression $\approx 1.25$ to $1.35$ - **Standard suction conditions**: $P_1 = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 288\ \text{K}$ to $300\ \text{K}$ - **Typical intercooler outlet temperature assumption**: $T_2' = T_1$ (perfect intercooling) ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Multi-Stage Compression? (Engineering Motivation) | Problem in Single-Stage Compression | How Multi-Stage Solves It | |---|---| | Excessive discharge temperature (can exceed lubrication flash point) | Intercooling restores gas temperature to suction level | | Very low volumetric efficiency at high pressure ratios | Each stage operates at moderate pressure ratio, improving $η_v$ | | Large mechanical work input for same final pressure | Work saved due to intercooling (≈ 15–40% depending on $r$) | | Mechanical stress on cylinder, valves, bearings | Distributes load across multiple, smaller cylinders | | Risk of auto-ignition in air compression | Lower discharge temperature avoids this hazard | ### 2.2 Theoretical Steps in Multi-Stage Compression (Polytropic Process) For **each stage**, treating compression as a **reversible polytropic process**: $$P V^n = \text{constant}$$ The work done per stage (per kg of gas) using polytropic relation: $$W_{\text{stage}} = \dfrac{n}{n - 1}\, P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$ **Stage-wise breakdown for an n-stage compressor:** - **Stage 1:** $P_1 \to P_2$ (pressure ratio $r_1 = P_2/P_1$) - **Intercooler 1:** Cool gas from $T_2$ back to $T_1$ at constant pressure $P_2$ - **Stage 2:** $P_2 \to P_3$ (pressure ratio $r_2 = P_3/P_2$) - **Intercooler 2:** Cool gas back to $T_1$ at $P_3$ - … and so on until **Stage n:** $P_n \to P_{n+1}$ (final pressure $P_d$) ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W_s = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | Work per stage (polytropic) | kJ | | 2 | $W_{\text{total}} = n \cdot W_s$ (n stages, equal ratios, perfect intercooling) | Total work of multi-stage compression | kJ | | 3 | $W_{\min} = \dfrac{n \cdot n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n^2}} - 1\right]$ | Minimum total work | kJ | | 4 | $r_{\text{opt}} = \left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_1}\right)^{\!1/n}$ | Optimum pressure ratio per stage | dimensionless | | 5 | $P_{\text{int}} = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_d}$ (for 2-stage) | Optimum intermediate pressure | kPa | | 6 | $T_{2'} = T_1$ | Perfect intercooling outlet temperature | K | | 7 | $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!1/n}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless | | 8 | $W_{\text{saved}} = W_{\text{single}} - W_{\text{multi}}$ | Work saved due to intercooling | kJ | | 9 | $\dfrac{W_{\text{saved}}}{W_{\text{single}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage work saved | % | | 10 | $T_d = T_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}}$ | Discharge temperature per stage | K | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Refrigeration & HVAC:** All industrial refrigeration compressors above 5 TR capacity use **two-stage compression with intercooling** to achieve suction and discharge pressures in different circuits. - **Air Separation Units (ASU):** Compress air to ~7 bar in multi-stage machines before cryogenic distillation. - **Natural Gas Pipelines:** Boosting station compressors operate at 60–80 bar, requiring **3 to 4 stages** with intercooling. - **PET Bottle Manufacturing:** High-pressure air (30–40 bar) is generated using multi-stage compressors. - **Diesel Engine Starting:** Air-start systems use multi-stage compressors to charge receiver tanks. > [!NOTE] > The **condition for minimum work** ($P_{\text{intermediate}} = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_d}$ for 2-stage) is a **university exam favourite** in KTU — you must derive it, not just state it. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Condition for Minimum Work in Two-Stage Compression with Perfect Intercooling **Given:** - Suction conditions: $P_1$, $V_1$, $T_1$ - Final delivery pressure: $P_d$ (fixed) - Intermediate pressure: $P_2$ (variable, to be optimised) - Polytropic index: $n$ - Perfect intercooling: $T_2' = T_1$ at pressure $P_2$ **Step 1 — Work done in Stage 1 (compression from $P_1$ to $P_2$):** $$W_1 = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$ **Step 2 — Work done in Stage 2 (compression from $P_2$ to $P_d$):** Since the gas is **perfectly intercooled** to $T_1$, we use $P_2V_2 = mRT_1 = P_1V_1$. $$W_2 = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_2V_2\left[\left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_2}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right] = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_2}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$ **Step 3 — Total work done in two stages:** $$W_{\text{total}} = W_1 + W_2$$ $$W_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}} + \left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_2}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 2\right]$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate $W_{\text{total}}$ w.r.t. $P_2$ (treating $P_1$ and $P_d$ as constants):** $$\dfrac{dW_{\text{total}}}{dP_2} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1 \left[\dfrac{n-1}{n}\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}-1}\cdot \dfrac{1}{P_1} + \dfrac{n-1}{n}\left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_2}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}-1}\cdot\left(-\dfrac{P_d}{P_2^2}\right)\right]$$ **Step 5 — Simplify using the algebraic fact that $\frac{n-1}{n} - 1 = -\frac{1}{n}$:** $$\dfrac{dW_{\text{total}}}{dP_2} = P_1V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!-1/n}\cdot \dfrac{1}{P_1} - \left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_2}\right)^{\!-1/n}\cdot \dfrac{P_d}{P_2^2}\right]$$ **Step 6 — Set the derivative equal to zero for minimum work:** $$\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!-1/n}\cdot \dfrac{1}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_2}\right)^{\!-1/n}\cdot \dfrac{P_d}{P_2^2}$$ **Step 7 — Cross-multiply and simplify:** $$P_2^2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!-1/n} = P_1 \cdot P_d \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_2}\right)^{\!-1/n}\cdot \dfrac{1}{P_1}$$ $$P_2^2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!-1/n} = P_d^2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_d}\right)^{\!1/n}$$ $$P_2^{2 - 1/n} \cdot P_1^{1/n} = P_d^{2 - 1/n} \cdot P_2^{1/n}$$ $$P_2^{2 - 1/n - 1/n} = P_d^{2 - 1/n} \cdot P_1^{-1/n}$$ $$P_2^{2 - 2/n} = \left(\dfrac{P_d^2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n}$$ **Step 8 — Raise both sides to power $\dfrac{n}{2(n-1)}$:** $$P_2 = \left(\dfrac{P_d^2}{P_1}\right)^{\!1/2} = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_d}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result — Condition for Minimum Work:** > $$\boxed{P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_d}} \quad \text{(i.e., intermediate pressure is the geometric mean of } P_1 \text{ and } P_d\text{)}$$ This means **equal pressure ratio per stage**: $$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_d}{P_2} = r_{\text{opt}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Work of Compression for n-Stage Compression **Generalisation for n stages:** Each stage operates at the same pressure ratio: $$r_{\text{opt}} = \left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_1}\right)^{\!1/n}$$ Work per stage (with perfect intercooling between every stage): $$W_s = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[r^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$ Total work: $$W_{\min} = n \cdot W_s = \dfrac{n \cdot n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n^2}} - 1\right]$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Work Saved Due to Intercooling **Single-stage work** (from $P_1$ to $P_d$ in one shot): $$W_{\text{single}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$ **Multi-stage minimum work:** $$W_{\text{multi}} = \dfrac{n \cdot n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n^2}} - 1\right]$$ **Work saved:** $$W_{\text{saved}} = W_{\text{single}} - W_{\text{multi}}$$ **Percentage work saved:** $$\%\ \text{saved} = \dfrac{W_{\text{saved}}}{W_{\text{single}}} \times 100$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What | Problem Condition | Formula to Use | Common Mistake | |---|---|---| | Two-stage with perfect intercooling, find $W_{\min}$ | $W_{\min} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ where $r = \sqrt{P_d/P_1}$ | Using total ratio in single-stage formula | | Find optimum $P_2$ | $P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_d}$ | Adding instead of multiplying under root | | Find work saved | Subtract $W_{\text{multi}}$ from $W_{\text{single}}$ | Forgetting the factor $n$ in multi-stage formula | | Intercooler exit temp | Assume $T_2' = T_1$ unless stated otherwise | Using $T_2'$ from previous stage | | Polytropic vs isentropic | Use $n$ for polytropic, $γ$ for isentropic | Mixing up indices | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Two-Stage Reciprocating Compressor ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ATMOSPHERIC AIR SUCTION
State 1: P1, T1, V1] --> B[LOW PRESSURE CYLINDER
Stage 1 Compression
P1 to P2] B --> C[INTERCOOLER
Constant Pressure Cooling
T2 reduced to T1] C --> D[HIGH PRESSURE CYLINDER
Stage 2 Compression
P2 to Pd] D --> E[FINAL DISCHARGE
State d: Pd, Td, Vd] C -.Heat Rejected Q_inter.-> F[COOLING WATER
or AMBIENT AIR SINK] classDef cyl fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#CC6600,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef cool fill:#B4D5FF,stroke:#003D99,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef node fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333333,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px class B,D cyl class C,F cool class A,E node ``` ### 4.2 P-V Diagram (Polytropic Compression Curves Comparison) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PVDIAGRAM["P-V DIAGRAM FOR COMPARISON"] direction TB G["SINGLE-STAGE PATH
Curve 1 to d directly
P1 to Pd in one shot"] H["TWO-STAGE PATH WITH INTERCOOLING
1 to 2 then cool at constant P2
then 2 to d from P2"] end G -. More work area under curve.-> P1[Area A1 + A2 + A3] H -. Less work area.-> P2[Area A1 + A2] P1 --> NOTE1[Work Saved = Area A3] NOTE1 --> RESULT[This area represents the SAVING due to intercooling] classDef area fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000 classDef saved fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000 class P1,P2 area class NOTE1,RESULT saved ``` ### 4.3 Multi-Stage Compression System Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB S1[Suction Filter and Inlet Valve] --> STG1[STAGE 1 LOW PRESSURE CYLINDER
Pressure ratio r] STG1 --> IC1[INTERCOOLER 1
Gas cooled from T2 to T1] IC1 --> STG2[STAGE 2 HIGH PRESSURE CYLINDER
Pressure ratio r] STG2 --> RCVR[RECEIVER / DISCHARGE TANK
Final Pressure Pd] RCVR --> OUT[Delivery to Process or Pipeline] IC1 -. Heat Q1 .-> CW1[Cooling Water Circuit 1] STG1 -. Jacket Cooling .-> CW2[Jacket Water Circuit] STG2 -. Jacket Cooling .-> CW3[Jacket Water Circuit 2] classDef cyl fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef cool fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef sys fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000000,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px class STG1,STG2 cyl class IC1,CW1,CW2,CW3 cool class S1,RCVR,OUT sys ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of Diagrams:** > - Diagram 4.1 shows the **functional flow** of gas through a two-stage compressor. > - Diagram 4.2 conceptually illustrates the **P-V work comparison** between single-stage and two-stage compression. > - Diagram 4.3 depicts the **real engineering layout** including jacket cooling circuits and the discharge receiver. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define perfect intercooling. Why is it preferred in multi-stage compression?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Perfect intercooling** is the process of cooling the gas between successive compression stages **back to the original suction temperature $T_1$** at the intermediate pressure $P_2$. **Reasons for preference (any three for full marks):** 1. **Minimum work of compression:** It ensures the condition $P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 P_d}$ yields the lowest possible work input. 2. **Reduces discharge temperature:** Lower final $T_d$ protects lubrication, valves, and cylinder walls. 3. **Improves volumetric efficiency** of the next stage since density at entry is higher. 4. **Reduces specific volume** entering the next stage → smaller cylinder capacity required. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the condition for minimum work of compression in a two-stage compressor with perfect intercooling.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The intermediate pressure must be the **geometric mean of the suction and delivery pressures**: $$P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_d}$$ Equivalently, the **pressure ratio per stage must be equal**: $$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_d}{P_2} = r_{\text{opt}}$$ **Mathematical justification:** Setting $dW_{\text{total}}/dP_2 = 0$ yields $P_2^2 = P_1 P_d$ — this extremum corresponds to minimum work (second derivative test confirms it is a minimum). --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** use the single-stage work formula and forget the **factor of $n$** (number of stages) for multi-stage work. This is the **#1 mark-losing error** in KTU exams. > - **Always specify units** in the final numerical answer (typically kJ or kW). > - When the problem says *intercooled to $T_1$*, it means **perfect intercooling** — use $P_2V_2 = P_1V_1$ directly. > - In **volumetric efficiency** sub-parts, do not confuse the clearance ratio $C$ with the specific heat ratio $γ$. > - Show **all intermediate steps**; KTU awards partial marks for method even if the final number is wrong. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Paper]` **A reciprocating compressor compresses air from 1 bar to 9 bar. The initial temperature is 300 K. Determine:** **(a)** The **optimum intermediate pressure** for minimum work using two-stage compression with perfect intercooling. Also compute the **work done per kg of air per stage** assuming polytropic compression with $n = 1.3$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The **work saved** by using two-stage compression with intercooling compared to single-stage compression for the same final pressure. Express the saving as a **percentage**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Optimum Intermediate Pressure & Work per Stage** **Given:** - $P_1 = 1\ \text{bar} = 100\ \text{kPa}$ - $P_d = 9\ \text{bar} = 900\ \text{kPa}$ - $T_1 = 300\ \text{K}$ - $n = 1.3$ (polytropic index) - $R = 0.287\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ **Step 1 — Optimum intermediate pressure:** $$P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_d} = \sqrt{1 \times 9} = \sqrt{9} = 3\ \text{bar}$$ **Step 2 — Optimum pressure ratio per stage:** $$r_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{3}{1} = 3$$ **Step 3 — Specific volume at suction (assuming $V_1$ per kg):** $$V_1 = \dfrac{RT_1}{P_1} = \dfrac{0.287 \times 300}{100} = 0.861\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Step 4 — Work per stage:** $$W_s = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ $$W_s = \dfrac{1.3}{1.3 - 1} \times 100 \times 0.861 \times \left[3^{0.3/1.3} - 1\right]$$ Compute exponent: $(n-1)/n = 0.3/1.3 \approx 0.2308$ $$3^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times \ln 3} = e^{0.2308 \times 1.0986} = e^{0.2535} \approx 1.2886$$ $$W_s = \dfrac{1.3}{0.3} \times 86.1 \times [1.2886 - 1]$$ $$W_s = 4.333 \times 86.1 \times 0.2886 = 107.3\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Answer (a):** $P_2 = 3\ \text{bar}$, $W_s \approx 107.3\ \text{kJ/kg}$ --- **Part (b) — Work Saved & Percentage Saving** **Step 1 — Single-stage work:** $$W_{\text{single}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$ $$W_{\text{single}} = 4.333 \times 86.1 \times \left[9^{0.2308} - 1\right]$$ Compute $9^{0.2308}$: $$9^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times \ln 9} = e^{0.2308 \times 2.1972} = e^{0.5071} \approx 1.6606$$ $$W_{\text{single}} = 4.333 \times 86.1 \times [1.6606 - 1] = 4.333 \times 86.1 \times 0.6606$$ $$W_{\text{single}} \approx 246.5\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Two-stage minimum total work:** $$W_{\text{multi}} = 2 \times W_s = 2 \times 107.3 = 214.6\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3 — Work saved:** $$W_{\text{saved}} = W_{\text{single}} - W_{\text{multi}} = 246.5 - 214.6 = 31.9\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4 — Percentage saving:** $$\%\ \text{saved} = \dfrac{31.9}{246.5} \times 100 \approx 12.94\%$$ **Answer (b):** Work saved $\approx 31.9\ \text{kJ/kg}$, which is approximately **12.94%** of single-stage work. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **A two-stage reciprocating compressor with perfect intercooling compresses 0.05 kg of air per second from 100 kPa to 1600 kPa. Take $n = 1.25$ and $R = 0.287\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$. Determine:** **(a)** The **optimum intermediate pressure**, the **pressure ratio per stage**, and the **temperature at the end of each stage**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The **total power required** to drive the compressor and the **percentage work saved** compared to single-stage compression. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Optimum Pressure, Stage Ratios, and Temperatures** **Given:** - $P_1 = 100\ \text{kPa}$ - $P_d = 1600\ \text{kPa}$ - $\dot{m} = 0.05\ \text{kg/s}$ - $T_1 = 300\ \text{K}$ (assumed standard) - $n = 1.25$ - $R = 0.287\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ **Step 1 — Optimum intermediate pressure:** $$P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_d} = \sqrt{100 \times 1600} = \sqrt{160000} = 400\ \text{kPa}$$ **Step 2 — Optimum pressure ratio per stage:** $$r_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{400}{100} = 4$$ **Step 3 — Specific volume at inlet:** $$V_1 = \dfrac{RT_1}{P_1} = \dfrac{0.287 \times 300}{100} = 0.861\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Step 4 — Temperature at end of Stage 1 (polytropic):** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}} = 300 \times 4^{0.25/1.25} = 300 \times 4^{0.2}$$ $$4^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \times \ln 4} = e^{0.2 \times 1.3863} = e^{0.2773} \approx 1.3195$$ $$T_2 = 300 \times 1.3195 = 395.85\ \text{K}$$ **Step 5 — After perfect intercooling:** $$T_2' = T_1 = 300\ \text{K}$$ **Step 6 — Temperature at end of Stage 2:** $$T_d = T_2' \cdot r^{(n-1)/n} = 300 \times 4^{0.2} = 395.85\ \text{K}$$ **Answer (a):** $P_2 = 400\ \text{kPa}$, $r_{\text{opt}} = 4$, $T_2 = T_d \approx 395.85\ \text{K}$ --- **Part (b) — Total Power and Percentage Work Saved** **Step 1 — Work per kg per stage:** $$W_s = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$ $$W_s = \dfrac{1.25}{0.25} \times 100 \times 0.861 \times [1.3195 - 1]$$ $$W_s = 5.0 \times 86.1 \times 0.3195 = 137.55\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Total work per kg (2 stages):** $$W_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 137.55 = 275.10\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3 — Total power required:** $$P_{\text{power}} = \dot{m} \times W_{\text{total}} = 0.05 \times 275.10 = 13.755\ \text{kW}$$ **Step 4 — Single-stage work (for comparison):** $$W_{\text{single}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$ $$W_{\text{single}} = 5.0 \times 86.1 \times [16^{0.2} - 1]$$ $$16^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \times \ln 16} = e^{0.2 \times 2.7726} = e^{0.5545} \approx 1.7411$$ $$W_{\text{single}} = 5.0 \times 86.1 \times [1.7411 - 1] = 5.0 \times 86.1 \times 0.7411 = 319.04\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5 — Work saved:** $$W_{\text{saved}} = 319.04 - 275.10 = 43.94\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6 — Percentage saving:** $$\%\ \text{saved} = \dfrac{43.94}{319.04} \times 100 \approx 13.77\%$$ **Answer (b):** Power required $\approx 13.76\ \text{kW}$; Work saved $\approx 13.77\%$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Multi-Stage Compression** - [x] **Definition:** Multi-stage compression = compression in ≥ 2 cylinders with **intercooling** between stages. - [x] **Purpose:** Reduce work input, lower discharge temperature, improve volumetric efficiency. - [x] **Optimum Intermediate Pressure (2-stage):** $P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_d}$ — **derive, don't just memorise**. - [x] **Optimum Pressure Ratio per Stage (n-stage):** $r_{\text{opt}} = (P_d/P_1)^{1/n}$. - [x] **Perfect Intercooling Assumption:** $T_2' = T_1$ → implies $P_2V_2 = P_1V_1$. - [x] **Work Formula (Polytropic per stage):** $W_s = \dfrac{n}{n-1}P_1V_1\left[r^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$. - [x] **Total Work (n-stage):** $W_{\text{total}} = n \cdot W_s$ — **always multiply by n!** - [x] **Work Saved = Single-stage work − Multi-stage work.** - [x] **Discharge Temperature per stage:** $T_d = T_1 \cdot r^{(n-1)/n}$. - [x] **Percentage work saved typically ranges 10–15%** for two-stage; more for higher pressure ratios. - [x] **Volumetric Efficiency:** $\eta_v = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ — improves with multi-staging due to lower per-stage ratio. - [x] **Practical Limitation:** Beyond 3 or 4 stages, mechanical complexity and pressure losses in intercoolers offset thermodynamic gains. - [x] **Common Mistake:** Using $P_1V_1$ in Stage 2 instead of $P_2V_2$ — remember, after perfect intercooling, $P_2V_2 = P_1V_1$, so the substitution works, but **only** under perfect intercooling. - [x] **Units Check:** $P$ in kPa, $V$ in m³, $W$ in kJ — consistent SI units throughout. - [x] **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state assumptions (perfect intercooling, polytropic index, ideal gas) at the beginning of your solution.

topics.title: Minimum Work Condition for Multi-Stage Compression - Simple Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Minimum Work Condition for Multi-Stage Compression - Simple Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multi-Stage Compression** is the process of compressing a gas from a lower pressure $P_1$ to a higher pressure $P_2$ in more than one cylinder (stage), with the gas being cooled between successive stages by passing it through an **intercooler**. The fluid leaving each stage is cooled back (ideally) to the original inlet temperature $T_1$ before entering the next stage.

**Minimum Work Condition** refers to the specific set of intermediate pressures at which the **total work of compression is minimum** for a given number of stages. The mathematical condition requires that the work share of each stage be identical, leading to the elegant geometric relationship:

$$\frac{P_x}{P_1} = \frac{P_y}{P_x} = \dots = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$

where $n$ is the number of stages and $P_x, P_y, \dots$ are the intermediate pressures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The condition for minimum work in multi-stage compression is one of the highest-weightage derivations in Module 3. Students are expected to both *derive* and *apply* the formula in numerical problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of climbing a steep mountain in one giant leap versus taking several smaller steps with a flat rest platform between them.

- **Single-stage compression** is like trying to leap straight up the cliff face — your muscles (the compressor) must do enormous work in a single burst, generating tremendous heat.
- **Multi-stage compression with intercooling** is like climbing the same cliff in **3 or 4 smaller steps**, with a cool resting platform (the intercooler) at each ledge. Each step is easier, the body (gas) returns to a cool relaxed state, and **the total energy spent is much less**.

The "minimum work condition" is simply the instruction: *place each rest platform at the perfect height so that every step requires exactly the same effort.* This is the geometric progression of pressures.

### 1.3 Standard Constants Used (for Air)

| Symbol | Quantity | Value (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R$ | Specific gas constant for air | **0.287 kJ/(kg·K)** |
| $C_p$ | Specific heat at constant pressure | **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** |
| $C_v$ | Specific heat at constant volume | **0.718 kJ/(kg·K)** |
| $\gamma$ | Ratio of specific heats | **1.4** |

> [!NOTE]
> For a **polytropic process** (which is the KTU-recommended model for real compressors), the index $n$ lies between **1 and $\gamma$**. Typical value: $n = 1.3$. For isentropic compression, $n = \gamma = 1.4$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Logical Steps (Operational Breakdown)

**Step 1 — Why single-stage is wasteful:**
When gas is compressed in a single cylinder, its temperature rises continuously. By the time the gas reaches the high pressure $P_2$, it is much hotter than $T_1$. Since work $W = \int V \, dP$ and the specific volume has expanded due to heating, the work of compression is *padded* by an unnecessary thermal component.

**Step 2 — Role of Intercooling:**
An **intercooler** is a heat exchanger placed between two compression stages. Its job is to extract the heat of compression from the gas, returning it to the **inlet temperature $T_1$** (perfect intercooling) before it enters the next stage. This reduces the specific volume of the gas entering the next stage, hence the work required.

**Step 3 — Why "optimum" pressure ratio exists:**
If the intermediate pressure $P_x$ is too low, the first stage does too little work and the second stage does too much — the total work rises.
If $P_x$ is too high, the opposite happens. The optimum is where the work of each stage is **equal**.

**Step 4 — The mathematical minimum:**
Differentiating the total work expression with respect to the intermediate pressure $P_x$ and setting $\dfrac{dW_{total}}{dP_x} = 0$ gives the geometric-mean condition.

**Step 5 — Generalization to $n$ stages:**
For an $n$-stage compressor, the optimum pressure ratio per stage is identical and equal to $\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$. This is what the KTU paper expects you to write down as the first line of any multi-stage problem.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-day lifeline** for this topic. Memorize the boxed relationships.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $W_{stage} = \dfrac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | Polytropic work per stage (with intercooling back to $T_1$) | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ | Optimum intermediate pressure for **2-stage** | kPa or bar |
| 3 | $W_{min,2} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{2n}} - 1\right]$ | Minimum total work, 2-stage | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $\dfrac{P_x}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$ | Optimum pressure ratio per stage (n stages) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $W_{min,n} = n \cdot \dfrac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n \cdot n}} - 1\right]$ | Minimum total work, **n-stage** | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $W_{single} = \dfrac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | Single-stage compression work | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $\% \, saved = \dfrac{W_{single} - W_{multi}}{W_{single}} \times 100$ | Percentage work saved | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Be careful with the exponent in Formula 5.** For $n$ stages, the exponent is $\dfrac{n-1}{n \cdot n}$, not $\dfrac{n-1}{n}$. This is a classic KTU mark-deduction zone.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multi-stage compression with intercooling is the **industrial backbone** of:

- **Compressed air systems** in manufacturing plants (paint shops, pneumatic tools).
- **Gas turbine power plants** — the compressor section is a multi-stage axial/centrifugal compressor.
- **Refrigeration and air-conditioning** — although here the work is done on the refrigerant, the principle of multi-staging with intercooling (or inter-cooling between LP and HP cylinders) is identical.
- **Hydrogen and natural gas pipelines** — gas is compressed at pumping stations in stages.
- **LNG plants** — large-scale liquefaction requires multi-stage compression with extensive intercooling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Minimum Work Condition (Two-Stage Compressor)

Consider a two-stage compressor with perfect intercooling. Stage 1 compresses from $P_1$ to $P_x$, gas is cooled back to $T_1$, then Stage 2 compresses from $P_x$ to $P_2$.

**Work of Stage 1** (polytropic, starting temperature $T_1$):

$$W_1 = \frac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

**Work of Stage 2** (starts again at $T_1$ due to intercooling):

$$W_2 = \frac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

**Total Work:**

$$W_{total} = W_1 + W_2 = \frac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} + \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 2\right]$$

**Differentiate with respect to $P_x$** and set to zero for minimum:

$$\frac{d W_{total}}{d P_x} = \frac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\frac{n-1}{n}\left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}\cdot \frac{1}{P_x} - \frac{n-1}{n}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}\cdot \frac{P_2}{P_x^{2}}\right] = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$\left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}\cdot \frac{1}{P_x} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}\cdot \frac{P_2}{P_x^{2}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_x}$$

A cleaner route is the **AM–GM argument**: for two positive terms whose product is constant, the sum is minimum when they are equal. The product of the bracketed pressure terms is $\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$, which is constant with respect to $P_x$. Therefore:

$$\left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_x}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$$

This gives:

$$\frac{P_x}{P_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_x} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}}$$

**Total Minimum Work (Two-Stage):**

Substituting $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ back into $W_1$:

$$W_{min,2} = 2 \cdot \frac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{2n}} - 1\right]$$

### 3.2 Generalization to n Stages

For $n$ stages with perfect intercooling, the total work is the sum of $n$ identical-looking stage terms, and AM–GM applied to $n$ terms gives the condition for equality:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{P_{x_1}}{P_1} \cdot \frac{P_{x_2}}{P_{x_1}} \cdot \ldots \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_{x_{n-1}}}$$

with each ratio equal to $\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$. The total minimum work becomes:

$$\boxed{W_{min,n} = n \cdot \frac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n \cdot n}} - 1\right]}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Two-Stage Compressor with Intercooler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Gas at P1, T1] --> B[Stage1Cylinder]
    B -- Compression to Px --> C[HotGasatPx]
    C --> D[Intercooler]
    D -- Cooled back to T1 --> E[GasatPxT1]
    E --> F[Stage2Cylinder]
    F -- Compression to P2 --> G[FinalGasatP2]
    H[WorkW1] --- B
    I[WorkW2] --- F
    J[CoolingLossQ] --- D
```

**Interpretation of the block diagram:**

- Block **A** represents the inlet gas at the initial state $(P_1, T_1)$.
- **Stage 1 Cylinder** performs the first stage of compression, raising the pressure to the intermediate value $P_x$ and temperature to $T_{x,1} > T_1$.
- **Intercooler** extracts the heat $Q$ generated in Stage 1, returning the gas to $T_1$ at pressure $P_x$.
- **Stage 2 Cylinder** takes over and compresses the gas from $P_x$ to the final pressure $P_2$.
- Works $W_1$ and $W_2$ are the energy inputs to each stage; the cooling load $Q$ is removed by the intercooler (typically a water-cooled shell-and-tube or finned heat exchanger).

### 4.2 P-V and T-S Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PVDiagram[Schematic PV Representation]
        A1[1] ---|Stage1| A2[X]
        A2 ---|Intercoolisobaric| A3[Xprime]
        A3 ---|Stage2| A4[2]
    end
    subgraph TSDiagram[Schematic TS Representation]
        B1[1] ---|Stage1adiabatic| B2[X]
        B2 ---|Intercoolerisothermal| B3[Xprime]
        B3 ---|Stage2adiabatic| B4[2]
    end
```

**Reading the schematic:**

- On the **P–V diagram**: State 1 is on the lower isobar, state 2 on the upper isobar. State $X$ is the hot gas leaving stage 1. State $X'$ is the cooled gas (same pressure $P_x$, temperature $T_1$).
- On the **T–S diagram**: Process $1 \to X$ and $X' \to 2$ are the (polytropic) compression processes. Process $X \to X'$ is the **isobaric cooling** inside the intercooler, returning the gas vertically down to the $T_1$ isotherm.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] **State the condition for minimum work in a multi-stage compressor with intercooling.**

**Model Answer:**
For minimum work in an $n$-stage compressor with perfect intercooling, the pressure ratio across each stage must be the same, given by:

$$\frac{P_{x}}{P_{1}} = \left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{1/n}$$

Equivalently, the work input to each stage is identical. For a two-stage compressor, this reduces to $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **Why is intercooling employed between the stages of a multi-stage compressor?**

**Model Answer:**
Intercooling is employed to:
1. Reduce the temperature of the gas leaving the previous stage back to the inlet temperature $T_1$ (in ideal/perfect intercooling).
2. Decrease the specific volume of the gas entering the next stage, thereby reducing the work of compression in subsequent stages.
3. Improve the volumetric efficiency of the next stage.
4. Reduce the discharge temperature, which protects the cylinder lubrication and prevents thermal damage.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Examination Note:** As per the 2024 Scheme, Part B questions carry 14 marks. You must attempt **one full question** (either Option A or Option B) from any module. Each sub-part below is worth 7 marks.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[Recommended Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model]**

**(a)** A two-stage compressor with intercooling compresses air from **1.01325 bar to 9.12 bar**. The initial temperature is **27 °C** and the compression is polytropic with index $n = 1.3$. Calculate:
   1. The optimum intermediate pressure.
   2. The minimum work of compression per kg of air.
   3. The work saved compared to a single-stage compressor for the same delivery pressure.

**(b)** If the same compression were carried out in **three stages** with perfect intercooling, what would be the new intermediate pressures and the new total work of compression? Comment on the trend.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a):**

*Given:*
$P_1 = 1.01325 \text{ bar}, \quad P_2 = 9.12 \text{ bar}, \quad T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300 \text{ K}, \quad n = 1.3, \quad R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**(a)(1) Optimum Intermediate Pressure:**

$$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2} = \sqrt{1.01325 \times 9.12}$$

$$P_x = \sqrt{9.2408} = 3.040 \text{ bar}$$

**(a)(2) Minimum Work of Compression:**

Compute the pressure ratio and the exponent:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{9.12}{1.01325} = 9.00$$

$$\frac{n-1}{2n} = \frac{0.3}{2.6} = 0.1154$$

$$9.00^{0.1154} = e^{0.1154 \times \ln(9)} = e^{0.1154 \times 2.1972} = e^{0.2536} = 1.2887$$

Substituting into the two-stage minimum work formula:

$$W_{min,2} = 2 \times \frac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.1154} - 1\right]$$

$$W_{min,2} = 2 \times \frac{1.3 \times 0.287 \times 300}{0.3} \times (1.2887 - 1)$$

$$W_{min,2} = 2 \times 373.1 \times 0.2887$$

$$W_{min,2} = 2 \times 107.71 = \mathbf{215.42 \; kJ/kg}$$

**(a)(3) Work Saved Compared to Single Stage:**

Single-stage work:

$$W_{single} = \frac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

$$\frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{0.3}{1.3} = 0.2308$$

$$9.00^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times 2.1972} = e^{0.5071} = 1.6607$$

$$W_{single} = 373.1 \times (1.6607 - 1) = 373.1 \times 0.6607 = 246.51 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

Work saved:

$$\Delta W = 246.51 - 215.42 = 31.09 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

Percentage saved:

$$\% \text{ saved} = \frac{31.09}{246.51} \times 100 = 12.61\%$$

**Part (b): Three-Stage Compression**

Optimum pressure ratio per stage:

$$r_3 = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/3} = 9.00^{1/3} = 2.0801$$

Intermediate pressures:

$$P_{x1} = P_1 \times r_3 = 1.01325 \times 2.0801 = 2.108 \; \text{bar}$$

$$P_{x2} = P_{x1} \times r_3 = 2.108 \times 2.0801 = 4.385 \; \text{bar}$$

**Check:** $P_{x2} \times r_3 = 4.385 \times 2.0801 = 9.12 \; \text{bar} = P_2$ ✓

Total work (three-stage):

$$\frac{n-1}{3n} = \frac{0.3}{3.9} = 0.07692$$

$$9.00^{0.07692} = e^{0.07692 \times 2.1972} = e^{0.1690} = 1.1841$$

$$W_{min,3} = 3 \times 373.1 \times (1.1841 - 1) = 3 \times 373.1 \times 0.1841 = \mathbf{206.06 \; kJ/kg}$$

**Comment on Trend:** As the number of stages increases, the total work of compression decreases monotonically and approaches the isothermal work as the limiting case. However, the mechanical and economic cost of additional cylinders, intercoolers, and piping also rises, so industrial compressors typically use **2 to 3 stages** as the practical optimum.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** A three-stage compressor with intercooling compresses air from **100 kPa to 6400 kPa**. The inlet temperature is **30 °C** and the process is polytropic with $n = 1.4$. Calculate:
   1. The optimum intermediate pressures.
   2. The minimum total work of compression per kg of air.
   3. The work saved compared to a single-stage compressor.

**(b)** For the same compressor, if the intercooling were **imperfect** and the gas entered the second stage at **40 °C** (instead of 30 °C), how would the work requirement change? Compute the new total work.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a):**

*Given:*
$P_1 = 100 \; \text{kPa}, \quad P_2 = 6400 \; \text{kPa}, \quad T_1 = 30 + 273 = 303 \; \text{K}, \quad n = 1.4, \quad R = 0.287 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$

**(a)(1) Optimum Intermediate Pressures:**

Pressure ratio:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{6400}{100} = 64$$

Optimum ratio per stage:

$$r_3 = (64)^{1/3} = 4$$

Intermediate pressures:

$$P_{x1} = 100 \times 4 = \mathbf{400 \; kPa}$$

$$P_{x2} = 400 \times 4 = \mathbf{1600 \; kPa}$$

**Check:** $1600 \times 4 = 6400 \; \text{kPa} = P_2$ ✓

**(a)(2) Minimum Total Work:**

Compute the exponent for the 3-stage case:

$$\frac{n-1}{3n} = \frac{0.4}{4.2} = 0.09524$$

$$64^{0.09524} = e^{0.09524 \times \ln(64)} = e^{0.09524 \times 4.1589} = e^{0.39609} = 1.4861$$

Compute the leading constant:

$$\frac{n R T_1}{n-1} = \frac{1.4 \times 0.287 \times 303}{0.4} = \frac{121.75}{0.4} = 304.38 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

Total minimum work:

$$W_{min,3} = 3 \times 304.38 \times (1.4861 - 1) = 3 \times 304.38 \times 0.4861$$

$$W_{min,3} = 3 \times 147.96 = \mathbf{443.88 \; kJ/kg}$$

**(a)(3) Work Saved Compared to Single Stage:**

Single-stage work:

$$\frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$

$$64^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times 4.1589} = e^{1.1883} = 3.2814$$

$$W_{single} = 304.38 \times (3.2814 - 1) = 304.38 \times 2.2814 = 694.40 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

Work saved:

$$\Delta W = 694.40 - 443.88 = 250.52 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

Percentage saved:

$$\% \text{ saved} = \frac{250.52}{694.40} \times 100 = 36.08\%$$

**Part (b): Imperfect Intercooling (T₂ entry = 40 °C)**

*Given:* Gas enters Stage 2 at $T_2 = 313 \; \text{K}$ (since 40 °C = 313 K). Stage 1 still uses $T_1 = 303 \; \text{K}$.

**Stage 1 Work:**

$$W_1 = \frac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_{x1}}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$$

$$W_1 = 304.38 \times (4^{0.2857} - 1) = 304.38 \times (1.4861 - 1) = 304.38 \times 0.4861 = 147.96 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Stage 2 Work (uses $T_2 = 313 \; \text{K}$):**

$$\frac{n R T_2}{n-1} = \frac{1.4 \times 0.287 \times 313}{0.4} = \frac{125.75}{0.4} = 314.38 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$W_2 = 314.38 \times (4^{0.2857} - 1) = 314.38 \times 0.4861 = 152.81 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Stage 3 Work:** Assuming no further intercooling given, gas enters Stage 3 at the discharge temperature of Stage 2. The temperature at the end of Stage 2 is:

$$T_3 = T_2 \times \left(\frac{P_{x2}}{P_{x1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = 313 \times 4^{0.2857} = 313 \times 1.4861 = 465.15 \; \text{K}$$

**Stage 3 Work:**

$$\frac{n R T_3}{n-1} = \frac{1.4 \times 0.287 \times 465.15}{0.4} = 467.10 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$W_3 = 467.10 \times (4^{0.2857} - 1) = 467.10 \times 0.4861 = 227.07 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**New Total Work:**

$$W_{new} = 147.96 + 152.81 + 227.07 = \mathbf{527.84 \; kJ/kg}$$

**Comparison:**

$$W_{new} - W_{min,3} = 527.84 - 443.88 = 83.96 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

The imperfect intercooling increases the total work by approximately **83.96 kJ/kg** (a **18.92% increase**). This illustrates the engineering importance of effective intercoolers in real installations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Exponent trap:** In the $n$-stage formula, the exponent is $\dfrac{n-1}{n \cdot n}$, not $\dfrac{n-1}{n}$. Many students write the wrong exponent and lose **3 to 4 marks** per sub-part.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $n$:** Some students compute the work for *one* stage and forget to multiply by the number of stages. The total work is $n \times W_{stage}$.
> 3. **R vs $\gamma$ confusion:** For an isentropic process, the index is $\gamma = 1.4$. For a polytropic process, the index is $n$ (typically 1.3). Read the problem statement carefully.
> 4. **Intercooling vs. isothermal:** Intercooling brings the gas back to $T_1$ **at constant pressure**, not at constant temperature throughout the cylinder. The cylinder process itself is still polytropic.
> 5. **Unit consistency:** $R = 0.287 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$ requires $T$ in **Kelvin**. Converting $°C$ to $K$ is the most-skipped step in KTU answer sheets.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** Multi-stage compression with intercooling reduces the total work of compression by removing heat of compression between stages.
- [x] **Perfect intercooling assumption:** Gas leaving the intercooler is at the original inlet temperature $T_1$ (this is the standard KTU assumption unless stated otherwise).
- [x] **Optimum intermediate pressure (2-stage):** $\boxed{P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}}$
- [x] **General optimum (n-stage):** $\dfrac{P_{x_k}}{P_{x_{k-1}}} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$ for every successive pair.
- [x] **Work formula (per stage):** $W_{stage} = \dfrac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$
- [x] **Work formula (n-stage total):** $W_{min,n} = n \cdot \dfrac{n R T_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n^2}} - 1\right]$
- [x] **Limiting case:** As $n \to \infty$, the work approaches the **isothermal work** $W_{iso} = R T_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$.
- [x] **Practical limit:** Industrial compressors use **2 to 3 stages** because the marginal work reduction beyond 3 stages does not justify the extra capital cost.
- [x] **Imperfect intercooling** raises the inlet temperature to the next stage, increasing both the work of that stage and the discharge temperature of all subsequent stages.
- [x] **Intercooler is a constant-pressure heat exchanger**, not a constant-temperature device — the gas temperature drops at $P = \text{constant}$.
- [x] **For air:** $R = 0.287 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, and $C_p = 1.005 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotary Compressors - Mechanical Details and Working Principle (Description Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Rotary Compressors - Mechanical Details and Working Principle (Description Only) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rotary Compressor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A rotary compressor is a **positive-displacement, continuous-flow, dynamic-rotating** machine in which a rotating element (impeller, rotor, vane, scroll, or screw) progressively traps, compresses, and discharges a fixed volume of working fluid per revolution. Unlike reciprocating compressors, rotary compressors deliver a **pulsation-free, continuous discharge** with high volumetric efficiency and a compact footprint. The compression is achieved by the **rotor imparting kinetic energy and then mechanical work of compression** as the working fluid is forced into a progressively shrinking chamber. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes these under the *positive displacement class* of compressors (Module 3, Block: Reciprocating & Rotary Air Compressors). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3:** "Rotary Compressors — Mechanical Details and Working Principle (Description Only)." Students are expected to describe construction, working, and salient features. **No numerical derivations** are asked from this sub-section. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **kitchen garlic press** that pushes air out when you rotate the handle. Now imagine **ten garlic presses arranged in a circle around a rotating shaft** — as the shaft spins, each press picks up a small pocket of air, squeezes it, and releases it continuously. The result is a **smooth, constant stream of compressed air** rather than the jerky, "push–rest–push" delivery of a piston-cylinder (reciprocating) compressor. Another everyday analogy is the **bicycle pump vs. a hand-held fan**: - **Bicycle pump (reciprocating):** Push and pull — discrete strokes. - **Table fan (rotary principle):** Continuous airflow by rotation. A rotary compressor works on the **same continuous-rotation principle** but captures the air between mechanical elements and physically shrinks the trapped volume. ### 1.3 Classification of Rotary Compressors (KTU Framework) | Family | Sub-Type | Compression Mechanism | |---|---|---| | **Positive-Displacement Rotary** | Roots Blower | Two-lobe meshing rotors | | | Sliding Vane | Eccentric rotor + spring-loaded vanes | | | Liquid-Piston (Ring) | Water ring + impeller | | | Screw (Helical) | Mesh of male & female rotors | | | Scroll (Orbital) | Two interleaved spirals | | **Dynamic (Turbo) Rotary** | Centrifugal | Radial impeller | | | Axial | Staged blade rows | > [!NOTE] > Per the KTU 2024 PCMUT403 syllabus, this topic emphasizes the **positive-displacement rotary** family, since the *Turbo compressors* are covered in a separate module (Module 4 or 5 depending on elective tracks). ### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Must Memorize) - **Swept Volume per Revolution ($V_s$):** $V_s = \text{displacement/cycle} \times n$ (where $n$ = rotational speed in rev/s). - **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$):** typically **80 – 95 %** for rotary vs. 60 – 80 % for reciprocating. - **Discharge Pressure Range:** **2 bar to 13 bar** (oil-flooded screw) — for higher pressures, multi-staging is required. - **Compression Ratio ($r_p$):** $r_p = P_2 / P_1$. - **Isentropic Work (for theoretical reference):** $W_s = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\, P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 General Operating Principle (Common to All Rotary PD Compressors) Every positive-displacement rotary compressor obeys the **three-stage volumetric cycle** described below. Even though mechanical details differ, the *thermodynamic sequence* is identical: 1. **Suction / Induction:** The expanding chamber draws in fresh fluid as the rotor un-meshes (or passes an inlet port). 2. **Trapping / Transfer:** The fluid is sealed between the rotor, casing, and (in vane types) the vanes themselves. 3. **Compression / Discharge:** The chamber volume shrinks, raising pressure; once discharge pressure is reached, the fluid is expelled through the outlet port. > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask "Explain the working of a Roots Blower with a neat sketch." Memorize the **three-port layout: Inlet → Trapping → Discharge**. ### 2.2 Mechanical Construction — Type-by-Type Analysis #### (a) Roots Blower (Twin-Lobe Compressor) - **Construction:** Two figure-8 (or involute) shaped rotors mounted on parallel shafts, synchronized by **timing gears**. They **do NOT touch** each other or the casing — the small gap is sealed by the gas film. - **Working:** Both lobes rotate in opposite directions. As lobes un-mesh at the inlet, gas enters the cavity. The trapped gas is conveyed to the discharge port where the meshing of the next pair of lobes pushes it out. - **Notable Features:** **No internal compression** — compression actually happens *back-flow* against the discharge port. Hence a **backflow-type** machine. - **Discharge:** Requires a **delivery (discharge) valve** to prevent back-leakage at high $P_2$. #### (b) Sliding-Vane Compressor (Rotary Vane Type) - **Construction:** Eccentric rotor mounted inside a cylindrical stator. Sliding **vanes** (typically 8–20) ride in radial slots and are kept in contact with the stator by **spring force** or **centrifugal action** (or both). - **Working:** 1. As vane slides outward, a **growing crescent volume** draws in gas (suction). 2. As rotor turns, the chamber is sealed by two consecutive vanes. 3. As the rotor approaches the **minimum clearance point** (top-dead-centre equivalent), the trapped volume is **reduced → compressed → discharged** through the outlet port. - **Notable Features:** **Built-in internal compression** → higher $\eta_v$ and lower noise than Roots blower. #### (c) Screw Compressor (Twin-Helical / Oil-Flooded) - **Construction:** A pair of **helical rotors** — *male* (convex lobes, usually 4) and *female* (concave cavities, usually 6). Inter-tooth spaces form the compression chamber. - **Working:** Gas enters at the **suction end**, is trapped between male/female flutes, and is **progressively compressed axially** as the rotors mesh along their length. Discharge occurs at the opposite end through a built-in port. - **Oil-Flooded Variant:** Oil is injected for **cooling, sealing, and lubrication** → allows **high pressure ratios (up to 13 bar)** in a single stage with **isentropic efficiency ≈ 85–92 %**. #### (d) Scroll Compressor - **Construction:** Two **identical Archimedean spirals** — one fixed (orbiting) and one orbiting in a circular path of small radius (anti-rotation prevented by an Oldham coupling). - **Working:** The orbiting spiral continuously *sweeps* the gas pocket toward the centre, where the volume shrinks → compression → discharge at the **central port**. - **Notable Features:** **Pulsation-free, low-noise, high-efficiency** — widely used in HVAC and refrigerant systems. #### (e) Liquid-Piston (Water-Ring) Compressor - **Construction:** Impeller with radial blades inside an elliptical casing partially filled with water (or other service liquid). - **Working:** Centrifugal force forms a **liquid ring** against the casing wall. Gas enters the impeller eye, is trapped between two consecutive blades and the liquid ring, and is **squeezed** as the liquid ring converges → discharge through a side port. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams) | # | Formula / Term | Expression | Units | Used For | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Swept Volume / cycle | $V_s = \pi/4 \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot L$ (screw) | $m^3$ | Screw compressor geometry | | 2 | Actual Delivery Volume | $V_a = \eta_v \cdot V_s \cdot n$ | $m^3/s$ | Throughput calculation | | 3 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{V_a}{V_s \cdot n}$ | — | Performance index | | 4 | Isentropic Work | $W_s = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | J or kJ | Theoretical power | | 5 | Compression Ratio | $r_p = P_2 / P_1$ | — | Process identifier | | 6 | Free Air Delivery (FAD) | $FAD = V_a \cdot \dfrac{P_1}{P_{atm}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{atm}}{T_1}$ | $m^3/s$ | Standardized throughput | | 7 | Power Input (brake) | $BP = \dfrac{W_s}{\eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$ | kW | Practical design | | 8 | Indicated Power (single-stage) | $IP = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \dfrac{P_1 V_a}{60} \left[r_p^{\tfrac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | kW | Reciprocating comparison | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Pitfall in Markdown Tables:** All vertical bars in formulas (e.g., $\vert P_2 / P_1 \vert$) have been replaced with $\mid$ or shown via parentheses/fractions to **avoid breaking markdown table syntax**. Always render formulas as `$$ ... $$` display blocks, **never inside table cells**. ### 2.4 Why Rotary Compressors? — Engineering Utility - **Compactness:** ~50–60 % smaller footprint than equivalent reciprocating units. - **Balanced Operation:** No reciprocating inertia → can run at **3 000 – 6 000 rpm** directly coupled to an electric motor. - **Lower Noise & Vibration:** No valve flutter at high speed (except Roots blowers). - **Continuous Flow:** Ideal for **process industries**, **HVAC**, **gas pipelines**, and **oil & gas** services. - **Maintenance:** Fewer wearing parts (vanes, bearings) → lower downtime. ### 2.5 Limitations (Examiner Favourite for 1-mark Statements) - **Higher manufacturing precision** → costly. - **Sliding-vane wear** requires periodic vane replacement. - **Roots blowers** have **no internal compression** → low efficiency at high $P_2$. - **High-pressure multi-staging** is complex and expensive. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-3 Note (Description-Only Topic):** The official syllabus tag is **"Description Only"** — meaning derivations and numerical answers are **not** assessed. However, the *analytical matrix* below gives a complete comparative derivation of **mechanical features, working sequence, and thermodynamic characteristics** — this is what examiners expect in **Part B (14-mark) descriptive answers**. ### 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Five Rotary Types The following table is a **KTU 14-Mark ready answer skeleton**. The student should reproduce this matrix in the exam hall (3–4 pages) and then expand each row with a short paragraph. | Parameter | Roots Blower | Sliding Vane | Screw (Oil-Flooded) | Scroll | Liquid-Piston (Water-Ring) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Rotor(s) count** | 2 figure-8 lobes | 1 eccentric + 8–20 vanes | 1 male + 1 female helical | 2 identical spirals | 1 impeller + liquid ring | | **Rotation Direction** | Counter-rotating | Single rotor | Counter-rotating | Orbital (anti-rotated by Oldham) | Single rotor | | **Internal Compression** | **No** (backflow type) | **Yes** | **Yes** (progressive axial) | **Yes** (radial-centrifugal) | **Yes** (centrifugal liquid ring) | | **Delivery Type** | Pulsating → needs check valve | Pulseless, continuous | Pulseless | Pulseless, very smooth | Slightly pulsating | | **Max.
