Pressure (single-stage)** | 1 – 1.5 bar | 7 – 8 bar | 10 – 13 bar | 8 – 10 bar | 5 – 6 bar | | **Volumetric Efficiency** | 70 – 80 % | 80 – 90 % | 85 – 95 % | 85 – 95 % | 75 – 85 % | | **Noise Level** | High (gear & lobe mesh) | Moderate | Low | Very low | Moderate | | **Lubrication** | Gearbox oil | Oil-flooded (often) | Oil-flooded mandatory | Oil-flooded | Water/liquid is the seal | | **Typical Application** | Pneumatic conveying, sewage aeration, supercharging | Workshop compressors, LPG transfer | Industrial shop-air, refrigeration | HVAC, refrigerant (R-410A, R-134a) | Vacuum pumps, chlorine/ammonia service | | **KTU Weight (marks allocation)** | High (most-asked) | High | High | Medium | Low | ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Working Description (Roots Blower — Model Answer) > **Step 1 — Suction:** > As the two-lobe rotors rotate in opposite directions, the lobes un-mesh at the **inlet port** (suction side). The volume between the un-meshing lobe and the casing increases, creating a partial vacuum (pressure drop below atmospheric). Atmospheric air rushes in to fill this expanding volume. > **Step 2 — Conveyance / Trapping:** > The trapped pocket of air is carried around the casing (peripherally) by the rotating lobe. **No compression occurs in this zone** — pressure remains close to the inlet pressure. The rotor geometry and tight clearances (~0.1 – 0.2 mm) prevent back-leakage. > **Step 3 — Discharge:** > At the discharge port, the oncoming lobe of the *other rotor* begins to mesh with the carrying lobe. The trapped air is **physically squeezed** out through the discharge port. A **spring-loaded delivery (check) valve** prevents reverse flow into the casing during the discharge pulse. > **Step 4 — Backflow Loss:** > For $P_2 > P_1$, a small backflow occurs during the time the gas must reach discharge pressure. This is why Roots blowers are **inefficient at high $P_2$** and are **commonly used in two-stage arrangements** with an inter-cooler for pressures > 1.5 bar. ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Working Description (Sliding Vane Compressor — Model Answer) > **Step 1 — Intake:** > Rotor is **eccentric** inside the cylindrical stator. As the rotor turns, the vane closest to the inlet port slides **outward** (centrifugal + spring force) — the crescent volume between vane, rotor, and casing expands → suction. > **Step 2 — Compression:** > As the rotor continues to rotate, the volume enclosed between two consecutive vanes **progressively decreases** (because the rotor eccentricity is closer to the stator at the top). Pressure rises smoothly. > **Step 3 — Discharge:** > When the leading vane uncovers the **discharge port**, the trapped gas at delivery pressure is pushed out. The cycle repeats continuously for every vane-to-vane cavity. > **Step 4 — Practical Limits:** > Vane tip wear is the major service concern. Modern vane materials (carbon, phenolic resin, PTFE-impregnated bronze) extend service intervals to **8 000 – 12 000 hours**. ### 3.4 Step-by-Step Working Description (Screw Compressor — Model Answer) > **Step 1 — Suction:** > The unmeshing of male and female rotor teeth at the **suction end** creates an expanding volume. Air (or gas) is drawn in axially. > **Step 2 — Compression (Progressive):** > As the rotors turn, the inter-tooth volume is *isolated* from both suction and discharge, and **axial progression** of the meshing point causes the trapped volume to shrink. Compression is therefore **continuous, internal, and progressive** — not instantaneous. > **Step 3 — Discharge:** > When the trapped volume reaches the **discharge port** (pre-set by the casing port geometry), the compressed gas at final pressure is released. No check valve is needed because the meshing rotors act as a self-sealing barrier. > **Step 4 — Oil-Flooded Cooling:** > Injected oil absorbs the heat of compression, lubricates the bearings, and seals the small rotor-to-casing clearances. Modern oil-flooded screws achieve **discharge temperatures of only 60 – 80 °C** above ambient. ### 3.5 Step-by-Step Working Description (Scroll Compressor — Model Answer) > **Step 1 — Orbiting Motion:** > The **orbiting scroll** rotates through a small circular path (typically 1–2° angular oscillation, but radial translation of ~5–8 mm) relative to the **fixed scroll**. The **Oldham coupling** prevents self-rotation of the orbiting scroll. > **Step 2 — Suction:** > At the **outer periphery**, the unmeshing spirals create a continuous expanding pocket that draws in gas along the entire 360° inlet ring. > **Step 3 — Compression:** > As the orbiting scroll sweeps, the trapped gas is **pushed toward the centre**, with the pocket's cross-sectional area continuously decreasing. Multiple pockets compress simultaneously at different radii — guaranteeing **continuous, pulsation-free** flow. > **Step 4 — Discharge:** > The central port of the fixed scroll releases the fully-compressed gas. There are no valves to chatter; the orbital motion itself performs the suction and discharge functions. ### 3.6 Step-by-Step Working Description (Liquid-Piston Compressor — Model Answer) > **Step 1 — Liquid Ring Formation:** > The impeller, mounted eccentrically inside the casing, **sloshes** the service liquid (water for air, sulfuric acid for chlorine, oil for vacuum) outward → forms a **liquid ring** on the casing inner wall. > **Step 2 — Suction:** > Where the impeller blades *emerge* from the liquid ring, the cavity is largest → inlet port opens → gas is drawn in. > **Step 3 — Compression:** > As the impeller rotates, the blades *re-enter* the liquid ring, **reducing the gas cavity volume** → pressure rises. > **Step 4 — Discharge:** > The compressed gas exits through a **side discharge port**, while the liquid simply re-circulates. The casing may need cooling to remove heat absorbed by the service liquid. ### 3.7 Comparative Performance Summary (Engineering Perspective) The **second law of thermodynamics** tells us that for an isentropic compression, the **minimum work is independent of the machine type**; the difference lies in: - **Real-machine irreversibilities** ($\eta_{is}$) - **Mechanical friction losses** ($\eta_{mech}$) - **Heat-transfer losses** to the casing (lower in oil-flooded machines) The *overall efficiency* of rotary compressors compared to reciprocating types is typically: $$\eta_{overall}^{rotary} \approx 1.10 \text{ to } 1.30 \;\text{times}\; \eta_{overall}^{reciprocating}$$ for the **same pressure ratio and throughput**, primarily because: - Lower dead-volume → higher $\eta_v$ - Lower reciprocating friction → higher $\eta_{mech}$ - Continuous cooling (oil-flooded) → closer to isothermal → lower $T_2$ → lower work input. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** When asked *"Compare rotary and reciprocating compressors"*, always present the comparison in a **tabulated form** with at least **8–10 parameters**. Marks are awarded for *headings*, *tabulation*, and *concluding remark*. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagrams follow the **Alpha-Rule** (every node ID prefixed with alphanumeric text, no reserved keywords as standalone IDs, no bold/italic inside node labels) and render a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of rotary compressor working. ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Universal Rotary Compressor Working Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inlet Port
Atmospheric Air] --> B[Suction Chamber
Expanding Volume] B --> C[Trapping Zone
Sealed Rotor Casing] C --> D[Compression Zone
Shrinking Volume] D --> E[Discharge Port
High Pressure Gas] E --> F[Receiver Tank
Optional Storage] F --> G[End Use
HVAC OR Pneumatic Tools] D -. heat-of-compression .-> H[Cooling System
Oil OR Water] H -. cooled gas .-> E ``` ### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — Five Rotary Types ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Rotary Compressors] --> B[Roots Blower] A --> C[Sliding Vane] A --> D[Screw Compressor] A --> E[Scroll Compressor] A --> F[Liquid Piston] B --> B1[Two Counter Rotating Lobes] B --> B2[No Internal Compression] B --> B3[Low Pressure Blower] C --> C1[Eccentric Rotor Plus Spring Vanes] C --> C2[Progressive Internal Compression] C --> C3[Medium Pressure Industrial] D --> D1[Male Plus Female Helical Rotors] D --> D2[Axial Progressive Compression] D --> D3[High Pressure Oil Flooded] E --> E1[Two Archimedean Spirals] E --> E2[Orbital Sweep Compression] E --> E3[Low Noise HVAC] F --> F1[Impeller Plus Liquid Ring] F --> F2[Liquid Sealed Compression] F --> F3[Vacuum Plus Corrosive Gas] ``` ### 4.3 Functional Block — Cooling & Lubrication Pathways ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Rotor Shaft] --> B[Bearing Housing] B --> C[Gearbox Oil Sump] C --> D[Timing Gears] D --> E[Synchronized Rotor Pair] E --> F[Compression Chamber] F --> G[Oil Injection Port] G --> H[Oil Cooler] H --> I[Oil Separator] I --> J[Receiver Tank] J --> K[Discharge Line] F -. heat .-> L[Intercooler or Aftercooler] L --> J ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Suction → Compression → Discharge Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[STEP 1 - Suction] --> S2[STEP 2 - Trapping] S2 --> S3[STEP 3 - Compression] S3 --> S4[STEP 4 - Discharge] S4 --> S5[Continuous Rotation Repeats Cycle] S5 --> S1 ``` ### 4.5 Block Diagram — Components Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart TB M[Main Rotary Compressor Unit] --> A1[Casing with Cooling Fins] M --> A2[Inlet Filter and Silencer] M --> A3[Rotor OR Impeller Assembly] M --> A4[Drive Coupling] M --> A5[Discharge Check Valve] M --> A6[Oil Pump and Filter] M --> A7[Control Panel and Safety Valves] M --> A8[Pressure Gauge and Temperature Sensors] ``` ### 4.6 KTU Examination Visual Aid — Pressure-Volume Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Low Pressure Inlet P1] --> V1[High Volume V1] V1 --> V2[Mid Volume V2 at Pmid] V2 --> V3[Small Volume V3 at P2] V3 --> P2[High Pressure Outlet P2] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual Note for Examiners:** When sketching in the answer script, a student should draw a **cross-section of the casing** with the **rotor**, **inlet port**, **discharge port**, and **arrows for airflow direction**. Always label the **rotation direction** and mark the **clearance gaps**. A labelled sketch typically earns **3–4 marks** out of 14. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, 3 Marks]** > *Define a rotary compressor and list any three types.* **Model Answer:** A rotary compressor is a positive-displacement machine in which a **rotating element** progressively compresses a fixed volume of working fluid per revolution, delivering a **continuous, pulsation-free discharge**. Three types are: 1. **Roots Blower** (twin-lobe) 2. **Sliding-Vane Compressor** 3. **Screw (Helical) Compressor** > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, 3 Marks]** > *Why is a Roots Blower called a "back-flow type" compressor?* **Model Answer:** In a Roots Blower, **no internal compression** takes place. The trapped gas is carried at inlet pressure from the suction port to the discharge port. At the discharge port, the oncoming lobe squeezes the gas out, but a small **backflow** of high-pressure gas into the casing is inevitable until the delivery valve opens. This reverse leakage into the casing during the discharge stroke is why the machine is called a **back-flow type** compressor. --- ### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, 14 Marks]** > > **Question A (Chosen):** > **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **Roots Blower**. Mention its applications and limitations. (7 Marks) > **(b)** Compare **sliding-vane** and **screw compressors** in a tabular form covering at least eight parameters. (7 Marks) > > **OR** > > **Question B (Alternative):** > **(a)** Explain the **mechanical construction** of an **oil-flooded screw compressor** with a labelled diagram. (7 Marks) > **(b)** Describe the **working principle** of a **scroll compressor**. Why is it preferred for HVAC applications? (7 Marks) --- #### 5.2.1 MODEL SOLUTION — Question A (a): Roots Blower **Construction:** - Two **figure-8 (or involute) lobe-shaped rotors** mounted on parallel shafts inside a close-fitting casing. - Rotors are **synchronized by external timing gears** to maintain a constant small clearance (~0.1 – 0.2 mm) between them. - Each rotor has lobes (typically 2 or 3); 2-lobe is more common. - The casing has a single **inlet port** (suction side) and a single **discharge port** (pressure side). - A **spring-loaded delivery (check) valve** is mounted at the discharge port to prevent backflow. **Working:** 1. **Suction:** As the rotors un-mesh at the inlet port, the gas-filled cavity expands → pressure drops below atmospheric → fresh gas is drawn in. 2. **Conveyance:** The trapped gas is carried around the periphery of the casing by the rotating lobe, **without any compression** inside the chamber. 3. **Discharge:** At the discharge port, the meshing of the next pair of lobes **squeezes the gas out**. The delivery valve opens to release the gas to the receiver. 4. **Backflow:** When $P_2 > P_1$, a small backflow of discharge gas enters the casing until valve opening pressure is reached. This is an inherent loss. **Applications:** - Pneumatic conveying of granular solids (cement, grain, plastics). - Sewage aeration in effluent treatment plants. - Supercharging of internal combustion engines. - Gas boosting in blast furnaces and coke ovens. - Vacuum service (low absolute pressure ranges). **Limitations:** - No internal compression → low efficiency at high $P_2$. - Pulsating discharge → requires pulsation dampers. - High noise and vibration due to lobe meshing. - Sensitive to dust and contaminants → requires inlet filtration. - Limited to low pressure ratios (single-stage ≤ 1.5 bar). --- #### 5.2.2 MODEL SOLUTION — Question A (b): Comparison Table | Parameter | Sliding-Vane Compressor | Screw (Oil-Flooded) Compressor | |---|---|---| | **Rotor** | Single eccentric rotor with 8–20 sliding vanes | Male + female helical rotors | | **Lubrication** | Often oil-flooded (oil carries away heat) | Oil-flooded mandatory (sealing + cooling) | | **Internal Compression** | Yes (radial, progressive) | Yes (axial, progressive) | | **Pressure Capability** | 7 – 8 bar single-stage | 10 – 13 bar single-stage | | **Volumetric Efficiency** | 80 – 90 % | 85 – 95 % | | **Noise Level** | Moderate | Low | | **Vane/Rotor Wear** | Vane tip wear → periodic replacement | Negligible (oil film protects) | | **Application** | Workshop compressors, LPG transfer | Industrial shop-air, refrigeration | | **Discharge Temp.** | 80 – 100 °C above ambient | 60 – 80 °C above ambient | | **Cost** | Moderate | High (precision machining) | | **Cooling** | Air-cooled or oil-injected | Oil-injected (multi-stage cooler) | | **Starting Torque** | Low (unloaded start) | Low (unloader valve standard) | --- #### 5.2.3 MODEL SOLUTION — Question B (a): Oil-Flooded Screw Compressor **Construction (Block-by-Block Description):** 1. **Casing:** Cast-iron or steel casing with internal cavities housing the rotor pair; port design determines the **built-in pressure ratio** (Vi ratio). 2. **Male Rotor:** 4-lobe convex helical rotor, typically driven by the motor through the drive coupling. 3. **Female Rotor:** 6-groove concave rotor, gate-shaped, driven by the male rotor. 4. **Timing Gears:** External synchronizing gears maintaining the meshing phase. 5. **Bearings:** Anti-friction roller bearings supporting both rotors axially and radially. 6. **Oil Injection System:** Oil pump draws oil from the sump and injects it at the discharge end of the rotors. 7. **Oil Cooler & Separator:** Oil absorbs the heat of compression → cooled in an oil cooler → separated from the discharge air in an oil separator vessel. 8. **Capacity Control:** Inlet unloader valve + variable-speed drive (VSD) options. **Working Principle:** - Air enters axially at the suction end where the rotors unmesh → inter-tooth volume expands → suction. - Trapped volume is conveyed axially toward the discharge end. The **axial meshing of the rotors progressively reduces** the inter-tooth volume → internal compression. - At the discharge port (preset by casing port geometry), the compressed gas exits into the receiver. - Oil injected at 2 – 4 bar above discharge pressure seals clearances, lubricates bearings, and absorbs ~80 % of the heat of compression. --- #### 5.2.4 MODEL SOLUTION — Question B (b): Scroll Compressor **Mechanical Construction:** - **Fixed Scroll:** Bolted to the casing; has an involute spiral groove and a **central discharge port**. - **Orbiting Scroll:** Identical spiral groove, but mounted on a crankshaft that drives it in a **circular path** (small radius, no self-rotation). - **Oldham Coupling:** A key mechanical element that translates the crankshaft's rotation into the **anti-rotational orbital motion** of the orbiting scroll. - **Tip Seals:** PTFE or similar polymer seals on scroll flanks to minimize leakage. **Working Principle:** 1. As the orbiting scroll moves through its small circular path, the **interleaved spirals create sealed gas pockets** at the outer periphery. 2. The **outermost pockets** are at suction pressure — air enters continuously from the inlet. 3. As the orbit progresses, the **pocket is pushed radially inward**; the volume shrinks → pressure rises. 4. Multiple pockets are simultaneously at different stages of compression, ensuring **continuous, smooth, pulsation-free** discharge. 5. At the **centre**, the gas is fully compressed and discharged through the central port of the fixed scroll. **Why Preferred for HVAC:** - **Pulsation-free** operation → low noise (60–65 dB(A) vs. 75+ dB(A) for reciprocating). - **High volumetric efficiency** (no re-expansion loss because there is no clearance volume). - **Few moving parts** (no valves, no vanes) → high reliability, long life. - **Suitable for variable-speed drives** → energy-efficient capacity modulation. - **Compact and light** → ideal for rooftop units, chillers, and residential split ACs. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:** > 1. **Skipping the labelled sketch** — Examiners deduct up to **2–3 marks** if no diagram is drawn. Always include a **neatly labelled cross-section** with arrows showing gas flow. > 2. **Confusing "vane compressor" and "Roots blower"** — Students often write the working of one when the question asked for the other. Read carefully. > 3. **Writing "rotary = centrifugal"** — Rotary compressors (per KTU Module 3) are **positive-displacement**, not turbo (centrifugal/axial). This is a **fatal conceptual error** that costs full marks. > 4. **Omitting applications & limitations** — Even a perfectly drawn diagram without applications typically scores only **4–5 out of 7 marks**. > 5. **Forgetting the delivery check valve** in Roots Blower answers — A classic 1-mark loss. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PCMUT403 Module 3 — Rotary Compressors (Description Only)** - ✅ Rotary compressors are **positive-displacement, continuous-flow** machines (NOT turbo). - ✅ **Five types** to memorize: Roots Blower, Sliding-Vane, Screw, Scroll, Liquid-Piston (Water-Ring). - ✅ **Roots Blower:** No internal compression → backflow type → needs delivery check valve → low pressure only. - ✅ **Sliding-Vane:** Eccentric rotor + spring-loaded vanes → progressive internal compression → medium pressure (7–8 bar). - ✅ **Screw Compressor:** Male + female helical rotors → axial progressive compression → oil-flooded for high pressure (10–13 bar). - ✅ **Scroll Compressor:** Two Archimedean spirals + Oldham coupling → pulsation-free → preferred for HVAC. - ✅ **Liquid-Piston:** Liquid ring (water/oil) acts as the piston → ideal for vacuum & corrosive gases. - ✅ Rotary compressors deliver **higher volumetric efficiency** (80–95 %) than reciprocating (60–80 %). - ✅ Rotary compressors are **compact, balanced, low-vibration**, and run at **3 000 – 6 000 rpm** directly coupled to motors. - ✅ KTU 2024 marks this topic as **"Description Only"** → expect **labelled sketches + tabulated comparisons** in 14-mark answers. - ✅ Examiner's gold standard: **neat diagram + 4-step working sequence + applications + limitations + comparison table**. - ✅ Always state the **rotation direction**, **inlet/discharge port locations**, and **clearance gaps** in your sketch. - ✅ Avoid confusing **rotary PD** with **turbo (centrifugal/axial)** — they are separate families. - ✅ Free-Air Delivery (FAD) and volumetric efficiency are the most-tested performance metrics in numerical questions from other sub-topics of Module 3 (kept for readiness, though not asked from this descriptive sub-section).

topics.title: Gas Turbines - Classification, Thermodynamic Analysis of Gas Turbine Cycles - Open, Closed and Semi-Closed Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gas Turbines - Classification, Thermodynamic Analysis of Gas Turbine Cycles - Open, Closed and Semi-Closed Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Gas Turbine

A **Gas Turbine** is a rotary internal combustion (or external combustion, in closed type) engine that converts the energy of a pressurized hot gas (typically products of combustion of air and fuel) into mechanical work by expanding the gas through a series of turbine stages. The complete system, in its ideal form, is analyzed thermodynamically using the **Air Standard Brayton Cycle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *gas turbine power plant* is a continuous-flow, internal combustion (or external for closed cycle) heat engine in which the working fluid (air or gas) is compressed, heated, and expanded to produce net shaft work, which is utilized to drive a load (typically an electrical generator or a mechanical compressor).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **powerful windmill in a box**. A gas turbine works just like this:

1. A large fan (the **compressor**) at the inlet sucks in air and pushes it into a smaller space, increasing its pressure (just like blowing air into a balloon makes it feel pressurized).
2. The pressurized air is then mixed with fuel and ignited (or heated externally), turning it into a high-energy, fast-moving hot gas — analogous to "inflating a balloon with hot, energetic air."
3. This high-energy gas is then directed at another set of fan blades (the **turbine**), causing them to spin at very high speed.
4. Some of this spinning energy is used to drive the front compressor; the rest is used to power a generator or propeller.

The energy chain is: **Chemical Energy (fuel) → Heat Energy (combustion) → Kinetic/Pressure Energy (hot gas) → Mechanical Energy (shaft rotation) → Electrical Energy (generator output).**

## 1.3 Classification of Gas Turbines

Gas turbines are classified based on the **path of the working fluid** and the **method of heat addition/removal**. This is the heart of Module 4 of PCMUT403.

| **Basis of Classification** | **Types** | **Key Feature** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cycle Path | Open, Closed, Semi-Closed | Whether exhaust is discharged or recirculated |
| Heat Addition | Internal Combustion (Open), External Combustion (Closed) | Combustion chamber type |
| Number of Shafts | Single-shaft, Twin-shaft (free turbine), Multi-shaft | Mechanical arrangement of compressor & turbine |
| Application | Industrial, Aviation, Marine, Power Generation | Operational use |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Concept:** The thermodynamic *cycle* (Open, Closed, Semi-Closed) is the analysis framework. The *mechanical* arrangement (Single-shaft, Twin-shaft) is the design framework. KTU 2024 exam questions typically focus on the cycle framework.

## 1.4 The Three Cycles — A Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 1. **Open Cycle Gas Turbine**
> Air is drawn from the atmosphere, compressed, heated in a **combustion chamber**, expanded through the turbine, and finally **exhausted to the atmosphere**. The working fluid is not recirculated. Most aviation and industrial gas turbines use this cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 2. **Closed Cycle Gas Turbine**
> The same working fluid (air, helium, or CO₂) is continuously recirculated in a sealed loop. Heat is added via an **external heat exchanger** (e.g., nuclear reactor, solar concentrator, or external furnace). Heat is rejected via a **cooling system** after expansion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 3. **Semi-Closed Cycle Gas Turbine**
> A hybrid design where a portion of the exhaust gas is recirculated and mixed with fresh air before compression, while the remaining portion is exhausted. The heat addition is partially internal and partially external. This cycle improves the heat recovery and fuel utilization.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Gas Constant for Air (R_air):** **0.287 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Specific Heat Ratio for Air (γ):** **1.4** (at room temperature, for cold air-standard analysis)
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of typical jet fuel:** **≈ 43,000 kJ/kg**
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** **101.325 kPa**
- **Standard Atmospheric Temperature:** **288.15 K (15°C)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Air-Standard Brayton Cycle (Foundation for All Three Cycles)

All three cycles (Open, Closed, Semi-Closed) are thermodynamically analyzed using the **ideal Brayton Cycle** as the baseline, with appropriate corrections for real-world irreversibilities.

The ideal Brayton cycle consists of **four reversible processes**:

1. **Process 1→2: Isentropic Compression** — In an ideal (frictionless) compressor.
2. **Process 2→3: Constant Pressure Heat Addition** — Heat is added at constant pressure (combustion chamber in open cycle; external heater in closed cycle).
3. **Process 3→4: Isentropic Expansion** — In an ideal turbine.
4. **Process 4→1: Constant Pressure Heat Rejection** — Heat is rejected to bring the fluid back to initial state (in closed cycle via cooler; in open cycle, fluid is exhausted to atmosphere).

## 2.2 Component-Wise Operational Analysis

### A. Compressor (Process 1 → 2)
- **Why?** Atmospheric air is at low pressure and low energy. To do useful work in the turbine, we must first raise the pressure of the air.
- **How?** A multi-stage axial or centrifugal compressor does this with minimum work.
- **Real (Actual) Work Input:** W_c = (h_{2a} - h_1) = c_p (T_{2a} - T_1)
- **Isentropic Work Input:** W_{c,ideal} = c_p (T_{2s} - T_1)
- **Compressor Isentropic Efficiency (η_c):** The ratio of ideal work to actual work.

### B. Combustion Chamber / Heater (Process 2 → 3)
- **Why?** The compressed air must be energized before expansion.
- **How?** Fuel is burned in the combustion chamber (open cycle) or external heat is added (closed cycle).
- **Heat Added:** Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2) per unit mass of air.

### C. Turbine (Process 3 → 4)
- **Why?** The high-energy hot gas must be expanded to extract work.
- **How?** Multi-stage turbine stages extract energy.
- **Real (Actual) Work Output:** W_t = c_p (T_3 - T_{4a})
- **Isentropic Work Output:** W_{t,ideal} = c_p (T_3 - T_{4s})
- **Turbine Isentropic Efficiency (η_t):** The ratio of actual work to ideal work.

### D. Heat Rejection (Process 4 → 1)
- **Why?** Required to complete the cycle in a closed system.
- **How?** Cooling water, atmospheric air (in open cycle the fluid just leaves).
- **Heat Rejected:** Q_{out} = c_p (T_4 - T_1) per unit mass.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for the KTU 2024 PCMUT403 Module 4 exam.

> [!NOTE]
> All temperatures are in **Kelvin (K)**, pressures in **kPa**, work and heat in **kJ/kg** unless otherwise stated.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Remarks** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Isentropic Compression Temperature | $T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | $r_p = P_2 / P_1$ is the pressure ratio |
| 2 | Isentropic Expansion Temperature | $T_{4s} = T_3 \cdot (1/r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Same exponent as compression |
| 3 | Isentropic Compressor Work | $W_{c,s} = c_p (T_{2s} - T_1)$ | kJ/kg |
| 4 | Isentropic Turbine Work | $W_{t,s} = c_p (T_3 - T_{4s})$ | kJ/kg |
| 5 | Compressor Isentropic Efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1}$ | Always $\leq 1$ |
| 6 | Turbine Isentropic Efficiency | $\eta_t = \dfrac{T_3 - T_{4a}}{T_3 - T_{4s}}$ | Always $\leq 1$ |
| 7 | Net Specific Work Output (Actual) | $W_{net} = c_p[(T_3 - T_{4a}) - (T_{2a} - T_1)]$ | kJ/kg of air |
| 8 | Heat Supplied | $Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_{2a})$ | kJ/kg of air |
| 9 | Heat Rejected (Closed Cycle) | $Q_{out} = c_p (T_{4a} - T_1)$ | kJ/kg of working fluid |
| 10 | Thermal Efficiency (Ideal Brayton) | $\eta_{th,ideal} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | Function of $r_p$ only |
| 11 | Thermal Efficiency (Actual) | $\eta_{th,actual} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | Always $<$ ideal |
| 12 | Optimum Pressure Ratio (with η_c, η_t) | $r_{p,opt} = \left[\dfrac{\gamma \cdot \eta_c \cdot T_3}{T_1 (\eta_c + \eta_t - \eta_c \eta_t + \eta_t \cdot (\gamma - 1))}\right]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | When TIT is fixed |
| 13 | Work Ratio (Closed Cycle) | $r_w = \dfrac{W_{net}}{W_{t,actual}}$ | Ratio of net to gross work |
| 14 | Mass Flow for Given Power | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_{net}}{W_{net}}$ | kg/s |
| 15 | Power Output | $\dot{P}_{net} = \dot{m} \cdot W_{net}$ | kW (or MW) |
| 16 | Air-Fuel Ratio (Open Cycle) | $(A/F) = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Dimensionless |
| 17 | Fuel-Air Ratio (Open Cycle) | $f = \dfrac{1}{(A/F)} = \dfrac{c_p (T_3 - T_{2a})}{LHV \cdot \eta_{comb}}$ | Used in combustion analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The exponent $(\gamma - 1)/\gamma$ for air (γ = 1.4) equals **0.2857**. The reciprocal $(\gamma)/(\gamma-1)$ equals **3.5**. Memorize these two values — they appear in nearly every KTU problem.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Open Cycle:** Used in jet engines (turbojets, turbofans), industrial power plants, marine propulsion, and offshore platforms.
- **Closed Cycle:** Used in **nuclear power plants** (helium is the working fluid in HTGRs), **solar power towers**, and certain **submarine propulsion** systems (because the cycle can be run silently and the working medium can be a non-flammable, non-toxic gas).
- **Semi-Closed Cycle:** Used in **combined cycle power plants** where the gas turbine exhaust is used to run a steam Rankine cycle (STAG/CCGT) — a key modern power generation technology. Semi-closed designs are also used in **chemical process industries** for syngas generation.

## 2.5 Performance Insights (The "Why" Behind the Math)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight 1:** The **ideal Brayton efficiency depends ONLY on the pressure ratio**. It does NOT depend on turbine inlet temperature (TIT). This is a famous KTU short-answer question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight 2:** In **real cycles**, the *optimum* pressure ratio (for maximum net work) depends on the TIT, the ambient temperature, and the component efficiencies. Increasing TIT shifts the optimum to higher pressures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight 3:** The **closed cycle** has one major advantage — the heat addition and heat rejection are both external, allowing independent control of pressure ratio and TIT. The **open cycle** is limited because the lowest temperature is fixed by the atmosphere.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Brayton Cycle

We begin with the **air-standard assumptions**:
- Air is the working fluid and behaves as an ideal gas.
- All processes are internally reversible.
- Compression and expansion are isentropic.
- Constant specific heats ($c_p$, $c_v$ constant) at room temperature.

**Starting from the definition of thermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}
$$

**Step 1 — Net work output of the cycle (from the First Law for a cyclic device):**

$$
W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat added (constant pressure process 2 → 3):**

$$
Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Heat rejected (constant pressure process 4 → 1):**

$$
Q_{out} = c_p (T_4 - T_1)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{c_p (T_3 - T_2) - c_p (T_4 - T_1)}{c_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

**Step 5 — Use isentropic relations for processes 1→2 and 3→4:**

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

**Step 6 — Let $x = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. Then $T_2 = x T_1$ and $T_4 = T_3 / x$.**

**Step 7 — Substitute into the efficiency expression:**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{(T_3/x) - T_1}{T_3 - x T_1}
$$

**Step 8 — Divide numerator and denominator by T₁:**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{(T_3/T_1 x^{-1}) - 1}{(T_3/T_1) - x} = 1 - \frac{(T_3/T_1)/x - 1}{T_3/T_1 - x}
$$

**Step 9 — Cross-check by setting $T_3/T_1 = \tau$ and rearranging (standard textbook reduction):**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{x} = 1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The ideal Brayton cycle thermal efficiency is a function of pressure ratio only. For air ($\gamma = 1.4$), $\eta_{th} = 1 - 1/(r_p)^{0.2857}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Compressor and Turbine Efficiencies on Cycle Performance

In a real (actual) cycle, both the compressor and the turbine are not ideal. Let us derive the **actual net work output** and **actual thermal efficiency** when component efficiencies are considered.

**Given:**
- Compressor isentropic efficiency: $\eta_c$
- Turbine isentropic efficiency: $\eta_t$
- Pressure ratio: $r_p$
- Turbine inlet temperature: $T_3$
- Ambient inlet temperature: $T_1$

**Step 1 — Isentropic compressor exit temperature:**

$$
T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual compressor exit temperature (using definition of η_c):**

$$
\eta_c = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1} \implies T_{2a} = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Isentropic turbine exit temperature:**

$$
T_{4s} = \frac{T_3}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual turbine exit temperature (using definition of η_t):**

$$
\eta_t = \frac{T_3 - T_{4a}}{T_3 - T_{4s}} \implies T_{4a} = T_3 - \eta_t (T_3 - T_{4s})
$$

**Step 5 — Actual net work output (per kg of air):**

$$
W_{net,actual} = c_p (T_3 - T_{4a}) - c_p (T_{2a} - T_1)
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the expressions for T_{2a} and T_{4a}:**

$$
W_{net,actual} = c_p \left[\eta_t (T_3 - T_{4s}) - \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_c}\right]
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $T_{2s}$ and $T_{4s}$ in terms of T₁ and T₃:**

$$
T_{2s} - T_1 = T_1 \left[(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]
$$

$$
T_3 - T_{4s} = T_3 \left[1 - (r_p)^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]
$$

**Step 8 — Final compact form for actual net work:**

$$
W_{net,actual} = c_p \left[\eta_t T_3 \left(1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}\right) - \frac{T_1}{\eta_c}\left((r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right)\right]
$$

**Step 9 — Actual thermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th,actual} = \frac{W_{net,actual}}{Q_{in,actual}} = \frac{W_{net,actual}}{c_p (T_3 - T_{2a})}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Optimum Pressure Ratio for Maximum Net Work (Real Cycle)

To find the pressure ratio $r_p$ that maximizes $W_{net,actual}$, we differentiate the expression in Step 8 with respect to $r_p$ and set the derivative to zero.

**Step 1 — Let $y = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. Note that $dy/dr_p = \frac{(\gamma-1)}{\gamma} \cdot r_p^{-1/\gamma}$.**

**Step 2 — Express net work in terms of y:**

$$
W_{net,actual} = c_p \left[\eta_t T_3 \left(1 - \frac{1}{y}\right) - \frac{T_1}{\eta_c}\left(y - 1\right)\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to y and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{dW_{net}}{dy} = c_p \left[\eta_t T_3 \cdot \frac{1}{y^2} - \frac{T_1}{\eta_c}\right] = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for y:**

$$
\frac{1}{y^2} = \frac{T_1}{\eta_c \eta_t T_3} \implies y = \sqrt{\frac{\eta_c \eta_t T_3}{T_1}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to $r_p$ using $y = (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$:**

$$
r_{p,opt} = \left[\sqrt{\frac{\eta_c \eta_t T_3}{T_1}}\right]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = \left[\frac{\eta_c \eta_t T_3}{T_1}\right]^{\gamma/[2(\gamma-1)]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** The optimum pressure ratio for maximum net work is proportional to $\sqrt{T_3/T_1}$. Doubling TIT (e.g., from 1000 K to 2000 K) increases the optimum pressure ratio by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Work Ratio of a Gas Turbine

The **work ratio** is defined as the ratio of net work output to the gross turbine work output. It is a measure of how much of the turbine's power is "leftover" after driving the compressor.

**Step 1 — Gross turbine work (actual):**

$$
W_{t,actual} = c_p (T_3 - T_{4a})
$$

**Step 2 — Net work output (actual):**

$$
W_{net,actual} = c_p \left[(T_3 - T_{4a}) - (T_{2a} - T_1)\right] = W_{t,actual} - W_{c,actual}
$$

**Step 3 — Work ratio:**

$$
r_w = \frac{W_{net,actual}}{W_{t,actual}} = 1 - \frac{W_{c,actual}}{W_{t,actual}}
$$

**Step 4 — In terms of temperatures:**

$$
r_w = 1 - \frac{T_{2a} - T_1}{T_3 - T_{4a}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A *low* work ratio means the compressor consumes a large portion of the turbine output. This is undesirable. Closed-cycle gas turbines (with high pressure ratios) have lower work ratios than open-cycle units. Typical values: 0.25 to 0.55.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** An open-cycle gas turbine takes in air at $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 100$ kPa. The pressure ratio is $r_p = 8$. The turbine inlet temperature is $T_3 = 1300$ K. The compressor and turbine isentropic efficiencies are $\eta_c = 0.85$ and $\eta_t = 0.90$ respectively. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) and $\gamma = 1.4$. Calculate: (a) the net work output per kg of air, (b) the actual thermal efficiency, (c) the work ratio, and (d) the ideal Brayton efficiency for comparison.

### Solution

**Given Data:**
- $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 100$ kPa
- $r_p = 8$, $T_3 = 1300$ K
- $\eta_c = 0.85$, $\eta_t = 0.90$
- $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$
- Exponent $n = (\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.2857$

**Step 1 — Compute the isentropic exit temperatures:**

$$
T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot (r_p)^{0.2857} = 300 \cdot 8^{0.2857}
$$

$$
8^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln(8)} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 2.0794} = e^{0.5941} = 1.8114
$$

$$
T_{2s} = 300 \cdot 1.8114 = 543.43 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_{4s} = \frac{T_3}{(r_p)^{0.2857}} = \frac{1300}{1.8114} = 717.66 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute actual exit temperatures:**

$$
T_{2a} = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_c} = 300 + \frac{543.43 - 300}{0.85} = 300 + 286.39 = 586.39 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_{4a} = T_3 - \eta_t (T_3 - T_{4s}) = 1300 - 0.90 \cdot (1300 - 717.66) = 1300 - 523.11 = 776.89 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Net work output per kg of air:**

$$
W_{net} = c_p \left[(T_3 - T_{4a}) - (T_{2a} - T_1)\right]
$$

$$
W_{net} = 1.005 \left[(1300 - 776.89) - (586.39 - 300)\right]
$$

$$
W_{net} = 1.005 \left[523.11 - 286.39\right] = 1.005 \cdot 236.72 = 237.90 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual thermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th,actual} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{W_{net}}{c_p (T_3 - T_{2a})}
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 1.005 \cdot (1300 - 586.39) = 1.005 \cdot 713.61 = 717.18 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\eta_{th,actual} = \frac{237.90}{717.18} = 0.3318 = 33.18\%
$$

**Step 5 — Work ratio:**

$$
W_{t,actual} = c_p (T_3 - T_{4a}) = 1.005 \cdot 523.11 = 525.73 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
r_w = \frac{237.90}{525.73} = 0.4524
$$

**Step 6 — Ideal Brayton efficiency (for comparison):**

$$
\eta_{th,ideal} = 1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{0.2857}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5520 = 0.4480 = 44.80\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Component irreversibilities reduced the efficiency from 44.80% (ideal) to 33.18% (actual) — a loss of about 11.6 percentage points. This is the typical penalty observed in real KTU 2024 problem sets.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Open vs. Closed vs. Semi-Closed Cycles

| **Parameter** | **Open Cycle** | **Closed Cycle** | **Semi-Closed Cycle** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Working Fluid | Air (replenished) | Same fluid (air, He, CO₂) | Air + recirculated exhaust |
| Heat Addition | Internal (combustion) | External (heater) | Internal + external recuperation |
| Heat Rejection | To atmosphere (no cooler) | External cooler required | Partially to atmosphere |
| T_1 (Min Temp) | Fixed by atmosphere (T_atm) | Independently controlled | Fixed by atmosphere |
| Pressure Ratio | Limited (by TIT & materials) | Independently controlled | Limited |
| Thermal Efficiency | Modest (30–40%) | Can be higher (40–50%) | Higher (45–55%) with regeneration |
| Work Ratio | Higher (0.4–0.6) | Lower (0.2–0.4) | Intermediate |
| Starting Load | Light | Heavy | Heavy |
| Maintenance | Moderate | Low (clean fluid) | High (corrosion, fouling) |
| Typical Use | Jet engines, peak power | Nuclear, solar, submarines | Combined cycle (CCGT) |
| Air Mass Flow | Large | Small (high pressure, low flow) | Moderate |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Open Cycle Gas Turbine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> B[Air Filter]
    B --> C[Compressor]
    C --> D[Compressed Air to Combustion Chamber]
    D --> E[Combustion Chamber with Fuel Injection]
    E --> F[High Energy Hot Gas]
    F --> G[Turbine Stages for Expansion]
    G --> H[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    G --> I[Power Shaft Output to Load]
    I --> J[Electrical Generator or Load]
    G --> K[Turbine Drives Compressor]
    K --> C
```

**Block description:** Atmospheric air is filtered, compressed, mixed with fuel and burned in the combustion chamber, then expanded through the turbine. A portion of the turbine shaft power drives the compressor; the remainder is the net output.

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Closed Cycle Gas Turbine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Working Fluid Inlet from Cooler] --> B[Compressor]
    B --> C[High Pressure Working Fluid]
    C --> D[External Heater Nuclear Solar or Furnace]
    D --> E[High Energy Hot Gas]
    E --> F[Turbine Stages]
    F --> G[Expanded Low Pressure Gas]
    G --> H[External Cooler Water or Air Cooled]
    H --> A
    F --> I[Net Power Output to Load]
    I --> J[External Load Generator]
    F --> K[Turbine Drives Compressor]
    K --> B
```

**Block description:** The working fluid is fully sealed. Heat is added externally (no combustion occurs inside the loop), the fluid is expanded, and the heat is rejected via an external cooler before returning to the compressor inlet.

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Semi-Closed Cycle Gas Turbine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh Air Inlet from Atmosphere] --> B[Mixing Chamber with Recirculated Exhaust]
    B --> C[Compressor]
    C --> D[Combustion Chamber with Fuel]
    D --> E[Hot High Pressure Gas]
    E --> F[Turbine Expansion]
    F --> G[Expanded Hot Gas at Lower Pressure]
    G --> H[Splitter for Recirculation and Exhaust]
    H --> I[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    H --> J[Recirculation Line to Mixing Chamber]
    J --> B
    F --> K[Net Shaft Power to Load]
    K --> L[Generator or Mechanical Load]
    F --> M[Turbine Drives Compressor]
    M --> C
```

**Block description:** A fraction of the turbine exhaust is recirculated and mixed with fresh air, reducing the oxygen concentration and improving combustion efficiency. The remainder is exhausted. This is the basis of many combined cycle and chemical process gas turbines.

## 4.4 Thermodynamic State Diagram — T-s and P-v Representations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BlockA[T-s Diagram of Brayton Cycle]
        A1["State 1: Compressor Inlet T1 P1"] --- A2["Process 1 to 2: Isentropic Compression vertical line"]
        A2 --- A3["State 2: Compressor Exit T2s P2"]
        A3 --- A4["Process 2 to 3: Constant Pressure Heat Addition horizontal line to right"]
        A4 --- A5["State 3: Turbine Inlet T3 P3 equal to P2"]
        A5 --- A6["Process 3 to 4: Isentropic Expansion vertical line down"]
        A6 --- A7["State 4: Turbine Exit T4s P4 equal to P1"]
        A7 --- A8["Process 4 to 1: Constant Pressure Heat Rejection horizontal line left"]
    end

    subgraph BlockB[P-v Diagram of Brayton Cycle]
        B1["State 1: Low P High v"] --- B2["Process 1 to 2: Isentropic Compression curve"]
        B2 --- B3["State 2: High P Low v"]
        B3 --- B4["Process 2 to 3: Constant Pressure top horizontal line"]
        B4 --- B5["State 3: High P Slightly Higher v"]
        B5 --- B6["Process 3 to 4: Isentropic Expansion curve"]
        B6 --- B7["State 4: Low P High v"]
        B7 --- B8["Process 4 to 1: Constant Pressure bottom horizontal line"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The T-s and P-v diagrams are identical for Open, Closed, and Semi-Closed cycles under the air-standard assumption. The difference is in the **physical realization** of the heat addition and rejection (internal vs. external).

## 4.5 Performance Comparison Chart — Effect of Pressure Ratio

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EffectAnalysis[Effect of Pressure Ratio on Gas Turbine Performance]
        A[Pressure Ratio rp increases] --> B[Thermal Efficiency increases but plateaus]
        A --> C[Net Work Output first increases then decreases optimum at rpopt]
        A --> D[Compressor Work increases monotonically]
        A --> E[Compressor Discharge Temperature T2 increases]
        A --> F[Specific Work Output depends on rp and TIT]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The thermal efficiency of an ideal Brayton cycle is monotonically increasing with $r_p$, but in **real cycles**, the **net work output** peaks at a specific optimum pressure ratio (derived in Section 3.3). For TIT = 1300 K, T₁ = 300 K, η_c = 0.85, η_t = 0.90, the optimum $r_p$ is approximately 8–10, matching the value used in the worked example.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style]**
**"Differentiate between open cycle and closed cycle gas turbines with suitable examples." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Open Cycle** | **Closed Cycle** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Working Fluid | Air is taken fresh and discharged | Air/gas is recirculated |
| Heat Addition | Internal combustion | External heat exchanger |
| Heat Rejection | Exhaust to atmosphere | Separate cooler |
| Example | Jet engine, industrial peaking plant | Nuclear gas-cooled reactor plant |
| Compactness | More compact | Requires separate cooler/heater |
| Working Pressure | Atmospheric at inlet/exit | Pressurized throughout |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme (3 marks):** One mark for each of the three correct distinguishing criteria, with at least one example. Use a table for clarity.

---

### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**"Define isentropic efficiency of a compressor and a turbine. Why is it not 100% in practice?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Compressor Isentropic Efficiency** is defined as the ratio of the ideal (isentropic) work input to the actual work input:

$$
\eta_c = \frac{W_{c,ideal}}{W_{c,actual}} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1}
$$

- **Turbine Isentropic Efficiency** is the ratio of actual work output to ideal work output:

$$
\eta_t = \frac{W_{t,actual}}{W_{t,ideal}} = \frac{T_3 - T_{4a}}{T_3 - T_{4s}}
$$

- **Why not 100% in practice?** (1 mark) In real machines, there are irreversibilities such as: **fluid friction, turbulence, heat transfer across finite temperature differences, leakage past blades, and bearing friction**. These cause the actual processes to deviate from the ideal isentropic processes, making the efficiencies less than unity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and has a **Module Internal Choice** — meaning you may be given a choice between two questions from the same module. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Model Question]**

**"An open cycle gas turbine plant operates with the following data:**
- **Ambient conditions: T₁ = 300 K, P₁ = 1.0 bar**
- **Pressure ratio: r_p = 8**
- **Turbine inlet temperature: T₃ = 1300 K**
- **Compressor isentropic efficiency: η_c = 0.85**
- **Turbine isentropic efficiency: η_t = 0.90**
- **Combustion efficiency: η_comb = 0.98**
- **Calorific value of fuel (LHV): 43,000 kJ/kg**
- **Take c_p = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K), γ = 1.4**

**(a)** Determine the net work output per kg of air, the actual thermal efficiency, and the work ratio of the cycle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the plant is required to deliver 5 MW of net power output, calculate the air mass flow rate, the fuel mass flow rate, and the rate of heat rejected to the surroundings. **(7 Marks)**"

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute isentropic temperatures:**

$$
T_{2s} = T_1 (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \cdot 8^{0.2857} = 300 \cdot 1.8114 = 543.43 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_{4s} = T_3 / (r_p)^{0.2857} = 1300 / 1.8114 = 717.66 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute actual temperatures using component efficiencies:**

$$
T_{2a} = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_c} = 300 + \frac{543.43 - 300}{0.85} = 300 + 286.39 = 586.39 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_{4a} = T_3 - \eta_t (T_3 - T_{4s}) = 1300 - 0.90 \cdot (1300 - 717.66) = 1300 - 523.11 = 776.89 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Net work output:**

$$
W_{net} = c_p \left[(T_3 - T_{4a}) - (T_{2a} - T_1)\right] = 1.005 \cdot \left[523.11 - 286.39\right] = 237.90 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual thermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{c_p (T_3 - T_{2a})} = \frac{237.90}{1.005 \cdot (1300 - 586.39)} = \frac{237.90}{717.18} = 33.18\%
$$

**Step 5 — Work ratio:**

$$
W_{t,actual} = c_p (T_3 - T_{4a}) = 1.005 \cdot 523.11 = 525.73 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
r_w = \frac{W_{net}}{W_{t,actual}} = \frac{237.90}{525.73} = 0.4524
$$

**Summary of Results (a):**
- Net work output = **237.90 kJ/kg of air**
- Actual thermal efficiency = **33.18%**
- Work ratio = **0.4524**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Air mass flow rate for 5 MW power:**

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{\dot{P}_{net}}{W_{net}} = \frac{5000 \text{ kW}}{237.90 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 21.018 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat supplied per kg of air:**

$$
Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_{2a}) = 1.005 \cdot (1300 - 586.39) = 717.18 \text{ kJ/kg of air}
$$

**Step 3 — Fuel mass flow rate (using combustion efficiency):**

$$
\eta_{comb} = \frac{Q_{in}}{f \cdot LHV} \implies f = \frac{Q_{in}}{\eta_{comb} \cdot LHV}
$$

$$
f = \frac{717.18}{0.98 \cdot 43000} = 0.01702 \text{ kg fuel/kg air}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = f \cdot \dot{m}_{air} = 0.01702 \cdot 21.018 = 0.3577 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Total heat input rate:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot Q_{in} = 21.018 \cdot 717.18 = 15073.3 \text{ kW} = 15.07 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 5 — Heat rejected rate (energy balance):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{out} = \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{P}_{net} = 15073.3 - 5000 = 10073.3 \text{ kW} = 10.07 \text{ MW}
$$

**Summary of Results (b):**
- Air mass flow rate = **21.02 kg/s**
- Fuel mass flow rate = **0.358 kg/s**
- Heat rejected = **10.07 MW**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style Model Question]**

**"(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **closed cycle gas turbine** plant. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages over the open cycle gas turbine. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A closed cycle gas turbine uses helium as the working fluid. The maximum and minimum temperatures in the cycle are 1100 K and 300 K respectively. The pressure ratio is 6.0. Assuming ideal Brayton cycle, calculate the **thermal efficiency**, **net work output per kg of helium**, and the **work ratio**. Take for helium: $c_p = 5.193$ kJ/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.667$. **(7 Marks)**"

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Working Principle of Closed Cycle Gas Turbine:**

In a closed cycle gas turbine, the working fluid (helium, air, CO₂, or nitrogen) is continuously circulated in a sealed loop. The main components are:

1. **Compressor:** Compresses the working fluid from low pressure P₁ to high pressure P₂.
2. **External Heater (Heat Exchanger):** Heat is added to the working fluid from an **external source** (such as a nuclear reactor, a coal-fired furnace, or a solar receiver). The fluid is heated from T₂ to T₃ at constant pressure.
3. **Turbine:** The high-energy hot gas expands from T₃ to T₄, producing shaft work. A portion of this work drives the compressor, and the remainder is the net output.
4. **External Cooler (Pre-cooler):** The expanded fluid is cooled from T₄ back to T₁ at constant pressure before re-entering the compressor.

**(Sketch description — 3 marks):** A rectangular closed loop with arrows showing: Compressor → Heater → Turbine → Cooler → back to Compressor. Turbine shaft connected to Generator and to Compressor via a gearbox.

**Advantages of Closed Cycle (3 marks):**
1. The working fluid is sealed — no contamination, no fouling, low maintenance.
2. Pressure ratio and TIT are **independent of atmospheric conditions** — can use any heat source.
3. Can use non-flammable fluids (helium, CO₂) — safe in nuclear/solar applications.
4. The system pressure can be set optimally at design, increasing efficiency.
5. Lower exhaust emissions (combustion occurs outside the loop).

**Disadvantages of Closed Cycle (1 mark):**
1. Requires **two large heat exchangers** (heater and cooler) — high capital cost.
2. Lower work ratio than open cycle (high pressure → high compressor work).
3. Higher initial cost; not economical for small-scale applications.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** T₃ = 1100 K (max), T₁ = 300 K (min), r_p = 6.0, $c_p = 5.193$ kJ/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.667$.

**Step 1 — Compute the exponent:**

$$
\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.667}{1.667} = 0.4001
$$

**Step 2 — Compute T₂s and T₄s:**

$$
T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot (r_p)^{0.4001} = 300 \cdot 6^{0.4001}
$$

$$
6^{0.4001} = e^{0.4001 \cdot \ln 6} = e^{0.4001 \cdot 1.7918} = e^{0.7169} = 2.0481
$$

$$
T_{2s} = 300 \cdot 2.0481 = 614.43 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify T₃ using isentropic relation for ideal Brayton:**

For an ideal Brayton cycle in a closed system, $T_3 = T_4 \cdot (r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = T_4 \cdot 2.0481$. Given T₃ = 1100 K, we get:

$$
T_4 = \frac{1100}{2.0481} = 537.08 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4 — Net work output per kg of helium (ideal):**

$$
W_{net} = c_p \left[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_{2s} - T_1)\right]
$$

$$
W_{net} = 5.193 \cdot \left[(1100 - 537.08) - (614.43 - 300)\right]
$$

$$
W_{net} = 5.193 \cdot \left[562.92 - 314.43\right] = 5.193 \cdot 248.49 = 1290.5 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Heat supplied:**

$$
Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_{2s}) = 5.193 \cdot (1100 - 614.43) = 5.193 \cdot 485.57 = 2521.6 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Thermal efficiency (Ideal Brayton):**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.0481} = 1 - 0.4883 = 0.5117 = 51.17\%
$$

**Step 7 — Work ratio:**

$$
W_{t,ideal} = c_p (T_3 - T_4) = 5.193 \cdot 562.92 = 2923.0 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
r_w = \frac{W_{net}}{W_{t,ideal}} = \frac{1290.5}{2923.0} = 0.4415
$$

**Summary of Results (b):**
- Thermal efficiency = **51.17%**
- Net work output = **1290.5 kJ/kg of helium**
- Work ratio = **0.4415**

> [!NOTE]
> **Discussion (for partial credit, 1 mark):** The high thermal efficiency (51.17%) compared to air (33.18% in open cycle) is because helium has a higher $\gamma$ (1.667 vs. 1.4), which gives a stronger benefit from pressure ratio. This is why helium is the preferred working fluid in high-temperature gas-cooled nuclear reactors (HTR, HTR-PM).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing isentropic efficiency direction:** Students often write $\eta_c = (T_{2a} - T_1)/(T_{2s} - T_1)$ — this is **WRONG**. The correct form is $\eta_c = (T_{2s} - T_1)/(T_{2a} - T_1)$ because the *ideal* work should be smaller than the *actual* work for the compressor.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the actual vs. isentropic distinction:** When the question says "actual cycle," you MUST use $\eta_c$ and $\eta_t$ to find $T_{2a}$ and $T_{4a}$. Forgetting to convert gives inflated work and efficiency, and you lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Open vs. Closed cycle temperature limits:** In **Open cycle**, T₁ = atmospheric temperature (no cooler). In **Closed cycle**, T₁ is set by the cooler outlet. Forgetting this leads to wrong $Q_{out}$ calculations.
>
> 4. **Pressure ratio units:** KTU questions often give pressures in **bar** or **atm**. You may use ANY consistent units because $r_p$ is a ratio. Never convert to SI units for $r_p$ — just divide.
>
> 5. **Combustion efficiency vs. cycle efficiency:** In Part B, students often confuse $\eta_{comb}$ (combustion efficiency) with $\eta_{th}$ (thermal efficiency). Remember: $\eta_{comb}$ affects only fuel rate, not work output.
>
> 6. **No clear conclusion:** Always end with a boxed **Summary of Results**. Examiners look for this — without it, you may lose 1 mark even if all calculations are correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Gas Turbine Cycles)**
>
> ✅ **Three cycles:** Open (atmospheric fluid), Closed (recirculated), Semi-Closed (hybrid). All analyzed via the air-standard Brayton cycle.
>
> ✅ **Four ideal processes:** Isentropic compression → Constant pressure heat addition → Isentropic expansion → Constant pressure heat rejection.
>
> ✅ **Ideal Brayton efficiency** depends ONLY on pressure ratio: $\eta_{th,ideal} = 1 - 1/(r_p)^{0.2857}$ for air.
>
> ✅ **Key exponent for air:** $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.2857$. **For helium:** $0.4001$. **For CO₂:** $0.2258$.
>
> ✅ **Compressor efficiency:** $\eta_c = (T_{2s} - T_1)/(T_{2a} - T_1)$ — ideal over actual.
>
> ✅ **Turbine efficiency:** $\eta_t = (T_3 - T_{4a})/(T_3 - T_{4s})$ — actual over ideal.
>
> ✅ **Optimum pressure ratio** for maximum net work: $r_{p,opt} \propto \sqrt{T_3/T_1}$ (real cycle).
>
> ✅ **Work ratio:** Net work to gross turbine work. Closed cycles have low work ratios (0.2–0.4); open cycles have higher (0.4–0.6).
>
> ✅ **Closed cycle advantage:** Independent control of pressure ratio and TIT; can use any heat source (nuclear, solar).
>
> ✅ **Closed cycle disadvantage:** Requires two large heat exchangers; higher capital cost.
>
> ✅ **Semi-closed cycle:** Used in combined cycle plants; improves heat recovery by recirculating exhaust.
>
> ✅ **KTU favorite numerical:** "Open cycle, 8:1 pressure ratio, TIT = 1300 K, η_c = 0.85, η_t = 0.90" — practice this until you can solve it in under 10 minutes.
>
> ✅ **Forgotten to show isentropic temperatures?** Always show $T_{2s}$ and $T_{4s}$ as the first step — KTU examiners expect them.
>
> ✅ **Final answer must be boxed** with correct units (kJ/kg, %, kg/s, kW).
>
> ✅ **P-v and T-s diagrams must be drawn** for any cycle question (worth 2–3 marks).
>
> ✅ **Definitions to memorize verbatim:** Isentropic efficiency, work ratio, thermal efficiency, optimum pressure ratio, pressure ratio.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT403 - THERMODYNAMICS AND THERMAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Ideal Working Cycle - Brayton Cycle - P-V and T-S Diagram, Thermal Efficiency. Effect of Compressor and Turbine Efficiencies. Comparison of Gas Turbine and IC Engines, Analysis of Open Cycle Gas Turbine, Improvements of the Basic Gas Turbine Cycles - Regeneration, Intercooling and Reheating - Cycle Efficiency and Work Output. Combustion Chambers for Gas Turbines. Fuels and Combustion - Types of Fuels, Properties of Fuels, Combustion Equations, Combustion Analysis by Mass and Volume - Conversion of Gravimetric to Volumetric Analysis - Conversion of Volumetric to Gravimetric Analysis, Analysis of Fuel Combustion - Air Fuel Ratio, Equivalence Ratio, Minimum Quantity of Air, Flue Gas Analysis, Excess Air ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Brayton Cycle (Ideal Gas Turbine Cycle) **Formal Definition:** The **Brayton Cycle** is the idealized thermodynamic cycle that models the operation of a continuous-flow, constant-pressure open or closed gas turbine. It consists of four internally reversible processes: two isentropic (adiabatic reversible) processes and two isobaric (constant pressure) processes. It serves as the reference standard against which real gas turbine performance is evaluated. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Brayton Cycle is the *defining cycle* of gas turbine engineering. Any question on gas turbine efficiency, work output, or modifications begins with a clear Brayton Cycle sketch. **Conceptual Analogy — The Wind-Powered Kite Cycle:** Imagine blowing air continuously through a wind-sock (compressor) to pressurize it, then heating it with a flame (combustion chamber), then letting the hot high-pressure air escape through a pinwheel (turbine) that spins a shaft. Finally, the cooled low-pressure air is dumped out into the atmosphere. Repeat continuously. The pinwheel does useful work, but you had to put energy into the wind-sock first. The Brayton Cycle precisely describes this continuous "blow–heat–expand–exhaust" rhythm. ### 1.2 Four Constituent Processes | Process | Name | Path on T-S Diagram | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 → 2 | Isentropic Compression | Vertical curve | Ambient air is squeezed by compressor | | 2 → 3 | Constant Pressure Heat Addition | Horizontal line upward | Fuel burns and adds heat at constant pressure | | 3 → 4 | Isentropic Expansion | Vertical curve | Hot gases expand through turbine doing work | | 4 → 1 | Constant Pressure Heat Rejection | Horizontal line downward | Exhaust rejects heat to surroundings (closed cycle only) | ### 1.3 Gas Turbine vs Internal Combustion (IC) Engine — A First Look > [!NOTE] > **Core Distinction:** In a gas turbine, combustion is **continuous and external** (in a separate chamber). In an IC engine, combustion is **intermittent and internal** (inside the cylinder itself). | Feature | Gas Turbine | IC Engine (Otto/Diesel) | |---|---|---| | Combustion Type | Continuous | Intermittent | | Cycle | Open or closed (Brayton) | Closed (Otto/Diesel) | | Power-to-Weight | Very High | Moderate | | Starting | Requires auxiliary starter | Self-starting | | Efficiency Range | 35–45% (simple) | 35–45% (modern) | | Speed | Very High (10,000+ rpm) | Moderate | | Fuel Flexibility | Wide (gas + liquid) | Restricted | ### 1.4 Fuels and Combustion — Foundation Concepts **Definition (Fuel):** A *fuel* is any combustible substance that, upon reaction with an oxidizer (typically atmospheric oxygen), releases thermal energy usable for engineering work. **Conceptual Analogy — The Stove and the Recipe:** Think of combustion as baking a cake. The fuel is your ingredient (flour), oxygen is the leavening agent (yeast), and the products (CO₂, H₂O) are your finished cake. If you add too little "yeast" (oxygen), you'll get *incomplete combustion* (smoke, CO). If you add exactly the right amount, you get *stoichiometric* combustion. Add more, and you're *wasting* your leavening (excess air) — your cake still rises, but inefficiently. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Reaction (for hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ):** > $$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Since air is the practical oxidizer (21% O₂ + 79% N₂ by volume), each mole of O₂ is supplied with **3.76 moles of N₂**. ### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Properties > [!NOTE] > **Universal Gas Constant:** R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** > **Molecular Weight of Air:** M = **28.97 kg/kmol** > **Specific Gas Constant of Air:** R = **0.287 kJ/(kg·K)** > **γ (Air):** **1.4** > **cp (Air):** **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** > **cv (Air):** **0.718 kJ/(kg·K)** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Logic of the Brayton Cycle **Step 1 — Air Intake & Compression (1 → 2):** Atmospheric air enters the compressor at state 1 (T₁, P₁). The compressor consumes work to raise pressure from P₁ to P₂. In the *ideal* case, this is **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic). **Step 2 — Heat Addition (2 → 3):** Fuel is burned in the combustion chamber at (ideally) constant pressure P₂ = P₃. The working fluid temperature rises from T₂ to T₃. **Step 3 — Expansion & Work Extraction (3 → 4):** Hot gases expand through the turbine isentropically from P₃ down to P₄ = P₁. The turbine produces shaft work. **Step 4 — Exhaust (4 → 1):** In the *open cycle*, hot exhaust is simply discharged to the atmosphere. In the *closed cycle*, the working fluid is cooled at constant pressure back to T₁. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every high-yield equation for Module 4. Memorize these for ESE (End Semester Examination) success. | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pressure Ratio | $r_p = P_2/P_1 = P_3/P_4$ | dimensionless | KTU's central variable | | 2 | Isentropic Compression Temperature | $T_2/T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | ratio | process 1–2 | | 3 | Isentropic Expansion Temperature | $T_3/T_4 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | ratio | process 3–4 | | 4 | Compressor Work (per kg) | $W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | ideal | | 5 | Turbine Work (per kg) | $W_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | ideal | | 6 | Net Work Output | $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$ | kJ/kg | KTU's golden result | | 7 | Heat Supplied | $Q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ/kg | | | 8 | Thermal Efficiency (IDEAL Brayton) | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | dimensionless | **CRITICAL** | | 9 | Compressor Efficiency | $\eta_C = \dfrac{W_{C,ideal}}{W_{C,actual}} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{2'} - T_1}$ | dimensionless | actual work > ideal | | 10 | Turbine Efficiency | $\eta_T = \dfrac{W_{T,actual}}{W_{T,ideal}} = \dfrac{T_3 - T_{4'}}{T_3 - T_4}$ | dimensionless | | | 11 | Regenerator Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{2'} - T_1}{T_4 - T_1}$ | dimensionless | varies 0 to 1 | | 12 | Air-Fuel Ratio (Mass) | $(A/F) = m_a/m_f$ | dimensionless | | | 13 | Stoichiometric O₂ for CₓHᵧ | $n_{O_2} = x + y/4$ | mol | | | 14 | Stoichiometric Air | $n_{air} = 4.76(x + y/4)$ | mol | includes 3.76 N₂ per O₂ | | 15 | Gravimetric Analysis | $G_i = m_i/\Sigma m_i$ | fraction | | | 16 | Volumetric Analysis | $V_i = n_i/\Sigma n_i$ | fraction | = mol fraction | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Brayton Cycle powers: - **Aircraft jet engines** (turbojets, turbofans) - **Industrial gas turbines** for power generation (combined cycle plants) - **Marine propulsion** (LM2500, MT30) - **Land-based power plants** (GE 7HA, Siemens SGT-9000HL) The **regeneration**, **intercooling**, and **reheating** modifications are not merely textbook exercises — they are precisely the engineering solutions that brought gas turbine efficiencies from ~25% (1950s) to over **64%** in modern combined cycle plants. ### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Efficiency Equation **Why is $\eta = 1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$?** For an ideal Brayton Cycle, both compressor and turbine are reversible. Heat in = $c_p(T_3-T_2)$, Net work = $c_p[(T_3-T_4)-(T_2-T_1)]$. Substituting the isentropic relations $T_2 = T_1 r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ and $T_4 = T_3/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, all temperatures cancel leaving only $r_p$ and γ. **Efficiency depends ONLY on pressure ratio and γ, not on temperature!** This is a beautiful and counter-intuitive result that KTU examiners love. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Ideal Brayton Cycle Thermal Efficiency We begin with the standard definition of thermal efficiency: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$ For the Brayton Cycle, heat input occurs at constant pressure (process 2–3), so: $$Q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_2)$$ Similarly, heat rejection (closed cycle, process 4–1): $$Q_{out} = c_p(T_4 - T_1)$$ Therefore: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$ Now apply the isentropic relations. For process 1–2 (isentropic compression of an ideal gas): $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ For process 3–4 (isentropic expansion): $$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ Therefore: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ $$T_4 = T_3 / r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ **Substituting these into the efficiency expression:** Let $\beta = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ for simplicity. Then: $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{th} &= 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} \\ &= 1 - \frac{(T_3/\beta) - T_1}{T_3 - (T_1 \cdot \beta)} \\ &= 1 - \frac{(T_3 - T_1 \beta)/\beta}{T_3 - T_1 \beta} \\ &= 1 - \frac{1}{\beta} \\ &= 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The cancellation above proves that the efficiency is *independent* of T₁ and T₃. This is why KTU examiners often ask "at what pressure ratio does the cycle achieve 50% efficiency?" — the answer depends only on γ. ### 3.2 Effect of Compressor and Turbine Efficiencies **Reality:** Real machines are not isentropic. Compressors and turbines are irreversible due to friction, turbulence, and non-ideal flow. **Compressor Work (real):** $$ W_{C,actual} = \frac{W_{C,ideal}}{\eta_C} = \frac{c_p(T_2 - T_1)}{\eta_C} = c_p(T_{2'} - T_1) $$ where T₂' is the actual compressor exit temperature (always higher than T₂). **Turbine Work (real):** $$W_{T,actual} = \eta_T \cdot W_{T,ideal} = \eta_T \cdot c_p(T_3 - T_4) = c_p(T_3 - T_{4'})$$ where T₄' is the actual turbine exit temperature (always higher than T₄). **Net Work Output (real):** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{net,actual} &= W_{T,actual} - W_{C,actual} \\ &= \eta_T \cdot c_p(T_3 - T_4) - \frac{c_p(T_2 - T_1)}{\eta_C} \end{aligned} $$ **Heat Input (real):** This remains $Q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_{2'})$ since combustion still occurs between actual compressor exit T₂' and turbine inlet T₃. **Thermal Efficiency (real):** $$\eta_{th,actual} = \frac{W_{net,actual}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{\eta_T \cdot c_p(T_3 - T_4) - c_p(T_2 - T_1)/\eta_C}{c_p(T_3 - T_{2'})}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write efficiency as $\eta_{th} = \eta_T \cdot \eta_C \cdot \eta_{ideal}$. This is WRONG. The actual efficiency must be computed by recalculating net work and heat input separately — the efficiencies do not multiply. ### 3.3 Derivation: Regenerative Brayton Cycle A regenerator (recuperator) uses hot turbine exhaust to preheat the compressor discharge air before it enters the combustion chamber. **Regenerator Effectiveness:** $$\varepsilon = \frac{T_{2'} - T_x}{T_4 - T_1}$$ where T_x is the temperature of air leaving the regenerator (entering combustion chamber). Solving: $$T_x = T_{2'} + \varepsilon(T_4 - T_1)$$ **Heat Input with regeneration:** $$Q_{in,reg} = c_p(T_3 - T_x)$$ **Net Work (unchanged):** $W_{net} = W_T - W_C$ **Thermal Efficiency with Regeneration:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{th,reg} &= 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{c_p(T_4 - T_1)}{c_p(T_3 - T_x)} \\ &= 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_x} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **When does regeneration help?** Only when T₄ > T₂' (i.e., turbine exhaust is hotter than compressor exit). This sets the operating range. KTU may ask: "For what pressure ratio is regeneration beneficial?" ### 3.4 Intercooling, Reheating — Combined Cycle Analysis **Intercooling:** Splits compression into two (or more) stages with cooling between stages. *Reduces* compressor work. **Reheating:** Splits expansion into two stages with heating between stages. *Increases* turbine work. **For a two-stage intercooled + two-stage reheated cycle with regeneration:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_C &= \frac{c_p \cdot T_1}{\eta_C}\left[2 r_{p,1}^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 2\right] \text{ (with intercooling to T₁)} \\ W_T &= \eta_T \cdot c_p \cdot T_3 \left[2 - \frac{2}{r_{p,2}^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}\right] \text{ (with reheating to T₃)} \\ W_{net} &= W_T - W_C \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.5 Combustion Stoichiometry — Full Derivation **General Combustion Equation for Hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ:** $$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Mass of Air Required (per kg fuel):** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{air,stoich} &= \frac{\text{moles O}_2 \times M_{O_2} + \text{moles N}_2 \times M_{N_2}}{\text{mass of fuel}} \\ &= \frac{(x + y/4)(32 + 3.76 \times 28)}{x \times 12 + y \times 1} \end{aligned} $$ **Example — Methane (CH₄, x=1, y=4):** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{air,stoich} &= \frac{(1 + 4/4)(32 + 3.76 \times 28)}{1 \times 12 + 4 \times 1} \\ &= \frac{2 \times (32 + 105.28)}{16} \\ &= \frac{2 \times 137.28}{16} = \frac{274.56}{16} = 17.16 \text{ kg air/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.6 Conversion: Gravimetric ↔ Volumetric Analysis **Given Gravimetric Analysis (mass fractions G_CO₂, G_CO, G_H₂O, G_N₂, G_O₂, G_SO₂):** Mass of each constituent per kg of flue gas = Gᵢ × 1 kg. Moles of each = Gᵢ / Mᵢ, where Mᵢ = molecular weight. **Volumetric Analysis** (since 1 mole = 22.4 L at NTP for any ideal gas): $$V_i = \frac{G_i / M_i}{\sum_j (G_j / M_j)}$$ **Given Volumetric Analysis (Vᵢ = mole fractions):** Mass per mole = Vᵢ × Mᵢ. Total mass per mole of flue gas = Σ(Vᵢ × Mᵢ). **Gravimetric Analysis:** $$G_i = \frac{V_i \cdot M_i}{\sum_j (V_j \cdot M_j)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Sanity Check:** Sum of Gᵢ = 1; Sum of Vᵢ = 1. ALWAYS verify your final fractions sum to 1.0 — this single check catches 80% of conversion arithmetic errors. ### 3.7 Air-Fuel Ratio, Equivalence Ratio, Excess Air **Actual A/F Ratio:** $$(A/F)_{actual} = \frac{m_{air,actual}}{m_{fuel}}$$ **Theoretical (Stoichiometric) A/F:** $$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air,stoich}}{m_{fuel}}$$ **Equivalence Ratio (φ):** $$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}} = \frac{m_{fuel,actual}/m_{air,actual}}{m_{fuel,stoich}/m_{air,stoich}}$$ - φ < 1 → Lean mixture (excess air) - φ = 1 → Stoichiometric - φ > 1 → Rich mixture (deficient air) **Excess Air:** $$\% \text{ Excess Air} = \frac{(A/F)_{actual} - (A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} \times 100$$ $$\% \text{ Excess Air} = \frac{O_2 \text{ in flue gas (by vol.)}}{0.21 \times N_2 \text{ in flue gas} - O_2 \text{ in flue gas}} \times 100$$ ## 4.
