KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Define the Brayton cycle. Draw its T-S and P-V diagrams, clearly labeling all four processes.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Brayton cycle is the ideal thermodynamic cycle for gas turbines, consisting of: - **Process 1–2:** Isentropic compression in the compressor - **Process 2–3:** Constant-pressure heat addition in the combustion chamber - **Process 3–4:** Isentropic expansion in the turbine - **Process 4–1:** Constant-pressure heat rejection (closed cycle) or exhaust to atmosphere (open cycle) Efficiency: $\eta_{th} = 1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ Diagrams: T-S diagram shows a "horizontal-then-vertical-then-horizontal-then-vertical" pattern; P-V diagram shows curves. **Marks Allocation:** Definition (1), Processes (1), Diagrams (1). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **Define the following terms with respect to gas turbine cycles: (a) Regenerator effectiveness, (b) Equivalence ratio, (c) Excess air.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** (a) **Regenerator Effectiveness (ε):** Ratio of actual temperature rise of compressed air to the maximum possible rise. $\varepsilon = (T_x - T_{2'})/(T_4 - T_1)$. (b) **Equivalence Ratio (φ):** Ratio of stoichiometric A/F to actual A/F. φ < 1 lean, φ = 1 stoichiometric, φ > 1 rich. (c) **Excess Air:** The amount of air supplied beyond stoichiometric requirement, expressed as a percentage of stoichiometric air. **Marks Allocation:** 1 mark per definition. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Internal Choice Format) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Module 4]** **(a) [7 Marks]** A gas turbine operates on the Brayton cycle with the following data: Pressure ratio = 8; T₁ = 300 K; T₃ = 1200 K; γ = 1.4; cₚ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K. Calculate: (i) Thermal efficiency, (ii) Net work output per kg of air, (iii) Work ratio, (iv) Mean effective pressure. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare gas turbine and IC engines on the basis of: working principle, combustion process, size, weight, starting, fuel flexibility, efficiency range, and applications (any 6 points). --- **Model Solution (a):** Given: r_p = 8, T₁ = 300 K, T₃ = 1200 K, γ = 1.4, cₚ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K. **Step 1: Compute β = r_p^((γ-1)/γ):** $$(\gamma - 1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$$ $$\beta = 8^{0.2857} = 1.8114$$ **Step 2: T₂ (isentropic compression exit):** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \beta = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.4 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: T₄ (isentropic expansion exit):** $$T_4 = T_3/\beta = 1200/1.8114 = 662.5 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4: (i) Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - 1/\beta = 1 - 1/1.8114 = 1 - 0.5520 = 0.4480 = \mathbf{44.80\%}$$ **Step 5: (ii) Net Work Output:** $$W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (543.4 - 300) = 244.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 662.5) = 540.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 540.7 - 244.7 = \mathbf{295.96 \approx 296 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Step 6: (iii) Work Ratio:** $$W_R = W_{net}/W_T = 296/540.7 = \mathbf{0.5475}$$ **Step 7: (iv) Mean Effective Pressure (MEP):** First, find volumes (using ideal gas law, R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K): $$v_1 = RT_1/P_1; \quad v_2 = RT_2/(r_p P_1); \quad v_4 = RT_4/P_1$$ Work ratio approach: $W_{net} = MEP \times (v_1 - v_2)$, so: $$MEP = W_{net}/(v_1 - v_2) = W_{net}/[RT_1/P_1 - RT_2/(r_p P_1)]$$ Using P₁ = 100 kPa as reference: $$v_1 - v_2 = (R/P_1)[T_1 - T_2/r_p] = (0.287/100)[300 - 543.4/8] = 0.00287 \times (300 - 67.925) = 0.6660 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ $$MEP = 296/0.6660 = \mathbf{444.4 \text{ kPa}}$$ --- **Model Solution (b):** | Feature | Gas Turbine | IC Engine | |---|---|---| | Combustion | Continuous, external | Intermittent, internal | | Cycle | Brayton (open/closed) | Otto/Diesel (closed) | | Size & Weight | Compact, light per kW | Bulkier per kW | | Starting | Needs starter motor | Self-starting (Otto, Diesel) | | Fuel Flexibility | Wide (gas, liquid) | Restricted to specific fuels | | Speed | Very high (10,000+ rpm) | Limited by reciprocating inertia | | Efficiency | 35–45% simple | 35–45% modern | | Applications | Aviation, large power | Automobiles, small power | | Torque | Low at low speed | High at low speed | | Noise | High whine | Moderate | | Maintenance | Less frequent | More frequent | | Cooling | Mostly air | Water/air | **Marks:** 7 marks → 6 points × 1 mark each + 1 mark for presentation. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Module 4]** **(a) [7 Marks]** A regenerative gas turbine has the following data: Pressure ratio = 6; T₁ = 300 K; T₃ = 1100 K; T₄ = 700 K; regenerator effectiveness = 0.75. Assume ideal compressor and turbine. Find: (i) Temperature of air leaving the regenerator (entering combustion chamber). (ii) Net work output per kg. (iii) Thermal efficiency with regeneration. (iv) Thermal efficiency without regeneration (for comparison). **(b) [7 Marks]** Coal has the following composition by mass: C = 78%, H = 5%, O = 7%, N = 2%, S = 3%, Ash = 5%. Calculate: (i) Minimum air required per kg of coal for complete combustion. (ii) Dry flue gas analysis (by volume, ignoring moisture). (iii) Wet flue gas analysis (by volume, including moisture). Assume 20% excess air. --- **Model Solution (a):** Given: r_p = 6, T₁ = 300 K, T₃ = 1100 K, T₄ = 700 K, ε = 0.75, γ = 1.4. **Step 1: Find T₂ (compressor exit, ideal):** $$\beta = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 6^{0.2857} = 1.668$$ $$T_2 = 300 \times 1.668 = 500.4 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: (i) Regenerator outlet temperature T_x:** $$T_x = T_2 + \varepsilon(T_4 - T_1) = 500.4 + 0.75 \times (700 - 300)$$ $$T_x = 500.4 + 300 = \mathbf{800.4 \text{ K}}$$ **Step 3: (ii) Net Work Output (unchanged by regeneration):** $$W_C = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (500.4 - 300) = 201.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_T = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1100 - 700) = 402.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{net} = 402.0 - 201.6 = \mathbf{200.4 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Step 4: (iii) Thermal Efficiency WITH Regeneration:** $$Q_{in,reg} = c_p(T_3 - T_x) = 1.005 \times (1100 - 800.4) = 301.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\eta_{th,reg} = W_{net}/Q_{in,reg} = 200.4/301.0 = \mathbf{0.6658 = 66.58\%}$$ **Step 5: (iv) Thermal Efficiency WITHOUT Regeneration:** $$Q_{in,no-reg} = c_p(T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1100 - 500.4) = 602.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\eta_{th,no-reg} = 200.4/602.5 = \mathbf{0.3326 = 33.26\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Observation:** Regeneration **doubled** the thermal efficiency (33% → 66%) in this case! This dramatic improvement explains why all modern gas turbines include regenerators. --- **Model Solution (b):** Given: C = 0.78, H = 0.05, O = 0.07, N = 0.02, S = 0.03, Ash = 0.05 (per kg coal). **Step 1: Determine oxygen needed for each element per kg coal:** - For C: $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$ → moles O₂ = 0.78/12 = 0.065 kmol - For H₂ equivalent: $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ → moles O₂ = 0.05/(4) = 0.0125 kmol - For S: $\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2$ → moles O₂ = 0.03/32 = 0.0009375 kmol - Available O₂ in fuel = 0.07/32 = 0.0021875 kmol **Step 2: Net O₂ required (stoichiometric):** $$O_2 \text{ required} = 0.065 + 0.0125 + 0.0009375 - 0.0021875 = 0.07625 \text{ kmol/kg fuel}$$ **Step 3: (i) Minimum air required (stoichiometric):** Mass of O₂ = 0.07625 × 32 = 2.44 kg/kg fuel Mass of N₂ = 0.07625 × 3.76 × 28 = 8.027 kg/kg fuel **Minimum Air = 2.44 + 8.027 = 10.47 kg air/kg fuel** **Step 4: With 20% excess air:** Actual air = 10.47 × 1.20 = **12.56 kg air/kg fuel** **Step 5: Determine flue gas composition:** Products (per kg coal): - CO₂ = 0.78/12 = 0.065 kmol → mass = 0.065 × 44 = 2.86 kg - H₂O = 0.05/2 = 0.025 kmol → mass = 0.025 × 18 = 0.45 kg - SO₂ = 0.03/32 = 0.0009375 kmol → mass = 0.0009375 × 64 = 0.06 kg - N₂ from air = 0.07625 × 1.20 × 3.76 = 0.3441 kmol → mass = 0.3441 × 28 = 9.635 kg - O₂ excess = 0.07625 × 0.20 = 0.01525 kmol → mass = 0.01525 × 32 = 0.488 kg Total flue gas moles (dry basis, excluding H₂O): - CO₂ = 0.065 - SO₂ = 0.0009375 - N₂ = 0.3441 - O₂ = 0.01525 - **Total dry = 0.4253 kmol** **(ii) Dry Flue Gas Analysis (by volume):** - CO₂ = 0.065/0.4253 = **15.28%** - SO₂ = 0.0009375/0.4253 = **0.22%** - N₂ = 0.3441/0.4253 = **80.91%** - O₂ = 0.01525/0.4253 = **3.59%** Total = 100.00% ✓ **(iii) Wet Flue Gas Analysis (by volume, including H₂O):** Total wet moles = 0.4253 + 0.025 = 0.4503 kmol - CO₂ = 0.065/0.4503 = **14.43%** - SO₂ = 0.0009375/0.4503 = **0.21%** - H₂O = 0.025/0.4503 = **5.55%** - N₂ = 0.3441/0.4503 = **76.42%** - O₂ = 0.01525/0.4503 = **3.39%** Total = 100.00% ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > > 1. **Confusing T₂ and T₂':** T₂ is the *ideal* isentropic exit; T₂' is the *actual* exit (with efficiency < 1). Always check which the problem is asking for. > 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 3.76 for N₂:** When converting from O₂ requirement to air requirement, students often give air in terms of O₂ alone. ALWAYS multiply by 4.76 for total air moles (or 3.76 for N₂ moles). > 3. **Mixing dry and wet flue gas:** KTU's Orsat analysis is *dry*. If a question gives Orsat + asks for wet, remember to add H₂O. If it gives wet + asks for dry, subtract H₂O. > 4. **Neglecting N₂ in fuel:** Some fuels (coal, biomass) contain N. Account for it in flue gas N₂ total. > 5. **Equivalence ratio direction:** φ = stoich/actual. Many students invert this. Always verify: φ = 1.5 means rich (less air than stoich needed). > 6. **Unit mismatch:** Always convert temperatures to Kelvin, pressures to kPa, and masses to kg before substituting. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 4 — High-Density Revision Checklist** **🔹 Brayton Cycle Fundamentals** - ☐ 4 processes: 2 isentropic + 2 isobaric (constant P) - ☐ Ideal efficiency depends ONLY on pressure ratio and γ: $\eta = 1 - r_p^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ - ☐ Pressure ratio optimal ~ 8–12 for simple cycle; higher for combined cycles - ☐ Net work = Turbine work − Compressor work = $c_p[(T_3-T_4)-(T_2-T_1)]$ **🔹 Real Cycle Effects** - ☐ Compressor efficiency < 1 → T₂' > T₂ → MORE heat input needed - ☐ Turbine efficiency < 1 → T₄' > T₄ → LESS work produced - ☐ Actual efficiency is NOT simply η_T × η_C × η_ideal - ☐ Real efficiency ALWAYS less than ideal efficiency **🔹 Improvements** - ☐ **Regeneration:** Uses turbine exhaust to preheat compressed air → reduces Q_in → higher η. Effectiveness ε = (T_x − T₂')/(T₄ − T_1) - ☐ **Intercooling:** Lowers compressor exit T → reduces W_C - ☐ **Reheating:** Raises intermediate pressure → increases W_T - ☐ Best when combined: regeneration + intercooling + reheating **🔹 Combustion & Fuels** - ☐ Combustion equation: $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + (x + y/4)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + (y/2)\text{H}_2\text{O}$ - ☐ Air: 1 mol O₂ + 3.76 mol N₂ → 4.76 mol air per O₂ - ☐ Stoichiometric A/F for methane = 17.16 kg/kg (memorize!) - ☐ Stoichiometric A/F for octane = 15.13 kg/kg **🔹 Combustion Analysis** - ☐ Gravimetric (mass) ↔ Volumetric (mole) conversion via molecular weights - ☐ Conversion formula: $V_i = (G_i/M_i)/\Sigma(G_j/M_j)$ - ☐ Reverse: $G_i = (V_i M_i)/\Sigma(V_j M_j)$ - ☐ ALWAYS verify fractions sum to 1.0 **🔹 Operating Parameters** - ☐ (A/F)_actual vs (A/F)_stoich - ☐ Equivalence ratio φ = (A/F)_stoich / (A/F)_actual - ☐ % Excess Air = [(A/F)_actual − (A/F)_stoich]/(A/F)_stoich × 100 - ☐ φ < 1 = lean; φ = 1 = stoichiometric; φ > 1 = rich **🔹 Gas Turbine vs IC Engine** - ☐ Gas turbine: continuous combustion, high power-to-weight, needs starter - ☐ IC engine: intermittent combustion, self-starting, lower speed - ☐ Gas turbine used for aviation and large power; IC for automobiles **🔹 Combustion Chambers** - ☐ Three types: Can-type, Annular-type, Can-annular - ☐ Modern jets use annular for compactness and uniform flow - ☐ Key requirements: stable flame, complete combustion, low pressure loss **🔹 Flue Gas Composition** - ☐ Orsat analysis: dry basis → CO₂, CO, O₂, N₂ only (no H₂O) - ☐ Excess air deduced from O₂ in flue gas: %EA = O₂/(0.21N₂ − O₂) × 100 - ☐ Incomplete combustion forms CO (toxic) — design goal to minimize
